Life of Imam al-Juwayni
beginner
4 min
Imam al-Juwayni: Lineage and Nicknames
Editor provides biographical details of Juwayni's lineage, birth, death, and nickname.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the biographical background of Imam al-Juwayni, including his lineage, attributions, and the significance of his nickname 'Imam al-Haramayn'.

Core Idea
A scholar's name and titles carry the story of his life and learning.

Before diving into the theological content of al-Aqida al-Nizamiyya, it is essential to know its author. Imam al-Haramayn al-Juwayni is a towering figure in Islamic intellectual history. This passage from the editor's introduction provides key details about his lineage, birth, and the origins of his famous nickname. Understanding these details enriches our appreciation of his work.

Guided Path
1
Read the biography carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the detailed vocalization of the name 'Hayawayh'.
2
Trace the attributions
Identify why he is called 'al-Juwayni' and 'al-Naysaburi'. How did the nisbah transfer from his father? What does this tell about scholarly lineage?
His father was known as al-Juwayni from his birthplace, and it passed to his son.
3
Understand the nickname
Why 'Imam al-Haramayn'? What did he do during his four years in Mecca? Reflect on how this nickname signifies his scholarly authority.
He debated and taught during his stay, earning him the title.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المؤلف : هو الشيخ عبد الملك بن عبد الله بن يوسف بن محمد بن عبد الله بن حيوية - بضم الياء الأولى مشددة وفتح الثانية - وقد نسب إلى جوين و « نيسابور » وهما بلدتان من بلدان «فارس». وكان أبوه عبد الله من العلماء، وكذلك ابن عمه من العلماء وكان يلقب بشيخ الحجاز. وقد نسب إلى جوين ولم يولد بها، لأن أباه كان معروفا بالجوينى نسبة إلى مسقط رأسه جوين، ولما مات وجلس ابنه عبد الملك مكانه للتدريس انتقلت إليه هذه النسبة. وقد نسب إلى نيسابور لطول إقامته فيها. ولقب بإمام الحرمين لأنه - كما قيل - جاور بمكة أربع سنوات كان خلالها يناظر ويلقى الدروس. وقد اختلف المترجمون في تحديد تاريخ ميلاد عبد الملك إمام الحرمين فيثبته البعض على أنه كان في ١٨ محرم عام ٤١٩ هـ ويرى البعض الآخر أنه كان في عام ٤١٧ هـ غير أنهم أجمعوا على أنه توفى عام ٤٧٨ هـ وله من العمر تسع وخمسون سنة. وتقول الدكتورة الأستاذة فوقية حسين محمود : « وهذا ينتهي بنا بعد التحقيق والتمحيص إلى أن مولده كان في اليوم الثامن عشر من المحرم عام ٤١٩ هـ المرافق لليوم الثاني والعشرين من شهر فبراير عام ١٠٢٨م ».
The author: He is the Sheikh 'Abd al-Malik ibn 'Abd Allah ibn Yusuf ibn Muhammad ibn 'Abd Allah ibn Hayawayh - with the first ya' having a dammah and shaddah, and the second having a fatha -. And he was attributed to Juwayn and 'Naysabur', both towns of 'Fars'. His father 'Abd Allah was among the scholars, and likewise his cousin was among the scholars and was nicknamed Shaykh al-Hijaz. And he was attributed to Juwayn but not born there, because his father was known as al-Juwayni due to his birthplace Juwayn, and when he died and his son 'Abd al-Malik sat in his place for teaching, this attribution transferred to him. And he was attributed to Naysabur due to his long residence there. And he was nicknamed Imam al-Haramayn because - as it is said - he resided in Mecca for four years during which he debated and gave lessons. And the biographers differed in determining the date of birth of 'Abd al-Malik Imam al-Haramayn: some affirm it was on 18 Muharram 419 AH, and others hold it was in 417 AH. However they agreed that he died in 478 AH at the age of fifty-nine. And Dr. Professor Fawqiyyah Husayn Mahmud says: 'This leads us, after investigation and scrutiny, to the conclusion that his birth was on the eighteenth of Muharram 419 AH, corresponding to the twenty-second of February 1028 CE.'
Source pages p. 4

Commentary
This passage provides a biography of the author, Imam al-Haramayn al-Juwayni. It begins by stating his full name: 'Abd al-Malik ibn 'Abd Allah ibn Yusuf ibn Muhammad ibn 'Abd Allah ibn Hayawayh, with a note on the pronunciation of the last name. It mentions his attribution to two places: Juwayn and Naysabur, both in the region of Fars. It explains that his attribution to Juwayn came from his father, who was originally from Juwayn; when the father died and 'Abd al-Malik took over his teaching position, he inherited the nisbah. He was also called al-Naysaburi due to his long stay in Naysabur. The passage then explains the nickname 'Imam al-Haramayn', meaning 'Imam of the Two Sanctuaries' (Mecca and Medina), because he resided in Mecca for four years, engaging in debates and teaching. Finally, it addresses the controversy over his birth year: some say 419 AH, others 417 AH, but all agree he died in 478 AH at age 59. It then quotes Dr. Fawqiyyah Husayn Mahmud who, after verification, concludes the birth was on 18 Muharram 419 AH (22 Feb 1028 CE). This biography is typical of introductory sections in classical works, establishing the author's lineage and scholarly credentials.

Key Terms
إمام الحرمين
الجويني
نيسابور
عبد الملك
حيوية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Imam al-Juwayni was born in Juwayn.
Correction: The passage clarifies that he was not born in Juwayn; his father was. He inherited the nisbah 'al-Juwayni' from his father after taking over his teaching position.

Checkpoint
Why was Juwayni given the nickname 'Imam al-Haramayn'?
A) He was born in Mecca
B) He taught in both Mecca and Medina
C) He resided in Mecca for four years debating and teaching
D) He was the imam of two mosques

Key Takeaway
Imam al-Juwayni's name and titles reflect his scholarly lineage, his geographical associations, and his prestigious role as a teacher in the Two Sanctuaries.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Juwayni earned the title 'Imam al-Haramayn' through years of teaching and debate in Mecca. What title or reputation are you building through your daily efforts—especially in seeking and sharing knowledge? Does it align with what you want to be remembered for before Allah?
?
Your name and lineage—like Juwayni's—carry a story. What story does your own name tell? Are you living up to the legacy of those who taught you, or are you content with simply inheriting labels without earning them?
?
Scholars differed over Juwayni's birth year, but his death year and impact are certain. Reflect on your own life: what are you doing with the years you have? If your biography were written today, would it mention a legacy of teaching, raising the next generation, or something less lasting?

Life of Imam al-Juwayni
beginner
4 min
Imam al-Juwayni: Name, Lineage, and Geographical Affiliations
Identifies the author's full name, lineage, and his nisbas Juwaini and Naysaburi.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the full name and lineage of the author of Al-'Aqida al-Nizamiyya and how he acquired his famous geographical attributions (nisbas).

Core Idea
A scholar's nisba often tells a story of family and migration, not birthplace.

This opening passage of Al-'Aqida al-Nizamiyya introduces the author himself: Imam al-Haramayn al-Juwayni. Before you study his creedal work, it's important to know who he was, where he came from, and how he earned his famous titles. The text clarifies his full lineage and explains the origins of his two key geographical attributions, Juwayni and Naysaburi.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Begin by reading the Arabic passage and its literal translation. Notice the author's full name and the two nisbas mentioned.
2
Understand the lineage
Identify the chain of names: Abd al-Malik ibn Abd Allah ibn Yusuf ibn Muhammad. Note that his father and cousin were also scholars.
This shows a family of learning.
3
Trace the nisba 'al-Juwayni'
The text explains that although he is called al-Juwayni, he was not born in Juwain. His father was from Juwain, and the nisba transferred to him when he inherited his father's teaching position.
A nisba can be inherited, not just indicate birthplace.
4
Understand the nisba 'al-Naysaburi'
He was also attributed to Naysabur due to his long residence there. This shows that nisba can be based on place of residence.
5
Reflect on the significance
Consider why the author begins his book with his own name and nisbas. This establishes his authority and lineage in Islamic scholarship.
Classical scholars often introduced themselves to authenticate their works.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مؤلف کتاب العقيدة النظامية في الأركان الإسلامية الملك بن عبد عبد الله بن يوسف بن محمد. المؤلف : هو الشيخ : عبد الملك بن عبد الله بن يوسف بن محمد حيوية - بضم الياء الأولى مشددة وفتح الثانية ـ وقد نسب الى جوين » و « نيسابور ( وهما بلدتان من بلدان «فارس » وكان أبوه عبد الله من العلماء . وكذلك كابن عمه من العلماء وكان يلقب بشيخ الحجاز وقد نسب الى جوين » ولم يولد بها . لأن أباه كان معروفا بالجوينى نسبة الى مسقط رأسه جوين ، ولما مات وجلس ابنه عبد الملك مكانه للتدريس انتقلت اليه هذه النسبة . وقد نسب الى نيسابور » لطول اقامته فيها .
Author of the book Al-Aqida Al-Nizamiyyah on the pillars of Islam: Al-Malik ibn Abd Abd Allah ibn Yusuf ibn Muhammad. The author is: the Shaykh: Abd al-Malik ibn Abd Allah ibn Yusuf ibn Muhammad Juwayni - with the first ya' doubled and damma and the second ya' fatha. And he was attributed to Juwain and Naysabur (two towns of Persia). And his father Abd Allah was among the scholars, as was his cousin, and he was called Shaykh al-Hijaz. And he was attributed to Juwain but was not born there, because his father was known as al-Juwayni by attribution to his birthplace Juwain; when he died, his son Abd al-Malik took his place for teaching, so this attribution transferred to him. And he was attributed to Naysabur due to his long residence there.
Source pages p. 4

Commentary
This passage introduces the author of the book Al-Aqida Al-Nizamiyyah. It begins by stating the author's full name: Abd al-Malik ibn Abd Allah ibn Yusuf ibn Muhammad, with a note on the pronunciation of 'Juwayni'. The text then explains his two main geographical attributions: al-Juwayni (from Juwain) and al-Naysaburi (from Naysabur), both towns in Persia. It clarifies that although he was attributed to Juwain, he was not born there; rather, his father was from Juwain, and after his father's death, when Abd al-Malik inherited his teaching position, the attribution transferred to him. His attribution to Naysabur stems from his lengthy stay there. Additionally, the passage notes that his father was a scholar and his cousin was also a scholar, and that his father was known as Shaykh al-Hijaz.

Key Terms
العقيدة النظامية
الجويني
نيسابور
شيخ الحجاز

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The nisba 'al-Juwayni' indicates that the author was born in Juwain.
Correction: The author was not born in Juwain; the nisba transferred from his father who was from Juwain.

Checkpoint
Why was Abd al-Malik ibn Abd Allah attributed to Juwain despite not being born there?
A) He was born in Juwain.
B) His father was from Juwain and the nisba transferred to him.
C) He taught in Juwain.
D) He founded a school in Juwain.

Key Takeaway
Imam al-Juwayni's nisba 'al-Juwayni' was inherited from his father, not his birthplace, while 'al-Naysaburi' came from long residence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The author inherited his father's nisba and teaching position. Is there a spiritual or intellectual legacy from your family that you are expected to carry forward? How are you actively taking it up, and what holds you back?
?
This lesson shows that identity can be shaped by where we live rather than where we are born. When you look at your own life, are you more defined by where you come from or where you are going? Which one should matter more to your faith?

Life of Imam al-Juwayni
beginner
5 min
The Title and Life Dates of Imam al-Juwayni
Explains the title 'Imam al-Haramayn' and presents the scholarly disagreement on birth and death dates.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the origin of Imam al-Juwayni's title 'Imam al-Haramayn' and the scholarly consensus on his death date despite disagreement on his birth.

Core Idea
Imam al-Juwayni's title reflects his years of teaching in Mecca, and his death date is universally agreed upon.

This lesson introduces a key biographical detail about Imam al-Juwayni: his honorific title and the timeline of his life. The passage explains why he is called 'Imam al-Haramayn' and presents the scholarly discussion around his birth and death dates. Understanding these facts helps contextualize his theological works and his place in Islamic intellectual history.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the translation to grasp the full narrative.
Notice the structure: reason for title, then dates, then a researcher's conclusion.
2
Focus on the title explanation
Identify the reason given for the title 'Imam al-Haramayn'. Note the duration of his stay in Mecca and his activities there.
The title is linked to his teaching and debating in Mecca.
3
Examine the date disagreement
Look at the two conflicting birth years (419 AH vs 417 AH) and the agreement on the death year (478 AH). Understand that biographers differ but find common ground on his age at death.
4
Consider the resolution
Read Dr. Fawqiyya's conclusion that after verification, the correct birth date is 18 Muharram 419 AH. Reflect on how scholarly investigation can resolve such discrepancies.
This shows the importance of rigorous historical research.
5
Reflect on the significance
Think about why knowing these details matters for studying Imam al-Juwayni's theology. His life context enriches understanding of his works.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ولقب بامام الحرمين لأنه - كما قيل - جاور بمكة أربع سنوات كان خلالها يناظر ويلقى الدروس . وقد اختلف المترجمون في تحديد تاريخ ميلاد عبد الملك امام الحرمين فيثبته البعض على أنه كان فى ١٨ محرم عام ٤١٩ هـ ويرى البعض الآخر أنه كان فى عام ٤١٧ هـ غير أنهم أجمعوا على أنه توفى عام ٤٧٨ هـ وله من العمر تسع وخمسون سنة وتقول الدكتورة الأستاذة فوقية حسين محمود : « وهذا ينتهى ينا بعد التحقيق والتمحيص الى أن مولده كان في اليوم الثامن عشر من المحرم عام ٤١٩ هـ المرافق لليوم الثانى والعشرين من شهر فبراير عام ١٠٢٨م »
And he was titled Imam al-Haramayn because - as is said - he resided in Mecca for four years, during which he debated and gave lessons. And the biographers differed in determining the date of birth of Abd al-Malik, Imam al-Haramayn. Some affixed it as 18 Muharram 419 AH, and others saw it as 417 AH. However, they agreed that he died in 478 AH at the age of fifty-nine. And Dr. Fawqiyya Husayn Mahmud says: 'And this leads us, after verification and scrutiny, to the conclusion that his birth was on the 18th of Muharram 419 AH, corresponding to the 22nd of February 1028 CE.'
Source pages p. 4

Commentary
This passage continues the biography of Imam al-Juwayni, focusing on his honorific title 'Imam al-Haramayn' (Leader of the Two Sanctuaries) and the controversy surrounding his birth date. The title is explained by his four-year stay in Mecca, where he engaged in scholarly debates and gave lessons. The text then notes that biographers disagree on his birth date: some say 18 Muharram 419 AH, others say 417 AH. Despite this, they unanimously agree he died in 478 AH at age 59. The passage concludes by citing Dr. Fawqiyya Husayn Mahmud, who concludes after investigation that the correct birth date is 18 Muharram 419 AH, corresponding to 22 February 1028 CE.

Key Terms
إمام الحرمين
محرم
هجري

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: People might think 'Imam al-Haramayn' was a formal office or position, but it was a title earned through teaching in Mecca.
Correction: The passage explains it was a title given because he resided in Mecca for four years, debating and giving lessons, not an official appointment.

Checkpoint
On what point did biographers of Imam al-Juwayni agree?
A) His birth date is 419 AH.
B) His birth date is 417 AH.
C) He died in 478 AH at age 59.
D) He was born in Juwain.

Key Takeaway
Imam al-Juwayni earned the title 'Imam al-Haramayn' by teaching in Mecca, and while his birth date is disputed, scholars agree he died in 478 AH at age 59.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Imam al-Juwayni earned his title by sacrificing four years of comfort in Mecca to teach and debate. What are you sacrificing right now – in your time, energy, or reputation – to earn lasting honor with Allah? Name one concrete action you will take this week to prioritize seeking sacred knowledge or serving others over your own ease.
?
The scholars agreed on his death date but not his birth date, reminding us that it's not where you start but where you end that truly matters. Are you more preoccupied with your past mistakes or with how you want to leave this world? What one change must you make today so that your final chapter brings you closer to Allah?
?
Dr. Fawqiyya's careful investigation resolved the birth date dispute, showing the importance of verifying knowledge. When it comes to your own beliefs and practices, do you blindly follow what others say or do you actively seek out authentic sources? Commit to verifying one matter of your deen this month that you have been uncertain about.

Life of Imam al-Juwayni
beginner
5 min
Imam al-Juwayni's Scholarly Formation and Independence
Biographical account of al-Juwayni's early education and independence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Juwayni's early education under his father shaped his mastery of Islamic sciences and his independent critical approach to scholarship.

Core Idea
True scholarship combines humble learning with respectful critique.

Imam al-Juwayni's intellectual journey began at his father's feet, where he absorbed the foundations of Islamic law, Arabic, and Quran. Yet his story is not one of blind imitation; it is a model of how deep learning leads to thoughtful independence. This passage introduces both his educational foundation and his courageous yet respectful review of his father's work.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once to get the flow, then read the translation. Notice the two-part structure: education and independence.
The passage is short but rich in meaning.
2
Identify the subjects he studied
List the specific fields Imam al-Juwayni learned from his father: fiqh, madhhab, khilaf, usul, Arabic, and Quran. Reflect on the breadth of his training.
These are core Islamic sciences.
3
Contrast his studies with his independence
After his education, the text says he reviewed his father on some issues. Notice the phrase 'رجاحة عقله' (soundness of his intellect) that enabled this.
This shows education did not limit his thinking.
4
Analyze the respectful critique
Examine the phrase he used for his father's errors: 'هذه زلة من الشيخ رحمه الله' (This is a slip of the shaykh, may Allah have mercy on him). How does this balance criticism with respect?
This phrase is widely quoted as an example of adab.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وتعلم أول ما تعلم على يد والده ، فأخذ عنه الفقه واجتهد معه فى المذهب والخلاف والأصول وتعلم العربية واتقن علومها وحفظ القرآن . وكان الرجاحة عقله وغزارة علمه وحرية رأيه يراجع أباه في بعض مسائل من العلم فى حياته وبعد مماته . فقد روى عنه الرواة : أنه يردد عبارة خاصة كلما وقع على بعض أخطاء لوالده في كتاباته وهى : « هذه زلة من الشيخ رحمه الله ) (۱) ا.هـ.
He learned first what he learned at the hands of his father, taking from him fiqh and striving with him in madhhab, khilaf, and usul, and he learned Arabic, mastered its sciences, and memorized the Quran. And his intellect, vast knowledge, and independence of opinion led him to review his father on some issues of knowledge during his lifetime and after his death. It has been narrated from him by transmitters that he used to repeat a special phrase whenever he came across errors of his father in his writings: 'This is a slip of the shaykh, may Allah have mercy on him.'
Source pages p. 5

Commentary
This passage describes Imam al-Juwayni's formative education. He studied first under his father, from whom he learned fiqh (jurisprudence), and he worked hard with him on madhhab (legal school), khilaf (disputed issues), and usul (principles). He also studied Arabic, mastering its sciences, and memorized the Quran. The text highlights his intellectual independence: despite being a student of his father, his sound intellect, abundant knowledge, and free opinion led him to critique his father's works, both during his father's life and after his death. Transmitters report that whenever he found mistakes in his father's writings, he would say: 'This is a slip of the shaykh, may Allah have mercy on him.' This shows a respectful yet objective scholarly attitude.

Key Terms
الفقه
المذهب
الخلاف
الأصول
رجاحة العقل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that Imam al-Juwayni blindly followed his father without question.
Correction: The passage explicitly states that he reviewed his father on some issues and pointed out errors, showing that he was not a blind follower.

Checkpoint
How did Imam al-Juwayni refer to his father's errors in his writings?
A) 'This is wrong.'
B) 'This is a slip of the shaykh, may Allah have mercy on him.'
C) 'My father was mistaken.'
D) 'This is not correct.'

Key Takeaway
Imam al-Juwayni exemplifies how rigorous education under a revered teacher can coexist with respectful intellectual independence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you discover an error in a teacher's or parent's understanding, do you respond with the same respect and mercy as al-Juwayni, or does it shake your respect for them? What would it take for you to adopt his attitude?
?
Al-Juwayni's independence came only after he had mastered his father's teachings. Are you still building a solid foundation of knowledge before forming independent opinions, or are you jumping to criticism without first learning deeply?
?
If you were to find a genuine slip in someone you admire, would you have the courage to note it and still pray for them as al-Juwayni did—or would you remain silent or let it diminish your view of them?

Life of Imam al-Juwayni
beginner
4 min
Imam al-Juwayni's Rejection of Blind Following
Quotes Imam al-Juwayni's own words rejecting taqlid and describing his scholarly quest.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Imam al-Juwayni's rejection of blind imitation and his courageous pursuit of truth through comprehensive study, including philosophy.

Core Idea
True scholarship requires courage to explore beyond inherited dogmas.

Imam al-Juwayni, known for his intellectual independence, openly criticized blind imitation. In this passage, he describes his own scholarly journey—reading vast amounts, then daring to venture into fields others avoided, all in sincere search of truth. This lesson examines his words and the scholarly attitude they embody.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Start by reading the Arabic passage followed by the English translation. Notice Juwayni's strong language and the vivid metaphors he uses.
Pay attention to the key terms: ta'lif (bigotry) and taqlid (blind following).
2
Understand the key terms
Identify the meaning of التعصب (bigotry) and التقليد (blind imitation). These are central to Juwayni's critique.
In Islamic scholarship, taqlid is often contrasted with ijtihad.
3
Analyze Juwayni's metaphor
Consider the phrase 'ركبت البحر الخضم' (I mounted the deep sea) and 'غصت في الذي نهى أهل الإسلام عنه' (plunged into what the people of Islam forbade). What does this convey about his scholarly courage?
This metaphor suggests a journey into unknown and potentially dangerous intellectual waters.
4
Reflect on the term 'philosophy'
The commentary explains that 'falsafa' at the time meant all knowledge. Why is it significant that Juwayni studied it despite societal disapproval?
This shows his commitment to seeking truth wherever it may be found.
5
Connect to the lesson's core idea
How does Juwayni's attitude challenge the common misunderstanding mentioned? Write a one-sentence summary of the lesson's core idea.
The core idea is that true scholarship requires courage to explore beyond inherited dogmas.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وكان يكره التعصب والتقليد . ومن عباراته قوله فى هذا الشأن : « لقد قرأت خمسين ألفا ، في خمسين ألفا ، ثم خليت أهل الاسلام بإسلامهم فيها ، وعلومهم الظاهرة ، وركبت البحر الخضم ، وغصت في الذي نهى أهل الإسلام عنه ، كل ذلك في طلب الحق . وكنت أهرب في سالف الدهر من التقليد (۲) ا.هـ . ويبدو أن امام الحرمين قد خاض فى العلوم على اختلافها ، وحصل كل ما كان مندرجا يومئذ تحت لفظ « فلسفة » اذ كان مفهوم فى تلك الأيام يعنى جميع المعارف
He used to dislike bigotry and blind following. Among his statements on this matter is his saying: 'I have read fifty thousand [books], then fifty thousand, then I left the people of Islam with their Islam and their manifest sciences, and I mounted the deep sea and plunged into what the people of Islam forbade, all of that in search of truth. And I used to flee from blind imitation in ages past.' And it appears that Imam al-Haramayn delved into various sciences and acquired everything that was included at that time under the term 'philosophy', for the concept in those days meant all knowledge.
Source pages p. 5

Commentary
This passage highlights Imam al-Juwayni's scholarly attitude. It begins by stating his dislike for bigotry (ta'assub) and blind following (taqlid). Then it quotes his own words: he read an enormous number of books (fifty thousand, twice), then left behind the superficial obedience of Muslims to their own Islam and apparent sciences, and instead embarked on a deep intellectual journey—'riding the deep sea' and 'plunging' into what was forbidden by Muslims—all for the sake of seeking truth. He emphasizes that he had always fled from taqlid. The passage then notes that he studied various sciences, including what was then called 'philosophy' (falsafa), which in those days encompassed all branches of knowledge.

Key Terms
التعصب
التقليد
البحر الخضم
فلسفة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think Juwayni's statement about plunging into forbidden subjects indicates heresy or abandoning Islam.
Correction: Actually, he was exploring philosophy and other sciences without blind rejection, all in sincere pursuit of truth. His phrase underscores his intellectual courage, not disbelief.

Checkpoint
What did Imam al-Haramayn say he did in search of truth?
A) He only followed his father.
B) He read fifty thousand books and then immersed himself in forbidden subjects.
C) He confined himself to Islamic sciences.
D) He rejected philosophy entirely.

Key Takeaway
Imam al-Juwayni exemplifies a scholar who rejects blind conformity and seeks truth through bold, comprehensive inquiry.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
What 'deep seas' of knowledge or difficult questions have you been avoiding because they challenge your comfortable assumptions? Will you now dare to plunge in for the sake of truth?
?
Imam al-Juwayni read fifty thousand books before setting aside superficial understanding. Have you ever been satisfied with shallow knowledge in your faith? What is one area where you need to go deeper?
?
You claim to seek truth—but are you actually fleeing from thoughts that might unsettle you? When was the last time you questioned a belief you inherited without examining it yourself?

Lists Imam al-Juwayni's teachers after his father and notes his continuation of teaching at his father's school.

Imam al-Juwayni's education extended beyond his father to other prominent Ash'ari scholars.

This passage introduces two important teachers of Imam al-Juwayni after his father's death. It shows how he deepened his knowledge in kalam, Quranic sciences, and exegesis, and then continued teaching at his father's school, thus preserving his father's legacy.

The text concludes by stating that Imam al-Juwayni continued teaching at his father's school for the duration (the sentence is cut off at the end of the page, likely referring to his tenure).

Juwayni's Early Education and Rejection of Taqlid
Juwayni describes his early education under his father and his critical, anti-taqlid approach.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's early education under his father and his methodological rejection of blind imitation (taqlid) in pursuit of truth.

Core Idea
Truth-seeking requires critical thinking, not inherited allegiance.

Imam al-Juwayni, a towering figure in Islamic theology and jurisprudence, began his scholarly journey under his father's tutelage. But from the very start, his approach was far from passive acceptance. This passage reveals how he combined deep traditional learning with a fearless critical spirit, even towards his own father's writings. It sets the stage for understanding his later contributions to kalam.

Guided Path
1
Read the biographical account
Read the Arabic passage and its translation to get a sense of Juwayni's intellectual character.
Pay attention to the specific phrase he used regarding his father's mistakes.
2
Identify the key themes
Notice how the passage moves from his education to his critical mindset, then to his explicit rejection of taqlid.
The passage includes both general statements and a direct quote from Juwayni.
3
Reflect on the 'fifty thousand' phrase
Consider what this dramatic statement reveals about his commitment to seeking truth, even at the cost of challenging conventional norms.
The phrase is idiomatic and indicates extensive study, not a literal number.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وتعلم أول ما تعلم على يد والده ، فأخذ عنه الفقه واجتهد معه فى المذهب والخلاف والأصول وتعلم العربية واتقن علومها وحفظ القرآن . وكان الرجاحة عقله وغزارة علمه وحرية رأيه يراجع أباه في بعض مسائل من العلم فى حياته وبعد مماته . فقد روى عنه الرواة : أنه يردد عبارة خاصة كلما وقع على بعض أخطاء لوالده في كتاباته وهى : « هذه زلة من الشيخ رحمه الله ) (۱) ا.هـ. وكان يكره التعصب والتقليد . ومن عباراته قوله فى هذا الشأن : « لقد قرأت خمسين ألفا ، في خمسين ألفا ، ثم خليت أهل الاسلام بإسلامهم فيها ، وعلومهم الظاهرة ، وركبت البحر الخضم ، وغصت في الذي نهى أهل الإسلام عنه ، كل ذلك في طلب الحق . وكنت أهرب في سالف الدهر من التقليد (۲) ا.هـ .
He learned first at the hand of his father, taking jurisprudence from him and striving with him in the school of thought, disagreement, and principles, and he learned Arabic and mastered its sciences and memorized the Quran. And his soundness of mind, abundance of knowledge, and freedom of opinion led him to review his father regarding some issues of knowledge during his life and after his death. It is narrated from him that he would repeat a particular phrase whenever he came across some mistakes of his father in his writings: 'This is a slip of the Shaykh, may Allah have mercy on him.' He disliked bigotry and blind imitation (taqlid). Among his statements on this matter is: 'I have read fifty thousand, then fifty thousand, then I left the people of Islam with their Islam and their apparent sciences, and I embarked on the deep sea and plunged into what the people of Islam had forbidden, all of that in search of truth. And I used to flee from blind imitation in times past.'
Source pages p. 5

Commentary
This passage is a biographical account describing Imam al-Haramayn al-Juwayni's early education and methodological approach. It first states that he began his studies under his father, from whom he learned jurisprudence (fiqh) and engaged in scholarly debate regarding the school of thought, points of disagreement, and foundational principles (usul). He also mastered Arabic and the Quran. The text then highlights his intellectual independence: despite his father being his first teacher, he would critically review his father's works and point out errors, even saying 'This is a slip of the Shaykh.' This illustrates that his commitment to truth trumped personal loyalty. The passage further reports that he detested bigotry (ta'assub) and blind imitation (taqlid). It then quotes his own dramatic statement: he read fifty thousand (lines or books) multiple times, then set aside the received opinions of Muslims in favor of directly seeking truth, even venturing into forbidden areas of inquiry—all in pursuit of truth. He concludes by saying he always fled from taqlid. This establishes Juwayni as a rational, critical thinker who valued direct investigation over inherited authority.

Key Terms
تعلم
تقليد
تعصب
الحق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what did Juwayni do whenever he found mistakes in his father's writings?
A) He would correct them openly.
B) He would say 'This is a slip of the Shaykh, may Allah have mercy on him.'
C) He would conceal them out of respect.
D) He would debate his father about them.

Key Takeaway
Juwayni's early life shows that genuine scholarship requires both mastery of tradition and the courage to critically evaluate it.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have inherited beliefs from your family and teachers. When was the last time you critically examined one of those beliefs in light of the truth, even if it meant acknowledging an error in someone you respect? What would it cost you to do so today?
?
Juwayni said he left the apparent sciences and plunged into the deep sea. What is the 'deep sea' in your own pursuit of truth? Are you avoiding any uncomfortable truths about your own faith or practice because it would require you to question what you've been taught?
?
He fled from blind imitation. Are you currently imitating anyone in your religious practice without understanding the evidence? Will you commit to studying one matter you currently take on authority until you can see the proof?

Juwayni's Broad Education and His Teachers
Juwayni's comprehensive education and his two post-father teachers.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the scope of Juwayni's early education, including the meaning of 'philosophy' in his time, and the influence of his two main teachers after his father.

Core Idea
Juwayni mastered all knowledge—philosophy meant 'all sciences'—through two key teachers.

Imam al-Haramayn Juwayni did not limit himself to a single discipline. His education covered everything then called 'philosophy', which included all known sciences. This passage introduces two of his teachers who shaped his mastery of kalam and Quranic studies.

Guided Path
1
Understand the broad meaning of 'philosophy'
Read the passage and note that 'falsafa' at that time included all sciences, not just Greek philosophy. This reflects the comprehensive nature of Juwayni's learning.
This corrects a common misunderstanding.
2
Identify Teacher 1: al-Asfarayini
Find the name of the first teacher and note that Juwayni learned kalam from him. Also note his Ash'ari affiliation, which shows Juwayni's theological school.
3
Identify Teacher 2: al-Khabbazi
Find the second teacher and note that Juwayni learned Quranic sciences (tafsir, qira'at) from him. This shows the breadth of his education.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويبدو أن امام الحرمين قد خاض فى العلوم على اختلافها ، وحصل كل ما كان مندرجا يومئذ تحت لفظ « فلسفة » اذ كان مفهوم فى تلك الأيام يعنى جميع المعارف . وصحب بعد والده فى العلم : أبو القاسم . عبد الجبار بن على ابن محمد بن حسكان الأسفرايبني الاسكافى ٤٥٢ هـ وقد أخذ عنه الكثير فى علم الكلام .. وكان الأسفراييني على مذهب الأشعرى . وصحب أيضا : أبو عبد الله محمد بن على بن محمد النيسابورى الخبازى ٤٤٩ هـ وقد أخذ عنه الكثير من علوم القرآن وقراءاته و تفاسيره
It appears that Imam al-Haramayn delved into the sciences in their various kinds and acquired everything that was included at that time under the word 'philosophy', for the concept in those days meant all branches of knowledge. And after his father, he kept company in knowledge with: Abu al-Qasim 'Abd al-Jabbar ibn 'Ali ibn Muhammad ibn Huskan al-Asfarayini al-Isfakani (d. 452 AH) and he took from him extensively in the science of kalam. Al-Asfarayini followed the Ash'ari school. He also kept company with: Abu 'Abd Allah Muhammad ibn 'Ali ibn Muhammad al-Naysaburi al-Khabbazi (d. 449 AH) and he took from him extensively the sciences of the Quran, its readings, and its exegeses.
Source pages p. 5

Commentary
This passage explains the breadth of Juwayni's learning. It first notes that he studied all the sciences then categorized as 'philosophy' (falsafa), which at that time encompassed all knowledge, not just Greek philosophy. Then it lists two of his teachers after his father. The first is Abu al-Qasim al-Asfarayini (d. 452 AH), from whom he took much in kalam (dialectical theology). Importantly, it notes that al-Asfarayini was an Ash'ari, indicating Juwayni's own theological affiliation. The second teacher is Abu 'Abd Allah al-Khabbazi (d. 449 AH), from whom he learned Quranic sciences, including the various readings (qira'at) and tafsir (exegesis). This shows that Juwayni's education was both broad (covering all sciences) and deep (specializing in kalam and Quranic studies).

Correction: In Juwayni's time, 'philosophy' encompassed all sciences, not just Greek philosophy.

Juwayni's Career and the Nizamiyya Schools
Juwayni's work at his father's school, travel, and leadership at the Nizamiyya of Nishapur.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni's career as a teacher, traveler, and head of the Nizamiyya school illustrates the revival of Ash'ari theology under Nizam al-Mulk's patronage.

Core Idea
Juwayni's life shows how political support revived Ash'ari scholarship.

This passage from al-Subki's Tabaqat outlines Juwayni's professional journey from his father's school to becoming a leading figure in the Nizamiyya schools founded by Vizier Nizam al-Mulk. It highlights the institutional support that helped spread Ash'ari-Shafi'i teachings across the Islamic world. As you read, notice how Juwayni's travels and appointments connect to the broader revival of Sunni orthodoxy.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Note the key events: his work at his father's school, travel to Mecca, return under new rulers, and his leadership roles.
2
Identify the political context
The passage mentions Sultan Alp Arslan and Vizier Nizam al-Mulk. How did their policies affect Ash'ari scholars? Look for the phrase about bringing back scholars who were exiled.
Nizam al-Mulk built many schools to spread Sunni Islam.
3
Analyze Juwayni's roles
List the positions Juwayni held: teacher at his father's school, head of the Nizamiyya of Nishapur, khatib, overseer of endowments. What does this tell you about his status?
He was a leader of the Shafi'i-Ash'ari community.
4
Reflect on the significance
Consider why this biographical information is included in a theology text. How does it establish Juwayni's authority? Connect to the broader theme of institutional support for theology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وظل عبد الملك امام الحرمين يعمل بمدرسة أبيه طوال الفترة التى أقامها بنيسابور يفسر المذهب الشافعى ويدافع عن العقيدة الأشعرية التى كانت تواجه هجمات الخصوم . وقد سافر الى « مكة المكرمة ( ورجع الى ( نيسابور ) بعد سنوات أربع بعد عام ٤٥١ هـ - على رأى (١) - وفى رجوعه وجد الملك ) ألب أرسلان ( قد اعتلى كرسى الحكم ومعه وزيره «نظام الملك » وعمل على ارجاع شيوخ الأشاعرة الذين هاجروا من قبل عن ديارهم . ويقول السبكي في «طبقات الشافعية الكبرى » أن الوزير نظام الملك : « بنى مدرسة ببغداد ومدرسة ببلخ ومدرسة بنيسابور ومدرسة بهراة ومدرسة بأصبهان ومدرسة بالبصرة ومدرسة برو ومدرسة بآمل طبرستان ومدرسة بالموصل ( (۲) لنشير المذهب السنى على أيدى أئمة كبار من أهل المذهب . وكان الجوينى عبد الملك من رؤساء مدرسة نيسابور النظامية وكان أبو اسحاق الشيرازى من رؤساء مدرسة بغداد النظامية . ويذكر المترجمون للجويني عبد الملك : أنه قد آلت اليه زعامة الأصحاب في هذه الفترة كما أسندت اليه رئاسة الطائفة وأمور الأوقاف وصار خطيبا لجامع المنيعي
And 'Abd al-Malik, Imam al-Haramayn, continued to work at his father's school during the entire period he stayed in Nishapur, interpreting the Shafi'i school and defending the Ash'ari creed, which was facing attacks from opponents. He traveled to Mecca and returned to Nishapur after four years after the year 451 AH, according to one view. Upon his return, he found King Alp Arslan had ascended the throne, with his vizier Nizam al-Mulk, and he worked to bring back the Ash'ari scholars who had previously been exiled from their lands. Al-Subki says in 'Tabaqat al-Shafi'iyya al-Kubra' that the vizier Nizam al-Mulk built a school in Baghdad, a school in Balkh, a school in Nishapur, a school in Herat, a school in Isfahan, a school in Basra, a school in Rayy, a school in Amul Tabaristan, and a school in Mosul to spread the Sunni school at the hands of great imams of the school. And al-Juwayni 'Abd al-Malik was one of the heads of the Nizamiyya school of Nishapur, and Abu Ishaq al-Shirazi was one of the heads of the Nizamiyya school of Baghdad. And the biographers of al-Juwayni 'Abd al-Malik mention that the leadership of the companions came to him in this period, and the presidency of the group and the affairs of endowments were assigned to him, and he became the khatib of the Jami' al-Manai'i.
Source pages p. 5

Commentary
This passage describes Juwayni's professional life. It first states that he remained at his father's school in Nishapur, teaching the Shafi'i school and defending the Ash'ari creed against its opponents. Then it narrates his travel to Mecca and return after four years (around 451 AH). He found Alp Arslan as sultan with Nizam al-Mulk as vizier, who restored Ash'ari scholars to their positions. The passage quotes al-Subki listing the many Nizamiyya schools built by Nizam al-Mulk across the Islamic world to propagate Sunni Islam. Juwayni became one of the heads of the Nizamiyya in Nishapur, alongside Abu Ishaq al-Shirazi in Baghdad. Finally, it notes that Juwayni assumed leadership of his peers, managed endowments, and became a khatib (preacher) of a major mosque. This establishes his high status as a scholar and leader in the Ash'ari-Shafi'i tradition.

Key Terms
المذهب الشافعى
العقيدة الأشعرية
المدرسة النظامية
نظام الملك

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Nizamiyya school refers only to the one in Baghdad.
Correction: Nizam al-Mulk built many Nizamiyya schools across the Islamic world, including in Nishapur, where Juwayni was a head.

Checkpoint

B) Head (ra'is)
C) Treasurer
D) Librarian

Key Takeaway
Juwayni's career as a head of the Nizamiyya school and defender of Ash'ari theology was made possible by the political support of Nizam al-Mulk and his network of institutions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

To what extent are your own religious practices and opportunities dependent on the political climate you live in? Are you prepared to uphold your faith even if the government or society turned hostile?
?
The passage lists nine cities where Nizam al-Mulk built schools to spread Sunni Islam. What institutions in your community are actively preserving and teaching authentic Islamic knowledge? Are you personally contributing to their mission, or are you merely a passive consumer?
?
Juwayni returned from pilgrimage to find that the situation had changed dramatically, enabling him to take on a leadership role. If you were to return to your community after a prolonged absence, would you find that you had grown spiritually, or would you find the same weaknesses? What would you need to change now to be ready for such a moment?

Life of Imam al-Juwayni
beginner
4 min
Al-Juwayni's Life and Role in Nishapur
Biographical narrative of al-Juwayni's teaching, travels, and leadership.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the key events in Imam al-Juwayni's life, his role as a teacher and defender of the Ash'ari creed, and the political support he received from Nizam al-Mulk.

Core Idea
Al-Juwayni's leadership was shaped by political shifts and institutional support.

Imam al-Juwayni was not only a great scholar but also a central figure in the Ash'ari revival. This lesson traces his journey from teaching in Nishapur to his travels and eventual leadership under the patronage of Nizam al-Mulk. You will see how political and institutional factors enabled his scholarly rise.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the narrative flow before you interpret the details.
Pay attention to the political context upon al-Juwayni's return.
2
Identify key events and roles
List the main events: establishing the school in Nishapur, travel to Mecca, return after four years, the change in political leadership, Nizam al-Mulk's building program, and al-Juwayni's eventual positions.
The word 'أقامها' means 'ran/established', not 'built physically'.
3
Reflect on the significance
Consider how political support from Nizam al-Mulk and the network of Nizamiyya schools helped al-Juwayni become a leader of the Shafi'i-Ash'ari community.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
أقامها بنيسابور يفسر المذهب الشافعى ويدافع عن العقيدة الأشعرية التى كانت تواجه هجمات الخصوم وقد سافر الى « مكة المكرمة ( ورجع الى ( نيسابور ) بعد سنوات أربع بعد عام ٤٥١ هـ - على رأى (١) - وفى رجوعه وجد الملك ) ألب أرسلان ( قد اعتلى كرسى الحكم ومعه وزيره «نظام الملك » وعمل على ارجاع شيوخ الأشاعرة الذين هاجروا من قبل عن ديارهم ويقول السبكي في «طبقات الشافعية الكبرى » أن الوزير نظام الملك : « بنى مدرسة ببغداد ومدرسة ببلخ ومدرسة بنيسابور ومدرسة بهراة ومدرسة بأصبهان ومدرسة بالبصرة ومدرسة برو ومدرسة بآمل طبرستان ومدرسة بالموصل ( (۲) لنشير المذهب السنى على أيدى أئمة كبار من أهل المذهب أن وكان الجوينى عبد الملك من رؤساء مدرسة نيسابور النظامية وكان أبو اسحاق الشيرازى من رؤساء مدرسة بغداد النظامية . ويذكر المترجمون للجويني عبد الملك : أنه قد آلت اليه زعامة الأصحاب في هذه الفترة كما أسندت اليه رئاسة الطائفة وأمور الأوقاف وصار خطيبا لجامع المنيعي .
He established [the school] in Nishapur, explaining the Shafi'i school and defending the Ash'ari creed which was facing attacks from opponents. He traveled to Mecca and returned to Nishapur after four years, after 451 AH – according to one opinion. Upon his return, he found Sultan Alp Arslan had ascended the throne, along with his vizier Nizam al-Mulk, who worked to bring back the Ash'ari scholars who had previously emigrated from their lands. Al-Subki says in 'Tabaqat al-Shafi'iyya al-Kubra' that the vizier Nizam al-Mulk built a school in Baghdad, a school in Balkh, a school in Nishapur, a school in Herat, a school in Isfahan, a school in Basra, a school in Ray, a school in Amul Tabaristan, and a school in Mosul, to spread the Sunni school through the hands of great imams. And al-Juwayni 'Abd al-Malik was among the heads of the Nizamiyya school in Nishapur, and Abu Ishaq al-Shirazi was among the heads of the Nizamiyya school in Baghdad. The biographers mention of al-Juwayni 'Abd al-Malik that leadership of the companions devolved to him in this period, and the leadership of the sect and the affairs of endowments were entrusted to him, and he became the khatib of al-Manai'iyya mosque.
Source pages p. 6

Commentary
This passage is a biographical narrative about Imam al-Juwayni's life and role. It begins by stating that he established (or was in charge of) a school in Nishapur, where he taught the Shafi'i school and defended the Ash'ari creed against opponents. It then recounts his travel to Mecca and his return to Nishapur after four years, around 451 AH. Upon his return, the political landscape had changed: Sultan Alp Arslan was in power with his vizier Nizam al-Mulk, who actively facilitated the return of Ash'ari scholars who had previously been forced to leave. The passage quotes al-Subki to highlight Nizam al-Mulk's extensive building of schools across the Islamic world to promote Sunni Islam. It then notes that al-Juwayni became one of the heads of the Nizamiyya school in Nishapur, while Abu Ishaq al-Shirazi was head of the Nizamiyya school in Baghdad. Finally, it summarizes his rise to leadership among the Shafi'i-Ash'ari community, as he assumed leadership of the sect and management of endowments, and became the khatib (preacher) of al-Manai'iyya mosque.

Key Terms
نظام الملك
أشعرية
المنيعي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Al-Juwayni personally built the school in Nishapur.
Correction: The Arabic phrase 'أقامها' means he established or ran the school, not that he physically constructed it.

Checkpoint
According to the quote from al-Subki, why did Nizam al-Mulk build schools in many cities?
A) To promote the Ash'ari creed exclusively
B) To spread the Sunni school through the hands of great imams
C) To create jobs for local builders
D) To compete with al-Juwayni's school

Key Takeaway
Al-Juwayni's career illustrates how scholarly authority in the medieval Islamic world often depended on political patronage and institutional support, as seen through Nizam al-Mulk's Nizamiyya schools.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Al-Juwayni's authority came not only from his knowledge but also from the support of a powerful vizier. In your own life, whose support do you rely on to fulfill your responsibilities? Are you grateful for them, or do you take them for granted? Now, commit to thanking one person today who has enabled your growth.
?
He defended the Ash'ari creed when it was under attack. Is there a belief you hold that feels unpopular or criticized in your circles? How can you defend it with patience and wisdom, as al-Juwayni did, without becoming harsh or retreating into silence? Write down one practical step you will take this week.
?
Al-Juwayni managed endowments and led the community. What trust has Allah placed in your hands—whether your time, wealth, or influence—that you are neglecting? What would change if you treated that trust as seriously as he treated his? Make a specific intention to address it today.

Life of Imam al-Juwayni

Al-Juwayni's Promise of a Book on Imamate
Al-Juwayni announces his plan to write a separate book on Imamate.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand al-Juwayni's decision to write a separate book on the Imamate and his critique of prior works.

Core Idea
Al-Juwayni's promised book on Imamate was a necessary corrective to past excesses.

In this passage, al-Juwayni shares his personal resolve to write a dedicated work on the Imamate. Dissatisfied with earlier treatments that either overreached or fell short, he envisions a comprehensive book that would begin with the Imamate and conclude with Sultanic Rulings. This lesson explores his motivation and the intended scope of that work.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic and translation to grasp the author's announcement.
2
Identify the problem
Notice al-Juwayni's critique of prior works: sects have strayed, and no group was free from excess or negligence.
He mentions transgression, excess, exaggeration, and negligence.
3
Understand the solution
He decides to write a separate book called 'The Great Imamate' covering both the Imamate and Sultanic Rulings.
The intended book is al-Imama al-Kabira.
4
Reflect on the metaphor
He poetically describes the book as a virgin bride awaiting a suitor, indicating its untouched subject matter.
This emphasizes the novelty and importance of his planned work.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد كنت وعدت أن أذكر فصولا فى الامامة ، ثم بدالى : أن أفرد للمجلس السامى كتابا فى الامامة . فقد تاهت فيها الفرق ، ولم يخل فريق عن تعدى الحد والسرف والافراط والتفريط والايجاز لا يوصل
الى بداياتها ، فضلا عن مبانيها ومعانيها ، والداعى لأيام مولانا مرتقب سامى أمره فى افتتاح كتاب ، نسميه بالامامة الكبيرة ، وهى مصدرة بالامامة ، مختتمة بالأحكام السلطانية (۱) وقد حوم عليها مصنفون ، ولم يردوها ، وكما تركوها عذراء في خدرها ، وهي لا تخطب
.
فان شرف مولانا وليها بالخطبة بادر الى زفافها ، نافضة مزوديها ، مختالة فى أعطافها ان شاء الله تعالى .
(1) وهي الكتاب المعروف بالغيائى ، نسبة الى غياث الدولة نظام الملك . وأما غياث الأمم له فى الامامة ، فكتاب آخر لابن الجويني يستحق . النشر ، لولا أهمال الأمة بالمرة فى آخر الدهر ، أمر الامامة والخلافة
And I had promised to mention chapters on the Imamate, then it occurred to me to write a book for the noble assembly on the Imamate. For the sects have strayed therein, and no group has been free from transgression, excess, exaggeration, and negligence, and brevity does not lead to its beginnings, let alone its foundations and meanings. And the caller for the days of our master is awaiting his noble command to open a book, which we call 'The Great Imamate', and it is prefaced with the Imamate and concluded with the Sultanic Rulings (1). And authors have hovered around it but not produced it, and as they left it a virgin in its veil, and it is not betrothed. So if our master honors it with the proposal, he hastens to its wedding, shaking its dowry, strutting in its garments, Allah willing. (1) That is the book known as al-Ghiyathi, attributed to Ghiyath al-Dawla Nizam al-Mulk. As for 'Ghiyath al-Umam' on the Imamate, it is another book by Ibn al-Juwayni deserving publication, were it not for the utter neglect of the community at the end of time regarding the matter of the Imamate and the Caliphate.
Source pages p. 95

Commentary
This passage is a personal note from al-Juwayni about his plan to write a book on the Imamate. He had earlier promised to include chapters on Imamate in the present work, but decided instead to write a separate book for the 'noble assembly' (probably the vizier Nizam al-Mulk). He laments that previous writings on the Imamate have been incomplete and that the topic is poorly handled. He names his intended book 'The Great Imamate', which would begin with the Imamate and end with Sultanic Rulings (i.e., the regulatory laws of governance). He then praises the book as if it were a bride awaiting a suitor, and expresses the hope that his patron (the 'master') will allow him to present it. The footnote (which may be an editorial addition) identifies the book as 'al-Ghiyathi' and notes that al-Juwayni also wrote another work on Imamate called 'Ghiyath al-Umam'.

Key Terms
إمامة
الأحكام السلطانية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think that the promised book is the same as 'Ghiyath al-Umam' or that al-Juwayni actually completed the work in this text.
Correction: Al-Juwayni distinguishes between his intended book 'The Great Imamate' and his later work 'Ghiyath al-Umam.' The footnote clarifies that 'The Great Imamate' is likely the book known as al-Ghiyathi, while Ghiyath al-Umam is a separate treatise.

Checkpoint

A) Al-Aqida al-Nizamiyya
B) Al-Imama al-Kabira
C) Ghiyath al-Umam
D) Al-Ghiyathi

Key Takeaway
Al-Juwayni decided to write a separate comprehensive book on the Imamate to correct the excesses and negligence of previous works.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Al-Juwayni saw that previous authors 'hovered around' the topic of Imamate but never fully addressed it, leaving it 'a virgin in its veil.' What important matter in your own life have you only hovered around, never fully confronting or committing to? Will you leave it untouched, or will you 'hasten to its wedding'?
?
Al-Juwayni awaited his patron's command to begin writing. Are you waiting for someone else's permission or encouragement before you act on a good intention? What would it look like for you to take the first step today, without waiting?
?
The sects fell into extremes—either exaggeration or negligence. In your own understanding or practice of faith, are you prone to excess (going beyond what is required) or neglect (falling short)? How can you seek balance as al-Juwayni aimed for in his book?

Sects and Disagreements
intermediate
4 min
Juwayni's Critique of False Sufis and Qushayri's Definition
Juwayni's era saw false Sufis; Qushayri defines true Sufism as adherence to Quran and Sunnah.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Qushayri defined true Sufism as adherence to Quran and Sunnah, and why Juwayni distinguished it from false claims.

Core Idea
True spirituality is grounded in scripture and moral struggle, not in mere claims.

In Juwayni's time, many appeared claiming to be Sufis while lacking genuine devotion. Abu al-Qasim al-Qushayri responded by defining Sufism in terms of adherence to the Quran and Sunnah, coupled with internal struggle against the soul. This lesson explores that definition and its significance.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the literal translation. Notice the flow: context of false claimants, Qushayri's response, his definition, and Juwayni's conclusion.
Pay attention to the phrase 'محواً من كل ذلك' which describes the false claimants as bereft of high states.
2
Identify the three components of Qushayri's definition
In the passage, Qushayri lists three elements that constitute true Sufism. Find each one and note how they interconnect.
The components are: adherence to Quran and Sunnah, struggle against the soul, and avoidance of innovations and lusts.
3
Understand the distinction between true Islam and claims
Notice Juwayni's concluding statement: 'Such we call true Islam, not Sufism.' Consider why he makes this distinction.
This highlights that authentic spirituality is not a separate label but the essence of Islam itself.
4
Reflect on contemporary relevance
Think about how this critique applies to modern spiritual claims that may deviate from scriptural guidance.
The lesson guards against antinomianism and emphasizes balance between inner struggle and outer adherence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد ظهر فى عصر الجويني كثير من أدعياء التصوف - مع الدين عند الله الاسلام وليس التصوف - وقد تعرض لهم أبو القاء القشيري في رسالته [ووصفهم؟] بأنهم كانوا : محواً من كل ذلك » أى من الأحوال العليا التى كانوا يدعون التحقق بها . وكان يرى القشيرى أن التصوف : ( ملازمة للكتاب والسنة مع مجاهدة النفس لأهوائها ومداومة النضال مع نزواتها ، والبعد عن البدع والشهوات والرخيص الأعمال ( (۳) ا.هـ. ومثل هذا نسميه : اسلاما حقيقيا ، ولا نسميه من التصوف
And there appeared in the era of al-Juwayni many claimants to Sufism - though the religion with Allah is Islam, not Sufism - and Abu al-Qasim al-Qushayri addressed them in his epistle, describing that they were 'bereft of all that' - meaning the high states they claimed to have realized. And al-Qushayri held that Sufism is 'adherence to the Book and the Sunnah along with struggling against one's soul for its desires and persistence in combating its whims, and distance from innovations, lusts, and low deeds.' Such [as this] we call true Islam, and we do not consider it part of Sufism.
Source pages p. 6

Commentary
This passage addresses the phenomenon of false Sufi claimants in Juwayni's time. It first states that many appeared claiming to be Sufis, but the author asserts that true religion is Islam, not Sufism as a separate entity. Abu al-Qasim al-Qushayri wrote against them, describing them as being devoid of the high spiritual states they pretended to have. Qushayri then gives his definition of Sufism: it is (1) adhering to the Quran and Sunnah, (2) struggling against the soul's desires and whims, and (3) avoiding innovations (bida'), lusts (shahawat), and base actions. The passage concludes by stating that such a practice is actually true Islam, not something to be called Sufism. This distinguishes authentic spirituality from antinomian or deviant claims, grounding religious practice in scriptural adherence and moral struggle.

Key Terms
تصوف
الكتاب والسنة
مجاهدة النفس
بدع
شهوات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The passage is against all Sufism.
Correction: It targets false claimants, not authentic Sufism which is defined as adherence to Quran and Sunnah.

Checkpoint
According to al-Qushayri, what is required for true Sufism?
A) Only inner purification without external rituals
B) Adherence to the Book and Sunnah, struggle against the soul, and avoidance of innovations
C) Renouncing all worldly possessions
D) Performing miracles

Key Takeaway
Al-Qushayri defines true spirituality as adherence to the Book and Sunnah, combined with inner struggle against the ego and avoidance of innovations.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
How many times today did I claim to be spiritual or religious while ignoring a clear command of the Quran or Sunnah? Can I name one specific verse I disobeyed this week?
?
What is the one desire of my soul that I have been unwilling to struggle against because it feels too difficult? What would it cost me to fight it tomorrow?
?
Am I secretly attached to an innovation—a practice I love but that has no basis in the Book and Sunnah? Will I sincerely investigate and abandon it if found to be a bid‘a?

Sects and Disagreements
beginner
4 min
Al-Qushayri's Critique of False Sufis
Narrative about false Sufis and al-Qushayri's definition of true Sufism.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Qushayri distinguishes true Sufism from the claims of false Sufis, and why the author considers this definition to be true Islam.

Core Idea
True Sufism is not about claims but about strict adherence and inner struggle.

In the time of Imam al-Juwayni, many people pretended to be Sufis while lacking true spiritual states. The great scholar al-Qushayri wrote his famous 'Risala' to expose them and to define what authentic Tasawwuf really is. In this lesson, we will examine his definition and see why the author of our text calls it 'true Islam' rather than Sufism.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic passage once without stopping. Then read the English translation. Let the words of the scholars sink in.
2
Identify the context
Notice that the passage begins by mentioning 'claimants to Sufism' and then quotes al-Qushayri. Understand that this is a critique of false pretenders.
The author is not rejecting Sufism itself, only its false claimants.
3
Examine al-Qushayri's definition
Al-Qushayri lists several key components of true Sufism. List them in your mind: adherence to Quran and Sunna, struggling against the soul, avoiding innovations and base desires.
These are all rooted in Islamic practice, not in esoteric claims.
4
Reflect on the author's conclusion
The author states that this definition is 'true Islam', not Sufism. This underscores that the core of Islam is sufficient without labeling.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد ظهر فى عصر الجويني كثير من أدعياء التصوف - مع الدين عند الله الاسلام وليس التصوف - وقد تعرض لهم أبو القاء القشيرى في رسالته قوصفهم بأنهم كانوا : محواً من كل ذلك » أى من الأحوال العليا التى كانوا يدعون التحقق بها . وكان يرى القشيرى أن التصوف : ( ملازمة للكتاب والسنة مع مجاهدة النفس لأهوائها ومداومة النضال مع نزواتها ، والبعد عن البدع والشهوات والرخيص الأعمال ( (۳) ا.هـ. ومثل هذا نسميه : اسلاما حقيقيا ، ولا نسميه من التصوف
There appeared in the era of al-Juwayni many claimants to Sufism – while religion according to Allah is Islam, not Sufism – and Abu al-Qasim al-Qushayri addressed them in his epistle, describing them as 'effaced from all of that,' i.e., from the high states they claimed to attain. And al-Qushayri held that Sufism is: adherence to the Book and Sunna, along with struggling against the soul's whims, persistently combating its caprices, and avoiding innovations, lusts, and base deeds. [The editor says:] 'And this we call true Islam, and we do not consider it as Sufism.'
Source pages p. 6

Commentary
This passage discusses the phenomenon of false claimants to Sufism during al-Juwayni's time. It states that many pretenders to Sufism appeared, and the author notes that the true religion with Allah is Islam, not Sufism as a separate entity. It then mentions that Abu al-Qasim al-Qushayri addressed these claimants in his famous 'Risala' (Epistle on Sufism). Al-Qushayri described them as being 'effaced' from the high spiritual states they falsely claimed. He then defines true Sufism as a combination of strict adherence to the Qur'an and Sunna, along with inner struggle (mujahada) against the ego, continuous combat against whims, and avoidance of innovations (bida'), lusts, and lowly actions. The passage ends with an editorial comment that such a definition is actually true Islam, not a separate Sufism.

Key Terms
أدعياء التصوف
القشيري
الكتاب والسنة
مجاهدة النفس

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The passage is anti-Sufi and rejects all forms of Sufism.
Correction: The passage actually criticizes false claimants, while al-Qushayri's definition of true Sufism is praised and considered identical to true Islam.

Checkpoint
According to al-Qushayri, what does true Sufism require?
A) Isolation from society
B) Adherence to the Book and Sunna with self-struggle
C) Claiming high spiritual states
D) Abandoning all worldly actions

Key Takeaway
Al-Qushayri defines true Sufism as strict adherence to the Qur'an and Sunna combined with inner struggle against the ego, not by claiming lofty spiritual states.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
This lesson shows that false Sufis make claims they cannot fulfill. Are there areas in your life where you claim spiritual states or piety without the corresponding struggle against your own ego? What would it take to move from claim to reality?
?
Al-Qushayri's definition requires avoidance of innovations, lusts, and base deeds. Which of these three is the most difficult for you to avoid in your current environment, and what practical step can you take starting today?
?
The editor says this is true Islam, not Sufism. If someone asked you what your religion demands of you today, would you describe it as strict adherence to Quran and Sunna plus inner struggle, or something else? What are you actually relying on?

Sects and Disagreements
intermediate
6 min
Juwayni's Refutation of the Mu'tazilite View on Created Speech
Juwayni refutes the Mu'tazilite claim that God's speech is a created act.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's argument that the Mu'tazilite position on divine speech effectively denies that God is a speaker, and how he uses Quranic verses to refute them.

Core Idea
The Mu'tazilites reduce speech to a creation, denying that God truly speaks.

Juwayni begins by correcting a common misunderstanding about the Mu'tazilite (Qadariyya) position on divine speech. He then exposes their actual belief—that speech is merely a created act, not an attribute of God—and shows how this contradicts clear Quranic texts where God explicitly speaks. This lesson unpacks his refutation and the key evidence he presents.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Pay attention to how Juwayni first addresses a misunderstanding, then states the Mu'tazilite position, and finally brings Quranic evidence.
The passage is dense; take your time.
2
Identify the Mu'tazilite belief
Juwayni says the Mu'tazilites believe speech is a created act, like creating substances. Note that they deny God is truly a speaker or commander.
This is the core of their error.
3
Examine the Quranic verses
Juwayni quotes three verses where God speaks. Reflect on how these verses attribute speech (qawl) directly to God, contradicting the Mu'tazilite claim.
The verses are clear proofs.
4
Understand Juwayni's conclusion
Juwayni argues that if someone creates sounds to indicate a purpose, we do not say 'he said'—so the Mu'tazilite view fails to align with how speech is understood.
This is a logical refutation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فظن من لم يحصل علم هذا الباب أن القدرية وصفوا الرب تبارك وتعالى بكونه متكلما، وزعموا أن كلامه مخلوق، وليس هذا مذهب القوم. بل حقيقة معتقدهم: أن الكلام فعل من أفعال الله عز وجل كخلقه الجواهر وأعراضها، ولا يرجع إلى حقيقة وجوده حكم من الكلام. فمحصول أصلهم: أنه ليس الله تعالى عن قولهم كلام، وليس قائلا آمرا ناهيا. وإنما يخلق أصواتا في جسم من الأجسام دالة على إرادته. وليس يخفى على ذي بصيرة أن آيات القرآن نصوص في اتصاف الرب تبارك وتعالى بالقول. فكم في سياق الآي من أخبار الرب عن نفسه بالاتصاف بالقول. كما قال تعالى: (قال الله هذا يوم ينفع الصادقين صدقهم) المائدة 119، وقال تبارك وتعالى: (يا نار كوني بردا وسلاما) الأنبياء 69، وقال جل وعز: (وقال ربكم ادعوني أستجب لكم) غافر 60. ومن لزم الإنصاف، وجانب الاعتساف، تبين أن هذه الآيات مصرحة باتصاف الرب بقوله. ومن أحدث أصواتا في جسم دالة على غرض له، لا يقال قال كذا وكذا.
So he who has not obtained knowledge of this chapter thought that the Qadariyya describe the Lord, blessed and exalted, as being mutakallim, and they claim that His speech is created, but this is not the doctrine of the people. Rather the reality of their belief is that speech is an act of God, like His creation of substances and their accidents, and no judgment of speech reverts to His essential reality. So the conclusion of their principle is that God — exalted far above their saying — is not speech, and is not a speaker, commander, or prohibition-giver. Rather, He creates sounds in a body indicating His will. And it is not hidden from one of insight that the verses of the Qur'an are explicit texts regarding the description of the Lord, blessed and exalted, with speech (al-qawl). How many in the context of verses are reports of the Lord about Himself as being described with speech, as He said: 'Allah will say, this is a day when the truthful shall benefit from their truthfulness' (al-Ma'idah 119), and He said: 'O fire, be cool and safe' (al-Anbiya' 69), and He said: 'And your Lord said: Call upon Me, I will answer you' (Ghafir 60). And whoever adheres to fairness and avoids bias, it becomes clear that these verses declare explicitly that the Lord is described with His speech. And whoever creates sounds in a body indicating some purpose of his, it is not said that 'he said such and such'.
Source pages p. 26

Commentary
This passage refutes the Mu'tazilite (Qadariyya) position on divine speech. Juwayni begins by noting a common misunderstanding: that the Qadariyya affirm God is mutakallim but claim His speech is created. He clarifies that their belief actually reduces speech to a created act, not an attribute of God. They deny that God is a speaker in the true sense; rather, God creates sounds in a body to convey His will. Juwayni then adduces Quranic verses that explicitly attribute speech (qawl) to God. He argues that if someone creates sounds in a body to indicate a purpose, we do not say that person 'said' something; thus, the Mu'tazilite view contradicts the clear Quranic texts.

Key Terms
القدرية (al-Qadariyya)
الكلام مخلوق (created speech)
القول (speech/utterance)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the Mu'tazilites affirm God as a speaker and only say His speech is created.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies that the Mu'tazilites actually deny that God is a speaker in reality; they reduce speech to a created act, not an attribute.

Checkpoint
Why does Juwayni say the Mu'tazilite view contradicts the Quran?
A) Because the Quran says God's speech is uncreated
B) Because the Quran explicitly attributes speech to God, but the Mu'tazilites deny that God is a speaker
C) Because the Quran says God creates sounds
D) Because the Quran says speech is an attribute of bodies

Key Takeaway
Juwayni refutes the Mu'tazilites by showing that the Quran explicitly attributes speech to God, contradicting their claim that speech is merely a created act.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite the Quran, do you truly believe you are hearing God's own personal address to you, or do you sometimes treat it as ancient text? How does Juwayni's emphasis on God being a speaker challenge you to listen more attentively today?
?
Juwayni warns that reducing God's speech to a created act strips Him of being a true speaker. In your own life, have you ever reduced any of God's attributes—like His mercy, justice, or power—to mere concepts without letting them transform how you live? What would change if you took His attributes as real and active?
?
The Mu'tazilites argued that God only creates sounds to indicate His will, not that He truly speaks. This makes God distant. Do you ever feel distant from God, as if He doesn't speak directly to you? What would it mean for you to believe—truly—that God is a speaker who addresses you through the Quran right now?
