The Nature of Divine Speech: Refutation of Transfer
Juwayni refutes the Hashwiyya's transfer theory and states the correct doctrine of divine speech.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Juwayni argues that divine speech cannot be transferred to books or become sounds, and that it is an eternal attribute subsisting in God's essence.

Core Idea
God's speech is an eternal attribute, not a transferable physical entity.

In this passage, Juwayni confronts the Hashwiyya's belief that God's speech consists of letters and sounds that can be transferred. He asserts that even basic reason shows the impossibility of such transfer. He then affirms the orthodox doctrine that kalam Allah is written, recited, and memorized but is actually an eternal attribute in God's essence.

Guided Path
1
Read Juwayni's diagnosis
Read the opening lines where Juwayni notes how the erroneous view spread among the Hashwiyya. Understand the context of the refutation.
2
Analyze the rational argument
Identify Juwayni's core rational point: speech does not transfer from a speaker to a book, nor does inner meaning become sounds or shapes. This is a self-evident truth.
Notice the phrase 'لا يخفى على من معه مسكة من عقل' — it underscores the obviousness of the argument.
3
Grasp the positive doctrine
Read the concluding sentence where Juwayni states that God's speech is written in mushafs, recited, and memorized, but it subsists in God's essence. Understand that the attribute is eternal, not the created letters.
The phrase 'قائم بذات البارى وجودا' is key: the speech attribute exists in God's essence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وتمادى العصر ، فرسخ هذا الكلام فى قلوب الحشوية ، ولولا ذلك لما خفى على من معه مسكة من عقل : أن الكلام لا ينتقل من متكلم الى دفتر . ولا ينقلب معنى النفس الى الأصوات سطورا ورسوما وأشكالا ورقوما ، فاذن تقول - بعد الاحاطة بحقيقة هذه الفصول - كلام الله وقد نقل المؤلف في الشامل عن النقض الكبير للباقلاني : « من زعم أن السين من بسم بعد الباء . والمميم بعد السين الواقعة بعد الباء ، لا أول له : فقد خرج عن المعقول وجحد الضرورة وانكر البديهة ، فان اعترف بأوليته و أدعى أنه لا أول له ، فقد سقطت محاجنه ، وتعين لحوقه بالسفسطة وكيف يرجى أن يرشد بالدليل من يتواقح فى جحد الضرورى ؟ « اهـ فيكون موقفهم أخطر مما يتصور . والله سبحانه الهادي كلمه الله تبارك وتعالى فى المصاحف مكتوب ، وعلى ألسنة القراء مقروء : والصدور محفوظ ، وهو قائم بذات البارى وجودا
And the age continued, so this speech spread in the hearts of the Hashwiyya. Were it not for that, it would not be hidden from one who has a trace of intellect that speech does not transfer from a speaker to a book, nor does the meaning of the soul turn into sounds, lines, drawings, shapes, and marks. Then we say—after encompassing the reality of these sections—the speech of God. And the author has narrated in al-Shamil from al-Naqd al-Kabir of al-Baqillani: 'Whoever claims that the sin of bism after the ba, and the mim after the sin occurring after the ba, has no beginning has departed from reason, denied necessity, and rejected the self-evident. If he admits its beginning but claims it has no beginning, then his arguments have fallen, and he is definitely attached to sophistry. How can one who is shameless in denying the necessary be guided by proof?' So their position is more dangerous than imagined. And God, glorified, is the Guide. The speech of God, blessed and exalted, is written in the mushafs, recited on the tongues of the reciters, preserved in the hearts, and it is subsisting in the essence of the Creator as an existence.
Source pages p. 30

Commentary
This passage is a refutation and definition of the nature of divine speech (kalam Allah). Juwayni begins by lamenting how the erroneous anthropomorphist view (that God's speech is composed of letters and sounds) has become entrenched among the Hashwiyya. He asserts that even a person with minimal intellect would realize that speech cannot be physically transferred from a speaker to a book, nor can an inner meaning be transformed into audible sounds or written shapes. After clarifying this, he affirms the correct doctrine: God's speech (kalam Allah) is written in the mushafs (texts), recited on tongues, and memorized in hearts, but it is an eternal attribute subsisting in God's essence—not identical to the created script or sounds. He then cites al-Baqillani's argument against those who claim that the letters of the Basmala have no beginning. Al-Baqillani argues that such a claim is contrary to reason, self-evident truth, and sensory necessity. If someone admits that the letters have a beginning (i.e., they are created) but then claims they have no beginning, they fall into sophistry. Juwayni concludes that the position of these anthropomorphists is extremely dangerous.

Key Terms
الحشوية
الكلام
المصاحف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The belief that the letters and sounds of the Quran are eternal and uncreated.
Correction: Only the attribute of speech in God's essence is eternal; the recited words and written script are created.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the relationship between God's speech and the written mushaf?
A) God's speech is identical to the letters and sounds of the mushaf.
B) God's speech is an eternal attribute subsisting in His essence, while the mushaf is created.
C) God's speech is transferred from God to the mushaf as sounds.
D) God's speech is a physical attribute that changes when recited.

Key Takeaway
God's speech is an eternal attribute, not a transferable physical entity.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite the Quran, do you sense that you are engaging with an eternal attribute of God, or do you treat it as a mere historical text? What would change if you truly internalized that the speech you recite subsists in God's essence?
?
The Hashwiyya believed that God’s speech could be moved from place to place. Reflect: Have you ever reduced the Quran to a physical object—a book on a shelf, a screen—such that its living, eternal reality faded from your heart? How can you restore the reverence due to it?
?
If God’s speech is an eternal attribute, not a created thing, then every time you hear a verse, you are encountering something that has always been with God. Does that awe you? Or has familiarity dulled your heart? What will you do today to hear the Quran as if it is being revealed to you for the first time, directly from the One whose attribute it is?

Al-Baqillani's Refutation of the Beginninglessness of Letters
Al-Baqillani refutes the claim that the letters have no beginning.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand al-Baqillani's argument that claiming the letters of the Basmala have no beginning is a denial of reason and necessity.

Core Idea
Claiming created letters are eternal is a contradiction that leads to sophistry.

Juwayni cites al-Baqillani's powerful refutation of the Hashwiyya's claim that the letters of the Basmala have no beginning. Al-Baqillani argues that such a claim either denies the self-evident truth that letters have a beginning, or if admitted but then contradictoryly claimed to have no beginning, leads to sophistry. This highlights the danger of their position.

Guided Path
1
Read al-Baqillani's hypothetical claim
Read the claim that the letters of the Basmala (e.g., sin after ba, mim after sin) have no beginning. Understand what is being asserted.
Al-Baqillani is targeting the anthropomorphist view that the letters are eternal.
2
Understand the first consequence
If someone claims the letters have no beginning, they are rejecting reason, necessity, and self-evident truth. Identify why this is irrational.
The letters are clearly originated in time; denying that is absurd.
3
Analyze the second consequence
If someone admits the letters have a beginning but then claims they have no beginning, they fall into contradiction and sophistry. Note the phrase 'سقطت محاجنه'.
This is a classic reductio ad absurdum.
4
Grasp the conclusion
Al-Baqillani concludes that such a person cannot be guided by proof. Juwayni adds that their position is extremely dangerous.
The danger is that they reject obvious truths, making intellectual discourse impossible.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وتمادى العصر ، فرسخ هذا الكلام فى قلوب الحشوية ، ولولا ذلك لما خفى على من معه مسكة من عقل : أن الكلام لا ينتقل من متكلم الى دفتر . ولا ينقلب معنى النفس الى الأصوات سطورا ورسوما وأشكالا ورقوما ، فاذن تقول - بعد الاحاطة بحقيقة هذه الفصول - كلام الله وقد نقل المؤلف في الشامل عن النقض الكبير للباقلاني : « من زعم أن السين من بسم بعد الباء . والمميم بعد السين الواقعة بعد الباء ، لا أول له : فقد خرج عن المعقول وجحد الضرورة وانكر البديهة ، فان اعترف بأوليته و أدعى أنه لا أول له ، فقد سقطت محاجنه ، وتعين لحوقه بالسفسطة وكيف يرجى أن يرشد بالدليل من يتواقح فى جحد الضرورى ؟ « اهـ فيكون موقفهم أخطر مما يتصور . والله سبحانه الهادي كلمه الله تبارك وتعالى فى المصاحف مكتوب ، وعلى ألسنة القراء مقروء : والصدور محفوظ ، وهو قائم بذات البارى وجودا
And the age continued, so this speech spread in the hearts of the Hashwiyya. Were it not for that, it would not be hidden from one who has a trace of intellect that speech does not transfer from a speaker to a book, nor does the meaning of the soul turn into sounds, lines, drawings, shapes, and marks. Then we say—after encompassing the reality of these sections—the speech of God. And the author has narrated in al-Shamil from al-Naqd al-Kabir of al-Baqillani: 'Whoever claims that the sin of bism after the ba, and the mim after the sin occurring after the ba, has no beginning has departed from reason, denied necessity, and rejected the self-evident. If he admits its beginning but claims it has no beginning, then his arguments have fallen, and he is definitely attached to sophistry. How can one who is shameless in denying the necessary be guided by proof?' So their position is more dangerous than imagined. And God, glorified, is the Guide. The speech of God, blessed and exalted, is written in the mushafs, recited on the tongues of the reciters, preserved in the hearts, and it is subsisting in the essence of the Creator as an existence.
Source pages p. 30

Commentary
This passage is a refutation and definition of the nature of divine speech (kalam Allah). Juwayni begins by lamenting how the erroneous anthropomorphist view (that God's speech is composed of letters and sounds) has become entrenched among the Hashwiyya. He asserts that even a person with minimal intellect would realize that speech cannot be physically transferred from a speaker to a book, nor can an inner meaning be transformed into audible sounds or written shapes. After clarifying this, he affirms the correct doctrine: God's speech (kalam Allah) is written in the mushafs (texts), recited on tongues, and memorized in hearts, but it is an eternal attribute subsisting in God's essence—not identical to the created script or sounds. الضرورة

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The belief that the letters of the Quran are eternal and uncreated.

Checkpoint
According to al-Baqillani, what happens if someone admits the letters have a beginning but then claims they have no beginning?
A) They become a true believer.
B) Their arguments fall and they are attached to sophistry.
C) They are praised for their consistency.
D) They are considered a rational thinker.

Key Takeaway
Denying the created nature of the letters leads to an insoluble contradiction.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Are there truths about your faith that you find uncomfortable because they challenge your intellectual pride? What would it mean for you to submit to them fully?
?

?
If you deny a self-evident truth about Allah’s attributes (like the created nature of these letters), what does that reveal about the state of your heart? Will you let this lesson shake you into sincere investigation?

The Wa'idiyya View on Major Sinners and Its Refutation
Juwayni states and refutes the Wa'idiyya claim that major sinners are eternally in Hell.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Wa'idiyya position that major sinners are eternally in Hell and Juwayni's rational and scriptural refutation of that view.

Core Idea
One major sin does not nullify a lifetime of obedience—reason and Quran refute the Wa'idiyya claim.

Juwayni addresses a controversial stance held by some early Muslim sects: that a believer who commits one major sin without repentance will be in Hell forever, even if they spent their lives in obedience. This lesson examines that claim and the logical and scriptural arguments Juwayni uses to refute it.

Guided Path
1
Understand the Wa'idiyya position
Read the passage describing their view: a lifetime of obedience is nullified by one major sin without repentance.
Note the sects mentioned: Khawarij, Zaydiyya, Qadariyya.
2
Identify Juwayni's rational critique
Juwayni finds it absurd that a lifetime of obedience is outweighed by one sin. He also points out a logical inconsistency in their reasoning.
They claim good deeds are surrounded by sin, but they don't apply the same logic in reverse.
3
Connect to the Quranic verse
Juwayni cites Quran 11:114 to show that good deeds can remove evil deeds, which contradicts the Wa'idiyya claim.
This verse supports the opposite of their conclusion.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الركن الثاني من الفصل في ذكر العصاة من أهل الايمان : ذهبت الوعيدية من الخوارج والزيدية والقدرية : الى أن من يستوعب عمره في طاعة الله تبارك وتعالى ، ثم قارف كبيرة واحدة ، ولم يوفق للتوبة عنها ومات عارفا بالله تبارك وتعالى ، فهو خالد في النار مع المشركين ، الذين ما أتوا حسنة قط . والعجب : أنهم يثبتون أحكام الله تبارك وتعالى على ما تجرى به عوائد العقلاء والذى ذكروه من أقبح القبائح فى مقتضى العقول شاهدا . وان زعموا : أن الحسنات تحيط بسيئة واحدة لتناقضهما . فهلا أحبطوا السيئة بالحسنات ؟ ولو فعلوا ذلك لشهدت لهم آية من كتاب الله تبارك وتعالى وهى قوله : « ان الحسنات يذهبن السيآت » ) هود : ١١٤ ) ..
The second pillar of the chapter on mentioning the disobedient from the people of faith: The Wa'idiyya from among the Khawarij, Zaydiyya, and Qadariyya held that whoever spends his entire life in obedience to Allah the Blessed and Exalted, then commits one major sin, is not granted repentance for it, and dies knowing Allah the Blessed and Exalted, he will be eternally in Hellfire along with the polytheists who never performed a single good deed. And the wonder is that they affirm the rulings of Allah the Blessed and Exalted according to the customs of rational people, yet what they mention is among the ugliest things in light of reason. And if they claim that good deeds surround a single sin because they contradict it, then why do they not annul the sin with good deeds? If they did that, a verse from the Book of Allah the Blessed and Exalted would testify for them, namely His saying: 'Indeed, good deeds remove evil deeds' (11:114).
Source pages p. 87

Commentary
This passage is a refutation of the Wa'idiyya (those who insist on threat), including the Khawarij, Zaydiyya, and Qadariyya. They claim that a believer who commits a major sin without repentance will be in Hell forever, even if he was obedient his whole life. Juwayni finds this absurd because it contradicts rational custom: it is unreasonable that a lifetime of obedience is nullified by one sin. He then points out an inconsistency: they claim good deeds and sins cancel each other, but they only cancel good deeds with sin, not the reverse. He argues that if they applied the same logic in the opposite direction—good deeds annulling sin—they would have Quranic support from 11:114. So their position is both rationally and textually flawed.

Key Terms
الوعيدية
الخوارج
الزيدية
القدرية
الكبيرة
الحسنات
السيئات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One major sin nullifies all good deeds and leads to eternal Hell without repentance.
Correction: Juwayni argues this is rationally absurd and contradicted by Quran 11:114 which states good deeds remove evil deeds.

Checkpoint
What is Juwayni's main rational objection to the Wa'idiyya position?
A) Good deeds cannot outweigh sins.
B) If good deeds and sins cancel, then sins should also be cancelled by good deeds.
C) The Quran explicitly says major sinners go to Hell forever.
D) Their view is based on weak hadith.

Key Takeaway
Juwayni refutes the Wa'idiyya view by showing that it contradicts reason and that the Quran supports the annulment of sins by good deeds.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever feel that one sin you committed has erased all your good deeds in your own eyes? How does Juwayni's refutation of the Wa'idiyya challenge you to see Allah's mercy as greater than your failures? What will you do today to hold onto hope in His forgiveness?
?
The Wa'idiyya lost hope in a believer who sinned once. Are there relationships—with Allah or with others—where you have given up because of a single mistake? How does this lesson invite you to rebuild trust and seek reconciliation?
?
If you truly believed that every sin could be annulled by good deeds, how would your daily actions change? What one good deed can you commit to regularly, trusting in Allah's promise that 'good deeds remove evil deeds'?

Intercession for Sinners: Juwayni vs. Mu'tazila
Juwayni affirms intercession for sinners and presents the Mu'tazilite objection with scriptural proofs.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the disagreement between Juwayni and the Mu'tazila regarding intercession (shafa'ah) and the scriptural evidence each side uses.

Core Idea
Intercession for sinners is a point of contention between Sunni and Mu'tazilite theology.

The doctrine of intercession (shafa'ah) is a central feature of Sunni eschatology, but its scope is debated. Imam Juwayni affirmed intercession for sinners using rational and hadith arguments, while the Mu'tazila limited it to increase of reward for the virtuous. In this lesson, we explore the Mu'tazilite scriptural case against intercession for wrongdoers and the broader theological stakes.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the complete Arabic text once. Notice how Juwayni's affirmation is stated briefly, and then the Mu'tazilite argument unfolds with Quranic citations.
The phrase 'يرحمهم الله تعالى' is likely an editor's addition; don't let it distract.
2
Identify the two positions
Find where the text describes Juwayni's view (affirming intercession for sinners) and where it presents the Mu'tazilite view (limiting intercession to increase of reward).
The Mu'tazila are introduced with 'قال المعتزلة'.
3
Analyze the Mu'tazilite evidence
Look at the Quranic verses cited (Al-Anbiya 26-29, Ghafir 18, An-Naba' 38) and understand how the Mu'tazila interpret them to exclude intercession for sinners.
These verses emphasize that angels only intercede for those God approves, and that wrongdoers have no intercessor.
4
Reflect on the significance
Consider why this disagreement matters: it relates to divine mercy, justice, and the efficacy of prophetic intercession for sinful believers.
This connects to broader discussions about faith and sin in Islamic theology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأثبت الجويني «الشفاعة» للعصاة بأدلة عقلية، وبأحاديث نبوية، ولم يذكر نصوصا من القرآن.. ولم يفند رأي القائلين بتقسيمها إلى: شفاعة في زيادة الثواب، أو زيادة الدرجات، وإلى شفاعة عامة لكل المذنبين. قال المعتزلة - يرحمهم الله تعالى - إن الشفاعة في زيادة الثواب أو زيادة الدرجات جائزة للنبي صلى الله عليه وسلم أو غير النبي بدليل وقالوا: اتخذ الرحمن ولدا. سبحانه، بل عباد مكرمون. لا يسبقونه القول، وهم بأمره يعملون. يعلم ما بين أيديهم وما خلفهم ولا يشفعون إلا لمن ارتضى، وهم من خشيته مشفقون. ومن يقل منهم: إني إله من دونه فذلك تجزيه جهنم كذلك نجزى الظالمين) (الأنبياء: ٢٦-٢٩) قال صاحب الكشاف: (ومن تحفظهم أنهم لا يجبرون أن يشفعوا إلا لمن ارتضاه الله وأهله للشفاعة في ازدياد الثواب والتعظيم، وأما الشفاعة للعصاة الذين لم تؤهلهم أعمالهم لدخول الجنة فلا شفاعة لهم، ويدخلون النار كل على عمله. بدليل قوله تعالى: «ما للظالمين من حميم ولا شفيع يطاع» (غافر: ١٨) وقالوا: إن الله سيأذن للشفعاء في زيادة الثواب أو زيادة الدرجات بدليل «لا يتكلمون إلا من أذن له الرحمن وقال صوابا» (النبأ: ٣٨) فإن الصواب محدد بما نص الله عليه في القرآن من آيات الوعد للطائع والوعيد للعاصي.
And Juwayni affirmed intercession for sinners with rational proofs and prophetic hadiths, and he did not mention texts from the Quran... and he did not refute the opinion of those who divided it into: intercession for increase of reward, or increase of degrees, and general intercession for all sinners. The Mu'tazila - may God have mercy on them - said: Intercession for increase of reward or increase of degrees is permissible for the Prophet or other than the Prophet, as evidence. And they said: 'They have taken the Most Merciful a son. Exalted is He! Rather, they are honored servants. They do not precede Him in speech, and they act by His command. He knows what is before them and what is behind them, and they do not intercede except for whom He approves, and they are apprehensive from fear of Him. And whoever among them says, "I am a god besides Him," that one We will recompense with Hell. Thus do We recompense the wrongdoers.' (Al-Anbiya: 26-29) The author of al-Kashshaf said: 'Among their traits is that they do not force themselves to intercede except for whom God has approved and made worthy of intercession for increase of reward and magnification. As for intercession for sinners whose deeds have not qualified them to enter Paradise, they have no intercession, and they enter the Fire, each according to his deeds, based on His statement: "For the wrongdoers there is no friend nor intercessor to be obeyed." (Ghafir: 18) And they said: God will permit the intercessors for increase of reward or increase of degrees, based on "They do not speak except whom the Most Merciful gives permission and who speaks correctly." (An-Naba': 38) For correctness is determined by what God has prescribed in the Quran of verses of promise for the obedient and threat for the disobedient.'
Source pages p. 118

Commentary
This passage presents the issue of intercession (shafa'ah). First, it states that Juwayni affirmed intercession for sinners using rational and hadith proofs, but he did not cite Quranic verses nor did he refute those who divide intercession into types. Then it presents the Mu'tazilite position: they allow intercession only for increase of reward or degrees, not for forgiveness of sins. They support this with Quranic verses that describe angels as only interceding for those with whom God is pleased, and that there is no intercessor for wrongdoers. The Mu'tazila conclude that intercession is limited to the virtuous, not for sinners who deserve punishment. The passage then cites Zamakhshari's tafsir confirming that intercession is only for those whom God approves for increase of reward.

Key Terms
الشفاعة
العصاة
زيادة الثواب
زيادة الدرجات
المعتزلة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Mu'tazila completely deny intercession.
Correction: The passage shows they affirm intercession for increase of reward, only denying it for forgiveness of sins.

Checkpoint
What is the Mu'tazilite position on intercession for sinners?
A) They affirm it fully.
B) They deny it completely.
C) They allow it only for increase of reward, not for forgiveness.
D) They say it is only for prophets.

Key Takeaway
The debate over intercession highlights the tension between divine mercy and justice, with the Mu'tazila limiting intercession to reward increase based on their reading of Quranic restrictions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever believed that your sins are too great for God's mercy, limiting His forgiveness in your heart? How does the Sunni affirmation of intercession for sinners challenge you to trust in God's infinite mercy rather than your own deeds?
?
The Mu'tazila used Quranic verses to justify a restrictive view of God's mercy. In your life, do you sometimes use religious knowledge to judge others harshly rather than to hope for mercy? Reflect on a specific instance and commit to a change.
?
Juwayni affirmed intercession for sinners without fully refuting the Mu'tazilite division. Are you willing to accept that God's mercy can override strict justice even when you don't understand it? What does that mean for how you view your own shortcomings and those of others?

Juwayni's View on Grave Sinners and Opposing Sects
Juwayni states his view on grave sinners and summarizes opposing positions.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's position on the fate of grave sinners who die without repentance, and how it differs from the views of the Khawarij, Hasan al-Basri, and the Mu'tazila.

Core Idea
A grave sinner's fate is hidden in God's will, not fixed by any sect.

This passage from Imam al-Juwayni presents his own Ash'ari position on a major theological dispute: the status of a Muslim who commits a major sin and dies without repentance. Juwayni affirms that such a person's final outcome is known only to God—He may forgive, accept intercession, or punish temporarily, but ultimate salvation is certain. He then contrasts his view with three other theological schools, setting the stage for the detailed arguments that follow.

Guided Path
1
Read Juwayni's statement carefully
Start by reading the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the phrase 'amruhu mugheeb' (his affair is hidden). This is the core of Juwayni's position—he refuses to make a definitive judgment on the grave sinner's fate in this world.
The word 'mugheeb' comes from ghayb (unseen), indicating the matter is beyond human knowledge.
2
Identify the three options in Juwayni's view
Juwayni presents three possible outcomes under God's will: direct forgiveness, intercession, or temporary punishment followed by salvation. Each rests on divine mercy and justice.
3
Compare the other sects' positions
The passage lists the views of the Khawarij (kufr), Hasan al-Basri (hypocrisy), and Mu'tazila (intermediate status). Notice how each assigns a definitive worldly label, whereas Juwayni leaves the matter to God.
The Mu'tazila introduced a 'middle position' (al-manzila bayna al-manzilatayn).
4
Reflect on the significance of Juwayni's view
Ask yourself why Juwayni avoids labeling the grave sinner as kafir, munafiq, or fasiq in a definitive sense. His position upholds God's absolute sovereignty and mercy while affirming that grave sin is not negligible.
This is the Ash'ari position, balancing hope and fear.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقال الجوينى: من مات من عصاة أهل الايمان من غير توبة ، فأمره مغيب ان شاء الله غفر له ، أو شفع فيه شفيع .. وان شاء عرضه على النار بقدر ذنبه ثم عاقبته الفوز الأكبر والنجاة .. وقال الخوارج : ان مرتكب الكبيرة اذا لم يتب فهو كافر . و قال الحسن البصري رحمه الله : انه منافق وقال المعتزلة : انه لا مؤمن ولا كافر بل هو فاسق
Juwayni said: Whoever dies from the disobedient people of faith without repentance, his affair is hidden: if God wills, He forgives him, or an intercessor intercedes for him, and if He wills, He exposes him to the Fire according to his sin, then his final end is the great victory and salvation. And the Khawarij said: The committer of a major sin, if he does not repent, he is a disbeliever. And al-Hasan al-Basri, may God have mercy on him, said: He is a hypocrite. And the Mu'tazila said: He is neither a believer nor a disbeliever but rather a sinner.
Source pages p. 122

Commentary
This passage presents four positions on the status of a Muslim who commits a major sin and dies without repentance. Imam Juwayni (the author) articulates the Ash'ari position: such a person's final state is uncertain in this world; it is subject to God's will. God may forgive directly, or accept intercession, or punish temporarily in Hell, but ultimately they attain salvation (al-fawz al-akbar wa al-najat). This contrasts with the Khawarij who deem him a kafir (disbeliever), with Hasan al-Basri who calls him a munafiq (hypocrite), and with the Mu'tazila who assign him an intermediate status as a fasiq (sinner) – neither believer nor disbeliever. The passage sets up the doctrinal disagreement before presenting the proofs of the Khawarij in subsequent sections.

Key Terms
عصاة أهل الإيمان
مغيب
الخوارج
الحسن البصري
المعتزلة
فاسق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Juwayni is uncertain about the fate of grave sinners.
Correction: Juwayni affirms that God's will is decisive; he does not express uncertainty but rather theological humility, acknowledging that God's mercy and justice are beyond human determination. The final salvation is guaranteed, but the mode is hidden.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the fate of a grave sinner who dies without repentance?
A) He is definitely in Hell forever
B) His affair is hidden in God's will, possibly forgiven or punished temporarily
C) He is a disbeliever
D) He is a hypocrite

Key Takeaway
Juwayni's position on grave sinners is that their ultimate fate rests entirely with God's will, not with any fixed worldly label, balancing divine mercy and justice.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you encounter a fellow Muslim who openly commits a major sin, do you find yourself mentally categorizing them as a 'bad Muslim'? How does Juwayni's teaching that their affair is 'hidden' challenge your instinct to judge their ultimate fate? What concrete step can you take this week to leave that judgment to God?
?
If you were to die tonight with an unrepented sin, where would your hope lie? Would you rely on your own good deeds or on God's mercy as described in this lesson? How does this passage force you to examine the foundation of your own salvation?
?
Have you ever, even silently, considered someone less of a believer because of a sin? The Khawarij openly declared such people disbelievers. What would it look like for you to truly practice the Ash'ari position in your heart, treating every sinner as a potential recipient of God's mercy?

First Four Proofs of the Khawarij
Khawarij enumerate four Quranic verses as evidence for their doctrine.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the first four scriptural proofs used by the Khawarij to argue that a grave sinner is a disbeliever.

Core Idea
The Khawarij weaponize verses about divine judgment and punishment to label sinners as disbelievers.

The Khawarij, an early sect, sought to prove that anyone who commits a major sin becomes a kafir. They cited specific Quranic verses as evidence. In this lesson, we examine the first four of their eight proofs, each appealing to a different aspect of divine justice.

Guided Path
1
Read the first four proofs
Read the Arabic passage containing proofs 1–4. Note how each proof begins with 'الأول', 'الثاني', etc. and cites a verse with a short comment.
The Khawarij's method is to take verses that mention disbelief and apply them to the fasiq.
2
Identify the common theme
Observe that each verse threatens disbelief, ingratitude, denial, or turning away. The Khawarij argue that a fasiq shares these attributes.
3
Consider the literalist method
Reflect on how the Khawarij interpret these verses literally without considering context or other Qur'anic principles like repentance.
This literal approach is central to their deviation from mainstream Islam.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وحجة الخوارج وجوه : الأول : تقوله تعالى : ( ومن لم يحكم بما أنزل الله فأولئك هم الكافرون ) ( المائدة : ٤٤) والثاني : قوله تعالى : ( وهل نجازى إلا الكفور ) ( سبأ : ١٧ ) والثالث : قوله تعالى بعد ايجاب الحج : ( ومن كفر فإن الله غني عن العالمين ) ( آل عمران : ٩٧ ) والرابع : قوله تعالى : ( إن العذاب على من كذب وتولى ) ( طه : ٤٨ ) والخامس : قوله تعالى : ( فأنذرتكم نارا تلظى ، لا يصلاها إلا الأشقى ، الذي كذب وتولى ) ( الليل : ١٤ - ١٦ ) والفاسق يصلاها . والسادس : قوله تعالى في حق من خفت موازينه : ( ألم تكن آياتي تتلى عليكم فكنتم بها تكذبون ) ( المؤمنون : ١٠٥) والفاسق ممن خفت موازينه . والسابع : قوله تعالى : ( يوم تبيض وجوه وتسود وجوه ) ( آل عمران : ١٠٦) والفاسق ممن وجهه مسود . والثامن : أنه من أصحاب المشأمة قال تعالى : ( والذين كفروا بآياتنا هم أصحاب المشأمة ) ( البلد : ١٩ )
And the proof of the Khawarij are aspects: First: His statement, the Exalted: (And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed – then it is those who are the disbelievers) (al-Ma'idah 5:44). Second: His statement, the Exalted: (And do we recompense except the ungrateful?) (Saba' 34:17). Third: His statement, the Exalted, after making Hajj obligatory: (And whoever disbelieves – then indeed, Allah is free from need of the worlds) (Aal 'Imran 3:97). Fourth: His statement, the Exalted: (Indeed, the punishment is upon whoever denies and turns away) (Taha 20:48). Fifth: His statement, the Exalted: (So I have warned you of a blazing Fire. None will [enter to] burn therein except the most wretched one, who had denied and turned away) (al-Layl 92:14-16), and the fasiq will enter it. Sixth: His statement, the Exalted, regarding those whose scales are light: (Were not My verses recited to you, but you used to deny them?) (al-Mu'minun 23:105), and the fasiq is among those whose scales are light. Seventh: His statement, the Exalted: (On the Day [some] faces will turn white and [some] faces will turn black) (Aal 'Imran 3:106), and the fasiq is among those whose face is blackened. Eighth: That he is among the companions of the left hand; the Exalted said: (And those who disbelieve in Our signs – they are the companions of the left hand) (al-Balad 90:19).
Source pages p. 122

Commentary
This passage presents the first eight scriptural proofs used by the Khawarij to support their claim that a committer of a major sin (kabirah) who dies without repentance is a disbeliever (kafir). The Khawarij argue that such a person is included in verses that threaten those who do not judge by divine law, those who are ungrateful, those who disbelieve after Hajj, those who deny and turn away, those whose scales are light, those whose faces are blackened, and those who are companions of the left hand (ashab al-mash'ama). Each verse is interpreted as applying to the fasiq (grave sinner). For example, the verse “And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed – then it is those who are the disbelievers” is used by the Khawarij to claim that any sin involving failure to judge by divine law constitutes kufr. Similarly, the verse about the blazing Fire (Surah al-Layl) is applied directly: “and the fasiq will enter it.” The Khawarij's method is to take these general threats as evidence that the fasiq is included among the disbelievers. These proofs set up the scriptural basis for their position.

Key Terms
الخوارج
كافرون
كفور
كفر
تكذيب
تولى

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
Which verse do the Khawarij use as their first proof?
A) 'And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed – then it is those who are the disbelievers'
B) 'And do we recompense except the ungrateful?'
C) 'And whoever disbelieves – then indeed, Allah is free from need of the worlds'
D) 'Indeed, the punishment is upon whoever denies and turns away'

Key Takeaway
The Khawarij's first four proofs appeal to verses that explicitly mention disbelief, ingratitude, denial, and turning away, which they apply literally to all grave sinners.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read verses about divine punishment, do you instinctively think of others who 'deserve' it, or do you first examine your own soul? The Khawarij used these verses to condemn others. How can you protect yourself from using God's words as a weapon against sinners instead of a mirror for your own heart? What is one specific change you will make in how you recite or reflect on such verses beginning today?
?
The Khawarij were certain they understood these verses correctly, yet their certainty led to bloodshed. Are there beliefs you hold so firmly that you reject any possibility of being mistaken? Could pride be masquerading as piety in your heart? Write down one area where you need to humble yourself before Allah and seek deeper understanding before judging.
?
These four proofs all revolve around labels: disbeliever, ungrateful, denier. Do you ever label people or groups in your mind as 'lost' or 'condemned' based on a few verses? How would your relationship with fellow Muslims change if you saw every apparent 'proof' against them as a call for mercy and guidance first? Commit to a specific action this week that reflects that mercy.

Last Four Proofs of the Khawarij (Fire, Scales, Faces, Left Hand)
Khawarij present four additional Quranic verses to support their claim that a grave sinner is among the disbelievers.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the final four scriptural proofs used by the Khawarij to argue that a grave sinner is a disbeliever.

Core Idea
The Khawarij argue that the Fire is for the worst sinners, and the fasiq fits that description.

The Khawarij continue their scriptural argument with four more verses that associate punishment with denial and sin. They directly apply verses about the blazing Fire, light scales, blackened faces, and the companions of the left hand to the fasiq.

Guided Path
1
Read the last four proofs
Read the Arabic passage containing proofs 5–8. Notice that the Khawarij explicitly write phrases like 'والفاسق يصلاها' (the fasiq will enter it).
This explicit connection is stronger than in the first four proofs.
2
Identify the descriptions used
Observe the descriptors: 'the most wretched', 'those whose scales are light', 'those whose faces are blackened', 'companions of the left hand'. The Khawarij equate these with the fasiq.
3
Reflect on the cumulative argument
Consider how these four proofs build on the previous ones to portray the fasiq as deserving eternal punishment in Hell.
The Khawarij's case appears strong if taken literally, but mainstream scholars argue these verses refer to disbelievers, not sinners.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وحجة الخوارج وجوه : الأول : تقوله تعالى : ( ومن لم يحكم بما أنزل الله فأولئك هم الكافرون ) ( المائدة : ٤٤) والثاني : قوله تعالى : ( وهل نجازى إلا الكفور ) ( سبأ : ١٧ ) والثالث : قوله تعالى بعد ايجاب الحج : ( ومن كفر فإن الله غني عن العالمين ) ( آل عمران : ٩٧ ) والرابع : قوله تعالى : ( إن العذاب على من كذب وتولى ) ( طه : ٤٨ ) والخامس : قوله تعالى : ( فأنذرتكم نارا تلظى ، لا يصلاها إلا الأشقى ، الذي كذب وتولى ) ( الليل : ١٤ - ١٦ ) والفاسق يصلاها . والسادس : قوله تعالى في حق من خفت موازينه : ( ألم تكن آياتي تتلى عليكم فكنتم بها تكذبون ) ( المؤمنون : ١٠٥) والفاسق ممن خفت موازينه . والسابع : قوله تعالى : ( يوم تبيض وجوه وتسود وجوه ) ( آل عمران : ١٠٦) والفاسق ممن وجهه مسود . والثامن : أنه من أصحاب المشأمة قال تعالى : ( والذين كفروا بآياتنا هم أصحاب المشأمة ) ( البلد : ١٩ )
And the proof of the Khawarij are aspects: First: His statement, the Exalted: (And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed – then it is those who are the disbelievers) (al-Ma'idah 5:44). Second: His statement, the Exalted: (And do we recompense except the ungrateful?) (Saba' 34:17). Third: His statement, the Exalted, after making Hajj obligatory: (And whoever disbelieves – then indeed, Allah is free from need of the worlds) (Aal 'Imran 3:97). Fourth: His statement, the Exalted: (Indeed, the punishment is upon whoever denies and turns away) (Taha 20:48). Fifth: His statement, the Exalted: (So I have warned you of a blazing Fire. None will [enter to] burn therein except the most wretched one, who had denied and turned away) (al-Layl 92:14-16), and the fasiq will enter it. Sixth: His statement, the Exalted, regarding those whose scales are light: (Were not My verses recited to you, but you used to deny them?) (al-Mu'minun 23:105), and the fasiq is among those whose scales are light. Seventh: His statement, the Exalted: (On the Day [some] faces will turn white and [some] faces will turn black) (Aal 'Imran 3:106), and the fasiq is among those whose face is blackened. Eighth: That he is among the companions of the left hand; the Exalted said: (And those who disbelieve in Our signs – they are the companions of the left hand) (al-Balad 90:19).
Source pages p. 122

Commentary
This passage presents the first eight scriptural proofs used by the Khawarij to support their claim that a committer of a major sin (kabirah) who dies without repentance is a disbeliever (kafir). The Khawarij argue that such a person is included in verses that threaten those who do not judge by divine law, those who are ungrateful, those who disbelieve after Hajj, those who deny and turn away, those whose scales are light, those whose faces are blackened, and those who are companions of the left hand (ashab al-mash'ama). Each verse is interpreted as applying to the fasiq (grave sinner). For example, the verse “And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed – then it is those who are the disbelievers” is used by the Khawarij to claim that any sin involving failure to judge by divine law constitutes kufr. Similarly, the verse about the blazing Fire (Surah al-Layl) is applied directly: “and the fasiq will enter it.” The Khawarij's method is to take these general threats as evidence that the fasiq is included among the disbelievers. These proofs set up the scriptural basis for their position.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Are you quick to label someone as 'outside the fold' based on one sin or mistake you see? How would you feel if Allah judged you by your worst moment? Today, commit to see the believer in every Muslim, no matter their sin, and leave judgment to Allah.
?
The Khawarij used literal verses to condemn others. Do you ever use isolated verses to justify your own harshness or to ignore the bigger picture of mercy? What is one action you can take this week to practice 'tender-heartedness' (riqqat al-qalb) toward a fellow Muslim you previously judged harshly?
?
If your own inner sins were exposed on the Day of Judgment, would you still be confident in calling someone else a kafir? How does that realization change how you view those who sin? This very moment, turn to Allah in repentance for any arrogance you have had toward others, and ask Him to purify your heart.

Sects and Disagreements
intermediate
8 min
Khawarij Scriptural Proofs 9-16
Khawarij enumerate Quranic proofs 9-16.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the Khawarij use Quranic verses to argue that committing major sins (fisq) constitutes disbelief (kufr).

Core Idea
The Khawarij weaponize Quranic verses to erase the distinction between sin and disbelief.

The Khawarij present a barrage of Quranic proofs to support their claim that any major sinner is a disbeliever. In this lesson, we examine proofs nine through sixteen, which invoke verses about disgrace, curse, and the fate of wrongdoers. These verses are taken out of context to equate fisq with kufr.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage of proofs 9-16
Read the Arabic and translation for proofs nine through sixteen carefully. Notice the pattern: each proof cites a verse that the Khawarij interpret as applying to all fasiqun.
These verses are originally about specific disbelievers, not all sinners.
2
Identify the Kharijite interpretation
For each verse, ask: how do the Khawarij use this to prove that a fasiq is a kafir? Look for key words like 'fasiqun', 'disgrace', 'curse', 'fire'.
They often take a description of disbelievers and apply it to anyone who commits a grave sin.
3
Reflect on the pattern
Notice that the Khawarij rely on equating terms. For example, the ninth proof equates 'fasiq' with 'kafir' because the verse calls disbelievers 'fasiqun'. Consider why this reasoning is flawed.
Context and other verses show that not all fasiqun are disbelievers.
4
Prepare for the next lesson
These scriptural proofs set the stage for the hadith and rational proofs that follow. In the next lesson, we will see the Khawarij's strongest arguments and their refutation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والتاسع : « ومن كفر بعد ذلك فأولئك هم الفاسقون » ( النور : ٥٥ ) فانه يقتضى المبتدأ فى الخبر ، أي الكافر فاسق والعاشر : قوله تعالى : « انه لا ييأس من روح الله الا القوم الكافرون » ( يوسف : ۸۷ ) والفاسق آيس من روح الله والحادي عشر : قوله تعالى : ( انك من تدخل النار فقد أخزيته ) ( آل عمران : ۱۹۲ ) مع قوله تعالى : « ان الخزى اليوم والسوء على الكافرين ( ) النحل : ۲۷ ) . والثانى عشر : قوله تعالى ( وأما من أوتى كتابه بشماله « الى قوله تعالى : ( انه كان لا يؤمن بالله العظيم ) ( الحاقة : ٢٥ - ٣٣ ) .. والثالث عشر : قوله تعالى : أن لعنة الله على الظالمين (الأعراف ٤٤). والرابع عشر : قوله تعالى : ( وأما الذين فسقوا فمأواهم النار » ( السجدة : ٢٠ ) . والخامس عشر : قوله تعالى : يتساءلون عن المجرمين . ما سلككم فى سقر » ؟ الى قوله : « وكنا نكذب بيوم الدين » ) المدثر : ٤٦٤٠ ( والسادس عشر : قوله تعالى : ( وسيق الذين كفروا » الى قوله : وسبق الذين اتقوا ) ( الزمر : ۷۳۷۱) والسابع عشر : قوله عليه السلام : « من ترك الصلاة متعمدا فقد كفر ) وقوله عليه السلام : « من مات ولم يحج فليمت ان شاء يهوديا وان شاء نصرانيا (١) . والثامن عشر : ولاية الله وعداوته ضدان فلا واسطة بينهما ، وولاية الله ايمان فعدواته كفر (۱) رد صاحب المواقف على هذين الحديثين بقوله : هما من أحاديث الآحاد ، وأحاديث الآحاد لا تعارض الاجماع .
And the ninth: (And whoever disbelieves after that – then it is those who are the defiantly disobedient) (an-Nur 24:55), for it implies the predicate of the subject, meaning the disbeliever is a fasiq. And the tenth: His statement, the Exalted: (Indeed, no one despairs of relief from Allah except the disbelieving people) (Yusuf 12:87), and the fasiq despairs of Allah's mercy. And the eleventh: His statement, the Exalted: (Indeed, whoever You admit to the Fire – You have disgraced him) (Aal 'Imran 3:192), with His statement, the Exalted: (Indeed, disgrace this day and evil are upon the disbelievers) (an-Nahl 16:27). And the twelfth: His statement, the Exalted: (But as for he who is given his record in his left hand) until His statement: (Indeed, he did not believe in Allah, the Most Great) (al-Haqqah 69:25-33). And the thirteenth: His statement, the Exalted: (Indeed, the curse of Allah is upon the wrongdoers) (al-A'raf 7:44). And the fourteenth: His statement, the Exalted: (But as for those who defiantly disobey, their refuge is the Fire) (as-Sajdah 32:20). And the fifteenth: His statement, the Exalted: (They will question each other about the criminals: "What put you into Saqar?"... until: "And we used to deny the Day of Recompense") (al-Muddaththir 74:40-46). And the sixteenth: His statement, the Exalted: (And those who disbelieved are driven) until: (And those who feared Allah are led) (az-Zumar 39:71-73). And the seventeenth: His statement, peace be upon him: "Whoever intentionally abandons prayer has disbelieved" and his statement, peace be upon him: "Whoever dies without performing Hajj, let him die as a Jew or as a Christian if he wishes." And the eighteenth: The friendship of Allah and His enmity are opposites, so there is no intermediate between them, and the friendship of Allah is faith, so His enmity is disbelief. (1) The author of al-Mawaqif responded to these two hadiths by saying: They are solitary hadiths (ahad), and solitary hadiths do not contradict consensus (ijma).
Source pages p. 123

Commentary
This passage completes the Khawarij's proofs from nine to eighteen and includes a critical note on two hadiths. The ninth proof uses the verse from Surah an-Nur where 'fasiqun' is applied to disbelievers, arguing that the fasiq is a kafir. The tenth uses the verse that only disbelievers despair of mercy, claiming the fasiq despairs. The eleventh combines two verses about disgrace to link the fasiq to disbelievers. The twelfth uses the description of those given their book in the left hand, who are described as not believing. The thirteenth declares curse upon wrongdoers (zalimin), equating them with disbelievers. The fourteenth directly states that the fasiq's refuge is the Fire. The fifteenth uses the dialogue of the people of Saqar who admit to denying the Day. The sixteenth contrasts the driving of disbelievers with the leading of the righteous. The seventeenth presents two hadiths: abandoning prayer intentionally is kufr, and dying without Hajj is like dying as a Jew or Christian. The eighteenth is a rational argument: God's friendship (wilayah) and enmity (adawah) are opposites with no intermediate; since wilayah is faith, enmity is kufr, so the fasiq must be a kafir. The passage ends with a note from the author of al-Mawaqif, who responds to the two hadiths in the seventeenth proof, arguing that they are ahad (solitary) narrations and thus cannot override the consensus (ijma) of the Muslim community. This shows that the Khawarij's scriptural proofs are not decisive, as the consensus upholds the view that grave sinners are not kafir.

Key Terms
فاسق
كفر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That these verses directly prove that every fasiq is a kafir without considering context or other verses.
Correction: The verses are about specific categories of disbelievers; they do not address the general sinner who may still have faith.

Checkpoint
Which proof uses the verse that only disbelievers despair of Allah's mercy?
A) Proof 9
B) Proof 10
C) Proof 11
D) Proof 12

Key Takeaway
The Khawarij compile verses to conflate fisq with kufr, but these verses are directed at specific categories of disbelievers, not every sinner.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever used a single verse to pass judgment on someone without considering its full context? When you next feel tempted to do so, will you pause and ask yourself if you are falling into the Khawarij mentality?
?
Think of a fellow Muslim who committed a sin you found severe. Did you subtly consider their iman lessened? How can you train your heart to hate the sin but not the sinner, and to never equate sin with disbelief?
?
If you were to apply the Khawarij method of proof text to your own life, which verse would they use to declare you a disbeliever? How does that realization compel you to seek Allah's mercy and to extend mercy to others?

6 min
Proofs 17-18 and Refutation of Khawarij Hadiths
Khawarij present hadith and rational proof, then al-Mawaqif refutes the hadiths as ahad.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Khawarij's use of hadith and rational argument to equate sin with disbelief, and how al-Mawaqif refutes them using hadith methodology.

Core Idea
The Khawarij's strongest proof is refuted by the principle that solitary hadith cannot override consensus.

The Khawarij's seventeenth proof relies on two hadiths that seem to equate abandoning prayer or Hajj with disbelief. They also present a rational argument that God's friendship and enmity are opposites. The author of al-Mawaqif responds by pointing out the hadiths are solitary (ahad) and therefore cannot contradict the consensus of the community, which holds that grave sinners are not kafir.

Guided Path
1
Read the section on proof 17 and 18
Read the Arabic and translation for proofs 17 and 18. Note the two hadiths and the rational argument.
The hadith about prayer is well-known; the one about Hajj is less so.
2
Understand the rational argument
The eighteenth proof argues that God's friendship (wilayah) and enmity (adawah) are opposites with no middle ground. Since wilayah is faith, enmity must be disbelief. The Khawarij then conclude that the fasiq, who is not a friend of God, must be an enemy and thus a kafir.
This argument assumes that all people are either friends or enemies of God, ignoring the possibility of believers who sin.
3
Read the refutation
Al-Mawaqif responds by stating that the two hadiths are solitary (ahad) and cannot override the consensus (ijma) of the Muslim community. The consensus is that a person who commits a major sin is not a kafir unless he considers the sin permissible.
This shows the importance of hadith classification in theological debates.
4
Reflect on the methodology
Consider why ahad hadiths are not sufficient to overturn established consensus. This is a key principle in both fiqh and kalam.
Consensus is considered a decisive proof, while ahad hadiths are probabilistic.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That the hadith about abandoning prayer is definitive and proves every such person is a kafir.

Checkpoint
According to the author of al-Mawaqif, why are the two hadiths in the Khawarij's seventeenth proof not decisive?
A) They are fabricated
B) They are solitary (ahad) and cannot contradict consensus
C) They are abrogated
D) They are ambiguous

Key Takeaway
Ahad hadiths, even if authentic, cannot override the established consensus of the community, which is a primary source of Islamic law and theology.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever been quick to label someone a disbeliever based on a single action or a hadith you heard without checking the consensus of scholars? How does this lesson correct that impulse? Commit to verifying scholarly consensus before making such grave judgments.
?
The Khawarij used what they thought were strong proofs. In your own life, where might you be using partial evidence (like isolated verses or hadith) to form harsh opinions about others? Ask Allah to guide you to the balanced understanding of the mainstream.
?
The consensus of the Companions and scholars is a mercy that protects the community from extremism. Do you give proper weight to this consensus in your own understanding of faith? What concrete step can you take today to rely more on established teachings rather than isolated narrations?

5 min
The First Mu'tazila Argument: Practice of the Early Community
First proof for the intermediate position based on early practice.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the first Mu'tazila proof for the intermediate position, which is based on the practice of the early Muslim community.

Core Idea
The early community treated the fasiq as neither believer nor disbeliever.

The Mu'tazila argued that the fasiq (grave sinner) occupies a middle state between belief and disbelief. To support this, they pointed to the actual treatment of fasiqs by the early Muslim community. This lesson examines their first proof, which draws on the community's practices of punishment and burial.

Guided Path
1
Read the first proof
Read the Arabic passage that presents the first argument. Notice how it starts by stating a consensus.
The passage uses 'بالإجماع' to claim agreement.
2
Identify the practices cited
List the three practices mentioned: applying punishment, not killing as apostate, and burial in Muslim cemeteries. Consider what each implies.
3
Consider the wife analogy
The passage adds an analogy about a husband accusing his wife. Think about how this supports the argument.
This part might be slightly confusing; focus on the main claim.
4
Reflect on the logical structure
The argument is that the community's actions show they did not consider the fasiq a disbeliever (since they didn't kill him) nor a believer (since they punished him). How does this lead to the intermediate position?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واحتج المعتزلة بوجهين : الأول : أن الفاسق ليس مؤمنا ، ولا كافرا بالاجماع .. لأنهم كانوا يقيمون عليه الحد ولا يقتلونه ، ولا يحكمون بردته ، ويدفنونه في مقابر المسلمين . وأيضا . فيلزم بينونة المرأة بمجرد رمى الزوج اياها بالزنى من غير لعان وقضاء قاض .. لأنه ان صدق فهى كافرة . وان كذب فهو كافر . والثانى : ما قاله واصل بن عطاء لعمرو بن عبيد ، وهو : أن فسقه معلوم ، وايمانه مختلف فيه ، فنترك الختلف فيه ونأخذ بالمتفق عليه
And the Mu'tazila argued with two points: First: That the fasiq is not a believer, nor a disbeliever by consensus... because they used to apply the prescribed punishment on him and not kill him, nor rule his apostasy, and they buried him in Muslim cemeteries. Also, it would necessitate the separation of the wife simply by the husband's accusation of fornication without mutual cursing and a judge's ruling... because if he is truthful, she is a disbeliever, and if he is lying, he is a disbeliever. Second: What Wasil ibn Ata said to Amr ibn Ubayd: His sinfulness is known, and his faith is disputed, so we leave what is disputed and take what is agreed upon.
Source pages p. 124

Commentary
This passage presents the Mu'tazila's arguments for their doctrine of 'al-manzila bayna al-manzilatayn' (the intermediate position), according to which a person who commits major sins (fasiq) is neither a believer nor a disbeliever but occupies a middle state. The first proof is based on the historical practice of the early community: they would apply the prescribed punishment (hadd) to a fasiq but would not kill him as an apostate, nor rule his apostasy, and they would bury him in Muslim cemeteries. This demonstrates that they did not consider him a disbeliever, yet they punished him, implying he is not a believer either. The author adds a further implication: if a husband accuses his wife of adultery without proof, and if the wife is truthful she would be a disbeliever (because adultery implies kufr? Or because the accusation is false?), and if the husband lies he would be a disbeliever — but the default ruling does not separate them without li'an (mutual cursing) and a judge's ruling, showing that a fasiq is not automatically a disbeliever. The second proof is attributed to Wasil ibn Ata (founder of the Mu'tazila) addressing Amr ibn Ubayd: the fasiq's sin is known, but his faith is disputed; therefore, they set aside the disputed matter (faith) and base their ruling on the agreed-upon matter (that he is a fasiq). Hence they treat him as neither a believer nor a disbeliever.

Key Terms
المعتزلة
فاسق
مؤمن
كافر
إجماع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that the consensus claimed here is an actual historical consensus of all Muslims, but it is the Mu'tazila's interpretation of early practice.
Correction: The 'consensus' refers specifically to the treatment of fasiqs by the early community, not a formal consensus of scholars.

Checkpoint
According to the first Mu'tazila argument, what does burying the fasiq in Muslim cemeteries indicate?
A) He is a disbeliever
B) He is a believer
C) He is not a disbeliever
D) He is an apostate

Key Takeaway
The first Mu'tazila proof argues that the early community's treatment of the fasiq as a punished yet buried Muslim shows he is neither a believer nor a disbeliever.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you see a fellow Muslim committing a major sin, do you instinctively distance yourself from them or judge them as having weak faith? How does the early community's practice of burying the fasiq in Muslim cemeteries challenge your response, and what will you do differently the next time you encounter such a person?
?
The Mu'tazila used the absence of killing or excommunication as evidence that the fasiq is not a disbeliever. Is there someone in your life whom you have mentally 'excommunicated' from the community of believers due to their sins? What would it mean for you to reconsider that judgment and treat them as a Muslim who needs correction, not condemnation?
?
If the early community applied hadd to the fasiq but still buried him in a Muslim cemetery, what does that say about the balance between upholding justice and maintaining brotherhood? Where in your own life do you tip too far toward harshness or leniency in dealing with sinners, and what concrete step will you take to restore balance?

intermediate
4 min
The Second Mu'tazila Argument: Wasil ibn Ata's Principle
Second proof using the principle of taking the agreed upon.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the second Mu'tazila proof for the intermediate position, based on Wasil ibn Ata's logical principle of focusing on agreed matters.

Core Idea
We should set aside disputed matters and rule based on what is agreed upon.

The second argument attributed to Wasil ibn Ata, the founder of the Mu'tazila, presents a logical principle: since the fasiq's sin is agreed upon while his faith is disputed, we should take the agreed fact (that he is a fasiq) and leave the disputed (his faith). This leads to the intermediate position.

Guided Path
1
Read Wasil's statement
Read the short Arabic passage. Note the key terms: فسقه معلوم (his sinfulness is known) and ايمانه مختلف فيه (his faith is disputed).
2
Understand the logical principle
The principle is to base judgment on what is certain and set aside what is uncertain. This is a common legal and theological principle.
This is similar to the principle of 'al-yaqin la yazulu bi al-shakk' (certainty is not removed by doubt).
3
Apply to the intermediate position
Since we agree he is a fasiq but disagree about his faith, we treat him according to his sinfulness, which places him in a middle state between believer and disbeliever.
The Mu'tazila conclude that the fasiq is neither a believer nor a disbeliever because faith is uncertain.
4
Reflect on the implication
Consider: if faith is disputed, does that mean he is not a believer? The Mu'tazila argue that we cannot affirm faith when it is disputed, so we withhold judgment.
This is different from the Sunni position that the fasiq remains a believer.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واحتج المعتزلة بوجهين : الأول : أن الفاسق ليس مؤمنا ، ولا كافرا بالاجماع .. لأنهم كانوا يقيمون عليه الحد ولا يقتلونه ، ولا يحكمون بردته ، ويدفنونه في مقابر المسلمين . وأيضا . فيلزم بينونة المرأة بمجرد رمى الزوج اياها بالزنى من غير لعان وقضاء قاض .. لأنه ان صدق فهى كافرة . وان كذب فهو كافر . والثانى : ما قاله واصل بن عطاء لعمرو بن عبيد ، وهو : أن فسقه معلوم ، وايمانه مختلف فيه ، فنترك الختلف فيه ونأخذ بالمتفق عليه
And the Mu'tazila argued with two points: First: That the fasiq is not a believer, nor a disbeliever by consensus... because they used to apply the prescribed punishment on him and not kill him, nor rule his apostasy, and they buried him in Muslim cemeteries. Also, it would necessitate the separation of the wife simply by the husband's accusation of fornication without mutual cursing and a judge's ruling... because if he is truthful, she is a disbeliever, and if he is lying, he is a disbeliever. Second: What Wasil ibn Ata said to Amr ibn Ubayd: His sinfulness is known, and his faith is disputed, so we leave what is disputed and take what is agreed upon.
Source pages p. 124

Commentary
This passage presents the Mu'tazila's arguments for their doctrine of 'al-manzila bayna al-manzilatayn' (the intermediate position), according to which a person who commits major sins (fasiq) is neither a believer nor a disbeliever but occupies a middle state. The first proof is based on the historical practice of the early community: they would apply the prescribed punishment (hadd) to a fasiq but would not kill him as an apostate, nor rule his apostasy, and they would bury him in Muslim cemeteries. This demonstrates that they did not consider him a disbeliever, yet they punished him, implying he is not a believer either. The author adds a further implication: if a husband accuses his wife of adultery without proof, and if the wife is truthful she would be a disbeliever (because adultery implies kufr? Or because the accusation is false?), and if the husband lies he would be a disbeliever — but the default ruling does not separate them without li'an (mutual cursing) and a judge's ruling, showing that a fasiq is not automatically a disbeliever. The second proof is attributed to Wasil ibn Ata (founder of the Mu'tazila) addressing Amr ibn Ubayd: the fasiq's sin is known, but his faith is disputed; therefore, they set aside the disputed matter (faith) and base their ruling on the agreed-upon matter (that he is a fasiq). Hence they treat him as neither a believer nor a disbeliever.

Key Terms
واصل بن عطاء
عمرو بن عبيد
فاسق
إيمان
متفق عليه
مختلف فيه

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to Wasil ibn Ata, what is the agreed-upon fact about the fasiq?
A) His faith
B) His disbelief
C) His sinfulness
D) His repentance

Key Takeaway
The second Mu'tazila proof uses the logical principle of preferring certainty over doubt to argue that since sinfulness is certain and faith is doubtful, the fasiq is in an intermediate state.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Reflect on a time when you were uncertain about your own faith. Did you rely on that uncertainty to excuse your actions, or did you focus on the certain knowledge of your sins? How would applying Wasil's principle change your self-assessment?
?
When you observe someone else's sin, do you often jump to conclusions about their faith? This lesson demands that you separate what you know for certain (their sin) from what you cannot know (their inner faith). How can you practice that discipline in your judgments?
?
Wasil's argument prioritizes certainty over doubt. In your own spiritual life, are there areas where you cling to comfortable doubts rather than face the certain call of repentance? What one step will you take today to act on what you know is true?

Juwayni: Imamate is not from the fundamentals of faith
Juwayni states that imamate is not part of creed and promises a separate book.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Imam al-Juwayni considered imamate outside the fundamental creedal beliefs of Sunni Islam and how his methodological approach distinguishes theology from political leadership.

Core Idea
In Sunni theology, imamate is important but not essential for faith.

Imam al-Juwayni, a leading Ash'ari theologian, made a clear distinction between the essential doctrines of faith and other important but non-essential teachings. In his work al-Aqida al-Nizamiyyah, he explicitly states that imamate (the leadership of the Muslim community) is not among the creedal beliefs (aqa'id) and that neglecting it does not harm one's faith. This passage reveals his methodological approach to theology and sets the stage for understanding the Sunni position on imamate.

Guided Path
1
Read the two statements from Juwayni
Read the Arabic passage and its translation carefully. Notice that the first statement is from the beginning of his book, and the second from the end.
The two statements bracket the entire work.
2
Analyze the first statement
Juwayni says imamate is not from the creedal beliefs. What does 'creedal beliefs' mean? Why would he need to say this? Consider the context of debates with Shi'a theology.
This is a direct refutation of the Shi'a view that imamate is a pillar of faith.
3
Understand the second statement
Juwayni had promised to include chapters on imamate but then decided to write a separate book. What does this tell you about the importance of imamate in his view? Is it unimportant or just different in kind?
It shows imamate is a distinct discipline, not part of theology.
4
Synthesize the overall message
Combine the two statements. Juwayni is drawing a boundary: theology deals with beliefs necessary for salvation; imamate is a separate, though important, subject.
This is a key methodological point in Sunni kalam.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقال أبو المعالي الجوينى : فى أول الكتاب ان الامامة ليست من العقائد ، ولو غفل عنها المرء لم تضره .. وقال الجوينى فى نهاية الكتاب : « وقد كنت وعدت أن أذكر فصولا فى الامامة ، ثم بدا لي : أن أفرد للمجلس السامي كتابا في الامامة
And Abu al-Ma'ali al-Juwayni said: At the beginning of the book, that imamate is not among the creedal beliefs, and if a person neglects it, it does not harm him. And al-Juwayni said at the end of the book: 'I had promised to mention chapters on imamate, then it occurred to me to devote a book for the lofty assembly on imamate.'
Source pages p. 126

Commentary
This passage contains two direct statements from Imam al-Juwayni about the status of imamate (leadership of the Muslim community). First, at the outset of his work 'Al-Aqida al-Nizamiyyah', he declares that imamate is not part of the fundamental creedal beliefs ('aqa'id). A person who ignores it does not suffer spiritual harm, implying it is not essential for salvation or correct faith. Second, at the conclusion of the same book, he mentions that he had previously promised to include chapters on imamate but then decided instead to write a separate work dedicated to the subject, addressed to the 'lofty assembly' (likely a patron or ruler). This shows that Juwayni views imamate as a distinct topic, important enough for a treatise but not belonging to the core of Islamic theology.

Key Terms
الإمامة
العقائد
المجلس السامي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might assume imamate is a pillar of faith in Sunni theology, but Juwayni explicitly excludes it from the fundamentals.
Correction: Juwayni's statement clarifies that imamate is not a creedal belief required for salvation, contrary to Shi'a theology.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the ruling of neglecting imamate?
A) It is a major sin.
B) It does not harm a person.
C) It invalidates one's faith.
D) It requires a separate book to explain.

Key Takeaway
Juwayni firmly places imamate outside the core creedal beliefs of Sunni Islam, reserving it for a separate treatise.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Is there a political leader, party, or system you have implicitly treated as essential to your faith — such that disagreeing with it feels like a betrayal of Islam? Are you willing to examine that today?
?
When you study deen, do you sometimes confuse things that are important (like good governance) with things that are foundational to your salvation (like tawheed)? What would change if you clearly separated the two?
?
Juwayni promised a separate book on imamate, showing it matters, but he refused to make it a matter of creed. Is there a topic in your life where you've elevated a practical or political issue to the level of non-negotiable faith? Are you ready to demote it?

The Rafidah View on Imamate: Reason, Revelation, and the Quest for Nass
Juwayni reports the Rafidah claim that imamate is obligatory by reason and established by nass.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Rafidah (Imami Shia) position that imamate is rationally obligatory but known only through explicit textual designation (nass), and the resulting disputes among them and Sunni scholars.

Core Idea
Imamate is obligatory by reason, but its identity is only known by revelation.

The Rafidah present a distinct theological position on imamate: it is a rational necessity, but only revelation can tell us who the imam is. Imam Juwayni reports their claim and then examines the options for how the imam is determined, leading to three sectarian positions and the Sunni rejection of a specific nass. This lesson unpacks that argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the Rafidah's basic claim
Understand the first sentence: they say appointment is obligatory by reason, and revelation only confirms reason. Note the separation between obligation and knowledge.
The distinction between 'wujub' (obligation) and 'ma'rifa' (knowledge) is key.
2
Identify the three possible methods
Once revelation is accepted as the source of obligation, three ways to determine the imam are given: nass, choice by ahl al-hall wa al-aqd, or self-qualification. Consider each.
3
Examine the Imamiyyah's insistence on nass
The Imamiyyah and others hold that only nass works. Then they split into three sects over who was designated. Note the three names: Abu Bakr, al-Abbas, Ali.
This internal division shows that even among those who accept nass, there is disagreement.
4
Conclude with the Sunni position
The passage ends: many scholars say nass on a specific person is missing. This is the majority Sunni view. Reflect on how this contrasts with the Rafidah.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقالت الرافضة : يجب نصبه عقلا . وإن السمع انما ورد على جهة التأكيد لقضية العقل . فأما معرفة الامام فان ذلك مدرك من جهة السمع دون العقل . واذا سلم أن طريق وجوب الامامة السمع ، فهل يجب من جهة السمع بالنص على الأمام من جهة الرسول صلى الله عليه وسلم أم من جهة اختيار أهل الحل والعقد له ؟ أم بكمال خصال الأئمة فيه ، ودعاؤه ذلك الى نفسه كاف فيه ؟ فذهبت الامامية وغيرها الى أن الطريق الذي يعرف به الامام هو النص من الرسول صلى الله عليه وسلم ، ولا مدخل للاختيار فيه ، ثم اختلفوا على ثلاث فرق : فرقة تدعى النص على أبي بكر ، وفرقة تدعى النص على العباس ، وفرقة تدعى النص على على بن أبي طالب رضى الله عنهم . وذهب كثير من العلماء إلى أن النص على أمام بعينه مفقود
And the Rafidah said: It is obligatory to appoint him by reason. And revelation came only as an emphasis of the rational judgment. As for knowing the imam, that is perceived through revelation, not reason. And if it is conceded that the way of the obligation of imamate is revelation, then is it obligatory through revelation that the imam be designated by explicit text from the Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him, or by the choice of the people of solution and contract? Or is it sufficient that he possesses the complete qualities of imams and his calling to that [position]? The Imamiyyah and others held that the way by which the imam is known is the explicit text from the Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him, and there is no room for choice in it. Then they disagreed into three sects: a sect claiming the text upon Abu Bakr, a sect claiming the text upon al-Abbas, and a sect claiming the text upon Ali ibn Abi Talib, may Allah be pleased with them. And many of the scholars held that explicit text upon a specific imam is missing.
Source pages p. 127

Commentary
This passage reports the Rafidah (Imami Shia) position on imamate. They assert that appointing an imam is rationally obligatory, and revelation only confirms what reason dictates. However, they concede that knowing who specifically is the imam is only via revelation (sam'), not reason. The author then raises a conditional: if we accept that the obligation of imamate is via revelation, then there are three possible methods: explicit designation (nass) by the Prophet, selection by the people of authority (ahl al-hall wa al-aqd), or self-proclamation based on personal merit. The Imamiyyah and associated sects insist on nass as the only valid method, and within that they split into three groups over who was specifically designated: some say Abu Bakr, some say al-Abbas, and some say Ali. The passage ends by noting that many Sunni scholars hold that no explicit nass exists for any specific individual, leaving the door open for election.

Key Terms
الرافضة
الإمامية
النص
أهل الحل والعقد
السمع
العقل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Imami Shia reject reason in establishing imamate.
Correction: They hold imamate is obligatory by reason, but knowledge of the specific imam is only from revelation.

Checkpoint
According to the Imamiyyah, what is the only valid method to know the imam?
A) Choice by the people of solution and contract
B) The imam's personal merit and self-calling
C) Explicit text (nass) from the Prophet
D) Consensus of the scholars

Key Takeaway
The Rafidah differentiate between the rational obligation of imamate and the revealed knowledge of the imam, leading to a firm insistence on nass that splits into three claims, while Sunnis deny any specific nass.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The Rafidah argue that imamate is rationally obligatory, meaning their intellect compelled them to seek a divinely guided leader. Do you feel a similar rational duty to align your life under divine authority, or do you resist such obligation? Has your reason ever led you to a truth that your ego did not want to accept?
?
They then say that the identity of the imam is known only through revelation, not reason. In your own life, when you face a crucial decision—like who to follow or what path to take—do you primarily consult revelation (the Quran and Sunnah) or rely on your own reasoning? What is the result of each approach?
?
The passage recounts that those who insisted on nass split into three groups, each claiming a different person. This shows how even a shared principle can lead to division when applied. Is there a principle you hold so strongly that it has caused division with others? How can you uphold truth without falling into sectarian strife?
