Three Opinions on Appointment of the Imam
Juwayni classifies three ways an imam is appointed.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's classification of the three ways an imam can be appointed, including the role of nass and consensus, and the ruling that one qualified person can validly contract the imamate.

Core Idea
The imam's appointment comes by nass, designation, or community consensus.

Juwayni begins by presenting the three opinions of the scholars on how an imam is legitimately appointed. He then focuses on the third method—consensus of the people of influence—and clarifies a key ruling: the contract is valid even if made by a single qualified person, contrary to some who require a group. This passage establishes the foundations of legitimate leadership in Sunni political thought.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then the translation. Notice the three opinions enumerated.
The passage includes a corrupted section marked with [?].
2
Identify the three opinions
List the three ways an imam can be appointed according to Juwayni: nass on one, nass on a group, and consensus of ahl al-hall wa al-'aqd.
3
Understand the third opinion
Focus on the explanation of consensus: when the imam dies without a successor, the community in the capital chooses an imam, and that choice binds all Muslims.
4
Note the subsidiary ruling
Juwayni affirms that one person from ahl al-hall wa al-'aqd can validly contract the imamate, contrary to those who require a group. He cites consensus.
This is a key point of Sunni political jurisprudence.
5
Reflect on the implications
Consider why this classification matters for Sunni political theology and how it limits or legitimizes leadership.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واختلف فيما يكون به الإمام إماما على ثلاثة أقوال: أحدها: إذا نص الإمام على واحد معين من بعده، فإنه يكون إماما. والثاني: إذا نص الإمام على جماعة يختارون منهم واحدا. والثالث: إجماع أهل الحل والعقد. وذلك أن الجماعة في مصر من أمصار المسلمين، إذا مات إمامهم ولم يكن لهم إمام، ولا استخلف، فأقام أهل ذلك المصر الذي هو حضرة الإمام وموضعه إماما لأنفسهم اجتمعوا عليه ورضوه، فإن كل من خلفهم وأمامهم من المسلمين في الآفاق يلزمهم الدخول في طاعة ذلك الإمام. [؟] فان عقدها واحد من أهل الحل والعقد فذلك ثابت ويلزم الغير فعله، خلافا لبعض الناس حيث قال: لا تنعقد إلا بجماعة من أهل الحل والعقد، قال الإمام أبو المعالي: (من انعقدت له الإمامة بعقد واحد، لزمت، ولا يجوز خلعه من غير حدث وتغير أمر) قال: (وهذا مجمع عليه)
And they differed concerning that by which the imam becomes an imam, on three opinions: One of them: When the imam designates (nassa) a specific person after him, then he becomes imam. The second: When the imam designates a group from whom they choose one. The third: Consensus (ijma) of the people of loosening and binding (ahl al-hall wa al-'aqd). That is, the community in a city of the Muslim cities, when their imam dies and they have no imam, and he did not appoint a successor, the people of that city which is the seat of the imam and his place set up an imam for themselves, they agreed upon him and approved him, then every one of the Muslims in the regions who is behind them and before them is obliged to enter into obedience to that imam. So if one person from ahl al-hall wa al-'aqd contracts it, that is established and it is obligatory for others to do it, contrary to some people who said: It is not concluded except by a group from ahl al-hall wa al-'aqd. Imam Abu al-Ma'ali said: 'For whom the imamate is concluded by a single contract, it is binding, and it is not permissible to remove him without an event and a change of circumstances.' He said: 'And this is agreed upon.'
Source pages p. 128

Commentary
This passage is a classification and ruling on the appointment of the imam. Juwayni begins by stating that there are three opinions on how an imam becomes imam: (1) the previous imam designates a specific successor (nass), (2) the imam designates a group from which they choose one, or (3) the consensus of ahl al-hall wa al-'aqd (the people of influence and decision). He then explains the third method: when the imam dies without appointing a successor, the community in the capital city can choose and agree upon an imam, and that choice is binding on all Muslims. He then affirms that the contract of imamate by a single person from ahl al-hall wa al-'aqd is valid and obligatory for others to follow, contrary to some who require a group. He quotes Imam Abu al-Ma'ali (al-Juwayni himself) stating that once the imamate is concluded by one contract, it is binding and cannot be removed without a just cause (event and change). And he asserts this is a matter of consensus.

Key Terms
إمام
نص
أهل الحل والعقد
إجماع
عقد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The imamate can only be concluded by a group of electors.
Correction: Juwayni affirms that one qualified person from ahl al-hall wa al-'aqd can validly contract the imamate, and this is a matter of consensus.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, which of the following is NOT one of the three opinions on how an imam is appointed?
A) By nass of the previous imam on one person
B) By nass on a group who choose one
C) By direct divine revelation
D) By consensus of ahl al-hall wa al-'aqd

Key Takeaway
Juwayni presents three valid methods of imam appointment: explicit designation, designation with choice, and consensus of the community leaders; and a single qualified elector suffices to make the contract binding.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
This lesson shows that the imam can be appointed by consensus of the community leaders. When you see disputes over leadership today, do you consider the Islamic principles of selection, or do you follow personal opinions and emotions? What step will you take to align your views with the Sunnah?
?
Juwayni asserts that once the imamate is concluded by a valid contract, it is binding and cannot be removed without cause. Think of a time you criticized a leader. Did you check whether the criticism was based on a valid 'event and change' according to this standard? How can you apply this lesson to your attitude towards authority?
?
If a single person from ahl al-hall wa al-'aqd can contract the imamate, does that challenge your assumptions about how leadership should be decided? Are you willing to accept a leader chosen by qualified scholars even if you personally disagree? What would you do if you found yourself in a community without a leader?

The Usurper's Imamate
Juwayni discusses the imamate of a usurper by force.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Islamic ruling on the imamate of a usurper who meets the qualifications, and the duties of subjects towards such a ruler.

Core Idea
A qualified usurper becomes imam by force; subjects owe him obedience and payment.

Juwayni addresses a practical political reality: when a capable person seizes power by force, does he become a legitimate imam? The author argues that if the usurper possesses the qualifications for imamate, his seizure by force and domination renders him imam. He supports this with a fatwa from the early Sufi Sahl al-Tustari, outlining the community's duties.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic and translation once to get the overall argument.
2
Identify the condition
Notice that Juwayni conditions legitimacy on the usurper having 'ahl al-imama' (qualification for imamate).
3
Examine the fatwa
Sahl al-Tustari's responses list duties: answering, paying, not opposing, not fleeing, keeping secrets.
This shows pragmatic acceptance.
4
Reflect on implications
Consider why such a ruling might be necessary to prevent chaos and preserve social order.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فإن تغلب من له أهلية الإمامة وأخذها بالقهر والغلبة، فإنه يكون إماما. فقد سئل سهل بن عبد الله التستري: ما يجب علينا لمن غلب على بلادنا وهو إمام؟ قال: تجيبه وتؤدي إليه ما يطالبك من حقه، ولا تنكر فعاله ولا تفر منه، وإذا ائتمنك على سر من أمر الدين لم نفشه.
And if one who has the qualification for the imamate overpowers and takes it by force and domination, then he becomes imam. For Sahl ibn Abd Allah al-Tustari was asked: What is obligatory upon us regarding one who has overpowered our land and he is an imam? He said: You answer him, and you pay to him what he demands of you from his right, and you do not deny his actions, nor flee from him, and if he entrusts you with a secret concerning the religion, do not disclose it.
Source pages p. 128

Commentary
This passage addresses the legitimacy of a usurper (mutaghallib) who seizes power by force. Juwayni states that if a person qualified for the imamate seizes it by force (qahr and ghalaba), he becomes imam. He then provides a supporting fatwa from Sahl al-Tustari, who was asked about the duty towards a ruler who has taken over their land. Sahl instructs: answer his summons, pay him what he demands as his right (presumably taxes), do not openly oppose his actions, do not flee from him, and if he entrusts you with a secret concerning religion, do not disclose it. This indicates a pragmatic acceptance of de facto rulers to avoid chaos.

Key Terms
تغلب
أهلية الإمامة
قهر
غلبة
سهل بن عبد الله التستري

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Only an imam appointed through proper election or designation can be legitimate; a usurper can never be a true imam.
Correction: Juwayni shows that if the usurper has the qualifications for imamate and seizes power by force, he becomes imam, and the community must obey him.

Checkpoint
According to Sahl al-Tustari's fatwa, what is NOT a duty towards a usurper imam?
A) Answer his summons
B) Pay him what he demands of his right
C) Deny his actions openly
D) Not disclose secrets entrusted to you

Key Takeaway
A qualified usurper who seizes power by force becomes the legitimate imam, and the community must obey him in certain duties to maintain order.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Think of a leader in your life—at work, in community, or government—who holds power without your full consent. What obligations does this lesson impose on you towards them? Can you discern any qualification in them? Are you tempted to flee or openly oppose? What does Sahl's advice demand from your heart?
?
If you were entrusted with a secret concerning religion by a ruler you dislike, would you keep it? Why is that command so heavy for you personally? What does this reveal about your trust in God's wisdom versus your own judgment?
?
When have you denied or criticized a leader's actions publicly? Did you consider the alternative of quiet obedience for the sake of harmony? What would this lesson have you do differently now, and what would that cost you?

Witnessing the Contract of Imamate
Juwayni enumerates disagreements on witnessing imamate.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the differences of opinion among scholars regarding the requirement and number of witnesses for the contract of imamate.

Core Idea
Witnessing the imamate contract is debated; even those who affirm it differ on the number.

Juwayni introduces a point of disagreement among scholars: whether witnessing the contract of imamate is necessary at all. Then, among those who affirm its necessity, there is further dispute about the minimum number of witnesses. This lesson clarifies these positions.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic passage once and its translation. Note the two levels of disagreement mentioned.
The passage is concise; each phrase carries a distinct point of contention.
2
Identify the first disagreement
The first disagreement is between those who affirm the need for witnesses (muthbit) and those who suspend judgment (waqif). Understand that some scholars do not consider witnesses necessary at all.
3
Identify the second disagreement
Among those who affirm, there is further disagreement on the required number of witnesses. Juwayni does not specify the numbers, but this indicates a lack of consensus.
This shows that even procedural details of imamate are subject to scholarly debate.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واختلف في الشهادة على عقد الإمامة بين مثبت وقاف. واختلف المثبتون في عدد الشهود. ويجوز نصب المفضول مع وجود الفاضل خوف الفتنة وألا يستقيم أمر الأمة.
And they differed concerning witnessing the contract of the imamate between one who affirms it and one who suspends. And those who affirm differed concerning the number of witnesses. And it is permissible to appoint the less virtuous (mafdul) in the presence of the more virtuous (afdal) out of fear of fitna and so that the affair of the community may not be set straight.
Source pages p. 128

Commentary
This passage discusses two procedural issues related to imamate. First, there is a difference of opinion on whether witnessing the contract of imamate is required: some affirm the need for witnesses, while others suspend judgment (waqif). Among those who affirm, they differ on the number of witnesses required (e.g., one, two, etc.). Second, Juwayni states that it is permissible to appoint a less virtuous person (mafdul) as imam even when a more virtuous (afdal) person is available, if doing so prevents fitna (discord) and maintains community stability. This is a pragmatic concession to avoid conflict.

Key Terms
شهادة
عقد الإمامة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: There is consensus that witnesses are required for the imamate contract.
Correction: Juwayni shows that scholars disagree on both the necessity and the number of witnesses.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is one point of disagreement among scholars regarding the contract of imamate?
A) Whether it requires witnesses
B) The number of witnesses
C) Both A and B
D) The qualification of the imam

Key Takeaway
Juwayni highlights that the requirement and number of witnesses for the imamate contract are matters of scholarly disagreement.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read that scholars disagreed on whether witnessing the imamate is even required, what does that reveal about your own readiness to accept leadership without evidence? Are there areas in your life where you have 'suspended judgment' (waqif) about a matter of faith because you were unsure, yet you have not actively sought clarification? What is one step you will take this week to move from suspension to affirmation based on evidence?
?
The source shows that even those who affirm the necessity of witnessing disagree on the number. In your own life, do you ever find yourself so focused on the exact 'number' of requirements (e.g., how many times you should pray, how much charity) that you lose sight of the core command? What is one area where you can simplify your approach to religious obligations this week?
?
This lesson is a reminder that even great scholars did not always agree on procedural matters. How does that confront your own tendency to dismiss those who differ from you on secondary issues? Will you commit to learning about the views of others before forming strong opinions?

Appointing the Less Virtuous Imam to Avoid Fitna
Juwayni states the permissibility of appointing a less virtuous imam.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the conditions under which it is permissible to appoint a less virtuous imam over a more virtuous one, according to Juwayni.

Core Idea
Appointing a less virtuous imam is allowed to prevent fitna and preserve community unity.

Juwayni addresses a practical scenario: what if the most qualified person for imamate is also controversial? He rules that it is permissible to appoint a less virtuous person to avoid discord and ensure the community's affairs are set straight. This lesson examines this pragmatic ruling.

Guided Path
1
Read the ruling
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Focus on the permissibility and the conditions mentioned.
The ruling is conditional: it only applies when there is fear of fitna.
2
Identify the condition
The condition is 'with the presence of the more virtuous' — meaning a more virtuous person is available but passed over.
This shows that in normal circumstances, the more virtuous should be preferred.
3
Understand the rationale
The rationale is twofold: 'out of fear of fitna' and 'so that the affair of the community may be set straight'. This reflects a principle of preventing greater harm.
Fitna refers to discord, tribulation, or civil strife.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واختلف في الشهادة على عقد الإمامة بين مثبت وقاف. واختلف المثبتون في عدد الشهود. ويجوز نصب المفضول مع وجود الفاضل خوف الفتنة وألا يستقيم أمر الأمة.
And they differed concerning witnessing the contract of the imamate between one who affirms it and one who suspends. And those who affirm differed concerning the number of witnesses. And it is permissible to appoint the less virtuous (mafdul) in the presence of the more virtuous (afdal) out of fear of fitna and so that the affair of the community may not be set straight.
Source pages p. 128

Commentary
This passage discusses two procedural issues related to imamate. First, there is a difference of opinion on whether witnessing the contract of imamate is required: some affirm the need for witnesses, while others suspend judgment (waqif). Among those who affirm, they differ on the number of witnesses required (e.g., one, two, etc.). Second, Juwayni states that it is permissible to appoint a less virtuous person (mafdul) as imam even when a more virtuous (afdal) person is available, if doing so prevents fitna (discord) and maintains community stability. This is a pragmatic concession to avoid conflict.

Key Terms
مفضول
فاضل
فتنة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The imam must always be the most virtuous person available.
Correction: Juwayni allows appointing a less virtuous imam if necessary to prevent fitna and ensure community stability.

Checkpoint
Why does Juwayni permit appointing a less virtuous imam?
A) Because the more virtuous is not available
B) To avoid fitna and keep the community united
C) Because it is easier
D) To follow the majority

Key Takeaway
Juwayni allows appointing a less virtuous imam when necessary to prevent fitna and ensure community stability.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever held out for the 'best' leader or candidate even when it caused division or delay? How does this ruling challenge your instinct to demand perfection from those who lead you?
?
In your family, workplace, or community, are you willing to accept a less qualified person to lead if that preserves unity—or do you insist on your own ideal? What would it cost you to let go of that standard for the sake of avoiding fitna?
?
This ruling permits a temporary compromise for the greater good. Is there a situation in your life where you have clung to a principle so rigidly that you caused harm? How can you apply the wisdom of prioritizing stability over personal preference starting today?

Removal of Imam for Sin
Juwayni states the majority and minority views on removal for sin.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the majority and minority views on when an imam is removed due to sin.

Core Idea
Not every sin removes an imam; only open sin or major failures.

Juwayni presents a key debate in Islamic political thought: when does an imam lose his position due to sin? The majority hold that open, known sin invalidates his trustworthiness, while others restrict removal to disbelief or abandoning prayer.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on removal
Read the Arabic text focusing on the two views about removal for sin.
Note the key terms: فسق, خلع, أمانة.
2
Identify the two views
Find the majority view (الجمهور) and the minority view (آخرون). Notice what conditions each sets for removal.
3
Understand the majority's reasoning
The majority says open sin (الفسق الظاهر المعلوم) invalidates trustworthiness. Why might open sin be the criterion?
Think about the impact on public trust.
4
Analyze the minority view
The minority limits removal to disbelief, abandoning prayer, or abandoning something from Sharia. What do they consider more severe?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والإمام إذا نصب، ثم فسق بعد انبرام العقد، قال الجمهور: تنتقض أمانته ويخلع بالفسق الظاهر المعلوم. وقال آخرون: لا يخلع إلا بالكفر أو بترك إقامة الصلاة أو الترك إلى دعائها أو شيء من الشريعة. ويجب عليه أن يخلع نفسه إذا وجد في نفسه نقصا يؤثر في الإمامة. فأما إذا لم يجد نقصا، فهل له أن يعزل نفسه ويعقد لغيره؟ اختلف الناس فيه: فمنهم من قال ليس له أن يفعل ذلك وإن فعل لم تخلع إمامته، ومنهم من قال: له أن يفعل ذلك.
And when the imam is appointed, then he becomes sinful (fasaqa) after the contract is concluded, the majority said: his trustworthiness is invalidated and he is removed for open, known sin. And others said: he is not removed except for disbelief (kufr) or abandoning the establishment of prayer, or abandoning [calling?] to it, or something from the Sharia. And it is obligatory upon him to remove himself if he finds in himself a deficiency affecting the imamate. As for if he does not find a deficiency, does he have the right to remove himself and appoint someone else? The people differed on it: among them are those who said he does not have the right to do that, and if he did, his imamate is not removed, and among them are those who said he has the right to do that.
Source pages p. 128

Commentary
This passage addresses two issues: removal of an imam for sin, and the imam's resignation. First, if an imam commits sin after appointment, the majority (jumhur) hold that his trustworthiness is invalidated and he is removed for open and known sin. Others say he is only removed for kufr, abandoning prayer, or abandoning something from the Sharia. Second, if the imam finds a deficiency in himself that affects his ability to lead, he is obliged to remove himself. But if he does not find such deficiency, there is disagreement: some say he cannot resign and his resignation is ineffective, while others say he may resign. This shows the tension between the necessity of leadership and the imam's personal capacity.

Key Terms
فسق
خلع
أمانة
فسق ظاهر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think any sin (even hidden) removes the imam immediately.
Correction: The majority require the sin to be open and known, and the minority require even more severe failings such as kufr or abandoning prayer.

Checkpoint
According to the majority view in this passage, what leads to the removal of an imam?
A) Any sin
B) Open and known sin
C) Only disbelief
D) Sin committed in secret

Key Takeaway
The majority require open sin for removal; the minority require more severe failures.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think about the imam's trustworthiness being invalidated by open sin, what hidden sin in your life are you keeping secret? Are you living with a false sense of trustworthiness that God sees through? Today, commit to confessing that sin to Allah and seeking His forgiveness.
?
The minority view only removes an imam for abandoning prayer. How often do you abandon your prayers, and yet still consider yourself a leader in your home, workplace, or community? What does that say about your own criteria for leadership? Will you strive to establish your prayer consistently as a first step toward worthy leadership?
?
The majority require open sin for removal – but God sees what is hidden. Are you more concerned with your public image than with your inner state before Allah? What hidden sin are you protecting while appearing trustworthy? Resolve to make sincere tawbah (repentance) for that sin today, regardless of whether anyone else knows about it.

Resignation of the Imam
Juwayni rules on when an imam must resign and the dispute on voluntary resignation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the rules regarding an imam's resignation, including when it is obligatory and when it is disputed.

Core Idea
An imam must resign if found deficient, but voluntary resignation is debated.

The second part of Juwayni's discussion turns to the imam's own resignation. He clarifies when it is obligatory—if the imam finds a deficiency—and then presents the disagreement on whether an imam may resign without deficiency.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on resignation
Read the Arabic text focusing on the imam's resignation.
Look for the phrase 'ويجب عليه أن يخلع نفسه'.
2
Identify the obligatory condition
When must the imam remove himself? Find the condition and understand what 'deficiency affecting the imamate' means.
3
Understand the disputed case
If there is no deficiency, can the imam resign? There are two opinions. Find each and note their reasoning.
4
Reflect on the implications
Think about why scholars might disagree on voluntary resignation. Consider the stability of leadership.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والإمام إذا نصب، ثم فسق بعد انبرام العقد، قال الجمهور: تنتقض أمانته ويخلع بالفسق الظاهر المعلوم. وقال آخرون: لا يخلع إلا بالكفر أو بترك إقامة الصلاة أو الترك إلى دعائها أو شيء من الشريعة. ويجب عليه أن يخلع نفسه إذا وجد في نفسه نقصا يؤثر في الإمامة. فأما إذا لم يجد نقصا، فهل له أن يعزل نفسه ويعقد لغيره؟ اختلف الناس فيه: فمنهم من قال ليس له أن يفعل ذلك وإن فعل لم تخلع إمامته، ومنهم من قال: له أن يفعل ذلك.
And when the imam is appointed, then he becomes sinful (fasaqa) after the contract is concluded, the majority said: his trustworthiness is invalidated and he is removed for open, known sin. And others said: he is not removed except for disbelief (kufr) or abandoning the establishment of prayer, or abandoning [calling?] to it, or something from the Sharia. And it is obligatory upon him to remove himself if he finds in himself a deficiency affecting the imamate. As for if he does not find a deficiency, does he have the right to remove himself and appoint someone else? The people differed on it: among them are those who said he does not have the right to do that, and if he did, his imamate is not removed, and among them are those who said he has the right to do that.
Source pages p. 128

Commentary
This passage addresses two issues: removal of an imam for sin, and the imam's resignation. First, if an imam commits sin after appointment, the majority (jumhur) hold that his trustworthiness is invalidated and he is removed for open and known sin. Others say he is only removed for kufr, abandoning prayer, or abandoning something from the Sharia. Second, if the imam finds a deficiency in himself that affects his ability to lead, he is obliged to remove himself. But if he does not find such deficiency, there is disagreement: some say he cannot resign and his resignation is ineffective, while others say he may resign. This shows the tension between the necessity of leadership and the imam's personal capacity.

Key Terms
خلع
عزل نفسه
نقص

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think an imam can always resign at will.
Correction: Juwayni shows that resignation is only obligatory if there is a deficiency; otherwise it is disputed, and some hold that resignation is not effective.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, when is it obligatory for an imam to resign?
A) When he wants to
B) When he finds a deficiency affecting the imamate
C) When the people demand it
D) Never

Key Takeaway
Resignation is obligatory only if the imam finds a deficiency; voluntary resignation is a matter of scholarly disagreement.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you were in a leadership position and discovered a deficiency in yourself that harmed those under your care, would you have the courage to step down? What might hold you back?
?
When scholars disagree on an issue, do you tend to follow the easier opinion without examining the reasoning? How does this passage challenge that tendency?
?
Are you willing to let go of a position you hold if it is best for others, even if you do not personally feel deficient? How do you distinguish between duty and ambition?

The Obligation of Bay'a and Refusing It
Juwayni states the obligation of bay'a and consequences of refusal.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the obligation of bay'a after the imam is appointed, and the difference between refusing with an excuse and without.

Core Idea
Obedience to the imam is obligatory; refusal without excuse is met with force to preserve unity.

After the imam is chosen by ahl al-hall wa al-'aqd, the entire community must pledge allegiance, committing to hear and obey. This passage outlines the ruling for those who refuse, distinguishing between excused and unexcused refusal.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on bay'a
Read the Arabic passage once and its translation. Notice the three elements of bay'a: hearing, obeying, and establishing the Sharia.
2
Identify the two types of refusal
The passage mentions two cases: refusal with an excuse and refusal without. Note the different consequences for each.
The excused are excused; the unexcused are compelled.
3
Reflect on the purpose
Why is compulsion used? The text says to prevent the disunity of the Muslims. Consider the priority of communal unity in Islamic political thought.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وإذا انعقدت الإمامة باتفاق أهل الحل والعقد أو بواحد على ما تقدم وجب على الناس كافة مبايعته على السمع والطاعة وإقامة كتاب الله وسنة رسوله صلى الله عليه وسلم. ومن تأبى عن البيعة لعذر عذر، ومن تأبى لغير عذر جبر وقهر، تفترق كلمة المسلمين. وإذا بويع الخليفتين، فالخليفة هو الأول ويقتل الآخر. واختلف في قتله هل هو محسوس أو معنى، أي عزله قتل له وموت: والأول وهو قتله المحسوس هو الحق لقول رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم (إذا بويع لخليفتين، فاقتلوا الآخر منهما) لكن إن تباعدت الأقطار وتباينت كالأندلس وخراسان جاز ذلك. ولو خرج خارجي على إمام معروف العدالة وجب على الناس جهاده، فإن كان الإمام فاسقا، والخارجي مظهر للعدل لم ينبغ للناس أن يسرعوا إلى نصرة الخارجي حتى يتبين أمره فيما يظهر من العدل، أو تنفق كلمة الجماعة على خلع الأول. وذلك أن كل من طلب مثل هذا الأمر أظهر من نفسه الصلاح حتى إذا تمكن رجع إلى عادته من خلاف ما أظهر. فأما إقامة إمامين أو ثلاثة في عصر واحد وبلد واحد، فلا يجوز إجماعا، لما ذكرنا. قال الإمام أبو المعالي: (ذهب أصحابنا إلى منع عقد الإمامة لشخصين في طرفي العالم) ثم قالوا: لو اتفق عقد الإمامة لشخصين نزل ذلك منزلة تزويج ولبين امرأة واحدة من زوجين من غير أن يشعر أحدهما بعقد الآخر. قال: (والذي عندي فيه: أن عقد الإمامة... )
And when the imamate is concluded by agreement of ahl al-hall wa al-'aqd or by one person as previously mentioned, it becomes obligatory upon all the people to pledge allegiance (bay'a) to him on hearing and obeying and establishing the Book of Allah and the Sunnah of His Messenger. And whoever refuses the bay'a for an excuse, he is excused; and whoever refuses without an excuse is compelled and coerced, and the word of the Muslims would disunite. And if two caliphs are pledged allegiance, the caliph is the first, and the other is killed. And they differed concerning his killing, whether it is physical or figurative, i.e., his removal is killing him and death: and the first, which is his physical killing, is the truth because of the saying of the Messenger of Allah: 'If two caliphs are pledged allegiance, kill the latter of them.' But if the regions are far apart and distinct, like al-Andalus and Khurasan, it is permitted. And if a khariji comes out against an imam known for justice, it is obligatory upon the people to fight him. But if the imam is sinful (fasiq) and the khariji is manifesting justice, the people should not hasten to support the khariji until his affair becomes clear concerning what he manifests of justice, or the word of the community agrees on removing the first. That is because everyone who seeks such a matter shows piety from himself until he gains power, then he returns to his habit contrary to what he showed. As for setting up two or three imams in one era and one land, it is not permissible by consensus, for what we have mentioned. Imam Abu al-Ma'ali said: 'Our companions went to prohibiting the contract of imamate for two persons in two ends of the world.' Then they said: If the contract of imamate for two persons should coincide, that would be like the marriage of one woman to two husbands without one knowing of the other's contract. He said: 'What I hold concerning it is that the contract of imamate...'
Source pages p. 129

Commentary
This passage summarizes key rulings on political leadership. After an imam is appointed, all Muslims must give bay'a (pledge allegiance) to him, committing to hearing and obeying and upholding the Sharia. Refusal without a valid excuse is met with compulsion to prevent disunity. If two caliphs are elected, the first is the rightful one and the second must be killed—Juwayni considers this physical killing, based on the hadith 'kill the latter.' However, if the regions are far apart (e.g., al-Andalus and Khurasan), multiple imams are permissible. Regarding rebels (khariji) against a just imam, they must be fought. But if the imam is sinful and the rebel claims justice, people should wait until the rebel's true nature is known or the community agrees to remove the first imam, because opportunists often feign piety. Finally, Juwayni, quoting the school, affirms consensus (ijma') that having two or more imams in one era and one land is prohibited. He compares it to marrying one woman to two men. His own view is given but the text cuts off.

Key Terms
بيعة
سمع وطاعة
أهل الحل والعقد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what happens to someone who refuses bay'a without a valid excuse?
A) They are excused and left alone
B) They are compelled and coerced
C) They are killed
D) They are exiled

Key Takeaway
Bay'a is mandatory after the imam's appointment, and those who refuse without a valid excuse are compelled to preserve Muslim unity.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When have I ever refused to submit to a legitimate authority in my life, and was my excuse truly valid or just a preference?
?
What does this teaching about compelled bay'a reveal about my own commitment to the unity of the Muslims when I disagree with a leader?
?
If I were in a situation where giving bay'a required personal sacrifice, would my heart seek a valid excuse or would I willingly obey for the sake of unity?

Ruling on Two Imams and Prohibition of Multiple Imams in One Land
Juwayni outlines the ruling for two imams and the consensus against multiple imams in one land.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the ruling when two imams are elected, the hadith about killing the later, the exception for far regions, and the consensus against multiple imams in one era and land.

Core Idea
If two caliphs are chosen, the first is valid and the second must be killed, except when regions are far apart.

The situation of two rival imams is a critical issue in Islamic political thought. Juwayni presents the ruling based on a hadith, discusses a scholarly difference on the nature of the killing, and then provides an exception for geographically separated regions. He also mentions the consensus that multiple imams cannot coexist in one land.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on two imams
Read the Arabic and translation carefully. Notice the immediate ruling that the first is caliph and the other is killed.
2
Understand the hadith and the difference of opinion
The passage cites the hadith 'kill the latter' and discusses whether killing is literal or figurative. Juwayni prefers literal interpretation.
This shows scholarly ijtihad on the meaning.
3
Note the exception and the consensus
The exception is for far-apart regions like al-Andalus and Khurasan. Then, consensus prohibits multiple imams in one land.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وإذا انعقدت الإمامة باتفاق أهل الحل والعقد أو بواحد على ما تقدم وجب على الناس كافة مبايعته على السمع والطاعة وإقامة كتاب الله وسنة رسوله صلى الله عليه وسلم. ومن تأبى عن البيعة لعذر عذر، ومن تأبى لغير عذر جبر وقهر، تفترق كلمة المسلمين. وإذا بويع الخليفتين، فالخليفة هو الأول ويقتل الآخر. واختلف في قتله هل هو محسوس أو معنى، أي عزله قتل له وموت: والأول وهو قتله المحسوس هو الحق لقول رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم (إذا بويع لخليفتين، فاقتلوا الآخر منهما) لكن إن تباعدت الأقطار وتباينت كالأندلس وخراسان جاز ذلك. ولو خرج خارجي على إمام معروف العدالة وجب على الناس جهاده، فإن كان الإمام فاسقا، والخارجي مظهر للعدل لم ينبغ للناس أن يسرعوا إلى نصرة الخارجي حتى يتبين أمره فيما يظهر من العدل، أو تنفق كلمة الجماعة على خلع الأول. وذلك أن كل من طلب مثل هذا الأمر أظهر من نفسه الصلاح حتى إذا تمكن رجع إلى عادته من خلاف ما أظهر. فأما إقامة إمامين أو ثلاثة في عصر واحد وبلد واحد، فلا يجوز إجماعا، لما ذكرنا. قال الإمام أبو المعالي: (ذهب أصحابنا إلى منع عقد الإمامة لشخصين في طرفي العالم) ثم قالوا: لو اتفق عقد الإمامة لشخصين نزل ذلك منزلة تزويج ولبين امرأة واحدة من زوجين من غير أن يشعر أحدهما بعقد الآخر. قال: (والذي عندي فيه: أن عقد الإمامة... )
And when the imamate is concluded by agreement of ahl al-hall wa al-'aqd or by one person as previously mentioned, it becomes obligatory upon all the people to pledge allegiance (bay'a) to him on hearing and obeying and establishing the Book of Allah and the Sunnah of His Messenger. And whoever refuses the bay'a for an excuse, he is excused; and whoever refuses without an excuse is compelled and coerced, and the word of the Muslims would disunite. And if two caliphs are pledged allegiance, the caliph is the first, and the other is killed. And they differed concerning his killing, whether it is physical or figurative, i.e., his removal is killing him and death: and the first, which is his physical killing, is the truth because of the saying of the Messenger of Allah: 'If two caliphs are pledged allegiance, kill the latter of them.' But if the regions are far apart and distinct, like al-Andalus and Khurasan, it is permitted. And if a khariji comes out against an imam known for justice, it is obligatory upon the people to fight him. But if the imam is sinful (fasiq) and the khariji is manifesting justice, the people should not hasten to support the khariji until his affair becomes clear concerning what he manifests of justice, or the word of the community agrees on removing the first. That is because everyone who seeks such a matter shows piety from himself until he gains power, then he returns to his habit contrary to what he showed. As for setting up two or three imams in one era and one land, it is not permissible by consensus, for what we have mentioned. Imam Abu al-Ma'ali said: 'Our companions went to prohibiting the contract of imamate for two persons in two ends of the world.' Then they said: If the contract of imamate for two persons should coincide, that would be like the marriage of one woman to two husbands without one knowing of the other's contract. He said: 'What I hold concerning it is that the contract of imamate...'
Source pages p. 129

Commentary
This passage summarizes key rulings on political leadership. After an imam is appointed, all Muslims must give bay'a (pledge allegiance) to him, committing to hearing and obeying and upholding the Sharia. Refusal without a valid excuse is met with compulsion to prevent disunity. If two caliphs are elected, the first is the rightful one and the second must be killed—Juwayni considers this physical killing, based on the hadith 'kill the latter.' However, if the regions are far apart (e.g., al-Andalus and Khurasan), multiple imams are permissible. Regarding rebels (khariji) against a just imam, they must be fought. But if the imam is sinful and the rebel claims justice, people should wait until the rebel's true nature is known or the community agrees to remove the first imam, because opportunists often feign piety. Finally, Juwayni, quoting the school, affirms consensus (ijma') that having two or more imams in one era and one land is prohibited. He compares it to marrying one woman to two men. His own view is given but the text cuts off.

Key Terms
خليفتين
قتل الآخر
محسوس
معنى
إجماع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the hadith 'kill the latter' applies to all cases, even when regions are far apart.
Correction: Juwayni allows multiple imams in far-apart regions like al-Andalus and Khurasan.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, when is it permissible to have two caliphs?
A) Never
B) When they are in far-apart regions like al-Andalus and Khurasan
C) When both are just
D) When the people agree

Key Takeaway
The first elected imam is the rightful one; the later must be killed unless the regions are far apart, and multiple imams in one land are prohibited by consensus.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever found yourself following a leader or community decision that later turned out to be illegitimate or divisive? What did you do, and what does this lesson teach you about the priority of the first legitimate authority?
?
The prohibition of two imams in one land is meant to prevent fitna. Where in your life have you seen the dangers of divided leadership—in your family, community, or workplace? Are you contributing to or healing that division?
?
Juwayni allows multiple imams when necessity demands. Are there times when you have compromised a principle for practical necessity, and did you do so with the same careful reasoning and regard for the greater good?

Fighting Rebels and Conditions for Supporting a Khariji
Juwayni gives rulings on fighting khariji rebels and cautions when the imam is sinful.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the obligation to fight a khariji against a just imam, and the caution required when the imam is sinful and the rebel claims justice.

Core Idea
A rebel against a just imam must be fought, but if the imam is oppressive, one should not rush to support a seemingly just rebel.

This passage deals with the practical issue of rebellion (khuruj) against the imam. It distinguishes between two scenarios: when the imam is known for justice, the rebels must be fought; when the imam is sinful and the rebel appears just, caution is needed because pretenders often hide their true nature.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on fighting rebels
Read the Arabic and translation. Notice it presents two cases based on the imam's character.
2
Identify the two cases
Case 1: Just imam → fighting the rebel is obligatory. Case 2: Sinful imam and rebel claims justice → do not rush to support.
In case 2, wait until the rebel's sincerity is clear or the community agrees to remove the first imam.
3
Understand the rationale for caution
The text explains that those who seek power often feign piety until they attain it, then revert to their old ways. This is a warning against naively supporting rebels.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وإذا انعقدت الإمامة باتفاق أهل الحل والعقد أو بواحد على ما تقدم وجب على الناس كافة مبايعته على السمع والطاعة وإقامة كتاب الله وسنة رسوله صلى الله عليه وسلم. ومن تأبى عن البيعة لعذر عذر، ومن تأبى لغير عذر جبر وقهر، تفترق كلمة المسلمين. وإذا بويع الخليفتين، فالخليفة هو الأول ويقتل الآخر. واختلف في قتله هل هو محسوس أو معنى، أي عزله قتل له وموت: والأول وهو قتله المحسوس هو الحق لقول رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم (إذا بويع لخليفتين، فاقتلوا الآخر منهما) لكن إن تباعدت الأقطار وتباينت كالأندلس وخراسان جاز ذلك. ولو خرج خارجي على إمام معروف العدالة وجب على الناس جهاده، فإن كان الإمام فاسقا، والخارجي مظهر للعدل لم ينبغ للناس أن يسرعوا إلى نصرة الخارجي حتى يتبين أمره فيما يظهر من العدل، أو تنفق كلمة الجماعة على خلع الأول. وذلك أن كل من طلب مثل هذا الأمر أظهر من نفسه الصلاح حتى إذا تمكن رجع إلى عادته من خلاف ما أظهر. فأما إقامة إمامين أو ثلاثة في عصر واحد وبلد واحد، فلا يجوز إجماعا، لما ذكرنا. قال الإمام أبو المعالي: (ذهب أصحابنا إلى منع عقد الإمامة لشخصين في طرفي العالم) ثم قالوا: لو اتفق عقد الإمامة لشخصين نزل ذلك منزلة تزويج ولبين امرأة واحدة من زوجين من غير أن يشعر أحدهما بعقد الآخر. قال: (والذي عندي فيه: أن عقد الإمامة... )
And when the imamate is concluded by agreement of ahl al-hall wa al-'aqd or by one person as previously mentioned, it becomes obligatory upon all the people to pledge allegiance (bay'a) to him on hearing and obeying and establishing the Book of Allah and the Sunnah of His Messenger. And whoever refuses the bay'a for an excuse, he is excused; and whoever refuses without an excuse is compelled and coerced, and the word of the Muslims would disunite. And if two caliphs are pledged allegiance, the caliph is the first, and the other is killed. And they differed concerning his killing, whether it is physical or figurative, i.e., his removal is killing him and death: and the first, which is his physical killing, is the truth because of the saying of the Messenger of Allah: 'If two caliphs are pledged allegiance, kill the latter of them.' But if the regions are far apart and distinct, like al-Andalus and Khurasan, it is permitted. And if a khariji comes out against an imam known for justice, it is obligatory upon the people to fight him. But if the imam is sinful (fasiq) and the khariji is manifesting justice, the people should not hasten to support the khariji until his affair becomes clear concerning what he manifests of justice, or the word of the community agrees on removing the first. That is because everyone who seeks such a matter shows piety from himself until he gains power, then he returns to his habit contrary to what he showed. As for setting up two or three imams in one era and one land, it is not permissible by consensus, for what we have mentioned. Imam Abu al-Ma'ali said: 'Our companions went to prohibiting the contract of imamate for two persons in two ends of the world.' Then they said: If the contract of imamate for two persons should coincide, that would be like the marriage of one woman to two husbands without one knowing of the other's contract. He said: 'What I hold concerning it is that the contract of imamate...'

This passage summarizes key rulings on political leadership. After an imam is appointed, all Muslims must give bay'a (pledge allegiance) to him, committing to hearing and obeying and upholding the Sharia. Refusal without a valid excuse is met with compulsion to prevent disunity. His own view is given but the text cuts off.

Fight a rebel against a just imam, but be cautious when the imam is unjust and the rebel claims reform.

?

?
The lesson warns that power-seekers feign piety. When you see someone calling for justice, do you examine their past actions and consistency, or do you get swept by emotional appeal? Write one specific test you will use to discern genuine reformers from pretenders.

Causes for Removal of an Imam
Juwayni reports two opinions on the removal of an imam.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the different scholarly opinions on what causes an imam to be removed from office.

Core Idea
Removal of an imam is disputed: some require transgression, others only apostasy or neglect of duties.

Juwayni presents two opposing views on the grounds for removing an imam. One view holds that the imam is removed if his trustworthiness is corrupted and he commits open transgression. Another restricts removal to apostasy or abandoning essential religious duties. This passage shows the range of opinion among early scholars.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Note the two distinct opinions.
The first opinion uses 'تتفسح أمانته' which may mean corruption of trustworthiness.
2
Identify the two views
Find the view that requires corruption of trustworthiness and transgression, and the view that requires apostasy or neglect of religious duties.
3
Consider why the second view is more restrictive
Think about the role of the imam as a custodian of religion. The stricter view ties removal to core religious failures.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الله تتفسح أمانته ويخلع بالفسق الظاهر المعلوم . وقال آخرون : لا ينخلع الا بالكفر أو بترك اقامة الصلاة أو الترك الى دعائها أو شيء من الشريعة : ويجب عليه أن يخلع نفسه اذا وجد فى نفسه نقصا يؤثر فى الامامة. فأما اذا لم يجد نقصا . فهل له أن يعزل نفسه ويعقد لغيره ؟ اختلف الناس فيه : فمنهم من قال ليس له أن يفعل ذلك وان فعل لم تتخلع امامته ومنهم من قال : له أن يفعل ذلك .
If his trustworthiness becomes corrupted [؟], he is removed due to apparent known transgression. Others said: he is not removed except by disbelief or by abandoning the establishment of prayer or abandoning calling to it or something from the Shariah. And he must remove himself if he finds in himself a deficiency that affects the imamate. As for if he finds no deficiency, does he have the right to remove himself and appoint someone else? People differed about that: among them are those who said he does not have the right to do that, and if he did, his imamate is not removed; and among them are those who said he has the right to do that.
Source pages p. 129

Commentary
This passage discusses the grounds for removal of an imam and whether he may voluntarily abdicate. First, it presents two views on what causes removal: one view holds that the imam is removed if his trustworthiness becomes corrupted and he commits evident transgression; another view restricts removal to apostasy (kufr) or abandoning the prayer or something of the Sharia. Then the author addresses the situation where the imam himself perceives a deficiency in himself that affects his leadership – he must then remove himself. However, if he perceives no deficiency, the question arises whether he may voluntarily abdicate and appoint someone else. Juwayni reports that there is disagreement: some say he may not, and his abdication is ineffective; others say he may. Note that the author does not give his own preference here, merely reports the difference.

Key Terms
الامامة
الفسق
الخلع
الكفر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that an imam can be removed for any minor sin.
Correction: The passage shows that some scholars require major transgressions like apostasy or abandoning prayer, not minor sins.

Checkpoint
According to the second opinion mentioned, what is NOT a ground for removal?
A) Apostasy
B) Abandoning prayer
C) Committing a minor sin
D) Neglecting to call to prayer

Key Takeaway
Scholars disagreed on the grounds for removing an imam, with one view requiring severe neglect and another including transgression.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you consider the two opinions on removing an imam, which one challenges your own assumptions about leadership and accountability? Are you more inclined to demand perfection or to overlook failings? How does this affect your expectations of those in authority over you?
?
The first view allows removal for open transgression and corruption of trust. Reflect on a time when you witnessed a leader's trustworthiness fail. Did you feel the need for accountability, or did you remain silent? What does your response reveal about your commitment to justice?
?
If you were to be entrusted with authority tomorrow, which view would you want applied to you? The strict one or the lenient one? Why? Let this question lead you to examine your own integrity and fear of accountability.

Voluntary Abdication of the Imam
Juwayni discusses the ruling on an imam voluntarily abdicating.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the rules regarding an imam voluntarily abdicating power, including when it is obligatory and when it is disputed.

Core Idea
An imam must abdicate if he has a deficiency, but voluntary abdication without cause is disputed.

After discussing removal, Juwayni turns to whether an imam may voluntarily step down. He clarifies that if the imam finds a deficiency in himself affecting leadership, he must abdicate. However, if no deficiency exists, scholars disagreed on whether he may abdicate. This passage shows the diversity of opinion on this matter.

Guided Path
1
Read the second part of the passage
Focus on the conditions and the two scenarios: with deficiency and without deficiency.
Notice the word 'يجب' indicates obligation.
2
Identify the two scenarios
First, when the imam finds a deficiency – he must abdicate. Second, when he finds no deficiency – the ruling is disputed.
3
Notice the two opinions on voluntary abdication
Some say it is not valid and the imam remains; others say it is valid.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الله تتفسح أمانته ويخلع بالفسق الظاهر المعلوم . وقال آخرون : لا ينخلع الا بالكفر أو بترك اقامة الصلاة أو الترك الى دعائها أو شيء من الشريعة : ويجب عليه أن يخلع نفسه اذا وجد فى نفسه نقصا يؤثر فى الامامة. فأما اذا لم يجد نقصا . فهل له أن يعزل نفسه ويعقد لغيره ؟ اختلف الناس فيه : فمنهم من قال ليس له أن يفعل ذلك وان فعل لم تتخلع امامته ومنهم من قال : له أن يفعل ذلك .
If his trustworthiness becomes corrupted [؟], he is removed due to apparent known transgression. Others said: he is not removed except by disbelief or by abandoning the establishment of prayer or abandoning calling to it or something from the Shariah. And he must remove himself if he finds in himself a deficiency that affects the imamate. As for if he finds no deficiency, does he have the right to remove himself and appoint someone else? People differed about that: among them are those who said he does not have the right to do that, and if he did, his imamate is not removed; and among them are those who said he has the right to do that.
Source pages p. 129

This passage discusses the grounds for removal of an imam and whether he may voluntarily abdicate. First, it presents two views on what causes removal: one view holds that the imam is removed if his trustworthiness becomes corrupted and he commits evident transgression; another view restricts removal to apostasy (kufr) or abandoning the prayer or something of the Sharia. Then the author addresses the situation where the imam himself perceives a deficiency in himself that affects his leadership – he must then remove himself. However, if he perceives no deficiency, the question arises whether he may voluntarily abdicate and appoint someone else. Note that the author does not give his own preference here, merely reports the difference.

Key Terms

العزل
الامامة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: An imam can always resign whenever he wants.
Correction: The passage shows that abdication is obligatory only if there is a deficiency, and without deficiency, it is disputed.

Checkpoint
What is the ruling if an imam finds a deficiency in himself that affects the imamate?
A) He may abdicate if he wishes
B) He must abdicate
C) He cannot abdicate
D) He should consult others

Key Takeaway
An imam must abdicate if he has a deficiency, but voluntary resignation without deficiency remains a point of scholarly disagreement.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?
When you voluntarily walk away from a responsibility without a clear reason, are you doing so to serve others or to avoid discomfort? How does the disagreement in this passage challenge your assumptions about leadership and duty?
?
If you were an imam or leader and felt no deficiency, but others believed you were inadequate, would your conscience allow you to remain? This passage forces you to consider: whose judgment matters more—your own or the community's?

The Obligation of Bay'ah and Its Enforcement
Juwayni explains the obligation of bay'ah and enforcement against refusal.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the obligation of bay'ah once the imam is appointed and the consequences of refusal.

Core Idea
Bay'ah is a binding duty that preserves Muslim unity.

Juwayni establishes the communal duty of allegiance after the imamate is validly contracted. He explains that refusal without excuse disrupts unity and must be compelled. This passage grounds the political obligation in revelation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the conditional structure and the two categories of refusal.
The passage begins with a condition for the obligation.
2
Identify the conditions
Juwayni lists conditions for bay'ah obligation: agreement of ahl al-hall wa al-'aqd or one person. Note that refusal requires excuse.
3
Understand the enforcement
Why is compulsion necessary? To prevent division among Muslims. Reflect on the phrase 'تفترق كلمة المسلمين'.
Unity is the goal.
4
Connect to the broader context
The obligation is based on establishing the Book and Sunnah. Consider how this links to the imam's role.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واذا انعقدت الامامة باتفاق أهل الحل والعقد أو بواحد على ما تقدم وجب على الناس كافة مبايعته على السمع والطاعة واقامة كتاب الله وسنة رسوله صلى الله عليه وسلم . ومن تأبى عن البيعة لعذر عذر . ومن تأبى لغير عذر جبر وقهر ، تفترق كلمة المسلمين . واذا بويع الخليفتين ، فالخليفة هو الأول ويقتل الآخر . واختلف فى قتله هل هو محسوس أو معنى . أى عزله قتل له وموت : والأول وهو قتله المحسوس هو الحق لقول رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم ( اذا بويع لخليفتين ، فاقتلوا الآخر منهما ) لكن ان تباعدت الأقطار وتباينت كالأندلس وخراسان جاز ذلك
And when the imamate is concluded by agreement of the people of loosening and binding or by one as previously stated, it becomes obligatory upon all the people to pledge allegiance to him on hearing and obedience and establishing the Book of Allah and the Sunnah of His Messenger, may Allah bless him and grant him peace. And whoever refuses the pledge of allegiance due to an excuse is excused. And whoever refuses without an excuse is compelled and forced, lest the word of the Muslims become divided. And if two caliphs are given allegiance, the caliph is the first, and the other is killed. And they differed concerning his killing: whether it is physical or metaphorical, i.e., his removal is a killing and death? And the first, which is his physical killing, is the truth, based on the saying of the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace: 'If two caliphs are given allegiance, kill the latter of them.' However, if the regions are distant and distinct, like al-Andalus and Khurasan, that is permissible.
Source pages p. 129

Commentary
This passage covers several points regarding the implementation of the imamate. First, once the imamate is validly concluded, all Muslims must give bay'ah, promising to hear and obey and uphold the Book and Sunnah. Those who refuse without a valid excuse are to be compelled, to prevent disunity. Then the passage addresses the scenario where two individuals receive bay'ah as caliphs. The first who received bay'ah is the legitimate caliph, and the other must be killed. There is a difference of opinion whether 'killing' is literal (physical execution) or figurative (removal from office is like death). Juwayni holds that physical killing is correct, citing the hadith. However, an exception is made: if the two regions are far apart, like al-Andalus and Khurasan, then it is permissible to have two separate imams, implying the second is not killed.

Key Terms
البيعة
السمع والطاعة
أهل الحل والعقد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Bay'ah is optional and can be refused freely.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies that refusal without excuse must be compelled to prevent division; it is an obligation.

Checkpoint
What is the consequence for refusing bay'ah without a valid excuse according to Juwayni?
A) They are excused
B) They are compelled and forced
C) They are ignored
D) They are killed

Key Takeaway
Bay'ah is a binding duty for all Muslims once the imam is validly appointed, and unjustified refusal is suppressed to maintain unity.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever justified disobeying a legitimate authority in your life because you didn't like their decision? What would it take for you to genuinely submit 'hearing and obeying' even when it's hard?
?
Examine your heart: do you prioritize the unity of Muslims over your own preferences? When you criticize a leader, are you seeking reform or merely excusing yourself from unity?
?
If bay'ah requires establishing the Book of Allah and the Sunnah, does your loyalty to a leader include holding them accountable to that standard? How do you balance obedience and commanding good?

Ruling When Two Imams Are Appointed
Juwayni rules on the double imamate scenario and its exception.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the ruling when two individuals receive bay'ah as imams and the exception for distant regions.

Core Idea
Only the first imam is legitimate; the second must be killed, except for faraway lands.

Juwayni addresses a potential crisis: two claimants to the imamate. He affirms the first is the caliph and the second must be killed, but notes a difference of interpretation whether killing is literal. He rules it is literal based on a hadith, but allows two imams when regions are far apart.

Guided Path
1
Read the ruling carefully
Read the Arabic and translation. Notice the conditional: if two caliphs are given allegiance, the first is the caliph and the other is killed.
Pay attention to the hadith quoted.
2
Understand the disagreement
Some scholars interpret 'killing' metaphorically as removal from office. Juwayni mentions this and then gives his preference.
3
Examine Juwayni's proof
He cites the hadith 'kill the latter' to support literal killing. Consider why he chooses the literal meaning.
The hadith is from the Prophet.
4
Note the exception
For distant regions like al-Andalus and Khurasan, two imams are permissible. This shows flexibility.
Exception prevents unnecessary conflict.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واذا انعقدت الامامة باتفاق أهل الحل والعقد أو بواحد على ما تقدم وجب على الناس كافة مبايعته على السمع والطاعة واقامة كتاب الله وسنة رسوله صلى الله عليه وسلم . ومن تأبى عن البيعة لعذر عذر . ومن تأبى لغير عذر جبر وقهر ، تفترق كلمة المسلمين . واذا بويع الخليفتين ، فالخليفة هو الأول ويقتل الآخر . واختلف فى قتله هل هو محسوس أو معنى . أى عزله قتل له وموت : والأول وهو قتله المحسوس هو الحق لقول رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم ( اذا بويع لخليفتين ، فاقتلوا الآخر منهما ) لكن ان تباعدت الأقطار وتباينت كالأندلس وخراسان جاز ذلك
And when the imamate is concluded by agreement of the people of loosening and binding or by one as previously stated, it becomes obligatory upon all the people to pledge allegiance to him on hearing and obedience and establishing the Book of Allah and the Sunnah of His Messenger, may Allah bless him and grant him peace. And whoever refuses the pledge of allegiance due to an excuse is excused. And whoever refuses without an excuse is compelled and forced, lest the word of the Muslims become divided. And if two caliphs are given allegiance, the caliph is the first, and the other is killed. And they differed concerning his killing: whether it is physical or metaphorical, i.e., his removal is a killing and death? And the first, which is his physical killing, is the truth, based on the saying of the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace: 'If two caliphs are given allegiance, kill the latter of them.' However, if the regions are distant and distinct, like al-Andalus and Khurasan, that is permissible.
Source pages p. 129

Commentary
This passage covers several points regarding the implementation of the imamate. First, once the imamate is validly concluded, all Muslims must give bay'ah, promising to hear and obey and uphold the Book and Sunnah. Those who refuse without a valid excuse are to be compelled, to prevent disunity. Then the passage addresses the scenario where two individuals receive bay'ah as caliphs. The first who received bay'ah is the legitimate caliph, and the other must be killed. There is a difference of opinion whether 'killing' is literal (physical execution) or figurative (removal from office is like death). Juwayni holds that physical killing is correct, citing the hadith. However, an exception is made: if the two regions are far apart, like al-Andalus and Khurasan, then it is permissible to have two separate imams, implying the second is not killed.

Key Terms
الخليفتان
القتل المحسوس
القتل المعنى

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Any second imam is always killed without exception.
Correction: Juwayni allows two imams in distant regions, showing the exception.

Checkpoint
According to the hadith cited by Juwayni, what should be done to the second person who receives bay'ah?
A) Depose him
B) Kill him
C) Obey him
D) Exile him

Key Takeaway
The first imam is legitimate and the second must be killed, unless regions are far apart allowing two imams.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you witness disputes over leadership among Muslims today, do you recognize the danger of splitting allegiance? How do you personally ensure your loyalty is to the rightful authority as defined by this lesson, not your own desires?
?
Juwayni allowed two imams in far-off lands. Are there divisions in your own life—between communities, groups, or even within your own heart—that you excuse as 'distant' when they are actually close? What would it take to realign yourself under one true leadership?
?
The command to 'kill' a second imam is severe. How seriously do you take the sin of causing disunity in the Muslim community? Have you ever, by word or action, contributed to splitting the jama'ah? What will you change starting today?

When to Support a Rebel against an Unjust Imam
Juwayni advises caution when a rebel rises against an unjust imam.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's cautious approach to supporting a rebellion against an unjust imam and the rationale behind it.

Core Idea
Power-seekers often feign righteousness, so verify before supporting a rebel.

Imam al-Juwayni addresses a delicate political situation: a rebel rises against an unjust ruler. While one might assume support is always justified, Juwayni advises caution. He provides two clear scenarios and a psychological insight into human nature. This lesson explores his reasoning, which balances justice with practical wisdom.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage and its literal English translation to grasp Juwayni's original wording and flow.
Notice the two conditional scenarios separated by 'فان'.
2
Distinguish the two scenarios
Identify the ruling for a just imam vs. an unjust imam. Understand why the response differs.
The first scenario demands immediate action; the second demands patience.
3
Understand the rationale
Juwayni gives a reason for caution: those seeking power pretend to be righteous. Reflect on this realism.
This is a practical insight, not a theological judgment on the rebel's intention.
4
Apply the lesson
Consider how this principle applies to modern contexts of political upheaval and leadership transitions.
The lesson encourages discernment over blind support.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ولو خرج خارجى على امام معروف العدالة وجب على الناس جهاده، فان كان الامام فاسقا ، والخارجى مظهر للعدل لم ينبغ للناس أن يسرعوا الى نصرة الخارجى حتى يتبين أمره فيما يظهر من العدل ، أو تنفق كلمة الجماعة على خلع الأول . وذلك أن كل من طلب مثل هذا الأمر أظهر من نفسه الصلاح حتى اذا تمكن رجع الى عادته من خلاف ما أظهر .
And if a rebel (kharaji) rises against an imam known for justice, then it is obligatory upon the people to fight him. But if the imam is a transgressor (fasiq) and the rebel outwardly manifests justice, it is not appropriate for the people to hasten to support the rebel until his matter becomes clear regarding what he manifests of justice, or until the consensus of the community agrees to remove the first [imam]. This is because everyone who seeks such a matter shows himself to be righteous until he gains power, then he returns to his habit contrary to what he manifested.
Source pages p. 129

Commentary
This passage addresses the case of a rebel (kharaji) who rises against the established imam. It presents two scenarios. First, if the imam is known for justice, the people must fight the rebel. Second, if the imam is unjust (fasiq) and the rebel outwardly appears just, the people should not rush to support the rebel. Instead, they should wait to see if the rebel truly embodies justice, or until there is general consensus to remove the first imam. The reasoning is pragmatic: anyone who seeks power will initially display righteousness, but once in power, he often reverts to his former bad habits. This caution prevents premature support of a potentially insincere rebel.

Key Terms
الخارجي
العدالة
الفسق
الخلع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that rebellion against an unjust imam is always obligatory or permissible without conditions.
Correction: Juwayni teaches that even against an unjust imam, one must not rush to support a rebel until his sincerity is verified. The danger of replacing one oppressor with another is real.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what should the people do if a rebel rises against an unjust imam and the rebel claims justice?
A) Immediately support the rebel
B) Wait until the rebel's true character is clear
C) Fight both the imam and the rebel
D) Appoint a new imam

Key Takeaway
Cautious discernment is required before supporting a rebel against an unjust imam, as power-seekers often feign righteousness.

Permissibility of Multiple Imams: Juwayni's Nuanced Stance
Juwayni presents his view and the Karramiyya view on multiple imams.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's nuanced position on multiple imams, including the consensus on a single territory and the permissibility in distant regions, as well as the contrasting view of the Karramiyya.

Core Idea
One imam per territory is consensus; distance allows flexibility.

This lesson examines Imam al-Juwayni's treatment of a key political question: can there be more than one imam at the same time? Juwayni affirms the Sunni consensus that multiple imams in one land are invalid, but he opens the door for separate imams in far-flung regions. He also reports the Karramiyya school's more permissive stance.

Guided Path
1
Grasp the universal consensus on a single land
Juwayni begins by stating that having two or three imams in one era and one land is unanimously forbidden. Understand this baseline.
2
Examine the Shafi'i hardline position
His companions prohibit multiple imams even at opposite ends of the world, likening it to a bigamous marriage. Note the strength of this analogy.
3
Learn Juwayni's own opinion
Juwayni distinguishes: same contiguous territory is forbidden; far distances leave room for consideration. This is his key nuance.
He says the matter is outside definitive proofs.
4
Contrast with the Karramiyya view
The Karramiyya permit two imams without restriction, arguing each manages his own region better and citing the analogy with multiple prophets.
5
Reflect on the implications
Consider how this debate reflects the balance between unity and practicality in Muslim political thought.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فأما اقامة امامين أو ثلاثة فى عصر واحد وبلد واحد فلا يجوز اجماعا، لما ذكرنا . قال الامام أبو المعالى : ( ذهب أصحابنا الى منع عقد الامامة لشخصين فى طرفى العالم ) ثم قالوا : لو اتفق عقد الامامة لشخصين نزل ذلك منزلة تزويج امرأة واحدة من زوجين من غير أن يشعر أحدهما بعقد الآخر . قال : والذي عندى فيه : أن عقد الامامة لشخصين في صقع واحد متضايق الخطط والمخاليف - الأطراف والنواحى - غير جائزة . وقد حصل الاجماع عليه . فأما اذا بعد المدى ، وتخلل بين الأمامين شسوع النوى فللاحتمال في ذلك مجال وهو خارج عن القواطع . وذهب الكرامية الى جواز نصب امامين من غير تفصيل . واذا كانا اثنين فى بلدين أو ناحيتين كان كل واحد منهما أقوم بما في يديه ، واضبط بما يليه . ولأنه لما جاز بعثة نبيين في عصر واحد ، ولم يؤد ذلك الى ابطال النبوة كانت الامامة أولى ، ولا يؤدى ذلك الى ابطال الإمامة .
As for the appointment of two or three imams in one era and one land, it is not permissible by consensus, for what we have mentioned. Imam Abu al-Ma'ali said: 'Our companions held the view of forbidding the contract of imamate for two persons at opposite ends of the world.' Then they said: 'If the contract of imamate happened to be for two persons, it would be like the marriage of one woman from two husbands without one knowing of the other's contract.' He said: 'My own opinion is that the contract of imamate for two persons in one territory with compressed districts and counties – the extremities and regions – is not permissible, and consensus has been reached on it. As for when the distance is far and between the two imams there is a great gap of distance, then there is room for consideration, and it is outside the definitive proofs.' The Karramiyya went to the permissibility of appointing two imams without detail. And if there are two in two countries or two regions, each one is more capable of managing what is in his hands and more precise in overseeing what is near him. And because when the sending of two prophets in one era was permissible, and that did not lead to nullification of prophethood, then the imamate is even more so, and that does not lead to nullification of the imamate.
Source pages p. 129

Commentary
This passage presents Juwayni's position on multiple imams, along with the contrasting view of the Karramiyya. First, he states that having two or three imams in the same era and land is unanimously prohibited. He then reports his Shafi'i companions' view that it is forbidden even at opposite ends of the world, comparing it to marrying the same woman to two husbands. However, Juwayni gives his own opinion: it is only impermissible if the two imams are in the same contiguous territory. If the distance is great, there is room for permissibility, and the matter is not definitively settled. Then the Karramiyya view is mentioned: they permit two imams without any restriction, arguing that in separate countries each imam manages his own region more effectively. They also draw an analogy with prophethood: just as two prophets can be sent simultaneously without nullifying prophethood, two imams can coexist without nullifying the imamate.

Key Terms
امامان
الكرامية
النبوة
الإمامة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Multiple imams are absolutely forbidden in all cases.
Correction: Juwayni permits two imams when territories are far apart, showing that the prohibition is not absolute.

Checkpoint
What is Juwayni's personal opinion on having two imams in the same contiguous territory?
A) Not permissible
B) Permissible
C) Permissible only with distance
D) Obligatory

Key Takeaway
Juwayni permits two imams only when territories are far apart, rejecting absolute prohibition and absolute permissibility.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Juwayni's allowance of multiple imams in distant lands challenges the idea of a single caliphate. In your heart, do you cling to a romanticized notion of Muslim unity that ignores practical realities? Are you willing to accept that diversity in leadership can be a mercy, even if it means giving up a cherished ideal? What would it take for you to surrender that ideal to Allah's wisdom?
?
The analogy of one woman married to two husbands shows the impossibility of two imams in one territory. When you see division among Muslims today, does it grieve you as an impossibility? Or have you become numb to it? Ask yourself: What is one concrete step you will take this week to foster unity in your own community, not just in your heart?
?
Juwayni says the matter of distant imams is 'outside the definitive proofs.' He leaves room for ijtihad. In your own life, do you hold some religious opinions as absolute when they might be open to flexibility? Can you identify a belief you treat as 'definitive' that might actually be 'outside the qawatiʿ'? Will you have the courage to re-examine it, even if it makes you uncomfortable?
