The Third Pillar: The Commanded Act Must Be Possible
Juwayni states the third pillar and its foundational principle.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the commanded act must be possible in itself, and how this principle underlies all divine obligations.

Core Idea
God only commands what is possible for the servant.

Juwayni continues his discussion of the three pillars of divine command by presenting the third: the commanded act must be possible in itself. He illustrates this with examples of the impossible—combining opposites or commanding disbelief—and then shows that all three pillars rest on a single foundation: the servant is accountable only for what is possible.

Guided Path
1
Identify the third pillar
Read the opening sentence: 'The third pillar: that the commanded thing be possible in itself, in existence and occurrence.' This is the core claim.
Note the Arabic term 'mumkin'.
2
Examine the examples
Juwayni gives three examples of what cannot be commanded: combining opposites, being in two places at once, and commanding disbelief. Understand why each is impossible according to the nature of command.
The third example is subtle: commanding disbelief would require knowledge of God and ignorance simultaneously.
3
Understand the foundational principle
The passage concludes that all three pillars derive from one principle: the servant is accountable for the possible, not the impossible. Ask yourself how this applies to the first two pillars as well.
4
Connect to the broader argument
Consider how this principle upholds divine justice and the intelligibility of revelation. God's commands are not arbitrary but rationally coherent.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الركن الثالث : أن يكون المأمور به ممكنا في نفسه وجودا ووقوعا فلا يجوز ورود التكليف بجمع الضدين والكون في مكانين في وقت واحد ، ويستحيل ورود الأمر والكفر بالله تبارك وتعالى لأن من ضرورة تصوير الأمر فهم المأمور الأمر وعلمه بالأمر ، وكيف يتصور مع العلم بالله ذى الأمر ، الجهل به ؟ فهو من قبيل جمع الضدين فقد خرجت هذه الأركان الثلاثة على أصل واحد هو قاعدة العقيدة ، أن العبد ، مطالب بالجائز دون المستحيل ، فانه مطالب بفعل أو اضراب عن فعل ، وكلاهما جائزان ، وكما لا يجرى على العبد من تقدير بارئه ، الا ما يجوز . فكذلك لا يطالبه الا بما يجوز
The third pillar: that the commanded thing be possible in itself, in existence and occurrence. So it is not permissible to issue obligation of combining two opposites or being in two places at one time. And it is impossible to issue the command and disbelief in Allah, Blessed and Exalted, because a necessary consequence of conceptualizing the command is the commanded one's understanding of the command and his knowledge of the command. How can ignorance of Him be conceived along with knowledge of Allah, the Lord of the command? That is a case of combining two opposites. So these three pillars are derived from one principle, which is the foundation of the creed: that the servant is accountable for the possible, not the impossible. For he is required to perform an act or refrain from an act, and both are possible. And just as nothing runs upon the servant from the decree of his Creator except what is possible, so too He does not require him except what is possible.
Source pages p. 55

Commentary
This passage defines the third pillar: the commanded act must be possible (mumkin) in itself, both in existence and occurrence. Therefore, it is not permissible for obligation to involve combining opposites (jami' al-diddayn) or being in two places at one time — both are inherently impossible. Furthermore, it is impossible for the command and disbelief in Allah to be issued together, because conceptualizing a command requires that the commanded person understands the command and knows the Commander. How can one know Allah, the Lord of the command, and simultaneously be ignorant of Him? That would be a combination of opposites. The author then concludes that these three pillars (the first, second, and third) rest on one foundational principle: the servant is accountable for the possible, not the impossible. He is required either to perform an act or to refrain from an act — both are possible. Just as nothing happens to the servant from the decree of his Creator except what is possible, so too does the Creator not require of him except what is possible.

Key Terms
ممكن (possible)
جمع الضدين (combination of opposites)
جائز (permissible/possible)
مستحيل (impossible)
قاعدة العقيدة (foundation of creed)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's absolute power means He can command anything, even logical impossibilities.
Correction: Juwayni argues that commanding the impossible is itself conceptually impossible because it violates the nature of command and knowledge. God's power does not extend to logical contradictions.

Checkpoint
What is the foundational principle underlying the three pillars?
A) God does not decree evil.
B) The servant is accountable only for the possible.
C) The servant has free will.
D) God rewards obedience necessarily.

Key Takeaway
God's commands are always possible for the servant, because accountability is based on possibility.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever felt that Allah commanded you with something too difficult? How does this lesson—that He only commands the possible—challenge you to reinterpret that struggle?
?
If Allah only commands what is possible, what impossible burdens have you been placing on yourself that He never asked you to carry? Will you release them today?
?
The servant is accountable only for the possible—including refraining from sin. Are there sins you have been making excuses for, claiming they are too hard to avoid? How does knowing that Allah only commands the possible confront your excuse, and what step will you take this week to prove it is within your capacity?

The Secret of Tawhid and the Rationalist Claim on Reward
Juwayni introduces a secret of tawhid and presents the rationalist view on deserving reward.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's presentation of the rationalist argument that reason alone dictates that gratitude deserves reward and ingratitude deserves punishment.

Core Idea
Reason alone cannot determine reward; it only leads to conflicting thoughts.

Juwayni begins by announcing a profound secret of tawhid, then presents the view of those who claim that reason obligates God to reward or punish based on human actions. He sets up the rationalist position for later critique.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once carefully, then read the English translation. Notice the tone of Juwayni's announcement and the logical steps of the rationalist argument.
The passage contains a strong rhetorical opening.
2
Identify the 'secret' Juwayni refers to
Juwayni says he will reveal a secret of tawhid. What is that secret? It is the rationalist position that the servant deserves recompense from God based on reason.
3
Outline the rationalist argument
List the steps: (1) intellect reflects, (2) it realizes God created and blessed him, (3) gratitude merits reward, ingratitude merits punishment, (4) when these thoughts conflict, reason chooses salvation.
This sets up the claim that reason obligates God to act.
4
Note the role of conflicting thoughts
The rationalists admit that two thoughts (gratitude versus ingratitude) conflict. Reason then impels the servant to choose salvation—this shows reason is not a sure guide.
This internal conflict is key to Juwayni's refutation.
5
Prepare for Juwayni's refutation
Recognize that Juwayni presents this view as a prelude to criticism. The rationalist claim is not yet endorsed; it is a straw man or a position to be refuted.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأنا الآن أبدى سرا من أسرار التوحيد . لو قوبل بكل ما يدخل في مقدور البشر وميسوره لما كان كفاء له . فأقول : ذهب الصائرون الى أن العبد يستحق على الرب تبارك وتعالى جزاء عمله الى أن سبيل درك الوجوب على العبد أن ينظر بعقله فيخطر له أنه يؤمن أن له ربا خلقه وبرأه وأسبغ عليه نعمه ، وهو ان شكره استحق الثواب وان أبى واستكبر وكفر استحق العقاب واذا تعارض الخاطران . وتقابلا استحثه العقل على سلوك مسالك النجاة ، والتوقى من المهلكات .
And now I will reveal a secret of the secrets of tawheed (monotheism). If it were to be compared with everything that enters human capability and facility, it would not be equal to it. I say: Those who hold [the opinion] have gone to the conclusion that the servant deserves from the Lord, exalted and majestic, recompense for his action, and that the way of perceiving obligation upon the servant is that he should reflect with his intellect and it occurs to him that he believes that he has a Lord who created him, originated him, and bestowed His favors upon him, and that if he thanks Him, he deserves reward; and if he refuses, becomes arrogant, and disbelieves, he deserves punishment. And when the two thoughts conflict and confront each other, reason impels him to follow the paths of salvation and to guard against destruction.
Source pages p. 57

Commentary
In this passage, Juwayni announces that he will reveal a profound secret of tawheed, something so valuable that it cannot be compared to any human attainment. He then presents the view of those who claim that the servant deserves recompense from God based on reason. According to them, the way to know obligation is for the servant to use his intellect: he realizes that God created him and granted blessings; if he shows gratitude, he merits reward; if he disbelieves, he merits punishment. When these two considerations (gratitude vs. ingratitude) arise, reason encourages him to choose the path of safety and avoid destruction. This sets up the rationalist position that Juwayni will refute in the subsequent discussion.

Key Terms
سر التوحيد
استحقاق
وجوب
خاطر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That rational obligation is self-evident and universally agreed upon; reason always points clearly to reward and punishment.
Correction: Juwayni shows that reason can lead to conflicting thoughts (gratitude vs. ingratitude), indicating that rational obligation is not straightforward.

Checkpoint
According to the rationalists presented by Juwayni, what does reason impel the servant to do when the two thoughts conflict?
A) To follow the paths of salvation
B) To disregard reason and follow desires
C) To seek worldly pleasure
D) To deny God's favors

Key Takeaway
Juwayni presents the rationalist claim that reason alone dictates reward and punishment, but he will refute it in the following discussion.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever relied solely on your own reasoning to decide what is right, without turning to revelation? What was the outcome? Could your reasoning lead you to salvation, or only to conflicting thoughts, as this passage suggests?
?
The rationalist view says that if you thank God, you deserve reward. Do you ever feel entitled to God's reward because of your good deeds? How does Juwayni's framing of this as a 'secret' beyond human capability challenge that feeling of entitlement?
?
Juwayni says the secret of tawhid is of such immense value that nothing human can match it. Do you approach tawhid as something to be intellectually mastered, or as a mystery that humbles you? What would change in your daily life if you lived with the awe of that secret?

Rational Judgments
intermediate
6 min
Why Revelation Alone Establishes Obligation
Juwayni refutes rational obligation with a counter-argument and concludes that revelation alone establishes obligation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Juwayni and the Ahl al-Haqq argue that revelation, not reason, establishes the obligation to obey God's commands.

Core Idea
Without revelation, there is no obligation — reason alone cannot make a duty.

This lesson presents Juwayni's decisive refutation of the rationalist claim that reason can independently determine moral obligation. Through a careful counter-argument, he shows that unaided reason leads to hesitation, not certainty. Only the threat of punishment in revelation creates genuine duty. Juwayni then concludes that God's command must be obeyed by virtue of His divinity, but ultimately the obligation is grounded in revealed warning.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the English translation. Pay attention to the flow of the argument.
2
Identify the two opposing views
Notice how Juwayni first states the position of Ahl al-Haqq (obligation only through revelation) and then presents a rational counter-argument against those who claim reason alone can obligate.
The rationalists mentioned here are likely the Mu'tazila.
3
Follow the counter-argument
Juwayni describes a rational consideration that would cause a servant to pause: obedience does not benefit God, and acting without permission risks punishment. See how this leads to hesitation, not clear duty.
4
Understand Juwayni's final clarification
Juwayni concludes that without revelation's threat, there is no obligation. He dismisses the superficial claim that God's divinity alone demands obedience.
This is the key point: moral obligation depends on revealed warning.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فقال أهل الحق : يجب امتثال أوامره تبارك وتعالى اذا وردت ، ولا ترشد العقول الى درك واجب على العبد . وقالوا فى معارضة هؤلاء : لئن كان يخطر للعبد ما ذكرتموه فقد يعارضه مسلك آخر هو لباب العقل ، وهو أن يجرى هو فى نفسه ومجارى حدسه أنه عبد مربوب وربه لا ينفعه عمل ولا يضره فعل ولا يزيده طاعة ولا تنقصه معصية وهو إن أكب على الشكر والطاعة أنهك بدن نفسه وأكده وقطعه عن ملاذه ثم لا ينفع ربه به ، بل يكون متصرفا فى نفسه بما ينقصها ، وهو من ملك خلقه وربما يتعرض بتصرفه فى نفسه من غير اذن المالك لعقاب المالك .. فهذا يتضمن أن يتوقف في العمل . وهذا قاطع من كلام الأئمة : ثم انتهى القول بسلف الأصحاب الى أن أمر الله تبارك وتعالى يجب امتثاله ، واذا ورد لعينه فانه تبارك وتعالى بعزته والهيته يستحق أن يمتثل أمره . وهذا موقف يجب على العاقل أن يتأنى فيه إن كانت همته تحمله على توقى التقليد ، والترقى الى ثلج اليقين فأنا أقول : لولا ورود الشرع بالوعيد على من ترك ما أمر به لما العبد وجوبا عليه ، ولا طائل تحت قول من يقول : ان الله مطاع فهم الأمر لالهيته ، وهو من الكلمات التي يرسلها من لا يغوص على مغاصات الحقائق وأمثالها ، ولا يصير على سير العقول
The people of truth said: Obeying His commands, exalted and majestic, is obligatory when they come, and intellects do not guide to the perception of any obligation upon the servant. And they said in opposition to those [rationalists]: If what you mentioned occurs to the servant, then another path may oppose it, which is the core of reason, namely that he runs in himself and his intuitive considerations that he is a servant, owned, and his Lord does not benefit from any deed nor is He harmed by any action; obedience does not increase Him nor does disobedience decrease Him. And if he persists in gratitude and obedience, he exhausts his own body, tires it, and cuts it off from its pleasures; yet he does not benefit his Lord thereby. Rather, he acts in his own soul in a way that diminishes it. And he is under the ownership of his Creator. Perhaps by acting in his own soul without the owner's permission, he exposes himself to the owner's punishment. This entails that he should pause in action. This is a decisive statement from the speech of the imams. Then the final word of the early companions reached the point that the command of God, exalted and majestic, must be obeyed, and when it comes concerning a specific matter, then He, exalted and majestic, by His might and divinity, deserves that His command be obeyed. And this is a stance in which the sensible person must reflect, if his aspiration drives him to avoid taqlid (blind following) and ascend to the coolness of certainty. So I say: Were it not for the coming of revelation with the threat against one who abandons what is commanded, there would be no obligation upon the servant. And there is no substance in the saying of one who says that God is obeyed because He is understood to have command due to His divinity. And it is among the words that are uttered by one who does not dive into the depths of realities and their likes, nor does he follow the course of intellects.
Source pages p. 58

Commentary
This passage presents the definitive position of Ahl al-Haqq and Juwayni's own concluding remarks. First, they affirm that obedience to God's commands is obligatory only when the commands are revealed; intellects on their own cannot discern any duty incumbent upon the servant. Then they offer a counter-argument against the rationalists: even if reason suggests gratitude deserves reward, another rational consideration arises—the servant realizes that his obedience does not benefit God at all, only harms himself, and that acting without the Owner's permission might invite punishment. This second thought would cause hesitation, not a clear obligation. Thus reason alone is inconclusive. Juwayni then reports that the early masters concluded that God's command must be obeyed because of His might and divinity—He deserves obedience by virtue of who He is. However, Juwayni adds his own important clarification: without the threat of punishment in revelation, there would be no obligation whatsoever. The claim that God is simply 'understood' to be obeyed because of His divinity is meaningless; it is a superficial statement made by those who do not penetrate the depths of truth or follow sound reasoning. The passage thus firmly grounds moral obligation in revelation, not unaided reason.

Key Terms
امتثال
عقل
وجوب
تقليد
الهية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That the Ahl al-Haqq completely deny any role for reason; they limit its ability to establish obligation independently of revelation.
Correction: Juwayni does not reject reason entirely; he argues that reason alone cannot establish obligation. Reason can lead to opposing considerations and uncertainty. Obligation requires the decisive factor of revealed warning.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what would be the case if revelation had not come with a threat?
A) Reason would still make obedience obligatory
B) There would be no obligation upon the servant
C) People would still be punished for disobedience
D) God's divinity would still demand obedience

Key Takeaway
Revelation is the sole source of moral obligation; reason alone cannot establish duty.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever felt a sense of moral duty about something without checking whether Allah has actually commanded it in revelation? How can you train yourself to pause and seek His command before acting?
?
If you truly believed that no action of yours benefits Allah at all, would it change the way you worship? Does your obedience rely on feeling that God 'needs' it, or on His command alone?
?
Juwayni says that without the threat of punishment in revelation, there would be no obligation. Does your obedience to Allah stem more from fear of His threat or from awe of His majesty? Which should it be, and how can you strengthen the latter?

Reason and Obligation: The View of Ahl al-Haqq
Juwayni presents the view of Ahl al-Haqq that reason does not independently know obligation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the Ahl al-Haqq hold that reason alone cannot know obligation and why revelation is necessary.

Core Idea
Reason gives conflicting signals; only revelation resolves them.

In this passage, Juwayni presents the core argument of the Ahl al-Haqq that the intellect, when left to itself, produces contradictory thoughts about God. This establishes that rational obligation is not known independently. The passage then shows how the Ahl al-Haqq refute the rationalist position by highlighting the need for revelation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation to grasp the flow of Juwayni's argument.
The passage contains two opposing thoughts that the intellect generates.
2
Identify the two conflicting thoughts
Find the two khatiran: one that leads to belief and expectation of reward, and another that leads to skepticism because God is not benefited by any action.
These are presented as contradictory intuitions of the same intellect.
3
Understand the Ahl al-Haqq's conclusion
Notice that the Ahl al-Haqq say intellects do not guide to any obligation; obligation is only known when revelation comes.
This is the central thesis of the passage.
4
Reflect on the decisive statement
The imams' statement emphasises that one should pause and not rely solely on reason. Think about why this is decisive.
This reinforces the need for revelation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الى أن العبد يستحق على الرب تبارك وتعالى جزاء عمله الى أن سبيل درك الوجوب على العبد أن ينظر بعقله فيخطر له أنه يؤمن أن له ربا خلقه وبرأه وأسبغ عليه نعمه ، وهو ان شكره استحق الثواب وان أبى واستكبر وكفر استحق العقاب واذا تعارض الخاطران . وتقابلا استحثه العقل على سلوك مسالك النجاة ، والتوقى من المهلكات . فقال أهل الحق : يجب امتثال أوامره تبارك وتعالى اذا وردت ، ولا ترشد العقول الى درك واجب على العبد . وقالوا فى معارضة هؤلاء : لئن كان يخطر للعبد ما ذكرتموه فقد يعارضه مسلك آخر هو لباب العقل ، وهو أن يجرى هو فى نفسه ومجارى حدسه أنه عبد مربوب وربه لا ينفعه عمل ولا يضره فعل ولا يزيده طاعة ولا تنقصه معصية وهو إن أكب على الشكر والطاعة أنهك بدن نفسه وأكده وقطعه عن ملاذه ثم لا ينفع ربه به ، بل يكون متصرفا فى نفسه بما ينقصها ، وهو من ملك خلقه وربما يتعرض بتصرفه فى نفسه من غير اذن المالك لعقاب المالك .. فهذا يتضمن أن يتوقف في العمل . وهذا قاطع من كلام الأئمة .
That the servant deserves from the Lord, blessed and exalted, reward for his work... until... and the way of perceiving obligation upon the servant is that he looks with his intellect and it occurs to him that he believes that he has a Lord who created him, formed him, and bestowed His favors upon him; and if he thanks Him, he deserves reward, and if he refuses, is arrogant, and disbelieves, he deserves punishment. And when the two thoughts are contradictory and oppose each other, the intellect urges him to follow paths of salvation and guard against destructive things. So the people of truth said: It is obligatory to obey His commands, blessed and exalted, when they come, and intellects do not guide to perceiving any obligation upon the servant. And they said in opposition to these: If what you mentioned occurs to the servant, another path which is the core of the intellect may oppose it: that he runs in himself and his intuition that he is a subjected servant, and his Lord is not benefited by any deed nor harmed by any action, nor does obedience increase Him nor disobedience decrease Him. And if he persists in gratitude and obedience, he exhausts his body, strains it, and cuts himself off from his pleasures, then he does not benefit his Lord by it; rather, he acts in himself in a way that diminishes it, while he is from the property of his Lord, and perhaps by his action in himself without the owner's permission he incurs the owner's punishment. So this entails that one pauses in action. And this is decisive from the words of the imams.
Source pages p. 58

Commentary
This passage presents the argument of the Ahl al-Haqq (the people of truth) regarding the basis of obligation (wujub). They argue that when a person uses his intellect, two conflicting thoughts may occur: one that leads to belief and expectation of reward, another that leads to skepticism because God is not benefited by obedience. Reason alone cannot determine which thought is correct. Therefore, the Ahl al-Haqq say that obligation to obey God is only known when revelation (shar') comes. Reason does not independently guide to any obligation upon the servant. The passage also includes a decisive statement from the imams supporting this view.

Key Terms
الخاطران
الوجوب
أهل الحق
العقل
الشرع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Reason alone can determine religious obligation towards God.
Correction: The passage shows that reason produces conflicting thoughts, so it cannot independently determine obligation; revelation is necessary to resolve the conflict.

Checkpoint
What is the position of Ahl al-Haqq according to Juwayni regarding obligation?
A) Reason alone determines obligation.
B) Obligation is known only through revelation.
C) Both reason and revelation independently impose obligation.
D) There is no obligation.

Key Takeaway
The Ahl al-Haqq maintain that intellectual reasoning alone cannot establish religious obligation; revelation is necessary to resolve conflicting rational possibilities.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever felt the tension between your intellect pushing you to worship and the inner whisper that questions why God would need your worship? How did you resolve that tension, and does this lesson change your approach?
?
If your obedience never added anything to God's perfection, would your motivation to worship remain? What does your answer reveal about what you truly rely on—reason or revelation?
?
This passage shows that reason alone can lead to a state of paralysis. In what areas of your religious life do you hesitate because you are relying on your own logic rather than on God's commands? What step will you take today to submit to revelation instead?

The Basis of Obligation is Revelation's Threat
Juwayni argues that without revelation's threat, no obligation on the servant would be established.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Juwayni argues that the servant's obligation is grounded in revelation's threat, not merely in God's divinity.

Core Idea
Obligation is not automatic; it requires the threat of punishment.

Juwayni refines a common theological position. He affirms that God's command must be obeyed, but then argues that without the threat of punishment conveyed by revelation, the servant would not perceive any obligation. He criticizes those who think God's divinity alone suffices to impose obligation, calling such a view shallow. This lesson unpacks his reasoning and the secret of servitude.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the flow: affirmation, condition, criticism, mechanism, and secret.
The key phrase is 'لولا ورود الشرع بالوعيد'.
2
Identify the key claims
Find where Juwayni states that without the threat, there would be no obligation. Then find his criticism of those who say God is obeyed due to divinity.
3
Reflect on the secret of servitude
Juwayni says the servant cannot be free from seeking interest (hazz). Understand why the threat is necessary for the servant's intellect to recognize obligation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وانتهى القول بسلف الأصحاب الى أن أمر الله تبارك وتعالى يجب امتثاله ، واذا ورد لعينه فانه تبارك وتعالى بعزته والهيته يستحق أن يمتثل أمره . وهذا موقف يجب على العاقل أن يتأنى فيه إن كانت همته تحمله على توقى التقليد ، والترقى الى ثلج اليقين فأنا أقول : لولا ورود الشرع بالوعيد على من ترك ما أمر به لما العبد وجوبا عليه ، ولا طائل تحت قول من يقول : ان الله مطاع فهم الأمر لالهيته ، وهو من الكلمات التي يرسلها من لا يغوص على مغاصات الحقائق وأمثالها ، ولا يصير على سير العقول نعم اذا استشعر العبد وعيدا حمله عقله على معرفة وجوب مالو تركه الأوفى على ما لا طاقة له به . ومن أسرار العبودية - وهو معقود الفصل ومقصوده - أنه كما يستحيل على الله تبارك وتعالى الأغراض والضر والنفع والحظوظ وتفاوت الأفعال يستحيل خروج العبد عن طلب الحظوظ ومسالك التكاليف فلو لم يثبت حظ العبد في تنكب العقاب لما تقرر فى حقه الواجب .
And the statement of the predecessors of the companions ended with the fact that the command of God, blessed and exalted, must be obeyed, and when it comes concerning a specific matter, then He, blessed and exalted, by His might and divinity, deserves that His command be obeyed. And this is a stance that requires the rational person to be deliberate if his ambition drives him to avoid imitation and ascend to the coolness of certainty. So I say: If revelation had not come with a threat (wa'id) against one who abandons what is commanded, there would be no obligation upon the servant, and there is no substance under the saying of one who says: 'God is obeyed just because the command is due to His divinity,' and it is among the words that are uttered by one who does not dive into the depths of realities and their likes, nor can he endure the course of intellects. Yes, when the servant perceives a threat, his intellect carries him to know the obligation of what, if he abandons it, would bring him to what he has no capacity to bear. And among the secrets of servitude — and it is the knot of the chapter and its purpose — is that just as it is impossible for God, blessed and exalted, to have aims, harm, benefit, interests, and differentiation of actions, it is impossible for the servant to exit from seeking interests and the paths of duties. So if the servant's interest in avoiding punishment were not established, the obligation in his right would not be established.
Source pages p. 58

Commentary
This passage refines the position. Juwayni states that the command of God must be obeyed when it comes, but then clarifies that the basis for the servant's obligation is the revelation of the threat (wa'id). Without the threat of punishment, reason would not perceive any obligation. He criticizes those who say God is obeyed simply because of His divinity, calling it shallow. The secret of servitude is that just as God is free from aims and benefits, the servant cannot be free from seeking his own interest (hazz), which is avoiding punishment. Thus, the threat is necessary for the servant's obligation.

Key Terms
الوعيد
الوجوب
الحظ
الشرع
العبودية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's command alone, by virtue of His divinity, imposes obligation on the servant without any need for revelation's threat.
Correction: Juwayni argues that without revelation's threat of punishment, the servant would not perceive any obligation; the threat is necessary for the servant's reason to recognize duty.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, why does the servant perceive obligation?
A) Reason alone.
B) God's divinity.
C) The threat of punishment in revelation.
D) Nature of servitude.

Key Takeaway
The servant's obligation is established only through the threat of punishment revealed in scripture, not through divine command alone.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you find yourself neglecting a command from God, is it because the threat of His punishment has faded from your awareness? How can you actively remind yourself of the wa'id each day to strengthen your obedience?
?
Juwayni says the servant cannot be free from seeking his own interest. In your worship, are you truly seeking God's pleasure, or are you mainly trying to avoid punishment? Which motive dominates your heart, and is that a problem or a reality to embrace?
?
If you believed there were absolutely no consequences for sin in this life or the next, would you still feel obligated to obey God? What does your answer reveal about the foundation of your faith?

Distinguishing Reason's Role from the Qadariyya
Juwayni answers an objection that his view resembles Qadariyya, distinguishing reason's role from theirs.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the key difference between Juwayni's view and that of the Qadariyya regarding the role of reason in obligation.

Core Idea
Reason does not obligate Allah; it only prompts after Allah's command.

Juwayni faces an accusation that his view aligns with the Qadariyya. He strongly denies this, explaining that while both mention reason, the Qadariyya claim reason imposes obligations on Allah, whereas Juwayni holds that reason only prompts after Allah's command and threat.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection and Juwayni's reply
Read the passage from 'فان قيل' to 'فان وعد الله حق، ووعيده صدق'. Notice the emphatic denial 'هيهات'.
The objector claims similarity with Qadariyya.
2
Identify the Qadariyya claim
Juwayni states: the Qadariyya claimed that intellects impose reward and punishment upon the Lord, and that they alone perceive obligations by reason.
3
Contrast with Juwayni's position
Juwayni affirms that nothing is obligatory upon Allah, and no obligation upon Him is perceived by reason. Instead, when Allah commands and threatens, reason prompts avoidance.
The basis is Allah's truthfulness.
4
Reflect on the distinction
Consider how this distinction preserves divine sovereignty while allowing reason a subordinate role.
This is a central issue in Islamic theology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فان قيل : فاذا عقلتم درك الوجوب باستشعار العقاب ، فقد ساويتم القدرية في عقدهم .. قلنا : هيهات : بينا وبينهم ما بين الثريا والثرى فانهم زعموا أن العقول توجب على الرب الثواب والعقاب . وأنهم ينفردون بدرك الواجبات بعقولهم . ونحن قلنا : لا يجب على الله تبارك وتعالى شيء . ولا يدرك بالعقل وجوب عليه . ولكن اذا أراد الرب الزام عبيده شيئا . أمرهم وتوعدهم على ترك المأمور فتستحثهم عقولهم على اجتناب المحذور ، فان وعد الله حق ، ووعيده صدق ، وقد تتبعت العلوم العقلية فى كل فن جهدى فما وجدت طائفة من ذوى العقول حائدين بالكلية عن مسلك جلى ، من مسالك العقول . ولكنهم يبتدرون القاعدة . ثم قد بزلون عن التفاصيل وهذا كما أن افتقار المتغيرات الى مدير ، لما كان من كليات العقول لم ينكره أحد ولكنهم اختلفوا في صفة المدبر فسماه بعض العقلاء : الطبع . وبعضهم : العقل الكلى . الى خبط لا أشغل به قريحة مولانا ، ثم استبد الموفقون لمنهج الحق ومن طال نظره في المقليات تبين له: أن مثار خلاف العقلاء ايل الى التفاصيل ، دون الأضول . والغرض من هذا التشبيه . أن النفوس مجبولة على طلب المحبوب ، وتوقى المحذور ، ومصير القدرية الى ذلك غير مستنكر أصلا . ولكنهم لم يحسنوا تفصيله فزلواء ونحن جميعا بين اعتباره ، وبين تنزيه الرب سبحانه عن النفع والضر . كما جرى في معرض هو أوضح من فلق الصبح لفاهمه . واذا نجز القول في أحكام الربوبية وصفات العبودية، واستبان أن مدرك التكاليف ، موقوف على ورود الشرائع ، فقد حان الآن : أن نوضح أن مدرك الشرائع : التلقى من الرسل والأنبياء عليهم السلام ، وهو الباب الثالث من أبواب العقيدة والله الموفق
If it is said: 'When you underrival the perception of obligation by sensing punishment, you have equaled the Qadariyya in their belief.' We say: 'Far from it! Between us and them is what is between the Pleiades and the earth. For they claimed that intellects impose reward and punishment upon the Lord, and that they alone perceive obligations by their intellects. But we say: Nothing is obligatory upon Allah, Blessed and Most High, and no obligation upon Him is perceived by reason. Rather, when the Lord intends to oblige His servants to something, He commands them and threatens them for leaving the commanded, so their intellects urge them to avoid the forbidden. For Allah's promise is true and His threat is true. I have pursued the rational sciences in every field to the best of my effort, and I have not found any group of rational people completely deviating from a clear path of the paths of reason. Rather, they rush to the principle, then they excel in the details. This is like the fact that the need of changing things for a Director, since it is among the universal notions of reason, no one denied it, but they differed over the attribute of the Director. Some rationalists called it 'nature', others called it 'universal intellect', leading to confusion that I will not occupy the mind of our master with. Then the successful ones adhered to the path of truth. And whoever extends his reflection on universals will see that the cause of the rationalists' disagreement reduces to details, not principles. The purpose of this analogy is that souls are naturally inclined to seek the beloved and avoid the feared, and the stance of the Qadariyya towards that is not objectionable in itself, but they did not refine its details, so they slipped. And we all are between taking it into account and between declaring the Lord, Glorified, free from benefit and harm, as has been discussed in a context clearer than the dawn to its understander. Now that the discussion on the rulings of lordship and attributes of servitude is concluded, and it is clear that the source of obligations depends on the arrival of the laws, it is time to clarify that the source of the laws is reception from the messengers and prophets, upon them peace, and that is the third chapter of the creed. And Allah is the Grantor of success.'
Source pages p. 59

Commentary
This passage is an objection-and-answer followed by a methodological explanation and a transition to the next topic. The objector claims that the author's position—that obligation is perceived through sensing punishment—makes him like the Qadariyya, who held that reason independently imposes obligations on Allah. Juwayni strongly denies this: the Qadariyya believed that intellects obligate reward and punishment upon Allah and that they alone perceive obligations by reason. In contrast, Juwayni affirms that nothing is obligatory upon Allah and no obligation is known by reason directly. Rather, when Allah intends to oblige His servants, He issues commands and threats; then reason prompts the servant to avoid the threatened harm. The author then draws on his broad study of rational sciences, noting that all rational people agree on fundamental principles (like the need for a Director for changing things) but differ on details. The Qadariyya's error lies not in their starting point (that souls seek benefit and avoid harm) but in their mishandling of details, leading to the false conclusion that Allah is obligated. The author then transitions, stating that since the rulings of lordship and servitude have been covered and it is clear that the source of obligations depends on revelation, the next chapter will explain that the source of laws is reception from prophets.

Key Terms
القدرية
الوجوب
العقول

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think any use of reason in ethics leads to Qadariyya.
Correction: Juwayni distinguishes: Qadariyya make reason independent obligator of Allah; Juwayni makes reason subordinate to revelation.

Checkpoint
What is the key difference between Juwayni's view and that of the Qadariyya regarding obligation?
A) Both agree reason can impose obligations on Allah
B) Juwayni says reason imposes obligations on humans, Qadariyya on Allah
C) Qadariyya say reason obligates Allah; Juwayni says reason does not obligate Allah but only prompts after divine command
D) Juwayni denies the role of reason entirely

Key Takeaway
Juwayni affirms that reason does not obligate Allah; it only prompts after Allah's command and threat, distinguishing his view from the Qadariyya.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have just learned that reason does not obligate Allah. Is there an area of your life where you have been expecting God to reward you for your deeds, as if He owes you something? Write one specific expectation you need to release today.
?
Juwayni says reason prompts us after we hear Allah's threat. When you read the Qur'an or hear a warning, does your heart tremble and drive you to act, or do you let the warning pass without change? What is one warning from Allah that you have been ignoring?
?
The Qadariyya believed their intellect alone could tell God what to do. In what subtle way might you be relying on your own reasoning over divine command? Will you commit to submitting your judgment to Allah's words today?

Rational Judgments
intermediate
5 min
Agreement on Principles, Disagreement on Details
Juwayni explains that rational disagreements are over details, not principles, and uses this to analyze the Qadariyya's error.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's observation that rational disagreements stem from details, not universal principles, and how this applies to the Qadariyya.

Core Idea
All rational people agree on universal principles; their disputes are over details.

Juwayni broadens his argument by surveying rational sciences, noting that no rational group completely deviates from clear logical paths. They all agree on fundamental principles, such as that changing things need a Director, but differ on the nature of that Director. The Qadariyya's error is not in their starting point—that souls seek benefit and avoid harm—but in how they apply it to the divine.

Guided Path
1
Read Juwayni's survey of rational sciences
Read from 'وقد تتبعت' to 'دون الأصول'. Notice his claim that no rational group completely abandons clear reasoning.
2
Understand the universal principle
He gives the example: all changing things need a Director. This is a universal principle of reason, agreed upon by all.
They differ on the attribute of the Director.
3
Apply to the Qadariyya
Juwayni states the Qadariyya's starting point (seeking benefit, avoiding harm) is not objectionable; their error is in detailing that leads to imposing obligation on Allah.
The analogy explains that souls are naturally inclined to seek the beloved and avoid the feared.
4
Transition to next chapter
Juwayni concludes that the source of obligations depends on revelation, and the next chapter will discuss receiving laws from prophets.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فان قيل : فاذا عقلتم درك الوجوب باستشعار العقاب ، فقد ساويتم القدرية في عقدهم .. قلنا : هيهات : بينا وبينهم ما بين الثريا والثرى فانهم زعموا أن العقول توجب على الرب الثواب والعقاب . وأنهم ينفردون بدرك الواجبات بعقولهم . ونحن قلنا : لا يجب على الله تبارك وتعالى شيء . ولا يدرك بالعقل وجوب عليه . ولكن اذا أراد الرب الزام عبيده شيئا . أمرهم وتوعدهم على ترك المأمور فتستحثهم عقولهم على اجتناب المحذور ، فان وعد الله حق ، ووعيده صدق ، وقد تتبعت العلوم العقلية فى كل فن جهدى فما وجدت طائفة من ذوى العقول حائدين بالكلية عن مسلك جلى ، من مسالك العقول . ولكنهم يبتدرون القاعدة . ثم قد بزلون عن التفاصيل وهذا كما أن افتقار المتغيرات الى مدير ، لما كان من كليات العقول لم ينكره أحد ولكنهم اختلفوا في صفة المدبر فسماه بعض العقلاء : الطبع . وبعضهم : العقل الكلى . الى خبط لا أشغل به قريحة مولانا ، ثم استبد الموفقون لمنهج الحق ومن طال نظره في المقليات تبين له: أن مثار خلاف العقلاء ايل الى التفاصيل ، دون الأضول . والغرض من هذا التشبيه . أن النفوس مجبولة على طلب المحبوب ، وتوقى المحذور ، ومصير القدرية الى ذلك غير مستنكر أصلا . ولكنهم لم يحسنوا تفصيله فزلواء ونحن جميعا بين اعتباره ، وبين تنزيه الرب سبحانه عن النفع والضر . كما جرى في معرض هو أوضح من فلق الصبح لفاهمه . واذا نجز القول في أحكام الربوبية وصفات العبودية، واستبان أن مدرك التكاليف ، موقوف على ورود الشرائع ، فقد حان الآن : أن نوضح أن مدرك الشرائع : التلقى من الرسل والأنبياء عليهم السلام ، وهو الباب الثالث من أبواب العقيدة والله الموفق
If it is said: 'When you underrival the perception of obligation by sensing punishment, you have equaled the Qadariyya in their belief.' We say: 'Far from it! Between us and them is what is between the Pleiades and the earth. For they claimed that intellects impose reward and punishment upon the Lord, and that they alone perceive obligations by their intellects. But we say: Nothing is obligatory upon Allah, Blessed and Most High, and no obligation upon Him is perceived by reason. Rather, when the Lord intends to oblige His servants to something, He commands them and threatens them for leaving the commanded, so their intellects urge them to avoid the forbidden. For Allah's promise is true and His threat is true. I have pursued the rational sciences in every field to the best of my effort, and I have not found any group of rational people completely deviating from a clear path of the paths of reason. Rather, they rush to the principle, then they excel in the details. This is like the fact that the need of changing things for a Director, since it is among the universal notions of reason, no one denied it, but they differed over the attribute of the Director. Some rationalists called it 'nature', others called it 'universal intellect', leading to confusion that I will not occupy the mind of our master with. Then the successful ones adhered to the path of truth. And whoever extends his reflection on universals will see that the cause of the rationalists' disagreement reduces to details, not principles. The purpose of this analogy is that souls are naturally inclined to seek the beloved and avoid the feared, and the stance of the Qadariyya towards that is not objectionable in itself, but they did not refine its details, so they slipped. And we all are between taking it into account and between declaring the Lord, Glorified, free from benefit and harm, as has been discussed in a context clearer than the dawn to its understander. Now that the discussion on the rulings of lordship and attributes of servitude is concluded, and it is clear that the source of obligations depends on the arrival of the laws, it is time to clarify that the source of the laws is reception from the messengers and prophets, upon them peace, and that is the third chapter of the creed. And Allah is the Grantor of success.'
Source pages p. 59

Commentary
This passage is an objection-and-answer followed by a methodological explanation and a transition to the next topic. The objector claims that the author's position—that obligation is perceived through sensing punishment—makes him like the Qadariyya, who held that reason independently imposes obligations on Allah. Juwayni strongly denies this: the Qadariyya believed that intellects obligate reward and punishment upon Allah and that they alone perceive obligations by reason. In contrast, Juwayni affirms that nothing is obligatory upon Allah and no obligation is known by reason directly. Rather, when Allah intends to oblige His servants, He issues commands and threats; then reason prompts the servant to avoid the threatened harm. The author then draws on his broad study of rational sciences, noting that all rational people agree on fundamental principles (like the need for a Director for changing things) but differ on details. The Qadariyya's error lies not in their starting point (that souls seek benefit and avoid harm) but in their mishandling of details, leading to the false conclusion that Allah is obligated. The author then transitions, stating that since the rulings of lordship and servitude have been covered and it is clear that the source of obligations depends on revelation, the next chapter will explain that the source of laws is reception from prophets.

Key Terms
العقول
المدبر
التفاصيل
الأصول

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the cause of disagreement among rational people?
A) They reject universal principles
B) They differ on details, not principles
C) They all follow different paths of reason
D) They completely abandon clear reasoning

Key Takeaway
Juwayni shows that rational disagreements stem from details, not universal principles, and the Qadariyya's error is in their application, not their initial insight.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Consider a disagreement you've had with a fellow Muslim. Was it over a universal principle of faith, or over a detail? Are you willing to concede the principle while re-examining your own details?
?
The Qadariyya's mistake was in the details of an otherwise sound principle. Where in your own life have you held a correct intention but applied it in a way that strayed from revelation?
?
Juwayni notes that all rational people seek benefit and avoid harm. Is your pursuit of benefit guided by divine revelation or by your own flawed reasoning? What is one practical step you can take this week to ensure your 'details' align with prophetic guidance?

Allah's Existence
intermediate
8 min
Juwayni's Proof of the World's Temporality from Possibility
Juwayni defines the world and argues for its temporality via the judgment of possibility.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's argument that the world is temporally originated because it is possible and therefore requires an external effector.

Core Idea
The possible cannot be eternal; the world is possible, so it is not eternal.

Juwayni begins by defining the world as everything besides God, consisting of bodies and accidents. He then observes that every part of the world can be imagined differently—its shape, motion, position, etc. This shows that the world is not necessary but possible. Since the possible cannot be self-sufficient, it must have an effector. This lesson unpacks that logical chain.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition of the world
Carefully read Juwayni's definition: 'The world is every existent other than God, composed of bodies and accidents.' Note that this includes everything we observe and what is absent.
This definition sets the scope of the argument.
2
Observe the proof of possibility
Go through the examples: shapes can change, rest can become motion, planets can rearrange. The conclusion is that reason judges all these as possible (ja'iz).
Possibility means it could be otherwise without logical contradiction.
3
Understand why possibility negates eternalness
Juwayni argues: whatever is possible cannot be necessary, and only the necessary is eternal. Therefore, the world cannot be eternal.
Eternalness (qidam) implies necessity (wujub).
4
Grasp the conclusion: need for an effector
Since the world is possible, it needs an external cause to bring it into existence and determine its specific state among many possibilities. Only the self-necessary needs no cause.
This is the core of the proof for the existence of God.
5
Consider the objection raised
The passage ends with an objection: what if the world is eternal because an eternal necessitator specified it? Think about how you might answer this.
The answer is likely on the next page; this objection highlights the need for a determinant.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
القول فى حدث العالم : العالم : كل موجود سوى الله تعالى ، وهو أجسام محدودة متناهية المنقطعات ، وأعراض قائمة بها ، كألوانها ، وهيئاتها ، في تركيبها ، وسائر صفاتها ، وما شاهدنا منها واتصلت به حواسنا ، وما غاب منها عن مدرك حواسنا ، متساوية فى ثبوت حكم الجواز لها ، ولا شكل يعاين أو يفرض منا صغر أو كبر أو قرب أو بعد أو غاب أو شهد إلا والعقل قاض بأن تلك الأجسام المشكلة لا يستحيل فرض تشكلها على هيئة أخرى . وما سكن منها لم يحل العقل تحركه . وما تحرك منها لم يحل سكونه . وما صودف مرتفعا إلى منتهى سمك من الجو لم يبعد تقدير انخفاضه . وما استدار على النطاق لم يبعد فرض تدواره نائيا عن مجراه . وترتب الكواكب على أشكالها يجوز على خلاف هيئاتها وأحوالها . فيتضح بأدنى نظر استمرار مقتضى الجواز على جميعها . وما ثبت جوازه استحال الحكم بوجوبه . ولا ينساغ في عقل موفق اعتقاد قديم عن وفاق ، وهو مجوز غير ممتنع تقديره على خلاف ما هو عليه . فإذا لزم العالم حكم الجواز استحال القضاء بقده . وتقرر أنه مفتقر إلى مقتضى اقتضاه على ما هو عليه . وإنما يستغنى عن المؤثر ما قضى العقل بوجوبه فيستقل بوجوبه ولزومه عن مقتضى يقتضيه . فأما ما ثبت جوازه وتعاورت [تعارضت] فيه جهات الإمكان فمن المحال ثبوته اتفاقا على جهة منها من غير مقتضى . فإن قيل : بم تنكرون على من يزعم أن العالم بما فيه قديم لا مبتدأ لكونه ولا مفتتح لوجوده لاختصاصه بما هو عليه بمقتضى قديم
The statement on the temporality of the world: The world is every existent other than God Almighty, and it is bodies limited and finite in their extremities, and accidents subsisting in them, such as their colors, their forms, their compositions, and the rest of their attributes. What we have observed of them and what our senses have contacted, and what is absent from the perception of our senses, are equal in the establishment of the judgment of possibility (jawaz) for them. There is no form that is seen or imagined, whether small or large, near or far, absent or present, except that reason judges that those formed bodies are not impossible to imagine being formed in another shape. What is at rest among them, reason does not forbid its movement; what is moving among them, reason does not forbid its rest. What is found raised to the highest depth of the air, the estimation of its lowering is not far-fetched. What revolves on the orbital sphere, the supposition of its rotation away from its path is not far-fetched. The arrangement of the planets in their forms is permissible to be contrary to their shapes and states. Thus it becomes clear with the slightest reflection that the import of possibility (muqtada al-jawaz) continues over all of them. Whatever is established as possible, judgment of its necessity is impossible. It is not permissible in the mind of a successful person to believe in eternalness without specification, while it is possible and not impossible to estimate it contrary to what it is. So if the judgment of possibility is incumbent on the world, the judgment of its eternalness is impossible. And it is established that it is in need of an effector (muqtaḍin) that necessitated it as it is. Only that which reason judges as necessary is independent of an effector, being self-sufficient in its existence and necessity without a necessitator. As for what is established as possible and the aspects of possibility conflict in it, it is impossible for it to occur coincidentally on one of those aspects without a necessitator. If it is said: What do you find objectionable against one who claims that the world and all it contains is eternal, having no beginning of its being nor opening of its existence, due to its specification as it is by an eternal necessitator?
Source pages p. 15

Commentary
This passage presents Juwayni's argument for the temporality (hadath) of the world. He begins by defining the world as every existent other than God, comprising bodies and accidents. Then he argues that all parts of the world are subject to the judgment of possibility (jawaz): they can be imagined differently in shape, movement, rest, position, etc. This shows that possibility (i.e., not being necessary) is a constant feature of all worldly entities. If something is possible, it cannot be judged as necessary, and therefore cannot be eternal (qadim) because eternalness implies necessity. Hence the world must be originated and in need of an effector (muqtaḍin) that determines its existence in one particular state among many possibilities. Only what is necessary in itself is independent of an external cause. The passage concludes by raising an objection: what if someone claims the world is eternal because an eternal necessitator specified it? The answer is presumably continued on the next page. Juwayni's proof relies on the observation of contingency and the principle that the possible requires a determinant.

Key Terms
العالم
حدث العالم
حكم الجواز
المقتضي
القديم
الموفق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The argument proves the world had a temporal beginning (a moment it started to exist).
Correction: The argument proves the world is contingent and caused, not that it had a first moment of time. Temporality (hadath) here means being preceded by non-existence, which could be interpreted as a temporal beginning in later kalam.

Checkpoint
Why does Juwayni argue the world cannot be eternal?
A) Because it is composed of bodies and accidents
B) Because it is subject to possibility and not necessary
C) Because it was created by God
D) Because it changes over time

Key Takeaway
Juwayni proves the world is temporally originated by showing its parts are possible and therefore require a determining cause.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You are a possible being — you could have not existed, and you could be other than you are. Do you truly live as if you are dependent on God for every moment, or do you slip into acting as if your existence is necessary? What one habit would you change today to remind yourself that you are contingent?
?
Juwayni says that even the smallest details of the world could have been otherwise. Look at your own circumstances — your health, your wealth, your relationships. They are not necessary; they could be different. How deeply does this realization humble you? Before you sleep tonight, write down three specific gifts God has given you that you take for granted, and thank Him for determining them for you rather than something else.
?
The passage argues that the world cannot be eternal and must have an effector. If God is that effector, then He chose to bring you into existence and specify your entire life. Are you living in a way that acknowledges His will in every detail, or are you acting as if you are the one who determines your own path? Name one area where you resist His determination and commit to surrendering it this week.

Allah's Existence
beginner
4 min
Why a Necessary Cause Cannot Create the World
Juwayni argues that a necessary cause cannot specify, requiring a free agent.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why a necessary cause without choice (mujib) cannot account for the world's specific features, and why a free agent (mu'aththir mukhtar) is necessary.

Core Idea
A blind cause cannot choose; only a free will can specify.

Juwayni presents a crucial argument for the necessity of a freely acting Creator. He argues that if the world's cause were a blind necessitator, it would produce all similar effects equally, but we see specification; therefore, the cause must have will. This passage sets the foundation for understanding divine agency in Islamic theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Begin by reading the Arabic passage and its literal translation. Notice the structure: a premise, a division into two possibilities, a proof against the first, and a conclusion.
Juwayni uses a condensed style; take your time.
2
Identify the two options for the determinant
Juwayni divides the possible determinant of the world into two: a necessitator (mujib) without choice, or an effective free-chooser (mu'aththir mukhtar). Understand these terms.
The first option is the target of his refutation.
3
Understand the argument against a necessitator
Follow Juwayni's reasoning: a blind cause cannot specify one thing among its likes. He illustrates with the naturalist example of a medicine attracting yellow bile. Ask: why would a blind cause attract one part and not another similar part?
The example shows the absurdity of a blind cause producing specific effects.
4
Apply the same logic to the world's location
Juwayni extends the argument: the universe occupies a specific region of space, but all directions are similar. A blind necessitator would have no reason to place it here rather than there, so a free agent is required.
This is the key application.
5
Conclude with the necessity of a free agent
The passage ends by stating that the impossibility of a necessitator without choice is established, so the determinant must be a free agent with will.
This is the desired conclusion.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فنقول - والله المستعان وعليه التكلان - اذا بطل ثبوت الجائزات من غير مقتضى قسمنا الكلام وراء ذلك . وقلنا : مقتضى العالم لا يخلو اما أن يكون موجبا من غير ايثار واختيار ، وأما أن يكون مؤثرا مختارا فان كان موجبا من غير ايثار كان ذلك مستحيلا • فان الموجب الذى لا يؤثر يستحيل أن يقتضى شيئا دون مماثلة ، وهذا يتضح بأن نضرب فاسد مذهب الطبائعى مثلا . فنقول : اذا قال : من ينتحل القول بآثار الطبائع : ان دواء مخصوصا يجذب المرة الصفراء دون غيرها من الاخلاط يستحيل عنده أن يجذب جزءا من المرة من القطر ، ولا يجذب جزءا آخر فى مثل ) ذلك القطر ( بعد ذلك المجذوب مع ارتفاع المواقع ، واستواء الأحوال .. هذا محال تخيله . واذا تقرر ذلك قلنا : العالم بحملته قار في جو معلوم ، وتقديره واقعا في ذلك الخلاء يماثل تقديره في خلاء عن اليمين أو عن الشمال .. وهذا يقرب من مدارك البدائة ، واذا تماثلت الأحياز والجهات ، استحال اعتقاد موجب يخصص العالم بقطر تمثله سائر الأقطار ، فان الموجب لا يخصص شيئا من أمثاله ، والمؤثر المختار : هو الذى يتحيز بارادته ومشيئته مثلا من أمثال . فلاح بطلان المصير الى موجب قديم لا اختيار له .
We say—and God is the seeker of help and upon Him is reliance—when it is invalid that contingent things exist without a determinant, we divide the discussion beyond that. And we say: the determinant of the world cannot be but either a necessitator (mūjib) without preference and choice, or an effective free-chooser (muʾaththir mukhtār). If it is a necessitator without preference, that is impossible. For the necessitator that does not choose cannot necessitate something without likeness. This becomes clear by giving as an example the corrupt doctrine of the naturalists. We say: if one who advocates the doctrine of the effects of natures says that a particular medicine attracts yellow bile to the exclusion of other humors, it is impossible in his view that it attracts a part of the bile from a region and does not attract another part in a similar region after that part is attracted, with the locations being elevated and the conditions equal—this is inconceivable. And when that is established, we say: the universe with its entirety is positioned in a known space, and its being located in that void is similar to its being located in a void to the right or to the left. This is close to intuitive understanding. And when the spaces and directions are similar, it is impossible to believe in a necessitator that specifies the world in one region while other regions are similar to it; for the necessitator does not specify any of its likes. The effective free-chooser is the one that distinguishes by His will and volition, for example, one of the likes. Thus the conclusion to a pre-eternal necessitator without choice is shown to be false.
Source pages p. 17

Commentary
This passage is a proof demonstrating the impossibility of a necessary cause (mūjib) without choice for the existence of the world. Juwayni begins by stating that since contingent things cannot exist without a determinant (muqtaḍā), we must consider the nature of that determinant. It is either a necessitator that acts without preference (i.e., a blind cause) or an effective free agent (muʾaththir mukhtār). He then argues that a necessitator without preference cannot produce a specific effect among equally possible alternatives; it would necessitate all similar outcomes equally. To illustrate, he gives the example of a naturalist who claims a medicine attracts yellow bile specifically. If the cause were blind, it would attract all similar bile in all similar conditions, which is not observed; therefore, such a cause is impossible. Applying this to the world: the universe occupies a specific location in space (an imagined void), but all directions are equivalent. A blind necessitator would have no reason to place it in one region rather than another. Hence, the necessitator cannot specify; only a free agent can specify by His will. Thus, the notion of a pre-eternal necessitator without choice is refuted.

Key Terms
مقتضى
موجب
مؤثر مختار
جواز
طبائع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: A necessary cause (mujib) without choice can still produce a variety of effects and specify one outcome among equals.
Correction: Juwayni demonstrates that a blind cause would necessitate all similar outcomes equally; it cannot specify one without a principle of selection, which requires will and choice.

Checkpoint
Why does Juwayni reject the possibility of a 'mujib' (necessitator) without choice?
A) Because a mujib cannot exist in reality.
B) Because a mujib would necessitate all similar outcomes equally, leaving no room for specification.
C) Because a mujib must be multiple.
D) Because a mujib is always material.

Key Takeaway
The world’s specific features cannot come from a blind cause; they require a Creator with will and choice.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If the universe's location in space is not due to blind necessity but to a deliberate choice of Allah, then your existence in this specific time, place, and circumstances is also a choice—not an accident. Do you live as if your life is a deliberate act of God's will, or as if it is just a random outcome of forces? What would change today if you truly believed that every detail of your existence was specifically chosen for you by a personal, willing Creator?
?
Juwayni exposes the absurdity of a blind cause that cannot specify. In your own life, when you attribute things to 'luck,' 'chance,' or 'fate' without seeing Allah's hand, are you not falling into the same error—imagining an impersonal force rather than a God who chooses? What is one area where you need to repent of thinking like a naturalist and instead recognize the Free Chooser behind it?
?
The proof shows that only a free agent can bring about the specific world we see. If the world's Creator is indeed a free agent, then your relationship with Him cannot be mechanical—it must be personal and responsive. Are you relating to Allah as a mere 'cause' that produces effects, or as a Being who chooses to engage with you, who listens and answers? What would it look like to have a conversation with Him today, acknowledging His freedom to act?

Module 6 · Lesson 3

Allah's Existence
intermediate
10 min
Why an Eternal World Cannot Be an Act of Will
Juwayni refutes the claim that the world is eternal yet from a free agent and establishes its temporal origination.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the world cannot be both pre-eternal and an act of will, and how this leads to the proof of its temporal origination.

Core Idea
Will implies bringing something into being from non-being; an eternal thing cannot be willed.

This passage tackles a key objection against the temporal origination of the world: could it be eternal yet created by God's will? Juwayni shows that the very concept of will excludes the eternal. Understanding this clarifies why the world must have a beginning.

Guided Path
1
Understand the objection
Read the opening objection: some claim the world is pre-eternal (qadīm) yet produced by a free agent. Juwayni declares this absolutely false.
Notice the objection is given as a hypothetical 'if it is said'.
2
Grasp Juwayni's refutation
Juwayni explains that will (irāda) only applies to something that comes into being after not having been. An eternal thing never began, so it cannot be an object of will. Therefore, an eternal world cannot be an act of will.
Key insight: 'what is placed and specified, which was not and then came to be, is what is intended by will.'
3
See the conclusion
Having refuted both blind necessity and free agency for an eternal world, the only remaining possibility is that the world is temporal (ḥādith), brought about by the will of a free agent. Juwayni praises this argument as stronger than other proofs.
This argument is foundational for the proof of the world's temporal origination.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فان قيل : العالم قديم، وموجبه مؤثر مختار . قلنا : هذا باطل قطعا .. فان القديم يستحيل أن يكون ثبوته بارادة ، اذ الموقع المخصص الذي لم يكن فكان . هو المراد . فأما ما لم يزل واقعا ، فيستحیل ارتباط كونه بارادة في الايقاع. ا، ١٧ وعلى الجملة : الواقع بالارادة فعل يؤثره المريد فيوقعه على حسب ارادته وما كان ثابتا أزلا فليس فعلا ، حتى يقال : وقع بالارادة على هذا الوجه . فاذا فسد القول بقدم العالم مع ظهور الجواز في أحكامه ، من غير موجب ومؤثر . بطل كونه قديما عن موجب قديم ، واستحال استناده قدمه الى ارادة . لم يبق الا القطع بأن العالم فعل موقع على وجه من وجوه الجواز ، بارادة مؤثر مختار أوقعه على مقتضى مشيئته .. وهذا الفصل فى اثبات حدث العالم أنجح وأوقع ، من طرق خوتها هو خير مجلدات ، وهو الفاهمه من الدنيا بحذافيرها ، لو ساوقه التوفيق .
If it is said: the world is pre-eternal (qadīm), and its necessitator is an effective free-chooser. We say: this is absolutely false. For the pre-eternal cannot have its existence by will, because that which is placed and specified, which was not and then came to be, is what is intended by will. As for that which has never ceased to be, it is impossible for its existence to be connected to will in bringing about. In summary: that which occurs by will is an act that the willing agent effects and brings about according to His will, and what is eternally established is not an act, such that it could be said to occur by will in this manner. So when the claim of the pre-eternity of the world is corrupted, given the appearance of contingency in its properties, without a necessitator and an effective agent, it is invalid that it be pre-eternal from a pre-eternal necessitator, and impossible to attribute its pre-eternity to will. Nothing remains but the certainty that the world is an act brought about in one of the modes of possibility, by the will of an effective free-chooser who brought it about according to His volition. And this chapter concerning proving the temporal origination (ḥudūth) of the world is more effective and more cogent than methods whose emptiness is... it is the best of volumes, and it is the one that comprehends the whole world, if success accompanies it.
Source pages p. 17

Commentary
This passage is an objection-and-answer followed by a conclusion. The objection claims that the world could be pre-eternal (qadīm) yet produced by a free agent. Juwayni refutes this categorically. He explains that the concept of will (irāda) applies only to something that is brought into existence after non-existence. A pre-eternal being that never began cannot be the object of will, because will implies bringing about something that was not. Therefore, if the world were eternal, it could not be an act of will. Thus, the hypothesis of an eternal world from a free will is self-contradictory. Having already shown that the world cannot be from a blind necessary cause, and now showing that it cannot be from a free agent if eternal, the only remaining position is that the world is temporal (ḥādith), brought into existence by the will of a free agent. Juwayni then praises this argument as superior to other proofs for the temporal origination of the world.

Key Terms
قديم
حادث
إرادة
فعل
مريد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's will could apply to something that has always existed.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies that will necessarily implies bringing about something that was not; the eternal is never an object of will.

Checkpoint
Why does Juwayni reject the idea that the world could be pre-eternal yet created by God's will?
A) Because will only applies to things that come into existence after non-existence.
B) Because God's will is not eternal.
C) Because the world cannot be both eternal and contingent.
D) Because an eternal object cannot have a cause.

Key Takeaway
The world must be temporal because will only applies to what begins to exist; an eternal world cannot be an act of will.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Juwayni says that will brings about something 'which was not and then came to be'. Consider something in your life that you know came to be by God's will — your existence, a blessing, a trial. If it is willed, it must have been new, a gift from non-being. Does that realisation fill you with humility and gratitude, or do you take it for granted? Today, consciously acknowledge one thing as a direct act of God's will, not a mere coincidence.
?
The argument concludes that the world cannot be eternal; it is temporal, brought into being by a free agent. If the world were eternal, nothing would be genuinely new, and your choices would be meaningless. Since it is temporal, every moment is an act of divine creativity. How does this affect the way you live this day? Will you live it as if God is constantly creating it anew, or as if it is routine?
?
Juwayni calls the claim that the world is pre-eternal from a free agent 'absolutely false'. Yet many people live as if the world is self-sufficient, forgetting that it depends on God's will every moment. Is there an area of your life where you act as if something is 'always there' — your health, your faith, your relationships — without acknowledging that it continues by God's will? What will you do today to remember that it is willed into existence?

6 min
Proof of the World's Createdness from Will and Contingency
Juwayni argues for the world's createdness via will and contingency.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's argument that the world's contingency proves it must be created by a willing agent, not an eternal cause.

Core Idea
Contingency demands a willed act, not just an eternal cause.

Juwayni presents a powerful proof for the temporal creation of the world based on the concepts of will and contingency. He first clarifies what it means for something to be an act of will, then shows why the world's contingent states cannot be explained by an eternal necessitating cause. This argument establishes that the world must be the act of a free chooser.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice Juwayni's compressed style and the logical flow.
2
Understand the definition of act by will
Juwayni begins by defining 'act by will' as something brought about according to the agent's will. Contrast this with what is eternally fixed, which cannot be an act in this sense.
This sets the groundwork: only originated things can be acts of will.
3
See why eternity is invalid given contingency
The world shows contingency (jawāz) in its states, meaning it could be other than it is. If it were eternal from an eternal cause, it would be necessary, not contingent. This contradiction invalidates the claim of eternity.
Contingency is the key evidence against an eternal necessitating cause.
4
Grasp the necessity of a willing agent
Since the world cannot be eternal from an eternal cause, and its eternity cannot be attributed to will (since will pertains only to originated acts), the only conclusion is that the world is a contingent act produced by the will of an efficient agent who chooses freely.
Juwayni concludes that a willing agent (mukhtār) is the only possible explanation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وعلى الجملة : الواقع بالارادة فعل يؤثره المريد فيوقعه على حسب ارادته وما كان ثابتا أزلا فليس فعلا ، حتى يقال : وقع بالارادة على هذا الوجه . فاذا فسد القول بقدم العالم مع ظهور الجواز في أحكامه ، من غير موجب ومؤثر . بطل كونه قديما عن موجب قديم ، واستحال استناده قدمه الى ارادة . لم يبق الا القطع بأن العالم فعل موقع على وجه (۱) من وجوه الجواز ، بارادة مؤثر مختار أوقعه على مقتضى مشيئته .. وهذه الفصل فى اثبات حدث العالم أنجح وأوقع ، من طرق خوتها هو خير مجلدات ، وهو الفاهمه من الدنيا بحذافيرها ، لو ساوقه التوفيق .
In sum: what occurs by will is an act that the agent effects and brings about according to His will. What is eternally fixed is not an act, so that it could be said to occur by will in this manner. Therefore, if the statement of the world's eternity is invalid, given the appearance of contingency in its states, without a necessitating cause and an efficient cause, then its being eternal from an eternal necessitating cause becomes null, and its eternity being attributed to will becomes impossible. Nothing remains except the definitive judgment that the world is an act situated in one (1) of the modes of contingency, by the will of an efficient agent, a chooser, who brought it about according to His volition. And this chapter on establishing the temporal creation of the world is more successful and more effective than methods that lack it, even if it were better than many volumes, and it is the understanding of the entire world, if success were to accompany it.
Source pages p. 18

Commentary
This passage is a proof for the temporal creation of the world (ḥudūth al-ʿālam). Juwayni begins by defining what it means for something to occur by will: it is an act that the agent brings about according to His will. Anything eternally fixed (thābit azalan) cannot be an act in this sense. Then he argues: the world displays contingency (jawāz) in its states, meaning it could be otherwise. If it were eternal, it would require an eternal cause (mūjib qadīm) that makes it necessary. But contingency contradicts necessity. Hence, the world cannot be eternal from an eternal cause. Moreover, its eternity cannot be attributed to will, because will pertains to acts that are temporally originated. Therefore, the only remaining possibility is that the world is a contingent act brought into existence by the will of an efficient agent (muʾaththir) who is a chooser (mukhtār). Juwayni then praises this argument as superior to other methods for proving the world's creation.

Key Terms
إرادة (irāda)
جواز (jawāz)
قديم (qadīm)
مؤثر (muʾaththir)
مختار (mukhtār)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that if the world is caused, it could be caused by an eternal cause without will.
Correction: Juwayni argues that contingency proves the need for a willed choice, not just any cause. An eternal cause would make the world necessary, not contingent.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, why can the world not be eternal even if it had an eternal cause?
A) Because an eternal cause would make it necessary, not contingent
B) Because an eternal cause cannot produce a temporal effect
C) Because the world is made of atoms
D) Because will is impossible for an eternal being

Key Takeaway
The world's intrinsic contingency proves it is a willed act of a free agent, not the necessary effect of an eternal cause.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If the world is contingent, then your very existence depends on a deliberate choice of God every moment. Does that thought make you more mindful of Him in your daily life? How will you remind yourself of this dependency today?
?
Juwayni argues that willed acts are not eternally fixed but occur in time. Your own choices are temporal — how does that reflect the divine will in your actions? Have you ever considered that your free will is a shadow of God's will?
?
The proof shows that the world could have been otherwise. If God had willed differently, you might not exist. How does this realization affect your gratitude for your existence and your purpose?
