Allah's Existence
intermediate
6 min
Juwayni's Refutation of the Necessitator
Juwayni refutes the possibility of the agent being a necessitator through a disjunctive proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the agent of the world cannot be a necessitator (mujib) without choice, and how Juwayni refutes both the possibility of an eternal necessitator and a temporal necessitator.

Core Idea
The world's temporality forces its Maker to be a free chooser, not a blind necessitator.

In this passage, Juwayni builds on the previously established temporality of the world to argue that its agent must be a free chooser (mukhtar). He considers and refutes the alternative: that the agent is a necessitator (mujib) without preference. By analyzing both possibilities for such a necessitator—eternal or temporal—he shows each leads to an absurdity. This lesson walks through that refutation.

Guided Path
1
Understand the context
Recall that Juwayni has already proven the world is temporal (hadith) and depends on an agent. Now he investigates the nature of that agent.
2
Identify the two candidates
The agent must be either a necessitator without preference or a free chooser. Juwayni first argues the necessitator is impossible.
Notice the word 'موجب' (causative) vs 'مختار' (chooser).
3
Follow the refutation of the eternal necessitator
If the necessitator is eternal, its effect would also be eternal, but the world is temporal—a contradiction.
Juwayni says 'استحال تخصيص أثره بوقت دون وقت' – it's impossible to specify a time for its effect without a temporal cause.
4
Follow the refutation of the temporal necessitator
If the necessitator is temporal, it would need another agent, leading to infinite regress (tasalsul), which is impossible by first principles.
Juwayni mentions 'بداية العقول' (primary intellects) – a foundational rational intuition.
5
Conclude the refutation
Both options are impossible, so the agent cannot be a necessitator. It must be a free chooser.
This sets up the next lesson on the positive attributes of the Maker.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
باب فى الالهيات نصدر هذا الباب قبل تفصيله باثبات العلم بالصانع المختار ، فنقول : اذا ثبت حدث العالم ، ووجب افتقاره الى موقع يوقع على ما هو عليه ، واستحال وقوعه بنفسه لم يخل موقعه اما أن يكون موجبا لا ايثار له ، أو يكون مختار .. وباطل أن يكون موجبا لا ايثار له . قاله لا يخلو اما أن يكون قديما أو حادثا فان كان قديما وجب عدم موجبه وأثره واستحال تخصيص أثره بوقت دوان وقت . وقد اتضح مما سلف خذت العالم ، وإن كان موجبه حادثا افتقر هو الى موقع ويتسلسل القول فيه الى أعداد غير متناهية وهذا مستحيل ببدائة العقول ، وما يتسلسل لا يتحصل ، ومن أثبت حوادثا منفصلة لا نهاية لها الى غير أول ، فقد جمع بين الحدوث والحكم بالقدم ، ومن انتهى معتقده الى اثبات حوادث أزلية فقد انسل عن مقتضى العقول فإن مقتضى الحوادث الابتداء عن عدم ، والأزل يشعر بنفى الأولية ، فبطل أن يكون موقع العالم موجبا لا ايثار له .. ووجب القول مختار مريد ، أوقع العالم على موجب مشيئة ، ولاح بما قدمناه : وجوب قدمه . اذ لو كان صانع العالم حادثا لافتقر الى محدث ، افتقار العالم اليه ، ثم ينجر القول الى ما سبق وضوح استحالته . فاذا تمهد صدر الباب فالكلام بعده ينقسم ثلاثة أقسام . قسم : في ذكر ما يستحيل على الله سبحانه . وقسم: فيما يجب الله سبحانه . وقسم في ما يجوز فى أحكامه . فالت مدارك الألهيات الى الاستحالة والوجوب .. والجواز فيما سبق في صدر هذا
Chapter on the divine matters. We begin this chapter before its detailed exposition by establishing knowledge of the Maker who chooses. We say: Since the temporality of the world is established, and its dependence on an agent that brings it into existence as it is is necessary, and its occurrence by itself is impossible, its agent is not free from either being a necessitator without preference or a chooser. And it is false that it be a necessitator without preference. He said: It is not free from either being eternal or temporal. If it is eternal, then its effect would be necessary and the specification of its effect to a time without time is impossible. And it has become clear from what preceded [regarding] the temporality of the world. And if its necessitator is temporal, then it itself depends on an agent, and the discourse would regress infinitely, and this is impossible by the primary intellects. And what regresses does not obtain. And whoever posits separate temporal events without end without a first, has combined temporality with the judgment of eternity. And whoever's belief ends at positing eternal temporal events has deviated from the requirement of intellects, for the requirement of temporal events is beginning from non-existence, and eternity implies negation of priority. So it is false that the agent of the world be a necessitator without preference. And it is necessary to say a chooser and willer, who brought about the world according to His will. And it has become clear by what we have presented: the necessity of His eternity. For if the Maker of the world were temporal, He would depend on a maker, as the world depends on Him, then the discourse leads to what preceded of clear impossibility. So when the preamble of the chapter is established, the discourse afterwards divides into three sections: a section on what is impossible for God (subhanahu), a section on what is necessary for God (subhanahu), and a section on what is possible in His rulings. So the bases of the divine matters are impossibility and necessity and possibility as was mentioned at the beginning of this.
Source pages p. 20

Commentary
This passage presents a proof for the existence of a free and eternal Creator. It begins by stating that since the world is temporal (hadith), it requires an agent (mu'aththir) that brings it into existence. The agent must be either a necessitator (mujib) without choice or a free chooser (mukhtar). Juwayni argues that it cannot be a necessitator. He examines both possibilities: if the necessitator is eternal (qadim), then its effect would also be eternal, but the world is temporal, so that is impossible. If the necessitator is temporal, then it would itself need an agent, leading to an infinite regress (tasalsul), which is impossible by first principles. Therefore, the agent must be a free chooser and willer (mukhtar murid), and it must be eternal (qadim) because if it were temporal, it would require another maker, again leading to an infinite regress. After establishing this, Juwayni divides the discussion into three categories: what is impossible for God (istihala), what is necessary for God (wujub), and what is possible in His rulings (jawaz). These form the basis of all divine matters.

Key Terms
الموجب
المختار
القديم
الحادث
التسلسل
بداية العقول

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that positing an eternal necessitator (a god without will) is possible if the world is eternal, but Juwayni refutes that because the world is temporal.
Correction: Juwayni's proof relies on the established temporality of the world. The argument does not assume the world's beginning in time from scratch; it builds on prior proof.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, why cannot the agent of the world be an eternal necessitator?
A) Because an eternal necessitator would make the world eternal, but the world is temporal.
B) Because an eternal necessitator would lead to infinite regress.
C) Because an eternal necessitator cannot have will.
D) Because eternal things cannot cause temporal effects.

Key Takeaway
The agent of the world cannot be a necessitator without choice, because both eternal and temporal necessitators lead to absurdities.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When life feels chaotic and uncertain, do you tend to believe events are driven by blind necessity or by the deliberate choice of a wise Creator? How does your answer shape your trust in God's plan? This week, when a disappointment arises, pause and remind yourself: 'This comes from the One who freely chooses what is best for me.'
?
Juwayni says infinite regress is impossible 'by the primary intellects.' Yet many people live as if there is no First Cause—they never think about why anything exists. Have you ever used your intellect to firmly ground your belief in God? What stops you from doing so more often? Commit to reasoning through one argument for God's existence this week.
?
If God is a free chooser, then your relationship with Him is personal and responsive. In your prayer, do you speak to a distant force or to a loving Agent who can choose to answer? Challenge yourself today to pray as if every word reaches a free, attentive, and powerful Being who decides to respond to you.

The Agent is a Free Chooser and the Tripartite Division
Juwayni asserts the Maker's free will and eternity, then introduces the threefold classification.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the agent of the world must be a free chooser (mukhtar) and eternal, and how Islamic theology divides discussions about God into three categories: impossible, necessary, and possible.

Core Idea
The Maker is a free willing Agent, and all divine discourse rests on three rational judgments.

Having refuted the necessitator, Juwayni now establishes the positive attributes of the Maker: He is a free chooser (mukhtar) and willer (murid). He also proves the Maker's eternity (qidam). Finally, he outlines the tripartite classification of theological discourse into what is impossible for God, what is necessary for Him, and what is possible in His actions. This framework underlies all subsequent discussions of divine attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the positive conclusion
Juwayni states the agent must be a free chooser and willer who brought about the world according to His will.
The word 'مشيئة' (will) indicates purposeful action.
2
Understand the proof of eternity
If the Maker were temporal, He would need a maker, leading to infinite regress again. So He must be eternal.
This is a second indirect proof.
3
Learn the three categories
Juwayni divides the discussion into: what is impossible for God (istihala), what is necessary for Him (wujub), and what is possible (jawaz) in His rulings or actions.
These correspond to the three rational judgments in kalam.
4
Recognize the foundation
These three categories are the bases (madarik) of all divine matters.
This sets the structure for the entire chapter.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
باب فى الالهيات نصدر هذا الباب قبل تفصيله باثبات العلم بالصانع المختار ، فنقول : اذا ثبت حدث العالم ، ووجب افتقاره الى موقع يوقع على ما هو عليه ، واستحال وقوعه بنفسه لم يخل موقعه اما أن يكون موجبا لا ايثار له ، أو يكون مختار .. وباطل أن يكون موجبا لا ايثار له . قاله لا يخلو اما أن يكون قديما أو حادثا فان كان قديما وجب عدم موجبه وأثره واستحال تخصيص أثره بوقت دوان وقت . وقد اتضح مما سلف خذت العالم ، وإن كان موجبه حادثا افتقر هو الى موقع ويتسلسل القول فيه الى أعداد غير متناهية وهذا مستحيل ببدائة العقول ، وما يتسلسل لا يتحصل ، ومن أثبت حوادثا منفصلة لا نهاية لها الى غير أول ، فقد جمع بين الحدوث والحكم بالقدم ، ومن انتهى معتقده الى اثبات حوادث أزلية فقد انسل عن مقتضى العقول فإن مقتضى الحوادث الابتداء عن عدم ، والأزل يشعر بنفى الأولية ، فبطل أن يكون موقع العالم موجبا لا ايثار له .. ووجب القول مختار مريد ، أوقع العالم على موجب مشيئة ، ولاح بما قدمناه : وجوب قدمه . اذ لو كان صانع العالم حادثا لافتقر الى محدث ، افتقار العالم اليه ، ثم ينجر القول الى ما سبق وضوح استحالته . فاذا تمهد صدر الباب فالكلام بعده ينقسم ثلاثة أقسام . قسم : في ذكر ما يستحيل على الله سبحانه . وقسم: فيما يجب الله سبحانه . وقسم في ما يجوز فى أحكامه . فالت مدارك الألهيات الى الاستحالة والوجوب .. والجواز فيما سبق في صدر هذا
Chapter on the divine matters. We begin this chapter before its detailed exposition by establishing knowledge of the Maker who chooses. We say: Since the temporality of the world is established, and its dependence on an agent that brings it into existence as it is is necessary, and its occurrence by itself is impossible, its agent is not free from either being a necessitator without preference or a chooser. And it is false that it be a necessitator without preference. He said: It is not free from either being eternal or temporal. If it is eternal, then its effect would be necessary and the specification of its effect to a time without time is impossible. And it has become clear from what preceded [regarding] the temporality of the world. And if its necessitator is temporal, then it itself depends on an agent, and the discourse would regress infinitely, and this is impossible by the primary intellects. And what regresses does not obtain. And whoever posits separate temporal events without end without a first, has combined temporality with the judgment of eternity. And whoever's belief ends at positing eternal temporal events has deviated from the requirement of intellects, for the requirement of temporal events is beginning from non-existence, and eternity implies negation of priority. So it is false that the agent of the world be a necessitator without preference. And it is necessary to say a chooser and willer, who brought about the world according to His will. And it has become clear by what we have presented: the necessity of His eternity. For if the Maker of the world were temporal, He would depend on a maker, as the world depends on Him, then the discourse leads to what preceded of clear impossibility. So when the preamble of the chapter is established, the discourse afterwards divides into three sections: a section on what is impossible for God (subhanahu), a section on what is necessary for God (subhanahu), and a section on what is possible in His rulings. So the bases of the divine matters are impossibility and necessity and possibility as was mentioned at the beginning of this.

Commentary
This passage presents a proof for the existence of a free and eternal Creator. It begins by stating that since the world is temporal (hadith), it requires an agent (mu'aththir) that brings it into existence. The agent must be either a necessitator (mujib) without choice or a free chooser (mukhtar). Juwayni argues that it cannot be a necessitator. He examines both possibilities: if the necessitator is eternal (qadim), then its effect would also be eternal, but the world is temporal, so that is impossible. If the necessitator is temporal, then it would itself need an agent, leading to an infinite regress (tasalsul), which is impossible by first principles. Therefore, the agent must be a free chooser and willer (mukhtar murid), and it must be eternal (qadim) because if it were temporal, it would require another maker, again leading to an infinite regress. After establishing this, Juwayni divides the discussion into three categories: what is impossible for God (istihala), what is necessary for God (wujub), and what is possible in His rulings (jawaz). Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, why must the Maker of the world be eternal?
A) Because if He were temporal, He would need a maker, leading to infinite regress.
B) Because the world is temporal and required an eternal cause.
C) Because the Maker is a free chooser and free choosers are eternal.
D) Because the primary intellects dictate that the Maker is eternal.

Key Takeaway
The Maker is a free eternal chooser, and all theological discussions are built upon the three categories of impossibility, necessity, and possibility.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believed that every aspect of theology falls under impossibility, necessity, or possibility for God, how would that change the way you approach a doubt about God's actions in your life? Would you accept that some things are possible for Him even if you don't understand why He does them? What would you commit to trusting Him about today?
?
The proof that the Maker cannot be temporal leads to infinite regress. Reflect on a fear or anxiety that feels like an infinite regress in your heart—something that has no end in your mind. How does knowing that God is the eternal, free chooser who brought all things into existence by His will stop that regress? What step can you take today to stop that mental chain?
?
Juwayni emphasizes that the Maker is a free chooser (mukhtar). Are there areas in your life where you live as if God is forced to act in a certain way—like He must answer your prayer in a specific manner? How can you repent from assuming God is compelled, and instead worship Him as the truly free Agent?

Module 6 · Lesson 7

Allah's Existence
beginner
8 min
The Maker is a Free Agent and Eternal
Juwayni proves that the Maker is a free agent and eternal by eliminating necessitarianism and infinite regress.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the Maker of the world must be both a free agent and eternal, and how Juwayni argues against the alternatives.

Core Idea
The Maker of the world cannot be a blind cause—He must be a free and eternal agent.

Juwayni now builds on the temporality of the world to deduce two essential attributes of its Maker: that He is a free agent (mukhtār) who creates by choice, and that He is eternal (qadīm). He does this by eliminating the alternatives—if the Maker were a mere necessitator, impossible consequences follow. In this passage, he walks through both horns of a dilemma to establish these two truths.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and identify the two alternatives
The passage first presents the dichotomy: the positioner of the world is either a necessitator (mūjib) or a free agent (mukhtār). Read carefully how Juwayni rejects the first option.
Notice the structure of the argument: it's a proof by elimination.
2
Follow the argument against the necessitator being eternal
Juwayni argues that if the necessitator were eternal, its effect would also be eternal, contradicting the world's temporality. See how he uses the already-established fact that the world is temporal.
This shows the necessity of connecting attributes to logical consequences.
3
Follow the argument against the necessitator being originated
If the necessitator were originated, it would need another positioner, leading to an infinite regress (tasalsul), which is impossible. Understand why infinite regress is rejected.
Juwayni says this is impossible by 'the obviousness of intellects.'
4
Understand the conclusion that the Maker is a free agent
After eliminating both possibilities, Juwayni concludes that the Maker must be a free agent who positions the world by His will. Reflect on what this means for divine attributes.
5
See how the same reasoning establishes the Maker's eternity
Juwayni then argues that if the Maker were originated, He would need another maker, leading to the same regress. So He must be eternal. Connect this to the overall proof.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
نصدر هذا الباب قبل تفصيله باثبات العلم بالصانع المختار، فنقول: إذا ثبت حدث العالم، ووجب افتقاره إلى موقع يوقع على ما هو عليه، واستحال وقوعه بنفسه لم يخل موقعه إما أن يكون موجبا لا إيثار له، أو يكون مختارا. وباطل أن يكون موجبا لا إيثار له. قاله لا يخلو إما أن يكون قديما أو حادثا، فإن كان قديما وجب عدم موجبه وأثره واستحال تخصيص أثره بوقت دون وقت. وقد اتضح مما سلف حدث العالم، وإن كان موجبه حادثا افتقر هو إلى موقع ويتسلسل القول فيه إلى أعداد غير متناهية، وهذا مستحيل ببداهة العقول، وما يتسلسل لا يتحصل. ومن أثبت حوادث منفصلة لا نهاية لها إلى غير أول فقد جمع بين الحدوث والحكم بالقدم، ومن انتهى معتقده إلى إثبات حوادث أزلية فقد انسل عن مقتضى العقول، فإن مقتضى الحوادث الابتداء عن عدم، والأزل يشعر بنفي الأولية. فبطل أن يكون موقع العالم موجبا لا إيثار له، ووجب القول مختارا مريدا، أوقع العالم على موجب مشيئة، ولاح بما قدمناه وجوب قدمه. إذ لو كان صانع العالم حادثا لافتقر إلى محدث، افتقار العالم إليه، ثم ينجر القول إلى ما سبق وضوح استحالته. فإذا تمهد صدر الباب.
We open this chapter before its detail with establishing knowledge of the Maker of choice. So we say: If the temporality of the world is established, and its dependence on a positioner that positions it as it is is necessary, and its occurrence by itself is impossible, then its positioner is not free from either being a necessitator (mūjib) with no preference, or being a free agent (mukhtār). And it is false that it be a necessitator with no preference. This [necessitator] is not free from either being eternal (qadīm) or temporally originated (hādith). If it is eternal, then its necessitating and its effect would necessarily be absent [in eternity], and it would be impossible to specify its effect to a time without time. And it has become clear from what preceded the temporality of the world. And if its necessitator is originated, then it would depend on a positioner, and the discourse would regress to infinite numbers, and this is impossible by the obviousness of intellects, and what regresses does not obtain. And whoever posits separate originated things with no end and no first has combined temporality with the judgment of eternity, and whoever’s belief ends at affirming originated eternals has deviated from the requirement of intellects, for the requirement of originated things is beginning from non-existence, and eternity implies negation of priority. So it is false that the positioner of the world be a necessitator with no preference, and it is necessary to affirm [that He is] a free agent (mukhtār) and willer (murīd), who positioned the world according to His will. And it became evident by what we have presented the necessity of His eternity. For if the Maker of the world were originated, He would need a maker, just as the world needs Him, then the discourse would lead to what was previously shown to be impossible. So when the introduction is established.
Source pages p. 20

Commentary
This passage is a proof that the Maker of the world is a free agent (mukhtār) and not a necessitator (mūjib), and that He is eternal (qadīm). Juwayni begins by stating that once the temporality of the world is established, it must have a positioner. That positioner cannot be a mere necessitator with no preference, because if it were eternal, its effect would also be eternal, contradicting the world's temporality. If it were originated, it would need another positioner, leading to infinite regress, which is impossible. Therefore, the positioner must be a free agent who chooses to create the world in time. Furthermore, that free agent must be eternal, because if He were originated, He would need a maker, and the same argument would apply. Thus, the Maker is both a free agent and eternal.

Key Terms
مختار (mukhtār)
موجب (mūjib)
قديم (qadīm)
حادث (hādith)
تسلسل (tasalsul)
أزل (azal)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think that God could be a necessitator like natural causes, acting without will.
Correction: Juwayni shows that a necessitator would either produce an eternal effect (contradicting the world's temporality) or require infinite regress, so the Maker cannot be a necessitator but must be a free agent.

Checkpoint
Why does Juwayni reject that the Maker could be a necessitator (mūjib)?
A) Because a necessitator cannot create.
B) Because a necessitator would either make the world eternal or lead to infinite regress.
C) Because a necessitator is not capable of will.
D) Because a necessitator is originated.

Key Takeaway
Juwayni establishes that the Maker of the world must be a free agent and eternal by showing that any other option leads to logical contradictions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believed that every moment of your existence is sustained by God's free choice, how would it change the way you face uncertainty or hardship? Do you live as if God's will is irrelevant to your daily affairs? What would it mean for you to rely completely on His choice right now?
?
Juwayni shows that an infinite regress is impossible. Yet we often avoid tracing our own actions back to their ultimate source. In what area of your life are you acting as if you are self-sufficient or as if your existence has no origin? How does the reality that you were created by a free Agent affect your sense of accountability?
?
The proof concludes that the Maker must be eternal. If you internalised that God has no beginning and no end, how would it affect your anxiety about the future? Does your worry reveal a hidden belief that God is limited by time? What one step can you take today to align your life with the truth of God's eternity?

Refuting the Negators: The Spirit and the Blind Man
Juwayni describes the negators' fallacy and refutes them using the spirit and blind man examples.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the fallacy of the negators (Mu'atila) and how Juwayni refutes it using the examples of the spirit and the blind man.

Core Idea
Inability to grasp reality does not justify denying existence.

Imam al-Juwayni now turns to the second extreme: those who deny God's reality because they cannot fully comprehend Him. This group, the Mu'atila, mistake inconceivability for non-existence. To reveal their error, Juwayni uses two powerful examples: the human spirit and a blind man's knowledge of colors. Let's see how these examples dismantle their argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the original passage
Read the Arabic passage and its translation carefully. Notice Juwayni's concise style and the logical flow of his argument.
2
Identify the negators' argument
The Mu'atila think that what the mind cannot grasp does not exist. Write down this claim in your own words.
3
Understand Juwayni's counter-examples
See how the spirit example (we know it exists but cannot perceive its essence) and the blind man example (he knows colors by testimony) show that existence can be affirmed without perceptual or intellectual grasp.
Reflect on how these examples apply to the knowledge of God.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وذهبت طائفة الى التعطيل من حيث تقاعدت عقولهم عن درك حقيقة الاله ، فظنوا أن ما لا يحويه الفكر منتف ، ولو وفقوا العلموا أنه لا تبعد معرفة موجود(ه) العجز عن درك حقيقة مع والذي ضربناه من الروح مثلا يعارض به هؤلاء ، قليس الوجود (١) الروح خفاء وليس الى درك حقيقته سبيل ولا طريق الى جحد وجوده للعجز عن درك حقيقته . والأكمة يعلم بالتسامع والاستفاضة : الألوان ولا يدرك حقيقتها . فهذا سبب زيغ المعطلة ، وهم على مناقضة المشبهة .
And a group went to negation because their intellects fell short of grasping the reality of the Divine, so they thought that what the mind does not encompass is non-existent. But if they had been granted success, they would have known that it is not far off to know the existence of a being while being incapable of grasping its reality. And the example we have given of the spirit is used to argue against these. For the existence of the spirit is not hidden, but there is no way to grasp its reality nor any path to deny its existence due to inability to grasp its reality. And the blind person knows colors through hearsay and widespread report, but does not perceive their reality. This is the cause of the deviation of the negators, and they are in opposition to the anthropomorphists.
Source pages p. 22

Commentary
This passage describes the second erroneous group: the mu'atila (negators), who deny God's attributes or existence because they cannot fully comprehend Him. Their fallacy is equating inconceivability with non-existence. Juwayni refutes this by invoking the earlier example of the spirit: we are certain of the spirit's existence yet we cannot perceive its reality. Similarly, a blind person can know colors through testimony without perceiving their essence. Thus, inability to grasp reality does not justify denial. The mu'atila are the opposite extreme of the anthropomorphists.

Key Terms
التعطيل
المعطلة
التسامع
الاستفاضة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: If something cannot be perceived or conceived by the mind, it does not exist.
Correction: Existence of a being can be known even if its reality is beyond full comprehension, as shown by the spirit and the blind man's knowledge of colors.

Checkpoint
What does Juwayni use to refute the negators' claim that what cannot be perceived is non-existent?
A) The Quranic verse about the spirit
B) The argument from causality
C) The blind man's knowledge of colors
D) Both the spirit and the blind man's knowledge

Key Takeaway
The Mu'atila's error is to confuse inconceivability with non-existence; reality can be affirmed even when full comprehension is absent.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Is there an aspect of your faith that you have doubted or rejected simply because your mind couldn't fully grasp it? How does Juwayni's argument challenge you to trust in what you know exists even without complete understanding? What step will you take this week to strengthen that trust?
?
Reflect on a moment when your intellect struggled with a divine concept—perhaps the nature of Allah's attributes or the reality of the afterlife. Did you respond with humility and acceptance, or did you allow doubt to creep in? What would it look like for you to embrace the unknown with certainty instead of denial?
?
The blind man trusts the reports about colors without seeing them. In your own life, where are you relying on hearsay or cultural habit rather than genuine conviction? What can you do to transform that passive acceptance into active, certain faith that shapes your actions?

The Criterion of True Monotheism
Juwayni presents the methodology of Ahl al-Haqq and the criterion for true monotheism.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's threefold criterion for true monotheism: affirming God's existence while acknowledging inability to comprehend His essence, avoiding both anthropomorphism and pure negation.

Core Idea
True monotheism is certainty of God's existence combined with inability to grasp His reality.

Juwayni concludes his work by presenting the defining methodology of the people of truth (Ahl al-Haqq). He distills the essence of correct tawhid into a simple threefold test, grounded in the famous saying of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq. This passage encapsulates the entire purpose of kalam: affirming God's existence without reducing Him to a concept the intellect can fully comprehend.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and identify the three groups
The passage contrasts three kinds of people: the anthropomorphist, the negator, and the true monotheist. Find each one and note how Juwayni defines them.
2
Understand the criterion for tawhid
Focus on Juwayni's statement: whoever is certain of a being and confesses his inability to grasp its reality is a muwaḥḥid. This is the core of the lesson.
This criterion balances affirmation and transcendence.
3
Reflect on the objection and response
Consider the objection that this leads to bewilderment, and Juwayni's answer that reason is incapable of grasping God's reality but certain of His existence.
The response shows that inability to comprehend is not agnosticism but the highest form of knowledge.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأما فئة الحق : فهدوا الى سواء الطريق ، وسلكوا جدد الطريق ، وعلموا أن الجائزات تفتقر الى صانع لا يتصف بالصفات الدانة على الافتقار ، وعلموا أنه لو أتصف بها لكابن شبيها لمصنوعاته (1) لم يميلوا الى النفى من حيث أن يدركوا حقيقة الاله ، ولم يتعدوا موجودا القطع بكوته مع العجز عن درك حقيقته ، اذ وجدوا في أنفسهم مخلوقاً لم يستريبوا في وجوده ، ولم يدركوا حقيقته . ونحن الآن نذكر عبارة حرية بأن يتخذها مولانا فى هذا الباب هجيراه ، فهى لعمرى المنجية في دنياه وآخراه فنقول : من انهض لطلب مدیره، فان اطمأن الى موجود اتنهى اليه فكره فهو مشبه ، وابن اطمان الى النفى المحض فهو معطل ، وأن قطع بموجود ، واعترف بالعجز عن درك حقيقته فهو موحد ، وهو معنى قول الصديق رضي الله عنه اذ قال : العجز عن درك الادراك : ادراك ( فإن قيل : فغايتكم اذن حيرة ودهشة قلنا : العقول جائزة فى درك الحقيقة ، قاطعة بالوجود المنزه عن صفات الافتقار مذا(۳) وهذا (۲) ما أردناه في هذا الفن . وقد تجاوزنا الحد المعزوم عليه قليلا
And as for the faction of truth: they were guided to the middle way and traversed the path of true guidance. They knew that contingent things need a Maker who is not described with attributes that indicate need, and they knew that if He were described with them, He would be similar to His creatures. They did not incline to negation because they could not perceive the reality of the Divine, nor did they go beyond affirming a being whose existence is certainly known while being incapable of grasping its reality, for they found in themselves a creature whose existence they did not doubt but whose reality they did not perceive. And now we will mention a statement worthy of being taken by our master as a constant motto in this chapter. It is, by my life, the saving statement in this world and the next. We say: Whoever sets out to seek his director [God], if he is content with a being that his thought reaches, he is an anthropomorphist. If he is content with pure negation, he is a negator. But if he is certain of a being and confesses his inability to grasp its reality, he is a monotheist. And this is the meaning of the saying of al-Siddiq, may God be pleased with him, when he said: 'Inability to grasp comprehension is comprehension.' If it is said: 'Your end is then bewilderment and confusion,' we say: 'The intellects are incapable of grasping the reality, but are certain of the existence [of God] free from attributes of need.' And this is what we intended in this art. And we have slightly exceeded the intended limit.
Source pages p. 22

Commentary
This final passage presents the correct methodology of Ahl al-Haqq. They recognize that contingent things require a Maker free from all attributes that imply need or contingency, thus avoiding anthropomorphism. They do not deny God because they cannot perceive Him, nor do they anthropomorphize Him. Juwayni then gives a succinct criterion: the true muwaḥḥid is one who affirms a necessary existent while acknowledging his inability to comprehend His reality. This is encapsulated in the famous saying of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq: 'Inability to grasp comprehension is comprehension.' He anticipates an objection that this leads to bewilderment, and responds that reason is incapable of grasping the reality but is certain of the existence of a Being transcending all attributes of need. This is the essence of correct theology.

Key Terms
الجائزات
الصانع
الموحد
العجز
الادراك
الصديق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Affirming inability to know God leads to agnosticism or denial.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies that it leads to true monotheism: affirming existence while admitting inability to comprehend essence.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the criterion for being a true monotheist (muwaḥḥid)?
A) Affirming God's existence while denying all attributes
B) Affirming God's existence while admitting inability to grasp His reality
C) Affirming God's existence and perceiving Him through reason
D) Affirming God's existence and describing Him with sensory attributes

Key Takeaway
True monotheism is not knowing God's essence but affirming His existence while confessing one's inability to grasp His reality.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think about God, do you tend to picture Him in ways that make Him relatable—or do you embrace the mystery of His transcendence? Which is more comfortable for you, and why?
?
Juwayni says the monotheist 'confesses his inability to grasp His reality.' Where in your life do you feel the tension between wanting to know God fully and accepting your limits? What would change if you truly surrendered to that tension?
?
The motto 'Inability to grasp comprehension is comprehension' challenges the ego's need for certainty. In what specific area of your faith are you most tempted to demand clear answers, and can you instead trust God's unknowability as part of worship?

Contingency Leads to a Necessary Creator
Juwayni establishes the necessity of a non-contingent Creator from the contingency of events.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the contingency of accidental events necessitates the existence of a necessary Creator, and that the Creator's existence cannot itself be contingent.

Core Idea
Contingency points to a Necessary Being.

Juwayni begins his proof by establishing that the existence of contingent events demands a Creator who is not contingent. This foundational argument prevents an infinite regress and sets the stage for deriving God's attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that Juwayni uses a very compressed style here.
2
Identify the two key inferences
The passage makes two main points: first, that contingency of events implies a necessary Creator; second, that the Creator's existence cannot be contingent. Find the sentences that express each.
The first inference is in the sentence starting with 'فدل'. The second is in the sentence starting with 'فإنه لو'.
3
Understand why the Creator cannot be contingent
Ask yourself: why would a contingent Creator need another creator? This leads to an infinite regress. Juwayni forestalls this by asserting the Creator's necessity.
This connects to the learning objective.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
كلام فيما يجب لله تبارك وتعالى. الصانع أحاط بالصفات الجائزة للمخلوقات أرشدته إلى ما يجب وبارئها من الصفات، فدل جواز وجود الحوادث على وجوب وجود صانعها فإن الجائز لا يقع بنفسه كما سبق ولا يتصف وجود صانعه بالجواز. فإنه لو كان جائزا لافتقر إلى صانع. وقد تقرر تقدير ذلك. يدل جواز الحادثات على كون بارئها قادرا، فإن على الاضطرار نعلم أن المؤثر الفعال يجب أن يكون مقتدرا على فعله، ويجب أن يكون مريدا له، فإن القدرة لا توقع الفعل لعينها بل بفعل القادر بالقدرة متى أراد. ثم يستحيل أن يريد ما لا يعلمه، ثم يستحيل الاتصاف بهذه الصفات دون الاتصاف بالحياة. فلاح أن جواز الصفات الثابتة للحوادث دال على وجوب هذه الصفات للصانع.
Discourse on what is necessary for God, Blessed and Exalted. The Creator encompasses the contingent attributes of creatures and guides to what is necessary and their Creator's attributes. So the contingency of the existence of accidental events indicates the necessity of the existence of their Maker, for the contingent does not occur by itself as previously mentioned, nor is the existence of its Maker described as contingent. For if He were contingent, He would need a Maker. And the estimation of that has been established. The contingency of accidental events indicates that their Creator is powerful. For we know by necessity that the effective agent must be capable of His act and must be willing for it, for power does not effect the act by itself, but rather by the action of the capable one through power whenever He wills. Then it is impossible that He wills what He does not know. Then it is impossible to be described with these attributes without being described with life. So it is clear that the contingency of the attributes established for accidents indicates the necessity of these attributes for the Creator.
Source pages p. 24

Commentary
This passage is a proof from contingency to the necessary attributes of God. Juwayni begins by stating that the Creator encompasses the contingent attributes of creatures, and this leads to knowledge of what is necessary for Him. The argument proceeds: the fact that contingent events (accidents) exist demonstrates the necessity of a Creator, because the contingent does not come into existence by itself. Furthermore, the Creator's existence cannot itself be contingent, for if it were, He would need another creator—leading to an infinite regress. Next, the contingency of events indicates that the Creator is powerful (qādir), because we intuitively know that an effective agent must be capable of its act and must will it; power does not act automatically but through the agent's choice. Then, it is impossible for Him to will something He does not know, so knowledge is necessary. Finally, these attributes (power, will, knowledge) cannot exist without life, so life is also necessary. The conclusion: the contingency of the attributes found in accidents (i.e., their being possible and requiring a cause) demonstrates the necessity of these very attributes (power, will, knowledge, life) for the Creator.

Key Terms
جواز
وجوب
حوادث
صانع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Creator's existence might be contingent like creatures.
Correction: Juwayni shows that if the Creator were contingent, He would need another creator, leading to an infinite regress. Therefore, the Creator must be necessary.

Checkpoint
Why does the contingency of events indicate the necessity of a Creator according to Juwayni?
A) Because the contingent cannot occur by itself.
B) Because the Creator is powerful.
C) Because accidents are eternal.
D) Because the Creator knows everything.

Key Takeaway
The contingency of accidental events necessitates a Creator whose existence is necessary, not contingent.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Every moment of your existence is contingent, meaning you could cease to exist at any instant. Do you live with that awareness, or do you act as if your life is self-sufficient? What would change if you deeply believed that your next breath depends on a Necessary Being?
?
Juwayni argues that the chain of causes cannot go on infinitely. Where in your own life have you been looking for ultimate security in created things—money, relationships, health—that are themselves contingent? How can you consciously turn to the only Necessary Being today?
?
The passage says the Creator encompasses all contingent attributes of creatures. If Allah knows every fleeting state of your heart, from your deepest fears to your hidden desires, how does that change the way you approach Him in prayer? Are you willing to lay bare your contingency before Him right now?

Allah's Existence

From Contingency to Power and Will
Juwayni argues that contingency implies the Creator's power and will.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the contingency of events implies that the Creator possesses both power and will.

Core Idea
Contingency requires a powerful and willing agent.

Building on the necessity of a Creator, Juwayni now argues that this Creator must be powerful and willing. He uses our intuitive knowledge of effective agency to show that power and will are inseparable.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic and translation. Note that Juwayni uses the phrase 'على الاضطرار' (by necessity) to indicate an intuitive truth.
2
Identify the argument for power
Find the sentence that asserts that the Creator must be powerful. Juwayni says we know by necessity that an effective agent must be capable of its act.
3
Understand the role of will
Why is will necessary? Because power alone does not produce the act; the agent must will it. This shows that power and will are distinct attributes.
This step ties to the learning objective.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
كلام فيما يجب لله تبارك وتعالى. الصانع أحاط بالصفات الجائزة للمخلوقات أرشدته إلى ما يجب وبارئها من الصفات، فدل جواز وجود الحوادث على وجوب وجود صانعها فإن الجائز لا يقع بنفسه كما سبق ولا يتصف وجود صانعه بالجواز. فإنه لو كان جائزا لافتقر إلى صانع. وقد تقرر تقدير ذلك. يدل جواز الحادثات على كون بارئها قادرا، فإن على الاضطرار نعلم أن المؤثر الفعال يجب أن يكون مقتدرا على فعله، ويجب أن يكون مريدا له، فإن القدرة لا توقع الفعل لعينها بل بفعل القادر بالقدرة متى أراد. ثم يستحيل أن يريد ما لا يعلمه، ثم يستحيل الاتصاف بهذه الصفات دون الاتصاف بالحياة. فلاح أن جواز الصفات الثابتة للحوادث دال على وجوب هذه الصفات للصانع.
Discourse on what is necessary for God, Blessed and Exalted. The Creator encompasses the contingent attributes of creatures and guides to what is necessary and their Creator's attributes. So the contingency of the existence of accidental events indicates the necessity of the existence of their Maker, for the contingent does not occur by itself as previously mentioned, nor is the existence of its Maker described as contingent. For if He were contingent, He would need a Maker. And the estimation of that has been established. The contingency of accidental events indicates that their Creator is powerful. For we know by necessity that the effective agent must be capable of His act and must be willing for it, for power does not effect the act by itself, but rather by the action of the capable one through power whenever He wills. Then it is impossible that He wills what He does not know. Then it is impossible to be described with these attributes without being described with life. So it is clear that the contingency of the attributes established for accidents indicates the necessity of these attributes for the Creator.
Source pages p. 24

Commentary
This passage is a proof from contingency to the necessary attributes of God. Juwayni begins by stating that the Creator encompasses the contingent attributes of creatures, and this leads to knowledge of what is necessary for Him. The argument proceeds: the fact that contingent events (accidents) exist demonstrates the necessity of a Creator, because the contingent does not come into existence by itself. Furthermore, the Creator's existence cannot itself be contingent, for if it were, He would need another creator—leading to an infinite regress. Next, the contingency of events indicates that the Creator is powerful (qādir), because we intuitively know that an effective agent must be capable of its act and must will it; power does not act automatically but through the agent's choice. Then, it is impossible for Him to will something He does not know, so knowledge is necessary. Finally, these attributes (power, will, knowledge) cannot exist without life, so life is also necessary. The conclusion: the contingency of the attributes found in accidents (i.e., their being possible and requiring a cause) demonstrates the necessity of these very attributes (power, will, knowledge, life) for the Creator.

Key Terms
قادر
مريد
قدرة
إرادة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, why is will necessary in addition to power?
A) Because power cannot do anything without will.
B) Because power acts by itself.
C) Because will is the same as power.
D) Because accidents have will.

Key Takeaway
Contingency demonstrates that the Creator must be both powerful and willing, as power does not act autonomously.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you achieve something, do you genuinely attribute the outcome to God's will and power, or do you privately credit your own ability? How would your gratitude change if you truly internalized Juwayni's argument that your power is only effective because of God's will?
?
In a moment of failure, do you assume God's will was absent or that His power failed? Reflect on a recent disappointment: can you see both His power and will at work even in that? What would it mean to accept that God's will is always present, even when it contradicts your own?
?
If you truly believed that no act occurs without God's will, how would your anxiety about the future transform? What would you need to stop fearing, and what would you need to start trusting?

Knowledge and Life as Necessary Attributes
Juwayni shows that will requires knowledge, and all attributes require life.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the Creator's will necessitates knowledge, and that life is necessary for these attributes to subsist.

Core Idea
Will requires knowledge, and attributes require a living being.

Juwayni completes the chain of attributes by showing that will is impossible without knowledge, and that all these attributes require life. He concludes that the contingency of accidents demonstrates the necessity of power, will, knowledge, and life in the Creator.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic and translation. Notice the logical connectors 'ثم' and 'فلاح' that structure the argument.
2
Trace the inference from will to knowledge
Juwayni says it is impossible to will something unknown. Therefore, the Creator must know whatever He wills.
3
Understand the necessity of life
Power, will, and knowledge cannot exist without life. Hence, the Creator must be living. This completes the set of necessary attributes.
This step ties directly to the learning objective.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
كلام فيما يجب لله تبارك وتعالى. الصانع أحاط بالصفات الجائزة للمخلوقات أرشدته إلى ما يجب وبارئها من الصفات، فدل جواز وجود الحوادث على وجوب وجود صانعها فإن الجائز لا يقع بنفسه كما سبق ولا يتصف وجود صانعه بالجواز. فإنه لو كان جائزا لافتقر إلى صانع. وقد تقرر تقدير ذلك. يدل جواز الحادثات على كون بارئها قادرا، فإن على الاضطرار نعلم أن المؤثر الفعال يجب أن يكون مقتدرا على فعله، ويجب أن يكون مريدا له، فإن القدرة لا توقع الفعل لعينها بل بفعل القادر بالقدرة متى أراد. ثم يستحيل أن يريد ما لا يعلمه، ثم يستحيل الاتصاف بهذه الصفات دون الاتصاف بالحياة. فلاح أن جواز الصفات الثابتة للحوادث دال على وجوب هذه الصفات للصانع.
Discourse on what is necessary for God, Blessed and Exalted. The Creator encompasses the contingent attributes of creatures and guides to what is necessary and their Creator's attributes. So the contingency of the existence of accidental events indicates the necessity of the existence of their Maker, for the contingent does not occur by itself as previously mentioned, nor is the existence of its Maker described as contingent. For if He were contingent, He would need a Maker. And the estimation of that has been established. The contingency of accidental events indicates that their Creator is powerful. For we know by necessity that the effective agent must be capable of His act and must be willing for it, for power does not effect the act by itself, but rather by the action of the capable one through power whenever He wills. Then it is impossible that He wills what He does not know. Then it is impossible to be described with these attributes without being described with life. So it is clear that the contingency of the attributes established for accidents indicates the necessity of these attributes for the Creator.
Source pages p. 24

Commentary
This passage is a proof from contingency to the necessary attributes of God. Juwayni begins by stating that the Creator encompasses the contingent attributes of creatures, and this leads to knowledge of what is necessary for Him. The argument proceeds: the fact that contingent events (accidents) exist demonstrates the necessity of a Creator, because the contingent does not come into existence by itself. Furthermore, the Creator's existence cannot itself be contingent, for if it were, He would need another creator—leading to an infinite regress. Next, the contingency of events indicates that the Creator is powerful (qādir), because we intuitively know that an effective agent must be capable of its act and must will it; power does not act automatically but through the agent's choice. Then, it is impossible for Him to will something He does not know, so knowledge is necessary. Finally, these attributes (power, will, knowledge) cannot exist without life, so life is also necessary. The conclusion: the contingency of the attributes found in accidents (i.e., their being possible and requiring a cause) demonstrates the necessity of these very attributes (power, will, knowledge, life) for the Creator.

The Necessity of Allah's Eternality (Baqa)
Juwayni proves Allah's eternality from the necessity of His existence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Allah's eternity (baqa) is a necessary consequence of His necessary existence.

Core Idea
Allah's eternality is a logical necessity from His necessary existence.

This short but powerful passage from Juwayni establishes that Allah's attribute of eternality (baqa) is not an independent fact but a direct implication of His necessary existence. By defining the qadim (pre-eternal) as that which reason judges necessary, Juwayni shows that if something has necessary existence, it cannot cease to exist. Let's read the passage and unpack its logic.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its English translation. Notice how Juwayni connects the attribute of baqa to the definition of the qadim.
Pay attention to the phrase 'as previously established' — it refers to earlier proofs for Allah's necessary existence.
2
Identify the key inference
Trace the logical chain: Allah is qadim → qadim is necessary existence → necessary existence cannot cease → therefore Allah is baqi.
3
Reflect on the contrast with possible existence
Consider why mere possible existence would imply temporality. This is a crucial distinction in kalam between necessary and contingent beings.
If existence is merely possible, non-existence is conceivable, which would make the being temporally originated.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فصل
يجب القطع بأن الله تعالى باق ، وما وجب قدمه استحال عدمه . فان القديم هو الذى قضى العقل بوجوب وجوده . اذ لو كان وجوده جائزا لوقت الحكم بحدوثه - كما سبق تقريره .
Section: It is necessary to be certain that Allah Almighty is eternal (baqi), and whatever has been necessarily qadim (pre-eternal) it is impossible for it to cease. For the qadim is that which reason judges the necessity of its existence. For if its existence were merely possible (ja'iz), then the judgment of its temporality (huduth) would apply—as previously established.
Source pages p. 32

Commentary
This is a brief but crucial proof for the attribute of baqa (eternal existence). Juwayni states that it is necessary to be certain that Allah is baqi (eternal). Whenever something is necessarily qadim (pre-eternal, i.e., its existence is necessary), it is impossible for it to cease to exist. Why? Because the qadim is defined as that which reason judges the necessity of its existence (wujub al-wujud). If its existence were merely possible (ja'iz), then it would be subject to the judgment of temporality (hadith). That is, if something could possibly not exist, then reason would judge that it came into existence after non-existence, making it hadith. Since Allah's existence is necessary, it cannot cease. The phrase 'as previously established' refers to earlier arguments for the necessity of Allah's existence.

Key Terms
باق
قديم
وجوب وجود
حدوث

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that 'eternal' just means 'very long-lasting' or 'endless duration' without necessary existence.
Correction: Juwayni uses 'eternal' as a necessary implication of necessary existence: if a being's existence is necessary, it cannot cease, so it is eternal.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the definition of 'qadim'?
A) That which exists without beginning
B) That which reason judges the necessity of its existence
C) That which cannot cease
D) That which is known through revelation

Key Takeaway
For Juwayni, Allah's eternality is a direct logical consequence of His necessary existence — if existence is necessary, non-existence is impossible.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If Allah's existence is necessary and cannot cease, does your heart truly rest in that permanence, or are you still clinging to fleeting things as if they will last? What would change today if you lived with the certainty that only Allah is eternal?
?
Juwayni argues that if Allah's existence were possible, He would be subject to origination—like everything else. Do you ever subconsciously treat Allah as one possibility among many, giving His commands weight only when convenient? How can you begin to treat Him as the only Necessity in your life?
?
The passage demands 'certainty' (yaqeen) in Allah's eternality. Are you certain? If there is hesitation, what doubt about Allah's permanence is lurking in your heart? What concrete step can you take this week to strengthen that conviction using this logical argument?

Allah's Existence
intermediate
4 min
The Eternal Cannot Cease to Exist
Juwayni proves that the eternal cannot cease to exist.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the eternal (al-qadim) necessarily cannot cease to exist, based on rational judgment.

Core Idea
The eternal is necessary in existence; therefore non-existence is impossible.

Juwayni opens this section by asserting a foundational truth: that which is eternal cannot cease. He defines the eternal in terms of rational necessity, not merely temporal priority. This brief but powerful argument grounds God's everlastingness in the very nature of His existence.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the literal translation to grasp the meaning.
Notice the concise style typical of Juwayni.
2
Identify key terms
Find the terms al-qadim, wujūb al-wujūd, and hadath in the passage. Understand their definitions.
These are central to the argument.
3
Analyze the definition
Juwayni defines al-qadim as that which reason judges to have necessary existence. Reflect on why this definition precludes non-existence.
Necessary existence means it cannot not exist.
4
Follow the conditional
Consider the conditional statement: if existence were possible, then hadath (temporal origination) would be permissible. How does this support the definition?
This shows that the eternal cannot be contingent.
5
Draw the conclusion
Synthesize: because the eternal has necessary existence, its non-existence is impossible. This is the core proof for God's everlastingness.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فصل يجب القطع بأن الله تعالى باق ، وما وجب قدمه استحال عدمه . فان القديم هو الذى قضى العقل بوجوب وجوده . اذ لو كان وجوده جائزا لوقت الحكم بحدوثه - كما سبق تقريره .
Section: It is necessary to be certain that Allah, exalted, is everlasting. And whatever is necessary in its eternity, its non-existence is impossible. For the eternal (al-qadīm) is that which reason judges to be necessary in its existence. For if its existence were merely possible, then judgment of its occurring (hadath) would have been permissible, as was previously established.
Source pages p. 32

Commentary
This passage is a brief proof for the necessity of God's essential attributes, specifically His eternity (baqā') and the impossibility of non-existence for that which is eternal. Juwayni states that one must be certain that God is everlasting (bāqin). He then invokes a general principle: whatever's eternity (qidam) is necessary, its non-existence is impossible. He defines the eternal (al-qadīm) as that which reason (al-'aql) judges to have necessary existence. This is because if its existence were contingent, then the judgment that it could come into existence (hudūth) would be permissible—meaning it would be subject to temporal origination, which contradicts the very meaning of eternity. The argument is grounded in the rational classification of existence into necessary and possible. The proof is presented concisely, referencing earlier discussions.

Key Terms
القديم
وجوب الوجود
الحدوث

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Eternity (qidam) is merely being old or first in time.
Correction: Juwayni defines the eternal as that which reason judges to have necessary existence. Temporal priority alone does not entail impossibility of non-existence.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, why is it impossible for the eternal (al-qadīm) to cease?
A) Because it was created after non-existence
B) Because reason judges it to have necessary existence
C) Because it is possible for it to be non-existent
D) Because it is subject to time

Key Takeaway
The eternal's existence is necessary by rational judgment, making its non-existence impossible.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If Allah's existence is necessary and cannot cease, what does that mean for your attachment to worldly things that inevitably fade? How does this truth challenge your daily priorities?
?
When you face trials that shake your faith, do you ever doubt that Allah will remain with you? What would change in your life if you truly internalized that His existence is absolutely necessary and ever-present?
?
Juwayni says reason judges the necessity of the eternal. Have you ever used your intellect to affirm this truth, or have you relied solely on blind adherence? What would it take for you to own this conviction personally?

Allah's Existence
intermediate
5 min
God is Not a Body
Juwayni asserts God's non-resemblance to bodies.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Juwayni holds that God cannot be a body or correspond to bodies.

Core Idea
God is utterly unlike any physical thing.

Juwayni begins by stating a fundamental principle: the Creator does not resemble created bodies. This sets the stage for proving His oneness and transcendence. The passage establishes a clear distinction between the eternal, non-spatial existence of God and the spatial, created world.

Guided Path
1
Understand the axiom
Read the opening statement: God does not correspond to bodies. This is a foundational principle for all that follows.
Note the mutual negation: God does not correspond to bodies, and bodies do not correspond to Him.
2
Grasp the implication
See that this makes it consistent to speak of a distinctness between spatial beings and eternal existence without space.
The term 'tahayyuz' (occupying space) is key.
3
Review key terms
Familiarize yourself with tahayyuz (spatial occupancy), ajram (bodies), and wujud azali (eternal existence).
These terms are central to Juwayni's theological method.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قليعلم العاقل : أن الاله تعالى لا يناسب الأجرام المتحيزة له ، والأجسام لا تناسبه ، فابتنى على ذلك : اتساق اطلاق القول بتغاير المتحيز والوجود الأزلى الذى لا يناسب الحيز . واذا فرضنا موجودين متحيزين كانا متغايرين . وان أتصفا بأصل التحيز لانفراد كل واحد بحيزه عن الثاني ، ولو قدرنا موجودين لا يتجي واحد منهما مستويان فى انتفاء التحيز عنهما ، فلا يتصور أن ينفرد أحدهما بحيز عن الثانى وليس أحدهما مختصا بالثاني اختصاص الصفة بالموصوف ، فلا يعقلان متميزين تميز اختصاص وليس أحدهما مختصا بالثانى اختصاص الصفة بالموصوف . فان لم يختص أحدهما عن الثانى ، ولم يختص بالثانى لم يعقلا قطعا . وها أنا أذكر لقطة يسعد - والله - من يعيها ، ويفوز الفوز
Let the rational person know that God the Exalted does not correspond to atomic bodies that occupy space, and bodies do not correspond to Him. Based on that, there follows the consistency of applying the statement of distinctness to what occupies space and to eternal existence that does not correspond to space. And if we suppose two existents that occupy space, they are distinct. And if they are characterized by the principle of occupying space, it is because each one is alone in its space apart from the other. And if we suppose two existents, neither of which is such that they are equal in the absence of space from them, so it is not conceivable that one of them is alone in a space apart from the other, and neither is one of them specifically related to the other with the specific relation of an attribute to its described thing, so they are not conceived as distinct with a distinction of specificity, and neither is one of them specifically related to the other with the specific relation of an attribute to its described thing. For if one is not distinct from the other, and is not specifically related to the other, they are absolutely not conceived. And here I mention a gem — by God — whoever comprehends it will be blessed, and will achieve success.
Source pages p. 40

Commentary
This passage is a proof for the impossibility of two eternal beings and the fact that God is not a body. Juwayni begins by stating that God does not correspond (la yunasibu) to corporeal bodies (ajram mutahayyiza) that occupy space, and bodies do not correspond to Him. From this it follows that there is a consistent distinction between things that occupy space and the eternal existence that does not correspond to space. He then argues: If we suppose two existents that occupy space, they are distinct because each occupies its own space. But if we suppose two existents both of which are devoid of spatial location (i.e., not occupying space), then they cannot be distinguished because there is no spatial separation, nor can one be specifically related to the other as an attribute is to its subject. Therefore, if neither is distinct from the other, nor specifically related to the other, they are absolutely not conceivable as two distinct entities. This proves that there cannot be two eternal, non-spatial beings. Hence, God is one. Juwayni then promises a gem (luqta) that will bring blessing to whoever understands it, though the gem itself is not fully stated in this passage.

Key Terms
تحيز (tahayyuz)
أجرام (ajram)
وجود أزلى (wujud azali)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God is a subtle body or light
Correction: Juwayni explicitly denies any correspondence between God and bodies, ruling out any form of physicality.

Checkpoint
What does Juwayni say about the relationship between God and bodies?
A) They correspond in some ways
B) God does not correspond to bodies and bodies do not correspond to Him
C) God is a special body
D) Bodies are eternal

Key Takeaway
God is absolutely transcendent beyond any resemblance to physical bodies.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think of God, do you unconsciously imagine Him 'somewhere'? How does Juwayni's explicit statement that God does not correspond to bodies challenge your mental picture? What concrete step will you take today to purify your concept of God from any spatial notions?
?
In your worship, do you ever feel that God is 'far away' or 'near by' in a physical sense? How can you reconcile His absolute transcendence with His intimate closeness mentioned in the Quran (e.g., 'We are closer to him than his jugular vein')? Write down one way you will adjust your internal attitude during prayer to reflect this truth.
?
Is there any part of your life where you treat God as limited—expecting Him to act in a human way or be bound by time and space? Commit to memorizing the first part of this passage ('قليعلم العاقل : أن الإله تعالى لا يناسب الأجرام المتحيزة') and reciting it whenever you catch yourself diminishing His majesty.

Allah's Existence
intermediate
8 min
Impossibility of Two Eternal Beings
Juwayni proves that two non-spatial existents cannot be distinct.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's argument that two non-spatial beings cannot be distinguished, thus proving the impossibility of two eternal gods.

Core Idea
Without spatial or relational distinction, two non-spatial beings are inconceivable.

Juwayni extends his previous point to prove that two eternal beings cannot exist. He uses a logical argument about distinction through spatial separation or specific relation. The gem he promises highlights the profundity of this reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Consider spatial beings
Juwayni first shows that two spatial beings are distinct because each occupies its own space.
2
Consider non-spatial beings
Then he supposes two beings without space; they cannot be distinguished spatially because they do not occupy space.
They are equal in the absence of tahayyuz.
3
Explore relational distinction
The only other way two things can be distinct is if one is an attribute of the other, but that is not the case here.
Attribute-subject relation is the only alternative.
4
Reflect on the gem
Juwayni calls this argument a gem that brings blessing to whoever comprehends it.
Try to internalize the logical force: multiplicity of eternal non-spatial beings is impossible.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قليعلم العاقل : أن الاله تعالى لا يناسب الأجرام المتحيزة له ، والأجسام لا تناسبه ، فابتنى على ذلك : اتساق اطلاق القول بتغاير المتحيز والوجود الأزلى الذى لا يناسب الحيز . واذا فرضنا موجودين متحيزين كانا متغايرين . وان أتصفا بأصل التحيز لانفراد كل واحد بحيزه عن الثاني ، ولو قدرنا موجودين لا يتجي واحد منهما مستويان فى انتفاء التحيز عنهما ، فلا يتصور أن ينفرد أحدهما بحيز عن الثانى وليس أحدهما مختصا بالثاني اختصاص الصفة بالموصوف ، فلا يعقلان متميزين تميز اختصاص وليس أحدهما مختصا بالثانى اختصاص الصفة بالموصوف . فان لم يختص أحدهما عن الثانى ، ولم يختص بالثانى لم يعقلا قطعا . وها أنا أذكر لقطة يسعد - والله - من يعيها ، ويفوز الفوز
Let the rational person know that God the Exalted does not correspond to atomic bodies that occupy space, and bodies do not correspond to Him. Based on that, there follows the consistency of applying the statement of distinctness to what occupies space and to eternal existence that does not correspond to space. And if we suppose two existents that occupy space, they are distinct. And if they are characterized by the principle of occupying space, it is because each one is alone in its space apart from the other. And if we suppose two existents, neither of which is such that they are equal in the absence of space from them, so it is not conceivable that one of them is alone in a space apart from the other, and neither is one of them specifically related to the other with the specific relation of an attribute to its described thing, so they are not conceived as distinct with a distinction of specificity, and neither is one of them specifically related to the other with the specific relation of an attribute to its described thing. For if one is not distinct from the other, and is not specifically related to the other, they are absolutely not conceived. And here I mention a gem — by God — whoever comprehends it will be blessed, and will achieve success.
Source pages p. 40

Commentary
This passage is a proof for the impossibility of two eternal beings and the fact that God is not a body. Juwayni begins by stating that God does not correspond (la yunasibu) to corporeal bodies (ajram mutahayyiza) that occupy space, and bodies do not correspond to Him. From this it follows that there is a consistent distinction between things that occupy space and the eternal existence that does not correspond to space. He then argues: If we suppose two existents that occupy space, they are distinct because each occupies its own space. But if we suppose two existents both of which are devoid of spatial location (i.e., not occupying space), then they cannot be distinguished because there is no spatial separation, nor can one be specifically related to the other as an attribute is to its subject. Therefore, if neither is distinct from the other, nor specifically related to the other, they are absolutely not conceivable as two distinct entities. This proves that there cannot be two eternal, non-spatial beings. Hence, God is one. Juwayni then promises a gem (luqta) that will bring blessing to whoever understands it, though the gem itself is not fully stated in this passage.

Key Terms
تحيز (tahayyuz)
تغاير (taghayur)
اختصاص (ikhtisas)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Juwayni's argument denies the existence of any non-physical entities like angels.
Correction: Juwayni is arguing specifically about eternal beings; angels are created and temporal, so the argument does not apply to them.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what are the two ways two existents can be distinct?
A) By size and color
B) By occupying different spaces or by one being an attribute of the other
C) By being created by different gods
D) By having different names

Key Takeaway
Two eternal, non-spatial beings are inconceivable because they lack spatial separation or relational distinction.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly internalize that no other being can exist alongside Allah, would any fear of creatures or reliance on them remain? What would it take for you to live as if this is absolutely certain?
?
Juwayni calls this logical gem a blessing. Have you ever felt spiritual elevation from understanding a deep rational proof? What would change in your worship if you sought that blessing intentionally?
?
Consider your thoughts: do you ever mentally entertain the possibility of other powers or deities, even hypothetically? This argument shows that is not just religiously wrong but logically absurd. How will you train your mind to reject such thoughts immediately?
