4 min
God's Transcendence Over Bodies
Juwayni asserts God's transcendence over spatiality.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that God is not similar to any spatial or material being, and why this principle is foundational for tawhid.

Core Idea
God is beyond all spatiality and physicality.

Juwayni begins this section by reminding us of the importance of correct order in reasoning. He then states a fundamental principle: God Most High is not similar to bodies or material atoms. This principle will be the basis for the proof of tawhid that follows.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on divine dissimilarity
Read the Arabic passage once and then the translation. Notice how Juwayni sets up the principle before the proof.
The repetition about order is a reminder that kalam requires patient, systematic thought.
2
Understand the negative statement
Juwayni says God is not similar to bodies. This is a negative theology statement – we affirm what God is not, not what He is.
3
Reflect on the necessity of this principle
Ask yourself why this principle is needed before proving that there cannot be two gods. The commentary will clarify.
Without this, we might wrongly imagine God in spatial terms.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وليس تقدير الثاني متعلقا بصفة الإله الحق ، وسبيل من انتهى الى هذا الموضع ألا يتبرم بترديد القول في الترتيب .. فإن أسرار المعقولات تتلقى من سداد ترتيبها ، وقد حان بعد ذلك أن نذكر معتمدا وجيزا فى الوحدانية ، فيشفي غلة الصدور، وينفس عن كل مصدور ، ليعلم العاقل : أن الإله تعالى لا يناسب الأجرام المتحيزة له ، والأجسام لا تناسبه ، فابتنى على ذلك : اتساق اطلاق القول بتغاير المتحيز والوجود الأزلى الذى لا يناسب الحيز. واذا فرضنا موجودين متحيزين كانا متغايرين . وان أتصفا بأصل التحيز لانفراد كل واحد بحيزه عن الثاني ، ولو قدرنا موجودين لا يتحيز واحد منهما مستويان فى انتفاء التحيز عنهما ، فلا يتصور أن ينفرد أحدهما بحيز عن الثانى وليس أحدهما مختصا بالثاني اختصاص الصفة بالموصوف ، فلا يعقلان متميزين تميز اختصاص وليس أحدهما مختصا بالثانى اختصاص الصفة بالموصوف. فان لم يختص أحدهما عن الثانى ، ولم يختص بالثانى لم يعقلا قطعا . وها أنا أذكر لقطة يسعد - والله - من يعيها ، ويفوز الفوز الأكبر من يدريها . وهى : أن استحالة موجودين متغايرين متغايرين لا يختص أحدهما عن الثانى بحيز ، ولا يختص به فى الخروج عن المعقولين كفرضين متحيزين فى حيز واحد ، فيا سعادة من أنعم فكره فى هذا قليلا ، ولم يتجاوزه حتى تنضجه نار الفكر ، وتنقده يد السير .
And the positing of a second is not related to the attribute of the True God. The way of one who has reached this point is not to be annoyed by repeating discourse on the ordering, for the secrets of intelligibles are received from the correctness of their ordering. Now after that it is time to mention a concise and reliable point concerning unity (al-waḥdāniyya) to heal the thirst of breasts and relieve every distressed one, so that the rational person may know that God Most High is not similar to the material atoms (al-ajrām al-mutaḥayyiza) and bodies are not similar to Him. Upon that is based the consistent use of the statement that the spatial being and the eternal existence which does not fit space are different. And if we suppose two spatial beings, they are distinct. For they are qualified by the principle of spatiality because each one is unique in its space from the other. And if we suppose two beings neither of which is spatial, both equal in the negation of spatiality from them, then it is not conceivable that one of them be unique in a space from the other, and neither is one of them particular to the other with the particularity of an attribute to its subject. So they are not intelligible as distinct with a distinction of particularity, and neither is one of them particular to the other with the particularity of an attribute to its subject. For if one is not particular from the other, and not particular to the other, they are absolutely not intelligible. And now I mention a gem (luqṭa) – by God, happy is he who comprehends it, and the greatest victory is for he who knows it. It is that the impossibility of two distinct beings neither of which is particular from the other in space nor particular to it is, in its departure from the two intelligibles, like the supposition of two spatial beings in one space. So blessed is he who ponders this a little and does not bypass it until the fire of thought ripens it and the hand of experience tests it.
Source pages p. 40

Commentary
This passage is a rational proof for tawḥīd (divine unity). Juwayni begins by clarifying that the issue of a second deity is not about an attribute of God; rather, it concerns existence itself. He urges patience with the orderly progression of reasoning. He then states the core principle: God is not similar to material bodies or spatial entities. Therefore, any talk of a second being must consider whether it is spatial or not. If both are spatial, they are automatically distinct by their different locations. But if both are non-spatial (like God), then there is nothing to distinguish one from the other – no location, no attribute of one toward the other. He then offers a powerful analogy: positing two distinct non-spatial beings is as absurd as positing two spatial beings occupying the exact same space. This impossibility is what he calls a 'gem' that rewards deep reflection. The passage ends with an exhortation to ponder it carefully until it is fully understood.

Key Terms
تحيز
أجرام

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think God is similar to human form or has a location in space.
Correction: Juwayni explicitly states that God is not similar to bodies or spatial entities. This rejects any anthropomorphism.

Checkpoint
What does Juwayni state about God and bodies?
A) God is like bodies.
B) God is not similar to bodies.
C) God is beyond our knowledge.
D) Bodies are like God.

Key Takeaway
God is entirely transcendent over any spatial or physical attributes, a key principle of Islamic theology.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever, even for a moment, pictured Allah as if He were in a certain place (e.g., above the sky) despite knowing He is not a body? How does this passage force you to correct that image? What will you do when the thought returns?
?
When you pray, do you truly feel that you are standing before a Being who has no location, no direction, no physical presence—yet is closer than your jugular vein? If not, what mental picture are you unconsciously clinging to, and are you willing to let it go today?
?
If someone asked you, 'Where is Allah?', would you be able to answer not just correctly but with conviction, from the depths of your heart? What would that answer cost you in terms of how you live, knowing He sees you everywhere and is not confined to any space?

The Proof of Tawhid: Two Non-Spatial Beings Cannot Be Distinct
Juwayni demonstrates the rational impossibility of two distinct non-spatial beings.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the rational proof that two eternal, non-spatial beings cannot be rationally conceived as distinct.

Core Idea
Without spatial distinction or mutual relation, two non-spatial beings are indistinguishable.

In this passage, Juwayni builds on the principle of divine transcendence to prove that there can only be one God. He compares spatial beings, which are distinct by their locations, with non-spatial beings, which lack any basis for differentiation. Finally, he presents a striking analogy to drive the point home.

Guided Path
1
Understand the contrast between spatial and non-spatial
Juwayni first says that spatial beings and the eternal, non-spatial existence are different. This sets up the comparison.
2
Analyze the two suppositions
Read carefully: two spatial beings are distinct by their spaces. Two non-spatial beings have no space and no relation; thus they are not distinct.
Notice the phrase 'not particular to the other with the particularity of an attribute to its subject.'
3
Ponder the 'gem' analogy
Juwayni compares the impossibility of two distinct non-spatial beings to two spatial beings in one space. Both are inconceivable.
This analogy is called a 'luqṭa' – a precious find.
4
Reflect on the conclusion
If two non-spatial beings cannot be distinct, then there can only be one such being – God. This proves tawhid.
The proof is a rational demonstration, not based on revelation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وليس تقدير الثاني متعلقا بصفة الإله الحق ، وسبيل من انتهى الى هذا الموضع ألا يتبرم بترديد القول في الترتيب .. فإن أسرار المعقولات تتلقى من سداد ترتيبها ، وقد حان بعد ذلك أن نذكر معتمدا وجيزا فى الوحدانية ، فيشفي غلة الصدور، وينفس عن كل مصدور ، ليعلم العاقل : أن الإله تعالى لا يناسب الأجرام المتحيزة له ، والأجسام لا تناسبه ، فابتنى على ذلك : اتساق اطلاق القول بتغاير المتحيز والوجود الأزلى الذى لا يناسب الحيز. واذا فرضنا موجودين متحيزين كانا متغايرين . وان أتصفا بأصل التحيز لانفراد كل واحد بحيزه عن الثاني ، ولو قدرنا موجودين لا يتحيز واحد منهما مستويان فى انتفاء التحيز عنهما ، فلا يتصور أن ينفرد أحدهما بحيز عن الثانى وليس أحدهما مختصا بالثاني اختصاص الصفة بالموصوف ، فلا يعقلان متميزين تميز اختصاص وليس أحدهما مختصا بالثانى اختصاص الصفة بالموصوف. فان لم يختص أحدهما عن الثانى ، ولم يختص بالثانى لم يعقلا قطعا . وها أنا أذكر لقطة يسعد - والله - من يعيها ، ويفوز الفوز الأكبر من يدريها . وهى : أن استحالة موجودين متغايرين متغايرين لا يختص أحدهما عن الثانى بحيز ، ولا يختص به فى الخروج عن المعقولين كفرضين متحيزين فى حيز واحد ، فيا سعادة من أنعم فكره فى هذا قليلا ، ولم يتجاوزه حتى تنضجه نار الفكر ، وتنقده يد السير .
And the positing of a second is not related to the attribute of the True God. The way of one who has reached this point is not to be annoyed by repeating discourse on the ordering, for the secrets of intelligibles are received from the correctness of their ordering. Now after that it is time to mention a concise and reliable point concerning unity (al-waḥdāniyya) to heal the thirst of breasts and relieve every distressed one, so that the rational person may know that God Most High is not similar to the material atoms (al-ajrām al-mutaḥayyiza) and bodies are not similar to Him. Upon that is based the consistent use of the statement that the spatial being and the eternal existence which does not fit space are different. And if we suppose two spatial beings, they are distinct. For they are qualified by the principle of spatiality because each one is unique in its space from the other. And if we suppose two beings neither of which is spatial, both equal in the negation of spatiality from them, then it is not conceivable that one of them be unique in a space from the other, and neither is one of them particular to the other with the particularity of an attribute to its subject. So they are not intelligible as distinct with a distinction of particularity, and neither is one of them particular to the other with the particularity of an attribute to its subject. For if one is not particular from the other, and not particular to the other, they are absolutely not intelligible. And now I mention a gem (luqṭa) – by God, happy is he who comprehends it, and the greatest victory is for he who knows it. It is that the impossibility of two distinct beings neither of which is particular from the other in space nor particular to it is, in its departure from the two intelligibles, like the supposition of two spatial beings in one space. So blessed is he who ponders this a little and does not bypass it until the fire of thought ripens it and the hand of experience tests it.
Source pages p. 40

Commentary
This passage is a rational proof for tawḥīd (divine unity). Juwayni begins by clarifying that the issue of a second deity is not about an attribute of God; rather, it concerns existence itself. He urges patience with the orderly progression of reasoning. He then states the core principle: God is not similar to material bodies or spatial entities. Misconception: Some might think that two non-spatial beings could be distinguished by their essences or by divine attributes.
Correction: Juwayni argues that without any relation or distinguishing factor, even essence cannot differentiate them because to be two, there must be a principle of differentiation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

Do you truly believe in tawhid with your mind as well as your heart, or do you harbor hidden doubts? Commit to reciting Ayat al-Kursi daily and reflecting on its meaning until no trace of shirk remains in your thinking.
?
When you make du'a, do you sometimes feel divided loyalty as if there were other forces at play? This lesson proves that only One can exist as the ultimate reality. Where have you unknowingly allowed the concept of 'other' to creep into your understanding of God? Write down one area where you tend to attribute power to something other than Allah, and resolve to correct it today.
?
Juwayni calls his argument a 'gem' and says 'blessed is he who ponders it.' Have you truly pondered it? Or do you recite 'La ilaha illallah' mechanically? Let this proof press into your soul until the oneness of Allah is as obvious to you as the impossibility of two objects occupying the same space. What will you do to ensure this conviction stays with you every moment of your life?

Rational Proof for Resurrection
Juwayni proves resurrection's possibility using first creation argument and natural analogy.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's rational argument for the possibility of resurrection using the analogy of first creation and seasonal renewal.

Core Idea
The power that created once can recreate.

Juwayni begins by stating that rational possibility must be established before discussing resurrection. He then invokes the Qur'anic argument from Surah Ya-Sin, where Allah argues from His power over first creation to His power over recreation. To further illustrate, Juwayni provides a natural analogy from springtime renewal, showing that what is possible in nature is possible for God.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis. Then read the literal translation to grasp Juwayni's flow.
Pay attention to the logical connectors like 'ف' and 'فان'.
2
Identify the Qur'anic proof
Find the verse from Surah Ya-Sin and note how Juwayni uses it to establish the principle that God's power over first creation implies power over its like.
3
Understand the logical principle
Juwayni states: 'Whoever has complete power over a thing has power over its like.' Grasp how this applies to first and second creation.
The second creation is similar in meaning to the first.
4
Follow the natural analogy
Read the analogy from springtime: plants and insects emerge from earth. Juwayni argues that if this happens by custom, God can make all animals emerge from earth similarly.
5
Conclude with scriptural confirmation
Once rational possibility is established, the Book and sunnah confirm resurrection. Note how reason and revelation work together.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فصيل في اعادة الخلق: هذا الفصل يستدعى اثبات تقديم جواز الاعادة عقلا، فتستثير الحجاج من احتجاج الله تعالى على منكرى الاعادة. اذ قال الله تبارك وتعالى: {قال من يحيى العظام وهي رميم؟ قل يحييها الذي أنشأها أول مرة} (يس: 78-79) فاحتج رب العزة بقدرته، على الإنشاء الأول، على قدرته على الاعادة. فان الاعادة نشأة ثانية. [؟] بالنشأة (1) ومن قدر بالقدرة الكاملة على شيء قدر على مثله. والنشأة الثانية في معنى النشأة الأولى قطعا. ومن لم يثبت الأولى فهو ملحد، والوجه: مكالمته في اثبات الضائع ومن اعتقد الأولى لم يبعد الثانية، ثم نقرب من ذلك قولا، فنقول: اذا حملت الأرض أوان الربيع فنشأ منها النبات، والنبات، وضروب من الحشرات، لا تعد، فما المانع من أن الله تعالى على الأرض على مجرى العادة صفات تقتضى أن تنشر منها الحيوانات كلها على حكم العادة في اثبات النبات واخراج الثمرات؟ فاذا ثبت الجواز فقد نطق الكتاب ومتواتر السنن بنشر الخلائق ليوم الدين وقيامهم لرب العالمين.
Section on the recreation of creatures: This section requires first establishing the possibility of recreation rationally, then we derive the proofs from Allah's argument against the deniers of recreation. When Allah the Blessed and Exalted said: {He says: Who will give life to bones while they are decayed? Say: He will give them life who produced them the first time} (Ya-Sin 78-79). So the Lord of Might argued, by His power over the first creation, for His power over recreation. For recreation is a second creation. [؟] with the creation (1). And whoever has complete power over a thing has power over its like. The second creation is without doubt similar in meaning to the first creation. And whoever does not affirm the first [creation] is an atheist. The way: to debate him about establishing the lost [creation]. And whoever believes in the first should not find the second unlikely. Then we bring a statement closer: we say: When the earth bears in spring, and plants arise from it, and plants, and types of insects beyond count, what prevents Allah from bestowing upon the earth, according to the customary course, qualities that require that all animals emerge from it according to the custom in establishing plants and producing fruits? So when the possibility is established, then the Book and mutawatir traditions have spoken about the resurrection of creatures for the Day of Judgment and their standing before the Lord of the worlds.
Source pages p. 77

Commentary
This passage is a proof argument. Juwayni begins by stating that the section requires first establishing the rational possibility of recreation (i'ada). He then invokes the Qur'anic argument from God Himself in Surah Ya-Sin: 'Who will give life to bones while they are decayed? Say: He will give them life who produced them the first time.' God argues from His power over the first creation to His power over recreation. Juwayni explains that recreation is a second creation (nash'ah thaniyah). The passage then states that whoever has complete power over a thing has power over its like, and the second creation is similar in meaning to the first. Whoever does not affirm the first creation is an atheist; the debate with him is about establishing the first creation. Anyone who believes in the first creation should not find the second unlikely. Juwayni then offers a further natural analogy: in spring, the earth produces countless plants and insects; what prevents God from giving the earth qualities that would cause all animals to emerge from it according to custom, just as plants and fruits are produced? Once the possibility is established, the Book and mutawatir traditions confirm the resurrection.

Key Terms
إعادة
نشأة
إنشاء
جواز
قدرة
عادة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Resurrection is impossible because bones decay and cannot be reconstituted.
Correction: Juwayni argues that the same power that created from nothing can easily recreate from decayed bones, as the second creation is similar to the first.

Checkpoint
What is the main rational argument Juwayni uses to prove the possibility of resurrection?
A) The soul is immortal and transmigrates.
B) Whoever has power over a thing has power over its like, and the second creation is similar to the first.
C) The earth naturally recycles matter without divine intervention.
D) The resurrection is a physical impossibility that must be accepted on faith alone.

Key Takeaway
Juwayni uses the principle that God's power over first creation implies power over its like, supported by a natural analogy, to establish the rational possibility of resurrection.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you look at the world around you—the cycles of seasons, the return of life after winter—does your heart truly recognize that the same power that brings this about can raise you from your grave? Or do you live as if this world is the end?
?
If you truly believed that God's power over your first creation guarantees His power to resurrect you, how would that change the way you spend your time today? What would you do differently if you were certain you will stand before Him tomorrow?
?
Juwayni argues that whoever affirms the first creation should not find the second unlikely. So ask yourself: Do you live as if the afterlife is 'unlikely'? Where in your life do you act as if this world is all that matters, and what will it take for you to align your actions with your belief in resurrection?

The Burhan al-Mudabbir: Proving a Necessary Existence
Juwayni presents the argument from the director of the world to prove a necessary existence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the logical structure of Juwayni's argument from the director of the world to prove the existence of a necessary being.

Core Idea
The world's director must be necessary, or we fall into circularity or infinite regress.

This lesson presents one of the most elegant proofs in kalam: the Burhan al-Mudabbir. Imam al-Juwayni argues that the director of the world cannot be a contingent being, because that would lead to an infinite chain or circular dependency—both impossible. The only rational conclusion is that the director is necessary in Himself.

Guided Path
1
Read the argument carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the three possibilities Juwayni considers.
2
Identify the logical fork
The argument presents a conditional: if the director is necessary, done; if not, then He is possible and requires an effecter.
3
Understand the two impossible outcomes
If the director is possible, the chain of effecters must end. Otherwise we get dawr (circularity) or tasalsul (infinite regress), both rejected.
4
See the conclusion
The only valid possibility is that the director is necessary of existence in Himself, which is the sought God.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مدير العالم إن كان واجب الوجود فهو المطلوب، وإلا كان ممكنا فله مؤثر، ويلزم إما الدور أو التسلسل وإما الانتهاء إلى مؤثر واجب الوجود لذاته.
The director of the world: if He is necessary of existence, then He is the sought; otherwise He is possible, so He has an effecter. And it necessarily follows either circularity or infinite regress, or [one must] end at an effecter that is necessary of existence in Itself.
Source pages p. 99

Commentary
This passage presents the Burhan al-Mudabbir (the argument from the director of the world). It is a proof for the existence of a necessary being (wajib al-wujud). The argument begins with the observation that the world has a director (mudabbir). If that director is necessary in Himself, then He is the sought God. But if He is possible (mumkin), then He must have an effecter that brings Him into existence. This second scenario leads either to an infinite chain (tasalsul) or a circular dependency (dawr), both of which are impossible. Therefore, one must eventually arrive at a first effecter that is necessary in His own essence. This is a classic kalam argument for the existence of a First Cause.

Key Terms
مدير العالم (mudabbir al-'alam)
واجب الوجود (wajib al-wujud)
ممكن (mumkin)
دور (dawr)
تسلسل (tasalsul)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the Burhan al-Mudabbir relies on empirical observation of a specific director (like a person).
Correction: The argument is purely rational: it takes the concept of 'director of the world' as a logical starting point and uses the impossibility of dawr and tasalsul to prove a necessary being.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni's argument, if the director of the world is not necessary in Himself, what necessarily follows?
A) He is impossible
B) Either circularity or infinite regress must occur
C) He becomes nonexistent
D) He is not needed

Key Takeaway
Juwayni's Burhan al-Mudabbir shows that the existence of a necessary being is the only rational explanation for the world's directedness.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If the director of the world must ultimately be a necessary being, and you are part of this directed world, what does that say about your own dependence on that necessary being? In what ways do you still live as if you are self-sufficient and not in need of that director?
?
Juwayni’s argument forces you to choose: either the director is necessary or you fall into infinite regress. Do you ever treat your own accountability to God as something that can be pushed back infinitely, like a chain of 'later' that never ends? When will you stop the regress and acknowledge the Necessary One today?
?
The proof ends with a necessary existence. If you claim to believe in this necessary being, why do you sometimes act as if the world runs on its own, without a director? What would change in your daily life if you truly lived as though every moment is under His direct direction?

4 min
The Spirit as an Analogy for Rational Belief in God
Juwayni uses the spirit as an analogy to argue for rational belief in God.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni uses the analogy of the spirit to demonstrate that God's existence can be known through reason alone, without sensory perception.

Core Idea
The unseen spirit proves that reason can know the unseen God.

Juwayni presents a simple yet powerful analogy: just as we know the spirit exists through reason despite never seeing it, we can know God exists through rational inference even though He is invisible. This lesson unpacks that analogy and shows why it is a valid argument for God's existence.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the translation. Notice the parallel structure between the spirit and God.
2
Identify the analogy's parts
Identify the spirit (subject), its perception by intellect not sense, and the parallel claim about God. Note the Quranic phrase 'al-mathal al-a'la'.
3
Understand the logic
Reflect on why the analogy works: we accept the spirit's existence rationally despite invisibility; similarly, we accept God's existence rationally.
The argument is from analogy, not a direct proof.
4
Apply the lesson
Think of other unseen realities (e.g., gravity, mind) that we know by reason. This helps internalize the validity of rational belief in the unseen.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وذكر الروح [؟] مثلا على وجود الله. فكما أن الروح تدرك بالعقل لا بالحس، إذ لم يرها أحد ولا يستطيع أحد أن ينكرها لعدم الرؤية، كذلك الله، والله المثل الأعلى، نؤمن به بالعقل وإن كان لا يرى.
He mentioned the spirit [؟] as an example for the existence of God. For just as the spirit is perceived by the intellect not by sense, since no one has seen it and no one can deny it due to not seeing it, likewise God — and to God belongs the highest attributes — we believe in Him by the intellect even though He is not seen.
Source pages p. 99

Commentary
This passage uses an analogy to show that God's existence can be known through reason even though He is not perceived by the senses. Juwayni mentions the example of the spirit (ruh): we know the spirit exists through its effects and reason, even though no one has seen it. Similarly, we believe in God through rational inference, despite not seeing Him. The phrase 'the highest attributes' (al-mathal al-a'la) is from the Quran. The point is that rational belief in the unseen is legitimate and necessary.

Key Terms
الروح (al-ruh)
العقل (al-'aql)
الحس (al-hiss)
المثل الأعلى (al-mathal al-a'la)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: People may think Juwayni is comparing God to the spirit in essence, implying some similarity.
Correction: The analogy is not a comparison of God to the spirit, but a rational argument: just as we accept the spirit's existence through reason without sensory evidence, we should accept God's existence through reason without seeing Him.

Checkpoint
Why does Juwayni use the example of the spirit?
A) To prove the spirit is God
B) To show God can be known by reason without sensory perception
C) To deny the existence of the spirit
D) To argue that spirits are visible

Key Takeaway
Juwayni's analogy shows that rational belief in the unseen God is justified, just as we rationally accept the existence of the spirit.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have never seen your own spirit, yet you are certain it exists. Why, then, do you sometimes allow doubt about God's existence to creep in because you cannot see Him? What does this reveal about your reliance on the intellect versus the senses?
?
If someone denied the existence of your spirit because they couldn't see it, you would think them foolish. When you fail to act on the certain knowledge of God's existence, are you not being equally foolish in your own life? What steps will you take today to live by that rational certainty?
?
Juwayni uses the spirit—something intimately known to you—to argue for God. How does your own awareness of your spirit (its thoughts, emotions, decisions) point you to a greater reality? Are you willing to let that inner witness guide your belief more than external senses?

Allah's Existence

Proof of God's Existence from the Necessary Cause
Juwayni presents the first part of his argument for God's existence via the necessity of a first cause.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's rational proof for the existence of a necessary being from the impossibility of infinite regress and circularity.

Core Idea
Every possible being needs a cause; only a Necessary Being can end the chain.

Juwayni opens his theological argument with a concise proof that the existence of a 'director' of the world must ultimately be a Necessary Existent. He uses the concepts of possibility and necessity to show that the chain of causes cannot go on forever or loop back. This lesson unpacks that proof step by step.

Guided Path
1
Read the proof passage
Read the Arabic passage and its translation carefully. Notice the logical flow: conditional statement, then the two possibilities.
The proof is very compressed; take your time.
2
Identify the two possibilities
The director of the world is either necessary (wajib) or possible (mumkin). If wajib, we have found God. If mumkin, he needs a cause.
3
Understand the termination argument
The cause of a possible being cannot go on infinitely or in a circle. It must end at a Necessary Existent. This is the core of the proof.
Reflect on why circularity (daur) and infinite regress (tasalsul) are considered invalid.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مدير العالم ان كان واجب الوجود فهو المطلوب والا كان ممكنا فله مؤثر . ويلزم اما الدور أو التسلسل واما الانتهاء إلى مؤثر واجب الوجود لذاته . وبين أن الله تعالى اله واحد لا يحده مكان ، ولا يحويه زمان ، وليس بجسم . وانما هو في كل مكان وليس كمثله شيء .
The director of the world: if he is necessary of existence, then he is the sought; otherwise he is possible and has an effective cause. There necessarily follows either circularity or regress, or ending at an effective cause that is necessary of existence in itself. And he made clear that Allah is one God, no place confines Him, no time contains Him, and He is not a body. Rather, He is in every place, and nothing is like Him.
Source pages p. 99

Commentary
This passage presents a concise proof for the existence of God from the governance of the world. It begins by considering the director of the universe: if that director is necessary in His own essence (wajib al-wujud bi-dhatihi), then He is the God we seek. If, however, He is merely possible (mumkin), then He requires an external cause (mu'aththir) to bring Him into existence. The argument then notes that the only alternatives are either an infinite regress or a circle, both of which are absurd, or arriving at a cause that is necessary of existence by itself. This establishes the existence of a necessary being. Juwayni then clarifies the nature of this God: He is one, not limited by space or time, not a body (jism), yet He is in every place (meaning His knowledge and power encompass all), and nothing resembles Him. This passage combines a rational proof with a statement of divine transcendence.

Key Terms
واجب الوجود (wajib al-wujud)
ممكن (mumkin)
مؤثر (mu'aththir)
دور (daur)
تسلسل (tasalsul)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The term 'possible' (mumkin) here is sometimes misunderstood to mean 'nonexistent' or 'unreal'.
Correction: In this context, 'possible' means contingent — something that can exist but needs a cause. It is not nonexistent; it is a real being that depends on another.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, if the director of the world is not necessary of existence, what necessarily follows?
A) He is nonexistent
B) He has an effective cause
C) He is eternal
D) He is part of an infinite chain

Key Takeaway
Juwayni's proof shows that the chain of contingent causes must end in a Necessary Existent, which is God.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You rely on countless 'causes' every day—your job, your health, your relationships. If you truly believed they are all contingent and could vanish instantly without the Necessary Being holding them, would you cling to them less? What would you need to change in your heart to depend solely on God?
?
Juwayni forces you to choose: either the chain of causes goes on forever or it ends in God. Have you ever caught yourself living as if there is no final end—as if your purpose, security, or worth is an endless pursuit? What would it mean for your daily decisions to truly rest in the fact that God is the ultimate end?
?
If you are merely a 'possible being' needing a cause to exist at every moment, then your existence is not a given—it is a gift sustained by the Necessary Being. Does your gratitude reflect that? When was the last time you thanked God not just for things, but for the very fact that you exist right now?

4 min
God's Transcendence: Beyond Space, Time, and Body
Juwayni lists the essential attributes of God: unity, transcendence beyond space, time, and body.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's description of God as transcendent, not confined by place, time, or material form.

Core Idea
God is not like anything; He is One, and His presence is not spatial.

After establishing the existence of a Necessary Being, Juwayni clarifies what that Being is not. He affirms God's oneness, then denies spatial and temporal confinement and corporeality. This lesson explains these negations and their implications for Islamic theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on God's attributes
Read the Arabic and translation. Notice that the passage starts with an affirmation of oneness, then lists negations, then a positive phrase, and ends with the ultimate incomparability.
2
Identify the negations
Juwayni explicitly denies that God is confined by place, contained by time, or is a body. Understand what each negation means.
These negations protect God from anthropomorphism.
3
Interpret 'He is in every place'
Juwayni says 'rather, He is in every place.' This does not mean physical indwelling; it means His knowledge and power encompass all places.
A common misunderstanding is to take this literally.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مدير العالم ان كان واجب الوجود فهو المطلوب والا كان ممكنا فله مؤثر . ويلزم اما الدور أو التسلسل واما الانتهاء إلى مؤثر واجب الوجود لذاته . وبين أن الله تعالى اله واحد لا يحده مكان ، ولا يحويه زمان ، وليس بجسم . وانما هو في كل مكان وليس كمثله شيء .
The director of the world: if he is necessary of existence, then he is the sought; otherwise he is possible and has an effective cause. There necessarily follows either circularity or regress, or ending at an effective cause that is necessary of existence in itself. And he made clear that Allah is one God, no place confines Him, no time contains Him, and He is not a body. Rather, He is in every place, and nothing is like Him.
Source pages p. 99

Commentary
This passage presents a concise proof for the existence of God from the governance of the world. It begins by considering the director of the universe: if that director is necessary in His own essence (wajib al-wujud bi-dhatihi), then He is the God we seek. If, however, He is merely possible (mumkin), then He requires an external cause (mu'aththir) to bring Him into existence. The argument then notes that the only alternatives are either an infinite regress or a circle, both of which are absurd, or arriving at a cause that is necessary of existence by itself. This establishes the existence of a necessary being. Juwayni then clarifies the nature of this God: He is one, not limited by space or time, not a body (jism), yet He is in every place (meaning His knowledge and power encompass all), and nothing resembles Him. This passage combines a rational proof with a statement of divine transcendence.

Key Terms
لا يحده مكان
لا يحويه زمان
ليس بجسم
في كل مكان
ليس كمثله شيء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The phrase 'He is in every place' is sometimes taken to mean that God is physically present everywhere.
Correction: Juwayni explicitly says that no place confines Him, so 'in every place' must be understood metaphorically, referring to His knowledge and power.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, which of the following is NOT true about God?
A) He is one God
B) He is contained by time
C) He is not a body
D) Nothing is like Him

Key Takeaway
Juwayni emphasizes that God is utterly transcendent: not bounded by space or time, not a body, and nothing compares to Him.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you pray, do you ever imagine Allah as being 'up in the sky' or somewhere far away? How does Juwayni's statement that no place confines Him challenge that mental picture? What step can you take today to worship Him as the One who is beyond all space?
?
If Allah cannot be contained by time, then He knows your every future moment right now. How does that make you feel when you plan your life? Do you plan as if your future is unknown to Him? What would it mean to truly trust His timeless knowledge in one decision you make today?
?
You have just learned that nothing is like Allah. Yet, in your daily life, do you sometimes compare His actions to human actions (e.g., 'Allah is angry like I get angry')? How can you practically remind yourself of His incomparability the next time you describe Him?

Allah's Existence
beginner
5 min
Analogy of the Spirit and the Three Groups
Juwayni uses the spirit as an analogy and classifies three groups regarding divine attributes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni uses the analogy of the spirit to demonstrate rational belief in God and how he classifies three attitudes toward the divine attributes.

Core Idea
True monotheism balances affirmation of God and transcendence without anthropomorphism or negation.

Juwayni presents a powerful analogy: just as we accept the existence of the soul through reason despite not seeing it, we accept God's existence through rational proof. He then warns against two extremes — anthropomorphism and pure negation — and defines the middle path of the true monotheist.

Guided Path
1
Read the source passage
Read the Arabic passage and its English translation. Notice how Juwayni builds his argument from the analogy to the threefold classification.
Pay attention to the sequence: analogy first, then three groups.
2
Understand the spirit analogy
Reflect on why the spirit is a fitting analogy: it is known by reason, not senses. This establishes that God's existence can be known rationally even without sensory evidence.
The analogy is not a proof but an illustration.
3
Identify the three groups
Identify the mushabbih, mu'attil, and muwahhid. Understand what each believes: one likens God to creation, one denies attributes, one affirms God while acknowledging His transcendence.
The muwahhid is the correct path.
4
Distinguish the muwahhid from extremes
Note that the muwahhid does not claim to fully grasp God's essence; he affirms what God says about Himself without forming a mental image or negating attributes.
Common misunderstanding: the muwahhid is not agnostic; he affirms.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وذكر (الروح) مثلا على وجود الله . فكما أن الروح : تدرك بالعقل لا بالحس .. اذ لم يرها أحد، ولا يستطيع أحد أن ينكرها لعدم الرؤية كذلك الله والله المثل الأعلى يؤمن به بالعقل وان كان لا يرى. ثم قال من تخيل الله بصورة في ذهنه فهو مشبه .. ومن اطمان الى النفي المحض فهو معطل . ومن اعترف بالله ثم اعترف بالعجز عن درك حقيقته فهو موحد .. يريد من يعترف بالله ، ثم يقول عنه : ليس كمثله شيء ( ولم يلزم نفسه بتخيله ، أو نفيه ، فهذا هو المسلم حقا وصدقا
And he mentioned the spirit as an example for the existence of God. For just as the spirit is perceived by reason, not by sense, since no one has seen it, yet no one can deny it due to lack of sight, likewise God – and to God belongs the highest attribute – is believed in by reason even though He is not seen. Then he said: Whoever imagines God in a form in his mind is a mushabbih (anthropomorphist). And whoever rests content with pure negation is a mu'attil (negator). And whoever acknowledges God and then acknowledges inability to comprehend His reality is a muwahhid (monotheist). He intends: whoever acknowledges God, then says about Him: 'Nothing is like unto Him' and does not oblige himself to imagine Him or negate Him, that is the true Muslim indeed.
Source pages p. 99

Commentary
This passage begins with an analogy: just as we accept the existence of the spirit (ruh) through rational inference despite not seeing it, we should accept God's existence through reason even though He is not seen. Juwayni then presents a threefold classification of people's attitudes toward God: (1) The mushabbih – one who imagines God in a form, likening Him to creation. (2) The mu'attil – one who goes to the opposite extreme of pure negation, denying any positive attributes. (3) The muwahhid – the true monotheist who affirms God's existence and His statement 'nothing is like Him', while neither forming a mental image nor negating His attributes. This balanced path is the correct Islamic position.

Key Terms
روح (ruh)
مشبه (mushabbih)
معطل (mu'attil)
موحد (muwahhid)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The muwahhid is someone who is agnostic about God or who claims to fully comprehend God.
Correction: The muwahhid affirms God's existence and attributes but admits human inability to grasp His essence. This is not agnosticism but humble faith.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what characterizes a muwahhid?
A) He denies God's existence
B) He imagines God as a form
C) He affirms God and says 'nothing is like Him' without forming an image or negating
D) He claims to fully comprehend God's essence

Key Takeaway
Juwayni teaches that the true monotheist affirms God's existence and transcendence, avoiding both imagining Him in creation and denying His attributes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think about God, do you ever catch yourself imagining a form or shape despite knowing 'Nothing is like Him'? What does that reveal about your heart's desire for a concrete image?
?
Juwayni says the true monotheist admits inability to comprehend God's reality. Are you comfortable with not fully understanding God, or do you find yourself trying to define or limit Him in your mind?
?
Reflect on your own speech about God: do you ever fall into 'pure negation' by denying His attributes to protect transcendence? How can you affirm His attributes as He has described without imagining them?

Juwayni on Reward and Punishment
Juwayni on reward as favor, punishment as justice, with reconciliation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's view that divine reward is a favor and punishment is just, how it contrasts with the view that reward is obligatory, and the proposed reconciliation.

Core Idea
Reward is God's grace; punishment is His justice.

Juwayni's discussion on reward and punishment addresses a key theological question: Is God obligated to reward the obedient? In this passage, Juwayni asserts that reward is a pure favor (tafaddul), while punishment is just requital. An opposing view claims that God has bound Himself to reward. The passage concludes with a reconciliation that highlights God's pre-eternal favor.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and its translation
Read the Arabic carefully, then the translation. Notice the key terms: tafaddul and jaza' wifaq.
2
Identify Juwayni's position
Locate where Juwayni states that reward is a favor and punishment is just. Contrast with the opposing view.
3
Understand the reconciliation
See how the passage attempts to reconcile the two views by referring to God's pre-eternal favor and His decrees.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وتحدث الجويني عن الثواب والعقاب . فبين أن الله يثيب الطائع على عمله الخير تفضلا منه ، ويعاقب العاصى على عمله السىء جزاء وفاقا بينما يقرر غيره ان اثابة الله للطائع ليست تفضلا من الله ، بل هو أمر واجب على الله .. أوجبه هو على نفسه بقوله تعالى « كتب ربكم على نفسه الرحمة ) ( الأنعام : ٥٤ ( وللتوفيق بين رأى الجويني وغيره يمكن القول بأن الله تعالى تفضل أزلا بخلق الخلق . ثم نص للخلق على أنه كتب الرحمة وكتب العذاب ، ليحملهم على عدم العذر اليه
And Juwayni discussed reward and punishment. He explained that God rewards the obedient for their good deed as a favor from Him, and punishes the disobedient for their evil deed as a just requital. Meanwhile, others assert that God's rewarding the obedient is not a favor from God, but is an obligation upon God which He imposed on Himself by His saying, 'Your Lord has written mercy upon Himself' (Al-An'am 6:54). And to reconcile between Juwayni's view and others, it can be said that God Almighty favored [creation] by creating creation in pre-eternity. Then He decreed for creation that He wrote mercy and wrote punishment, to leave them without excuse before Him.
Source pages p. 110

Commentary
This passage presents Juwayni's position on divine reward and punishment, contrasting it with the view that rewarding the obedient is obligatory upon God. The author explains that Juwayni sees reward as a pure favor (tafaddul) from God, while punishment is just and proportional. The opposing view, attributed to others, claims that God obligated Himself to reward, citing the Qur'anic verse 'Your Lord has written mercy upon Himself.' The passage then proposes a reconciliation: God's original act of creating was a favor, and then He decreed mercy and punishment for specific reasons, thereby eliminating any excuse.

Key Terms
الثواب
العقاب
تفضل
جزاء وفاقا
كتب الرحمة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think Juwayni denies that God has any obligation, including self-imposed obligations.
Correction: Juwayni does not deny that God can obligate Himself; he simply denies that reward is inherently owed. The reconciliation shows that God's decree of mercy is still a favor.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, how does Juwayni describe God's rewarding of the obedient?
A) As a just requital
B) As a favor (tafaddul) from God
C) As an obligation upon God
D) As a necessary consequence of God's nature

Key Takeaway
Juwayni views divine reward as a favor, not an obligation, while punishment is just, and the reconciliation shows that God's entire act of creation is a favor.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you look at your good deeds, do you see them as earning a right to paradise, or as opportunities for God's favor? How would your humility change if you truly saw every reward as tafaddul?
?
Is there a sin you minimize because you think God's punishment is too harsh? How does Juwayni's view of punishment as 'just requital' challenge you to take your sins more seriously?
?
The passage says God created and decreed mercy and punishment so that you have no excuse. What excuse are you still holding onto, and will you let this truth remove it today?

Allah's Decree and the Natural Lifespan
Juwayni explains natural lifespan as a divine deposit and warns against hastening death.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that while Allah decrees each person's natural lifespan (ajal), humans are held accountable for actions that hasten death, and that divine decree does not remove human choice and responsibility.

Core Idea
Divine decree sets the natural term, but hastening death violates Sharia and brings punishment.

Juwayni addresses a delicate balance: how divine predetermination of lifespan relates to human actions that shorten life. He uses examples from nature and human health to show that while Allah has placed in each body a certain potential lifespan, humans are commanded to preserve their lives and not hasten death. He refutes the idea that one can claim to know the exact term, citing the Prophet's instruction to choose a good spouse and Quranic injunctions against self-destruction.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Begin by reading the Arabic passage and its English translation side by side. Notice the flow: Juwayni starts with animal examples, moves to humans, then gives a concrete example from genetics, and finally addresses counterarguments.
The passage contains OCR uncertainties; focus on the main argument.
2
Identify the principle of natural lifespan
Juwayni says Allah deposits 'forces' (quwa) in the body that are consumed over a certain number of years. This is the ajal. Understand that this natural term assumes safety from diseases and afflictions.
3
See the consequence of hastening death
If a person exposes themselves to harm and dies earlier, they are punished because they violated Sharia commands to preserve life. This shows that human actions matter despite divine decree.
4
Study the counterarguments
Juwayni rejects the idea that one can specify the exact term by pointing to the hadith 'Choose for your sperm' (implying choice) and Quranic verse 'Do not throw yourselves into destruction.' Reflect on how these support human responsibility.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قوى ليعاش إلى كذا سنة، وأن جدي الماعز سيعيش سنينا أطول، وكذلك الخصان [؟] يعيش أطول من الديك وجدي الماعز. وهكذا الإنسان أودع الله في جسمه قوى تستهلك لو سلم من الأمراض والآفات في كذا من السنين. وهذا هو أجله الذي أودعه الله علمه بحسب ما يتحمل الجسم. فإذا استعجل الإنسان أجله بأن عرض نفسه للأمراض والآفات، عوقب من قبل الله عز وجل لأنه خالف أوامر الشريعة التي تهدف إلى حفظ الإنسان حتى يأتي أجله الطبيعي بانحلال خلايا الجسم وتلف أجهزته، وهذا يوضحه ضرب مثال على طريقة الجويني في عقيدته، وهو: لو أن طفلا خرج من أبوين صحيحين قويين، لماش [؟] من الطفل الذي يخرج من أبوين ضعيفين، خرج طفل من أب قريب لأمه لكانت صحته أقل من الطفل الذي خرج من أب غير قريب لأمه. أشد. وهذا مشاهد غير منكور. ولو ادعى مدع بتحديد ذلك في الأجل، لعورض بقول الرسول صلى الله عليه وسلم: «تخيروا لنطفكم» الذي يثبت الاختيار لا الخبر. وعورض بآيات في القرآن منها {ولا تلقوا بأيديكم إلى التهلكة}. وغير خاف أن التكوين كله وما فيه من الله، وأنه أودع في كل شيء أسبابه، وما يحدث أمام أعيننا من الموت الفجائي لأي حي على المقدرة بحكم العادة، فهو من سنن الحياة وطبيعتها التي فطرها عليها، وهذا معنى قوله تعالى: {كل من عليها فان [؟] فعال هؤلاء القوم لا يكادون يفقهون حديثا}، أي الكون وما فيه من عند الله.
Forces to live up to such a year, and that the kid goat will live longer years, and likewise the [؟] lives longer than the rooster and the kid goat. And likewise man – Allah has deposited in his body forces that are consumed if he is safe from diseases and afflictions in such and such years. This is his term (ajal) which Allah has deposited with His knowledge according to what the body can bear. So if man hastens his term by exposing himself to diseases and afflictions, he is punished by Allah Almighty because he violated the commands of the Sharia which aim to preserve man until his natural term comes through the dissolution of body cells and the spoilage of its organs. And this is clarified by an example according to Juwayni's doctrine, namely: If a child comes from strong, healthy parents, [؟] than a child who comes from weak parents; a child comes from a father who is a close relative of his mother, his health would be less than a child from a father not a close relative of his mother, even more so. And this is observed, not denied. And if someone claimed to specify that in the term, he would be countered by the saying of the Prophet ﷺ: 'Choose for your sperm' which proves choice, not information. And it is countered by verses in the Qur'an including {And do not throw yourselves into destruction}. And it is not hidden that all of creation and what is in it is from Allah, and that He has deposited in everything its causes, and what happens before our eyes of sudden death for any living being by ability according to custom, it is from the ways of life and its nature upon which He has originated it. And this is the meaning of His statement: {Everyone upon it is passing away [؟] 'fāʿāl hāʾulāʾ al-qawm lā yakādūna yafqahūna ḥadīthan'} i.e. the universe and what is in it is from Allah.
Source pages p. 120

Commentary
This passage is an argument about divine decree (qadar) and the natural lifespan. Juwayni begins by giving an example of different lifespans in animals: a kid goat lives longer than a rooster, etc. He then extends this to humans: Allah has placed in the body forces that are consumed over a certain number of years if free from disease. This is the individual's term (ajal) known to Allah according to the body's capacity. If a person hastens his term by exposing himself to harm, he is punished for violating Sharia commands to preserve life until the natural end. To illustrate, Juwayni gives an example: a child from healthy strong parents is healthier than one from weak parents, and a child from closely related parents (consanguinity) is less healthy than one from unrelated parents—this is observed empirically. If someone claimed to fix the term precisely, they are countered by the hadith 'Choose for your sperm' (which implies choice of spouse, not predetermination of term) and by Qur'anic verses like 'Do not throw yourselves into destruction.' Finally, Juwayni emphasizes that all creation and its causes are from Allah; sudden death by custom is part of life's nature. He interprets the verse {Everyone upon it is passing away} (Qur'an 55:26) but the OCR garbles it; the point is that the universe and all in it are from Allah.

Key Terms
أجل (ajal)
شريعة (Sharia)
قوى (quwa)
سبب (sabab)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Divine decree means humans have no control over their lifespan.
Correction: Juwayni affirms that while Allah decrees the natural term, humans are held responsible for actions that hasten death, as harming oneself violates Sharia.

Checkpoint
What does Juwayni say happens to a person who hastens their term by exposing themselves to diseases?
A) They are rewarded for seeking death.
B) They are punished by Allah for violating Sharia commands.
C) Their term is automatically extended.
D) They are forgiven because it is predestined.

Key Takeaway
Allah's decree of lifespan does not negate human responsibility; hastening death is forbidden and punishable.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever taken a risk with your health or life, believing it was in your control? How does this lesson's teaching on divine decree and human responsibility change how you view that action? What step will you take to align your choices with Sharia's preservation of life?
?
Do you truly trust Allah's decree for your lifespan, or do you secretly believe you can extend it by your own efforts? What actions in your life reflect that belief, and how can you surrender to Allah's plan while still taking lawful precautions?
?
This lesson warns against hastening death by self-harm. In what subtle ways do you hasten towards spiritual death by ignoring Sharia commands that protect your faith? What will you change today to live in accordance with the natural term Allah has deposited in you?

General and Specific Provision: Definitions and Quranic Proof
Juwayni distinguishes general and specific provision with Quranic proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's distinction between general provision (rizq 'āmm) and specific provision (rizq khāṣṣ) and the Quranic evidence for each.

Core Idea
Provision comes in two forms: general sustenance for all and specific portion earned through effort.

Juwayni begins by clarifying a key concept in the theology of sustenance: there is a difference between the universal provision Allah has placed in the earth and the particular provision that depends on human striving. This distinction is not merely academic; it shapes how we understand the relationship between divine decree and human responsibility.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and identify the two types of rizq
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing it. Then read the translation. Notice how Juwayni introduces the necessity of distinguishing between general and specific provision.
Pay attention to the keywords 'rizq 'āmm' and 'rizq khāṣṣ'.
2
Examine the definitions of each type
Juwayni defines general provision as what Allah deposited in the earth sufficient for all people. Specific provision is tied to human effort: 'according to the effort is the provision.'
3
Review the Quranic evidence
For general provision he cites 'He who made the earth subservient to you'; for specific provision, 'So walk in its pathways and eat of His provision.' Notice how both are from the same verse (Qur'an 67:15).
The verse connects divine provision with human action.
4
Reflect on the purpose of the distinction
Ask yourself: Why does Juwayni emphasize this distinction? How does it balance divine sovereignty and human responsibility?
This distinction guards against fatalism and encourages effort.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وكان أسبابه يجب أن يفرق بين الرزق العام والرزق الخاص. فإن الله تكفل بالأرزاق، فأودع في الأرض ما يكفي للناس. وهذا هو الرزق العام. وأمر الناس بالبحث والسعي والعمل، وعلى قدر الجهد يكون الرزق. وهذا هو الرزق الخاص. وعن الرزق العام يقول تعالى: {الذي جعل لكم الأرض ذلولا}، وعن الرزق الخاص يقول تعالى عقب القول السابق: {فامشوا في مناكبها وكلوا من رزقه} (الملك: 15). والله تعالى وعد الأمة البارة بزيادة الأرزاق والبركة فيها، وأوعد الأمة الفاجرة بنقص الأرزاق ونزع البركة منها في أكثر آية من آي القرآن، منها قوله تعالى: {ولو أن أهل القرى آمنوا واتقوا لفتحنا عليهم بركات من السماء والأرض ولكن كذبوا فأخذناهم بما كانوا يكسبون} (الأعراف: 96).
And its causes require that one distinguish between general provision (rizq 'āmm) and specific provision (rizq khāṣṣ). For Allah undertakes to provide sustenance, so He deposited in the earth what suffices for people. This is the general provision. And He commanded people to search, strive, and work, and according to the effort is the provision. This is the specific provision. Concerning the general provision, He says: {He who made the earth subservient to you}. And concerning the specific provision, He says following the previous statement: {So walk in its pathways and eat of His provision} (Qur'an 67:15). And Allah Almighty promised the righteous nation an increase in provisions and blessing in them, and threatened the sinful nation with a decrease in provisions and removal of blessing from them in most verses of the Qur'an, including His statement: {And if the people of the towns had believed and feared Allah, We would have opened upon them blessings from the heaven and the earth, but they denied, so We seized them for what they used to earn} (Qur'an 7:96).
Source pages p. 120

Commentary
This passage is a classification and scriptural proof regarding provision (rizq). Juwayni argues that it is necessary to distinguish between two types of rizq: general (ʿāmm) and specific (khāṣṣ). General provision is what Allah has deposited in the earth sufficient for all people—this is the basic sustenance provided by Allah without individual effort. Specific provision, however, depends on human effort: Allah commands people to search, strive, and work, and the amount of rizq is proportional to their effort. He supports this with two Quranic verses from Surah al-Mulk: the first part (verse 15) mentions Allah making the earth subservient (general provision), and the second part commands walking in its pathways and eating of His provision (specific provision). Finally, Juwayni adds that Allah has promised increase and blessing in rizq to the righteous nation and threatened decrease and removal of blessing to the sinful nation, citing the verse from Surah al-A'raf (7:96) as evidence.

Key Terms
رزق عام (rizq 'āmm)
رزق خاص (rizq khāṣṣ)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think provision is entirely fixed without human effort.
Correction: Juwayni shows that while general provision is guaranteed, specific provision depends on effort, balancing divine decree and human responsibility.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what determines the amount of specific provision?
A) It is fixed for everyone equally.
B) It is proportional to one's effort.
C) It depends only on divine will.
D) It is based on social status.

Key Takeaway
Allah provides general sustenance for all, but specific provision is tied to individual effort.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have just learned that Allah has already deposited enough provision in the earth for everyone. Yet you may still feel anxious about your income or future. What does this anxiety reveal about your trust in Allah's promise of general provision? Will you today repent for that lack of trust and actively rely on Him?
?
The specific provision is tied to your effort. Think of one area of your life where you have been lazy or reckless, expecting provision without striving. What is one concrete step you will take this week to seek provision as commanded in the verse?
?
The verse combines 'walk in its pathways' with 'eat of His provision'. This shows that your action is the means to receive what Allah has already decreed. In what specific way are you currently avoiding the 'pathways' that lead to your sustenance? What would it cost you to step out in obedience?

The Effect of Righteousness and Sin on Provision
Juwayni connects provision to righteousness and sin with Quranic evidence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's teaching that Allah grants increase and blessing in provision to the righteous and threatens the sinful with decrease and removal of blessing, as supported by the Quran.

Core Idea
Righteousness brings baraka in provision, while sin causes its loss.

Having established the two categories of provision, Juwayni now connects them to moral conduct. He asserts that divine provision is not static; it responds to human belief and piety. The Quran repeatedly promises increase for the righteous and warns of decrease for the sinful.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage about promise and threat
Read the Arabic passage and translation. Juwayni states that Allah has promised the righteous nation an increase in provisions and blessing, and threatened the sinful nation with decrease and removal of blessing.
Note the two key terms: 'al-umma al-bārra' (righteous nation) and 'al-umma al-fājira' (sinful nation).
2
Identify the two outcomes
For the righteous: increase in provisions and baraka. For the sinful: decrease in provisions and removal of baraka. Understand that baraka is a divine blessing that makes little go a long way.
3
Examine the Quranic evidence
Juwayni cites Qur'an 7:96 as an example: 'And if the people of the towns had believed and feared Allah, We would have opened upon them blessings from the heaven and the earth.' Notice the conditional: faith and piety lead to blessings; denial leads to punishment.
This verse illustrates the general principle with a specific case.
4
Reflect on the moral dimension of provision
Consider how this teaching encourages righteousness and deters sin. It shows that provision is not merely a matter of effort but also of divine response to moral conduct.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وكان أسبابه يجب أن يفرق بين الرزق العام والرزق الخاص. فإن الله تكفل بالأرزاق، فأودع في الأرض ما يكفي للناس. وهذا هو الرزق العام. وأمر الناس بالبحث والسعي والعمل، وعلى قدر الجهد يكون الرزق. وهذا هو الرزق الخاص. وعن الرزق العام يقول تعالى: {الذي جعل لكم الأرض ذلولا}، وعن الرزق الخاص يقول تعالى عقب القول السابق: {فامشوا في مناكبها وكلوا من رزقه} (الملك: 15). والله تعالى وعد الأمة البارة بزيادة الأرزاق والبركة فيها، وأوعد الأمة الفاجرة بنقص الأرزاق ونزع البركة منها في أكثر آية من آي القرآن، منها قوله تعالى: {ولو أن أهل القرى آمنوا واتقوا لفتحنا عليهم بركات من السماء والأرض ولكن كذبوا فأخذناهم بما كانوا يكسبون} (الأعراف: 96).
And its causes require that one distinguish between general provision (rizq 'āmm) and specific provision (rizq khāṣṣ). For Allah undertakes to provide sustenance, so He deposited in the earth what suffices for people. This is the general provision. And He commanded people to search, strive, and work, and according to the effort is the provision. This is the specific provision. Concerning the general provision, He says: {He who made the earth subservient to you}. And concerning the specific provision, He says following the previous statement: {So walk in its pathways and eat of His provision} (Qur'an 67:15). And Allah Almighty promised the righteous nation an increase in provisions and blessing in them, and threatened the sinful nation with a decrease in provisions and removal of blessing from them in most verses of the Qur'an, including His statement: {And if the people of the towns had believed and feared Allah, We would have opened upon them blessings from the heaven and the earth, but they denied, so We seized them for what they used to earn} (Qur'an 7:96).
Source pages p. 120

Commentary
This passage is a classification and scriptural proof regarding provision (rizq). Juwayni argues that it is necessary to distinguish between two types of rizq: general (ʿāmm) and specific (khāṣṣ). General provision is what Allah has deposited in the earth sufficient for all people—this is the basic sustenance provided by Allah without individual effort. Specific provision, however, depends on human effort: Allah commands people to search, strive, and work, and the amount of rizq is proportional to their effort. He supports this with two Quranic verses from Surah al-Mulk: the first part (verse 15) mentions Allah making the earth subservient (general provision), and the second part commands walking in its pathways and eating of His provision (specific provision). Finally, Juwayni adds that Allah has promised increase and blessing in rizq to the righteous nation and threatened decrease and removal of blessing to the sinful nation, citing the verse from Surah al-A'raf (7:96) as evidence.

Key Terms
بركة (baraka)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think provision is solely a matter of effort and is unaffected by faith or sin.
Correction: Juwayni shows that righteousness leads to increase and blessing, while sin leads to decrease and removal of blessing.

Checkpoint
What does Juwayni cite as evidence that sin decreases provision?
A) Qur'an 67:15
B) Qur'an 7:96
C) A hadith
D) A rational argument

Divine provision increases through faith and piety and decreases through disbelief and sin.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you examine your own life, do you see a correlation between your recent acts of disobedience and a lack of baraka in your provision? If not, could it be that you have become desensitized to the blessing? Commit this week to track one area of your income or sustenance and specifically repent from a sin you have normalized, then watch for changes.
?
Are you living as if Allah's promise in this verse is true for you personally? If you truly believed that your sins decrease your provision, what sin would you immediately abandon? Name that sin and resolve today to leave it, expecting Allah to open blessings from heavens and earth.
?
Have you reduced Allah's blessings to only material wealth? The verse speaks of blessings 'from the heaven and the earth'—what spiritual blessings (like peace, contentment, guidance) might you be missing due to a lack of taqwa? Make a list of non-material provisions you need and ask Allah for them while working on your faith and piety.

Method of Learning

4 min
Rational Obligations: Necessary and Theoretical
Juwayni classifies rational obligations into necessary and theoretical and states their rule.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's classification of rational obligations into necessary (dharuri) and theoretical (nazari) and the foundational principle that whatever reason judges as necessarily existent cannot be negated, and whatever it judges as impossible must be avoided.

Core Idea
Rational obligations are the unshakable premises from which all religious rulings branch.

Juwayni begins his discussion of rational obligations by distinguishing two fundamental categories: those that are self-evident (necessary) and those that require reasoning (theoretical). He then lays down a decisive principle: whatever reason affirms as necessary cannot be denied, and whatever reason deems impossible must be avoided. This passage establishes the bedrock of all subsequent theological arguments.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the English translation to grasp the flow of Juwayni's argument.
Notice the concise style and the use of examples.
2
Identify the two categories of rational obligations
Find where Juwayni divides rational obligations into necessary and theoretical. Note the example he gives for each.
The necessary example is about the maker being powerful; the theoretical example is about the inventor knowing details.
3
Understand the principle of necessary existence and impossibility
Examine the sentence: 'Whatever reason judges its existence as necessary, its non-existence is impossible; and whatever reason implies the impossibility of its existence, its avoidance is obligatory.' This is the core logical rule.
This principle underlies all subsequent proofs.
4
Reflect on why these are called premises
Juwayni calls these 'premises' and says no rational person disputes them. Consider how they serve as the foundation for religious rulings.
He promises to arrange the rulings in chapters later.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأما الواجبات العقلية ، فمثال الضرورى منها : العلم بأن صانع الشيء وموجده يجب أن يكون قادرا على فعله ، إلى غير ذلك . النظرى منها : العلم بأن مخترع الأشياء ، يجب أن يكون عالما بتفاصيل أفعاله - كما سيأتى شرح ذلك ، إن شاء الله عز وجل - وما قضى العقل بوجوب ثبوته استحال انتفاؤه ، وما تضمن نظر العقل استحالة ثبوته ، وجب اقتفاؤه . فهذه مقدمات . لا يتمارى فيها عاقل ، غير ذاهل عن سنن السداد [؟] قواعد الدين تتشعب عن هذه القضايا العقلية ، على ما سنرتبها أبوابا ، مستعينين بالله عز وجل ، وهو خير معين .
As for the rational obligations, an example of the necessary among them is: knowledge that the maker and originator of a thing must be powerful over its act, and so forth. The theoretical among them is: knowledge that the inventor of things must be knowledgeable of the details of His acts - as its explanation will come, if God Almighty wills. And whatever reason judges its existence as necessary, its non-existence is impossible; and whatever reason's consideration implies the impossibility of its existence, its avoidance is obligatory. These are premises. No rational person, not heedless of the path of rectitude, disputes them. The rules of religion branch out from these rational judgments, according to how we will arrange them in chapters, seeking help from God Almighty, and He is the best Helper.
Source pages p. 15

Commentary
This passage is a classification and explanation of rational obligations (wajibat aqliyya). Juwayni first distinguishes two categories: necessary (dharuri) and theoretical (nazari). The necessary rational obligation is illustrated by the knowledge that a thing's maker must be powerful over his act; this is self-evident. The theoretical rational obligation requires reasoning, such as knowing that the inventor of all things must know the details of His actions. He then states a key principle: whatever reason judges as necessarily existent cannot be negated, and whatever reason judges as necessarily non-existent must be avoided. These are foundational premises (muqaddimat) that no rational person disputes. Finally, he asserts that all rules of religion branch out from these rational judgments, and he will arrange them in chapters.

Key Terms
الواجبات العقلية
الضروري
النظري
وجب ثبوته
استحال ثبوته
اقتفاء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think rational obligations are optional or merely recommendations.
Correction: Juwayni emphasizes they are foundational and indisputable premises that no rational person denies. They are the bedrock of religious rulings.

Checkpoint
What is the example Juwayni gives for a necessary rational obligation?
A) The inventor must know the details of His acts
B) The maker of a thing must be powerful over its act
C) The world is temporally originated
D) The Creator is one

Key Takeaway
Rational obligations are the undeniable premises from which all religious rulings derive, and they are either self-evident (necessary) or require reasoning (theoretical).

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly live as if you know that God must be powerful, or do you fall into despair when you face challenges beyond your control? If this rational obligation is indisputable, why does your panic contradict it? What one change can you make today to submit your anxieties to His power?
?
Juwayni says that whatever reason judges as necessary cannot be negated. Have you ever negated God's all-encompassing knowledge by doing something in secret that you would never do in front of people? If you truly accept that He knows every detail, what would you have to confess and change right now?
?
The lesson calls these rational obligations 'premises that no rational person disputes.' Yet many Muslims practice religion without ever grounding it in these certainties. Is your faith built on intellectual conviction or just inherited custom? What is one step you will take this week to consciously build your belief on these unshakable rational foundations?

Method of Learning
