The Three Main Chapters of Kalam
Juwayni enumerates the three chapters of kalam.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three main chapters that constitute the science of kalam according to Imam Juwayni.

Core Idea
Kalam is structured around three essential chapters: God, human obligation, and prophecy.

Imam Juwayni provides a clear methodological outline for the entire discipline of kalam. After establishing that the world is created, he explains that the subject matter is summarized in three main chapters: knowledge of God, the basis of human obligation, and prophethood. These three chapters form the backbone of theological reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once to get the overall classification. Then read the translation.
Notice Juwayni's concise style.
2
Identify each chapter
Identify the three chapters: knowledge of God, basis of obligation, and prophethood. Note what each covers.
3
Understand the connection to scriptural matters
See how the third chapter (prophethood) is linked to scriptural matters like resurrection and reward/punishment.
These are called sam'iyyat.
4
Reflect on the classification's purpose
Ask yourself why Juwayni begins with this classification. It shows the systematic nature of kalam.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مل في ترتيب تراجم العقائد بعد تمهيد حدث العالم المحصول الكلام بعد ذكره ، تحصره ثلاثة أبواب ، ثم ينقسم كل باب فصولا ، باب فى العلم بأحوال الاله ، وباب في مناط التكليف من صفات العباد ، وباب فى النبوات التى تنصل الأوامر التكليفية بالعباد ، وبها ترتبط الأمور السمعية فى الحشر والنشر والوعد والوعيد ، المفسرين (۱) بالثواب والعقاب الى غيرهما مما أنبأ عنه المرسلون ، وأخبر عنه الصادقون ، وتنتجز قواعد الدين بنجاز هذه الأبواب ، ثم الامامة ليست العقائد ، ولو غفل عنها المرء لم تضره ولكن جرى الرسم باختتام علم التوحيد بها ، ونحن نذكر منها طرفا (۳) _ ان شاء الله مع ايثار الاختصار و الاقتصار على ما فيه مقنع وبلاغ ، يشفى الغليل ويوضح السبيل . ان  شاء الله عز وجل
[مل] On the arrangement of the chapters of creeds after establishing the temporal creation of the world: the product of kalam after mentioning it is summarized in three chapters. Then each chapter is divided into sections. A chapter on knowledge of the states of God, a chapter on the basis of obligation from the attributes of servants, and a chapter on prophethoods that convey the obligatory commands to servants. To them are connected the scriptural matters concerning resurrection, dispersal, promise, and threat, interpreted (1) as reward and punishment and others, which the messengers announced and the truthful ones reported. The foundations of religion are completed by the completion of these chapters. Then imamate is not part of the creeds; if one neglects it, it does not harm him, but the custom has been to conclude the science of tawhid with it. We will mention a portion of it (3) – God willing – while preferring brevity and confining ourselves to what is sufficient and effective, which quenches the thirst and clarifies the path. God willing, Almighty and Glorious.
Source pages p. 19

Commentary
This passage is a methodological classification of the subject matter of kalam (ʿilm al-kalām). After establishing the temporal creation of the world (ḥudūth al-ʿālam), Juwayni states that the entire subject is summarized in three main chapters (abwāb): (1) knowledge of the states of God (al-ʿilm bi-aḥwāl al-ilāh), i.e., divine attributes and acts; (2) the basis of obligation (manāṭ al-taklīf) concerning the attributes of servants, i.e., human actions and moral responsibility; and (3) prophethood (nubuwwāt) which conveys the obligatory commands to servants. Connected to these chapters are the scriptural matters (umūr samʿiyya) like resurrection, reward and punishment. Juwayni then adds a note: imamate (imāma) is not part of the fundamental creeds (al-ʿaqāʾid), and neglecting it does not harm one's faith, but by custom the science of tawḥīd is concluded with it. He promises to mention something about it briefly.

Key Terms
أبواب
علم بأحوال الإله
مناط التكليف
نبوات
سمعيات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, how many main chapters does the subject of kalam have after establishing the world's createdness?
A) Two
B) Three
C) Four
D) Five

Key Takeaway
The three chapters of kalam cover God's attributes, human obligation, and prophethood, with scriptural details falling under the third.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If kalam is divided into three essential chapters—God, human obligation, and prophethood—which of these areas have you neglected in your own journey of faith? What concrete step will you take this week to address that imbalance?
?
Juwayni says the foundations of religion are completed by these three chapters. Are there areas of your belief where you rely on inherited assumptions rather than the structured understanding kalam provides? How would your life change if you pursued that knowledge seriously?
?
The passage ties scriptural matters like resurrection and reward to the chapter on prophethood. Do you treat the unseen realities (heaven, hell, judgment) as integral to the message of the prophets, or do you push them to the margins? How does your answer affect how you live today?

The Status of Imamate in Sunni Creed
Juwayni clarifies that imamate is not part of creed.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Imam Juwayni states that imamate is not part of the fundamental creeds but is included by custom.

Core Idea
Imamate is not a core creedal matter; neglecting it does not harm one's faith.

After completing the main chapters of kalam, Juwayni addresses the topic of imamate (leadership). He makes a significant methodological point: imamate is not part of the genuine creeds (al-ʿaqāʾid), and one who neglects it is not harmed in their faith. However, he notes that it has become customary to include it at the end of works on tawḥīd. This lesson explores that distinction.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on imamate
Read the Arabic passage that begins with 'ثم الامامة ليست من العقائد' and its translation.
2
Identify Juwayni's key claim
Notice that Juwayni says imamate is 'laysat min al-ʿaqāʾid' (not from the creeds) and neglect does not harm.
3
Understand the custom
Juwayni adds that by custom (jarā al-rasm) the science of tawḥīd concludes with imamate. This explains why it appears in creedal texts.
4
Consider the implication
Reflect on why this distinction matters: imamate is important but not foundational to personal faith.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

The Three Chapters of Kalam and the Place of Imamah
Juwayni enumerates the three chapters of his book and clarifies the status of imamah.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three main chapters of kalam as outlined by Imam Juwayni, and why the imamate is considered a customary appendix rather than a creedal article.

Core Idea
Kalam's core is three chapters: Divinity, obligation, prophethood; imamate is appended by custom.

Before diving into detailed arguments, Imam Juwayni gives us a map of his book. He tells us what the main topics are and what is merely customary. This orienting passage helps us see the big picture and avoid mistaking a traditional addition for a necessary article of faith.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and its translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the English translation to get the overall meaning. Notice how Juwayni structures his announcement.
The Arabic is dense; let the translation guide you first.
2
Identify the three main chapters
Juwayni lists three 'doors' (abwab) that contain the entire product of kalam. Find each one in the text and note what each covers.
The third chapter includes the sam'iyyat (matters known only through revelation).
3
Understand the role of imamah
Juwayni explicitly says the imamate is not part of the creeds and its ignorance does not harm one. But then he says custom concludes tawhid with it. Reflect on why he includes it despite this disclaimer.
This disclaimer is important for understanding the scope of necessary belief.
4
Connect to the author's method
Notice Juwayni's desire for brevity and sufficiency. He promises to cover imamate briefly. Ask yourself why a scholar would emphasize conciseness.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
في ترتيب تراجم العقائد بعد تمهيد حدث العالم المحصول الكلام بعد ذكره ، تحصره ثلاثة أبواب ، ثم ينقسم كل باب فصولا ، باب فى العلم بأحوال الاله ، وباب في مناط التكليف من صفات العباد ، وباب فى النبوات التى تنصل الأوامر التكليفية بالعباد ، وبها ترتبط الأمور السمعية فى الحشر والنشر والوعد والوعيد ، المفسرين بالثواب والعقاب الى غيرهما مما أنبأ عنه المرسلون ، وأخبر عنه الصادقون ، وتنتجز قواعد الدين بنجاز هذه الأبواب ، ثم الامامة ليست العقائد ، ولو غفل عنها المرء لم تضره ولكن جرى الرسم باختتام علم التوحيد بها ، ونحن نذكر منها طرفا ان شاء الله مع ايثار الاختصار و الاقتصار على ما فيه مقنع وبلاغ ، يشفى الغليل ويوضح السبيل . ان شاء الله عز وجل
On the arrangement of the chapters of creeds after the preamble on the temporality of the world. The product of kalam after mentioning it is encompassed by three chapters, then each chapter divides into sections: a chapter on knowledge of the states of the Divine, a chapter on the basis of obligation from the attributes of servants, and a chapter on prophethoods which connect the obligatory commands to the servants, and with them are connected the sam'iyyat matters regarding resurrection, spreading, promise and threat, interpreted as reward and punishment and other things which the messengers informed about and the truthful ones reported, and the foundations of religion are concluded with the completion of these chapters. Then the imamate is not part of the creeds, and if one neglects it, it does not harm him, but the custom has been to conclude the science of tawhid with it, and we will mention a part of it, if God wills, with preference for brevity and limiting to what is satisfying and sufficient, that quenches the thirst and clarifies the way, if God Almighty wills.
Source pages p. 19

Commentary
This passage describes the structural outline of the book after the initial topic of the temporality of the world. Juwayni states that the content of kalam is encompassed by three main chapters: (1) on the attributes and states of the Divine, (2) on the basis of moral obligation from the attributes of human servants, and (3) on prophethoods which convey the obligatory commands. The third chapter also includes the sam'iyyat matters (matters known only through revelation) such as resurrection, spreading (al-nashr), and the promise and threat of reward and punishment. The completion of these chapters concludes the foundations of religion. Juwayni then clarifies that the topic of imamate (leadership) is not a part of the original creeds (aqa'id), and neglecting it does not harm a person's faith. However, customarily it is appended to conclude the science of tawhid. He promises to mention a portion of it briefly and concisely, aiming for what is satisfying and sufficient.

مناط التكليف
النبوّات
السّمعيات
الحشر
النشر
الوعد والوعيد
الثواب والعقاب
الإمامة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that belief in the imamate is as essential as belief in God or prophethood.
Correction: Juwayni explicitly states that imamate is not part of the creeds (laysat min al-'aqa'id) and neglect of it does not harm one's faith. It is appended by custom, not necessity.

Checkpoint

A) A chapter on knowledge of the states of the Divine
B) A chapter on the basis of obligation from the attributes of servants
C) A chapter on the imamate
D) A chapter on prophethoods that connect the obligatory commands

Key Takeaway
Juwayni's book is structured around three essential chapters on God, obligation, and prophethood, while the imamate is a customary addition, not a foundational creedal topic.

The Three Rational Categories for Divine Attributes
Juwayni divides the discourse on divine matters into three categories.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three rational categories (impossible, necessary, possible) that form the framework for discussing divine attributes in Islamic theology.

Core Idea
All divine attributes fall into three rational categories: impossible, necessary, possible.

Juwayni begins his substantive discussion by outlining the fundamental framework for analysing divine attributes. He divides the entire discourse on God into three rational categories. This classification is the bedrock of Islamic theological methodology, as all divine matters can be reduced to these three categories.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic text once without analysis. Let Juwayni's words set the stage.
Notice the clear tripartite division using 'قسم: في'.
2
Identify the three categories
Locate the three sections: what is impossible for God, what is necessary for God, and what is possible in His rulings.
These are not temporal but rational-moral categories.
3
Understand the concluding statement
Read the last sentence: all perceptions of divine matters refer back to these three categories. Reflect on its significance.
This shows the comprehensiveness of the classification.
4
Reflect on why this matters
Ask yourself: How does this classification help organize theological discussions? Why begin with rational categories?
It provides a universal logical framework for kalam.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فالكلام بعده ينقسم ثلاثة أقسام. قسم: في ذكر ما يستحيل على الله سبحانه. وقسم: فيما يجب على الله سبحانه. وقسم في ما يجوز في أحكامه. فجميع مدارك الإلهيات ترجع إلى الاستحالة والوجوب والجواز فيما سبق في صدر هذا.
The discourse after it divides into three sections: a section on mentioning what is impossible (mustaḥīl) for God exalted; a section on what is necessary (wājib) for God exalted; and a section on what is possible (jā’iz) in His rulings. For all the perceptions of divine matters refer to impossibility, necessity, and possibility in what preceded in this [introduction].
Source pages p. 20

Commentary
This passage is a classification of the topics in the study of divine matters (al-ilāhiyyāt). Juwayni states that after establishing the introduction, the discourse divides into three sections: (1) attributes that are impossible for God, (2) attributes that are necessary for Him, and (3) attributes that are possible for Him. All rulings concerning God can be reduced to these three categories.

Key Terms
مستحيل (mustaḥīl)
واجب (wājib)
جائز (jā’iz)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: These categories are temporal or historical phases in God's existence.
Correction: The categories are logical and rational, not temporal. They describe what is always impossible, always necessary, or possibly applicable to God.

Checkpoint
What are the three categories into which the discourse on divine matters is divided?
A) Eternal, temporal, possible.
B) Impossible, necessary, possible.
C) Existence, non-existence, possibility.
D) Substance, accident, attribute.

Key Takeaway
Juwayni establishes that all theological statements about God can be classified as rationally impossible, necessary, or possible.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Which of these three categories—impossible, necessary, or possible—do you most often neglect when thinking about God? How will you correct that this week?
?
Do you truly believe that God has the freedom to act as He wills (the possible category) in your own life? Where do you struggle to accept that possibility, and what does that reveal about your trust in Him?

Two Paths for Ambiguous Texts: Ta'wil or Tafwid
Juwayni presents the differing methods for interpreting ambiguous texts and endorses the salaf's way.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the two major approaches (ta'wil and tafwid) to ambiguous Quranic and hadith texts, and Juwayni's argument for following the salaf's method.

Core Idea
With ambiguous texts, follow the salaf: accept and delegate meaning to Allah.

Juwayni addresses a crucial methodological question: how should Muslims handle verses and hadiths that seem to imply problematic attributes of Allah? Scholars split into two camps: those who interpret (ta'wil) and those who refrain (tafwid). Juwayni clearly sides with the early generations (salaf) and provides a decisive proof. This lesson unpacks his reasoning and clarifies the key term 'zahir'.

Guided Path
1
Recognize the problem
Understand that certain texts cannot be taken literally in matters of Allah's attributes, creating a need for a method.
2
Identify the two approaches
Juwayni mentions two: ta'wil (allegorical interpretation) and tafwid (delegation to Allah). Notice how he presents each without yet endorsing.
3
See Juwayni's endorsement
He explicitly states he approves following the salaf, i.e., tafwid. This is his chosen position.
4
Examine the proof
He cites ijma' (consensus) of the Ummah as a binding proof, and notes that the Companions practiced tafwid.
The Companions are the elite of Islam, so their practice carries weight.
5
Clarify 'zahir'
The footnote distinguishes the term here from the technical usuli term; it means 'common usage' not 'apparent meaning'.
This prevents a common misunderstanding.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فصل
وقد اختلفت مسالك العلماء فى الظواهر التي وردت في الكتاب والسنة ، وامتنع على أهل الحق اعتقاد فحواها . واجراؤها على موجب ما تبتدره أفهام أرباب اللسان منها ، فرأى بعضهم تأويلها والتزام هذا المنهج في آى الكتاب ، وما يصح من سنن الرسول صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم وذهب أئمة لسلف الى الانكفاف عن التأويل ، واجراء الظواهر على مواردها وتفويض معانيها الى الرب تعالى . والذي ترتضيه رأيا : وندین الله به عقلا : اتباع سلف الأمة . فالأولى الاتباع ، وترك الابتداع والدليل السمعى القاطع فى ذلك : أن اجماع الأمة حجة متبعة ، وهو مستند معظم الشريعة . وقد درج صحب رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم ، ورضى عنهم على ترك التعرض لمعانيها ودرك ما فيها ، وهم صفوة الاسلام ، والمستقلون . [والمراد بالظاهر هنا ما استفيض في السنة إطلاقه على الله سبحانه نطلقه عليه جل شانه من غير خوض في المعنى فيما نوع ابهام. والظاهر هنا يقابل الغريب. كما في قول مالك : « خير العلم الظاهر . وشره الغريب » وليس المراد هنا : الظاهر الذي هو من اقسام الوضوح ، لأنه أعم من أن يكون رجحان احمد الاحتمالين على الآخر بالوضع أو بالدليل ، ولا ظهور في جانب الوضع اذا ناقضه البرهان ، فلا يكون هناك ظهور بهذا المعنى ، حتى يحمل عليه راجع تمهيد أبى الخطاب ]
Section: The approaches of scholars have differed regarding the apparent meanings (zawahir) that have come in the Book and the Sunnah, which it is impossible for the people of truth to believe in their literal import and to apply them according to what the understandings of the people of language would initially suggest. Some of them see ta'wil (interpretation) and adhering to this method in the verses of the Book and whatever is authentic of the Sunnah of the Messenger. The imams of the salaf (predecessors) went for refraining from ta'wil, letting the apparent meanings pass as they came, and delegating their meanings to the Lord Almighty. What we approve as an opinion and hold as our religion before Allah is to follow the salaf of the Ummah. So the best course is following (ittiba') and avoiding innovation (ibtida'). The decisive textual proof in that is that the consensus (ijma') of the Ummah is a binding proof, and it is the foundation of most of the Shari'ah. And the Companions of the Messenger of Allah, may Allah be pleased with them, proceeded upon refraining from delving into their meanings and attaining their realities, and they are the elite of Islam and the ones who bore the burden. [What is meant by 'zahir' here is that which is commonly used in the Sunnah as an attribute of Allah; we apply it to Him without delving into the meaning in a way that implies ambiguity. And 'zahir' here is the opposite of 'gharib' (uncommon). As in Malik's saying: 'The best knowledge is the zahir (common), and the worst is the gharib (strange).' It is not meant here the 'zahir' which is one of the categories of clarity (in usul), because it is more general than being a preponderance of one of two probabilities over the other by context or evidence, and there is no 'zuhur' (apparentness) on the side of context if proof contradicts it, so there is no 'zahir' in that sense, as it is explained in the Tamhid of Abu al-Khattab.]
Source pages p. 32

Commentary
This passage addresses a key methodological issue in theology: how to handle verses and hadiths that contain anthropomorphic expressions or apparently problematic descriptions of Allah. Juwayni explains that scholars differed: some adopted ta'wil (allegorical interpretation) to harmonize these texts with reason. Others, the salaf (early Muslims), refrained from ta'wil and instead accepted the texts as they came while delegating their true meaning to Allah (tafwid). Juwayni states his own position: he approves following the salaf, i.e., ittiba' (following) and avoiding ibtida' (innovation). He provides a decisive textual proof: the consensus (ijma') of the Ummah is a binding proof, and it is the basis of most of Shari'ah. Moreover, the Companions themselves practiced leaving aside the meanings of these ambiguous texts and did not delve into them. They were the best of the Ummah. The footnote clarifies that the term 'zahir' here does not mean the technical term from usul al-fiqh (apparent meaning with predominance), but rather it means 'common' and 'widespread' as opposed to 'gharib' (rare). This guards against misunderstanding: the salaf are not denying that these texts have a meaning; they simply refrain from specifying it.

Key Terms
ظواهر
تأويل
سلف
اتباع
ابتداع
إجماع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The term 'zahir' in this passage means the technical usuli term: the apparent meaning that is preponderant over other possibilities.
Correction: The footnote clarifies that 'zahir' here means 'common' or 'widespread' usage, not the technical term from usul al-fiqh. It is the opposite of 'gharib' (rare).

Checkpoint
What is the decisive textual proof Juwayni gives for following the salaf's approach?
A) The consensus of the Ummah
B) The sayings of the Companions
C) The Quranic verse about delegation
D) The authority of the imams of the salaf

Key Takeaway
Juwayni endorses following the salaf in delegating the meaning of ambiguous texts to Allah, citing the consensus of the Ummah and the practice of the Companions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you encounter an attribute of Allah that your mind struggles to grasp, do you feel restless until you find an interpretive explanation? Or can you sincerely say, like the salaf, 'We believe in it, and we delegate the true meaning to You, O Allah'? What does your reaction reveal about your reliance on revelation versus reason?
?
Juwayni says that the best course is 'following (ittiba') and avoiding innovation (ibtida').' Is there an area of your religious practice where you have innovated something that contradicts the way of the salaf—perhaps in how you understand Allah's names and attributes, or in a ritual? Will you repent from it today, or do you justify it as needed?
?
The Companions, whom Juwayni calls 'the elite of Islam,' refrained from delving into the meanings of ambiguous texts. Do you truly trust that their restraint was a virtue, or do you feel you are more in need of 'understanding' than they were? What would it cost you to adopt their humility before Allah?

Method of Learning
intermediate
5 min
Juwayni on the Salaf's Method for Ambiguous Verses
Juwayni presents two approaches to ambiguous texts and argues for following the salaf's tafwid.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Juwayni argues that the Salaf's method of tafwid (consigning the meaning to Allah) is the correct approach to ambiguous verses, and how he uses consensus (ijma') as the proof.

Core Idea
The Salaf's silence on ambiguous texts is the strongest evidence for tafwid.

Juwayni presents a foundational methodological issue: how should Muslims approach verses and hadith whose apparent meanings seem to imply anthropomorphism? He outlines two scholarly paths and then argues decisively for the way of the Salaf. The key proof he offers is not rational but scriptural: the consensus of the early community, whose practice becomes a binding precedent.

Guided Path
1
Identify the two approaches
Read the passage and note the two methods described: some scholars perform ta'wil (interpretation), while the Salaf refrain from ta'wil and consign the meaning to Allah (tafwid).
Juwayni states that the apparent meanings are impossible for the people of truth to believe literally.
2
Understand Juwayni's choice
Juwayni declares his own position: he follows the imams of the Salaf and considers that the better course is to follow and avoid innovation.
He says: 'What we choose as an opinion, and by which we worship Allah in mind, is following the imams of the Salaf.'
3
Examine the proof from consensus
Juwayni gives the categorical scriptural proof: the consensus (ijma') of the ummah. He explains that the Companions and Followers refrained from ta'wil, despite being the most knowledgeable. If ta'wil were required, they would have done it.
Their unanimous practice across generations is decisive evidence.
4
Reflect on the implication for the believer
Juwayni concludes that it is obligatory to affirm Allah's transcendence and not delve into ta'wil, but to consign the meaning to Allah. He also references the verse about 'none knows its interpretation except Allah' and the example of Imam Malik.
This sets a clear methodology for dealing with ambiguous texts.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فصل وقد اختلفت مسالك العلماء فى الظواهر التي وردت في الكتاب والسنة ، وامتنع على أهل الحق اعتقاد فحواها . واجراؤها على موجب ما تبتدره أفهام أرباب اللسان منها ، فرأى بعضهم تأويلها والتزام هذا المنهج في آى الكتاب ، وما يصح من سنن الرسول صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم وذهب أئمة السلف الى الانكفاف عن التأويل ، واجراء الظواهر على مواردها وتفويض معانيها الى الرب تعالى . والذي ترتضيه رأيا : وندين الله به عقلا : اتباع أئمة السلف . فالأولى الاتباع ، وترك الابتداع والدليل السمعى القاطع فى ذلك : أن اجماع الأمة حجة متبعة ، وهو مستند معظم الشريعة . وقد درج صحب رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم ، ورضى عنهم على ترك التعرض لمعانيها ودرك ما فيها ، وهم صفوة الاسلام ، والمستقلون بأعباء الشريعة . وكانوا لا يألون جهدا فى ضبط قواعد الملة . والتواصى يحفظها ، وتعليم الناس ما يحتاجون اليه منها . فلو كان تأويل هذه الآبى والظواهر مسوغا ، ومحتما ، لأوشك أن يكون اهتمامهم بها فوق اهتمامهم بفروع الشريعة . واذا انصرم عصرهم ، وعصر التابعين على الاضراب عن التأويل كان ذلك قاطعا بأنه الوجه المتبع . فحق على ذي دين : أن تتنزه الباري عن صفات المحدثين، ولا يخوض في تأويل المشكلات ، ويكل معناها الى الرب تبارك وتعالى وعد أمام القراء وسيدهم : الوقوف على قوله تبارك وتعالى : « وما يعلم تأويلة الا الله » من العزائم ، ثم الابتداء : « والراسخون في العلم » ( آل عمران ) ومما استحسن من كلام امام دار الهجرة رضى الله عنه : وهو : مالك بن أنس رضى الله عنه . أنه سئل عن قوله تبارك وتعالى : ( الرحمن على العرش استوى » ( طه ه ) فقال :
Section: And indeed the approaches of the scholars have differed regarding the apparent meanings (al-ẓawāhir) that occur in the Book and the Sunnah, and it is impossible for the people of truth to believe their apparent import. And their application according to what the understandings of the masters of the language grasp from them. Some of them saw [the approach of] interpreting them (ta’wīl) and adhering to this methodology in the verses of the Book and what is authentic of the traditions of the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family). And the imams of the Salaf went to refraining from interpretation (ta’wīl), and applying the apparent meanings to their occurrences and consigning (tafwīḍ) their meanings to the Lord, exalted. What we choose as an opinion, and by which we worship Allah in mind, is following the imams of the Salaf. So the better course is following and abandoning innovation. The categorical scriptural proof (al-dalīl al-sam’ī al-qāṭi‘) for that is that the consensus (ijmā‘) of the ummah is an authoritative proof (ḥujja muttabi‘a), and it is the foundation of most of the Sharia. And the Companions of the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him), may Allah be pleased with them, proceeded upon refraining from addressing their meanings and grasping what is in them, while they are the elite of Islam and the ones independently undertaking the burdens of the Sharia. They did not spare any effort in preserving the principles of the religion, and recommending its preservation, and teaching people what they need from it. For if the interpretation of these verses and apparent meanings were permissible and determined, it would have been likely that their concern for them would be above their concern for the secondary branches of the Sharia. And when their era and the era of the Followers passed upon refraining from interpretation, that is decisive that it is the followed course. So it is due upon one possessed of religion that the Creator be exalted above the attributes of originated beings, and not to delve into the interpretation of problematic texts, and to consign their meaning to the Lord, blessed and exalted. And the leader of the reciters and their master [said]: Stopping at His saying, blessed and exalted: “And none knows its interpretation except Allah” is among the firm resolutions, then beginning [with] “and those firmly grounded in knowledge” (Āl ‘Imrān). And among what is deemed good from the speech of the Imam of the Abode of Hijra, may Allah be pleased with him, namely Mālik ibn Anas, may Allah be pleased with him, that he was asked about His saying, blessed and exalted: “The Most Gracious established Himself upon the Throne” (Ṭā Hā), so he said:
Source pages p. 32

Commentary
This passage presents Juwayni’s discussion of the divergent approaches of scholars to the ambiguous (mutashābih) verses and hadiths. He first describes the dilemma: the apparent meanings (ẓawāhir) that occur in the Quran and Sunnah are such that it is impossible for the people of truth to believe them literally (because they would imply anthropomorphism). Some scholars have chosen the path of ta’wīl (interpretation) to reconcile these texts with reason. Conversely, the imams of the Salaf (the early pious predecessors) opted for tafwīḍ (consigning the meaning to Allah) while affirming the wording. Juwayni states his personal conviction: he follows the Salaf and worship Allah through that. He then gives the proof: the categorical scriptural evidence is the consensus (ijmā‘) of the ummah, which is an authoritative foundation. He elaborates that the Companions and the Followers refrained from engaging in ta’wīl, despite being the most knowledgeable and the ones responsible for preserving the religion. If ta’wīl were permissible and required, they would have been the first to engage in it. Since they did not, their practice is decisive evidence that refraining is the correct path. Hence, it is incumbent on a believer to affirm Allah’s transcendence (tanzīh) and not delve into ta’wīl, but to consign the meaning to Allah. Finally, Juwayni mentions a statement from the leader of reciters (possibly a qirā’a authority) about the verse “None knows its interpretation except Allah” being a stopping point, and then quotes the example of Imam Mālik who, when asked about the verse “al-Raḥmān ‘alā al-‘arshi istawā,” gave a reply that is not fully recorded in the text.

Key Terms
الظواهر
التأويل
التفويض
السلف
الإجماع
الراسخون في العلم
التنزيه

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That the Salaf's approach is mere literalism or irrational submission.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies it is based on the authoritative proof of ijma‘ and the example of the most knowledgeable community.

Checkpoint
What is the primary scriptural proof (dalīl sam‘ī) Juwayni gives for following the Salaf’s approach of refraining from ta’wīl?
A) A specific verse from the Quran
B) The consensus (ijmā‘) of the ummah
C) A hadith from the Prophet
D) A rational demonstration

Key Takeaway
Juwayni establishes that the Salaf's practice of refraining from ta'wil and consigning meaning to Allah is a binding precedent for all believers, supported by the consensus of the earliest generations.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you come across a verse like 'The Most Gracious established Himself upon the Throne,' do you feel a need to explain it rationally, or are you content to say 'as He wills'? What does your reaction reveal about your trust in the Salaf’s method?
?
Juwayni says the Companions refrained from ta'wil despite being the most capable. Is there any area of your religious understanding where you cling to your own reasoning over what the early generations practiced? Why?
?
The proof for tafwid is the consensus of the ummah. Have you ever examined your own beliefs to see if they align with the consensus of the early community, or have you adopted interpretations that contradict their silence? What would it cost you to surrender your need for a 'rational' explanation?

Method of Learning
beginner
6 min
The Salaf's Method of Affirming Attributes without Ta'wil
Juwayni argues that Salaf's refraining from ta'wil proves it is the correct path, citing Imam Malik.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Salaf's method of affirming Allah's attributes without ta'wil (interpretive departure from apparent meaning), as argued by Imam Juwayni and exemplified by Imam Malik's response on istiwa'.

Core Idea
The Salaf affirmed attributes while avoiding ta'wil and asking 'how'.

Imam Juwayni presents a key proof for the Salaf's approach: the Companions and Successors refrained from ta'wil of ambiguous verses, and their unanimous practice is decisive. He then illustrates this method through Imam Malik's famous words on istiwa': we affirm the attribute, we do not know its modality, and asking about it is innovation. This lesson unpacks that argument and its application to other attributes.

Guided Path
1
Understand Juwayni's argument from Salaf practice
Read the first part of the passage where Juwayni argues that if ta'wil were permissible, the Salaf would have been more concerned with it than branches of Shari'a. Their refraining is a decisive proof.
Notice the logical structure: if ta'wil were allowed, they would have done it; they didn't, so it's not the correct path.
2
Examine Imam Malik's statement on istiwa'
Read the famous quote from Imam Malik: 'al-istiwa' is known, the modality is unknown, and asking about it is innovation.' Reflect on how this encapsulates the Salaf method.
Affirmation of the attribute without delving into 'how' is key.
3
Apply the principle to other verses and hadith
See how Juwayni extends the same principle to the verses about hands, face, eyes, and the hadith of descent. Each is to be affirmed without ta'wil or modality.
This shows the method is consistent across all ambiguous texts.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ولو كان تأويل هذه الآبي والظواهر مسوغا، ومحتوما، لأوشك أن يكون اهتمامهم بها فوق اهتمامهم بفروع الشريعة. وإذا انصرم عصرهم، وعصر التابعين على الاضراب عن التأويل كان ذلك قاطعا بأنه الوجه المتبع. فحق على ذي دين: أن تتنزه الباري عن صفات المحدثين، ولا يخوض في تأويل المشكلات، ويكل معناها الى الرب تبارك وتعالى. وعد [أمام؟] القراء وسيدهم: الوقوف على قوله تبارك وتعالى: (وما يعلم تأويله إلا الله) من العزائم، ثم الابتداء: (والراسخون في العلم) [آل عمران]. وما استحسن من كلام امام دار الهجرة رضى الله عنه وهو مالك بن أنس رضى الله عنه: أنه سئل عن قوله تبارك وتعالى: (الرحمن على العرش استوى) [طه:5] فقال: الاستواء معلوم. والكيفية مجهولة. والسؤال عنه بدعة. فلتجرى آية الاستواء والمجيء، وقوله: (لما خلقت بيدي) [ص:75] و (يغى وجه ربك) [الرحمن:27] وقوله: (تجري بأعيننا) [القمر:14] وما صح من أخبار الرسول صلى الله عليه وسلم كخبر النزول وغيره، على ما ذكرناه. فهذا بيان ما يجب لله تبارك وتعالى.
And if the interpretation (ta'wil) of these verses and apparent meanings were permissible and obligatory, it would likely be that their concern with them would be above their concern with the branches of the Shari'a. And when their era and the era of the Followers passed while they refrained from ta'wil, that is decisive that it is the path to be followed. So it is the duty of a religious person: that the Creator be declared transcendent above the attributes of created beings, and he does not delve into the ta'wil of problematic texts, and consigns their meaning to the Lord, Blessed and Exalted. And the Imam promised the readers and their master: stopping at His saying, Blessed and Exalted: 'And none knows its interpretation except Allah' [Al 'Imran 3:7] as a firm resolve, then beginning: 'And those firmly grounded in knowledge...' And what is deemed good from the speech of the Imam of the Abode of Hijrah, may Allah be pleased with him, and he is Malik ibn Anas, may Allah be pleased with him: that he was asked about His saying, Blessed and Exalted: 'The Most Merciful settled over the Throne' [Ta-Ha 20:5], so he said: 'The settling (al-istwa') is known, the modality (al-kayfiyya) is unknown, and asking about it is innovation (bid'a).' So let the verse of al-istwa', and the coming, and His saying: 'When I created with My hands' [Sad 38:75], and 'the Face of your Lord remains' [al-Rahman 55:27], and His saying: 'Sailing under Our eyes' [al-Qamar 54:14], and whatever is authentically reported from the hadith of the Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him, such as the hadith of descent and others, proceed upon what we have mentioned. So this is an explanation of what is obligatory concerning Allah, Blessed and Exalted.
Source pages p. 33

Commentary
This passage is a proof from the practice of the Salaf (the early righteous predecessors) for the correct method of dealing with ambiguous Quranic verses and hadith about Allah's attributes. The author begins by stating that the companions (al-sahaba) and the followers (al-tabi'un) did not engage in ta'wil (interpretation that departs from the apparent meaning). If ta'wil were permissible, they would have been more concerned with it than with the branches of Shari'a, but they were not. Therefore, their refraining is a decisive proof that the correct path is to refrain. Then the author affirms that it is the duty of a religious person to declare Allah transcendent above the attributes of created beings, and not to delve into ta'wil of problematic texts, but to consign their meaning to Allah. Then he mentions the verse 'And none knows its interpretation except Allah' (Al 'Imran 3:7) and says that stopping at 'except Allah' is the firm resolve, and then beginning with 'and those firmly grounded in knowledge' – this refers to the well-known recitation where one stops at 'illa Allah' and then starts 'wa al-rasikhuna fi al-ilm' (i.e., they say 'and the firmly grounded in knowledge say "we believe in it"'). This was the practice of Imam Malik. Then he quotes Imam Malik's famous response when asked about istiwa': 'The istiwa' is known, its how is unknown, and asking about it is innovation.' This encapsulates the Salaf method: affirm the attribute (al-istiwa' is known) but negate any modality (al-kayfiyya majhula) and forbid speculative questions. The author then extends this principle to other verses like 'When I created with My hands', 'the Face of your Lord', 'sail under Our eyes', and hadith like the descent (nuzul). Finally, he concludes that this is the explanation of what is obligatory regarding Allah.

Key Terms
تأويل
استواء
كيفية
سلف
تفويض
بدعة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the Salaf's tafwid means they did not understand the verses at all, leaving them meaningless.
Correction: The Salaf affirmed the meaning (e.g., istiwa' is known) while denying modality; they understood the lexical meaning but consigned the true nature to Allah.

Checkpoint
According to Imam Malik, what is the ruling on asking about the modality (kayfiyya) of Allah's istiwa'?
A) Permissible
B) Recommended
C) Innovation (bid'a)
D) Obligatory

Key Takeaway
The Salaf's method is to affirm the attributes as revealed, without ta'wil, without asking how, and to consign the true nature to Allah.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read verses about Allah's hand, face, or His settling over the Throne, does your mind instinctively try to picture or explain 'how'? How does Imam Malik's statement challenge that impulse? Will you commit to stopping yourself from seeking the 'how' and instead say 'I believe, and I entrust the true reality to Allah'?
?
The Salaf were more concerned with branches of Shari'a than with interpreting ambiguous attributes. Is your own religious focus similarly balanced? Are you more eager to debate theological details than to act on clear commands like prayer and charity? What will you shift today?
?
Imagine you are asked by someone, 'What does Allah's hand mean?' How would you answer using the Salaf's method? Does that answer feel unsatisfying to your desire for certainty? Can you surrender that desire and accept that not knowing the modality is part of faith? Will you trust Allah's wisdom more than your own need to know?

The Authority of Consensus of the Early Muslims
Juwayni states the second reason and defends the consensus of the early community.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's argument for the authority of the consensus of the early Muslim community and why he prioritizes it over later consensus.

Core Idea
The early community's consensus is a binding proof because the Ummah is infallible.

In this passage, Juwayni presents the second reason supporting his theological claims: the unanimous practice of the early Muslims. He argues that their devotion to supplication is known with certainty, and denying it is obstinate. Building on the principle that the Ummah does not agree on error, he clarifies that the authoritative consensus is that of the early generations, not later ones fraught with division.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the literal translation to understand the flow of the argument.
Notice the short, direct style of Juwayni.
2
Identify the key claim
The passage states the 'second reason'. Find what that reason is: certain knowledge of the early community's practice.
3
Understand the proof structure
Juwayni combines two elements: the undeniable reality of the early Muslims' supplication and the principle that the Ummah is infallible. Together they establish the argument.
The infallibility of the Ummah is a foundational Ash'ari principle.
4
Note the distinction on consensus
Juwayni clarifies he does not claim consensus after disagreement has appeared, only the prior consensus of the salaf. This is a key methodological point.
This distinguishes his approach from claiming consensus in later periods of division.
5
Reflect on significance
Consider why Juwayni limits consensus to the early generations. How does this strengthen his argument?
The early consensus is considered purer and more reliable.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والثاني : أن تعلم قطعات علم من لا يتمارى أن الأولين كانوا مبتهلين إلى الله تبارك وتعالى في سؤال المرء ، وبه ابتهالهم إليه في سؤال كل ممكن من ثواب أو مغفرة . ومن جحد هذا فهو معاند والأمة معصومة لا تجتمع على الضلالة ، ولسنا ندعى الاجماع مع ظهور الخلاف الآن . ولكنا ندعى تقدم الاجماع من سلف الأمة قبل ظهور الآراء ، واختلاف الأهواء . فذلك ما أردناه في هذا الفصل ، وقد نجز بانتهاء هذا الفصل : غرضنا من هذا المعتقد في أقسام الأحكام الالهية
And the second: that you know definitely, with a knowledge not doubted, that the early ones were devotedly supplicating to God the Blessed and Exalted in asking for rain, and by their supplication to Him in asking for every possible thing of reward or forgiveness. And whoever denies this is obstinate, and the Ummah is infallible, not agreeing upon error. And we do not claim consensus while disagreement has appeared now. Rather, we claim the previous consensus of the predecessors of the Ummah before the emergence of opinions and the divergence of desires. That is what we intended in this chapter, and it has been completed by the conclusion of this chapter: our purpose from this treatise on the divisions of divine judgments.
Source pages p. 39

Commentary
This passage is an argument from consensus (ijma') of the early Muslim community. Juwayni presents this as the second reason (in a series) for a claim. He states that it is known with certainty that the early Muslims (al-awwalin) were devoted to supplication to God for every possible thing, whether reward or forgiveness. Whoever denies this is obstinate (mu'anid). The basis of this argument is that the Ummah (the Muslim community) is infallible (ma'suma) and does not agree upon error. Juwayni clarifies that he is not claiming consensus after disagreement has appeared (i.e., in later times), but rather he claims the prior consensus of the early generations (salaf al-ummah) before the emergence of different opinions and desires. This is a typical Ash'ari use of the concept of consensus as a proof, specifically the consensus of the Companions and early generations. He then concludes that this is what he intended in this chapter, and with this the chapter ends, stating that his purpose in this treatise is about the divisions of divine judgments (aqsam al-ahkam al-ilahiyya).

Key Terms
إجماع (ijma')
معصومة (ma'suma)
سلف (salaf)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Juwayni rejects all consensus after the early generations.
Correction: He clarifies that he is not claiming consensus now (in his time) but only the prior consensus of the salaf before opinions diverged. He does not reject later consensus entirely, but prioritizes the early one.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, why is the denial of the early Muslims' devotion considered obstinacy?
A) Because they were many
B) Because the Ummah is infallible
C) Because it is a rational proof
D) Because of revelation

Key Takeaway
Juwayni argues that the consensus of the early Muslim community is authoritative because the Ummah is infallible and their practice is known with certainty.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly trust the consensus of the early Muslims as authoritative in your own life? When you encounter a practice that was common among the Salaf but is now disputed, do you submit to their consensus or follow your own reasoning? This lesson confronts you with the claim that the Ummah is infallible—are you willing to let that belief override your personal opinions and desires?
?
How often do you supplicate to Allah with the same devotion as the early Muslims described by Juwayni? He argues that their devotion is known with certainty—what does that say about your own level of commitment in du'a'? Are you content with a shallow prayer life while knowing that the first generations poured out their hearts in every need? What will you change today to align your supplication with theirs?
?
Juwayni warns that denying the consensus of the early community is obstinacy (mu'anid). Is there an area where you have resisted a clear teaching from the Salaf because it is uncomfortable or inconvenient? Will you examine your heart for any trace of stubbornness and repent from it, embracing their consensus even when it challenges your own desires?

The Placement of the Proof of Unity Within the Three Categories
Juwayni responds to three objections about classifying the proof of unity

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Juwayni treats the proof of divine unity separately from the three rational categories of necessary, impossible, and possible.

Core Idea
Divine unity is a negation, not a positive attribute, so it does not fit neatly in the three categories.

Juwayni has just explained the three rational judgments—necessary, impossible, and possible—as applied to God. Now he faces a natural question: where does the proof that God is one fit into this scheme? In this passage, he anticipates three objections and responds to each, revealing that unity is fundamentally a negation of any equal, not a positive attribute. This distinction is crucial for understanding the structure of Islamic theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the question-and-answer structure. There are three objections, each beginning with 'فان قيل'.
Pay attention to how Juwayni responds to each challenge.
2
Identify the three objections
Find each objection: (1) why include unity in the three categories at all? (2) why not place it under necessary? (3) why not under impossible? Juwayni's answers are distinct.
3
Analyze Juwayni's reasoning
For each answer, note the key point: (1) the question of a second director falls outside the intended organization; (2) unity reduces to negation, not a positive attribute; (3) estimation of a second is not a divine attribute.
The third answer emphasizes that not every impossible thing needs mention.
4
Reflect on the importance of arrangement
Juwayni concludes that the secrets of rational matters depend on sound arrangement. Consider why he defends his ordering so firmly.
This shows his commitment to systematic theology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فضل فى الوحدانية . فان قيل لم تدرجوا اثبات الوحدانية في قسم من الأقسام الثلاثة ؟ قلنا : ذكرنا ما يجب الله تبارك وتعالى ويستحيل عليه ، ويجوز في حكمه فالسؤال عن تقدير مدير ثان يقع وراء الضبط المقصود . فانسل هذا الفضل المقصود عن ترتيب المعتقد، ونحن نذكر فيه بعد هذا التنبيه ما يستقل به اللبيب ، اذا وقف معانيه . فان قيل : هلا رتبتم هذا الفصل على ما يجب الله تعالى ، فابن الوحدانية صفته الواجبة ؟ قلنا : محصول الوجدانية يؤول الى نفى من مستوى الواحد فليست صفة ثابتة . فان قيل : فهلا ألحقتم القول في ذلك بما يستحيل فان تقدير الثاني محال ؟ قلنا : نجن ضمنا هذا الفصل ما يستحيل في صفات الاله ، ولم يلزم أن نذكر كل محال . وليس تقدير الثاني متعلقا بصفة الاله الحق ، وسبيل من انتهى الى هذا الموضع ألا يتبرم بترديد القول في الترتيب .. فإن أسرار المعقولات تتلقى من سداد ترتيبها ، وقد حان بعد ذلك أن نذكر معتمدا وجيزا فى الوحدانية ، فيشفي غلة الصدور، وينفس عن كل مصدور
A chapter on unity. For it is said: Why did you include the proof of unity within one of the three categories? We said: We mentioned what is necessary for God the Blessed and Exalted, what is impossible for Him, and what is possible in His judgment. So the question about the estimation of a second director falls beyond the intended organization. So this intended chapter slipped from the arrangement of the treatise, and we will mention in it after this reminder what the intelligent person stands alone in understanding, if he grasps its meanings. For it is said: Why did you not arrange this chapter according to what is necessary for God, since the proof of unity is His necessary attribute? We said: The outcome of unity reduces to negating the equal of the One, so it is not a positive attribute. For it is said: Then why did you not attach the discussion of that to what is impossible, since the estimation of a second is impossible? We said: We included in this chapter what is impossible in God's attributes, but it is not necessary to mention every impossible thing. And the estimation of a second is not related to the attribute of the True God. And the way of one who reaches this point is not to be annoyed by the repetition of speech about arrangement. Indeed, the secrets of rational matters are received from the soundness of their arrangement. And now after that it is time to mention a concise and reliable summary on unity, which will cure the thirst of breasts and relieve every distressed person.
Source pages p. 39

Commentary
This passage is a series of objections and answers regarding the classification of the proof of divine unity (al-wahdaniyya) within the three categories of necessary, possible, and impossible that were introduced earlier in the treatise. The author, Juwayni, addresses why the proof of unity was not placed as a necessary attribute, or as an impossible thing, but rather was treated separately. The first objection asks why the proof of unity was included under one of the three categories. Juwayni responds that they have already mentioned what is necessary, impossible, and possible for God, and the question about the estimation of a second (i.e., a second god) falls outside the intended scope. The chapter on unity slipped from the planned arrangement, but he will mention in it what the intelligent person can grasp. The second objection asks why it was not placed under what is necessary for God, since unity is His necessary attribute. Juwayni answers that the essence of unity is the negation of any equal to the One, so it is not a positive attribute. The third objection asks why it was not attached to the category of the impossible, since the existence of a second is impossible. Juwayni replies that they included in this chapter what is impossible in God's attributes, but it is not necessary to mention every impossible thing; moreover, the estimation of a second is not related to an attribute of the true God. He then advises the reader not to be vexed by the repetition about arrangement, because the secrets of rational matters are received from the soundness of their arrangement. Finally, he promises to give a concise and reliable summary on unity that will satisfy the seeker.

Key Terms
وحدانية
تقدير ثان
محصول

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think that divine unity is a positive attribute like power or knowledge, and thus should be categorized under 'necessary' for God.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies that unity (al-wahdaniyya) is not a positive attribute; its meaning is the negation of any equal to God. Hence it does not fit under the category of necessary attributes.

Checkpoint
Why does Juwayni say the proof of unity is not a necessary attribute of God?
A) Because it is impossible
B) Because it reduces to a negation
C) Because it is possible
D) Because it is an action

Key Takeaway
Divine unity is fundamentally a negation of any equal, which is why it does not neatly fall under the categories of necessary, impossible, or possible as usually defined.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you consider the oneness of God, have you ever felt that it is just a fact to be accepted, or have you wrestled with the reality that it means there is absolutely no equal to Him? How might understanding this as a negation change the way you speak about God and worship Him?
?
Juwayni says 'the secrets of rational matters are received from the soundness of their arrangement.' In your own spiritual life, are you patient enough to learn the proper order of truths, or do you rush to conclusions without sound reasoning? What arrangement in your beliefs might need reordering?
?
Reflect on a time when you assumed something about God's attributes was simple or neatly categorized. Have you ever reduced a profound truth to a box? How can you, like Juwayni, embrace the complexity and let it deepen your reliance on sound knowledge rather than convenience?

The Scope of Impossibilities in Divine Attributes
Juwayni responds to an objection about not listing all impossibilities.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why theological proofs focus on relevant impossibilities rather than enumerating every conceptual impossibility.

Core Idea
Theologians need not list every impossibility — only those relevant to the matter at hand.

In this brief exchange, a hypothetical objector challenges Juwayni's method for not including all impossibilities outright. Juwayni clarifies a key principle of theological reasoning: one is only obligated to address impossibilities directly pertaining to the subject, not to exhaustively catalogue every absurdity. This lesson unpacks that methodological stance.

Guided Path
1
Read the Objection
Read the first part of the passage: the objector asks why the author did not simply include a general impossibility like 'positing a second deity is impossible.'
Notice the phrase 'تقدير الثاني محال' (positing a second is impossible).
2
Understand the Implicit Criticism
The objector implies that a complete proof should mention all impossibilities, or at least the most obvious ones. Reflect on why this might seem necessary to some.
The criticism is about comprehensiveness.
3
Read Juwayni's Answer
Read the answer: he has already included what is impossible in the attributes of God, and he is not required to mention every impossibility.
Focus on 'لم يلزم أن نذكر كل محال' (it is not necessary to mention every impossibility).
4
Extract the Methodological Principle
Juwayni is asserting a principle of relevance: a theological proof should address impossibilities specific to its subject, not all conceivable impossibilities. Consider why this is a reasonable approach.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فان قيل : فهلا ألحقتم القول في ذلك بما يستحيل فان تقدير الثاني محال ؟ قلنا : نحن ضمنّا هذا الفصل ما يستحيل في صفات الإله ، ولم يلزم أن نذكر كل محال .
If it is said: 'Why did you not include the statement concerning that among what is impossible, for positing a second is impossible?' We said: We have included in this chapter what is impossible in the attributes of God, and it is not necessary that we mention every impossibility.
Source pages p. 40

Commentary
This passage is an objection-and-answer unit. The objector questions why the author did not simply declare the impossibility of a second deity outright, as a general impossibility. The author replies that he has already covered the relevant impossibilities specific to the attributes of God in this chapter. He is not obliged to list every single impossibility; the chapter's scope is focused on the divine attributes.

Key Terms
محال
تقدير الثاني

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: A valid theological proof must list every conceivable impossibility.
Correction: Juwayni shows that only impossibilities directly relevant to the subject (here, divine attributes) need to be mentioned. Listing every impossibility is neither required nor practical.

Checkpoint
Why does Juwayni say he did not mention every impossibility in his argument?
A) He forgot to include them.
B) He only included impossibilities related to divine attributes in this chapter.
C) He thought all impossibilities are irrelevant.
D) He was pressed for time.

Key Takeaway
Juwayni teaches that theological proofs are sufficient when they address the impossibilities relevant to the subject; there is no need to list every conceivable impossibility.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you defend a belief, do you feel pressured to address every possible objection, even far-fetched ones? How can Juwayni's principle free you from that burden and sharpen your focus?
?
Think of a time you argued about Islam: did you get sidetracked by hypothetical impossibilities? Will you now commit to staying within the relevant scope of the discussion?

Juwayni's Method: Tafwid and Rational Obligation
Al-Juwayni states his method: combining divine delegation with rational grounding of obligation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's methodology that harmonizes delegating all affairs to Allah with rationally grounding religious obligations.

Core Idea
Juwayni reconciles absolute divine decree with rational obligation.

In this passage, Imam al-Juwayni lays out his distinctive theological method. He insists on two principles that might seem contradictory: complete delegation of all matters to God, and the rational grounding of religious duties. By doing so, he avoids both fatalism and rationalist autonomy. This introduction sets the stage for his approach to obligation and divine attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then read the English translation. Pay attention to the key terms.
2
Identify the two combined elements
Find the two things Juwayni says he has combined. Notice how he uses 'bayna... wa-bayna' (between... and).
The two elements are tafwid (delegation) and tanzih/taklif (rational grounding of obligation).
3
Reflect on the implications
Consider how this combination points to God's free choice (mukhtar) and why Juwayni says it is undeniable. Also note his caution about sophistry.
This shows the Ash'ari via media between jabr and tafwid.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فقد جمعت - الله مولانا - بين تفويض الأمور كلها نفعها وضرها ، خيرها وشرها الى الاله جلت قدرته ، وبين تنقية حقائق التكليف ، وتقرير قواعد الشرائع على الوجه المعقول ، ألست فى هذا أيضا تدل على أنه مختار ، فلا يكون وراء انكار هذا الأمر الوجداني المقطوع به . والحكم الشرعى البات غاية حميدة ، وفكرة سديدة . الا ان البشر لا يخلو من مسفسط فى أجلى البديهيات بحيث يكسوه كسوة أعوص العويصات (ز)
So our Lord has gathered between delegating all affairs, their benefit and harm, their good and evil, to God whose power is exalted, and between purifying the realities of obligation and establishing the rules of laws on a rational basis. Do you not see that this also indicates that he is free? So there is no way to deny this evident experiential matter and the decisive legal ruling. It is a praiseworthy goal and a sound idea. But mankind is not free from a sophist regarding the clearest of axioms, to the extent that he clothes it in the garb of the most obscure of obscurities.
Source pages p. 52

Commentary
Juwayni outlines his theological methodology. He rejects both tafwīḍ (delegation that denies divine causality) and jabr (compulsion). Instead, he affirms that all affairs are from God, yet obligation is rationally grounded. This is the Ash'ari via media. He notes that even clear matters are subject to sophistry.

Key Terms
تفويض
تكليف
الوجه المعقول
وجداني
حكم شرعي بات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That tafwid (delegation to God) and rational grounding of obligation are incompatible.
Correction: Juwayni sees them as complementary: all affairs are from God, yet obligations are rationally grounded, reflecting God's free choice.

Checkpoint
What two things does Juwayni say he has combined?
A) Free will and predestination
B) Delegation of all matters to God and rational grounding of obligation
C) Reason and revelation
D) God's mercy and justice

Key Takeaway
Juwayni's theological method harmonizes absolute divine decree and rational obligation, showing that God's free choice underlies both.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you face a situation where you feel you have no control, do you truly delegate it to God, or do you still secretly believe your planning determines the outcome? What would change if you lived out tafwid consistently today?
?
Do you ever rationalize moral obligations in a way that makes you feel secure while forgetting that even that rational structure is a gift from a free God? How can you remind yourself that your reasoning itself depends on His decree?
?
Is there a sophistry in your heart that obscures the clear truth that God is absolutely free and in control? What specific belief or habit covers that truth with 'the garb of the most obscure obscurities'? Commit to exposing it.

Method of Learning
beginner
4 min
Reason Cannot Perceive Obligation on God
Juwayni states that reason cannot obligate God, and explains the role of divine command.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that, according to Juwayni, reason cannot impose obligation on God, but once God commands, reason urges compliance.

Core Idea
Reason does not obligate God; obligation flows from divine command alone.

Juwayni begins by clarifying a fundamental point: our intellect cannot deduce any obligation upon God. However, when God Himself chooses to command and threaten, reason then prompts us to obey. This sets the stage for his larger argument about the necessity of revelation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic and English translation carefully. Notice the clear contrast between reason's inability to obligate God and its role after divine command.
2
Identify the key claim
Juwayni states 'obligation upon Him is not perceived by the intellect.' This is the central point.
3
Consider the consequence
Reflect: if reason cannot obligate God, then morality and obligation must come from revelation. Juwayni uses this to justify the need for prophets.
This connects to the next chapter on prophethood.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ولا يدرك بالعقل وجوب عليه . ولكن اذا أراد الرب الزام عبيده شيئا . أمرهم وتوعدهم على ترك المأمور فتستحثهم عقولهم على اجتناب المحذور , فان وعد الله حق , ووعيده صدق , وقد تتبعت العلوم العقلية فى كل فن جهدى فما وجدت طائفة من ذوى العقول حائدين بالكلية عن مسلك جلى , من مسالك العقول . ولكنهم يبتدرون القاعدة . ثم قد بزلون عن التفاصيل وهذا كما أن افتقار المتغيرات الى مدير , لما كان من كليات العقول لم ينكره أحد ولكنهم اختلفوا في صفة المدبر فسماه بعض العقلاء : الطبع . وبعضهم : العقل الكلى . الى خبط لا أشغل به قريحة مولانا , ثم استبد الموفقون لمنهج الحق ومن طال نظره في المقليات تبين له: أن مثار خلاف العقلاء ايل الى التفاصيل , دون الأصول . والغرض من هذا التشبيه أن النفوس مجبولة على طلب المحبوب , وتوقى المحذور , ومصير القدرية الى ذلك غير مستنكر أصلا . ولكنهم لم يحسنوا تفصيله فزلواء ونحن جميعا بين اعتباره , وبين تنزيه الرب سبحانه عن النفع والضر . كما جرى في معرض هو أوضح من فلق الصبح لفاهمه . واذا نجز القول في أحكام الربوبية وصفات العبودية, واستبان أن مدرك التكاليف , موقوف على ورود الشرائع , فقد حان الآن : أن نوضح أن مدرك الشرائع : التلقى من الرسل والأنبياء عليهم السلام , وهو الباب الثالث من أبواب العقيدة والله الموفق
And obligation upon Him is not perceived by the intellect. But when the Lord wishes to oblige His servants with something, He commands them and threatens them upon abandoning the commanded, so their intellects urge them to avoid the prohibited. For the promise of God is true, and His threat is true. And I have examined the rational sciences in every field with my utmost effort, and I have not found a single group of intelligent people entirely deviating from a clear path among the paths of the intellect. Rather, they proceed from the principle. Then they disagree about the details. And this is like the fact that the need of changing things for a director, since it is among the universal [principles] of the intellect, no one denied it, but they differed concerning the attribute of the director. Some of the intelligent called it 'nature', and some called it 'the universal intellect', into a confusion with which I will not occupy the mind of our master. Then those guided to the path of truth succeeded. And whoever extensively examines the universals [or: the rational matters] realizes that the source of disagreement among rational people leads back to details, not to fundamentals. And the purpose of this analogy is that souls are naturally disposed to seek the beloved and avoid the harmful, and the position of the Qadariyya regarding that is not at all objectionable originally. But they did not perfect its detail, so they slipped. And we are all between its consideration and between declaring the Lord transcendent above benefit and harm. As it occurred in a context clearer than the crack of dawn to one who understands it. And when speech is concluded concerning the judgments of lordship and the attributes of servitude, and it has become evident that the source of obligations is dependent on the advent of revealed laws, now it is time that we clarify that the source of revealed laws is receiving from messengers and prophets, peace be upon them. And this is the third chapter of the chapters of creed. And God is the Grantor of success.
Source pages p. 60

Commentary
This passage is a transitional conclusion summarizing the limitations of reason and the necessity of revelation. Juwayni begins by stating that obligation upon God is not perceived by reason, but once God commands and threatens, reason urges compliance. He then reflects on his study of rational sciences, noting that all rational people agree on fundamental principles but differ in details, like the need for a director of changing things (God) while differing on His nature. He uses this to argue that disagreements among intelligent people are in details, not fundamentals. He then applies this to the Qadariyya (free will advocates), whose basic position (seeking benefit and avoiding harm) is not objectionable, but they mishandled the details. Finally, he concludes that since obligations depend on revealed laws, he will now turn to the third chapter on prophethood, i.e., the source of revealed laws is the messengers.

Key Terms
التكاليف
الشرائع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think reason alone can determine moral obligation on God and humans.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies that no obligation is perceived by reason upon God; obligation arises only from divine command. Reason's role is to urge compliance after the command is given.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni in this passage, what is the role of reason regarding obligation?
A) Reason perceives obligation upon God.
B) Reason cannot perceive obligation upon God, but urges compliance after divine command.
C) Reason creates obligation independently.
D) Reason rejects all obligation.

Key Takeaway
Reason does not obligate God; obligation is established by divine command, and reason then prompts obedience.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever act as if God owes you something because you think it's reasonable? How does this passage challenge that assumption?
?
If obligation comes only from God's command, are you treating His commands with the urgency they deserve, or do you weigh them against your own reasoning?
?
Since reason cannot obligate God, what does that mean for how you approach divine commands: do you accept them on His authority alone, or do you wait until they seem rational to you?

Method of Learning
intermediate
5 min
All Rational People Agree on Fundamentals, Differ on Details
Juwayni discusses universal agreement on principles and disagreement on details, with the director example.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's observation that all rational minds agree on universal principles but diverge in detailed applications, and see this through the example of the need for a director of the universe.

Core Idea
Universal principles are agreed upon; disagreements are only in details.

Juwayni shares a key methodological insight from his study of rational sciences: he found no group of rational people entirely straying from the clear paths of reason. All accept fundamental principles, but they differ when applying them to specific details. He illustrates this with the example of the universe's need for a director, which no one denies, yet opinions vary on who or what that director is.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic and translation. Note the confident claim about studying rational sciences.
2
Identify the universal principle
Juwayni gives the example: 'the need of changing things for a director' is a universal principle accepted by all.
3
Observe the disagreement on details
Some call the director 'nature', some 'universal intellect', etc. Juwayni dismisses the confusion, but notes that those guided to truth succeeded.
4
Understand the lesson
Juwayni concludes that the source of disagreement among rational people is in details, not fundamentals. This supports his broader argument that revelation clarifies the details.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ولا يدرك بالعقل وجوب عليه . ولكن اذا أراد الرب الزام عبيده شيئا . أمرهم وتوعدهم على ترك المأمور فتستحثهم عقولهم على اجتناب المحذور , فان وعد الله حق , ووعيده صدق , وقد تتبعت العلوم العقلية فى كل فن جهدى فما وجدت طائفة من ذوى العقول حائدين بالكلية عن مسلك جلى , من مسالك العقول . ولكنهم يبتدرون القاعدة . ثم قد بزلون عن التفاصيل وهذا كما أن افتقار المتغيرات الى مدير , لما كان من كليات العقول لم ينكره أحد ولكنهم اختلفوا في صفة المدبر فسماه بعض العقلاء : الطبع . وبعضهم : العقل الكلى . الى خبط لا أشغل به قريحة مولانا , ثم استبد الموفقون لمنهج الحق ومن طال نظره في المقليات تبين له: أن مثار خلاف العقلاء ايل الى التفاصيل , دون الأصول . والغرض من هذا التشبيه أن النفوس مجبولة على طلب المحبوب , وتوقى المحذور , ومصير القدرية الى ذلك غير مستنكر أصلا . ولكنهم لم يحسنوا تفصيله فزلواء ونحن جميعا بين اعتباره , وبين تنزيه الرب سبحانه عن النفع والضر . كما جرى في معرض هو أوضح من فلق الصبح لفاهمه . واذا نجز القول في أحكام الربوبية وصفات العبودية, واستبان أن مدرك التكاليف , موقوف على ورود الشرائع , فقد حان الآن : أن نوضح أن مدرك الشرائع : التلقى من الرسل والأنبياء عليهم السلام , وهو الباب الثالث من أبواب العقيدة والله الموفق
And obligation upon Him is not perceived by the intellect. But when the Lord wishes to oblige His servants with something, He commands them and threatens them upon abandoning the commanded, so their intellects urge them to avoid the prohibited. For the promise of God is true, and His threat is true. And I have examined the rational sciences in every field with my utmost effort, and I have not found a single group of intelligent people entirely deviating from a clear path among the paths of the intellect. Rather, they proceed from the principle. Then they disagree about the details. And this is like the fact that the need of changing things for a director, since it is among the universal [principles] of the intellect, no one denied it, but they differed concerning the attribute of the director. Some of the intelligent called it 'nature', and some called it 'the universal intellect', into a confusion with which I will not occupy the mind of our master. Then those guided to the path of truth succeeded. And whoever extensively examines the universals [or: the rational matters] realizes that the source of disagreement among rational people leads back to details, not to fundamentals. And the purpose of this analogy is that souls are naturally disposed to seek the beloved and avoid the harmful, and the position of the Qadariyya regarding that is not at all objectionable originally. But they did not perfect its detail, so they slipped. And we are all between its consideration and between declaring the Lord transcendent above benefit and harm. As it occurred in a context clearer than the crack of dawn to one who understands it. And when speech is concluded concerning the judgments of lordship and the attributes of servitude, and it has become evident that the source of obligations is dependent on the advent of revealed laws, now it is time that we clarify that the source of revealed laws is receiving from messengers and prophets, peace be upon them. And this is the third chapter of the chapters of creed. And God is the Grantor of success.
Source pages p. 60

Commentary
This passage is a transitional conclusion summarizing the limitations of reason and the necessity of revelation. Juwayni begins by stating that obligation upon God is not perceived by reason, but once God commands and threatens, reason urges compliance. He then reflects on his study of rational sciences, noting that all rational people agree on fundamental principles but differ in details, like the need for a director of changing things (God) while differing on His nature. He uses this to argue that disagreements among intelligent people are in details, not fundamentals. He then applies this to the Qadariyya (free will advocates), whose basic position (seeking benefit and avoiding harm) is not objectionable, but they mishandled the details. Finally, he concludes that since obligations depend on revealed laws, he will now turn to the third chapter on prophethood, i.e., the source of revealed laws is the messengers.

Key Terms
كليات العقول
المقليات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
What does Juwayni say about the source of disagreement among rational people?
A) They disagree on fundamental principles.
B) They disagree on details, not fundamentals.
C) They disagree on everything equally.
D) They have no disagreement.

Key Takeaway
All rational minds share fundamental principles; their disagreements are only over details, which revelation clarifies.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you encounter someone with a different religious belief, do you assume they are irrational or that they share your fundamental principles? How can Juwayni's observation change the way you approach such conversations?
?
Think of a time you disagreed with a fellow Muslim about a detail of practice or belief. Can you identify the universal principle you both agreed on? How does focusing on that principle change the nature of the disagreement?
?
Juwayni says he examined rational sciences and found agreement on principles. Have you ever sincerely examined your own deepest convictions to see if they are shared by those you disagree with? What would it cost you to do that today?
