The Qadariyya's Mistake and the Need for Revelation
Juwayni uses the Qadariyya example to illustrate detail error, then transitions to prophethood.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni uses the Qadariyya as an example of mishandling details, and how he transitions to the necessity of prophethood for receiving divine laws.

Core Idea
Even the Qadariyya's core desire for good is natural; their error is in the details.

Juwayni now applies his previous insight to a specific group, the Qadariyya (who advocated human free will). He notes that their basic inclination to seek benefit and avoid harm is natural and not objectionable. Their error lies in how they expressed it. After this illustration, he formally concludes his discussion on God's lordship and human servitude, and announces the next topic: the source of divine laws is the messengers and prophets.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic and translation. Focus on the Qadariyya example first.
2
Understand the Qadariyya's position
Juwayni says their basic drive is natural, but they did not perfect its detail, so they slipped.
Qadariyya argued for free will, but their reasoning was flawed.
3
Note the balance
Juwayni mentions the need to consider both the created inclination and God's transcendence above benefit and harm.
4
Recognize the transition
Juwayni concludes the discussion on lordship and servitude, and introduces the third chapter: the source of revealed laws is prophethood.
This is a clear structural marker in the text.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ولا يدرك بالعقل وجوب عليه . ولكن اذا أراد الرب الزام عبيده شيئا . أمرهم وتوعدهم على ترك المأمور فتستحثهم عقولهم على اجتناب المحذور , فان وعد الله حق , ووعيده صدق , وقد تتبعت العلوم العقلية فى كل فن جهدى فما وجدت طائفة من ذوى العقول حائدين بالكلية عن مسلك جلى , من مسالك العقول . ولكنهم يبتدرون القاعدة . ثم قد بزلون عن التفاصيل وهذا كما أن افتقار المتغيرات الى مدير , لما كان من كليات العقول لم ينكره أحد ولكنهم اختلفوا في صفة المدبر فسماه بعض العقلاء : الطبع . وبعضهم : العقل الكلى . الى خبط لا أشغل به قريحة مولانا , ثم استبد الموفقون لمنهج الحق ومن طال نظره في المقليات تبين له: أن مثار خلاف العقلاء ايل الى التفاصيل , دون الأصول . والغرض من هذا التشبيه أن النفوس مجبولة على طلب المحبوب , وتوقى المحذور , ومصير القدرية الى ذلك غير مستنكر أصلا . ولكنهم لم يحسنوا تفصيله فزلواء ونحن جميعا بين اعتباره , وبين تنزيه الرب سبحانه عن النفع والضر . كما جرى في معرض هو أوضح من فلق الصبح لفاهمه . واذا نجز القول في أحكام الربوبية وصفات العبودية, واستبان أن مدرك التكاليف , موقوف على ورود الشرائع , فقد حان الآن : أن نوضح أن مدرك الشرائع : التلقى من الرسل والأنبياء عليهم السلام , وهو الباب الثالث من أبواب العقيدة والله الموفق
And obligation upon Him is not perceived by the intellect. But when the Lord wishes to oblige His servants with something, He commands them and threatens them upon abandoning the commanded, so their intellects urge them to avoid the prohibited. For the promise of God is true, and His threat is true. And I have examined the rational sciences in every field with my utmost effort, and I have not found a single group of intelligent people entirely deviating from a clear path among the paths of the intellect. Rather, they proceed from the principle. Then they disagree about the details. And this is like the fact that the need of changing things for a director, since it is among the universal [principles] of the intellect, no one denied it, but they differed concerning the attribute of the director. Some of the intelligent called it 'nature', and some called it 'the universal intellect', into a confusion with which I will not occupy the mind of our master. Then those guided to the path of truth succeeded. And whoever extensively examines the universals [or: the rational matters] realizes that the source of disagreement among rational people leads back to details, not to fundamentals. And the purpose of this analogy is that souls are naturally disposed to seek the beloved and avoid the harmful, and the position of the Qadariyya regarding that is not at all objectionable originally. But they did not perfect its detail, so they slipped. And we are all between its consideration and between declaring the Lord transcendent above benefit and harm. As it occurred in a context clearer than the crack of dawn to one who understands it. And when speech is concluded concerning the judgments of lordship and the attributes of servitude, and it has become evident that the source of obligations is dependent on the advent of revealed laws, now it is time that we clarify that the source of revealed laws is receiving from messengers and prophets, peace be upon them. And this is the third chapter of the chapters of creed. And God is the Grantor of success.
Source pages p. 60

Commentary
This passage is a transitional conclusion summarizing the limitations of reason and the necessity of revelation. Juwayni begins by stating that obligation upon God is not perceived by reason, but once God commands and threatens, reason urges compliance. He then reflects on his study of rational sciences, noting that all rational people agree on fundamental principles but differ in details, like the need for a director of changing things (God) while differing on His nature. He uses this to argue that disagreements among intelligent people are in details, not fundamentals. He then applies this to the Qadariyya (free will advocates), whose basic position (seeking benefit and avoiding harm) is not objectionable, but they mishandled the details. Finally, he concludes that since obligations depend on revealed laws, he will now turn to the third chapter on prophethood, i.e., the source of revealed laws is the messengers.

القادرية
التكاليف
الشرائع

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, why did the Qadariyya go wrong?
A) Their basic desire for good was wrong.
B) They mishandled the details of their position.
C) They denied the need for revelation.
D) They were not rational.

Key Takeaway
The Qadariyya's mistake was in details, not fundamentals; revelation from prophets is needed to correct such errors.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
In your own life, where do you find yourself relying solely on your natural inclinations to judge right from wrong, without submitting to the revelation from prophets? How has that led you into 'detail errors' that you previously overlooked? What specific step will you take today to bring your reasoning under the authority of the prophetic message?
?
Juwayni says the Qadariyya slipped in the details, not in their original desire for good. Is there an area of your faith where you are so focused on the 'right intention' that you've become careless about the details of how you practice it? For example, in worship, relationships, or ethics? What is one detailed correction from revelation that you have been avoiding?
?
The passage transitions from discussing human error to affirming the need for prophets. Think of a time when you realized that your own reasoning was insufficient to guide you in a spiritual matter. Did you then turn to the prophetic guidance? Or did you continue to rely on your own understanding? What is one teaching from the Prophet (peace be upon him) that you need to obey today, even if it contradicts your natural inclination?

Sam'iyyat: The Realm of Revealed Knowledge
Juwayni defines sam'iyyat and contrasts rational with revealed knowledge.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni defines the field of sam'iyyat and distinguishes rational knowledge from revealed knowledge.

Core Idea
Revealed knowledge rests on trust in a truthful informant, not on intellectual satisfaction.

Juwayni opens the chapter on sam'iyyat by laying a foundational epistemological principle: true reports about possible things yield knowledge. He then reveals a key insight: knowledge divides into rational and revealed. Rational matters bring inner clarity, while revealed matters, though certain, do not produce the same intellectual ease. This distinction helps believers avoid doubts about their faith.

Guided Path
1
Read the opening principle
The passage begins with a general rule: if a truthful informant reports a possible thing, knowledge necessarily follows. Why is the thing's possibility important?
Juwayni stresses God's power over all possibilities.
2
Learn the division of knowledge
Juwayni states that information is divided into rational ('aqliyyat) and revealed (sam'iyyat). Notice how he describes each.
3
Compare the inner experience
Rational matters bring 'coolness' and 'expansion' in the heart; revealed matters do not. Yet the believer is not doubtful. Why?
The difference is due to the role of taqlid (following).
4
Understand the purpose
Juwayni mentions this to prevent believers from accusing their faith when they don't feel the same intellectual comfort in revealed matters.
Faith remains certain even without that feeling.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
باب في السمعيات: من ثبت صدق لهجته اذا أخبر عن كائن ممكن حصل العلم به لا محالة. لأن الخبر عنه ممكن، مقدور الله سبحانه، والمخبر صادق ثم من أسرار الدين - وهو على منصبه (1) - أن يعلم المنثبت: أن المعلومات تنقسم إلى العقليات، والسمعيات فما كان معقولا وجد العاقل له ثلجا فى نفسه .. وانشراحا في قلبه وما تلقاه من السمع فهو غير مرتاب فيه، ولكنه لا يجد من نفسه الثلج الذى يجده من المعقولات. فان المخبر كان صادقا فالمصدق فيه مقلد، ولن يبلغ العالم (3) عن تقليد الصادق مبلغ من أدرك الشيء بعقله. وانما ذكرت هذا حتى اذا وجد الموجد نفسه في السمعيات دون وجدانه نفسه في العقليات. لا يتهم إيمانه ولا يشك فى ايقانه. ثم ما يقتضيه الدين القويم والمنهج المستقيم: أن كل ما نقل عن النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم بطرق صحيحة، مرتضاة عند أهل الاثبات، وكان ممكنا غير مستحيل، فان كان النقل تواترا، علم قطعا على حد العلم بالسمعيات. وان نقل آحادا ثبت ذلك المظنون فى مأثور الأخبار .. وتلقى بالقبول. ولم يعارض بالاستبعاد فان الاستبعاد فيما هذا سبيله من شيم المرتانين في الدين.
Chapter on Sam'iyyat: Whoever's truthfulness is established, when he informs about a possible entity, knowledge necessarily occurs. Because the information about it is possible, within the power of Allah Almighty, and the informant is truthful. Then among the secrets of religion - and he is in his position (1) - is that the established one knows that information is divided into rational and revealed matters. What is rational, the rational person finds for it a coolness in his soul and expansion in his heart. What he receives from revelation, he is not doubtful about it, but he does not find from himself the coolness that he finds from rational matters. For the informant is truthful, so the one who affirms him is a follower (muqallid). The scholar through following a truthful person will never reach the level of one who comprehends a thing by his intellect. I only mentioned this so that if one finds himself in revealed matters without finding himself in rational matters, he does not accuse his faith nor doubt his certainty. Then what the upright religion and straight path require: that everything transmitted from the Prophet ﷺ through authentic chains, approved by the people of affirmation, and is possible not impossible, if the transmission is mutawatir, it is known categorically on the level of knowledge of revealed matters. If it is ahad transmission, that probability is established in inherited reports and is accepted. It is not opposed by remote possibility, for remote possibility in such a matter is a characteristic of the weak in religion.
Source pages p. 76

Commentary
This passage is a definition and classification passage. Juwayni begins the chapter on sam'iyyat by establishing a general epistemological principle: if a truthful informant reports about something possible, knowledge necessarily follows, because the report concerns something possible, God has power over it, and the informant is truthful. He then reveals a secret of religion: knowledge is divided into rational ('aqliyyat) and revealed (sam'iyyat). Rational matters bring the intellect a sense of coolness and expansion, while revealed matters, though not doubted, do not produce the same internal feeling. This is because in revealed matters the believer is a muqallid (follower) of the truthful Prophet, and such following never reaches the level of rational comprehension. Juwayni states he explained this so that if a believer finds himself experiencing less comfort in revealed matters, he should not suspect his faith or doubt his certainty. Then he lays down the ruling: every report from the Prophet with authentic chains, if mutawatir, gives certainty on the same level as knowledge of sam'iyyat; if ahad, it establishes probability (dhann) and is accepted, and should not be rejected due to remote possibility (isti'bad), as doing so is a trait of the weak in religion.

Key Terms
سمعيات
عقليات
تقليد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Revealed knowledge is less certain because it doesn't bring intellectual satisfaction.
Correction: Juwayni explains that revealed knowledge is certain because it comes from a truthful informant; the lack of intellectual ease is due to it being based on trust (taqlid), not doubt.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, how does rational knowledge affect the soul compared to revealed knowledge?
A) Rational knowledge brings coolness and expansion; revealed knowledge brings doubt.
B) Rational knowledge brings coolness and expansion; revealed knowledge does not bring that feeling but is not doubted.
C) Both bring the same level of intellectual satisfaction.
D) Revealed knowledge brings coolness; rational knowledge does not.

Key Takeaway
Revealed knowledge is certain because it rests on the truthfulness of the Prophet, even if it does not provide the same intellectual ease as rational knowledge.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you find that your heart feels less comfort in matters of revelation (such as belief in the unseen) than in matters you can reason out, do you secretly doubt your faith? Juwayni says you should not. Do you trust his word more than your feelings? Will you decide today to anchor your certainty on the truthfulness of the Prophet, not on your emotional temperature?
?
Juwayni says that the scholar via taqlid never reaches the level of rational comprehension. Are you tempted to seek that 'coolness' as proof of truth, rather than submitting to revelation because it comes from the truthful? Is your faith resting on internal feelings or on the authority of the Prophet? What can you do today to strengthen that submission?
?
Think of a revealed matter that you find intellectually uncomfortable—like the punishment of the grave or the scale of deeds. Do you accuse your faith when you lack clarity? Juwayni commands you not to. Will you commit to not expecting intellectual ease from every belief and instead trust the truthfulness of the informant?

Method of Learning

4 min
Accepting Transmitted Reports: Mutawatir and Ahad
Juwayni rules on accepting mutawatir and ahad reports.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's criteria for accepting reports from the Prophet and the epistemological status of mutawatir versus ahad transmissions.

Core Idea
Mutawatir reports give certainty; ahad reports give probability, and rejecting them due to remote possibility is weak faith.

After distinguishing rational from revealed knowledge, Juwayni addresses how to evaluate reports transmitted from the Prophet. He lays down two categories: mutawatir (mass-transmitted) reports yield categorical certainty, while ahad (solitary) reports yield probability and must be accepted. He warns against rejecting such reports simply because they seem unlikely, as that is a trait of those weak in religion.

Guided Path
1
Identify the two categories of transmission
Juwayni says everything transmitted through authentic chains, if mutawatir, gives certainty; if ahad, gives probability.
2
Understand 'possible not impossible'
He requires the content to be possible, not impossible. Why is this condition necessary?
To avoid attributing something impossible to God or the Prophet.
3
Learn the ruling for ahad reports
Ahad reports establish probability (dhann) and are accepted; they are not to be opposed by remote possibility (isti'bad).
4
Recognize the error of isti'bad
Juwayni states that using remote possibility to reject authentic ahad reports is a characteristic of the weak in religion.
This teaches us to submit to authentic transmissions.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
باب في السمعيات: من ثبت صدق لهجته اذا أخبر عن كائن ممكن حصل العلم به لا محالة. لأن الخبر عنه ممكن، مقدور الله سبحانه، والمخبر صادق ثم من أسرار الدين - وهو على منصبه (1) - أن يعلم المنثبت: أن المعلومات تنقسم إلى العقليات، والسمعيات فما كان معقولا وجد العاقل له ثلجا فى نفسه .. وانشراحا في قلبه وما تلقاه من السمع فهو غير مرتاب فيه، ولكنه لا يجد من نفسه الثلج الذى يجده من المعقولات. فان المخبر كان صادقا فالمصدق فيه مقلد، ولن يبلغ العالم (3) عن تقليد الصادق مبلغ من أدرك الشيء بعقله. وانما ذكرت هذا حتى اذا وجد الموجد نفسه في السمعيات دون وجدانه نفسه في العقليات. لا يتهم إيمانه ولا يشك فى ايقانه. ثم ما يقتضيه الدين القويم والمنهج المستقيم: أن كل ما نقل عن النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم بطرق صحيحة، مرتضاة عند أهل الاثبات، وكان ممكنا غير مستحيل، فان كان النقل تواترا، علم قطعا على حد العلم بالسمعيات. وان نقل آحادا ثبت ذلك المظنون فى مأثور الأخبار .. وتلقى بالقبول. ولم يعارض بالاستبعاد فان الاستبعاد فيما هذا سبيله من شيم المرتانين في الدين.

Then among the secrets of religion - and he is in his position (1) - is that the established one knows that information is divided into rational and revealed matters. For the informant is truthful, so the one who affirms him is a follower (muqallid). Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Ahad reports can be rejected if they seem improbable or unlikely.
Correction: Juwayni states that ahad reports are to be accepted and not opposed by remote possibility; doing so is a sign of weakness in religion.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the epistemological status of a report transmitted through ahad (solitary) chains?
A) It is categorically certain.
B) It is rejected if it seems unlikely.
C) It establishes probability (dhann) and is accepted.
D) It produces the same internal coolness as rational matters.

Key Takeaway
All authentic reports from the Prophet are to be accepted: mutawatir gives certainty, ahad gives probability, and rejecting ahad based on remote possibility is weak faith.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you encounter a teaching of the Prophet that is only reported through ahad chains, do you accept it with trust or find yourself questioning its authenticity because it seems unlikely or difficult? Is that not a form of remote possibility that Juwayni warns is a trait of the weak in religion?
?
Think of a specific hadith you have been hesitant to act upon or believe fully because you thought it might not be 'strong enough'—could it be that your hesitation stems from isti'bad? What would it cost you to wholeheartedly accept it as probable truth from the Prophet?
?
If the Prophet himself spoke a word to you today, would you receive it with certainty or with doubts? Your response to his preserved reports reveals the same posture. Are you satisfied with a faith that accepts only what is undeniably certain, or will you submit to whatever authentic report comes from him, even if it only gives probability?

Method of Learning
intermediate
6 min
Juwayni's Method for Clear and Ambiguous Verses in Attributes
Juwayni defines muhkam and mutashabih in attribute verses and gives interpretive rule.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni defines muhkam and mutashabih in attribute verses and how he uses the muhkam verse 'There is nothing like Him' to interpret ambiguous verses without anthropomorphism.

Core Idea
The clear verse 'Nothing like Him' is the key to interpreting ambiguous attribute verses.

Juwayni addresses a critical issue: how to understand verses that seemingly describe Allah with human-like organs or feelings. He distinguishes between clear (muhkam) and ambiguous (mutashabih) verses, and provides a rule for interpreting the ambiguous ones by referring them back to the clear verse that negates any likeness to creation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the literal translation. Let Juwayni's own words come through.
Notice the reference to Salaf and Juwayni's personal endorsement.
2
Identify the two categories
Find the muhkam verse and the two types of mutashabih verses (organs and sensations). Note Juwayni's definition.
3
Understand the interpretive method
The example 'Yad Allah' shows two meanings. The muhkam verse eliminates the literal meaning, leaving the metaphorical meaning (power).
This is not pure tafwid; it's a guided ta'wil.
4
Reflect on Juwayni's approach
Consider how Juwayni balances Salaf's method of consigning with rational interpretation using the muhkam.
This lesson clarifies a common misunderstanding.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم تحدث الجويني - رحمه الله - عن المحكم والمتشابه في آيات وأحاديث الصفات . ورأيه فيهما : « وذهب أئمة السلف الى الانكفاف عن التأويل واجراء الظواهر على مواردها وتفويض معانيها الى الرب تعالى . والذى ترتضيه رأيا ، وتدين الله به عقلا : اتباع سلف الأمة فالأولى الاتباع وترك الابتداع » و المحكم فى آيات الصفات قول الله تعالى : « ليس كمثله شيء » والمتشابه اما أن يثبت أعضاء الله تعالى كأعضاء الانسان كاليد والرجل والعين مثلا . واما أن يثبت صفات أحاسيس كأحاسيس الانسان كالاستحياء والمكر والنسيان مثلا. فمثال الأعضاء قوله تعالى : «يد الله فوق أيديهم » ( الفتح 10 ) فان هذا القول متشابه لاحتماله معنيين. الأول : اليد الحقيقية أى الجارحة كيد الانسان والثانى : أنه ليس بمعنى اليد الحقيقية بل بمعنى القدرة أى قدرة الله فوق قدرة الناس . ولما كان هذا القول متشابها فانه يتعين الرجوع الى الآية المحكمة التي لا تحتمل الا معنى واحدا وهى « ليس كمثله شيء » ( الشورى 11 ) وحيث أنها تنفى مشابهة الله للانسان فاذن يجب الأخذ بالمعنى الثانى وهو القدرة ، وترك المعنى الأول وهو باليد الحقيقية . ولا نقول الله يد
Then Juwayni - may Allah have mercy on him - spoke about muhkam (clear) and mutashabih (ambiguous) in the verses and hadiths of attributes. His view regarding them: 'The imams of the Salaf went to refraining from ta'wil (interpretation) and applying the apparent meanings as they come, and consigning their meanings to the Lord, Most High. What I approve as an opinion and by which I worship Allah as a creed is following the Salaf of the community, so following is better and leaving innovation is better.' And the muhkam regarding verses of attributes is the saying of Allah Most High: 'There is nothing like Him' (Quran 42:11). And the mutashabih is either that which affirms organs of Allah Most High like the organs of humans, such as the hand, the foot, and the eye, for example; or that which affirms attributes of sensations like the sensations of humans, such as shyness, deception, and forgetfulness, for example. An example of the organs is His saying Most High: 'The hand of Allah is above their hands' (Quran 48:10). For this saying is mutashabih because it admits two meanings: the first is the real hand, i.e., the limb like the hand of a human; and the second: that it is not in the sense of a real hand but in the sense of power, i.e., the power of Allah is above the power of people. And since this saying is mutashabih, it is necessary to refer to the muhkam verse which admits only one meaning, and it is 'There is nothing like Him' (Quran 42:11). And since it negates the likeness of Allah to humans, then one must take the second meaning, which is power, and abandon the first meaning, which is the real hand. And we do not say 'the hand of Allah.'
Source pages p. 104

Commentary
This passage presents Juwayni's discussion of the muhkam (clear) and mutashabih (ambiguous) verses and hadiths concerning Allah's attributes. He begins by stating the position of the Salaf (early righteous predecessors): they refrained from ta'wil (figurative interpretation) and left the apparent meanings as they are, entrusting their true meanings to Allah. Juwayni then declares his own theological commitment: he follows the Salaf's methodology, considering it the best path and avoiding innovation. Next, he defines what constitutes muhkam and mutashabih in this context. The muhkam verse on attributes is 'There is nothing like Him' (Quran 42:11); this verse is unequivocal and serves as the criterion. The mutashabih verses are of two types: those that seem to attribute bodily organs to Allah (like hand, foot, eye) and those that attribute human-like sensations (like shyness, deception, forgetfulness). As an example, he takes the verse 'The hand of Allah is above their hands' (Quran 48:10). This is mutashabih because it could either mean a literal hand (a limb) or it could mean power and ability. The proper method, according to Juwayni, is to refer the ambiguous verse back to the clear verse. Since 'There is nothing like Him' negates any similarity between Allah and humans, the literal-hand interpretation is invalid. Therefore, one must adopt the second meaning—power—and reject the first. He concludes by affirming that we do not say 'the hand of Allah' in a literal sense. This shows that Juwayni, while outwardly endorsing the Salafi approach of tafwid (consigning the meaning to Allah), in practice employs a ta'wil guided by the muhkam verse to avoid anthropomorphism.

Key Terms
مُحْكَم
مُتَشابِه
تَأْوِيل
تَفْوِيض
الصِّفات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think Juwayni advocates pure tafwid without any interpretation for mutashabih verses.
Correction: Juwayni explicitly uses the muhkam verse to determine the correct meaning of mutashabih, which is a form of ta'wil, not pure consignment.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the correct way to interpret a mutashabih verse like 'The hand of Allah is above their hands'?
A) Take it literally as affirming a physical hand for Allah.
B) Consign its meaning to Allah without any interpretation.
C) Refer it to the muhkam verse 'There is nothing like Him' and interpret 'hand' as power.
D) Reject the verse as unreliable.

Key Takeaway
Juwayni teaches that ambiguous attribute verses must be interpreted in light of the clear verse 'There is nothing like Him', which rules out anthropomorphic interpretations.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read 'The hand of Allah is above their hands', does your heart immediately want to imagine a physical hand? How does Juwayni's method of using 'Nothing like Him' force you to discipline your imagination and submit to Allah's transcendence? Are you willing to let go of anthropomorphic images even if they feel more tangible?
?
In what areas of your life do you cling to literal interpretations or superficial understandings because they feel familiar or comfortable? This lesson shows that clear verses must govern ambiguous ones. What clear truth from the Quran are you avoiding because it would require you to abandon a cherished but flawed belief about Allah?
?
Juwayni both praises the Salaf for refraining from interpretation and yet uses the muhkam to determine meaning. How does this balance between submission and understanding challenge your own approach to difficult texts? Will you commit to studying the clear verses before rushing to the ambiguous, and accept that some meanings are beyond your grasp?

Juwayni on Muhkam and Mutashabih
Juwayni endorses salaf's tafwid, defines muhkam, and categorizes mutashabih into two types.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's position on ambiguous verses (mutashabih) and his classification of muhkam and mutashabih in divine attributes.

Core Idea
The clear verse governs the ambiguous; the ambiguous is entrusted to Allah.

Imam al-Juwayni offers a concise statement on the methodology of the salaf regarding verses and hadiths of divine attributes. He distinguishes between the muhkam (clear) verse that establishes transcendence and two types of mutashabih (ambiguous) verses that require tafwid. Let's examine his precise formulation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic passage and its translation to grasp Juwayni's position. Notice his endorsement of the salaf's approach and his own agreement.
Pay attention to the phrases 'الانكفاف عن التأويل' and 'تفويض معانيها'.
2
Identify the key terms
Locate the terms 'محكم', 'متشابه', 'تأويل', and 'تفويض'. Understand their meanings in this context.
These terms are central to the discussion of divine attributes.
3
Analyze the two types of mutashabih
Juwayni divides mutashabih into two categories: those that affirm body parts (like hand, foot, eye) and those that affirm emotional attributes (like shyness, deceit, forgetfulness). Reflect on why these are considered ambiguous.
Both types are attributed to humans but their application to Allah is unclear.
4
Understand the salaf method
Juwayni advocates refraining from ta'wil (metaphorical interpretation) and instead practicing tafwid (consigning the meaning to Allah). This is the methodology of the salaf.
Contrast this with the approach of those who engage in ta'wil.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم تحدث الجويني - رحمه الله - عن المحكم والمتشابه في آيات وأحاديث الصفات . ورأيه فيهما : « وذهب أئمة السلف الى الانكفاف عن التأويل واجراء الظواهر على مواردها وتفويض معانيها الى الرب تعالى . والذي ترتضيه رأيا ، وتدين الله به عقلا : اتباع سلف الأمة فالأولى الاتباع وترك الابتداع » أي لا يوافق الجويني على التأويل . والمحكم في آيات الصفات قول الله تعالى : « ليس كمثله شيء » والمتشابه اما أن يثبت أعضاء الله تعالى كأعضاء الانسان كاليد والرجل والعين مثلا . واما أن يثبت صفات أحاسيس كأحاسيس الانسان كالاستحياء والمكر والنسيان مثلا.
Then al-Juwayni - may Allah have mercy on him - discussed the muhkam and mutashabih in the verses and hadiths of the attributes. His view regarding them: 'The imams of the salaf went to refraining from ta'wil, and applying the apparent meanings as they came, and consigning their meanings to the Lord Almighty. And what I approve as an opinion and profess as a creed is following the salaf of the ummah, so following is preferable and innovation is to be abandoned.' That is, al-Juwayni does not agree with ta'wil. And the muhkam in the verses of attributes is the saying of Allah Almighty: 'There is nothing like unto Him' (42:11). And the mutashabih is either that which affirms body parts of Allah like the body parts of humans, such as hand, foot, and eye, for example; or that which affirms attributes of sensations like the sensations of humans, such as shyness, deceit, and forgetfulness, for example.
Source pages p. 104

Commentary
This passage presents Juwayni's position on how to deal with ambiguous verses (mutashabih) regarding divine attributes. He explicitly follows the salaf method: no ta'wil (metaphorical interpretation), but rather taking the apparent meaning as it is (ijra' al-zawahir 'ala mawaridiha) and consigning the true meaning to Allah (tafwid). He does not agree with ta'wil. Then he defines the muhkam (clear) verse that serves as the reference: 'There is nothing like unto Him.' And he categorizes mutashabih into two types: those affirming physical members (like hand, foot, eye) and those affirming emotional attributes (like shyness, deceit, forgetfulness). This sets the stage for the examples.

Key Terms
محكم
متشابه
تأويل
تفويض
سلف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The salaf engaged in ta'wil or interpreted everything literally.
Correction: Juwayni states that the salaf refrained from ta'wil and practiced tafwid, meaning they accepted the apparent meanings without interpreting them metaphorically and consigned the true meaning to Allah.

Checkpoint
What does Juwayni say is the muhkam verse in the verses of attributes?
A) 'Hand of Allah over their hands'
B) 'There is nothing like unto Him'
C) 'They forgot Allah so He forgot them'
D) 'He is the First and the Last'

Key Takeaway
Juwayni distinguishes between muhkam and two types of mutashabih, advocating the salaf's method of tafwid.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you encounter an attribute of Allah that your mind struggles with, do you immediately try to rationalize it away, or do you trust Allah enough to say 'I affirm it as it came, and consign its true meaning to Him'? Which approach has your heart been leaning toward?
?
The salaf refrained from ta'wil and did not let their intellects override revelation. Is there an area in your faith where you have allowed your own reasoning to reinterpret or soften a clear teaching? How can you train yourself to submit more fully?
?
Juwayni calls following the salaf 'preferable' and warns against innovation. In your daily life, do you truly value following the way of the earliest Muslims, or do you find yourself more influenced by modern trends and opinions? What is one concrete step you can take this week to align your practice more closely with their path?

Quran 3:7: The Clear and the Ambiguous
Juwayni quotes Quran 3:7 and begins citing Tafsir al-Kashshaf on muhkamat.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Quranic distinction between muhkam and mutashabih verses and the proper response of the firmly grounded in knowledge.

Core Idea
The clear and ambiguous verses: a foundation for faith without overinterpretation.

Juwayni opens with Quran 3:7, which establishes the categorization of Quranic verses into muhkamat (clear, foundational) and mutashabihat (ambiguous). This verse has profound methodological implications for Islamic theology, particularly regarding the permissibility of ta'wil. In this lesson, we examine the verse itself and how it instructs the believers to respond to ambiguous passages.

Guided Path
1
Read the verse and its context
Read Quran 3:7 carefully. Note the two categories: muhkamat (foundational) and mutashabihat (ambiguous).
The verse is the key proof text for the salaf approach to ambiguous verses.
2
Understand the key terms
Focus on muhkamat, mutashabihat, ta'wil, and the phrase 'rasikhun fi al-'ilm'. Define each in light of the verse.
The tafsir begins to explain muhkamat, but only the start is given.
3
Identify the two groups
The verse describes two contrasting groups: those with deviation who seek ambiguous verses for fitna and ta'wil, and the firmly grounded in knowledge who affirm belief.
Note that the firmly grounded do not claim knowledge of ta'wil.
4
Reflect on the doctrinal implication
Why does this verse matter for kalam? It suggests that the true meaning of ambiguous verses is known only to Allah, discouraging speculative interpretation.
This supports the approach of tafwid (consigning meaning to Allah).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وتفسير قوله تعالى « هو الذي أنزل عليك الكتاب منه آيات محكمات من أم الكتاب وأخر متشابهات فأما الذين في قلوبهم زيغ فيتبعون ما تشابه منه ابتغاء الفتنة وابتغاء تأويله وما يعلم تأويله إلا الله والراسخون في العلم يقولون آمنا به كل من عند ربنا وما يذكر إلا أولو الألباب » ( آل عمران : ٧ ) هكذا في تفسير الكشاف : ( محكمات ( أحكمت عبارتها بأن
And the explanation of His saying: 'It is He who has sent down to you the Book. In it are verses that are muhkamat, they are the foundation of the Book, and others are mutashabihat. As for those in whose hearts is deviation, they follow what is mutashabih of it, seeking fitna and seeking its ta'wil. And no one knows its ta'wil except Allah. And those firmly grounded in knowledge say: "We believe in it; all is from our Lord." And none remember except the people of understanding.' (3:7) Thus in Tafsir al-Kashshaf: 'Muhkamat: their expression is made precise by...'
Source pages p. 105

Commentary
This lesson presents Juwayni quoting Quran 3:7, the key verse on muhkam and mutashabih. He then begins to cite Tafsir al-Kashshaf (by Zamakhshari) for the explanation of 'muhkamat.' The verse distinguishes between clear verses (muhkamat) that are the foundation, and ambiguous verses (mutashabihat). Those with deviation follow the ambiguous for fitna and ta'wil, but the true meaning is known only to Allah, while the firmly grounded in knowledge affirm belief in them. The tafsir that follows explains that muhkamat means the wording is precise. This verse supports the salaf position of tafwid and avoiding ta'wil.

Key Terms

متشابهات

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That the 'rasikhun fi al-ilm' know the ta'wil of mutashabih verses.
Correction: The verse clearly states that only Allah knows the ta'wil, and the firmly grounded say 'We believe' without claiming knowledge of it.

Checkpoint

A) They know its ta'wil
B) They follow it
C) They say 'We believe in it; all is from our Lord.'
D) They reject it

Key Takeaway
Quran 3:7 establishes that only Allah knows the true interpretation of ambiguous verses, and the proper response of believers is to affirm faith without seeking to determine that meaning.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
What are the ambiguous verses in your own life—the things Allah has not clearly explained—that you find yourself chasing after, seeking to know more than what He has revealed? Will you today join the ranks of the rasikhun and say 'We believe, all is from our Lord'?
?
When you read a verse about Allah's attributes that your mind cannot fully grasp, does your heart feel anxious to interpret it in a way that makes sense to you? What would it look like to trust Allah more than your own understanding, and to stop seeking ta'wil?
?
The verse warns against those with deviation who follow the ambiguous to cause fitna. Is there any area where your desire to 'understand' has led you to argue or cause division among believers? Will you repent and return to the simple faith of 'We believe'?

Rejection of Ahad Hadith in Creed
Juwayni critiques literalists for using ahad hadith in creed.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why ahad hadith are rejected as evidence for creedal matters, specifically regarding the sensory Bridge and Balance, and the methodological principle that distinguishes between virtuous deeds and creed.

Core Idea
Ahad hadith guide actions but cannot establish beliefs about the unseen.

This lesson focuses on a key methodological principle in Islamic theology: the distinction between reports used for virtuous deeds and those used for creed. Imam al-Juwayni explains that supporters of a literal Bridge and Balance rely on hadiths that are classified as ahad (solitary), and therefore are not valid for establishing doctrines. Understanding this rule is essential for proper theological reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Pay attention to the key terms: 'ahadith al-ahad', 'fada'il al-a'mal', and 'al-'aqa'id'.
Notice the structure: first the literalists' action, then the rejection, then the principle.
2
Identify the two categories of hadith usage
The passage states that ahad hadith are accepted for virtuous deeds (fada'il al-a'mal) but not at all for creedal matters ('aqa'id). Contrast these two categories.
This distinction is fundamental in usul al-fiqh and kalam.
3
Reflect on the requirement of certainty
Ask yourself why creed requires certain knowledge (yaqin) while actions can be based on probable reports. Connect this to the concept of ahad hadith providing only zann (speculative knowledge).
Creed deals with unseen realities; actions are outward and less demanding of certainty.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ولذلك رأى المثبتون للصراط والميزان الحسيين اللجوء إلى أحاديث منسوبة إلى رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم ليؤكدوا بها رأيهم. وقد ردت من ثقات المحدثين لأنها آحاد، وأحاديث الآحاد يؤخذ بها في فضائل الأعمال، ولا يؤخذ بها مطلقا في العقائد.
And therefore, those who affirm the sensory Bridge and sensory Balance saw recourse to hadiths attributed to the Messenger of God, may God bless him and grant him peace, to confirm their view. But these hadiths were rejected by reliable hadith scholars because they are solitary, and solitary hadiths are accepted in virtuous deeds, and are not accepted at all in matters of creed.
Source pages p. 118

Commentary
This passage identifies the evidential basis used by the literalists for their position on the sensory Bridge and Balance. They turned to hadiths, but these hadiths are ahad (solitary) reports. The author (likely al-Juwayni himself, or the commentator) states that these hadiths have been rejected by trustworthy hadith scholars precisely because they are ahad. The principle is that ahad hadith can be used for fada’il al-a‘mal (encouragement in virtuous deeds), but they are absolutely not valid as evidence for creedal doctrines. This establishes a key methodological rule in kalam: creed must be based on certain knowledge, and ahad reports provide only probable knowledge.

Key Terms
أحاديث الآحاد
فضائل الأعمال
العقائد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Any hadith is sufficient evidence for creedal matters.
Correction: Only mutawatir reports or the Qur'an can establish creed. Ahad hadith provide only probable knowledge and are limited to practical matters.

Checkpoint
Why did reliable hadith scholars reject the hadiths about the sensory bridge and balance?
A) Because they contradict the Qur’an
B) Because they are solitary (ahad) reports
C) Because they are fabricated
D) Because they are not from the Prophet

Key Takeaway
Ahad hadith provide probable knowledge and are accepted for virtuous deeds but not for establishing creedal doctrines.

The Balance (Mizan) is Metaphorical, Not Literal
Juwayni argues the Quranic balance is metaphorical, not literal.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Imam al-Juwayni argues that the Quranic balance is metaphorical and not a physical object, and why ahad hadiths cannot be used to establish creedal beliefs.

Core Idea
The Quranic balance is ambiguous; literalists rely on weak hadiths.

Imam al-Juwayni tackles the question of whether the 'balance' mentioned in the Quran on the Day of Judgment is a literal scale or a metaphor for divine justice. He points out that the Quranic verses themselves are ambiguous (mutashabihat) and can bear either interpretation. Therefore, he argues, one cannot use these verses as decisive proof for a physical balance. Moreover, the hadiths that literalists cite are weak (ahad) and not acceptable for establishing creedal beliefs.

Guided Path
1
Read the Quranic verses
Read the verses from Al-A'raf and As-Saffat quoted by Juwayni. Notice how he classifies them as mutashabih (ambiguous).
The verses are about scales and guidance to Hellfire.
2
Understand the two possibilities
Recognize that the verses could mean either a literal scale with a tongue and pans, or a metaphorical just decree.
Juwayni says both interpretations are possible.
3
Analyze the hadith argument
See how Juwayni critiques the use of ahad hadiths for creedal matters, limiting them to virtues of deeds.
He notes that trustworthy scholars reject these hadiths.
4
Grasp the methodological point
Understand the broader principle that ambiguous verses and weak hadiths cannot form the basis of Islamic creed.
This is a key principle in kalam methodology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
موازينه فأولئك هم المفلحون، ومن حفت موازينه فأولئك الذين خسروا أنفسهم بما كانوا بآياتنا يظلمون) (الأعراف: ٩٨) فنص متشابه يحتمل ميزانا له لسان وكفتان ويحتمل القضاء السوى والحكم العادلة فلا تنهض به حجة قاطعة وكذلك قوله تعالى (فاهدوهم إلى صراط الجحيم، وقفوهم أنهم مسئولون) (الصافات: ٢٢-٢٣) والمعنى: عرفوهم طريق النار حتى يسلكوها. وهذا تهكم بهم، وتوبيخ لهم بالعجز عن التناصر بعد ما كانوا على خلاف ذلك في الدنيا متعاضدين متناصرين. ولذلك رأى المشتصرون للصراط والميزان الحسيان اللجوء إلى أحاديث منسوبة إلى رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم ليؤكدوا بها رأيهم. وقد ردت من ثقات المحدثين لأنها آحاد، وأحاديث الآحاد يؤخذ بها في فضائل الأعمال، ولا يؤخذ بها مطلقا في العقائد.
Those whose scales are heavy, they are the successful. And those whose scales are light, they are the ones who have lost their souls for being unjust to Our verses. (Al-A'raf: 8-9) So it is a mutashabih text, possibly meaning a scale with a tongue and two pans, and possibly meaning the just decree and fair judgment. So it does not constitute a conclusive proof. Likewise His statement: 'Guide them to the path of Hellfire, and stop them, indeed they will be questioned.' (As-Saffat: 22-23) The meaning: Make them know the way to the Fire so that they follow it. This is a mockery of them and a rebuke for their inability to support each other after they were in the world helping and supporting each other. Therefore, those who insist on the literal bridge and balance resorted to hadiths attributed to the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) to confirm their view. But they have been rejected by trustworthy hadith scholars because they are ahad (singular reports), and ahad hadiths are accepted in matters of virtues of deeds, but are not accepted at all in matters of creed.
Source pages p. 118

Commentary
This passage is a critique of the literalist interpretation of the balance (mizan) on the Day of Judgment. Juwayni begins by citing two Quranic verses (Al-A'raf: 8-9 and As-Saffat: 22-23) that mention scales and guidance to hellfire. He argues that these verses are mutashabihat (ambiguous) and can bear two interpretations: either a literal scale with a tongue and pans, or a metaphorical meaning of just judgment. Because of this ambiguity, they do not provide a decisive proof (hujja qati'a) for a physical balance. He then points out that those who insist on the literal interpretation have had to resort to ahad hadiths, but these hadiths are rejected by reliable scholars because ahad reports are only accepted in matters of fada'il al-a'mal (supererogatory acts) and not in matters of creed (aqa'id). Thus, the literalist position is based on weak evidence.

Key Terms
الميزان
المتشابه
أحاديث الآحاد
فضائل الأعمال
العقائد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some Muslims believe that the Quranic mention of scales on Judgment Day proves a literal physical balance, and that any hadith about it is automatically valid evidence for creed.
Correction: Juwayni shows that the Quranic verses are ambiguous (mutashabih) and can be interpreted metaphorically. Moreover, the hadiths used by literalists are ahad (singular reports), which are not accepted as proof in matters of creed according to scholarly consensus.

Checkpoint
Why does Juwayni argue that the Quranic verses about the balance do not provide a conclusive proof?
A) Because they are clear and unambiguous.
B) Because they are mutashabihat and can be interpreted metaphorically.
C) Because they are contradicted by reason.
D) Because they are abrogated.

Key Takeaway
Juwayni concludes that the Quranic balance is not necessarily literal; the verses are ambiguous and the supporting hadiths are not strong enough for creedal matters.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever assumed that a Quranic description must be taken literally, without considering that scholars might interpret it metaphorically? What is the danger of that assumption for your faith?
?
Think of a belief you hold about the afterlife. Is it based on a clear Quranic text or on a hadith you heard? Are you willing to re-examine it if the evidence is weak?
?
Juwayni shows that even respected hadiths can be rejected for creedal matters. Does this challenge your trust in what you've been taught? Will you commit to verifying the basis of your core beliefs?

Method of Learning
intermediate
5 min
Ahad Hadith Cannot Override Consensus
A critical note refuting two hadith as ahad and incapable of overriding consensus.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why hadith classified as ahad cannot override established consensus in Sunni creedal methodology.

Core Idea
Ahad hadith are trustworthy in practice but cannot overturn the definitive proof of consensus.

This lesson examines a methodological critique from al-Mawaqif. The author dismisses two hadith used as proof for disbelief due to abandoning prayer or Hajj, arguing that they are ahad and therefore insufficient to contradict the consensus that these acts are not kufr. We will explore the principles behind this argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the source passage
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the literal translation. Notice the concise formulation of the objection.
The passage is a direct quote from al-Mawaqif.
2
Understand the key terms
Identify the terms 'ahad' (isolated hadith) and 'ijma' (consensus). Recall their meanings in Islamic legal theory.
Ahad hadith are those that do not meet the conditions of tawatur.
3
Analyze the argument
The author argues that these two hadith are ahad and therefore cannot be used to contradict the consensus of scholars on the matter. Reflect on why this is a valid methodological point.
In Sunni usul, definitive proofs (qat'i) take precedence over speculative (dhanni) ones.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
رد صاحب المواقف على هذين الحديثين بقوله : هما من أحاديث الآحاد ، وأحاديث الآحاد لا تعارض الاجماع .
The author of al-Mawaqif responded to these two hadith by saying: They are from the isolated hadith (ahad), and isolated hadith do not contradict consensus.
Source pages p. 123

Commentary
This is a brief critical note by the author of al-Mawaqif (likely a later scholar, possibly al-Iji or al-Jurjani) responding to the two hadith cited in the previous proof (seventeenth proof: 'Whoever deliberately abandons prayer has disbelieved' and 'Whoever dies without Hajj...'). The note dismisses these hadith as being ahad (isolated traditions, i.e., not mutawatir) and therefore cannot override the established consensus (ijma) that such acts do not constitute disbelief. In Sunni methodology, ahad hadith are accepted in matters of practice but cannot contradict definitive proofs like consensus or mutawatir texts in matters of creed. The note thus argues that these hadith are insufficient to prove that major sins amount to kufr.

Key Terms
آحاد
إجماع
مواقف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that any authentic hadith is sufficient to establish creedal rulings, regardless of its transmission category.
Correction: This note shows that ahad hadith, even if authentic, cannot override the consensus of scholars when it comes to matters of belief.

Checkpoint
What reason does the author of al-Mawaqif give for rejecting the two hadith used as proof?
A) They are fabricated
B) They are abrogated
C) They are isolated (ahad) and do not contradict consensus
D) They are ambiguous in meaning

Key Takeaway
The author of al-Mawaqif rejects two hadith as ahad, illustrating the principle that isolated traditions cannot overturn the consensus of scholars in matters of creed.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever clung to a single hadith because it supports a view you prefer, without checking whether it is ahad and whether it contradicts the consensus of the scholars? Are you willing to submit your personal leanings to the collective judgment of the ummah?
?
When you encounter a hadith that challenges something you believe, do you automatically accept it as proof, or do you pause to consider its chain and status? This lesson forces you to ask: Is my faith built on solid, definitive proofs, or on isolated reports that may not stand against ijma?
?
What would it cost you to abandon a cherished opinion if you discovered it relied on an ahad hadith that conflicts with consensus? Can you honestly say you would surrender it for the sake of following the way of the Salaf?

Refutation of Eternal Determination: The Agent Must Be a Free Chooser
Juwayni refutes the claim that an eternal cause could necessitate the world without choice.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Juwayni argues that the world's agent cannot be a mere necessitating cause (موجب) but must be a free chooser (مؤثر مختار) who specifies by will.

Core Idea
A blind cause cannot choose; only a free agent can pick one equal possibility.

In this passage, Juwayni confronts a powerful objection: could an eternal cause produce the world without any priority, like a cause-effect relationship? He first pauses to invoke divine guidance, then systematically refutes the idea. The key move is a dichotomy: the determinant is either a necessitator without choice or a free chooser. Using a vivid analogy from medicine and an argument about spatial location, Juwayni shows that a necessitator cannot specify a contingent world. Only a free agent with will can do so.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection and Juwayni's call for reflection
Read the opening objection carefully. Juwayni advises the reader to pause and pray. Notice the seriousness of the issue.
The objection claims the world is eternal, caused by an eternal determinant with no priority.
2
Identify the two possible determinants
After stating that contingent things need a determinant, Juwayni presents a dichotomy: the determinant must be either a necessitator without choice or a free chooser. Note the terms: موجب and مؤثر مختار.
This is a fundamental division in Islamic theology.
3
Understand why a necessitator is impossible
Juwayni gives an example from natural medicine: a drug that attracts yellow bile cannot, as a necessitator, fail to attract equally under identical conditions. Similarly, the world in space: if a necessitator existed, it would have to specify the world's location, but all locations are equal, so specification is impossible without choice.
The argument relies on the principle that a necessitator produces identical effects under identical conditions.
4
Conclude that only a free chooser is possible
Since a necessitator cannot specify, the only remaining option is a free chooser who specifies by will and volition. This refutes the claim that the world could be eternal with a mere cause.
Juwayni's conclusion establishes divine freedom and contingency of the world.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فان قيل : بم تنكرون على من يزعم أن العالم بما فيه قديم لا مبتدأ لكونه ، ولا مفتتح لوجوده ، لاختصاصه بما هو عليه ، بمقتضى قديم هو في حكم العلة ، والعالم في حكم المعلول والعلة والمعلول والموجب والموجب ، متلازمان ولا يسبق أحدهما التالي ؟ واذا انتهى مولانا إلى هذا المنتهى تثبت قليلا وتأمل برأيه الثاقب الوقاد على رسل وانشاد وابتهل الى الله جلت قدرته وهو ولى التأييد والارشاد فنقول - والله المستعان وعليه التكلان - اذا بطل ثبوت الجائزات من غير مقتضى قسمنا الكلام وراء ذلك . وقلنا : مقتضى العالم لا يخلو اما أن يكون موجبا من غير ايثار واختيار ، وأما أن يكون مؤثرا مختارا فان كان موجبا من غير ايثار كان ذلك مستحيلا • فان الموجب الذى لا يؤثر يستحيل أن يقتضى شيئا دون مماثلة ، وهذا يتضح بأن نضرب فاسد مذهب الطبائعى مثلا . فنقول : اذا قال : من ينتحل القول بآثار الطبائع : ان دواء مخصوصا يجذب المرة الصفراء دون غيرها من الاخلاط يستحيل عنده أن يجذب جزءا من المرة من القطر ، ولا يجذب جزءا آخر فى مثل ) ذلك القطر ( بعد ذلك المجذوب مع ارتفاع المواقع ، واستواء الأحوال .. هذا محال تخيله . واذا تقرر ذلك قلنا : العالم بحملته قار في جو معلوم ، وتقديره واقعا في ذلك الخلاء يماثل تقديره في خلاء عن اليمين أو عن الشمال .. وهذا يقرب من مدارك البدائة ، واذا تماثلت الأحياز والجهات ، استحال اعتقاد موجب يخصص العالم بقطر تمثله سائر الأقطار ، فان الموجب لا يخصص شيئا من أمثاله ، والمؤثر المختار : هو الذى يتحيز بارادته ومشيئته مثلا من أمثال . فلاح بطلان المصير الى موجب قديم لا اختيار له .
For it is said: 'How do you reject the one who claims that the world and what it contains is eternal, with no beginning to its being, and no commencement to its existence, due to its specification as it is, by an eternal determinant which is in the position of the cause, and the world is in the position of the effect, and the cause and the effect, and the necessitator and the necessitated, are concomitant, and neither precedes the other?' And when our master reaches this point, let him pause a little and ponder with his piercing brilliant mind slowly, and supplicate and pray to God, whose power is exalted, and He is the patron of support and guidance. Then we say – and God is the seeker of help and upon Him is reliance – if the establishment of contingent things without a determinant is invalid, we divide the discourse beyond that. And we say: the determinant of the world is not free from either being a necessitator without preference and choice, or being an effective chooser. If it is a necessitator without preference, that is impossible. For the necessitator that does not effect is impossible to determine something without likeness. This is clarified by giving an example of the corrupt doctrine of the naturalists. So we say: if someone who adopts the doctrine of the effects of natures says that a particular medicine attracts yellow bile to the exclusion of other humors, it would be impossible in his view for it to attract a part of the bile from a region and not attract another part in a similar region after that attracted part, with the removal of obstacles and equality of conditions. This is impossible to imagine. And when this is established, we say: the world in its entirety is stationary in a known space, and its being supposed to be in that void is similar to it being supposed in a void to the right or to the left. This is close to the level of perception. And when the spaces and directions are similar, it is impossible to believe a necessitator that specifies the world to a region while the other regions are equal to it. For the necessitator does not specify anything among its likes. And the effective chooser is the one that specifies by His will and volition one of the likes. So the invalidity of resorting to an eternal necessitator without choice became clear.
Source pages p. 16

Commentary
This passage presents an objection and its refutation. The objector claims the world could be eternal, existing with an eternal cause that is like a cause-effect relationship: no priority. Juwayni advises the reader to pause and pray, then responds. He argues that if contingent things need a determinant, that determinant must be either a necessitator (موجب) without choice or a free chooser (مؤثر مختار). A necessitator is impossible because it cannot specify one possibility over another when all are equal, illustrated by the example of a drug attracting bile: under identical conditions, a necessitator would produce the same effect always, but the world has many possible states (e.g., location). Only a free chooser can specify by will. Therefore, the agent must be a free chooser, not a mere necessitator.

Key Terms
الموجب
المؤثر المختار
الإيثار
الإرادة
المشيئة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believe that an eternal cause can necessitate the world without choice, making the world eternal and God a mere cause.
Correction: Juwayni shows that a necessitator cannot specify among equals; only a free chooser with will can specify the contingent world.

Checkpoint
Why does Juwayni argue that the world's agent cannot be a mere necessitating cause (موجب)?
A) Because a necessitating cause cannot create something from nothing.
B) Because a necessitating cause cannot specify one possibility among equals.
C) Because a necessitating cause must be eternal.
D) Because a necessitating cause requires a material.

Key Takeaway
Juwayni establishes that the world's agent must be a free chooser with will, not a blind necessitator.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you live as though your life is determined by blind forces or as a result of the deliberate choice of a wise Creator? How does your answer affect your sense of responsibility and trust in Allah's plan for you?
?
Think of a moment when you had to choose between two equally attractive options. What did that choice reveal about your own will? Now consider that the entire universe was chosen by Allah from infinite possibilities—does that change how you see His wisdom and care in creating this world?
?
Juwayni instructed the reader to pause and pray before engaging in this argument. How often do you seek Allah's guidance when thinking about deep matters of faith? Will you commit to praying for clarity before you study theology from now on?

Refutation of the Denial of Human Power's Effect
Juwayni refutes the denial of human power's effect on actions.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's argument that denying the effect of human power leads to absurdity and the nullification of the Sharia.

Core Idea
Denying human power's effect makes God's commands impossible to fulfill.

Juwayni addresses a key theological controversy: does human power have any real effect on actions? Some claimed no, citing God's absolute sovereignty. But Juwayni shows that such a claim contradicts both reason and revelation. In this passage, he refutes this denial by exposing its logical consequences.

Guided Path
1
Identify the false claim
Read the first part of the passage to find the claim that human power has no effect at all.
2
Examine the claimant's defense
Notice how the claimant uses the Quranic verse that God is not questioned to evade the issue.
Juwayni calls this 'a word of truth by which falsehood is intended'.
3
Understand the principle of capacity
Juwayni asserts that from reason and revelation, we know God only commands what servants are capable of doing.
This is a key principle in kalam.
4
See the absurd consequence
If human power has no effect, then demanding actions is like demanding impossible things, such as establishing colors in oneself.
5
Recognize the final verdict
Juwayni concludes that such a denial nullifies the Sharia and rejects the message of the prophets.
This shows the high stakes of the discussion.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ما جاء به المرسلون ، فإن زعم واعم ممن لم يوفق المنهج الرشاد أنه لا أثر لقدرة العبد في مقدوره أصلا، فاذا طولب بمتعلق طلب الله تعالى بفعل العبد تحريما وفرضا ، ذهب فى الجواب طولا وعرضا . وقال : الله أن يفعل ما يشاء ، ولا يتعرض للاعتراض عليه المعترضون ) لا يسأل عما يفعل ، وهم يسألون ( ) الأنبياء ۲۳ ) قيل : ليس لما جئت به حاصل ، كلمة حق أريد بها باطل . نعم يفعل الله ما يشاء ويحكم ما يريد ، ولكن يتقدس عن الخلف ، ونقيض الصدق وقد فهمنا بضرورات العقول من الشرع المنقول : أنه عزت قدرته : طالب عباده بما أخبر أنهم متمكنون من الوفاء به ، ولو يكلفهم الا على مبلغ الطاقة والوسع فى موارد الشرع .. ومن زعم أن لا أثر للقدرة الحادثة في مقدورها ، كما أثر للعلم فى معلومه ، فوجه مطالبة العبد بأفعاله عنده كوجه مطالبته أن يثبت فى نفسه ألوانا وادراكات ، وهذا خروج عن حد الاعتدال الى التزام الباطل والمحال وفيه ابطال الشرع ، ورد ما جاء به النبيون عليهم السلام .
What was brought by the messengers. Then if a claimant claims—and generally one who is not guided to the right path—that there is no effect of the servant's power in his object at all, then when he is asked about the object of God's command regarding the servant's act, prohibition and obligation, he goes far and wide in his response. And he says: 'God does what He wills, and those who object cannot object to Him. (They are not asked about what they do, but they will be asked) [Quran 21:23].' It is said: 'What you have brought has no substance; it is a word of truth by which falsehood is intended.' Yes, God does what He wills and judges what He desires, but He is exalted above contradiction and the opposite of truth. And we have understood by the necessities of intellects from the transmitted law that He—exalted is His power—requires His servants to do what He has informed them they are capable of fulfilling, and He only charges them according to the extent of energy and capacity in the sources of law… And whoever claims that there is no effect of the temporal power in its object, just as there is no effect of knowledge in its known object, then the reason for requiring the servant's actions according to him is like the reason for requiring that he establish in himself colors and perceptions. This is a departure from the limit of moderation to the adoption of falsehood and absurdity, and it involves the nullification of the Sharia and the rejection of what was brought by the prophets, peace be upon them.
Source pages p. 44

Commentary
This passage is a refutation. It begins by stating the false claim: that human power has no effect at all. Juwayni then shows the absurd consequence: when the claimant is pressed to explain how God's commands (obligation and prohibition) relate to human acts, he falls back on saying 'God does as He wills' and cites the Quranic verse that God is not questioned. Juwayni notes that this is a true statement used to support a false conclusion. He then asserts from reason and revelation that God only charges humans with what they are capable of; this is a known principle. To deny any effect of human power would make God's commands like demanding impossible things (e.g., establishing colors in oneself), which is absurd. The conclusion is that such a denial nullifies the Sharia and rejects the message of the prophets.

Key Terms
قدرة العبد
أثر
مقدور
طلب الله
الشرع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's absolute sovereignty means human power has no real effect; commands do not require capacity.
Correction: Juwayni shows that God's commands imply human capacity and that humans have real efficacy in their actions; otherwise, commands would be absurd.

Checkpoint
What does Juwayni say is the consequence of denying any effect of human power in its object?
A) It leads to affirming human creation.
B) It leads to the nullification of the Sharia.
C) It leads to polytheism.
D) It leads to free will.

Key Takeaway
Denying human power's effect leads to absurdity and undermines the entire Sharia.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you sin, do you ever inwardly excuse yourself by thinking 'it was decreed, I had no real choice'? How does Juwayni's assertion that God only commands what you can do strip away that excuse?
?
If you truly believe that your power has a real effect on your actions, what is one area of your life where you now realize you must take full responsibility instead of blaming circumstances?
?
This lesson warns against using a true statement (God does as He wills) to support a false conclusion. Can you identify a belief you hold that might be a 'word of truth used for falsehood'? What would it cost you to correct it?

Causality and Created Things
intermediate
5 min
Temporal Power Has Effect, Servant Is Not Creator
Juwayni concludes on human power and creation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why affirming that human power has an effect does not make the human a creator, and why a single act cannot be caused by two agents.

Core Idea
A single act cannot be split between God and the servant.

Juwayni concludes a key point: human power does have an effect, but that does not make the human a creator. He also argues that a single act cannot be from two powers. This lesson explains the reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the strong conclusion: temporal power has effect, but servant is not creator.
2
Identify the conclusion and its reasoning
The passage states that temporal power has effect, but it is impossible to say the servant is creator. Then it gives the reason: a single act cannot occur by two powers because it is indivisible.
The indivisibility of the act is key.
3
Reflect on the implication
Think about why this matters: if we affirm human effect without calling the human a creator, we avoid both innovation and the error of denying accountability.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فاذ به لزم المصير الى أن القدرة الحادثة تؤثر في مقدورها، واستحال اطلاق القول بأن العبد خالق أعماله فإن فيه الخروج عما درج عليه سلف الأمة واقتحام ورطات الضلال، ولا سبيل الى المصير الى وقوع فعل العبد بقدرته الحادثة . والقدرة القديمة، فان الفعل الواحد يستحيل حدوثه بقادرين، إذ الواحد لا ينقسم، فإن وقع بقدرة الله سبحانه استقل بها للعبد فلاحتمال صرفها الى جميعها سميت كلية كما يقال : أقبل على العلم بكليته یعنى بجميع قواه وسمى توجيهها وجهة خاصة أرادة جزئية لتحدد الاتجاه فيها فالأولى حقيقة موجودة، والثانية أمر اعتباری منتزع من بين المريد والمراد ، كباقى المعانى المصدرية ، فلا يكون : لمعنى الكلى والجزئي في مصطلح المناطقة أى مناسبة هنا ليمكن التشغيب بأن الكلى مفقود، والجزئى هو الموجود على خلاف رأي الماتريدية فى الارادة الكلية والجزئية فليتفطن (ز) وسقط أثر القدرة الحادثة ويستحيل أن يقع بعضه بقدرة الله جل وعز .. فان الفعل الواحد لا بعض له. وهذه مهواة لا يسلم من غوائلها الا مرشد موفق اذا المرء بين أن يسعى الاستبداد بالخلق ، وبين أن يخرج نفسه عن كونه مطالبا بالشرائع ، وفيه ابطال دعوة الأنبياء عليهم السلام ، وبين أن يثبت نفسه شريكا الله تبارك وتعالى في ايجاد الفعل الواحد ، وهذه الأقسام بجملتها باطلة
Thus it follows necessarily that we must affirm that the temporal power has an effect in its object, and it is impossible to say that the servant is the creator of his actions, for in that there is a departure from the precedent of the predecessors of the community and a plunging into difficulties of error. And there is no way to attribute the occurrence of the servant's act to both his temporal power and the eternal power, for a single act is impossible to occur by two powers, since the one is indivisible. So if it occurs by the power of God—glorified is He—it is independent of the servant. And if we consider its direction to all possibilities, it is called universal will, as it is said: 'He devoted himself entirely to knowledge,' meaning with all his powers. And its direction to a particular direction is called particular will, specifying the orientation towards it. The first is an existing reality, and the second is a derivative matter extracted from between the one who wills and the object of will, like other source-derived meanings. So there is no relevance to the terms 'universal' and 'particular' in the terminology of logicians here to allow quibbling that the universal is missing and the particular is the existing, contrary to the opinion of the Maturidis regarding universal and particular will. So let one be attentive. And the effect of the temporal power falls, and it is impossible for part of it to occur by the power of God, mighty and majestic, for a single act has no part. And this is a dangerous valley from whose disasters none is saved except a successful guide. For a person is between seeking to be independent in creation, and between removing himself from being obligated by the laws, which invalidates the call of the prophets, peace be upon them, and between establishing himself as a partner to God, blessed and exalted, in bringing about the single act. And all of these divisions are altogether false.
Source pages p. 44

Commentary
This passage is a doctrinal conclusion followed by a classification of false positions. It begins by stating the necessary consequence: temporal power does have an effect, but the servant is not a creator (that would be innovation). The reason given is that a single act cannot be caused by two independent powers because it is indivisible. Juwayni then digresses to clarify terms of will: universal will directs to all possibilities, particular will to a specific one. He notes that logicians' usage of universal and particular does not apply here, contrary to Maturidis. Returning to the main argument, he asserts that if the act is by God's power, the temporal power's effect is null. He then describes three false positions a person might fall into: (1) claiming independence in creation, (2) denying obligation (i.e., not being accountable), (3) claiming partnership with God in creating the act. All these are false, and the issue is a dangerous valley from which only a successful guide can escape.

Key Terms
قدرة حادثة
خالق
فعل واحد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that if human power has an effect, then humans must be creators of their actions.
Correction: Juwayni affirms effect but denies creation; the act is indivisible and cannot be from two creators.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, why is it impossible for a single act to be produced by both the servant's power and God's power?
A) Because God is too mighty.
B) Because the one act is indivisible and cannot be caused by two independent agents.
C) Because the servant's power is non-existent.
D) Because God does not share His power.

Key Takeaway
Temporal power has effect, but the servant is not creator; a single act cannot be from two agents because it is indivisible.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you accomplish something good, do you secretly feel that you are the creator of that success? How does Juwayni's insistence that you are not a creator challenge your pride and reshape your gratitude?
?
Can you identify a moment today when you acted as if your power were independent, forgetting that your act is a single, indivisible event that only God can originate? What will you do to remind yourself of your true dependence?
?
If a single act cannot be from two agents, then what are the implications for how you pray? Do you sometimes ask God for help while simultaneously trusting in your own ability—as if the act were shared? How will you surrender that illusion today?

Causality and Created Things
