6 min
Universal Will, Particular Will, and the Maturidi View
Juwayni explains universal and particular will and critiques Maturidis.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's distinction between universal and particular will, and his critique of the Maturidi position on these terms.

Core Idea
Universal will is real; particular will is derivative – not to be confused with logical universals.

Juwayni digresses to clarify two types of will: universal and particular. He explains that the universal is a real existing quality, while the particular is a conceptual derivative. He warns against equating these with the logicians' categories and critiques the Maturidi view.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on will
Read the Arabic passage that defines universal and particular will. Notice how Juwayni describes each.
The example 'He devoted himself entirely to knowledge' illustrates universality.
2
Distinguish the two types of will
Universal will is directed to all possibilities and is real; particular will is directed to a specific direction and is a derivative concept.
The first is a reality, the second is extracted from the relation between willer and willed.
3
Understand the critique of Maturidis
Juwayni argues that the logicians' terms of universal and particular do not apply here, contrary to the Maturidis. He insists that the universal will is existent, not missing.
He says 'let one be attentive' – a warning against confusion.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فاذ به لزم المصير الى أن القدرة الحادثة تؤثر في مقدورها، واستحال اطلاق القول بأن العبد خالق أعماله فإن فيه الخروج عما درج عليه سلف الأمة واقتحام ورطات الضلال، ولا سبيل الى المصير الى وقوع فعل العبد بقدرته الحادثة . والقدرة القديمة، فان الفعل الواحد يستحيل حدوثه بقادرين، إذ الواحد لا ينقسم، فإن وقع بقدرة الله سبحانه استقل بها للعبد فلاحتمال صرفها الى جميعها سميت كلية كما يقال : أقبل على العلم بكليته یعنى بجميع قواه وسمى توجيهها وجهة خاصة أرادة جزئية لتحدد الاتجاه فيها فالأولى حقيقة موجودة، والثانية أمر اعتباری منتزع من بين المريد والمراد ، كباقى المعانى المصدرية ، فلا يكون : لمعنى الكلى والجزئي في مصطلح المناطقة أى مناسبة هنا ليمكن التشغيب بأن الكلى مفقود، والجزئى هو الموجود على خلاف رأي الماتريدية فى الارادة الكلية والجزئية فليتفطن (ز) وسقط أثر القدرة الحادثة ويستحيل أن يقع بعضه بقدرة الله جل وعز .. فان الفعل الواحد لا بعض له. وهذه مهواة لا يسلم من غوائلها الا مرشد موفق اذا المرء بين أن يسعى الاستبداد بالخلق ، وبين أن يخرج نفسه عن كونه مطالبا بالشرائع ، وفيه ابطال دعوة الأنبياء عليهم السلام ، وبين أن يثبت نفسه شريكا الله تبارك وتعالى في ايجاد الفعل الواحد ، وهذه الأقسام بجملتها باطلة
Thus it follows necessarily that we must affirm that the temporal power has an effect in its object, and it is impossible to say that the servant is the creator of his actions, for in that there is a departure from the precedent of the predecessors of the community and a plunging into difficulties of error. And there is no way to attribute the occurrence of the servant's act to both his temporal power and the eternal power, for a single act is impossible to occur by two powers, since the one is indivisible. So if it occurs by the power of God—glorified is He—it is independent of the servant. And if we consider its direction to all possibilities, it is called universal will, as it is said: 'He devoted himself entirely to knowledge,' meaning with all his powers. And its direction to a particular direction is called particular will, specifying the orientation towards it. The first is an existing reality, and the second is a derivative matter extracted from between the one who wills and the object of will, like other source-derived meanings. So there is no relevance to the terms 'universal' and 'particular' in the terminology of logicians here to allow quibbling that the universal is missing and the particular is the existing, contrary to the opinion of the Maturidis regarding universal and particular will. So let one be attentive. And the effect of the temporal power falls, and it is impossible for part of it to occur by the power of God, mighty and majestic, for a single act has no part. And this is a dangerous valley from whose disasters none is saved except a successful guide. For a person is between seeking to be independent in creation, and between removing himself from being obligated by the laws, which invalidates the call of the prophets, peace be upon them, and between establishing himself as a partner to God, blessed and exalted, in bringing about the single act. And all of these divisions are altogether false.
Source pages p. 44

Commentary
This passage is a doctrinal conclusion followed by a classification of false positions. It begins by stating the necessary consequence: temporal power does have an effect, but the servant is not a creator (that would be innovation). The reason given is that a single act cannot be caused by two independent powers because it is indivisible. Juwayni then digresses to clarify terms of will: universal will directs to all possibilities, particular will to a specific one. He notes that logicians' usage of universal and particular does not apply here, contrary to Maturidis. Returning to the main argument, he asserts that if the act is by God's power, the temporal power's effect is null. He then describes three false positions a person might fall into: (1) claiming independence in creation, (2) denying obligation (i.e., not being accountable), (3) claiming partnership with God in creating the act. All these are false, and the issue is a dangerous valley from which only a successful guide can escape.

Key Terms
إرادة كلية
إرادة جزئية
ماتريدية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Maturidi view that universal will is missing and particular will is the only existing will.
Correction: Juwayni holds that universal will is a real existing attribute, and particular will is a derivative concept.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, which type of will is a real existing reality?
A) Universal will
B) Particular will
C) Both
D) Neither

Key Takeaway
Universal will is a real attribute; particular will is derivative; do not confuse with logical terms, contrary to Maturidis.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you study theological terms like 'universal will' and 'particular will', do you tend to borrow meanings from everyday language or other fields without checking their precise technical definitions? How has that caused confusion in your own understanding of Allah's attributes? Will you commit to verifying technical meanings before drawing conclusions?
?
Juwayni warns against quibbling over words without understanding the intended usage. In your discussions about religion with others, do you sometimes argue over terminology while missing the deeper point? Is your goal to win an argument or to arrive at truth? What can you change starting today to ensure your debates lead to clarity rather than confusion?
?
The Maturidis made a subtle but serious error by equating theological and logical universals. What subtle errors in your own beliefs about Allah might be lurking because you have not studied the precise distinctions made by the scholars? Are you willing to humbly examine your views and correct them according to the Qur'an and Sunnah as understood by the salaf? What step will you take this week to deepen your knowledge of Allah's names and attributes?

The Three False Positions on Human Action
Juwayni lists three false positions on human action.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three false positions that Juwayni identifies as dangerous alternatives to his doctrine of human action.

Core Idea
Three false positions: independence, exemption from law, or partnership with God.

Juwayni describes a dangerous valley with three false positions a person might fall into when considering human action: claiming independence in creation, denying accountability, or asserting partnership with God. All are false. This lesson explains each.

Guided Path
1
Read the final part of the passage
Read from 'Wa-saqata atharu al-qudrati al-haditha' to the end. This lists three false positions.
Note the strong warning: 'a dangerous valley from whose disasters none is saved except a successful guide.'
2
Identify each false position
The three positions are: (1) seeking independence in creation, (2) removing oneself from obligation by the laws, (3) establishing partnership with God in bringing about the act.
Each is introduced with 'bayna... wa-bayna...'
3
Understand why each is false
Claiming independence contradicts Quran. Denying obligation invalidates prophets' call. Partnership is impossible because a single act cannot be shared.
The indivisibility argument from earlier applies.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فاذ به لزم المصير الى أن القدرة الحادثة تؤثر في مقدورها، واستحال اطلاق القول بأن العبد خالق أعماله فإن فيه الخروج عما درج عليه سلف الأمة واقتحام ورطات الضلال، ولا سبيل الى المصير الى وقوع فعل العبد بقدرته الحادثة . والقدرة القديمة، فان الفعل الواحد يستحيل حدوثه بقادرين، إذ الواحد لا ينقسم، فإن وقع بقدرة الله سبحانه استقل بها للعبد فلاحتمال صرفها الى جميعها سميت كلية كما يقال : أقبل على العلم بكليته یعنى بجميع قواه وسمى توجيهها وجهة خاصة أرادة جزئية لتحدد الاتجاه فيها فالأولى حقيقة موجودة، والثانية أمر اعتباری منتزع من بين المريد والمراد ، كباقى المعانى المصدرية ، فلا يكون : لمعنى الكلى والجزئي في مصطلح المناطقة أى مناسبة هنا ليمكن التشغيب بأن الكلى مفقود، والجزئى هو الموجود على خلاف رأي الماتريدية فى الارادة الكلية والجزئية فليتفطن (ز) وسقط أثر القدرة الحادثة ويستحيل أن يقع بعضه بقدرة الله جل وعز .. فان الفعل الواحد لا بعض له. وهذه مهواة لا يسلم من غوائلها الا مرشد موفق اذا المرء بين أن يسعى الاستبداد بالخلق ، وبين أن يخرج نفسه عن كونه مطالبا بالشرائع ، وفيه ابطال دعوة الأنبياء عليهم السلام ، وبين أن يثبت نفسه شريكا الله تبارك وتعالى في ايجاد الفعل الواحد ، وهذه الأقسام بجملتها باطلة
Thus it follows necessarily that we must affirm that the temporal power has an effect in its object, and it is impossible to say that the servant is the creator of his actions, for in that there is a departure from the precedent of the predecessors of the community and a plunging into difficulties of error. And there is no way to attribute the occurrence of the servant's act to both his temporal power and the eternal power, for a single act is impossible to occur by two powers, since the one is indivisible. So if it occurs by the power of God—glorified is He—it is independent of the servant. And if we consider its direction to all possibilities, it is called universal will, as it is said: 'He devoted himself entirely to knowledge,' meaning with all his powers. And its direction to a particular direction is called particular will, specifying the orientation towards it. The first is an existing reality, and the second is a derivative matter extracted from between the one who wills and the object of will, like other source-derived meanings. So there is no relevance to the terms 'universal' and 'particular' in the terminology of logicians here to allow quibbling that the universal is missing and the particular is the existing, contrary to the opinion of the Maturidis regarding universal and particular will. So let one be attentive. And the effect of the temporal power falls, and it is impossible for part of it to occur by the power of God, mighty and majestic, for a single act has no part. And this is a dangerous valley from whose disasters none is saved except a successful guide. For a person is between seeking to be independent in creation, and between removing himself from being obligated by the laws, which invalidates the call of the prophets, peace be upon them, and between establishing himself as a partner to God, blessed and exalted, in bringing about the single act. And all of these divisions are altogether false.

Commentary
This passage is a doctrinal conclusion followed by a classification of false positions. It begins by stating the necessary consequence: temporal power does have an effect, but the servant is not a creator (that would be innovation). The reason given is that a single act cannot be caused by two independent powers because it is indivisible. Key Terms
استبداد بالخلق
مطالب بالشرائع
شريك الله

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that if humans are not independent creators, then they are not accountable for their actions.
Correction: Juwayni says removing obligation is false; humans are accountable even though God creates actions.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, which of the following is NOT one of the false positions?
A) Seeking independence in creation
B) Removing oneself from obligation
C) Affirming that God creates actions and humans acquire them
D) Establishing partnership in creation

Key Takeaway
The three false extremes are independence, exemption from law, and partnership; all are false, and the truth is the middle path of kasb.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever caught yourself attributing your successes solely to your own effort, forgetting that every ability comes from Allah? How does the first false position (independence in creation) expose the danger of this mindset, and what step can you take today to actively acknowledge Allah's role in your achievements?
?
When you fall into sin, do you sometimes whisper to yourself, 'It's Allah's decree, so I'm not to blame'? How does Juwayni's second false position (exemption from law) reveal this as a trap that invalidates the call of the prophets? What specific action will you take to repent sincerely instead of excusing yourself?
?
Think of a moment when you felt you 'made something happen' through your own strength or intelligence, as if you were a partner with Allah in creating the outcome. How does the truth that 'a single act has no parts' demolish this subtle sense of partnership? Will you commit to reciting 'La hawla wa la quwwata illa billah' whenever you feel a sense of personal ownership over your deeds?

Divine Transcendence
intermediate
10 min
The Principle of Impossible Attributes
Juwayni states the rule: attributes requiring a specifier are impossible for God.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the general principle that any attribute that requires a specifier in creatures is impossible for God, and how this rule applies to attributes like direction.

Core Idea
Attributes needing a specifier in creatures are impossible for God.

In this passage, Imam al-Juwayni establishes a foundational principle for understanding divine transcendence: whatever requires a cause or specifier in creation cannot be attributed to God. He explains that attributes like temporality, composition, and spatial location are possible in creatures – they could be otherwise – so they imply need for a specifier. Since God is absolutely independent, such attributes are impossible for Him. Juwayni then connects this to the famous hadith 'Whoever knows himself knows his Lord,' and applies the principle to the issue of God being restricted to a direction.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that Juwayni uses a very structured argument: principle, rule, examples, hadith, application.
2
Identify the general principle
Find the sentence that states the core principle about attributes that require a specifier (mukhaṣṣiṣ). Understand what a specifier is and why it matters.
3
Understand the rule linking possibility (jawāz) to need
Juwayni says that every attribute that is possible (could be otherwise) depends on a specifier. Why does possibility imply need?
Remember: God is eternal (qadīm) and possibility (jawāz) is a characteristic of temporal things.
4
Examine the examples
Look at the attributes listed: temporality, composition, spatial occupation, limitation, measure, etc. Why are these impossible for God?
These are all attributes that in us are possible, so they require a specifier.
5
Reflect on the hadith and its application
Read Juwayni's explanation of the hadith. Then see how he applies the principle to direction (jiha). Ask yourself: why is direction impossible for God?
This shows the practical use of the principle to refute anthropomorphism.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الكلام فيما يستحيل على الله عز وجل. تقدم قولا وجيزا يحوى الغرض، فإن رأيناه كافيا اجترينا به، وإن رأينا أن نبسط طرفا من الكلام جرينا فيه على ما تجرى به المقادير، والله سبحانه ولى التيسير. فنقول: كل صفة في المخلوقات دل ثبوتها على مخصص يؤثرها ويريدها ولا يعقل ثبوتها دون ذلك فهي مستحيلة على الإله، فانها لو ثبتت له لدلت على افتقاره إلى مخصص دلالتها في حق الحادث المخلوق. وضبط القول في الصفات المفتقرة: ما تمهد أولا من تقدير حكم الجواز فكل صفة فارقها حكم الجواز، فهي مستحيلة في نعت الإله تعالى. فإن القدم والجواز متناقضان. وتفصيل ذلك: أن الحدوث فينا منعوت بالجواز، فنقدس الإله عنه. والتركب والتصور عنه والتقدر في صفاتنا مرسومة بالجواز فلا تركب ولا يجوز فرض خلافه. ولا قد، ولا قدر، ولا حد، ولا طول ولا عرض إلا والعقل يجوز أمثالها وخلافها، وهذه الصفات لجوازها افتقرت إلى تخصيص بارئها، فتعالى الصانع عنها. وهذا معنى قول سيد البشر خاتم النبيين محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم إذ قال: «من عرف نفسه عرف ربه». أراد من عرف نفسه بصفات الافتقار، عرف استغناء الرب عن صفاته فإنه تقدست أسماؤه منتهى الحاجة، وهو بريء عنها. وعلى هذا الأصل يجب تقدس صانع العالم عن الاختصاص ببعض الجهات فإن العقل قاض بجواز التكوين في جهة دون أمثالها كما يقضي بجواز التصور والتقدر.
Discourse on what is impossible for God, Mighty and Majestic. A concise statement containing the goal has preceded. If we see it sufficient, we will be content with it, and if we see that we should expand a portion of the discourse, we will proceed in it according to what the circumstances dictate. God, exalted, is the Facilitator. So we say: Every attribute in created things whose existence indicates a specifier (mukhaṣṣiṣ) that effects them and wills them and whose existence is inconceivable without that, it is impossible for the deity (ilāh), for if it were established for Him, it would indicate His need for a specifier just as it indicates in the case of the temporally originated created thing. And the rule regarding the attributes that are in need [of a specifier] is: what was first established from the estimation of the ruling of possibility (jawāz), so every attribute from which the ruling of possibility departs is impossible in the description of God, exalted. For eternity (qidam) and possibility (jawāz) are contradictory. And its detail: that temporality (ḥudūth) in us is described by possibility, so we sanctify the deity from it. And composition (tarkīb) and spatial occupation (taṣawwur) and limitation (taqaddur) in our attributes are marked by possibility, so [there is] no composition, nor is it permissible to assume its contrary. And no measure (qadd), no power (qadr), no boundary (ḥadd), no length, no width, except that the intellect permits their like and their opposite. And these attributes, due to their possibility, depend on the specification of their Creator, so the Maker is exalted above them. And this is the meaning of the saying of the master of mankind, the Seal of the Prophets, Muhammad, may God bless him and grant him peace, when he said: 'Whoever knows himself knows his Lord.' He meant: whoever knows himself by the attributes of need knows the independence of the Lord from attributes. For He—exalted are His names—is the ultimate of need, and He is free from them. And upon this principle it is necessary that the Maker of the world be sanctified from being restricted to some directions (jihāt), for the intellect judges the possibility of [His] bringing into existence (takwīn) in one direction without its likes, just as it judges the possibility of spatial occupation (taṣawwur) and limitation (taqaddur).
Source pages p. 21

Commentary
This passage establishes the general principle for determining which attributes are impossible for God. Juwayni begins by stating that every attribute in creatures that requires a specifier (mukhaṣṣiṣ) to bring it about is impossible for God, because if it were attributed to God, it would indicate that He needs a specifier, which is impossible. The rule is that any attribute that is subject to possibility (jawāz)—meaning it could be otherwise—is impossible for God, because possibility implies contingency and need for a specifier. Eternity (qidam) and possibility are contradictory. He gives examples: temporality, composition, spatial occupation, and limitation are all possible in creatures, so they are impossible for God. He then quotes the hadith 'Whoever knows himself knows his Lord,' explaining that it means that when a person recognizes his own attributes of need, he recognizes that God is free from such attributes. Finally, he applies this principle to the issue of direction (jihāt): since the intellect judges that creation could occur in any direction, direction is a possible attribute, so God cannot be restricted to a particular direction.

Key Terms
مخصص (mukhaṣṣiṣ)
جواز (jawāz)
مستحيل (mustaḥīl)
افتقار (iftiqār)
جهة (jiha)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The hadith 'Whoever knows himself knows his Lord' means one can achieve unity with God or that self-knowledge is equivalent to knowledge of God.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies that it means recognizing one's own attributes of need (like temporality, composition) leads to recognizing that God is free from such attributes, i.e., independent. It is not pantheism or self-deification.

Checkpoint
According to the hadith 'Whoever knows himself knows his Lord', what does it mean?
A) One can become one with God.
B) Recognizing one's need leads to recognizing God's independence.
C) Self-knowledge is the same as knowledge of God.
D) Only prophets can know God.

Key Takeaway
The key principle: any attribute that in creation indicates need for a specifier is impossible for God, as it would imply dependence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have just learned that any attribute in you that requires a specifier proves your need for Allah. When you look at your own existence, your health, your knowledge, do you actively recognize that they are continuously specified by Allah every moment, or do you feel a false sense of independence? What change can you make today to live with that awareness?
?
The hadith says 'Whoever knows himself knows his Lord.' After studying Juwayni's explanation, how often do you pause to reflect on your own attributes of need—like your need for air, food, or guidance—and let them drive you to gratitude and humility before the Self-Sufficient? What specific time in your daily routine can you use for this reflection?
?
Juwayni asserts that possibility and eternity are contradictory. Your entire life is marked by possibility—every outcome could have been different. Does this realization unsettle your attachment to worldly plans? How can you channel that unsettlement into a deeper reliance on the Eternal, who alone is free from all possibility and change?

Pages p. 21 · Divine Transcendence

Module 9 · Lesson 2

Divine Transcendence
intermediate
6 min
Application of Contingency to Direction
Juwayni applies the contingency principle to negate direction from Allah.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni applies the principle of contingency to negate direction from Allah, establishing divine transcendence.

Core Idea
Direction is contingent; the Necessary Being cannot be confined.

Juwayni now applies the foundational principle of contingency to a specific attribute: direction. Since direction is a contingent matter, it cannot be attributed to the necessary Being. This leads to the crucial Islamic doctrine of divine transcendence beyond spatial confines.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once. Notice how Juwayni starts with 'upon this principle' and then states the conclusion.
2
Identify the principle being applied
The phrase 'upon this principle' refers to the contingency of the world. Juwayni uses it to argue about direction.
3
Understand the reasoning
The intellect judges that origination in one direction is permissible, just as being pictured or measured is permissible. Since direction is permissible (ja'iz), it cannot be for the necessary Being.
This is a direct application of the earlier distinction between necessary, impossible, and contingent.
4
Note the consequence
The consequence is that Allah must be sanctified from being particularized with any direction. This is a core doctrine of Islamic theology.
5
Recognize the divisive nature
Juwayni notes that this issue is a 'slippery place' that caused division. This sets up the refutation of deviant sects in subsequent lessons.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وعلى هذا الأصل يجب تقدس صانع العالم عن الاختصاص ببعض الجهات، فإن العقل قاض بجواز التكوين في جهة دون أمثالها كما يقضي بجواز التصور والتقدير، ثم لزم انتفاء الاختصاص بالاقرار ذاته من حيث كانت جائزة والتخصص بالجهات والأقطار في قضية الجواز كالاختصاص بالاقرار. وهذا مزلة الأقدام، ومثار خلال الأنام، وعندها افترق جماهير الخلق فريقين. وثبتت الفرقة المحققة الناجية. ولا بد من التنبيه على سبب الافتراق، وإيضاح ما استحث أهل الحق على الثبات واجتناب الشتات. فذهبت طوائف إلى وصف الرب بما تقدس في جلاله عنه عن التحيز بالجهة، حتى انتهى غلاة إلى التشكيك أو التمثيل أو التمسيك تعالى الله عن قول الزائغين. والذي دعاهم إلى ذلك طلبهم ربهم من المحسوسات، وما يتشكل في الأوهام ويتقدر في مجازي الوساوس، وخواطر الهواجس. وهذا عيد بالكلية عن صفات الإلهية، وأي فرق بين هؤلاء وبين من يعبد بعض الأجرام العلوية، ولو اجتمع الأولون والآخرون على أن يدركوا بهذا المسلك: الروح. وهو خلق الله تعالى لم يجدوا إليه سبيلا، فإنه معقول غير محسوس. وقد قال تبارك وتعالى في محكم كتابه، الذي لا يأتيه الباطل من بين يديه ولا من خلفه: «ويسألونك عن الروح؟ قل الروح من أمر ربي، وما أوتيتم من العلم إلا قليلا». وذهبت طائفة إلى التعطيل من حيث تقاعدت عقولهم عن درك حقيقة الإله، فظنوا أن ما لا يحويه الفكر منتف، ولو وفقوا لعلموا أنه لا تبعد معرفة موجود مع العجز عن درك حقيقة، والذي ضربناه من الروح مثلا يعارض به هؤلاء.
And upon this principle, the Maker of the world must be sanctified from being particularized with some directions, for the intellect judges the permissibility of origination in one direction without its likes, just as it judges the permissibility of being pictured and measured. Then the negation of particularization with measures necessarily follows inasmuch as it was permissible, and specialization with directions and regions in the case of contingency is like particularization with measures. This is a slippery place for feet, and a cause of trouble among people, and because of it the masses of creation split into two groups. And the true, saved group was established. It is necessary to alert to the cause of the division, and to clarify what impelled the people of truth to steadfastness and avoidance of dispersion. Some groups went to describing the Lord (exalted is His majesty above that) with confinement to a direction, until the extremists ended up in doubt or anthropomorphism or [؟] — God is exalted above the saying of the deviators. And what called them to that was their seeking their Lord through sensory things, and what takes shape in imaginations and is measured in the metaphors of whispers and thoughts of obsessions. This is a complete deviation from the attributes of divinity. And what difference is there between these and those who worship some celestial bodies? And even if the former and the latter gathered to perceive the spirit through this path — and it is a creation of God Almighty — they would not find a way to it, for it is known intellectually, not sensibly. And God Almighty said in the firm text of His Book, which falsehood cannot come to it from before it or behind it: 'And they ask you about the spirit. Say: the spirit is from the command of my Lord, and you have not been given of knowledge except a little.' And another group went to negation (ta'til) because their intellects fell short of grasping the reality of the Divine, so they thought that what the mind does not encompass is nonexistent. Had they been guided, they would have known that knowledge of an existent is not far-fetched while being incapable of grasping its reality. And what we have struck as an example of the spirit is used to refute these.
Source pages p. 21

Commentary
This passage applies the previous principle to the issue of direction and refutes two deviant sects. Juwayni first states that based on the principle of contingency, the Creator must be free from being confined to any particular direction, because the intellect considers direction as permissible (ja'iz). Just as the intellect can conceive of God being in any direction or none, direction is a contingent attribute and thus impossible for God. He then notes that this issue is a cause of major division among people; the saved group adheres to the correct negation of direction. He identifies two misguided groups: those who describe God with confinement to a direction (anthropomorphism), including extremists who fall into doubt (tashkik), anthropomorphism (tamthil), or [؟]. Their error stems from trying to perceive God through the senses and imagination. Juwayni compares them to idol worshippers and argues that even if they tried to perceive the spirit — a created thing — through sensory means, they could not, because the spirit is intellectually known (ma'qul) but not sensible (ghayr mahsus). He cites the Quranic verse about the spirit to support this. The second group is the mu'attila who negate God's attributes and existence because their intellects cannot grasp His true reality. They erroneously conclude that what cannot be fully conceived does not exist. Juwayni counters by pointing to the spirit: we know it exists despite not knowing its true nature. Thus, the example of the spirit refutes both sides.

Key Terms
جهة
تحيز
جواز

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
What principle does Juwayni apply to negate direction from Allah?
A) The principle of causality
B) The principle of contingency (al-imkan)
C) The principle of perfection
D) The principle of transcendence

Key Takeaway
Direction is a contingent attribute; therefore, the Necessary Being is free from it.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you pray, do you instinctively imagine Allah in a certain direction? How does this passage challenge that habit? Will you actively remind yourself of His transcendence before your next prayer?
?
What attachments to physical space or direction in your own life might you be projecting onto Allah? How can you train your heart to relate to a God who is beyond all directions?
?
The passage calls this issue a 'slippery place for feet.' Have you ever felt confused or unsettled about Allah's 'where'? What concrete step will you take to ground yourself in the truth that He is not confined by space?

Divine Transcendence
intermediate
7 min
Refutation of Anthropomorphists (Mushabbiha)
Juwayni refutes those who attribute direction and anthropomorphism to Allah.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why describing Allah with direction leads to anthropomorphism and how Juwayni refutes it using the spirit example.

Core Idea
Seeking God through the senses leads to anthropomorphism.

Juwayni now turns to those who mistakenly described Allah with direction and even anthropomorphism. He explains that their error stems from trying to perceive the divine through sensory faculties, and he compares them to idolaters. The spirit example serves as a powerful refutation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic passage describing the first deviant group and Juwayni's refutation.
2
Identify the error of the first sect
They describe Allah with confinement to a direction, leading to doubt, anthropomorphism, or worse. Their root cause is seeking Allah through sensory things.
Juwayni uses strong language: 'complete deviation from the attributes of divinity'.
3
Understand the comparison to idolaters
Juwayni asks: what difference is there between these people and those who worship celestial bodies? This highlights the gravity of their error.
4
Examine the spirit example
The spirit is a created thing that is known intellectually but not sensibly. Even if they tried to perceive it through the senses, they could not. This shows that existence can be beyond sensory perception.
Juwayni cites the Quranic verse about the spirit to reinforce this point.
5
Apply the spirit example to refute anthropomorphism
If even a created thing like the spirit cannot be perceived by the senses, how can one expect to perceive the Creator through the senses? Thus the anthropomorphist approach is invalid.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وعلى هذا الأصل يجب تقدس صانع العالم عن الاختصاص ببعض الجهات، فإن العقل قاض بجواز التكوين في جهة دون أمثالها كما يقضي بجواز التصور والتقدير، ثم لزم انتفاء الاختصاص بالاقرار ذاته من حيث كانت جائزة والتخصص بالجهات والأقطار في قضية الجواز كالاختصاص بالاقرار. وهذا مزلة الأقدام، ومثار خلال الأنام، وعندها افترق جماهير الخلق فريقين. وثبتت الفرقة المحققة الناجية. ولا بد من التنبيه على سبب الافتراق، وإيضاح ما استحث أهل الحق على الثبات واجتناب الشتات. فذهبت طوائف إلى وصف الرب بما تقدس في جلاله عنه عن التحيز بالجهة، حتى انتهى غلاة إلى التشكيك أو التمثيل أو التمسيك تعالى الله عن قول الزائغين. والذي دعاهم إلى ذلك طلبهم ربهم من المحسوسات، وما يتشكل في الأوهام ويتقدر في مجازي الوساوس، وخواطر الهواجس. وهذا عيد بالكلية عن صفات الإلهية، وأي فرق بين هؤلاء وبين من يعبد بعض الأجرام العلوية، ولو اجتمع الأولون والآخرون على أن يدركوا بهذا المسلك: الروح. وهو خلق الله تعالى لم يجدوا إليه سبيلا، فإنه معقول غير محسوس. وقد قال تبارك وتعالى في محكم كتابه، الذي لا يأتيه الباطل من بين يديه ولا من خلفه: «ويسألونك عن الروح؟ قل الروح من أمر ربي، وما أوتيتم من العلم إلا قليلا». وذهبت طائفة إلى التعطيل من حيث تقاعدت عقولهم عن درك حقيقة الإله، فظنوا أن ما لا يحويه الفكر منتف، ولو وفقوا لعلموا أنه لا تبعد معرفة موجود مع العجز عن درك حقيقة، والذي ضربناه من الروح مثلا يعارض به هؤلاء.

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believe that Allah must be perceptible by the senses if He exists.
Correction: Juwayni shows that even created entities like the spirit exist without being sensed; thus, existence does not require sensory perception.

Checkpoint
What example does Juwayni use to refute the anthropomorphists?
A) The throne of Allah
B) The spirit (al-ruh)
C) The angels
D) The celestial bodies

Key Takeaway
Anthropomorphists err by demanding sensory perception of Allah; the spirit example proves that existence transcends the senses.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think of Allah, do you find yourself picturing something in your mind? How does Juwayni's warning about seeking God through the senses challenge you to purify your conception of Him? Are you willing to let go of any imagined form you've held and instead affirm His transcendence, even if it feels uncomfortable?
?
The spirit is a creation of Allah that you cannot see, touch, or imagine—yet you know it exists within you. If you are certain of the spirit without sensory proof, why do you sometimes demand sensory proof for the existence of Allah or His attributes? What does your hesitation reveal about your heart's attachment to the material world?
?
Juwayni compares anthropomorphists to idolaters who worship celestial bodies. Examine your own worship: are there any 'celestial bodies'—status, wealth, recognition—that you treat as if they hold divine power or importance? Commit to redirecting your heart's devotion solely to the One who is beyond all physical limitations.

Divine Transcendence
intermediate
5 min
Refutation of the Negators (Mu'attila)
Juwayni refutes those who negate Allah due to inability to comprehend.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni refutes those who deny Allah's existence due to inability to fully comprehend Him, using the spirit example.

Core Idea
Inability to fully comprehend does not negate existence.

The second deviant group are those who negated God's attributes and existence altogether, because their intellects could not grasp His true reality. Juwayni refutes them by showing that existence does not require full comprehension. He uses the same spirit example to demonstrate that something can be known to exist even if its true nature is unknown.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the short Arabic passage describing the mu'attila and Juwayni's refutation.
2
Identify the error of the mu'attila
Their intellects failed to grasp the reality of the Divine, so they concluded that what the mind cannot encompass does not exist.
This is a logical fallacy: absence of proof is not proof of absence.
3
Understand the counter-argument
Juwayni states that knowledge of an existent is possible even while being incapable of grasping its true reality. He then references the spirit example already introduced.
4
Apply the spirit example
The spirit is a creation whose true nature is unknown, yet we affirm its existence. Similarly, we can affirm Allah's existence without fully comprehending His essence.
5
Contrast with the previous sect
The anthropomorphists err by demanding sensory perception; the mu'attila err by demanding intellectual comprehension. Both extremes are refuted by the spirit example.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وعلى هذا الأصل يجب تقدس صانع العالم عن الاختصاص ببعض الجهات، فإن العقل قاض بجواز التكوين في جهة دون أمثالها كما يقضي بجواز التصور والتقدير، ثم لزم انتفاء الاختصاص بالاقرار ذاته من حيث كانت جائزة والتخصص بالجهات والأقطار في قضية الجواز كالاختصاص بالاقرار. وهذا مزلة الأقدام، ومثار خلال الأنام، وعندها افترق جماهير الخلق فريقين. وثبتت الفرقة المحققة الناجية. ولا بد من التنبيه على سبب الافتراق، وإيضاح ما استحث أهل الحق على الثبات واجتناب الشتات. فذهبت طوائف إلى وصف الرب بما تقدس في جلاله عنه عن التحيز بالجهة، حتى انتهى غلاة إلى التشكيك أو التمثيل أو التمسيك تعالى الله عن قول الزائغين. والذي دعاهم إلى ذلك طلبهم ربهم من المحسوسات، وما يتشكل في الأوهام ويتقدر في مجازي الوساوس، وخواطر الهواجس. وهذا عيد بالكلية عن صفات الإلهية، وأي فرق بين هؤلاء وبين من يعبد بعض الأجرام العلوية، ولو اجتمع الأولون والآخرون على أن يدركوا بهذا المسلك: الروح. وهو خلق الله تعالى لم يجدوا إليه سبيلا، فإنه معقول غير محسوس. وقد قال تبارك وتعالى في محكم كتابه، الذي لا يأتيه الباطل من بين يديه ولا من خلفه: «ويسألونك عن الروح؟ قل الروح من أمر ربي، وما أوتيتم من العلم إلا قليلا». وذهبت طائفة إلى التعطيل من حيث تقاعدت عقولهم عن درك حقيقة الإله، فظنوا أن ما لا يحويه الفكر منتف، ولو وفقوا لعلموا أنه لا تبعد معرفة موجود مع العجز عن درك حقيقة، والذي ضربناه من الروح مثلا يعارض به هؤلاء.

Then the negation of particularization with measures necessarily follows inasmuch as it was permissible, and specialization with directions and regions in the case of contingency is like particularization with measures. Checkpoint
What faulty assumption did the mu'attila make about existence?
A) That existence requires sensory perception
B) That what the mind does not encompass is nonexistent
C) That existence is impossible without direction
D) That the spirit is the only thing that exists without being sensed

Key Takeaway
The mu'attila's error is equating lack of comprehension with non-existence; the spirit example proves otherwise.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you encounter a teaching about Allah that your mind cannot fully grasp—like His attributes or actions—does your heart lean toward denial or humble acceptance? Recall a specific belief you struggled with. How did you respond, and what does that reveal about your reliance on reason versus revelation?
?
The Mu'attila denied God because they couldn't comprehend Him. Think of one area in your faith where you have felt intellectual discomfort. Will you allow that discomfort to weaken your conviction, or will you affirm God's existence despite your limited understanding, just as you affirm your own soul's existence without knowing its reality?

Divine Transcendence
beginner
5 min
The Logical Pivot of Division
Juwayni presents the logical starting point of sectarian division.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the logical principle of negating specification (takhsis) from God's essence forces a decisive choice that splits people into two camps.

Core Idea
The negation of direction from God's essence is the logical pivot of all theological division.

Before Juwayni names the groups, he lays the logical ground: if God's essence is not subject to specification (takhsis), then attributing direction or place to Him is impossible. This one insight creates a watershed moment — some accept it, others reject it, and the entire history of sectarian division follows from this fork in the road.

Guided Path
1
Grasp the logical premise
Read the opening sentence carefully. Juwayni says that denying specification (takhsis) from God's essence entails that specifying Him by directions (jihāt) is impossible, just like spatial specification.
The term 'takhsis' here means assigning a particular location or direction.
2
Identify the consequence
Juwayni calls this point a 'slippery slope' (mazallat al-aqdām). Notice how he frames it as the cause of division among people.
3
See the two groups emerge
At this juncture, the masses split into two groups. One group is saved and truthful (al-firqah al-muḥaqqiqah al-nājiyah). Understand that Juwayni is setting up the division before describing each side.
4
Understand the need for clarification
Juwayni says it is necessary to point out the cause of division and what prompted the people of truth to adhere. This prepares you for the next part of the text.
This lesson focuses only on the logical pivot; the next lesson details the erring group.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم لزم انتفاء الاختصاص بالاقرار ذاته من حيث كانت جائزة والتخصص بالجهات والأقطار في قضية الجواز كالاختصاص بالاقرار (۲) وهذا مزلة الأقدام، ومثار خلال الأنام وعندها افترق جماهير الخلق فريقين . وثبتت الفرقة المحققة الناجية . ولا بد من التنبيه على سبب الافتراق ، وايضاح ما أستحث أهل الحق على النبات واجتناب الشتات . فذهبت طوائف الى وصف الرب بما تقدس فى جلاله عنه عن التحيز بالجهة (۳) ، حتى انتهى غلاة الى التشكيك أو التمثيل أو التمسيك تعالى الله عن قول الزائغين (٣) والذي دعاهم الى ذلك طلبهم ربهم من المحسوسات ، وما يتشكل فى الأوهام ويتقدر فى مجازى الوساوس ، وخواطر الهواجس . وهذا عيد بالكلية عن صفات الالهية ، وأى فرق بين هؤلاء ، وبين من يعبد بعض الأجرام العلوية ، ولو اجتمع الأولون والآخرون على أن يدركوا بهذا المسلك : الروح . وهو خلق الله تعالى لم يجدوا اليه سبيلا فانه معقول غير محسوس • وقد قال تبارك وتعالى في محكم كتابه ، الذي لا يأتيه الباطل من بين يديه ولا من خلفه : « ويسألونك عن الروح ؟ قل الروح من أمر ربي ، وما أوتيتم من العلم الا قليلا ) (٤) .
Then it follows that the negation of specification to the Essence in terms of it being possible, and specification by directions and regions in the matter of possibility, is like specification by [spatial] determinations. And this is a slippery slope for feet, and a source of discord among people. At this point the masses of creation split into two groups, and the saved and true group became evident. It is necessary to point out the cause of the division and clarify what prompted the people of truth to adhere [to the truth] and avoid dispersion. So some groups went to describe the Lord with what He is transcendent in His majesty from, regarding being confined by direction, until the extremists ended up in doubt or representation or anthropomorphism – exalted is Allah above the sayings of the deviants. What led them to that was their seeking their Lord from sensory things, and what takes form in imaginations and is measured in the realm of whispers and thoughts of misgivings. And this is entirely remote from the attributes of divinity. What difference is there between these and those who worship certain celestial bodies? Even if the former and the latter gathered to perceive the spirit through this path – and it is a creation of Allah – they would not find a way to it, for it is intelligible, not sensible. And Allah – blessed and exalted – said in the firmament of His Book, which falsehood cannot approach from before it or behind it: 'And they ask you about the spirit. Say: The spirit is from the command of my Lord, and you have not been given of knowledge except a little.' (17:85)
Source pages p. 22

Commentary
This passage is an analysis of the origin of sectarian division in theology. Juwayni starts by stating the problem: if one admits that God's essence is not specified by direction, then attributing direction to Him is like attributing spatial determination, which is impossible. This point became a cause for division. He then describes the first erroneous group: the anthropomorphists (mushabbiha). They attempted to describe God with what He is free from, such as confinement by direction, leading to extremes of doubt or outright representation. Their error stems from seeking God through sensory perception, imagining Him in forms and phantasms. Juwayni compares them to idolaters who worship celestial bodies, showing they are no different. He then uses the example of the spirit (ruh) to illustrate that even a created entity like the spirit is intelligible but not sensible; people cannot perceive it through the senses, yet they cannot deny its existence. He quotes the Quranic verse about the spirit to reinforce that human knowledge of the spirit is limited. Thus, the anthropomorphists' method is futile.

Key Terms
الاختصاص
الجهة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that the division among Muslims is arbitrary or merely historical; Juwayni shows it is rooted in a logical principle about God's transcendence.
Correction: The division is not accidental; it follows necessarily from whether one accepts the logical implication of negating specification from God's essence.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the direct consequence of negating specification from God's essence?
A) It becomes impossible to specify God by directions
B) God becomes limited by space
C) The saved group is identified
D) Anthropomorphism becomes acceptable

Key Takeaway
The single logical principle that God is not subject to specification forces a definitive split between those who affirm divine transcendence and those who do not.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Juwayni says this logical point is a 'slippery slope' and a source of discord. Is there a truth about God that you have avoided following through because it would force you to abandon a comfortable or familiar belief? What will you do to confront that today?
?
The saved group is defined by accepting a single logical consequence. Are you willing to let a rigorous truth about divine transcendence reshape your worship and conception of God, even if it makes you uncomfortable?
?
If the negation of specification is the pivot of division, then your stance on God's essence determines which group you belong to. Have you truly examined your own heart to see if you accept God's absolute transcendence without mental reservations? Make a concrete decision to affirm it tonight in your prayer.

Divine Transcendence
intermediate

The Anthropomorphist Error
Juwayni describes the anthropomorphist error and refutes it using analogy and Quran.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the anthropomorphists (mushabbiha) fell into error, how Juwayni refutes them by comparing them to idolaters, and how the example of the spirit (ruh) proves that God cannot be perceived through the senses.

Core Idea
Seeking God through sensory imagination leads to anthropomorphism, which is no better than idol worship.

After setting the logical stage, Juwayni now describes the erroneous group: those who tried to describe God with attributes like direction and shape. He exposes the root cause of their error — they sought God through physical senses and imagination. To show the absurdity, he compares them to worshippers of celestial bodies and uses the example of the human spirit: even a created, intelligible entity cannot be perceived by the senses, so how can the Creator be captured by sensory imagination?

Guided Path
1
Identify the error and its extreme forms
Read how Juwayni describes the anthropomorphists: they tried to describe God with what He is free from, like confinement by direction. Their extremes led to doubt (tashkīk), representation (tamthīl), or anthropomorphism (tamsīk).
Tashkīk means casting doubt on God's attributes, tamthīl is comparing God to creation, and tamsīk is holding firmly to corporeal descriptions.
2
Understand the root cause
Juwayni says their error came from seeking their Lord through sensory things (maḥsūsāt) and imaginations. They tried to conceive God through physical forms and mental images.
3
Analyze the comparison with idolaters
Note how Juwayni equates them with those who worship celestial bodies. He asks: what difference is there? This shows that anthropomorphism, in principle, is like idolatry.
4
Reflect on the analogy of the spirit (ruh)
Juwayni argues that even a created thing like the spirit (ruh) is intelligible (ma'qūl) but not sensible (maḥsūs). People cannot perceive it through senses, so how can they expect to perceive God through senses?
The Quranic verse confirms that knowledge of the spirit is limited.
5
Connect to the Quranic proof
The verse (17:85) is quoted to reinforce that human knowledge is limited, especially regarding spiritual realities. This undermines the anthropomorphist method.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم لزم انتفاء الاختصاص بالاقرار ذاته من حيث كانت جائزة والتخصص بالجهات والأقطار في قضية الجواز كالاختصاص بالاقرار (۲) وهذا مزلة الأقدام، ومثار خلال الأنام وعندها افترق جماهير الخلق فريقين . وثبتت الفرقة المحققة الناجية . ولا بد من التنبيه على سبب الافتراق ، وايضاح ما أستحث أهل الحق على النبات واجتناب الشتات . فذهبت طوائف الى وصف الرب بما تقدس فى جلاله عنه عن التحيز بالجهة (۳) ، حتى انتهى غلاة الى التشكيك أو التمثيل أو التمسيك تعالى الله عن قول الزائغين (٣) والذي دعاهم الى ذلك طلبهم ربهم من المحسوسات ، وما يتشكل فى الأوهام ويتقدر فى مجازى الوساوس ، وخواطر الهواجس . وهذا عيد بالكلية عن صفات الالهية ، وأى فرق بين هؤلاء ، وبين من يعبد بعض الأجرام العلوية ، ولو اجتمع الأولون والآخرون على أن يدركوا بهذا المسلك : الروح . وهو خلق الله تعالى لم يجدوا اليه سبيلا فانه معقول غير محسوس • وقد قال تبارك وتعالى في محكم كتابه ، الذي لا يأتيه الباطل من بين يديه ولا من خلفه : « ويسألونك عن الروح ؟ قل الروح من أمر ربي ، وما أوتيتم من العلم الا قليلا ) (٤) .
Then it follows that the negation of specification to the Essence in terms of it being possible, and specification by directions and regions in the matter of possibility, is like specification by [spatial] determinations. And this is a slippery slope for feet, and a source of discord among people. At this point the masses of creation split into two groups, and the saved and true group became evident. It is necessary to point out the cause of the division and clarify what prompted the people of truth to adhere [to the truth] and avoid dispersion. So some groups went to describe the Lord with what He is transcendent in His majesty from, regarding being confined by direction, until the extremists ended up in doubt or representation or anthropomorphism – exalted is Allah above the sayings of the deviants. What led them to that was their seeking their Lord from sensory things, and what takes form in imaginations and is measured in the realm of whispers and thoughts of misgivings. And this is entirely remote from the attributes of divinity. What difference is there between these and those who worship certain celestial bodies? Even if the former and the latter gathered to perceive the spirit through this path – and it is a creation of Allah – they would not find a way to it, for it is intelligible, not sensible. And Allah – blessed and exalted – said in the firmament of His Book, which falsehood cannot approach from before it or behind it: 'And they ask you about the spirit. Say: The spirit is from the command of my Lord, and you have not been given of knowledge except a little.' (17:85)
Source pages p. 22

Commentary
This passage is an analysis of the origin of sectarian division in theology. Juwayni starts by stating the problem: if one admits that God's essence is not specified by direction, then attributing direction to Him is like attributing spatial determination, which is impossible. This point became a cause for division. He then describes the first erroneous group: the anthropomorphists (mushabbiha). They attempted to describe God with what He is free from, such as confinement by direction, leading to extremes of doubt or outright representation. Their error stems from seeking God through sensory perception, imagining Him in forms and phantasms. Juwayni compares them to idolaters who worship celestial bodies, showing they are no different. He then uses the example of the spirit (ruh) to illustrate that even a created entity like the spirit is intelligible but not sensible; people cannot perceive it through the senses, yet they cannot deny its existence. He quotes the Quranic verse about the spirit to reinforce that human knowledge of the spirit is limited. Thus, the anthropomorphists' method is futile.

Misconception: One might think that because God is described in scripture with terms like 'hand' or 'throne', we can imagine Him in physical terms.
Correction: Juwayni shows that seeking God through sensory imagination leads to anthropomorphism, which is akin to idolatry; God is beyond all sensory perception.

What evidence does Juwayni use to show that the anthropomorphist method cannot perceive even a created spiritual entity?
A) The example of celestial bodies
B) The example of the spirit (ruh)
C) The Quranic verse about thunder
D) The argument from divine transcendence

Key Takeaway
Anthropomorphism is rooted in the mistaken attempt to grasp God through the senses; even a created entity like the spirit is beyond sensory perception, so how much more the Creator.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever caught yourself picturing Allah with human-like features or emotions? What concrete step can you take today to train your heart to worship Allah as He truly is, beyond any image?
?
Juwayni says that relying on the senses to know Allah is no different from idol worship. Where in your daily life do you trust your own imagination about Allah more than His revelation? Will you commit to replacing those imaginations with the descriptions from the Quran and Sunnah starting now?
?
The spirit is a creation yet you cannot see it or touch it. If even created realities can be beyond your senses, how much more is Allah beyond your imagination? How does this truth expose the arrogance of trying to define Allah by your own limited perceptions?

The Three Groups and the Middle Path
Juwayni defines three groups and cites Abu Bakr's saying.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three theological positions regarding knowledge of God and why the monotheist's path is distinct from anthropomorphism and negation.

Core Idea
True monotheism is neither claiming to know God's essence nor denying His existence.

Juwayni begins by describing the saved group who follow the middle path. They avoid two extremes: claiming to fully perceive God (anthropomorphism) or denying His existence (negation). He then classifies people into three groups based on their stance toward God's reality. This classification is crucial for understanding the Islamic position on divine transcendence.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on the party of truth
Notice how they are guided to the middle way and why they reject attributes of need for God.
They find a parallel in themselves: they know a created thing exists but cannot perceive its reality.
2
Identify the three groups
Find the definitions of mushabbih, mu'attil, and muwahhid. Understand what each rests content with.
The mushabbih stops at what his thought reaches; the mu'attil stops at pure negation.
3
Reflect on Abu Bakr's saying
Juwayni quotes the phrase 'Inability to perceive perception is perception.' Why is this the definition of the monotheist?
This saying resolves the tension between affirming God and not knowing His essence.
4
Summarize the lesson
Write in your own words why the monotheist's position is the correct middle path.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأما فئة الحق فهدوا إلى سواء الطريق وسلكوا جدد الطريق وعلموا أن الجائزات تفتقر إلى صانع لا يتصف بالصفات الدالة على الافتقار وعلموا أنه لو اتصف بها لكان شبيها بمصنوعاته لم يميلوا إلى النفي من حيث أن يدركوا حقيقة الإله ولم يتعدوا موجودا القطع بكونه مع العجز عن درك حقيقته إذ وجدوا في أنفسهم مخلوقا لم يستريبوا في وجوده ولم يدركوا حقيقته. ونحن الآن نذكر عبارة حري بأن يتخذها مولانا في هذا الباب هجيراه فهي لعمرى المنجية في دنياه وآخراه فنقول: من انهض لطلب مديره فإن اطمأن إلى موجود انتهى إليه فكره فهو مشبه وإن اطمأن إلى النفي المحض فهو معطل وإن قطع بموجود واعترف بالعجز عن درك حقيقته فهو موحد وهو معنى قول الصديق رضي الله عنه إذ قال: العجز عن درك الإدراك إدراك. فإن قيل: فغايتكم إذن حيرة ودهشة قلنا: العقول جائزة في درك الحقيقة قاطعة بالوجود المنزه عن صفات الافتقار. وهذا ما أردناه في هذا الفن وقد تجاوزنا الحد المعزوم عليه.
And as for the party of truth, they were guided to the middle way and traversed the paths of the way, and they knew that contingent things are in need of a Maker who is not described with attributes that indicate need, and they knew that if He were described with them, He would resemble His creatures. They did not incline to negation because they cannot perceive the reality of the Divine, nor did they go beyond a being whose existence is certain while being unable to perceive its reality, since they found in themselves a created thing that they did not doubt in its existence and did not perceive its reality. And we now mention a phrase worthy of being taken by our master as a constant theme in this chapter; it is, by my life, the saving thing in his world and his afterlife. So we say: Whoever arises to seek his ruler, if he rests content with a being that his thought reaches, he is a mushabbih (anthropomorphist); if he rests content with pure negation, he is a mu'attil (negator); and if he is certain of a being and admits inability to perceive its reality, he is a muwahhid (monotheist). And this is the meaning of the saying of as-Siddiq (may Allah be pleased with him) when he said: 'Inability to perceive perception is perception.' If it is said: Then your final point is bewilderment and perplexity. We say: Intellects are incapable of perceiving the reality, but are certain of the existence free from attributes of need. And this is what we intended in this topic, and we have exceeded the intended limit.
Source pages p. 23

Commentary
This passage is a definition and classification of three theological positions regarding the knowledge of God, followed by an objection and answer. Juwayni begins by describing the 'party of truth' – the monotheists who follow the middle path. They understand that contingent things (al-jā'izāt) require a Maker, and that Maker cannot have attributes that imply need, for that would make Him resemble His creatures. They neither fall into anthropomorphism (tashbīh) by claiming to perceive the true nature of God, nor do they fall into absolute negation (ta'tīl) by denying His existence. They find a parallel in their own souls: they know a created thing (the soul) exists but cannot fully perceive its reality. Juwayni then quotes the phrase from Abu Bakr as-Siddiq: 'Inability to perceive perception is perception' – meaning that acknowledging one's incapacity to fully comprehend God is itself a form of true knowledge. The passage then presents an objection: if the ultimate point is bewilderment, then what is the benefit? The answer is that while intellects cannot perceive the quiddity of God, they can be certain of His existence, free from any attributes of need. The passage concludes by noting that the discussion has exceeded its intended scope.

Key Terms
مشبه
معطل
موحد
العجز عن درك الإدراك إدراك

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Many think monotheism requires either anthropomorphism (claiming to know God's essence) or total negation (denying any knowledge).
Correction: Juwayni shows the correct path is to affirm God's existence while admitting inability to perceive His true reality, which is the essence of tawhid.

Checkpoint
What does the saying of Abu Bakr as-Siddiq 'العجز عن درك الإدراك إدراك' mean in the context of this passage?
A) One can fully perceive God's essence through intellect.
B) Admitting inability to fully perceive God is itself a form of true perception.
C) One must rely on sense perception to know God.
D) It is impossible to know anything about God.

Key Takeaway
The correct monotheistic position is to affirm God's existence while acknowledging human inability to perceive His true reality.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever, without realizing it, tried to 'reach' God with your thoughts, imagining Him in a way your mind could grasp? How does Juwayni's warning about the mushabbih challenge you to step back from such attempts? What will you do today to instead rest in your inability while affirming His existence?
?
When you face doubts or find yourself unable to make sense of God's nature, do you ever secretly lean toward pure negation—acting as if God is distant or unknowable in a way that diminishes your certainty? How can you, like the muwahhid, hold firm to certainty of His existence while humbly accepting your limits?
?
Abu Bakr's saying that 'inability to perceive perception is perception' turns your weakness into knowledge. Does your heart truly accept that not knowing God's essence is not a failure but the highest form of worship? How will you let this truth transform your prayer and trust in Him starting today?

Divine Transcendence
beginner
4 min
Answering the Charge of Skepticism
Juwayni gives an objection and its rebuttal.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni defends the monotheist's position against the charge that it leads to bewilderment and skepticism.

Core Idea
Certainty of existence is compatible with inability to perceive essence.

Having established the monotheist's admission of inability to perceive God's reality, Juwayni anticipates an objection: if we cannot perceive God, doesn't that leave us in bewilderment? He responds by distinguishing between types of knowledge: we can be certain of God's existence even without perceiving His quiddity. This clarifies the proper scope of reason in theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection
The interlocutor says: 'Then your final point is bewilderment and perplexity.' Notice the challenge: if we admit we cannot perceive God, what certainty remains?
2
Read Juwayni's answer
He says: 'Intellects are incapable of perceiving the reality, but are certain of the existence free from attributes of need.' Understand the distinction between knowing existence (wujūd) and knowing essence (māhiyya).
This answer parallels the example of the self: certain of existence, unable to perceive reality.
3
Connect to the earlier discussion
Reflect on how this answer completes the argument. The monotheist is not left in skepticism but has a rational certainty of God's existence.
This is the final point of the topic: affirming God's transcendence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأما فئة الحق فهدوا إلى سواء الطريق وسلكوا جدد الطريق وعلموا أن الجائزات تفتقر إلى صانع لا يتصف بالصفات الدالة على الافتقار وعلموا أنه لو اتصف بها لكان شبيها بمصنوعاته لم يميلوا إلى النفي من حيث أن يدركوا حقيقة الإله ولم يتعدوا موجودا القطع بكونه مع العجز عن درك حقيقته إذ وجدوا في أنفسهم مخلوقا لم يستريبوا في وجوده ولم يدركوا حقيقته. ونحن الآن نذكر عبارة حري بأن يتخذها مولانا في هذا الباب هجيراه فهي لعمرى المنجية في دنياه وآخراه فنقول: من انهض لطلب مديره فإن اطمأن إلى موجود انتهى إليه فكره فهو مشبه وإن اطمأن إلى النفي المحض فهو معطل وإن قطع بموجود واعترف بالعجز عن درك حقيقته فهو موحد وهو معنى قول الصديق رضي الله عنه إذ قال: العجز عن درك الإدراك إدراك. فإن قيل: فغايتكم إذن حيرة ودهشة قلنا: العقول جائزة في درك الحقيقة قاطعة بالوجود المنزه عن صفات الافتقار. وهذا ما أردناه في هذا الفن وقد تجاوزنا الحد المعزوم عليه.
And as for the party of truth, they were guided to the middle way and traversed the paths of the way, and they knew that contingent things are in need of a Maker who is not described with attributes that indicate need, and they knew that if He were described with them, He would resemble His creatures. They did not incline to negation because they cannot perceive the reality of the Divine, nor did they go beyond a being whose existence is certain while being unable to perceive its reality, since they found in themselves a created thing that they did not doubt in its existence and did not perceive its reality. And we now mention a phrase worthy of being taken by our master as a constant theme in this chapter; it is, by my life, the saving thing in his world and his afterlife. So we say: Whoever arises to seek his ruler, if he rests content with a being that his thought reaches, he is a mushabbih (anthropomorphist); if he rests content with pure negation, he is a mu'attil (negator); and if he is certain of a being and admits inability to perceive its reality, he is a muwahhid (monotheist). And this is the meaning of the saying of as-Siddiq (may Allah be pleased with him) when he said: 'Inability to perceive perception is perception.' If it is said: Then your final point is bewilderment and perplexity. We say: Intellects are incapable of perceiving the reality, but are certain of the existence free from attributes of need. And this is what we intended in this topic, and we have exceeded the intended limit.
Source pages p. 23

Commentary
This passage is a definition and classification of three theological positions regarding the knowledge of God, followed by an objection and answer. Juwayni begins by describing the 'party of truth' – the monotheists who follow the middle path. They understand that contingent things (al-jā'izāt) require a Maker, and that Maker cannot have attributes that imply need, for that would make Him resemble His creatures. They neither fall into anthropomorphism (tashbīh) by claiming to perceive the true nature of God, nor do they fall into absolute negation (ta'tīl) by denying His existence. They find a parallel in their own souls: they know a created thing (the soul) exists but cannot fully perceive its reality. Juwayni then quotes the phrase from Abu Bakr as-Siddiq: 'Inability to perceive perception is perception' – meaning that acknowledging one's incapacity to fully comprehend God is itself a form of true knowledge. The passage then presents an objection: if the ultimate point is bewilderment, then what is the benefit? The answer is that while intellects cannot perceive the quiddity of God, they can be certain of His existence, free from any attributes of need. The passage concludes by noting that the discussion has exceeded its intended scope.

Key Terms

دهشة

الحقيقة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that if we cannot fully perceive God, then we cannot be certain of His existence at all.
Correction: Juwayni shows that certainty of existence does not require perception of essence; the two are separate.

Checkpoint
How does Juwayni respond to the objection that his position leads to bewilderment?
A) He says that intellects can perceive God's essence fully.
B) He says that intellects are certain of existence but incapable of perceiving quiddity.
C) He says that we must rely on revelation alone, not reason.
D) He says that bewilderment is the intended final state.

Key Takeaway
Admitting inability to perceive God's essence does not lead to skepticism; it affirms a rational certainty of His existence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever feel that because you cannot fully comprehend God, your faith is less certain? How does Juwayni's distinction between knowing existence and knowing essence address that feeling? What can you do today to strengthen your certainty of God's existence despite your limited understanding?
?
When you face doubts about God's attributes, do you tend to demand full comprehension? How does this passage call you to accept your intellectual limits while still affirming certainty? What is one area of your belief where you need to apply this principle?
?
If someone accused you of being bewildered because you cannot explain God's reality, how would you respond using Juwayni's argument? Write down one situation where you can share this understanding.

Refuting the Dissenter and Warning Against Anthropomorphism
Juwayni refutes an objection and warns against anthropomorphism.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's refutation of the objection that God's actions can be analogized to human noble traits, and why this leads to anthropomorphism.

Core Idea
God's acts cannot be measured by human motives; doing so is anthropomorphism.

Juwayni addresses a specific objection: if a great person shows kindness to a worthless stranger, why can't God's acts be judged by similar wisdom? He dismantles this analogy by showing that human kindness stems from desire for praise or compassion—attributes utterly unlike God. He then warns that any differentiation of God's acts on moral grounds leads to anthropomorphism, tying this to those who affirm a direction for God.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection and dismissal
Read the dissenter's argument in Arabic and translation. Notice how Juwayni immediately labels it 'obfuscation' (تلبيس).
The dissenter's example is unclear in OCR but the structure is clear.
2
Analyze the two human motives
Juwayni breaks down the example into two motives: desire for praise and tenderness of compassion. Understand why these are human-specific.
Both motives are absent in God.
3
Grasp the warning against anthropomorphism
Juwayni warns that imagining a differentiation of God's acts (e.g., good vs. bad) is a form of anthropomorphism. See how he links this to corporealists.
This reinforces divine transcendence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فان عارض المخالف فقال : الكبير المعظم قد يلقى غريباً مهينا لا ينتفع باكرامه وايوائه ، ولا يتضرر بتركه في مضيعة ، ثم الحكمة تستحثه على مكارم الأخلاق فيه . وهذا تلبيس لا تحصيل له فإن الصورة التي ذكرها اتفاق وغيرها مما يلبسون به. فيحصر ذلك أمران . أحدهما : أن المكارم التي ذكرها سببها الاهتزاز بحسن الثناء في الغالب ، وقد يستمر المرء على أمر ويتعوده حتى ينتهي الأمر فيه إلى مبلغ يعسر عليه مخالفته. وللعادات آثار غير منكورة في الجيلات. والثاني : أن الإنسان قد تناله رقة الجنسية وتستحثه على إنقاذ الغرقى وإنجاء الملكي ولو لم ينهض لها لتتضرر ضرر بينا. والرب تبارك وتعالى متقدس عن هذه الصفات جمع، ومن تخيل تفصيل الأفعال في حق الإله فقد تعلق بطرف من التشبيه. والصائرون إلى التجسيم وإثبات الجهة يتمسكون بما يفضي إلى التشبيه. في الوجود الأزلي وهؤلاء مشبهون في الأفعال والفئتان زائغتان عن مدرك الحق. فالرب لا يناسب وجوده وجود، ولا يشبه في امتناع قبول الضر والنفع فاعل. فهذا - حرس التسديد - الله مولانا - لباب التوحيد، والله ولي التوفيق.
If the dissenter objects and says: The great and majestic one may encounter a humiliated stranger who does not benefit from honoring him and sheltering him, nor is he harmed by leaving him in neglect, and then wisdom urges him to noble moral traits towards him. This is obfuscation with no substance. For the picture he mentioned—coincidence and other things with which they obfuscate—is confined to two matters. One of them: the noble traits he mentioned are, for the most part, caused by being stirred by good praise, and a person may persist in a matter and become accustomed to it until it reaches a point where it is difficult for him to oppose it. And habits have undeniable effects in natures. And the second: that a human may be affected by the tenderness of compassion, and it urges him to save the drowning and rescue the distressed, even if he did not exert himself for them he would be harmed with evident harm. And the Lord, Blessed and Exalted, is sanctified above all these attributes. And whoever imagines differentiation of acts with respect to God has clung to a part of anthropomorphism. And those who incline to corporealism and affirmation of direction cling to what leads to anthropomorphism. In pre-eternal existence, these are anthropomorphists in acts, and both groups are deviated from the station of truth. So the Lord—no existence is similar to His existence, nor is He similar to an agent in the impossibility of receiving harm and benefit. This—may God protect us—God is our Master—is the core of tawhid. And God is the Granter of success.
Source pages p. 36

Commentary
This passage is an objection-and-answer followed by a warning. First, Juwayni presents the dissenter's argument: a great person may act kindly towards a worthless stranger out of wisdom, even without benefit. This is meant to analogize God's acts to human wisdom. Juwayni dismisses it as 'talbis' (obfuscation). He then analyzes the example into two underlying motives: first, the desire for praise; second, natural compassion (رقة الجنسية). Both are human attributes, and God is transcendent (متقدس) above them. Juwayni then warns that any attempt to differentiate God's acts as good or evil leads to anthropomorphism (تشبيه). He ties this to corporealists (مجسمة) who affirm direction. He concludes that both groups (those who differentiate acts and the corporealists) are deviated from truth. The final statement 'فهذا لباب التوحيد' declares that this principle—that God's acts are not subject to human moral categories and that He is unlike any agent—is the core of tawhid.

Key Terms
تشبيه
تجسيم
المخالف
التلبيس
مكارم الأخلاق
رقة الجنسية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's acts can be evaluated by human moral standards (e.g., good/bad) because He is wise.
Correction: Juwayni shows that human moral judgments are based on motives like desire for praise or compassion, which are inapplicable to God. Imposing such categories is anthropomorphism.

Checkpoint
What does Juwayni say is the result of imagining a differentiation of acts with respect to God?
A) It is permissible
B) It leads to atheism
C) It clings to a part of anthropomorphism
D) It is a sign of deep wisdom

Key Takeaway
Imposing human motives on God's acts leads to anthropomorphism, which is a deviation from the core of tawhid.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever justified your own actions by comparing them to what you thought God would do? In what subtle ways have you measured God's wisdom by your own understanding? What would it mean for you to truly let God be God, beyond your categories of good and evil?
?
When you read that 'whoever imagines differentiation of acts with respect to God has clung to a part of anthropomorphism,' does your heart resist? Do you find yourself wanting to argue that some acts are obviously good or evil even for God? What would it cost you to surrender that judgment?
?
This lesson says that human noble traits are often driven by desire for praise or compassion. If you honestly examine your own best deeds, how much of them are tainted by these motives? How does knowing that God is pure of such motives challenge your worship?
