The Hypothetical Youth and Free Will
Al-Juwayni illustrates human free will and divine decree with a hypothetical young man.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Juwayni employs a hypothetical example of a youth to illustrate that human beings possess free will and moral responsibility, and that divine decree does not negate accountability.

Core Idea
Free will and divine decree coexist; we are responsible for our choices.

In this passage, al-Juwayni presents a vivid hypothetical: a young man at the peak of his desires, surrounded by temptation, yet free. The purpose is to ground the Ash'ari position on human agency—that we choose our actions, even though God creates them. As you read, notice how al-Juwayni affirms both the youth's freedom and God's ultimate decree, showing they are not contradictory.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation once to get the overall narrative. Pay attention to the terms 'mukhtār' and 'mujbar'.
The passage is a single illustrative example.
2
Identify the condition of the youth
Al-Juwayni describes the youth as having desire, means, beauty, and bad company. Yet he says he is 'muʾthir mukhtār' (choosing and free), not compelled. Notice the emphasis on his intellect.
3
Analyze the conclusion about obligation
The passage concludes that it is rationally possible to impose obligation on such a person because he is not prevented (he has free choice). Then it adds that if a preventing decree precedes, he is still under God's judgment but can hope for mercy.
This shows that decree does not remove responsibility.
4
Reflect on the broader theological point
Ask yourself: How does this example uphold both human free will and divine predestination? How does it refute the idea that God compels people to sin?
The Ash'ari concept of kasb (acquisition) is at play.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
لو فرضنا شابا حديث العهد بحلمه (۱) ، لم تهذبه المذاهب ولم تحنكه التجارب وهو على نهايته فى علمته ، وشهوته . وقد استمكن من بلغة من الحطام ، وخص بمسحة من الجمال ولم يقم عليه قوام يزعه عن ورطات الردى ، ويمنعه عن الارتباك في شبكات الهوى ، فوافاه أخدان الفساد ، وهو فى غلواء شبابه ، يحدث نفسه بالبقاء أمدا بعيدا فما أقرب من هذا وصفه من خلع العذار والبدار الى شيم الأشرار ، وهو مع ذلك كله مؤثر مختار ، ليس مجبرا على المعاصى والزلات ، ولا مصدودا عن الطاعات، ومعه من العقل ما يستوجب به اللائمة اذا عصى . فمن هذا سبيله ، لا يستحيل في العقل تكليفه ، فانه ليس ممنوعا ، ولكن ان سبق له من الله تبارك وتعالى منوع القضاء ، فهو صائر الى حكم الله الجزم ، وقضائه الفصل ، محجوجا يحجة الله تعالى الا أن يتغمده الله برحمته ، وهو أرحم الراحمين
If we suppose a youth newly emerged from his boyhood, whom doctrines have not refined and experiences have not seasoned, and he is at his peak in his passion and desire. He has obtained a means of worldly goods, and is endowed with a touch of beauty, and there is no restraining authority to prevent him from pitfalls of ruin and to hinder him from entanglement in the nets of passion, so companions of corruption befall him in the height of his youth, while he tells himself that he will remain for a long time. How close is he whose description is this to casting off the rein and hastening to the traits of the wicked! Yet with all that, he is choosing and free (muʾthir mukhtār), not compelled (mujbar) to sins and slips, nor prevented from acts of obedience. And he possesses intellect by which he merits blame when he disobeys. So for one in this condition, it is not rationally impossible to impose obligation upon him, for he is not prevented. But if a preventing decree from God Almighty has preceded for him, then he is subject to God's decisive judgment and final decree, being refuted by God's proof, unless God envelops him with His mercy, and He is the Most Merciful of the merciful.
Source pages p. 52

Commentary
This passage is an illustrative example. Al-Juwayni describes a youth with full capacity and desire, yet he is not compelled. He has free choice and reason. The purpose is to show that such a person can be held accountable. Even if divine decree ultimately prevails, it does not negate his responsibility. Al-Juwayni emphasizes that the youth is 'muʾthir mukhtār' (choosing and free) and not 'mujbar' (compelled). This sets up the Ash'ari position that human acts are acquired (kasb) but created by God.

Key Terms
مختار
مؤثر
مجبور
تكليف
قضاء
منوع القضاء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that if God's decree ultimately determines everything, then humans cannot be held responsible for their actions.
Correction: Al-Juwayni shows that even though divine decree is final, the youth's free choice and intellect make him responsible. Decree does not negate accountability; it is a separate reality.

Checkpoint
What is the condition of the young man in the example?
A) He is compelled to sin
B) He is free and chooses, with intellect
C) He has no reason
D) He is prevented by God

Key Takeaway
Al-Juwayni's hypothetical shows that worldly temptations do not negate free will; humans are responsible for their choices despite divine decree.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Think of a situation where you felt 'forced' into sin because of your environment or circumstances. After reading this lesson, can you still honestly say you were not free? What would it mean to take full responsibility for that choice now?
?
Al-Juwayni uses a hypothetical youth with no guidance and every temptation. Who in your life do you consider 'less accountable' because of their upbringing? Is that view consistent with this lesson? If not, how will you change your judgment?
?
The passage ends with 'unless God envelops him with His mercy.' Do you ever use the hope of God's mercy to avoid confronting your own free will and blameworthiness? Write down one area where you need to stop excusing yourself and instead recognize your choice, then repent.

5 min
The Vision of God and the Unique Spirit
Juwayni defines the true nature of the hoped-for vision of God.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the vision of God (ru'ya) is cherished by Ahl al-Sunna because of a unique spirit God couples with it, and that the vision itself is not the ultimate object of hope.

Core Idea
Hope lies not in the vision alone, but in the spirit God pairs with it.

In this passage, Imam al-Juwayni explains the deep significance of the vision of God for Ahl al-Sunna. He clarifies that what makes the vision so precious is the special spirit that accompanies it, and that without understanding this, one cannot grasp the sweetness of faith.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Notice the author's emphasis on the 'spirit' (ruh).
The word 'جرس' may be a marginal note; focus on the main text.
2
Identify the object of hope
Juwayni says the vision is the hope of Ahl al-Sunna, but then clarifies that it is the spirit coupled with it that is the real object of hope. Find this distinction in the text.
The phrase 'وهنا مناط الآمال' indicates the locus of hopes.
3
Understand the role of the spirit
Ask: Why does Juwayni say the vision itself is not to be hoped for? Because God could couple it with punishment. So the spirit is what makes it desirable.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والرؤية آمال أهل السنة : جرس وأنا أقول فيها : أن الله تبارك وتعالى يقرن بها فنا من الروح لا يوازيه روح، وهنا مناط الآمال. والا فالرؤية في عينها لا يجوز أين تكون مأمولة، وكان يجوز فى قدرته، أن يقرن بها منتهى عقوبة الكفار، حتى يحذرها المؤمنون كما يرجونها، الآن. ولن يجد المرء الله مولانا - حلاوة الايمان، حتى يحيط بما ذكرته علما، ولولا ثقتى بأن مولانا بتوفيق الله، يبتدر برأيه الثاقب هذه الحقائق لما ثبت اليه أسرار هذه الأبواب، التى لم أضمنها شيئا من التصانيف. فان قيل: فاذا عقلتم درك الوجوب باستشعار العقاب، فقد ساويتم القدرية في عقدهم.. قلنا: هيهات: بينا وبينهم ما بين الثريا والثرى فانهم زعموا أن العقول توجب على الرب الثواب والعقاب. وأنهم ينفردون بدرك الواجبات بعقولهم. ونحن قلنا: لا يجب على الله تبارك وتعالى شيء.
And the vision (ru'ya) is the hope of the people of the Sunna: a call? And I say concerning it: God, blessed and exalted, will couple with it a kind of spirit (fan min al-ruh) that no other spirit equals, and there is the object of hopes. Otherwise, the vision in itself is not such that it should be hoped for, and it is within His power to couple with it the utmost punishment of the disbelievers, so that the believers would fear it as they hope for it now. And a person will not find, our Lord God, the sweetness of faith until he comprehends what I have mentioned as knowledge. Were it not for my confidence that our Lord, by the success of God, takes the lead with his sharp intellect in these realities, the secrets of these chapters—which I have not included in any writings—would not be established for him. If it is said: 'Since you have rationalized the perception of obligation by perceiving punishment, you have equaled the Qadariyya in their doctrine.' We say: Far from it! Between us and them is the distance between the Pleiades and the earth. For they claimed that intellects impose on God reward and punishment, and that they independently perceive obligations by their intellects. But we say: Nothing is obligatory upon God, blessed and exalted.
Source pages p. 59

Commentary
This passage discusses the vision of God (ru'ya) promised to believers. Juwayni says God will couple it with a unique kind of spirit, and that is the real hope; the vision itself is not to be hoped for in isolation. Then he responds to an objection: if you say obligation is perceived through fear of punishment, you agree with the Qadariyya. He refutes this by distinguishing: the Qadariyya claim that intellects impose obligations upon God (e.g., reward and punishment), whereas Juwayni and the Sunnis say nothing is obligatory upon God. The Sunni claim is that the servant perceives his own obligation through the threat, but God is not obligated.

Key Terms
الرؤية
الروح
الآمال

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The vision of God is inherently desirable regardless of context.
Correction: Juwayni teaches that the vision itself is neutral; it is the spirit God pairs with it that makes it a source of hope and joy.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the true object of hope for Ahl al-Sunna regarding the vision of God?
A) The vision itself
B) The spirit coupled with the vision
C) Fear of punishment
D) Love of God

Key Takeaway
The vision of God is only a source of hope because of the extraordinary spirit God will unite with it.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly hope for the vision of God, or do you merely hope for the pleasures of Paradise? And what does that reveal about the state of your heart?
?
If the vision of God could have been coupled with punishment, what does that say about your assumptions about God’s mercy and justice? Are you prepared to face Him regardless?
?
Juwayni says one will not find the sweetness of faith until comprehending this. Have you tasted that sweetness? If not, what might be missing in your understanding—and what will you do about it today?

Refuting the Qadariyya: Nothing is Obligatory upon God
Juwayni distinguishes the Sunni position from Qadariyya on divine obligation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the crucial difference between the Sunni position and the Qadariyya: while the Qadariyya claim that intellect imposes obligations on God, Sunnis affirm that nothing is obligatory upon Allah.

Core Idea
Sunnis say nothing obligates God; Qadariyya say intellect does.

An objection is raised: if you say obligation is perceived through fear of punishment, you are like the Qadariyya. Juwayni responds emphatically, showing the vast difference. The Qadariyya claim that human reason can impose duties on God, whereas the Sunni position is that nothing is incumbent on Allah.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection carefully
Read the Arabic from 'فان قيل' to 'في عقدهم'. Understand that the objector equates the Sunni view with Qadariyya by focusing on the rational perception of obligation.
2
Identify the Qadariyya claim
Juwayni explains that the Qadariyya say intellects obligate God regarding reward and punishment, and they claim to independently perceive obligations.
Key phrase: 'العقول توجب على الرب الثواب والعقاب'.
3
Grasp the Sunni response
Juwayni's rebuttal: 'لا يجب على الله تبارك وتعالى شيء' – nothing is obligatory upon God. The Sunni view is that obligations are on the servant, not on God.
The distance between the two positions is compared to the Pleiades and the earth.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والرؤية آمال أهل السنة : جرس وأنا أقول فيها : أن الله تبارك وتعالى يقرن بها فنا من الروح لا يوازيه روح، وهنا مناط الآمال. والا فالرؤية في عينها لا يجوز أين تكون مأمولة، وكان يجوز فى قدرته، أن يقرن بها منتهى عقوبة الكفار، حتى يحذرها المؤمنون كما يرجونها، الآن. ولن يجد المرء الله مولانا - حلاوة الايمان، حتى يحيط بما ذكرته علما، ولولا ثقتى بأن مولانا بتوفيق الله، يبتدر برأيه الثاقب هذه الحقائق لما ثبت اليه أسرار هذه الأبواب، التى لم أضمنها شيئا من التصانيف. فان قيل: فاذا عقلتم درك الوجوب باستشعار العقاب، فقد ساويتم القدرية في عقدهم.. قلنا: هيهات: بينا وبينهم ما بين الثريا والثرى فانهم زعموا أن العقول توجب على الرب الثواب والعقاب. وأنهم ينفردون بدرك الواجبات بعقولهم. ونحن قلنا: لا يجب على الله تبارك وتعالى شيء.
And the vision (ru'ya) is the hope of the people of the Sunna: a call? And I say concerning it: God, blessed and exalted, will couple with it a kind of spirit (fan min al-ruh) that no other spirit equals, and there is the object of hopes. Otherwise, the vision in itself is not such that it should be hoped for, and it is within His power to couple with it the utmost punishment of the disbelievers, so that the believers would fear it as they hope for it now. And a person will not find, our Lord God, the sweetness of faith until he comprehends what I have mentioned as knowledge. Were it not for my confidence that our Lord, by the success of God, takes the lead with his sharp intellect in these realities, the secrets of these chapters—which I have not included in any writings—would not be established for him. If it is said: 'Since you have rationalized the perception of obligation by perceiving punishment, you have equaled the Qadariyya in their doctrine.' We say: Far from it! Between us and them is the distance between the Pleiades and the earth. For they claimed that intellects impose on God reward and punishment, and that they independently perceive obligations by their intellects. But we say: Nothing is obligatory upon God, blessed and exalted.
Source pages p. 59

Commentary
This passage discusses the vision of God (ru'ya) promised to believers. Juwayni says God will couple it with a unique kind of spirit, and that is the real hope; the vision itself is not to be hoped for in isolation. Then he responds to an objection: if you say obligation is perceived through fear of punishment, you agree with the Qadariyya. He refutes this by distinguishing: the Qadariyya claim that intellects impose obligations upon God (e.g., reward and punishment), whereas Juwayni and the Sunnis say nothing is obligatory upon God. The Sunni claim is that the servant perceives his own obligation through the threat, but God is not obligated.

Key Terms
القدرية
الوجوب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Sunni position that obligation is known through threat is the same as the Qadariyya position.
Correction: Juwayni shows the difference: Qadariyya say intellect imposes obligations upon God, while Sunnis say nothing is obligatory upon God; obligations only relate to the servant.

Checkpoint
What is the key difference between Sunnis and Qadariyya according to Juwayni?
A) Sunnis deny punishment
B) Qadariyya say God is obligated, Sunnis say nothing is obligatory upon God
C) Both say God must reward
D) Sunnis reject rational obligation

Key Takeaway
Sunnis affirm that nothing is obligatory upon Allah, whereas the Qadariyya falsely claim that intellect can impose obligations on God.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever caught yourself feeling entitled to something from God—like good health, success, or forgiveness? How does the truth that nothing is obligatory upon God shake that entitlement, and what does it demand from you instead?
?
If God truly owes you nothing, then every blessing you have is pure grace. Does that realization make you more grateful or more uncomfortable? Why?
?
When life doesn't go your way, do you subconsciously believe God is 'obliged' to be fair in your terms? How does this lesson challenge that belief, and what would it mean to truly accept that God is not bound by human expectations?

Divine Transcendence
intermediate
6 min
The True Meaning of Divine Love and Servitude
Juwayni explains that love of Allah is metaphorical, tied to benefit and avoidance of harm.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why love of Allah must be understood metaphorically and not literally, based on divine transcendence and the nature of human servitude.

Core Idea
Divine love is metaphorical: Allah's love means benefaction, our love means obedience.

This passage from Imam al-Juwayni addresses a critical issue: can we speak of 'love' between God and humans in a literal sense? Juwayni argues that because Allah is transcendent, free from needs and emotions, our understanding of love must be purified. The passage reveals the secret of servitude and refutes those who claim literal love of God.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let Juwayni's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the dense theological style.
2
Identify the starting point
Juwayni begins with the servant's perception of threat and his rational obligation. Understand how this sets the stage for the discussion of love and servitude.
3
Grasp the key analogy
Just as purposes, harm, benefit, and variation are impossible for Allah, so is the servant's escape from seeking fortunes impossible. This parallel is central.
This establishes the framework for metaphorical love.
4
Understand the collapse of literal love
Juwayni argues that claiming literal love of Allah is invalid because Allah is exalted above any creature attaining fortune from Him. The creature is always driven by benefit and harm.
5
Learn the metaphorical reinterpretation
Juwayni redefines divine love: Allah's love for His servant is His intention to bestow favor; the servant's love for his Lord is his uprightness in obedience.
This is the core takeaway.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
نعم اذا استشعر العبد وعيدا حمله عقله على معرفة وجوب مالو تركه الأوفى على ما لا طاقة له به . ومن أسرار العبودية - وهو معقود الفصل ومقصوده - أنه كما يستحيل على الله تبارك وتعالى الأغراض والضر والنفع والحظوظ وتفاوت الأفعال يستحيل خروج العبد عن طلب الحظوظ ومسالك التكاليف فلو لم يثبت حظ العبد في تنكب العقاب لما تقرر فى حقه الواجب . وعن هذا اضمحل قول من ادعى محبة الله حقا. فان وجوده متعال عن أن يحظى به ذو حظ . والمخلوق تداوره على الحظوظ والأغراض التي يجمعها دفع الضرر وجلب النفع والمحبة الله تبارك وتعالى غير محمولة على حقيقتها ظاهرا فانه متقدس عن الميل والتحيز والرقة والتوقان . فمحبة الله تبارك وتعالى لعبده : ارادته الانعام عليه ، ومحبة العبد لربه : استقامته في طاعته . وهو متقدس لعز جلاله ، عن أن يناله حظ ، أو ينال حظا .
Yes, when the servant perceives a threat, his reason leads him to know the obligation of that which, if he were to leave it, would burden him beyond his capacity. Among the secrets of servitude—and it is the core of the chapter and its purpose—is that just as purposes, harm, benefit, fortunes, and variation of acts are impossible for Allah, Blessed and Most High, so is the servant’s escape from seeking fortunes and the paths of obligations impossible. For if no fortune for the servant were established in avoiding punishment, the obligatory would not be established for him. And because of this, the saying of one who claims love of Allah in reality has collapsed. For His existence is exalted above that any fortune bearer should obtain fortune from Him. The creature revolves around fortunes and purposes that are combined in repelling harm and attracting benefit. And love of Allah, Blessed and Most High, is not carried upon its apparent literal reality, for He is sanctified above inclination, partiality, tenderness, and longing. So love of Allah, Blessed and Most High, for His servant is His intention to bestow favor upon him; and the servant’s love for his Lord is his uprightness in obedience to Him. And He, by the might of His majesty, is sanctified above that a fortune should reach Him or that He should obtain a fortune.
Source pages p. 59

Commentary
This passage defines the nature of divine love and human servitude. Juwayni begins by noting that when a servant feels a threat of punishment, his reason compels him to recognize the obligation to avoid what would be too burdensome if neglected. Then he states a key secret of servitude: just as attributes such as purposes, harm, benefit, and variable actions are impossible for Allah (because He is transcendent), it is likewise impossible for the servant to be free from seeking his own benefit and the paths of obligations. If there were no benefit for the servant in avoiding punishment, the obligation would not be firmly established for him. Consequently, the claim of those who assert literal love of Allah is invalid. Allah's existence is too exalted for any creature to attain fortune from Him. The creature is always driven by fortunes and purposes that reduce to repelling harm and attracting benefit. Love of Allah is not to be understood in its apparent literal sense, for Allah is sanctified from inclination, partiality, tenderness, and yearning. Instead, Allah's love for His servant means His intention to bestow favor, and the servant's love for his Lord means his steadfastness in obedience. Allah is far above being reached by any fortune or attaining any fortune Himself.

Key Terms
الحظوظ
الأغراض
المحبة
العبودية
التكاليف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that love of Allah implies literal emotion or need in Allah.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies that Allah is sanctified from inclination and need; divine love is metaphorical, meaning His intention to bestow favor, while human love means obedience.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the real meaning of Allah's love for His servant?
A) Literal affection and emotional attachment
B) His intention to bestow favor upon him
C) His need for the servant's worship
D) A mutual exchange of benefits

Key Takeaway
Love of Allah is not a literal emotion but a metaphor for His will to bless and our commitment to obey.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Juwayni says the servant revolves around fortunes and purposes—repelling harm and attracting benefit. In your worship, are you genuinely seeking Allah's favor, or are you merely avoiding punishment? Which motive is stronger, and what does that reveal about the nature of your love for Him?
?
If Allah's love for you is defined as His intention to bestow favor, can you accept that even your trials are intentional favors from Him? What would change in your heart if you saw every hardship as an act of divine love?
?
The text denies literal love for Allah because it implies need and change in Him. Does your concept of loving Allah ever rely on feeling a reciprocal emotional connection? How would you worship if you knew that Allah does not 'feel' love as humans do, but only wills your good?

The Beatific Vision: Delight, Not the Sight Itself
Juwayni clarifies that the Beatific Vision is hoped for due to the accompanying delight.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the hope of Ahl al-Sunna is not the vision of Allah in itself, but the unique delight that accompanies it.

Core Idea
The vision is hoped for because of its delight, not in itself.

Imam Juwayni presents a subtle point about the Beatific Vision. Many assume the vision itself is the ultimate hope, but Juwayni argues that if that were the case, Allah could have attached punishment to it instead. The real hope is the incomparable delight that Allah will join to the vision. This insight preserves both the desirability of the vision and Allah's absolute sovereignty.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the translation. Let Juwayni's precise wording sink in.
Notice the conditional 'otherwise' (والا) which signals a key argument.
2
Identify Juwayni's main claim
Juwayni states that the vision is the hope of Ahl al-Sunna, but immediately specifies that it is because of the accompanying delight that is beyond compare.
He says 'there is the locus of hopes' (وهنا مناط الآمال).
3
Understand the hypothetical case
Juwayni considers: if the vision itself were hoped for, then Allah could have joined punishment to it, making believers fear it. This shows the vision alone is not inherently desirable.
This is a logical argument to show that the hope must be in something else.
4
Reflect on the implications
Juwayni concludes that the sweetness of faith depends on understanding this point. Without it, one cannot truly appreciate the hope of the vision.
This links doctrine to personal spiritual experience.
5
Appreciate the uniqueness of this teaching
Juwayni notes that these secrets are not found in his other writings, showing the special nature of this discussion.
This adds weight to the lesson.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والرؤية آمال أهل السنة : وأنا أقول فيها : أن الله تبارك وتعالى يقرن بها فنا من الروح لا يوازيه روح ، وهنا مناط الآمال . والا فالرؤية في عينها لا يجوز أين تكون مأمولة ، وكان يجوز فى قدرته ، أن يقرن بها منتهى عقوبة الكفار ، حتى يحذرها المؤمنون كما يرجونها ، الآن . ولن يجد المرء الله مولانا - حلاوة الايمان ، حتى يحيط بما ذكرته علما ، ولولا ثقتى بأن مولانا بتوفيق الله ، يبتدر برأيه الثاقب هذه الحقائق لما ثبت اليه أسرار هذه الأبواب ، التى لم أضمنها شيئا من التصانيف .
And the vision is the hope of the people of the Sunna: I say concerning it that Allah, Blessed and Most High, will join to it a kind of delight that no delight can match, and there is the locus of hopes. Otherwise, the vision in itself cannot be hoped for, and [otherwise] it would have been possible in His power to join to it the ultimate punishment of the disbelievers, so that believers would fear it just as they now hope for it. And a person will not find, our Lord—the sweetness of faith—until he encompasses with knowledge what I have mentioned. And were it not for my confidence that our Lord, by Allah's guidance, will hasten with his penetrating insight to these truths, the secrets of these chapters, which I have not included in any of my writings, would not have been established to him.
Source pages p. 59

Commentary
This passage clarifies the doctrine of the Beatific Vision (ru'ya) as the hope of Ahl al-Sunna. Juwayni explains that Allah will accompany the vision with a unique delight that is incomparable—that is the real object of hope. If the vision were hoped for in itself, it could just as well be accompanied by severe punishment, causing believers to fear it instead of hope. The true sweetness of faith is attained only when one understands this point. Juwayni expresses confidence that the esteemed reader (the addressee, possibly a ruler or scholar) will grasp these truths quickly, noting that these secrets are not found in his other writings.

Key Terms
الرؤية
أهل السنة
الروح
الآمال
حلاوة الإيمان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The vision of Allah is to be hoped for in itself.
Correction: Juwayni argues that the vision is hoped for only because of the incomparable delight Allah joins to it; otherwise, it could have been a source of fear.

Checkpoint
Why, according to Juwayni, is the vision of Allah not to be hoped for in itself?
A) Because it is impossible
B) Because it could be accompanied by punishment
C) Because it is only for the prophets
D) Because it has no reward

Key Takeaway
The Beatific Vision is the hope of Ahl al-Sunna not because of the sight alone, but because of the matchless delight Allah will join to it.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly desire Allah for Himself, or do you find yourself hoping for His reward more than His pleasure? How does Juwayni's distinction between the vision and its delight challenge your own heart's intention?
?
If the vision itself could have been joined with punishment, would you still long to see Allah? What does your answer reveal about the purity of your love for Him?
?
Juwayni says no one finds the sweetness of faith until they grasp this truth. Have you tasted that sweetness? If not, what is holding you back from seeking the delight that accompanies the vision rather than just the sight?

The Condition for Miracles to Indicate Prophethood
Juwayni explains the logical condition for a miracle to indicate prophethood.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that a miracle proves prophethood only when it matches the specific claim of the prophet, and not simply because it is extraordinary.

Core Idea
A miracle is a sign only when it confirms the claim it is meant to prove.

Juwayni begins by laying down a critical principle: not every extraordinary event is a proof of prophethood. The miracle gains its evidential power only when it occurs in perfect alignment with a specific claim. In this passage, he clarifies this rule through an analogy and then addresses potential exceptions and misunderstandings.

Guided Path
1
Understand the core principle
Read the first few sentences where Juwayni states that a miracle does not indicate merely by being extraordinary, but by occurring in accordance with the claim of prophethood.
Notice the distinction between 'absolute claim' and 'specific claim'.
2
Follow the royal court analogy
Juwayni gives an example of a man claiming closeness to a king, and the king's action confirming it. See how this illustrates the principle of confirmation through alignment.
The analogy shows that the same event would confirm different claims depending on context.
3
Distinguish miracles from other extraordinary events
Juwayni clarifies that God may cause extraordinary events (karamat) for saints without a claim, or trials through false claimants. These are not miracles because they do not accompany a true claim of prophethood.
Note his reference to the Antichrist and Pharaoh – these are not counterexamples to the rule.
4
Grasp the conclusion
The passage ends by reiterating that miracles do not indicate by themselves; they indicate prophethood only when God uses them to affirm the truthfulness of the prophet.
This understanding resolves many objections.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الاعجاز • فان المعجزة لا تدل من حيث تتعلق بالدعوى المطلقة المرسلة وانما تدل على النبوة ، من حيث تقع على وفق دعوى النبوة . فان تعلق خارق عادة بدعوى أخرى ، دل على صدق تلك الدعوى.. واذا استشهد من قام فى المجلس المشهود الذى صَوَّرهُ ياه . وقال أيضا الملك : انى من المقربين عندك ، والمختصين فى مجلسك : من فان كنت كذلك ذلك ، فقم واقعد ففعل الملك ذلك . دل على تصديقه .. ثم ما يجرى من هذا لا يدل على أن مثل هذا ، لو جرى متعلقا بدعوى الرسالة ، لم يدل على صدق مدعيها . نعم . لست أنكر أن سنة الله تبارك وتعالى اظهار الكرامات في الأغلب ، من غير ايثار واختيار . والذي ذكرناه فى التجويز ، لا فى الاخبار عما تجرى به منَّة الله جلت قدرته ، ولا على القاعدة الممهدة : أن يظهر الله فتنة على أيدى مدعى الربوبية من العباد، كما ورد في الأقاصيص : من اجراء الله النيل مع فرعون حيث دار. شاهى ثنا توافق دعوى وكما ورد فى الأخبار مما سيجرى الله من الفتن ، وخوارق الموائد على المسيخ الدجال ، وليست هذه الأشياء خارقة للمعجزة : فانا كررنا مرارا : أن المعجزة لا تدل لعينها . وانما تدل النبوة ، وليس مع من يدعى الالهية الهية ، طلب تصديق فقال : اذا وافق ما جاء به دلت على التصديق الله وكان هذا نازلا منزلة قول صدقت ، ومن أحاط بما ذكرناه ، هان عليه درك الجواب عن كل ما يرد عليه مما سواه ، وبالله التوفيق
Miracles. The miracle does not indicate in terms of being connected to an absolute, unrestricted claim, but rather it indicates prophethood in terms of occurring in accordance with the claim of prophethood. If an extraordinary event is attached to another claim, it indicates the truth of that claim. And when one who stood in the assembly which he portrayed [?] – and he also said: ‘I am among those close to you and those specialized in your council’ – if you are such, stand and sit, and the king did that, it indicated his affirmation. Then what occurs from this does not indicate that such a thing, if it occurred attached to a claim of prophethood, would not indicate the truth of its claimant. Yes. I do not deny that the way of God, blessed and exalted, is the manifestation of miracles (karamat) in most cases, without preference and choice. What we have mentioned is about permissibility, not about informing of what God’s grace, exalted is His power, proceeds with, nor on the established principle: that God may cause a trial at the hands of those who claim divinity among servants, as has come in the stories: of God’s causing the Nile to flow with Pharaoh when the claim corresponded. And as has come in reports of trials God will cause, and extraordinary table-spreads at the hands of the false messiah (al-masikh al-dajjal), and these things do not contradict the miracle; for we have repeated many times: the miracle does not indicate in itself. Rather it indicates prophethood, and one who claims divinity does not have a request for affirmation. He said: if what he brought matches, it indicates affirmation from God, and this is in the position of saying ‘You have told the truth.’ And whoever encompasses what we have mentioned, it becomes easy for him to grasp the answer to everything that comes to him besides this. And with God is success.
Source pages p. 70

Commentary
This passage is a demonstration of how miracles indicate prophethood. Juwayni first lays down the general principle: a miracle does not indicate in virtue of being extraordinary per se, but rather because it occurs in accordance with the specific claim of prophethood. If an extraordinary event is attached to another claim, it indicates the truth of that claim, not necessarily prophethood. He illustrates this with an analogy: a man in a court claims to be close to the king, and the king tells him to stand and sit; when the king does so, it confirms the man's claim. Similarly, if an extraordinary event is linked to a claim of prophethood, it confirms the claim. However, Juwayni clarifies that this does not mean every extraordinary event confirms any claim; he acknowledges that God sometimes causes miracles (كرامات) for saints without any deliberate request, but that is a matter of God's grace, not a basis for proof. He also warns against confusing miracles with extraordinary events that may accompany false claimants, such as the trials of the Antichrist (الدجال) or the splitting of the Nile for Pharaoh—these are not genuine miracles (معجزات) because they do not proceed from a true claim of prophethood. The core point is that a miracle (معجزة) does not prove truth by its own nature; it proves only when it aligns with the claim of prophethood. The passage ends with the assertion that whoever understands this can easily answer all objections on the topic.

Key Terms
المعجزة
النبوة
الدعوى
خارق العادة
الكرامات
التصديق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Any extraordinary event automatically confirms a claim of prophethood.
Correction: Juwayni explains that a miracle only confirms the specific claim it is attached to, and only if it matches that claim. Extraordinary events can also occur for saints (karamat) or as trials for false claimants.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what does a miracle indicate?
A) The truth of any claim it is attached to
B) Prophethood only when it occurs in accordance with the claim of prophethood
C) The divinity of the claimant
D) The occurrence of an extraordinary event

Key Takeaway
A miracle proves prophethood only when it perfectly aligns with the claim of prophethood, as a divine confirmation of truth.

Three Theological Categories: Tashbih, Ta'til, and Tawhid
Juwayni defines three theological stances on God's nature.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni classifies theological errors into tashbih and ta'til, and how the true Muslim position avoids both extremes through affirming tawhid.

Core Idea
True tawhid balances affirmation of God with inability to grasp His reality.

Juwayni presents a concise classification of how people relate to God's nature. Two extremes overshoot the mark: one likens God to creation, the other denies Him altogether. The correct path is to affirm God's existence while recognizing that His essence is beyond full human comprehension. This passage establishes the foundation for a balanced theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice how Juwayni defines three categories of people regarding their concept of God.
The passage is dense; take your time to absorb each category.
2
Identify the error of the mushabbih
The mushabbih imagines God as a form in the mind. Understand why this is an error: it likens God to creation.
Tashbih means 'likening' or 'anthropomorphism'.
3
Identify the error of the mu'attil
The mu'attil is content with pure negation. Understand why this is also an error: it denies God's attributes entirely.
Ta'til means 'emptying' or 'negation'.
4
Understand the muwahhid position
The muwahhid acknowledges God and confesses inability to grasp His reality, saying 'Nothing is like Him' without imagining or negating. This is the balanced middle path.
This is the definition of true tawhid according to Juwayni.
5
Reflect on the classification
Consider why this classification is foundational for Islamic theology. It provides a framework for avoiding extremes in understanding God.
This classification is often used in kalam to define orthodoxy.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
من تخيل الله بصورة في ذهنه فهو مشبه، ومن اطمأن إلى النفي المحض فهو معطل، ومن اعترف بالله ثم اعترف بالعجز عن درك حقيقته فهو موحد، يريد من يعترف بالله ثم يقول عنه ليس كمثله شيء ولم يلزم نفسه بتخيله أو نفيه، فهذا هو المسلم حقا وصدقا.
Whoever imagines God as a form in his mind is a likener (mushabbih), and whoever is content with pure negation is a negator (mu'attil), and whoever acknowledges God and then acknowledges inability to grasp His reality is a unitarian (muwahhid). He intends: whoever acknowledges God and then says about Him 'Nothing is like Him' and does not oblige himself to imagine Him or negate Him, then that person is the true and genuine Muslim.
Source pages p. 99

Commentary
This passage classifies people into three categories regarding their concept of God. First, the mushabbih: those who imagine God as a form or likeness in their mind. Second, the mu'attil: those who are content with pure negation, denying all attributes. Third, the muwahhid: those who affirm God's existence but confess inability to fully comprehend His reality, saying 'Nothing is like Him' while neither forming mental images nor negating His attributes. This third group represents true Islam, as they avoid both extremes.

Key Terms
تشبيه (tashbih)
تعطيل (ta'til)
توحيد (tawhid)
مشبه (mushabbih)
معطل (mu'attil)
موحد (muwahhid)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that the middle path is simple agnosticism or admitting ignorance without affirming attributes.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies that the muwahhid affirms God's existence and attributes ('Nothing is like Him') while acknowledging the ultimate inscrutability of His essence. It is not a denial of knowledge but a confession of limited human capacity.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, who is considered a true Muslim?
A) One who imagines God in the mind
B) One who denies all attributes
C) One who affirms God and says 'Nothing is like Him' without imagining or negating
D) One who ignores the issue

Key Takeaway
The true Muslim affirms God's existence and attributes while acknowledging that God's essence is beyond full human comprehension, avoiding both anthropomorphism and denial.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think about God, do you ever catch yourself picturing a form, even abstractly? Juwayni calls that tashbih. How does that make you feel, and what will you do to guard against it?
?
Are you sometimes more comfortable avoiding thinking about God's attributes altogether, to sidestep the difficulty? Could that be a subtle form of ta'til in your heart? What step can you take today to affirm God's attributes without reducing Him?
?
The true Muslim, Juwayni says, acknowledges inability to grasp God's essence. Does that humbling reality bring you closer to God, or does it unsettle you? What does your inner reaction reveal about your faith?

Three Types of Revelation and the Negation of Vision
Juwayni distinguishes three types of wahy and clarifies negation of vision not sound.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three types of revelation (wahy) according to Imam Juwayni and why the negation of vision does not negate sound.

Core Idea
Revelation has three modes; negating vision does not negate sound.

Imam Juwayni distinguishes three ways Allah communicates with prophets: inspiration to the heart, speech from behind a veil (sound without sight), and speech through an angel. This passage also clarifies a key point: when the Quran says believers will not see Allah in this world, it does not deny they may hear His speech.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its English translation. Notice how Juwayni structures the three types of revelation.
The passage is concise; each type is introduced with a specific phrase.
2
Identify the three types
List the three types of revelation mentioned: 1) inspiration to the heart, 2) from behind a veil (sound without vision), 3) through messengers (speech via intermediary).
3
Understand the negation of vision
Juwayni states that the apparent meaning of the text negates vision (ru'ya) but does not negate sound (sawt). Reflect on why this distinction is important in Islamic theology.
This addresses a common misunderstanding that negation of vision implies negation of all sensory perception of Allah.
4
Reflect on the doctrinal significance
Consider how this classification helps uphold both divine transcendence (Allah is not seen in this world) and the reality of revelation (He speaks).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الثلاثة فان الوحى من وراء حجاب غير الوحى عن طريق الرسل ، فالوحي قلب الهام والوحى من وراء حجاب صوت بدون رؤية والوحى عن طريق الرسل كلام بواسطة . والظاهر من النص نفى الرؤية ، لا ينفي الصوت .
As for the three, revelation from behind a veil is different from revelation through messengers. So revelation to the heart is inspiration, revelation from behind a veil is sound without vision, and revelation through messengers is speech through an intermediary. And the apparent meaning of the text negates vision, not sound.
Source pages p. 104

Commentary
This passage defines the three types of revelation (wahy) as described in the Quran. It specifies that the text's apparent meaning negates the vision of Allah (ru'ya) but does not negate sound (sam'). This is part of a larger discussion on divine attributes and how they are communicated.

Key Terms
وحي
رؤية
صوت

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some assume that if Allah cannot be seen, then He also cannot be heard, or that all sensory perception of Allah is negated.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies that negation of vision (ru'ya) does not negate sound (sawt). Allah can be heard without being seen, as in revelation from behind a veil.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the type of revelation that is 'sound without vision'?
A) Revelation to the heart (inspiration)
B) Revelation from behind a veil
C) Revelation through messengers
D) None of the above

Key Takeaway
Revelation occurs in three modes: inspiration, sound without sight, and speech through an angel; negating vision does not negate sound.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You claim to believe that Allah spoke to Prophet Musa from behind a veil without being seen. Does your prayer reflect that same awe for a God who can be heard but not seen? What would change if you truly internalized that when you say 'Allahu Akbar'?
?
If revelation can be sound without sight, are you listening for Allah's guidance in your life with the same intensity as if you could see Him? What does it say about your faith that you rely more on sight than hearing when both are created?

Divine Transcendence
beginner
4 min
The Muhkam Text Negates Vision of Allah
Juwayni argues that the muhkam verse 'Lan tarani' negates vision of Allah and refutes metaphorical interpretation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni uses the muhkam verse 'Lan tarani' to decisively negate the possibility of seeing Allah, and why ambiguous texts must be interpreted in light of clear ones.

Core Idea
The clear Quranic text 'You will not see Me' is the definitive proof against vision of Allah.

In this passage, Imam al-Juwayni presents a key argument from the Quran against the metaphorical interpretation of verses that might suggest the possibility of seeing Allah. He anchors his reasoning in the clear (muhkam) verse 'Lan tarani' (You will not see Me), which he argues decisively settles the matter. Before you read the original text, consider how this approach of referring ambiguous texts to clear ones is a foundational principle of Islamic theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then the literal translation. Notice the flow from principle to specific proofs.
2
Identify the two text types
Juwayni distinguishes between muhkam (clear) and mutashabih (ambiguous). See how he uses this principle to anchor his argument.
The muhkam here is 'Lan tarani'.
3
Understand the supporting arguments
Moses' question was not out of ignorance, and Allah warned the Muslim nation not to ask like the previous nation. Reflect on how these strengthen the case.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المعنى المجازى عن نعمة الله وحيث النص متشابه فليرجع الى المحكم الذى ينفي الرؤية . ويقوى المحكم قول الله لموسى « لن ترانی » وقد كان سؤال موسى عن غير ذهول منه عن الغيب . والا كان يعاد السؤال مرة أخرى . وما كان الله عز وجل يؤكد على الأمة الاسلامية أن يسألوا بما سألت عنه الأمة الأولى فقد قال تعالى : ( أم تريدون أن تسألوا رسولكم كما سئل موسى من قبل ؟ » ) البقرة : ١٠٧
The metaphorical meaning of God's favor, and where the text is mutashabih, reference is made to the muhkam which negates vision. And the muhkam is strengthened by the saying of Allah to Moses: 'You will not see Me' (Lan tarani). And Moses' question was not out of ignorance of the unseen; otherwise he would have repeated the question again. And Allah, Mighty and Majestic, warned the Islamic nation against asking as the first nation asked, for He said: 'Or do you wish to ask your Messenger as Moses was asked before?' (Quran 2:107).
Source pages p. 111

Commentary
This passage presents an argument against the metaphorical interpretation of verses that suggest the possibility of seeing Allah. The author states that when a text is mutashabih (ambiguous), it must be referred back to the muhkam (clear and decisive) text. The muhkam here is the verse 'Lan tarani' (You will not see Me), which explicitly negates the vision of Allah for Moses in this world. The author then argues that Moses' request to see Allah was not due to ignorance of the unseen; otherwise he would have repeated the question. Moreover, Allah warned the Muslim ummah not to ask their Messenger as the previous nation asked Moses. This is supported by Quran 2:107. The overall argument is that the clear texts (muhkam) decisively negate the possibility of seeing Allah, and any ambiguous texts must be interpreted in light of these clear texts.

Key Terms
المحكم
المتشابه
الرؤية
لن تراني

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the verse 'Lan tarani' only applies to Moses' specific request and does not universally negate vision of Allah.
Correction: Juwayni argues that the verse is a muhkam text that decisively negates vision in general, and Moses' question was not out of ignorance, making the negation binding.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the muhkam text that negates the vision of Allah?
A) The verse 'Lan tarani'
B) The metaphorical interpretation of God's favor
C) The verse about asking the Messenger
D) The ambiguous texts

Key Takeaway
The clear Quranic text 'Lan tarani' decisively negates the possibility of seeing Allah, and any ambiguous verses must be interpreted in light of this clear proof.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read 'Lan tarani', do you truly accept in your heart that Allah is beyond being seen in this life, or do you secretly wish for a visible encounter? How does this affect your trust in the Unseen?
?
Moses asked to see Allah, but his request was not out of ignorance. When you demand explanations or signs from Allah before obeying, is your heart truly free from ignorance of His majesty?
?
Allah warned the Muslim community not to ask excessive questions like the previous nations. What question have you been persistently asking—about the future, about divine decrees, or about the unseen—that you need to surrender to Allah's wisdom today?

What Is Kalam?
beginner

The Criterion for Impossible Attributes
Juwayni defines impossible attributes and states the rule of contingency.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's criterion for determining which attributes are impossible for God: they must be contingent, requiring a specifier.

Core Idea
Contingent attributes imply need; God is beyond need.

Juwayni begins by defining the key concept: an attribute that in created things always requires a specifier (mukhassis) to exist cannot be attributed to God, because that would imply dependence. He then states the rule: every attribute characterized by contingency (jawaz) is impossible for God, since eternity and contingency are mutually exclusive. This lesson establishes the foundational principle.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition of an impossible attribute
Juwayni says that any attribute in creatures whose existence indicates a specifier (mukhassis) that brings it about and intends it, and cannot be conceived without that, is impossible for God.
The key is the need for a specifier.
2
Understand why that attribute cannot apply to God
If such an attribute were established for God, it would indicate His need for a specifier, which is impossible for the Divine.
3
Learn the rule of contingency
Juwayni states the precise criterion: every attribute that is characterized by contingency (jawaz) is impossible for God, because eternity (qidam) and contingency are contradictory.
Contingency means the possibility of existence or non-existence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الكلام فيما يستحيل على الله عز وجل. تقدم قولا وجيزا يحوى الغرض، فإن رأيناه كافيا اجترينا به، وإن رأينا أن نبسط طرفا من الكلام جرينا فيه على ما تجرى به المقادير، والله سبحانه ولى التيسير. فنقول: كل صفة في المخلوقات دل ثبوتها على مخصص يؤثرها ويريدها ولا يعقل ثبوتها دون ذلك فهى مستحيلة على الإله، فانها لو ثبتت له لدلت على افتقاره إلى مخصص دلالتها في حق الحادث المخلوق. وضبط القول في الصفات المفتقرة: ما تمهد أولا من تقدير حكم الجواز، فكل صفة فارقها حكم الجواز، فهي مستحيلة في نعت الإله تعالى. فإن القدم والجواز متناقضان. وتفصيل ذلك: أن الحدوث فينا منعوت بالجواز. فنقدس الإله عنه، والتركب والتصور والتقدير في صفاتنا مرسومة بالجواز فلا تركيب ولا يجوز فرض خلافه. ولا قد، ولا قدر، ولا حد، ولا طول ولا عرض إلا والعقل يجوز أمثالها وخلافها، وهذه الصفات لجوازها افتقرت إلى تخصيص بارئها، فتعالى الصانع عنها. وهذا معنى قول سيد البشر خاتم النبيين محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم إذ قال: «من عرف نفسه، عرفه ربه». أراد: من عرف نفسه بصفات الافتقار، عرف استغناء الرب عن صفاته، فإنه تقدست أسماؤه منتهى الحاجة، وهو بريء عنها.
Discussion on what is impossible for God Almighty. We present a concise statement that contains the purpose; if we see it as sufficient we suffice with it, and if we see to expand a portion of the discourse we proceed in it according to what destinies determine, and God is the granter of ease. So we say: Every attribute in created things whose existence indicates a specifier that brings it about and intends it, and its existence cannot be conceived without that, is impossible for the Divine; for if it were established for Him, it would indicate His need for a specifier just as it indicates it in the case of the originated creature. And the precise statement concerning attributes that are in need: what was first established from the estimation of the ruling of contingency, so every attribute from which the ruling of contingency departs is impossible in the description of God Almighty. For eternity and contingency are contradictory. And the detail of that is that temporality in us is characterized by contingency; so we declare God free from it. And composition, being pictured, and measurement in our attributes are stamped with contingency, so there is no composition nor is it permissible to assume otherwise. And there is no measure, no quantity, no limit, no length, no width, except that the intellect deems their likes and their opposites permissible. And these attributes, because of their contingency, are in need of specification by their Creator, so the Maker is exalted above them. And this is the meaning of the saying of the master of humankind, the seal of the prophets, Muhammad (peace be upon him) when he said: 'Whoever knows himself, knows his Lord.' He intended: Whoever knows himself by attributes of need, knows the Lord's independence from attributes. For His names are sanctified; He is the ultimate need, and He is free from them.
Source pages p. 21

Commentary
This passage is a definition and proof. Juwayni begins by introducing the topic of attributes that are impossible for God. He then defines an impossible attribute: any attribute in creatures whose existence requires a specifier (mukhassis) that brings it about and intends it, and which cannot be conceived without such a specifier. Such attributes cannot be attributed to God because if they were, they would indicate that God needs a specifier, just as they do for originated beings. He then formulates a rule: the key to identifying such attributes is the judgment of contingency (jawaz). Every attribute characterized by contingency is impossible for God, because eternity (qidam) and contingency are contradictory. He elaborates: in us, temporality (huduth) is characterized by contingency, so God is free from it. Similarly, composition (tarkib), being in a direction (tasawwur), and measurement (taqdir) in our attributes are contingent, hence impossible for God. He gives examples: any measure, quantity, limit, length, or width is subject to mental variation, thus contingent. Such attributes need a specifier, so the Creator is beyond them. Juwayni then interprets the prophetic saying 'Whoever knows himself knows his Lord' as meaning that whoever recognizes his own contingent and needy attributes comes to know that God is utterly free from them and self-sufficient.

Key Terms
صفة مستحيلة
مخصص
جواز
قدم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Any attribute found in creatures is automatically impossible for God.
Correction: Only attributes that imply need for a specifier (i.e., contingent attributes) are impossible for God.

Checkpoint
Juwayni says that if an attribute implies need for a specifier in creatures, then attributing it to God would indicate what?
A) God's perfection
B) God's need for a specifier
C) God's independence
D) God's creativity

Key Takeaway
The impossibility of an attribute for God is determined by its contingency and need for a specifier.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If every contingent attribute in you (e.g., your strength, knowledge, mood) requires a specifier, and God has no such need, how does that change the way you view your own dependence on Him? Are you living as if your attributes are self-sufficient?
?
The rule of contingency exposes that any attribute you can imagine that could be otherwise is impossible for God. Does your concept of God still include attributes that you secretly think He could lack? What would it mean to purge every hint of contingency from your understanding of God?
?
When you describe God in your prayers, do you ever attribute to Him qualities that imply need—like expecting something from you? Reflect on a specific attribute you often use and test it against Juwayni's criterion. What must you change?

Impossible Attributes in Detail: Examples and the Prophetic Wisdom
Juwayni elaborates examples of contingent attributes and interprets the hadith 'Who knows himself knows his Lord'.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will recognize specific examples of contingent attributes (temporality, composition, measurement) that are impossible for God, and understand the hadith about self-knowledge.

Core Idea
Everything in creation that is variable or dependent is impossible for God.

After establishing the general rule, Juwayni delves into concrete examples: in humans, temporality (huduth) is contingent, composition (tarkib) is contingent, and all forms of measurement (length, width, etc.) are contingent because the intellect can conceive of their opposites. He then explains the famous prophetic saying 'Whoever knows himself knows his Lord' as a lesson in recognizing our own need and God's perfect independence.

Guided Path
1
Read the detailed examples of impossible attributes
Juwayni lists temporality, composition, being pictured, measurement, quantity, limit, length, width – all are contingent and thus impossible for God.
Each of these in creatures requires a specifier.
2
Reflect on the underlying logic
For each attribute, the intellect can imagine its opposite, so it is contingent. Hence it needs a specifier and cannot be attributed to God.
3
Understand the hadith interpretation
Juwayni explains that 'knowing yourself' means recognizing your attributes of need (contingency), which leads to knowing God's independence from such attributes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الكلام فيما يستحيل على الله عز وجل. تقدم قولا وجيزا يحوى الغرض، فإن رأيناه كافيا اجترينا به، وإن رأينا أن نبسط طرفا من الكلام جرينا فيه على ما تجرى به المقادير، والله سبحانه ولى التيسير. فنقول: كل صفة في المخلوقات دل ثبوتها على مخصص يؤثرها ويريدها ولا يعقل ثبوتها دون ذلك فهى مستحيلة على الإله، فانها لو ثبتت له لدلت على افتقاره إلى مخصص دلالتها في حق الحادث المخلوق. وضبط القول في الصفات المفتقرة: ما تمهد أولا من تقدير حكم الجواز، فكل صفة فارقها حكم الجواز، فهي مستحيلة في نعت الإله تعالى. فإن القدم والجواز متناقضان. وتفصيل ذلك: أن الحدوث فينا منعوت بالجواز. فنقدس الإله عنه، والتركب والتصور والتقدير في صفاتنا مرسومة بالجواز فلا تركيب ولا يجوز فرض خلافه. ولا قد، ولا قدر، ولا حد، ولا طول ولا عرض إلا والعقل يجوز أمثالها وخلافها، وهذه الصفات لجوازها افتقرت إلى تخصيص بارئها، فتعالى الصانع عنها. وهذا معنى قول سيد البشر خاتم النبيين محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم إذ قال: «من عرف نفسه، عرفه ربه». أراد: من عرف نفسه بصفات الافتقار، عرف استغناء الرب عن صفاته، فإنه تقدست أسماؤه منتهى الحاجة، وهو بريء عنها.
Discussion on what is impossible for God Almighty. We present a concise statement that contains the purpose; if we see it as sufficient we suffice with it, and if we see to expand a portion of the discourse we proceed in it according to what destinies determine, and God is the granter of ease. So we say: Every attribute in created things whose existence indicates a specifier that brings it about and intends it, and its existence cannot be conceived without that, is impossible for the Divine; for if it were established for Him, it would indicate His need for a specifier just as it indicates it in the case of the originated creature. And the precise statement concerning attributes that are in need: what was first established from the estimation of the ruling of contingency, so every attribute from which the ruling of contingency departs is impossible in the description of God Almighty. For eternity and contingency are contradictory. And the detail of that is that temporality in us is characterized by contingency; so we declare God free from it. And composition, being pictured, and measurement in our attributes are stamped with contingency, so there is no composition nor is it permissible to assume otherwise. And there is no measure, no quantity, no limit, no length, no width, except that the intellect deems their likes and their opposites permissible. And these attributes, because of their contingency, are in need of specification by their Creator, so the Maker is exalted above them. And this is the meaning of the saying of the master of humankind, the seal of the prophets, Muhammad (peace be upon him) when he said: 'Whoever knows himself, knows his Lord.' Source pages p. 21

Commentary
This passage is a definition and proof. Juwayni begins by introducing the topic of attributes that are impossible for God. He then defines an impossible attribute: any attribute in creatures whose existence requires a specifier (mukhassis) that brings it about and intends it, and which cannot be conceived without such a specifier. Such attributes cannot be attributed to God because if they were, they would indicate that God needs a specifier, just as they do for originated beings. ?

What would it mean for you to start each day by acknowledging that you are in absolute need of God?
?

Or have you used self-awareness for self-gratification, psychologizing your faults without letting them point you to His majesty? What would change if you saw every limitation as a signpost to the Limitless One?

When you are pleased with your intellect, your strength, or your virtue, do you recognize that these could have been otherwise? How does that recognition humble you in your daily life, and how might it increase your gratitude to the One who specified them for you?

The Definition of Miracle and the Miracle of the Quran
Juwayni defines the conditions of a miracle and states that the Quran's miracle is not its eloquence but the divine prevention of imitation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's definition of a miracle and why the Quran's inimitability is based on divine prevention rather than superhuman eloquence.

Core Idea
The Quran's miracle is not its eloquence but God's prevention of imitation.

Juwayni begins by setting a strict definition for what constitutes a miracle: it must exceed ordinary custom in a way that human effort cannot achieve. He then applies this definition to the Quran, arguing that eloquence is within human capability, so the true miracle lies in the fact that God prevented the Arabs from producing something similar. This passage establishes the doctrine of sarfah, a key concept in Ash'ari theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Notice the structure: first a definition, then an application.
The passage contains some unclear words due to OCR; focus on the main argument.
2
Identify the definition of a miracle
Find the conditions Juwayni sets for a true miracle. Note that it must exceed not just ordinary custom but also the highest human achievements in knowledge, talent, and effort.
3
Understand the application to the Quran
See how Juwayni argues that the Quran's eloquence does not qualify as a miracle by that definition. Instead, the miracle is the divine prevention (sarfah) of imitation, supported by the verse.
This is the key doctrinal point: the miracle is in the prevention, not the text itself.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قدمنَا أَنا تشترط في المعجزة أن يجاوز في خرق العادة حدود الطول، ويبلغ مبلغا لا يتوقع الانتهاء اليه بشرية علم، وجودة قريحة، والفنان طبع وثقابة رأى، واصابة فكر، وبعد الموز، واذا تقرر ذلك؟. فالوجه أن لا يدعى جزالة القرآن مبلغ تخرق العادة، بل تقول: التحدى الرسول صلى الله عليه وسلم فصحاء العرب بأن يأتوا بمثل القرآن كما أنبأ عنه قوله تبارك وتعالى: (قل لئن اجتمعت الإنس والجن على أن يأتوا بمثل هذا القرآن لا يأتون بمثله).
We have already stated that we require in a miracle that it exceeds in breaking custom the limits of length, and reaches a level that cannot be attained by human knowledge, quality of mind, [؟] natural talent, sharpness of opinion, correctness of thought, and depth of insight. And if that is established, then the proper approach is not to claim that the eloquence of the Qur'an reaches the level of breaking custom, but rather we say: The Prophet (may Allah bless him and grant him peace) challenged the eloquent Arabs to produce something like the Qur'an, as indicated by His saying, Blessed and Most High: (Say: If mankind and jinn gathered to produce the like of this Qur'an, they could not produce its like).
Source pages p. 72

Commentary
This passage defines the conditions of a miracle (mu'jiza) and applies it to the Qur'an. Juwayni first states the prerequisite: a true miracle must exceed the ordinary breaking of custom in such a way that it cannot be achieved by human knowledge, talent, or effort. Based on this definition, he argues that one should not claim the Qur'an's eloquence itself breaks custom, because eloquence is within human potential. Instead, the miracle is that God challenged the Arabs to produce something similar, and they failed despite being capable. The proof is the verse from Surah al-Isra'. This aligns with the doctrine of sarfah (divine prevention). Juwayni emphasizes that the miracle lies in the prevention, not in the text's inherent quality surpassing human ability.

Key Terms
mu'jiza
sarfah
i'jaz
qariha
khāriq al-‘ādah

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the Quran's inimitability is due to its eloquence being superhuman.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies that eloquence is within human power; the miracle is God's prevention of imitation.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the basis of the Quran's miracle?
A) Its superhuman eloquence
B) Its unique style
C) The inability of the Arabs to imitate it due to divine prevention
D) Its prophecies

Key Takeaway
The miracle of the Quran is that God prevented anyone from imitating it, not that its eloquence is beyond human capacity.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If the Quran's eloquence is within human potential, what does that mean for your awe of it? Do you revere the Quran because of its beauty, or because you sense God's direct intervention in preserving its uniqueness? How can you cultivate the latter?
?
The proof of the Quran's miracle rests on the failed challenge. Have you ever personally felt the weight of that challenge—that no human effort can match it? Or do you take its inimitability for granted? What would it take for you to truly feel the impossibility of producing its like?
?
Juwayni redirected the miraculous from the text to God's act of prevention. Does this shift challenge your faith? Are you comfortable with a miracle that is not inherent in the object but is a divine act? How does this affect your certainty that the Quran is from God?

What Is Kalam?
beginner
5 min
Most People Are Believers, Not Knowers
Juwayni argues that firm belief, not knowledge, is required for salvation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why, according to Juwayni, a firm belief suffices for salvation and why most people are not required to attain demonstrative knowledge of God.

Core Idea
Firm belief saves; demonstrative knowledge is for the few.

In this passage, Juwayni addresses a critical question: is everyone required to have deep, demonstrative knowledge of God? He argues that most people are not—their firm, correct belief is sufficient. He supports this with the example of the early Muslims and the famous hadith about the last words. This lesson unpacks his reasoning and its implications.

Guided Path
1
Read the source passage
Read the Arabic passage once and then the translation. Notice how Juwayni presents his argument from a general statement to a specific proof.
The hadith is the clinching evidence.
2
Identify the thesis
Find the sentence where Juwayni states his main claim: 'most beliefs are not knowledges, but stable, correct, firm beliefs.'
This is the key distinction between 'aqd and ma'rifa.
3
Examine the proof
Notice that his proof is based on the practice of the early Muslims (al-awwalūn), who were only required to have a firm belief, not to pursue proofs.
This historical argument shows that burdening everyone with proofs is not required.
4
Reflect on the hadith
Consider the hadith: 'Whoever’s last words are “There is no god but Allah” enters Paradise.' How does this support the idea that firm belief is enough?
The hadith does not mention knowledge of proofs.
5
Understand the conclusion
Juwayni concludes that the people of knowledge are the minority; the rest are people of beliefs. Reflect on the practical implication for the average believer.
Salvation is accessible to all, regardless of intellectual ability.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ولا تحصل ضرورة ، ولا يستقل بالأدلة كل من يعانى الكلام أيضا ، فمعظم العقود ليست معارف ، ولكنها عقود مستقرة صائبة مصممة ، وما كلف الله الخلائق حقيقة معرفته ، ودرك اليقين في الدين . والدليل على ذلك : أن الأولين ما كلفوا تتبع الأدلة ، وانما طولبوا بعقد مصمم وشهادة والتزام أحكام. وهم ان بقوا في عاقبتهم على عقدهم ، ناجون فائزون كما قال الرسول صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم : "من كان آخر كلامه : لا اله الا الله . دخل الجنة" ... فاذن أرباب المعارف فى العالم : الأقلون . والباقون أهل عقائد .
And necessity is not attained, nor does everyone who engages in kalam master proofs. So most beliefs (al-‘uqūd) are not knowledges (ma‘ārif), but they are stable, correct, and firm beliefs. And Allah did not burden the creatures with the reality of His knowledge and the attainment of certainty in religion. The proof of that is that the early ones were not obligated to pursue proofs, but were required only to have a firm belief, testimony, and adherence to rulings. If they remain upon their belief to the end, they are saved and successful, as the Messenger صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم said: "Whoever’s last words are ‘There is no god but Allah’ enters Paradise." So the masters of knowledge in the world are the few, and the rest are people of beliefs.
Source pages p. 90

Commentary
This passage argues that most people are not required to have demonstrative knowledge (ma‘rifa) of God; instead, a firm, correct belief (aqd) suffices. Juwayni begins by stating that necessity (absolute certainty) is not attained by everyone, nor can everyone independently use proofs—even those who study kalam. Therefore, most beliefs are not true knowledge but are stable and correct. He then asserts that Allah did not burden people with attaining true knowledge and certainty in religion; rather, they were only required to have a firm belief, to testify, and to adhere to rulings. As evidence, he cites the early Muslims (al-awwalūn) who were not required to pursue proofs but only to maintain a firm conviction. If they remain faithful to that conviction until death, they are saved, as the hadith states: 'Whoever’s last words are ‘There is no god but Allah’ enters Paradise.' From this, he concludes that the people of knowledge (arbāb al-ma‘ārif) are the minority; the rest are people of beliefs (ahl ‘aqā’id). Thus, knowledge is not necessary for salvation; a sound belief suffices.

Key Terms
عقد
معرفة
يقين
دليل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Only scholars who can prove God's existence with rational arguments are true believers and will be saved.
Correction: Juwayni explicitly states that most believers have firm beliefs ('uqūd) without demonstrative knowledge, and that such belief suffices for salvation, as shown by the early Muslims and the hadith.

Checkpoint
What is Juwayni's main argument in this passage?
A) Everyone must attain certainty through proofs.
B) Most people have sound beliefs that suffice for salvation, even without demonstrative knowledge.
C) Only prophets have true faith.
D) Knowledge is required for all believers.

Key Takeaway
For Juwayni, a firm, correct belief in God is enough for salvation; demonstrative knowledge is not obligatory for everyone.
