Answering the Objection: How Can the Wise Will Foolishness?
Juwayni answers the objection that the Wise God cannot will foolishness.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's response to the objection that a Wise God cannot will foolishness, including his argument that actions are neutral for God and the analogy of a wise master.

Core Idea
For God, actions are neutral; wisdom does not require preventing all sin.

This passage addresses a common objection: how can a perfectly wise God will what appears to be foolishness? Juwayni responds by clarifying that actions are equivalent for God, who neither benefits nor is harmed. He then provides an analogy to show that a wise master would not leave his slaves unchecked. This lesson unpacks his reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through.
Notice the objection starts with 'فان قيل'.
2
Identify the objection
The objection asks: How can the Wise God will foolishness? Understand why this seems problematic: wisdom and foolishness are opposites.
3
Understand Juwayni's initial reply
Juwayni says actions are equal in relation to God because He neither benefits nor is harmed. This means no action is inherently foolish for God.
He references earlier discussions: 'قد سبق في قدر كاف شاف'.
4
Analyze the analogy
Juwayni compares God to a wise master who sees his slaves sinning. The master does not leave them unchecked. This shows that God's wisdom does not require preventing all sin.
The word 'يستدرجهم' implies gradual drawing.
5
Reflect on the personal note
Juwayni expresses his love for teaching this chapter, showing his passion for the subject. Consider how this personal touch connects to the importance of the lesson.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فان قيل: كيف يريد الحكيم السفه؟ فقد سبق في قدر كاف شاف، لصدر كل ذى لب. وأوضحنا: أن الأفعال متساوية في حق من لا ينتفع ولا يتضرر. ولكن اذا أخبر أنه مكلف مطالب عنادة، مزيح عائلهم، فقوله الحق، وكلامه الصدق. وأقرب أمر بعا رضوان به: أن الحكيم منا اذا رأى جواريه وعبيده يموج بعضهم في بعض، وهم على محارمهم بمرآة منه ومسمع، فلا يحسن تركهم على ما هم عليه. والرب مطلع على سوء أفعال العباد ويستدرجهم من حيث لا يعلمون. وقد أطلت أنفاسي قليلا. ولكن - حرس الله مولانا - لو وجدت فى اقتباس هذا العلم من يسرد لى هذا الفصل لكان - وحق القائم على كل نفس بما كسبت - أحب الى من ملك الدنيا بحذافيرها طول أمدها.
If it is said: How can the Wise will foolishness? It has already preceded in a sufficient, curing decree for every possessor of intellect. And we have explained that actions are equal in relation to the One who neither benefits nor is harmed. But when He informs that He is a taskmaster demanding compliance, removing their excuse, then His word is the truth and His speech is the truth. And the nearest matter by which to be pleased is that the wise person among us, when he sees his slave girls and male slaves surging among each other, and they are violating their sanctities in his sight and hearing, then it is not good to leave them as they are. And the Lord is informed of the evil deeds of the servants and gradually draws them from where they do not know. And I have prolonged my breaths a little. But—may Allah guard our master—if I found in the acquisition of this knowledge someone who would recite to me this chapter, it would be—by the right of the One who stands over every soul for what it has earned—more beloved to me than possessing the entire world for its entire duration.
Source pages p. 54

Commentary
This passage presents another objection: How can the Wise God will foolishness (safah)? The author replies that this has already been covered: actions are equivalent in relation to the One who neither benefits nor is harmed. But when He informs that He is a taskmaster demanding compliance and removing their excuse, His word is truth. He then gives a comparison: a wise master who sees his slaves committing sins in his presence would not leave them unchecked. Similarly, the Lord is aware of the evil deeds of His servants and progressively draws them (yastadrijuhum) from where they do not know. The author then adds a personal note: he has prolonged his discussion, but if he had found someone to recite this chapter to him, it would be more beloved than the entire world.

Key Terms
الحكيم
السفه
مكلف
مطالب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's wisdom requires Him to prevent all sin.
Correction: Juwayni explains that actions are neutral for God; He may will them without foolishness, and His wisdom does not necessitate preventing all sin.

Checkpoint
How does Juwayni respond to the objection that the Wise God cannot will foolishness?
A) God's actions are beyond inquiry.
B) Actions are neutral in themselves for God, so willing them is not foolish.
C) God does not will evil, so the objection is invalid.
D) The objection is based on a misunderstanding of divine attributes.

Key Takeaway
Juwayni defends God's wisdom by showing that actions are inherently neutral for God, and that willing some evil does not contradict wisdom.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly accept that God's wisdom is beyond your comprehension, or do you secretly judge that God should have prevented certain sins in your life? Today, specifically, can you surrender that judgment and trust His wisdom even in the evil you see?
?
Juwayni says actions are equal in relation to God—your sin does not harm Him. But He still holds you accountable. Are you living as if your sin matters more to God than it does (feeling overly guilty) or as if it doesn't matter at all (presuming on His patience)? Which is your tendency, and what one step will you take to correct it?
?
The analogy of the master and slaves: God sees all your sins but gradually draws you. Are you grateful for His patience, or are you taking advantage of His deferral? What specific sin are you persisting in because God hasn't stopped you yet? Will you repent today before the gradual drawing becomes a trap?

Divine Attributes
beginner
4 min
Nothing is obligatory upon God – the proof
Juwayni states the Ash'ari position that nothing is obligatory upon God and gives the proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Ash'ari argument that nothing is obligatory upon God, based on the impossibility of harming or benefiting God.

Core Idea
Obligation only makes sense for beings that can be harmed or blamed; God is beyond both.

Juwayni begins by reporting the views of those who claim that reward and punishment are obligatory upon God. He then states the position of Ahl al-Sunna: nothing is obligatory upon God; His actions are either bounty or justice. The proof is concise but profound: obligation implies the possibility of blame or harm, and Allah is transcendent above such things. Let's examine the passage.

Guided Path
1
Identify the opposing views
Read the first part of the passage where Juwayni mentions two groups who assert obligations upon God. What do they claim is obligatory?
These are likely Mu'tazili views.
2
Understand the position of Ahl al-Haqq
Notice the phrase 'nothing is obligatory upon God' and the distinction between bounty and justice.
This is the Ash'ari consensus.
3
Analyze the proof
Break down the proof: why is obligation only possible for one who can be harmed or blamed? How does this apply to God?
The key is that God is transcendent above harm and benefit.
4
Reflect on the implication
Consider what this means for divine justice. Juwayni affirms that God's punishment is just, but not because it is obligatory.
This guards against the misconception that Ash'aris deny divine justice.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فقال قائلون : يجب على الله تبارك وتعالى ، أن يعاقبه ، ولا يجوز أن يعفو عنه ما لم يتب فإن تاب وجب عليه قبول توبته .. وذهب آخرون : الى أن العفو مسوغ فى العقل .. والثواب واجب على الله - تبارك وتعالى ، عن قولهم علوا كبيرا - من هذيان طويل ، وصار أهل الحق قاطبة : الى أنه لا يجب على الله شيء . فان أتاب وأنعم فيفضله ، وإن عاقب فبعدله . والدليل القاطع في تحقيق ما ارتضاه أهل الحق ، أن الوجوب انما يتحقق فى حق من لو فرض منه ترك الواجب لاستحق الذم واللائمة ، ولو ليم أو عوقب ، لناله ضرر ، والرب تبارك وتعالى يتقدس عن قبول الضر والنفع ، ولا يتحقق تفاوت الأفعال فى حكمه كما سبق .
Some said: It is obligatory upon Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, to punish him, and it is not permissible to forgive him unless he repents; and if he repents, then accepting his repentance is obligatory upon Him. And others went to the view that forgiveness is permissible in reason. And reward is obligatory upon Allah – high exalted is He above their saying – from lengthy nonsense. And the people of truth unanimously held that nothing is obligatory upon Allah. So if He grants repentance and bestows favor, it is by His bounty, and if He punishes, it is by His justice. And the decisive proof in confirming what the people of truth approved is that obligation is only realized in the case of one who, if he were supposed to abandon the obligatory, would deserve blame and censure, and if he were blamed or punished, harm would befall him. And the Lord, Blessed and Exalted, is transcendent above accepting harm and benefit, and no variation of actions is realized in His judgment, as has preceded.
Source pages p. 56

Commentary
This passage is a statement of the Ash'ari position on divine obligation, followed by a proof. Juwayni first reports two opposing views: (1) that God must punish the sinner and must accept repentance (likely the Mu'tazili view), and (2) that forgiveness is permissible but reward is obligatory. He dismisses these as 'lengthy nonsense'. Then he states the position of 'the people of truth' (Ahl al-Haqq) that nothing is obligatory upon God; if He grants repentance and favors, it is by His bounty, and if He punishes, it is by His justice. The proof is that obligation only makes sense for a being who could be blamed or harmed by failing to do the obligatory, and God is transcendent above harm and benefit. Furthermore, actions have no inherent variation in His judgment (i.e., good and evil are not intrinsic). This proof relies on the impossibility of affecting God.

Key Terms
يجب
الوجوب
أهل الحق
الضر والنفع
تفاوت الأفعال

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That Ash'aris deny God's justice; rather they affirm that God's actions are not subject to external obligation.
Correction: Juwayni's proof shows that obligation is not applicable to God, but His justice is affirmed in His punishment: 'if He punishes, it is by His justice.' The Ash'ari position does not deny divine justice; it denies that God is bound by external obligations.

Checkpoint
According to the proof given, why is nothing obligatory upon God?
A) Because God is generous and always rewards.
B) Because obligation only applies to one who can be harmed or blamed, and God is beyond harm.
C) Because the Shari'a does not impose obligations on God.
D) Because human reason cannot know obligation.

Key Takeaway
Nothing is obligatory upon God because obligation requires the possibility of harm or blame, and Allah is beyond both.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believe nothing is obligatory upon God, how does that reshape your plea for forgiveness tonight? Will you beg as a desperate slave, or assume He must accept your repentance?
?
When you consider your greatest sin, do you rely on the fact that God's mercy is His bounty (not an obligation)? What if He chose justice instead? Does that thought drive you to sincere repentance now?
?
If God's actions are not bound by external standards of fairness, how does that affect your trust in His decree when you face hardship? Can you still say 'He is just' without human preconceptions of justice?

The Way of the People of Truth and a Secret of Tawhid
Juwayni concludes reward/punishment and hints at a tawhid secret.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni concludes the discussion on reward and punishment by grounding it in God's promise, and you will be introduced to the idea of a profound secret of tawhid that transcends human capability.

Core Idea
Reward is by promise, not desert; tawhid holds a secret beyond human capacity.

Imam al-Juwayni brings the section on reward and punishment to a close by citing the verse of the blessed ones in Paradise. Their praise is for God's fulfilled promise — not for any inherent right. Then, without warning, he announces a secret of tawhid so weighty that all human capacity could not match its worth. This lesson unpacks that transition and the meaning of the verse, while setting the stage for the secret that follows.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Notice the flow from concluding reward and punishment to the sudden announcement of a secret.
The passage ends mid-sentence. Observe how Juwayni uses the verse to affirm his doctrine.
2
Understand the significance of the verse
The verse from Surah Az-Zumar (39:74) is quoted as the meaning of the speech of the blessed ones (ahl al-jannah). Identify why Juwayni considers this verse encapsulating the doctrine of the people of truth on reward and punishment.
The key point: reward is a fulfillment of God's promise, not a due recompense for human deeds.
3
Reflect on the announcement of the secret
Juwayni declares he will reveal a secret of tawhid. Note his assessment: if compared to all human capability, it would not be equivalent. Ask yourself: what kind of secret could be so profound?
The secret is likely a theological point affirming God's absolute sovereignty and the limits of human reason.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وهذا معنى قول السعداء فيما أخبر الله تبارك وتعالى عنهم وقالوا : الحمد لله الذى صدقنا وعده وأورثنا الأرض ) ( الزمر : ٧٤ ( فهذا مذهب أهل الحق في الثواب والعقاب وأنا الآن أبدى سرا من أسرار التوحيد . لو قوبل بكل ما يدخل في مقدور البشر وميسوره لما كان كفاء له . فأقول : ذهب الصائرون
And this is the meaning of the saying of the blessed ones, as Allah, Blessed and Exalted, informed about them, and they said: 'Praise be to Allah who has fulfilled His promise to us and has made us inherit the land' (Az-Zumar 39:74). So this is the doctrine of the people of truth concerning reward and punishment. And now I will reveal a secret from the secrets of tawhid. If it were compared with everything that enters into human capability and capacity, it would not be equivalent to it. So I say: The ones who went...
Source pages p. 57

Commentary
This passage concludes the discussion on reward and punishment by citing the Qur'anic verse from Surah Az-Zumar where the people of Paradise praise Allah for fulfilling His promise. This, Juwayni says, is the way of Ahl al-Haqq: reward is by promise, not obligation. Then he announces that he will now disclose a secret of tawhid, so profound that all human capability cannot match it. However, the text is cut off at 'So I say: The ones who went...' (probably beginning of the secret). This serves as a transition to the next topic.

Key Terms
السعداء
وعده
أسرار التوحيد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That the 'secret of tawhid' is something esoteric or mystical, accessible only through spiritual experience.
Correction: The secret is likely a rational theological point about God's absolute sovereignty and the limits of human capacity, as indicated by Juwayni's statement that all human capability cannot match it.

Checkpoint
What is the 'meaning of the saying of the blessed ones' that Juwayni refers to?
A) That God rewards the obedient.
B) That God fulfilled His promise to them.
C) That God is just.
D) That humans deserve Paradise.

Key Takeaway
Juwayni summarizes the doctrine of Ahl al-Haqq on reward as God's fulfillment of His promise, and then signals a profound secret of tawhid that transcends all human capability.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you examine your good deeds, do you secretly feel that God owes you something? How does Juwayni’s teaching that reward is by promise, not desert, challenge that attitude? What would it mean for you to truly rely on God’s promise rather than your own merit?
?
The secret of tawhid surpasses all human capability. In your daily life, do you reduce God to what you can understand, or do you embrace the mystery? Today, will you intentionally acknowledge a limit to your own understanding and submit to God’s transcendence?
?
If this secret is beyond all human capacity, then your intellect alone cannot grasp it. Are you willing to accept truths from revelation that you cannot fully rationalize? What step can you take this week to humble your intellect before divine mystery?

The Metaphorical Nature of Love for God
Juwayni refutes literal love of God and offers metaphorical interpretation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Juwayni argues that love for God cannot be literal and must be interpreted metaphorically in terms of divine intention and human obedience.

Core Idea
Divine love is not literal affection but God's intention to bless and the servant's steadfast obedience.

Juwayni confronts a common claim: that humans can truly love God. He argues that since God is transcendent and humans are driven by interests, love cannot be literal. Instead, he reinterprets it in terms of divine will and human obedience, guarding against anthropomorphism.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the strong opening: 'And because of this, the statement of those who claim to love God truly collapses.'
Pay attention to the structure: refutation, reason, reinterpretation.
2
Identify the refutation
Juwayni refutes the claim of literal love by stating God's transcendence above being an object of interest (hazz). Humans always act to repel harm or gain benefit.
The key term is 'الحظ' (interest/fortune).
3
Understand the reason
Because God is sanctified (mutakaddis) from inclination, partisanship, tenderness, and longing, love cannot be literal.
This is based on divine transcendence (tanzih).
4
Grasp the metaphorical interpretation
Juwayni redefines love: God's love for His servant is His intention to bestow goodness; the servant's love for God is steadfastness in obedience.
This avoids anthropomorphism while preserving the scriptural terms.
5
Reflect on the goal
Ask yourself: How does this reinterpretation protect God's transcendence? Why is literal love problematic?
This connects to the broader Islamic principle of tanzih.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وعن هذا اضمحل قول من ادعى محبة الله حقا. فان وجوده متعال عن أن يحظى به ذو حظ. والمخلوق تداوره على الحظوظ والأغراض التي يجمعها دفع الضرر وجلب النفع والمحبة الله تبارك وتعالى غير محمولة على حقيقتها ظاهرا فانه متقدس عن الميل والتحيز والرقة والتوقان. فمحبة الله تبارك وتعالى لعبده: ارادته الانعام عليه، ومحبة العبد لربه: استقامته في طاعته. وهو متقدس لعز جلاله، عن أن يناله حظ، أو ينال حظا.
And because of this, the statement of those who claim to love God truly collapses. For His existence is transcendent above being attained by any possessor of interest. And the created being revolves around interests and aims, which are summarized as repelling harm and attracting benefit. And love of God, blessed and exalted, is not taken according to its literal reality, for He is sanctified above inclination, partisanship, tenderness, and longing. So God's love for His servant is His intending goodness for him, and the servant's love for his Lord is his steadfastness in obedience to Him. And He is sanctified by the might of His majesty from being reached by any interest, or attaining any interest.
Source pages p. 59

Commentary
This passage refutes the claim of those who say they truly love God (mahabbat Allah haqqan). Since God is transcendent (muta'āl) and free from being an object of interest (hazz), the creature is always seeking interests (repelling harm, gaining benefit). Therefore, love of God cannot be literal; it must be interpreted. God's love for the servant means His intention to bestow goodness (irada al-in'am). The servant's love for God means his steadfastness in obedience (istiqamah fi ta'atihi). This guards against anthropomorphism (tashbih) regarding divine love.

Key Terms
محبة الله
الحظ
التأويل
الإرادة
الاستقامة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think humans can literally love God in an anthropomorphic emotional sense.
Correction: Juwayni shows that God is beyond human emotions and attributes; love must be understood metaphorically as divine will and human obedience.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what does the servant's love for God mean?
A) Literal affection.
B) Steadfastness in obedience.
C) Seeking reward.
D) Annihilation in God.

Key Takeaway
Love for God is not anthropomorphic emotion but God's intention to bless and the servant's obedient steadfastness.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If love for God is defined as steadfastness in obedience, where in your life do you claim to love God but lack obedience? Which specific command of His are you ignoring, and what will you do about it today?
?
When you worship, are you seeking God's pleasure or your own benefit? Examine your intentions this past week: did you pray to feel good, or to truly obey? Commit to one act of worship this week solely for God's sake, expecting nothing in return.
?
The passage says God cannot be attained by any interest. Are you trying to 'use' God for worldly gain? Repent from treating Him as a means to an end, and resolve to serve Him as the ultimate end.

Definition and Division of Provision
Juwayni defines rizq, divides it into halal and haram, and affirms God as sole provider.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's definition of provision (rizq), its division into halal, haram, and non-moral categories, and the affirmation that Allah alone is the Provider.

Core Idea
Provision is whatever benefits; only Allah provides.

This lesson introduces a foundational concept in Islamic theology: provision (rizq). Imam al-Juwayni defines rizq broadly to include anything beneficial, then divides it into lawful and unlawful, and even the provision of animals. He concludes by affirming that Allah alone is the Provider, reinforcing tawhid.

Guided Path
1
Read Juwayni's definition
Carefully read the Arabic text and its translation to grasp the author's own words on rizq.
Notice that the definition is functional: whatever benefits is provision.
2
Understand the broad scope
The definition covers all beneficial things, not just food or wealth.
3
Identify the three categories
Locate the division into halal, haram, and non-moral (like animal provision).
The third category is not described as halal or haram.
4
Grasp the theological conclusion
Note the final sentence: Allah is the Provider, none else provides or creates.
This connects rizq to tawhid.
5
Reflect on the common misunderstanding
Some think haram is not provision, but Juwayni includes it because it still provides benefit in this world.
This highlights the difference between worldly benefit and religious approval.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأما الرزق: فكل ما انتفع به منتفع فهو رزقه. ثم الرزق ينقسم الى الحلال والحرام، والى ما لا يتصف بالتحليل والتحريم كرزق البهائم. فالله الرازق، ولا رازق غيره، ولا خالق سواه.
As for provision (rizq): Everything that a beneficiary benefits from is his provision. Then provision is divided into halal and haram, and into what is not described as halal or haram, like the provision of beasts. So Allah is the Provider, and there is no provider other than Him, and no creator besides Him.
Source pages p. 82

Commentary
This passage is a definition and classification of provision (rizq). The author defines it functionally: whatever brings benefit is provision for the one who benefits from it. He then classifies it into three types: halal, haram, and that which is neither (like the natural provision for animals). Finally, he asserts that God alone is the Provider (al-Razzaq) and the sole Creator, emphasizing tawhid in provision.

Key Terms
الرزق
الحلال
الحرام
الرازق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that haram (unlawful provision) is not actually provision because it is sinful to consume.
Correction: Juwayni includes haram as provision because one can still benefit from it in this world, even though it is unlawful.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the definition of provision (rizq)?
A) Only halal sustenance
B) Anything that a beneficiary benefits from
C) Food and drink exclusively
D) What is given by God without human effort

Key Takeaway
Provision is anything beneficial, whether lawful or unlawful, and Allah alone is the ultimate Provider.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that even the haram income you receive or desire is provision from Allah? How does that change your attitude towards it and your reliance on Him alone?
?
If Allah alone provides, why do you fear loss from people or cling to unlawful gains? What would it look like to trust solely in the Provider?
?
Your provision includes everything that benefits you—do you thank Allah even for the unlawful benefits you may have enjoyed? How does this definition of rizq affect your gratitude and repentance?

Allah's Provision Includes Both Lawful and Unlawful
Juwayni affirms that all provision is from Allah, refuting those who exclude oppressors.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that all provision (rizq) comes from Allah, including both lawful and unlawful means, and that denying this contradicts Allah's universal sustenance.

Core Idea
All provision is from Allah, whether halal or haram.

Imam Juwayni concludes his discussion on divine provision by affirming a crucial point: Allah's sustenance covers all creatures, regardless of whether they obtain it through lawful or unlawful means. He warns against the mistaken belief that oppressors and wrongdoers are outside Allah's provision, which would contradict the clear Quranic verse that every creature's provision is upon Allah. This passage solidifies the universality of Allah's provision.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once carefully, then read the literal translation. Notice the footnote markers (1) but do not let them distract from the argument.
The markers likely indicate textual variants; the core meaning remains clear.
2
Identify the key claim Juwayni refutes
Find the statement Juwayni rejects: the claim that oppressors and those who engage in [unlawful transactions] are not included in Allah's provision. Understand why this is problematic.
Juwayni says this would exclude most creatures from being sustained by Allah.
3
Understand the Quranic evidence
Reflect on the verse from Surah Hud (11:6) that every creature's provision is upon Allah. See how it directly supports Juwayni's point that no one is outside Allah's provision.
4
Reflect on the implication for tawheed
Consider why it is important to affirm that all provision, both halal and haram, is from Allah. How does this uphold Allah's sovereignty and sustainer attribute?
It prevents a misconception that limits Allah's provision.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم أنه تبارك وتعالى قسم أرزاق العباد حلالا وحراما ، كما صرفهم بحكمة فى الطاعات والزلات ، توفيقا وخذلانا ، وعطاء وحرمانا . ومن زعم أن الظلمة والذين يتعاطون (۱) ، ليسوا فى رزق الله ، فقد أخرج معظم الخلائق فى معظم الأوقات (1) عن كونهم مرتزقة الله تعالى . وقال تبارك وتعالى : ( وما من دابة فى الأرض الا على الله رزقها ) هود : ٦.
Then, He (Blessed and Exalted) divided the provisions of the servants into lawful and unlawful, just as He directed them by wisdom in acts of obedience and slips, granting success and abandoning, giving and depriving. And whoever claims that the oppressors and those who engage in (1) are not in Allah's provision has removed most of the creatures most of the time (1) from being sustained by Allah the Exalted. And He (Blessed and Exalted) said: 'There is no creature on earth but its provision is upon Allah' (Hud:6).
Source pages p. 83

Commentary
This passage is a concluding argument about divine provision (rizq). It affirms that Allah divides provisions into lawful and unlawful, and that He directs people by wisdom in their actions—whether obedience or sin, through granting success (tawfiq) or abandonment (khidhlan), giving or withholding. Juwayni then refutes the claim that oppressors and those who engage in [unlawful transactions] are not within Allah's provision. He argues that such a claim excludes most creatures from being sustained by Allah, which contradicts the Quranic verse (Hud:6) that every creature's provision is from Allah. The point is that all provision, whether halal or haram, comes from Allah's decree, and denying this undermines His universal sustenance.

Key Terms
رزق
توفيق
خذلان
مرتزقة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that only lawful provision comes from Allah, and that unlawful provision is not from Him.
Correction: Juwayni affirms that Allah provides both halal and haram. The Qur'anic verse (11:6) covers all creatures, so no one's provision is outside Allah's decree.

Checkpoint
What does Juwayni say about those who claim oppressors are not in Allah's provision?
A) They are correct because oppressors do not deserve Allah's provision.
B) They have removed most creatures from being sustained by Allah.
C) They are referring only to lawful provision.
D) They are referring to a specific group of oppressors.

Key Takeaway
Allah's provision encompasses all creatures, whether they obtain it lawfully or unlawfully, as affirmed by the Qur'an.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you acquire wealth through a means you suspect is questionable, do you truly believe that Allah provided it, and that you will be held accountable for how you obtained it? How does this lesson change your approach to earning?
?
Are you quick to judge those who obtain wealth unlawfully, forgetting that even that provision is from Allah? How does this humble you before Him?
?
If you have ever felt your lawful provision is insufficient, do you recognize that Allah's provision is comprehensive but divided by His wisdom? How can you increase your reliance on His decree?

6 min
Two Interpretations of the Threat in Q 4:93
Juwayni presents two exegetical ways to limit the application of Q 4:93.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni defends the possibility of forgiveness for a killer by reinterpreting the apparent threat in Q 4:93 through two scholarly approaches.

Core Idea
Qur'anic threats of eternal punishment can be reconciled with divine mercy through careful interpretation.

Juwayni addresses a key sectarian dispute: does Q 4:93 condemn every intentional killer to eternal Hell? He presents two interpretive strategies from classical scholarship that restrict the verse's application, showing that the threat is not absolute.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and identify the two interpretations
Read the Arabic passage carefully, noting the two distinct interpretations Juwayni presents for Q 4:93.
The first is from Ibn Abbas, the second is based on the meaning of 'khālidan'.
2
Understand the first interpretation from Ibn Abbas
Ibn Abbas interprets 'whoever kills a believer intentionally' as referring only to those who consider killing lawful (mustahill). Juwayni provides evidence: true intent requires no deterrent, and other verses treat the killer as a brother and encourage pardon.
3
Examine the second interpretation regarding 'khālidan'
Juwayni suggests that 'abiding forever' can mean long but finite periods, as in supplications for kings. This allows the threat to be reconciled with mercy.
The term does not necessarily imply eternal duration.
4
Reflect on the implications
Consider how these interpretations protect the possibility of forgiveness for grave sinners, countering the Khawarij and Mu'tazila who took the verse literally.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد تمسكوا بأي من القرآن، فمن أظهرها عندهم، قوله تبارك وتعالى: «ومن يقتل مؤمنا متعمدا فجزاؤه جهنم خالدا فيها» (النساء: 93). وعلى ظاهر هذه الآية وجوه من الكلام، ونحن نأثر منها وجهين. أحدهما: ما روي عن ابن عباس رضي الله عنهما أنه قال: معناه: ومن يقتل مؤمنا مستحلا قتله. ويشهد لذلك: أن العمد إنما يتمحض من يقدم على الشيء إقداما لا يعتقد يزعه عنه وازع. ومن أن القتل من أكبر الكبائر، فقد يدعوه إليه هواه، ويزعه إيمانه عنه فيقدم رجلا مشفقا، والعامد حقا هو الذي لا وازع له في رأيه. والدليل عليه: أنه تبارك وتعالى ذكر في آيات القصاص أحكامه، وصدره بقلب الإيمان، وأثبت للقاتل اسم الأخ، آخذا من أخوة الإيمان، وندب إلى العفو عنه، ولم يتعرض للوعيد، ولم يذكر في آية الوعيد حكم القصاص البتة. فهذا وجه. والثاني: قوله تبارك وتعالى: «خالدا فيها» ظاهر في التأييد، ولا يبعد حمله على الآماد الطوال وإن كانت تنتهي. وقد تجري في مخاطبة الملوك وتحياتهم الدعاء بالخلود إذ يقول القائل: خلد الله ملك الملك، ولو عنوا به تأييدا لزجروا عن سؤال المحال. والنص القاطع في وعد الله تبارك وتعالى: التجاوز عن المذنبين، وهو قوله تبارك وتعالى: «إن الله لا يغفر أن يشرك به ويغفر ما دون ذلك لمن يشاء» (النساء: 116). ولم يرد تعالى أنه يغفر لمن تاب، فإنه ولو أراد ذلك لما انتظم الفرق بين الشرك وغيره. والشرك مغفور له إذا تاب. فإذن من مات من عصاة أهل الإيمان من غير توبة، فأمره مغيب: إن شاء الله غفر له، أو شفع فيه شفيع، وإن شاء عرضه على النار بقدر ذنبه، ثم عاقبته الفوز الأكبر والنجاة. قال رسول الله صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم: «لا يبقى في النار من قلبه مثقال ذرة من الإيمان».
And they have clung to some verses of the Qur’an, and among the most apparent to them is His saying, Blessed and Exalted: 'And whoever kills a believer intentionally, his recompense is Hell, abiding therein forever' (Al-Nisa’: 93). Concerning the literal sense of this verse, there are aspects of discussion, and we will relate two aspects from them. The first: what was narrated from Ibn Abbas, may Allah be pleased with him, that he said: Its meaning is: whoever kills a believer considering his killing lawful. And evidence for that: that intentionality is only realized from one who proceeds to a thing with a proceeding not believing that a deterrent will deter him. And since killing is among the major sins, his desire may call him to it, but his faith deters him, so he advances with apprehensive feet, and the truly intentional is the one who has no deterrent in his view. And the evidence for it: that He, Blessed and Exalted, mentioned in the verses of retaliation its rulings, and prefaced it with [mention of] the heart of faith, and established for the killer the name of brother, derived from brotherhood of faith, and encouraged pardon for him, and did not address the threat, and did not mention in the verse of threat the ruling of retaliation at all. So this is one aspect. And the second: His saying, Blessed and Exalted: 'abiding therein forever' is apparent in perpetuity, and it is not far-fetched to carry it upon lengthy periods even if they end. And it may occur in addressing kings and their greetings the supplication for perpetuity when the speaker says: May Allah perpetuate the king’s kingdom, and if they meant by it eternity, they would be prevented from asking for the impossible. And the decisive text concerning Allah’s promise, Blessed and Exalted, is pardon for the sinners, and that is His saying, Blessed and Exalted: 'Indeed, Allah does not forgive association with Him, but He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills' (Al-Nisa’: 116). And He, Exalted, did not mean that He forgives for one who repents, for if He meant that, the distinction between shirk and other sins would not be established. And shirk is forgiven if one repents. So whoever dies from among the disobedient of the people of faith without repentance, his affair is concealed: if Allah wills, He forgives him, or an intercessor intercedes for him, and if He wills, He exposes him to the Fire according to his sin, then his final outcome is the great success and salvation. The Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him and his family and grant peace, said: 'There will not remain in the Fire anyone in whose heart is a mustard seed’s weight of faith.'
Source pages p. 88

Commentary
This passage is a theological argument defending the possibility of forgiveness for grave sinners (murtakib al-kabira) from Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama‘ah, against the Khawarij and Mu‘tazila who interpreted the Qur’anic threats absolutely. Juwayni presents two ways of interpreting the apparent meaning of Q 4:93, which threatens eternal punishment for killing a believer. The first interpretation, attributed to Ibn Abbas, restricts the threat to those who consider killing lawful (mustahill), not to every intentional killer. The evidence: true intent (al-‘amd) is only present when no deterrent exists; a believer who kills despite his faith is internally conflicted (rajulan mushfiqan), not fully purposed. Moreover, verses about retaliation (Q 2:178) refer to the killer as a brother, encourage pardon, and mention no eternal threat. Thus, the apparent threat is not universally applicable. The second interpretation: the word 'khālidan fīhā' (abiding forever) can denote long periods in Arabic usage, as in supplications for kings ('khallada Allahu mulka al-malik'), which do not imply actual eternity. Furthermore, the decisive text is Q 4:116, which promises forgiveness for all sins except shirk if Allah wills. Juwayni argues that if Q 4:93 were absolute, it would contradict Q 4:116. Therefore, the fate of a sinner who dies without repentance is unresolved (maghib): he is under Allah’s will — either forgiven through His mercy or intercession, or punished temporarily and then saved. The hadith confirms that no one with faith equal to a mustard seed will remain eternally in Hell. Thus, the lesson affirms the doctrine of the possibility of eventual salvation for all monotheists.

Key Terms
مستحل
العمد
الوعيد
الخلود

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Q 4:93 proves that every intentional killer of a believer will be eternally damned.
Correction: Juwayni shows the verse is interpreted restrictively: either limited to those who deem killing lawful, or the term 'khālidan' can mean long but finite duration, allowing for eventual mercy.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni's first interpretation of Q 4:93, to whom does the threat of eternal punishment apply?
A) Every intentional killer of a believer
B) Only those who kill a believer considering it lawful
C) Only those who kill a polytheist
D) Those who kill after committing shirk

Key Takeaway
Juwayni provides two scholarly interpretations that prevent Q 4:93 from contradicting Allah's mercy for monotheists.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you commit a sin you know is grave, do you truly tremble at the threat of Allah's punishment, or do you secretly assume mercy will cover you without wrestling with the Qur'an's warnings? How does Juwayni's careful handling of this verse challenge you to take both the threat and mercy seriously in your own heart?
?
Are there sins in your life that you have normalized or made excuses for—perhaps by telling yourself 'I'm not really that bad' or 'I don't intend to disobey'? How does the concept of 'mustahill' (considering something lawful) force you to examine whether you have unconsciously justified what Allah has forbidden?
?
Have you ever dismissed a verse of seeming unconditionally punishing—like Q 4:93—without digging into how scholars like Juwayni actually interpreted it? What would it cost you to personally wrestle with such verses, rather than avoid them, and let them shape your hope in Allah's mercy and fear of His justice?

intermediate
5 min
The Fate of Grave Sinners: Between Threat and Mercy
Juwayni establishes that grave sinners may be forgiven or eventually saved, using Q 4:116 and a hadith.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Ash'ari position on the ultimate fate of grave sinners who die without repentance, based on Q 4:116 and the hadith about a mustard seed of faith.

Core Idea
No believer with any faith will remain in Hell forever; ultimate salvation is assured.

After addressing the apparent threat, Juwayni turns to the decisive Qur'anic promise that Allah forgives all sins other than shirk as He wills, and cites a hadith confirming that even the smallest amount of faith guarantees eventual exit from Hell.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on the decisive text Q 4:116
Read the Arabic passage starting from 'والنص القاطع' and its translation, paying attention to the argument.
This verse is key: it promises forgiveness for all sins except shirk.
2
Understand Juwayni's argument
Juwayni argues that the verse does not require repentance before forgiveness, because if it did, the distinction between shirk and other sins would collapse. Shirk is also forgiven upon repentance.
Thus, it applies to those who die unrepentant.
3
Consider the three possibilities
For a believer who dies in sin without repentance, his affair is concealed: either Allah forgives him, intercession is accepted, or he is punished temporarily and then enters Paradise.
All outcomes lead to eventual salvation.
4
Note the hadith
The Prophet said: 'No one with faith equal to a mustard seed will remain in the Fire.' This confirms that no believer will be eternally punished.
This is a fundamental Ash'ari doctrine.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد تمسكوا بأي من القرآن، فمن أظهرها عندهم، قوله تبارك وتعالى: «ومن يقتل مؤمنا متعمدا فجزاؤه جهنم خالدا فيها» (النساء: 93). وعلى ظاهر هذه الآية وجوه من الكلام، ونحن نأثر منها وجهين. أحدهما: ما روي عن ابن عباس رضي الله عنهما أنه قال: معناه: ومن يقتل مؤمنا مستحلا قتله. ويشهد لذلك: أن العمد إنما يتمحض من يقدم على الشيء إقداما لا يعتقد يزعه عنه وازع. ومن أن القتل من أكبر الكبائر، فقد يدعوه إليه هواه، ويزعه إيمانه عنه فيقدم رجلا مشفقا، والعامد حقا هو الذي لا وازع له في رأيه. والدليل عليه: أنه تبارك وتعالى ذكر في آيات القصاص أحكامه، وصدره بقلب الإيمان، وأثبت للقاتل اسم الأخ، آخذا من أخوة الإيمان، وندب إلى العفو عنه، ولم يتعرض للوعيد، ولم يذكر في آية الوعيد حكم القصاص البتة. فهذا وجه. والثاني: قوله تبارك وتعالى: «خالدا فيها» ظاهر في التأييد، ولا يبعد حمله على الآماد الطوال وإن كانت تنتهي. وقد تجري في مخاطبة الملوك وتحياتهم الدعاء بالخلود إذ يقول القائل: خلد الله ملك الملك، ولو عنوا به تأييدا لزجروا عن سؤال المحال. والنص القاطع في وعد الله تبارك وتعالى: التجاوز عن المذنبين، وهو قوله تبارك وتعالى: «إن الله لا يغفر أن يشرك به ويغفر ما دون ذلك لمن يشاء» (النساء: 116). ولم يرد تعالى أنه يغفر لمن تاب، فإنه ولو أراد ذلك لما انتظم الفرق بين الشرك وغيره. والشرك مغفور له إذا تاب. فإذن من مات من عصاة أهل الإيمان من غير توبة، فأمره مغيب: إن شاء الله غفر له، أو شفع فيه شفيع، وإن شاء عرضه على النار بقدر ذنبه، ثم عاقبته الفوز الأكبر والنجاة. قال رسول الله صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم: «لا يبقى في النار من قلبه مثقال ذرة من الإيمان».
And they have clung to some verses of the Qur’an, and among the most apparent to them is His saying, Blessed and Exalted: 'And whoever kills a believer intentionally, his recompense is Hell, abiding therein forever' (Al-Nisa’: 93). Concerning the literal sense of this verse, there are aspects of discussion, and we will relate two aspects from them. The first: what was narrated from Ibn Abbas, may Allah be pleased with him, that he said: Its meaning is: whoever kills a believer considering his killing lawful. And evidence for that: that intentionality is only realized from one who proceeds to a thing with a proceeding not believing that a deterrent will deter him. And since killing is among the major sins, his desire may call him to it, but his faith deters him, so he advances with apprehensive feet, and the truly intentional is the one who has no deterrent in his view. And the evidence for it: that He, Blessed and Exalted, mentioned in the verses of retaliation its rulings, and prefaced it with [mention of] the heart of faith, and established for the killer the name of brother, derived from brotherhood of faith, and encouraged pardon for him, and did not address the threat, and did not mention in the verse of threat the ruling of retaliation at all. So this is one aspect. And the second: His saying, Blessed and Exalted: 'abiding therein forever' is apparent in perpetuity, and it is not far-fetched to carry it upon lengthy periods even if they end. And it may occur in addressing kings and their greetings the supplication for perpetuity when the speaker says: May Allah perpetuate the king’s kingdom, and if they meant by it eternity, they would be prevented from asking for the impossible. And the decisive text concerning Allah’s promise, Blessed and Exalted, is pardon for the sinners, and that is His saying, Blessed and Exalted: 'Indeed, Allah does not forgive association with Him, but He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills' (Al-Nisa’: 116). And He, Exalted, did not mean that He forgives for one who repents, for if He meant that, the distinction between shirk and other sins would not be established. And shirk is forgiven if one repents. So whoever dies from among the disobedient of the people of faith without repentance, his affair is concealed: if Allah wills, He forgives him, or an intercessor intercedes for him, and if He wills, He exposes him to the Fire according to his sin, then his final outcome is the great success and salvation. The Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him and his family and grant peace, said: 'There will not remain in the Fire anyone in whose heart is a mustard seed’s weight of faith.'
Source pages p. 88

Commentary
This passage is a theological argument defending the possibility of forgiveness for grave sinners (murtakib al-kabira) from Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama‘ah, against the Khawarij and Mu‘tazila who interpreted the Qur’anic threats absolutely. Juwayni presents two ways of interpreting the apparent meaning of Q 4:93, which threatens eternal punishment for killing a believer. The first interpretation, attributed to Ibn Abbas, restricts the threat to those who consider killing lawful (mustahill), not to every intentional killer. The evidence: true intent (al-‘amd) is only present when no deterrent exists; a believer who kills despite his faith is internally conflicted (rajulan mushfiqan), not fully purposed. Moreover, verses about retaliation (Q 2:178) refer to the killer as a brother, encourage pardon, and mention no eternal threat. Thus, the apparent threat is not universally applicable. The second interpretation: the word 'khālidan fīhā' (abiding forever) can denote long periods in Arabic usage, as in supplications for kings ('khallada Allahu mulka al-malik'), which do not imply actual eternity. Furthermore, the decisive text is Q 4:116, which promises forgiveness for all sins except shirk if Allah wills. Juwayni argues that if Q 4:93 were absolute, it would contradict Q 4:116. Therefore, the fate of a sinner who dies without repentance is unresolved (maghib): he is under Allah’s will — either forgiven through His mercy or intercession, or punished temporarily and then saved. The hadith confirms that no one with faith equal to a mustard seed will remain eternally in Hell. Thus, the lesson affirms the doctrine of the possibility of eventual salvation for all monotheists.

Key Terms
الشفاعة
أهل الكبائر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Grave sinners who die without repentance will be eternally punished in Hell.
Correction: Juwayni shows they may be forgiven, interceded for, or punished temporarily then saved; no believer remains forever.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the fate of a believer who dies unrepentant after committing a major sin?
A) Eternal Hell
B) Automatically forgiven
C) His affair is concealed: either forgiveness, intercession, or temporary punishment then paradise
D) He is subject to the will of God only

Key Takeaway
The Ash'aris believe that no believer, regardless of sin, will remain in Hell permanently; the Qur'an and hadith assure eventual mercy.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you were to face Allah tonight with a major sin unrepented for, would your hope rest on His mercy, intercession, or the hadith? Which of these three possibilities do you most rely on in your daily life, and why?
?
The hadith says that even a mustard seed’s weight of faith ensures eventual salvation. But does your current faith feel like it could survive the Fire if it had to? How would you strengthen it starting today, not just for safety but for love?
?
Juwayni’s argument shows that your eternal destiny is tied to the reality of your faith, not just your repentance. What is one area where your actions might contradict the faith in your heart, and will you commit to change it before tonight?

Repentance: Rationally Obligatory and Scripturally Affirmed
Juwayni states the rational obligation of God accepting repentance, citing Quran and hadith.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's claim that God's acceptance of repentance is rationally obligatory and supported by revealed texts.

Core Idea
Repentance is not just recommended; God's acceptance of it is rationally necessary and scripturally affirmed.

Juwayni begins a new section on repentance (tawbah) by making a strong claim: it is rationally obligatory (wajib 'aqlan) upon God to accept repentance. He then immediately grounds this rational obligation in scriptural proofs from the Quran and hadith. This lesson unpacks that dual foundation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyze it. Then read the translation. Let Juwayni's own words come through first.
Note the phrase 'واجب عقلا' – this is a key technical term.
2
Identify the evidence
Juwayni gives two pieces of scriptural evidence: a Quranic verse and a hadith. Find them and note how they support his claim.
The Quranic verse is from Surah ash-Shura (42:25). The hadith is well-known.
3
Reflect on the rational obligation
Consider why Juwayni might say acceptance of repentance is rationally obligatory. What does this imply about God's justice and mercy?
This rational aspect is not expanded here but sets the tone for the section.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فصل يجب قبول التوبة على الله عقلا. ولكن ورد الشرع بقبولها (١) قال الله تبارك وتعالى: «وهو الذى يقبل التوبة عن عباده» (٢٥ الشورى) وقال صلى الله عليه وسلم: «التائب من الذنب كمن لا ذنب له).
Section: Acceptance of repentance is obligatory upon God rationally. But the Sharia came with its acceptance. (1) Allah, Blessed and Exalted, said: 'And He is the One who accepts repentance from His servants' (Surah ash-Shura, 25). And the Prophet, peace be upon him, said: 'The one who repents from sin is like one who has no sin.'
Source pages p. 93

Commentary
This passage is a definition and proof. Juwayni begins by stating that it is rationally obligatory (واجب عقلاً) upon God to accept repentance. He then notes that the revealed law (الشرع) confirms this acceptance. He provides two pieces of evidence: a Quranic verse from Surah ash-Shura (42:25) where Allah describes Himself as accepting repentance from His servants, and a hadith where the Prophet (peace be upon him) says that a repentant person is like one who has no sin. The rational obligation is implied by God's justice and mercy, though Juwayni does not expand here. The scriptural evidence is direct. The footnote (1) likely indicates a variant reading.

Key Terms
توبة
قبول التوبة
واجب عقلا

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think repentance is merely recommended or that God is not obliged to accept it.
Correction: Juwayni states that acceptance of repentance is rationally obligatory upon God, meaning reason itself demands it, and scripture confirms this.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, is the acceptance of repentance by God based on reason or revelation?
A) Only on revelation
B) Only on reason
C) On both reason and revelation
D) Neither

Key Takeaway
Juwayni argues that God's acceptance of repentance is rationally obligatory and affirmed by both Quran and hadith.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Juwayni states that God's acceptance of repentance is rationally obligatory. If you truly believed that God is obligated to accept your repentance, would you be more or less hesitant to turn to Him after a sin? Are you acting as if repentance is merely optional or as if it is a certainty that God will forgive? What sin have you been putting off repenting from because you doubted God's willingness to forgive?
?
The Prophet ﷺ said the repentant is like one without sin. Do you live in the light of that promise, or do you let past sins weigh you down as if they are unforgivable? What would it take for you to internalize that your past can be completely wiped away?
?
This lesson says acceptance of repentance is rationally obligatory upon God. Does your understanding of God's justice and mercy lead you to repentance quickly, or do you delay it? What is holding you back from turning to Allah right now, in this moment?

Necessary Attributes: Power, Will, Knowledge, Life
Juwayni deduces four necessary attributes from contingency.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the logical sequence of necessary attributes (power, will, knowledge, life) that Juwayni deduces from the contingency of the world.

Core Idea
The world's contingency logically implies a Creator who is living, powerful, willing, and knowing.

Juwayni presents a concise yet profound deduction: from the fact that the world is originated (hadith), we can infer that its Originator (muhdith) must possess certain attributes. This passage establishes a logical chain starting from power, then will, then knowledge, culminating in life. Understanding this sequence is key to the Ash'ari understanding of God's necessary attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis. Then read the literal translation. Notice the logical connectors (فإن, إذ, وقبل أن).
Pay attention to the repetition of 'before' (قبل أن) which indicates logical priority, not temporal.
2
Trace the logical deduction
Start from the premise 'the world is originated'. Work through each step: originator must be powerful because creation needs power; powerful must be willing to choose; willing must be knowing to know what is willed; and all three presuppose life.
This is a chain: world → power → will → knowledge → life.
3
Identify the key attributes
List the four attributes mentioned: power (qadir), will (murid), knowledge ('alim), life (hayy). Note that life is the foundation that enables the others.
All four are considered necessary attributes of God in Ash'ari theology.
4
Clarify the meaning of 'before'
The word 'before' (قبل أن) does not mean time but logical priority. God's attributes are eternal, but some logically depend on others. For example, will logically presupposes knowledge.
This avoids the misconception of temporal sequence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
حيث أن العالم حادث فإن محدثه يجب أن يكون قادرا، إذ لو لم يكن قادرا لما أوجد العالم. وقبل أن يكون الله قادرا يكون مريدا، وقبل أن يكون الله مريدا يكون عالما، وقبل أن يكون قادرا مريدا عالما يكون حيا.
Since the world is originated, then its originator must be powerful (qadir), for if He were not powerful He would not have brought the world into existence. And before God is powerful, He is willing (murid), and before God is willing, He is knowing ('alim), and before He is powerful, willing, knowing, He is living (hayy).
Source pages p. 99

Commentary
This passage deduces the necessary attributes of God from the fact that the world is created (hadith). Since the world is originated, its originator must be powerful (qadir), because power is needed to create. Furthermore, a powerful agent must also be willing (murid) to choose to create, and willing requires knowledge ('alim) to know what is being created. Finally, power, will, and knowledge together presuppose life (hayy). Thus, the originator must be living, powerful, willing, and knowing. This is a standard sequence in kalam for establishing the attributes of God from the fact of creation.

Key Terms
حادث (hadith)
محدث (muhdith)
قادر (qadir)
مريد (murid)
عالم ('alim)
حي (hayy)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The attributes of God are created or come into existence over time.
Correction: The passage uses logical priority (قبل أن) not temporal priority. All attributes are eternal; the sequence is about conceptual dependence.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni's argument, which attribute is logically prior to power?
A) Life
B) Will
C) Knowledge
D) Speech

Key Takeaway
The world's contingency logically entails that its Creator is living, powerful, willing, and knowing, with these attributes being eternally present.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If every breath you take is contingent upon a Creator who is living, powerful, willing, and knowing, are you living as if you are self-sufficient? What specific area of your life today—your work, your relationships, your planning—are you acting as if you are in control rather than acknowledging your absolute dependence on Him? Commit one concrete action this week to remind yourself of that dependence.
?
The passage says God must be willing before He acts. When you reflect on your own intentions, do you align your will with what God knows to be good? Think of a decision you are facing right now. Are you acting without truly knowing the outcome or without seeking God's guidance? How will you invite God's knowledge into that decision today?

Proof of God's Attributes from the Temporality of the World
Juwayni proves God's power, will, knowledge, and life from the temporality of the world.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni proves that God is powerful, willing, knowing, and living based on the fact that the world is temporally originated.

Core Idea
The world's createdness proves God's power, will, knowledge, and life in a logical chain.

Imam al-Juwayni presents a concise argument that begins with a shared premise—the world is temporally originated—and then deduces four essential attributes of God. Each attribute follows necessarily from the previous one. This passage shows how sound theology builds upon rational inference from observable reality.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the translation. Let the argument's flow come through before interpreting.
Pay attention to the word 'وجب' (must be) indicating necessity.
2
Identify the logical sequence
The passage presents four attributes in a specific order: power, will, knowledge, life. Notice how each attribute is introduced with 'وقبل أن' (and before that).
The sequence is: qadir → murid → alim → hayy.
3
Understand why each attribute is necessary
For each step, ask: why must a powerful creator also be willing? Why must a willing creator also be knowing? Why do these three require life? The commentary explains the reasoning.
Each prior attribute logically presupposes the next.
4
Reflect on the misconception
A common misunderstanding is that God acquires these attributes over time. Reflect on why the sequence is logical, not temporal. God has all attributes eternally.
The word 'before' (قبل) here means logical priority, not time.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وتحدث في صفات الله تعالى . فقال : حيث أن العالم حادث فان محدثه يجب أن يكون قادرا . اذ لو لم يكن قادرا لما أوجد العالم . وقبل أن يكون الله قادرا يكون مريدا وقبل أن يكون الله مريدا يكون عالما. وقبل أن يكون قادرا مريدا عالما يكون حيا .
And he spoke about the attributes of Allah the Exalted. He said: Since the world is temporally originated, its originator must be powerful. For if he were not powerful, he would not have brought the world into existence. And before God is powerful, He must be willing; and before God is willing, He must be knowing; and before He is powerful, willing, knowing, He must be living.
Source pages p. 99

Commentary
This passage presents a sequential proof of four essential attributes of God. The argument begins with the premise that the world is temporally originated (hadith). From this, it deduces that its originator must be powerful (qadir), since producing a temporally originated entity requires power. Next, it argues that power presupposes will (irada), because a powerful agent acts according to choice. Will in turn presupposes knowledge (ilm), as one cannot will something without knowing it. Finally, power, will, and knowledge together presuppose life (hayat), since only a living agent can possess these attributes. The proof is linear: temporality → power → will → knowledge → life. Each step is necessary for the previous to be meaningful. This establishes that God is living, knowing, willing, and powerful.

Key Terms
حادث (hadith)
قادر (qadir)
مريد (murid)
عالم (alim)
حي (hayy)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The sequence suggests God acquires power first, then will, then knowledge, then life over time.
Correction: The sequence is logical, not temporal. God possesses all attributes eternally; the order is for understanding, not chronology.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni's argument, why must the Creator be powerful?
A) Because He is eternal
B) Because the world is temporally originated
C) Because He is living
D) Because He is knowing

Key Takeaway
From the temporality of the world, we rationally deduce that its Creator is living, knowing, willing, and powerful.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God's power, will, and knowledge are not separate from His essence, how does that challenge any assumptions you may have had about God being composed of parts? Do you ever unconsciously imagine God with human-like limitations?
?
When you look at the world around you—its order, complexity, and temporality—do you truly see it as a sign of a powerful, willing, knowing, and living Creator, or do you take it for granted? What would change in your daily awareness if you internalized this logical chain?
?
The proof concludes that God is living. Since He is alive and fully aware of every moment, how does that reality confront you in your private thoughts or actions that no one else sees? Will you remind yourself of this when you are alone?

Juwayni's View on Divine Attributes: Identity with Essence
Juwayni presents his stance that attributes are identical to essence, contrasting with al-Razi.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's position that God's attributes are identical to His essence and how it differs from the view of Fakhr al-Din al-Razi.

Core Idea
For Juwayni, God's attributes are not separate; they are His essence itself.

This lesson examines Juwayni's stance in the classical debate on divine attributes. While Muslims agree that God has attributes, they differ on whether these attributes are identical to or additional to the essence. Juwayni argues for identity, meaning that God's knowledge is simply His being knowing, not an added entity.

Guided Path
1
Read Juwayni's key claim
Read the passage carefully and note Juwayni's explicit statement that God's attributes are identical to His essence (ʿayn al-dhāt).
Juwayni uses the phrase 'la maʿnā li-l-ʿilmi illā kawnu l-ʿālimi ʿāliman' to illustrate.
2
Contrast with al-Razi
Identify how Juwayni contrasts his view with that of Fakhr al-Din al-Razi, who holds that attributes are additional to the essence (zāʾidah ʿalā al-dhāt) and necessary through another.
Note the terms 'wājibāt bi-l-ghayr' and 'mumkināt fī ḥaddi dhātihā'.
3
Reflect on the implication
Consider how Juwayni's position avoids multiplicity in the divine essence while still affirming real attributes.
This is a key point of difference from the Ashʿarī position held by al-Razi.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم قال : لا جدال فى هذا بين كل من انتمى الى الاسلام . ولكن الجدال في ما معناه : هل صفات الله هي عين ذاته . أم صفاته زائدة على الذات غير منفكة عنها ؟ ويرى أن صفات الله هي عين ذاته يقول : لا معنى للعلم الا كون العالم عالما » - « وكونه مريدا عين ارادته » خلافا لفخر الدين الرازى الذى جاء في كلامه : ان الصفات زائدات على الذات ، واجبات بالغير ممكنات في حد ذاتها .
Then he said: There is no dispute about this among all who belong to Islam. But the dispute is about its meaning: Are God's attributes identical to His essence, or are His attributes additional to the essence, inseparable from it? And he holds that God's attributes are identical to His essence. He says: 'There is no meaning to knowledge except that the knower is knowing' and 'His being willing is exactly His will', contrary to Fakhr al-Din al-Razi, who stated in his speech that attributes are additional to the essence, necessary through another, and possible in themselves.
Source pages p. 99

Commentary
This passage introduces the classical theological debate on the nature of God's attributes. Juwayni first states that there is consensus among Muslims on the fact that God possesses attributes (no dispute about that). The disagreement is over whether the attributes are identical to God's essence (ayn al-dhat) or are additional to it (za'idah 'ala al-dhat) while being inseparable. Juwayni explicitly adopts the first position: the attributes are identical to the essence. For example, God's knowledge is nothing other than His being knowing; His being willing is exactly His will. He contrasts this view with that of Fakhr al-Din al-Razi, who held that attributes are additional entities that are necessary through God (wajibat bi'l-ghayr) but possible in themselves. This sets the stage for the subsequent arguments and refutations.

Key Terms
عين الذات (ayn al-dhat)
زائدة على الذات (za'idah 'ala al-dhat)
غير منفكة (ghayr munfakkah)
واجبات بالغير (wajibat bi'l-ghayr)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Juwayni's view that attributes are identical to essence means attributes are not real.
Correction: Juwayni affirms real attributes; his point is that they are not separate entities added to the essence, but rather the essence itself qualified by them.

Checkpoint
What is Juwayni's position on the relationship between God's attributes and His essence?
A) Attributes are additional but inseparable
B) Attributes are identical to the essence
C) Attributes are separate from the essence
D) Attributes are created

Key Takeaway
Juwayni asserts that God's attributes are identical to His essence, avoiding multiplicity while preserving real attributes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God's attributes are identical to His essence, then every attribute reveals Him directly. Which attribute of God have you most neglected in your daily awareness, and what specific action will you take this week to make it a living reality in your heart?
?
Juwayni says knowledge is nothing other than the knower being knowing. This means God is not just knowing but is knowledge itself. When you sin, do you truly act as if God's knowledge is identical to His being—that He knows you completely and yet you hide? What will you confess to Him today that you have been hiding?
?
Al-Razi thought attributes are additional, making them possible in themselves. Could it be that you sometimes treat God's attributes as optional extras rather than His very essence—for example, thinking His mercy is something He 'has' rather than what He 'is'? How would your life change if you believed God is mercy itself?

Juwayni's Refutation of Ash'ari Argument for Additional Attributes
Juwayni presents and refutes the Ash'ari argument for additional attributes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Ash'ari argument for additional attributes and Juwayni's refutation using the human analogy.

Core Idea
Divine attributes are real but not separate entities; they are the essence itself.

The Ash'aris argued that if attributes were identical to the essence, then knowledge and power would be one. Juwayni counters with a powerful analogy from common human experience, showing that distinct attributes can coexist in a single essence without multiplicity. This lesson unpacks that debate.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the structure: Ash'ari claim, their proof, then Juwayni's response.
The language is classical kalam; take your time.
2
Identify the Ash'ari proof
The Ash'aris argue that if knowledge and power were identical to the essence, they would be identical to each other. Find that logical step in the text.
This is a reductio ad absurdum.
3
Analyze Juwayni's refutation
Juwayni uses the example of one person who knows, wills, and is powerful. Notice how he shows that these attributes appear independently yet are inseparable from the person.
The analogy is meant to show that distinct attributes do not require separate essences.
4
Reflect on the underlying principle
Juwayni's point is that attributes are real but not additional entities; they are the essence itself in relation to different actions.
This aligns with the Maturidi position.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والأشاعرة يقولون : ان الله صفات زائدة فهو عالم يعلم ، قادر بقدرة مريد بارادة . وأقوى دليل لهم قولهم : لو كان العلم نفس الذات ، والقدرة نفس الذات . لكان العلم نفس القدرة ، فكان المفهوم من العلم والقدرة واحدا . ونقول لهم : انا نرى الشخص الواحد يعلم ويريد ويقدر . ويظهر علمه للناس باستقلال ، وتظهر ارادته باستقلال ، وتظهر قدرته باستقلال . وعلمه وارادته وقدرته كامنة في شخصه غير منفكة عنه بحيث لو رآه شخص آخر مقابلة لم يحكم بالعدد بين الشخص وصفاته ، بل يحكم برؤية شخص واحد . وكلما تظهر أثر صفة حكم له بها دون ما قول بالفصل بين الذات والصفة
And the Ash'aris say: God has additional attributes, so He is knowing by knowledge, powerful by power, willing by will. And their strongest proof is their statement: If knowledge were the essence itself, and power were the essence itself, then knowledge would be power, and the concept of knowledge and power would be one. And we say to them: We see one person knows, wills, and is powerful. His knowledge appears to people independently, His will appears independently, and His power appears independently. Yet his knowledge, will, and power are latent in his person, inseparable from him, such that if another person sees him face to face, he does not judge multiplicity between the person and his attributes, but judges that he sees one person. Whenever an effect of an attribute appears, one judges him by it, without saying separation between essence and attribute.
Source pages p. 100

Commentary
This passage presents the Ash'ari argument for attributes being additional to the essence, followed by Juwayni's refutation. The Ash'aris argue that if knowledge and power were identical to the divine essence, then knowledge would be identical to power, merging their concepts – but we know they are distinct. Therefore, they must be additional. Juwayni responds by drawing an analogy from human experience. When we see a person who knows, wills, and has power, we observe that these attributes appear independently in their effects, yet we do not posit that the person has multiple separate parts or that the essence is separate from the attributes. Rather, we regard the person as a single entity who is knowing, willing, and powerful. The attributes are latent in the person and inseparable. Similarly for God, we affirm distinct attributes without dividing the essence. The key is that the attributes are not separate entities, but the essence itself has these properties, even though conceptually distinct.

Key Terms
علم (ilm)
قدرة (qudra)
إرادة (irada)
زيادة الصفات (ziyadat al-sifat)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Juwayni denies the reality of divine attributes.
Correction: Juwayni affirms real attributes but argues they are not separate entities from the essence; they are the essence itself in different respects.

Checkpoint
What is the Ash'ari proof that Juwayni refutes?
A) Attributes are created
B) If attributes are identical to essence, then knowledge would be power
C) God cannot have attributes
D) Attributes are separable from essence

Key Takeaway
Juwayni refutes the Ash'ari argument for additional attributes by showing that distinct attributes can inhere in a single essence without multiplicity, as seen in human experience.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think of God's power and knowledge, do you ever unconsciously treat them as separate 'powers' that exist alongside His being? How might that affect your feeling of closeness to Him, knowing that these attributes are not separate from His essence but are Him?
?
The Ash'ari argument looked logical, but Juwayni showed that distinct concepts do not require separate existence. Where in your own understanding of God might you be making similar logical leaps—areas where you assume separation where there is unity?
?
If God's attributes are not additional but inherent, does that change the way you call upon Him? For example, when you ask for His mercy, do you see mercy as an extra thing He gives, or as an expression of who He essentially is? How would your trust shift if you truly internalized that His mercy is not a separate attribute but His very self acting?
