The Mu'tazili View on Attributes of Essence
Juwayni presents the Mu'tazili position on attributes and their argument against separate attributes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Mu'tazili position that God's attributes are identical with His essence, and their argument that separate attributes would imply multiple eternal beings.

Core Idea
For the Mu'tazila, attributes are the essence itself, not separate entities.

Imam Juwayni presents the Mu'tazili perspective on divine attributes, explaining their insistence that attributes are inherent in God's essence and not additional. This careful reasoning aims to preserve the oneness of God by avoiding any implication of multiple eternal beings. The passage sets the stage for understanding a key theological debate in Islamic thought.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the structure: first the Mu'tazili claim, then their reasoning.
Pay attention to the key terms like 'fi al-dhat' and 'qadim'.
2
Identify the core claim
The Mu'tazila say attributes are 'fi al-dhat' – within the essence. What does this mean in contrast to the view that attributes are additional?
3
Follow the argument step-by-step
Trace the logical steps: God is eternal; attribute is eternal; if separate, two eternals; therefore, attributes must be one with essence.
Consider why this reasoning is important for tawhid.
4
Reflect on implications
Consider how this view upholds tawhid but also shapes the understanding of God's attributes. Juwayni notes its alignment with his own stance.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والمعتزلة - يرحمهم الله يقولون ان صفات الله فى ذات الله ، - وهو هو بذاته وصفاته من قبل وجود العالم ومن بعده والى ما لا نهاية . ولم يسرحوا بصفات زائدة على ذات الله . لأن الله قديم ، والصفة : قديمة . فيلزم فى العالم قديمان : الله والصفة اذا قيل بانفصالها . أما اذا قيل بالصفة فى الذات وهى والذات شيء واحد فانه يلزم في العالم : قديم واحد موصوف بكل كمال وهو ما يجب القول به
And the Mu'tazila – may God have mercy on them – say that God's attributes are in God's essence, and He is the same in His essence and attributes before the existence of the world and after it and unto infinity. And they did not affirm attributes additional to God's essence, because God is eternal, and the attribute is eternal, so there would follow in the world two eternals: God and the attribute, if one says they are separate. But if one says the attribute is in the essence and it and the essence are one thing, then there follows in the world one eternal described with every perfection, and that is what must be said.
Source pages p. 100

Commentary
This passage presents the Mu'tazili position on the attributes. The Mu'tazila affirm that God's attributes are inherent in His essence (fi dhat Allah) and not additional entities. They argue that if attributes were separate from the essence (or considered as distinct realities), then there would be multiple eternal beings (qadimayn), which would compromise tawhid. Since God is eternal, and a separate attribute would also be eternal, that would necessitate two eternals. Therefore, they hold that the attributes are identical to the essence – the essence itself is described by every perfection, and there is only one eternal being. Juwayni presents this view without explicit rejection, though he himself holds a similar position. Note that Juwayni's own view (attributes identical to essence) aligns with the Mu'tazili view presented here, but he differs in that he still affirms real attributes without separation.

Key Terms
معتزلة (Mu'tazila)
قديم (qadim)
قديمان (qadiman)
في الذات (fi al-dhat)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Mu'tazila deny that God has any attributes at all.
Correction: They affirm attributes but as identical with the essence, not as separate entities. They affirm that God is knowing, powerful, etc., but without multiplicity of eternal beings.

Checkpoint
Why do the Mu'tazila reject additional attributes according to this passage?
A) Because attributes are created
B) Because it would imply two eternal beings
C) Because God cannot have attributes
D) Because attributes are the essence itself

Key Takeaway
The Mu'tazila maintained that God's attributes are not additional to His essence, for that would imply two eternal beings; rather, attributes are identical with the essence, ensuring the oneness of the Eternal.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think of God's attributes, do you sometimes mentally separate them from His essence, treating knowledge as an 'object' He possesses? This lesson shows the danger of inadvertently multiplying the Eternal. Examine your own heart: is there any association in your mind where you treat God's attributes as though they are alongside Him rather than being Him? Will you repent from such subtle shirk and affirm that He is knowing by His essence?
?
The Mu'tazila were so protective of tawhid they argued attributes are identical with the essence. But does your theological correctness keep you from actually experiencing His attributes? Have you reduced His knowledge to a concept, while forgetting that He knows you intimately right now? Today, let one of His attributes—like His seeing or His power—break into your daily actions. Will you live this day knowing He sees every move?
?
This passage reveals the Mu'tazila's desire to avoid two eternals. But what about you? What 'eternal' things do you cling to besides God—your wealth, reputation, relationships—as though they will last? These are the 'two eternals' of your heart. Will you, right now, identify one such attachment and consciously declare that only God is eternal, and submit to the reality that everything else will perish?

Juwayni's Proof for the Eternity of Divine Speech
Juwayni uses an analogy to prove eternal divine speech

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's argument that God's speech is eternal by analogy with human inner speech.

Core Idea
God's speech is as ancient as His essence, not created at revelation.

This lesson explores a concise but powerful analogy from Imam al-Juwayni. He uses a common human experience—planning a conversation—to demonstrate that meaningful speech exists in the soul before it is uttered. This analogy serves as a key proof in the Ash'ari school that divine speech is an eternal attribute of God.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Notice the analogy Juwayni presents.
2
Understand the analogy
Juwayni says a person who resolves to speak with a companion after a month already has the meanings in his soul. Why does he compare this to God's speech?
Think about what 'meanings in the soul' means for God.
3
Connect to the doctrine of divine speech
This analogy shows that meaningful speech pre-exists in the speaker's essence. For God, this means His speech is eternal, not brought into being at revelation.
Distinguish between eternal inner speech (kalam nafsi) and temporal uttered speech.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأكد الجويني على مذهبه في الصفات وهو يتحدث في كلام الله تعالى فقال : ( إن من يعزم على مفاوضة صاحب له بعد شهر ، فالمعاني التي سيوردها عند جريان الجواز يجدها بأعيانها قائمة في نفسه
And Juwayni emphasized his doctrine on attributes while speaking about the speech of Allah the Exalted. He said: 'Indeed, whoever resolves to converse with his companion after a month will find the very meanings that he will produce at the time of discourse already present in his soul.'
Source pages p. 100

Commentary
This passage presents Juwayni's argument for the pre-existent and eternal nature of God's speech (kalam). He uses an analogy: a person who determines to have a conversation with a friend a month from now already has in his mind the very meanings he intends to express. These meanings exist in his soul (nafs) before they are uttered. Similarly, God's speech is eternal and subsisting in His essence; it does not come into being at the moment of revelation. This is a standard Ash'ari-Juwayni argument to support that the Quran is uncreated and that God's attribute of speech is eternal, without implying that the sounds and letters are eternal. The passage is brief but encapsulates a key proof for the eternal nature of divine speech.

Key Terms
كلام الله
مفاوضة
معاني
نفس

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Quran (Arabic text) is eternal in its letters and sounds.
Correction: Juwayni does not claim that the actual Arabic letters are eternal; rather, the meaningful speech (kalam nafsi) is eternal. The uttered words are created expressions of the eternal attribute.

Checkpoint
What does Juwayni's analogy of a person's intention to speak after a month illustrate?
A) That speech is created at the moment of utterance
B) That meanings can exist in the soul before utterance
C) That God's speech is temporal
D) That human speech is eternal

Key Takeaway
God's speech is eternal because the meanings reside in His essence, just as intended meanings exist in the soul before utterance.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite the Quran, do you experience it as if God is speaking directly to you now, or do you treat it as a historical text? How would viewing it as the eternal speech of God—always present in His essence—change your attentiveness?
?
Think of a time you carefully prepared what you would say to someone. The words you eventually spoke were just an expression of meanings already in your mind. Does Juwayni's analogy help you trust that God's words in the Quran are perfectly intended and wise, not accidental? How does that affect your submission to them?
?
If God's speech is an eternal attribute of His being, then the Quran is not just a book but part of who He is. In what practical ways can you align your life daily with the reality that you are responding to God's eternal word, not just human prose?

Divine Attributes

The Ash'ari View: Attributes as Additional Yet Inseparable
Juwayni outlines the Ash'ari belief in additional attributes and their logical proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Ash'ari position that God's attributes are additional to His essence yet inseparable, and their logical proof against the identity view.

Core Idea
Ash'aris argue attributes are real and distinct from essence, yet not separate entities.

Juwayni now turns to the dominant Sunni position: the Ash'ari school teaches that God has attributes (knowledge, power, will) that are real and additional to His essence, but not external or separable. The strongest proof is a logical reductio: if attributes were identical to essence, they would collapse into each other. The passage also defends against an objection based on the unity of the person.

Guided Path
1
Read the Ash'ari passage
Read the Arabic passage describing the Ash'ari position and their strongest proof. Pay attention to the logical dilemma.
The proof aims to show that identifying attributes with essence leads to absurdity.
2
Analyze the proof structure
The proof says: if knowledge and power are both identical to essence, then they are identical to each other. But knowledge and power are distinct concepts. Therefore, they cannot be identical to essence.
This is a modus tollens argument.
3
Examine the reply to the objection
The Ash'ari then answers an objection: doesn't a single person have multiple attributes without separation? The reply is that attributes are latent in the person, not separate entities, and a viewer sees one person. Similarly, God's attributes are in His essence.
This rebuts the charge of multiplicity.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فى هذا بين كل من انتمى الى الاسلام . ولكن الجدال في ما معناه : هل صفات الله هي عين ذاته . أم صفاته زائدة على الذات غير منفكة عنها ؟ ويرى أن صفات الله هى عين ذاته يقول : لا معنى للعلم الا كون العالم عالما » - « وكونه مريدا عين ارادته » خلافا لفخر الدين الرازى الذى جاء في كلامه : أن الصفات زائدات على الذات ، واجبات بالغير ممكنات في حد ذاتها . والأشاعرة يقولون : ان الله صفات زائدة فهو عالم يعلم ، قادر بقدرة مريد بارادة . وأقوى دليل لهم قولهم : لو كان العلم نفس الذات ، والقدرة نفس الذات . لكان العلم نفس القدرة ، فكان المفهوم من العلم والقدرة واحدا . ونقول لهم : انا نرى الشخص الواحد يعلم ويريد ويقدر . ويظهر علمه للناس باستقلال ، وتظهر ارادته باستقلال ، وتظهر قدرته باستقلال . وعلمه وارادته وقدرته كامنة في شخصه غير منفكة عنه بحيث لو رآه شخص آخر مقابلة لم يحكم بالعدد بين الشخص وصفاته ، بل يحكم برؤية شخص واحد . وكلما تظهر أثر صفة حكم له بها دون ما قول بالفصل بين الذات والصفة . والمعتزلة - يرحمهم الله يقولون ان صفات الله فى ذات الله ، - وهو هو بذاته وصفاته من قبل وجود العالم ومن بعده والى ما لا نهاية . ولم يسرحوا بصفات زائدة على ذات الله . لأن الله قديم ، والصفة : قديمة . فيلزم فى العالم قديمان : الله والصفة اذا قيل بانفصالها . أما اذا قيل بالصفة فى الذات وهى والذات شيء واحد فانه يلزم في العالم : قديم واحد موصوف بكل كمال وهو ما يجب القول به
In this [work] is an explanation for everyone who belongs to Islam. But the debate is about its meaning: Are God's attributes identical to His essence? Or are His attributes additional to the essence, inseparable from it? And he holds that God's attributes are identical to His essence, saying: 'Knowledge has no meaning except that the knower is knowing' and 'His being willing is identical to His will'—contrary to Fakhr al-Din al-Razi, in whose speech it came that attributes are additional to the essence, necessary through another, possible in themselves. And the Ash'aris say: God has additional attributes, so He is knowing through knowledge, powerful through power, willing through will. And their strongest proof is their statement: If knowledge were the essence itself, and power were the essence itself, then knowledge would be power itself, and the concept of knowledge and power would be one. And we say to them: We see one person knows, wills, and has power. His knowledge appears to people independently, his will appears independently, and his power appears independently. His knowledge, will, and power are latent in his person, inseparable from him, such that if another person saw him face to face, he would not judge number between the person and his attributes, but rather judges seeing one person. And whenever an effect of an attribute appears, he is judged by it, without any statement of separation between essence and attribute. And the Mu'tazila—may God have mercy on them—say that God's attributes are in God's essence—and He is the same with His essence and attributes before the existence of the world, after it, and unto infinity. And they did not permit attributes additional to God's essence. Because God is eternal, and the attribute is eternal. So two eternals would follow in the world: God and the attribute, if it is said they are separate. But if it is said that the attribute is in the essence and they are one thing, then one eternal described with every perfection follows in the world, which is what must be said.
Source pages p. 100

Commentary
This passage presents the core debate in Islamic theology regarding God's attributes (ṣifāt). Juwayni first frames the question: Are God's attributes identical to His essence (dhāt) or are they additional (zāʾida) yet inseparable? He notes that some hold the former view, arguing that 'knowledge' is just the state of being knowing, and will is identical to volition. This contrasts with Fakhr al-Din al-Rāzī, who held attributes are additional, necessary through God, but possible in themselves. Juwayni then outlines the Ashʿarī position: God has real attributes that are additional to His essence—He is knowing through knowledge, powerful through power, willing through will. Their strongest proof is a logical dilemma: if knowledge were identical to essence and power also identical to essence, then knowledge would be identical to power, collapsing the distinction between them—something absurd because we distinguish knowledge from power. The Ashʿarī then address an objection: we observe one person possessing distinct attributes (knowledge, will, power) that manifest independently, yet they are not separate from him; they are latent in his person. A viewer sees one person, not many. The same applies to God. Next, Juwayni presents the Muʿtazilī position (with the phrase 'may God have mercy on them' indicating respect despite disagreement). They hold that God's attributes are within His essence—He is the same before and after creation. They reject additional attributes because both God and attribute would be eternal, implying multiple eternals if the attribute is separate. Instead, they affirm one eternal described with all perfections. This summary captures the key soteriological and metaphysical stances of the two schools.

Key Terms
الأشاعرة
الصفات الزائدة
العلم
القدرة
الإرادة
الذات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Ash'aris believe attributes are separate entities from God's essence.
Correction: Ash'aris hold attributes are additional in meaning (ma'na) but inseparable from essence; they are not independent beings.

Checkpoint
What is the strongest Ash'ari proof against the identity view?
A) If knowledge and power were both identical to essence, they would be identical to each other.
B) The Quran explicitly says attributes are additional.
C) Reason proves multiple eternals are impossible.
D) The Mu'tazila have no evidence.

Key Takeaway
Ash'aris affirm real, additional attributes through a logical proof that preserves God's unity and attribute distinctions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you affirm that God is 'Knowing, Powerful, Willing,' do you ever find yourself treating these as separate 'pieces' of God in your mind? How can you train your heart to see them as one with His essence, as the Ashʿarīs teach? This week, when you make dhikr, pause on one attribute and feel that it is not separate from God Himself.
?
The passage compares God's attributes to a person whose knowledge, will, and power are 'latent' and inseparable. In what area of your life do you habitually separate God's attributes from His essence—for example, trusting His power but forgetting His will? What concrete practice can you adopt to unify your perception of Him?
?
If a friend argued that believing in multiple attributes weakens tawḥīd, would you be able to explain the Ashʿarī position using the logical proof from this lesson? More importantly, does your own heart feel troubled by the idea of 'additional' attributes? Bring this fear to your prayer and ask God to grant you a sound understanding of His unity without stripping Him of His attributes.

Divine Attributes
intermediate
7 min
The Mu'tazila Position: Attributes Are Identical to Essence
Juwayni presents the Mu'tazila stance that attributes are identical to essence to avoid multiple eternals.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Mu'tazila view that God's attributes are identical to His essence, and their argument that separate attributes would imply multiple eternal beings.

Core Idea
Mu'tazila: God is one eternal; adding attributes creates multiple eternals.

Juwayni then presents the Mu'tazila position with a respectful prayer, showing their core concern: maintaining absolute divine unity. They argue that if attributes were additional and separate, they would be co-eternal with God, leading to polytheism. Instead, attributes are within the essence, making God one eternal being described by all perfections.

Guided Path
1
Read the Mu'tazila passage
Read the Arabic passage describing the Mu'tazila view and their argument against additional attributes.
Notice the phrase 'may God have mercy on them' indicating respect despite disagreement.
2
Understand the logical problem
The Mu'tazila argue that if attributes are separate from essence, then both God and the attribute are eternal, implying two eternals. This violates tawhid.
Their solution: attributes are within the essence, so only one eternal exists.
3
Contrast with Ash'ari view
Compare this to the Ash'ari position: both reject separation, but Ash'aris say attributes are additional in meaning yet inseparable; Mu'tazila say they are identical.
This is a key point of divergence in kalam.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فى هذا بين كل من انتمى الى الاسلام . ولكن الجدال في ما معناه : هل صفات الله هي عين ذاته . أم صفاته زائدة على الذات غير منفكة عنها ؟ ويرى أن صفات الله هى عين ذاته يقول : لا معنى للعلم الا كون العالم عالما » - « وكونه مريدا عين ارادته » خلافا لفخر الدين الرازى الذى جاء في كلامه : أن الصفات زائدات على الذات ، واجبات بالغير ممكنات في حد ذاتها . والأشاعرة يقولون : ان الله صفات زائدة فهو عالم يعلم ، قادر بقدرة مريد بارادة . وأقوى دليل لهم قولهم : لو كان العلم نفس الذات ، والقدرة نفس الذات . لكان العلم نفس القدرة ، فكان المفهوم من العلم والقدرة واحدا . ونقول لهم : انا نرى الشخص الواحد يعلم ويريد ويقدر . ويظهر علمه للناس باستقلال ، وتظهر ارادته باستقلال ، وتظهر قدرته باستقلال . وعلمه وارادته وقدرته كامنة في شخصه غير منفكة عنه بحيث لو رآه شخص آخر مقابلة لم يحكم بالعدد بين الشخص وصفاته ، بل يحكم برؤية شخص واحد . وكلما تظهر أثر صفة حكم له بها دون ما قول بالفصل بين الذات والصفة . والمعتزلة - يرحمهم الله يقولون ان صفات الله فى ذات الله ، - وهو هو بذاته وصفاته من قبل وجود العالم ومن بعده والى ما لا نهاية . ولم يسرحوا بصفات زائدة على ذات الله . لأن الله قديم ، والصفة : قديمة . فيلزم فى العالم قديمان : الله والصفة اذا قيل بانفصالها . أما اذا قيل بالصفة فى الذات وهى والذات شيء واحد فانه يلزم في العالم : قديم واحد موصوف بكل كمال وهو ما يجب القول به
In this [work] is an explanation for everyone who belongs to Islam. But the debate is about its meaning: Are God's attributes identical to His essence? Or are His attributes additional to the essence, inseparable from it? And he holds that God's attributes are identical to His essence, saying: 'Knowledge has no meaning except that the knower is knowing' and 'His being willing is identical to His will'—contrary to Fakhr al-Din al-Razi, in whose speech it came that attributes are additional to the essence, necessary through another, possible in themselves. And the Ash'aris say: God has additional attributes, so He is knowing through knowledge, powerful through power, willing through will. And their strongest proof is their statement: If knowledge were the essence itself, and power were the essence itself, then knowledge would be power itself, and the concept of knowledge and power would be one. And we say to them: We see one person knows, wills, and has power. His knowledge appears to people independently, his will appears independently, and his power appears independently. His knowledge, will, and power are latent in his person, inseparable from him, such that if another person saw him face to face, he would not judge number between the person and his attributes, but rather judges seeing one person. And whenever an effect of an attribute appears, he is judged by it, without any statement of separation between essence and attribute. And the Mu'tazila—may God have mercy on them—say that God's attributes are in God's essence—and He is the same with His essence and attributes before the existence of the world, after it, and unto infinity. And they did not permit attributes additional to God's essence. Because God is eternal, and the attribute is eternal. So two eternals would follow in the world: God and the attribute, if it is said they are separate. But if it is said that the attribute is in the essence and they are one thing, then one eternal described with every perfection follows in the world, which is what must be said.
Source pages p. 100

Commentary
This passage presents the core debate in Islamic theology regarding God's attributes (ṣifāt). Juwayni first frames the question: Are God's attributes identical to His essence (dhāt) or are they additional (zāʾida) yet inseparable? He notes that some hold the former view, arguing that 'knowledge' is just the state of being knowing, and will is identical to volition. This contrasts with Fakhr al-Din al-Rāzī, who held attributes are additional, necessary through God, but possible in themselves. Juwayni then outlines the Ashʿarī position: God has real attributes that are additional to His essence—He is knowing through knowledge, powerful through power, willing through will. Their strongest proof is a logical dilemma: if knowledge were identical to essence and power also identical to essence, then knowledge would be identical to power, collapsing the distinction between them—something absurd because we distinguish knowledge from power. The Ashʿarī then address an objection: we observe one person possessing distinct attributes (knowledge, will, power) that manifest independently, yet they are not separate from him; they are latent in his person. A viewer sees one person, not many. The same applies to God. Next, Juwayni presents the Muʿtazilī position (with the phrase 'may God have mercy on them' indicating respect despite disagreement). They hold that God's attributes are within His essence—He is the same before and after creation. They reject additional attributes because both God and attribute would be eternal, implying multiple eternals if the attribute is separate. Instead, they affirm one eternal described with all perfections. This summary captures the key soteriological and metaphysical stances of the two schools.

Key Terms
المعتزلة
القديم
الحدوث
الذات
الصفات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Mu'tazila deny that God has attributes entirely.
Correction: They affirm attributes but consider them identical to essence, not separate additions.

Checkpoint
Why do the Mu'tazila reject attributes additional to essence?
A) Because it would imply multiple eternal beings.
B) Because the Quran says so.
C) Because Ash'aris are wrong.
D) Because attributes are not real.

Key Takeaway
Mu'tazila maintain divine unity by identifying attributes with essence, avoiding the problem of multiple eternals.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you contemplate God's oneness, do you ever unconsciously imagine separate 'parts' of God (like His power as something distinct from His mercy)? How does the Mu'tazila's fear of multiple eternals expose a subtle shirk in your own heart? Commit this week to reciting 'Qul Huwa Allahu Ahad' while consciously affirming that Allah's attributes are not separate from Him.
?
This lesson shows sincere scholars disagreeing on a deep theological point. When you encounter a Muslim who holds a different view on a matter of aqeedah, do you seek to understand their reasoning, or do you instantly label them as misguided? Write down one area where you have been dismissive and ask Allah to grant you humility to learn.
?
The Mu'tazila were willing to risk logical difficulties to protect tawhid. Are you as zealous about God's oneness in your daily life—like in your trust in Him alone, or in avoiding showing off (riya)? Identify one practical situation where you can assert tawhid over your ego this week.

The Eternal Speech of God: Juwayni's Analogy
Juwayni defends the eternality of God's speech using the analogy of human planning.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni uses the analogy of a person planning speech to demonstrate that God's attribute of speech is eternal and subsisting in His essence.

Core Idea
God's speech is like a plan in the mind—eternal and not created.

This passage presents one of the most intuitive analogies in Ash'ari theology. Juwayni invites us to reflect on our own experience of planning what to say. The meanings we intend are present in our minds before we utter a single word. He then applies this to God, arguing that His speech—the meanings He will convey to creation—subsists eternally in His essence, just as His power subsists without beginning.

Guided Path
1
Read the Juwayni passage
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the translation. Notice the structure: a human analogy followed by its divine application.
The passage is dense; focus on the flow from human to divine.
2
Identify the human analogy
Juwayni describes a person who resolves to speak to a friend after a month. The meanings of that future speech are already present in his soul. Find this and consider why it matters.
The word 'نفس' (nafs) here means soul or self, not breath.
3
Apply the analogy to God
Juwayni extends the analogy to God: just as the human planner has meanings in his soul, God in His pre-eternity has the meanings of what He will say to His servants. Note how Juwayni emphasises God's freedom from forgetfulness.
This step is the core of the argument.
4
Understand the conclusion
Juwayni concludes that God's speech subsisting in Himself is like His eternal power—neither begins nor ceases. Reflect on how this defends the Ash'ari view of an eternal Qur'an.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأكد الجويني على مذهبه في الصفات وهو يتحدث في كلام الله تعالى فقال : ( إن من يعزم على مفاوضة صاحب له بعد شهر ، فالمعاني التي سيوردها عند جريان الجواز يجدها بأعيانها قائمة في نفسه ثم اذا حان الوقت أداها فأنهاها . والعالم بأنه سيكلم فلانا لا تخلو فسه عن وجود ثبوت ذلك الكلام على تقدير وجوده في العبارات من حين المفاوضة تبلغ تلك المعانى . والرب فى أزله كان عالما بأنه يتعبد عبادة اذا وجدوا وهو العالم المقدس عن أن يسهو أو يهفو فلا يخلى وجوده الأزلى عن معنى ما سيصل الى العباد اذا وجدوا : وسبيل ذلك الكلام القائم بنفسه . كسبيل قدرته القديمة ولم تزل » • يريد أن يقول : كما أن الانسان لو أراد أن يحدث صاحبا له بعد شهر فى أمر ما ، يرتب كلاما في نفسه ليقوله لصاحبه بعد شهر . فاذا جاء الشهر نطق بالكلام الذى كان قد رتبه فى نفسه ذلك . وهذا الانسان قد أراد وقد أوجد معانى فى نفسه ثم نطق بهذه المعاني . ومع أنه وصفاته شيء واحد من قبل . كذلك الله وجل - والله المثل الأعلى - صفاته من قبل وما تزال الكلام القائم بنفسه كسبيل قدرته القديمة . ولم تزل . ولما تحدث في « كلام الله » لم يمش على تعبيره « ولم يزل » ذلك أنه أثبت الله كلاما لم ينفصل عنه في قوله « كلام الله الأزلى لا يفارق الذات ولا يزايلها » وإن كان يقصد بهذه العبارة « النظم الدال على المعنى المتحقق لفظا ومعنى فى علم الله أزلا ( كترتيب الانسان كلام في نفسه ليؤديه الى صاحبه بعد شهر فلا اعتراض عليه . لأنه يكون كارادته و قدرته من قبل أن يحدث الله الذي أراده . فاذا أحدث الذي أراده فان الذى حدث يكون حادثا • كارادته خلق العالم أزلا فانه لما خلق العالم صار العالم منفصلا بالارادة ومخلوقا حادثا . كذلك لو تكلم فقال كن » مثلا . فان « كن » تعتبر فى المعنى كخلق العالم بعد أن كان فكرة . وكما تشاهد قدرة الله لا تزال ، كذلك كلامه لا يزال أو بتعبير الجويني نفسه « ولم يزل » لقد ظهرت قدرات كثيرة الله على طول الزمان .
And Juwayni emphasized his school regarding attributes while speaking about the speech of God Most High, saying: 'Indeed, whoever resolves to converse with a companion after a month, the meanings he will deliver at the time of meeting he finds in their very selves subsisting in his soul; then when the time comes, he performs them and completes them. And the one who knows that he will speak to so-and-so is not devoid in his soul of the existence of the affirmation of that speech, given its existence in expressions from the time of the conversation that delivers those meanings. And the Lord in His pre-eternity was knowing that He would worship servants when they exist, and He is the Knower sanctified from forgetfulness or oversight, so His pre-eternal existence is not devoid of the meaning of what will reach the servants when they exist. And the way of that speech subsisting in itself is like the way of His eternal power, which has not ceased.' He intends to say: Just as a person, if he wants to speak to a companion after a month concerning a matter, arranges speech in himself to say to his companion after a month, so when the month comes, he utters the speech that he had arranged in himself. And that person has willed and has originated meanings in himself, then uttered those meanings, while he and his attributes are one thing from before. Likewise God Almighty—and God has the highest example—His attributes from before and His speech subsisting in itself continues, like the way of His eternal power, which has not ceased. And when he spoke about 'the speech of God,' he did not proceed with his expression 'has not ceased,' that he affirmed that God has speech that does not separate from Him in his statement 'the eternal speech of God does not leave the essence nor depart from it.' And if he meant by this expression 'the order indicating the meaning realized verbally and conceptually in God's knowledge eternally,' as a person arranges speech in himself to deliver to his companion after a month, then there is no objection to it. Because it is like His will and His power from before He brings about what He willed. So when He brings about what He willed, then what occurs is originated. Like His will creating the world eternally, for when He created the world, the world became separate by the will and a created originated thing. Likewise if He spoke and said 'Be' for example, then 'Be' is considered in meaning like the creation of the world after it was an idea. And just as you see God's power does not cease, likewise His speech does not cease; or in Juwayni's own expression, 'has not ceased.' Many powers of God have appeared over the length of time.
Source pages p. 100

Commentary
This passage is a theological argument defending the Ashʿarī view that God's attribute of speech (kalām Allāh) is eternal and subsisting in His essence. Juwayni begins with an analogy: a person who plans to converse with a friend after a month already has the meanings of that speech present in his mind, and when the time comes, he delivers them. Similarly, the knower is not devoid of that intended speech in his soul. God, in His pre-eternity, knew He would address His servants when they exist, and He is free from forgetfulness, so His eternal existence includes the meaning of what will reach them. Juwayni then explicitly states that this speech subsisting in itself (al-kalām al-qāʾim bi-nafsih) is like His eternal power (al-qudra al-qadīma) which has never ceased (lam tazal). He rephrases the analogy: a human arranges speech in himself and then utters it; the human and his attributes are one. Likewise, God's attributes are eternal, and His speech subsists in His essence. Juwayni then addresses a possible objection to the phrase 'has not ceased' (lam yazal). He clarifies that he affirms a speech that never separates from the essence, as in his statement 'the eternal speech of God does not leave the essence nor depart from it.' He interprets this as referring to the ordered expression (al-naẓm) indicating the meaning realized linguistically and conceptually in God's knowledge eternally. The example of a person arranging speech in himself is given to show that there is no objection to this; it is like God's will and power preceding creation. When God creates, the created thing is originated (ḥādith). Similarly, if God says 'Be' (kun), it is like creation after it was an idea. God's power and speech never cease, as evidenced by the continuous appearance of His powers throughout time.

Key Terms
كلام الله
أزلي
قائم بالذات
لم يزل
قدرة
نفس

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That believing God's speech is eternal implies it is identical to created sounds or letters.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies that God's speech is a meaning subsisting in His essence, not a temporal event of sound or script. The human analogy shows that speech as a mental meaning precedes utterance, just as God's speech is eternal.

Checkpoint
What analogy does Juwayni use to explain the eternality of God's speech?
A) A person planning to speak to a friend after a month.
B) The creation of the world from nothing.
C) The comparison of God's speech to human writing.
D) The impossibility of multiple eternals.

Key Takeaway
God's speech is an eternal attribute subsisting in His essence, not a created event; it is like a plan in the mind that never ceases.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Juwayni argues that God's speech is not a created event but an eternal attribute. When you recite the Qur'an, do you truly feel you are uttering words that have always existed in God's essence? How does this truth affect your attention and reverence during recitation? What one change can you make today to approach the Qur'an with the awe it deserves?
?
If speech as a meaning in the soul can be present before utterance, then God's speech is not dependent on creation. How does this challenge any assumption you may have that God's actions are reactive to human affairs? In what area of your life do you need to trust that God's word to you—through revelation—was already perfectly planned before you existed?
?
The analogy compares human planning to God's eternal speech. But human plans often change or fail. God's speech never changes. When you face uncertainty or trial, do you seek comfort in God's unchanging words, or do you rely on your own fluctuating thoughts? What step can you take this week to anchor your heart more firmly in His eternal speech?

Analogy of Human Internal Speech to God's Eternal Speech
Juwayni explains the eternity of God's speech using an analogy with human internal speech.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's analogy that God's eternal speech (al-kalam al-qa'im bi nafsihi) is like human internal speech that exists before utterance, emphasizing its pre-existence and eternity.

Core Idea
God's speech is eternal in His essence, just as our internal thoughts exist before we speak.

Juwayni uses a relatable human experience—planning what to say to a friend—to explain a profound theological truth: God's speech is not created but eternal. This passage anchors the doctrine of divine speech as a subsisting attribute (sifah qa'imah) in God's essence, linking it to His eternal power and knowledge.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Don't worry about detailed analysis yet; let the author's flow come through.
Notice the repetition of 'لم تزل' (never ceases) which emphasizes eternity.
2
Identify the analogy
Juwayni compares God's eternal speech to human internal speech arranged before speaking. Find the sentence beginning with 'كما أن الانسان' and understand the comparison.
The analogy uses a human action (planning speech) to illustrate a divine attribute.
3
Trace the argument
Follow Juwayni's reasoning: from God's eternal knowledge to the eternity of speech and power as attributes. Notice how he links speech to power as both never ceasing.
The phrase 'كسبيل قدرته القديمة' (like the way of His eternal power) is key.
4
Understand the conclusion
The final lines affirm that God's speech never ceases, just like His power. Reflect on how this analogy resolves potential confusion about the temporal nature of revelation.
Juwayni concludes that God's attributes are eternal and never cease.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والرب فى أزله كان عالما بأنه يتعبد عباده اذا وجدوا وهو العالم المقدس عن أن يسهو أو يهفو فلا يخلو وجوده الأزلى عن معنى ما سيصل الى العباد اذا وجدوا : وسبيل ذلك الكلام القائم بنفسه كسبيل قدرته القديمة ولم تزل. يريد أن يقول : كما أن الانسان لو أراد أن يحدث صاحبا له بعد شهر فى أمر ما ، يرتب كلاما في نفسه ليقوله لصاحبه بعد شهر . فاذا جاء الشهر نطق بالكلام الذى كان قد رتبه فى نفسه ذلك. وهذا الانسان قد أراد وقد أوجد معانى فى نفسه ثم نطق بهذه المعاني . ومع هو وصفاته شيء واحد من قبل . كذلك الله وجل - والله المثل الأعلى - صفاته من قبل وما تزال الكلام القائم بنفسه كسبيل قدرته القديمة . ولم تزل.
And the Lord in His pre-eternity was knowing that He will worship servants when they exist, and He is the Holy One, far above forgetfulness or error, so His pre-eternal existence is not devoid of the meaning of what will reach the servants when they exist. And the way of that speech subsisting in His essence is like the way of His eternal power, and it never ceases. He means to say: Just as if a person wants to speak to his companion after a month about a matter, he arranges speech in himself to say to his companion after a month, then when the month comes, he utters the speech that he had arranged in himself. And this person has willed and has brought into existence meanings in himself, then he utters those meanings. And he, with his attributes, is one thing before. Likewise, God Almighty – and to God belongs the highest similitude – His attributes are from before and His speech subsisting in His essence never ceases, like the way of His eternal power, and it never ceases.
Source pages p. 101

Commentary
This passage presents an analogy to explain the eternity of God's speech (kalam). Juwayni begins by stating that God, in His pre-eternity, knew that He would worship His servants when they exist. God is holy and free from forgetfulness or error, so His pre-eternal existence necessarily includes the meaning of what will reach the servants. The speech that subsists in God's essence (al-kalam al-qa'im bi nafsihi) is likened to His eternal power (qudrah qadimah) – both are eternal and never cease. Then Juwayni elaborates: just as a human being, intending to speak to a friend after a month, arranges speech internally and later utters it, so God's speech is intrinsically eternal, and when He wishes to convey it, He brings it into existence in the form of revealed words. The human's internal speech exists before utterance; similarly, God's speech is eternal in His essence. The conclusion is that God's attributes, including speech and power, are eternal and never cease.

Key Terms
كلام الله
قديم
قدرة
الكلام القائم بالنفس

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think God's speech is created or temporal because it is heard in revelation at specific times.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies that God's speech is eternal in His essence, like His power; it is not created but subsists in Him and is expressed in time.

Checkpoint
What does Juwayni compare God's eternal speech to in this passage?
A) A human's written letter
B) A human's internal speech arranged before utterance
C) A human's physical voice
D) A human's power to act

Key Takeaway
God's speech (al-kalam al-qa'im bi nafsihi) is eternal in His essence, just as His power is eternal, and does not cease.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have learned that God's speech is eternal in His essence, not created. When you read the Quran, do you truly feel you are encountering an eternal word, or do you treat it as a human book from history? What would change if you consistently remembered its eternal nature?
?
Juwayni uses the analogy of internal speech to show that our inner thoughts exist before we express them. Are you mindful that every intention in your heart is already known to God from eternity? How does that awareness affect the way you guard your inner life?
?
The lesson connects God's speech to His power—both are eternal and never cease. Have you ever doubted God's promises or felt His word might fail? How can you strengthen your trust in the eternal, unchanging nature of His speech?

The Phrase 'لم يزل' and the Inseparability of God's Speech
Juwayni defends the phrase 'لم يزل' to affirm God's speech as inseparable from His essence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Juwayni insists on using the phrase 'لم يزل' to affirm that God's speech is an eternal attribute inseparable from His essence, similar to His will and power.

Core Idea
God's speech, like His will and power, is eternal and never ceases.

In discussing God's speech, Juwayni emphasizes that we must use the term 'لم يزل' to affirm its eternity. He argues that just as God's will and power are eternal attributes subsisting in His essence, so is His speech. This analogy clarifies that God's command 'Be' is not a temporal event but an eternal expression that brings about creation in time.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing it. Then read the full literal translation. Let Juwayni's own argument come through before you interpret.
Notice the key terms like 'لم يزل' and 'كلام الله الأزلى'.
2
Identify Juwayni's key claim
What is the central point Juwayni is making about God's speech? Look for his explicit statement that God's speech does not separate from the essence.
Also note the phrase 'لم يزل' is both a claim and a required expression.
3
Examine the analogy with will and power
How does Juwayni use the analogy of God's will (إرادة) and power (قدرة) to support his argument? What is the relationship between the eternal attribute and its temporal effect?
The example of 'Be' (كن) illustrates that the speech is eternal while creation is contingent.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ولما تحدث في « كلام الله » لم يمش على تعبيره « ولم يزل » ذلك أنه أثبت الله كلاما لم ينفصل عنه في قوله « كلام الله الأزلى لا يفارق الذات ولا يزايلها » وابن كان يقصد بهذه العبارة « النظم الدال على المعنى المتحقق لفظا ومعنى فى علم الله أزلا ( كترتيب الانسان كلام في نفسه ليؤديه الى صاحبه بعد شهر فلا اعتراض عليه . لأنه يكون كارادته و قدرته من قبل أن يحدث الله الذي أراده . فاذا أحدث الذي أراده فان الذى حدث يكون حادثا • كارادته خلق العالم أزلا فانه لما خلق العالم صار العالم منفصلا بالارادة ومخلوقا حادثا . كذلك لو تكلم فقال كن » مثلا . فان « كن » تعتبر فى المعنى كخلق العالم بعد أن كان فكرة . وكما تشاهد قدرة الله لا تزال ، كذلك كلامه لا يزال أو بتعبير الجويني نفسه « ولم يزل » لقد ظهرت قدرات كثيرة الله على طول الزمان
And when he spoke about 'the speech of God', he did not proceed with his expression 'لم يزل' because he affirmed that God has speech that is not separate from Him, in his saying: 'God's eternal speech does not separate from the essence nor does it leave it.' And Ibn [Kullab?] meant by this expression: 'The organized structure indicating the meaning realized in word and meaning in God's knowledge eternally, like the arrangement of a person's speech in himself to convey it to his companion after a month, so there is no objection to it. Because it is like His will and power before God brings about what He willed. So when He brings about what He willed, then what occurs is contingent. Like His willing the creation of the world eternally, for when He created the world, the world became separate by His will and created, contingent. Similarly, if He spoke and said 'Be' for example, then 'Be' is considered in meaning like the creation of the world after it was an idea. And just as you observe that God's power does not cease, likewise His speech does not cease, or in Juwayni's own expression 'لم يزل'. Indeed, many powers of God have appeared over time.
Source pages p. 101

Commentary
This passage discusses the theological phrase 'لم يزل' (never ceases) used for God's attributes. Juwayni explains that when the topic of God's speech (kalam Allah) is addressed, one should use the expression 'لم يزل' to affirm that God's speech is eternal and inseparable from His essence. He quotes the statement: 'God's eternal speech does not separate from the essence nor does it leave it.' This is attributed to Ibn Kullab (or Ibn?). The meaning is that God's speech is an eternal attribute subsisting in His essence, like His will (iradah) and power (qudrah). Just as God's will to create the world is eternal and the actual creation is contingent, similarly God's command 'Be' is an eternal speech that brings about contingent creation. The analogy with a person arranging speech internally before utterance reinforces that the internal speech is prior and eternal. Juwayni concludes that just as God's power is observed to be eternal and manifest over time, so His speech is eternal. The phrase 'لم يزل' is thus appropriate for affirming the eternity of God's speech.

Key Terms
لم يزل
كلام الله الأزلى
الذات
المعنى
النظم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that because God's speech is heard in time (e.g., the Quran revealed to prophets), it is a created attribute that began in time.
Correction: Juwayni argues that God's speech is eternal and inseparable from His essence; its temporal manifestation (like the command 'Be') does not imply that the speech itself is created. The speech is eternal, like His will and power.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what does the expression 'لم يزل' affirm about God's speech?
A) That it is created in time
B) That it is separate from God's essence
C) That it is eternal and inseparable from the essence
D) That it is only metaphorical

Key Takeaway
Juwayni insists on using 'لم يزل' to emphasize that God's speech is eternal and inseparable from His essence, akin to His will and power.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God's speech is eternal and inseparable from His essence, then every time you hear the Qur'an, you are encountering a permanent attribute of God—something that has never ceased to exist. How does that truth change the way you listen to or recite the Qur'an? What specific practice will you adopt to honor that reality starting today?
?
Juwayni uses the phrase 'لم يزل' to defend the eternity of God's speech. But examine your own heart: have you ever treated God's attributes—His speech, His will, His power—as if they could change or fail? Recall a moment when you doubted God's consistency. What step will you take to root your trust in the unchangeable nature of God?
?
The analogy of internal speech versus spoken words compares God's eternal attribute with its temporal expression. In your own life, you often express yourself through words and actions. Do you ever reflect that your words come and go, but God's speech remains eternally? How does that contrast affect your desire to align your own speech with the eternal truth of God's words?

Eternal Power Requires Eternal Speech
Juwayni argues that eternal power implies eternal speech via prophetic history.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why affirming God's eternal power logically requires affirming His eternal speech, based on the manifestation of both in prophetic history.

Core Idea
Eternal power and eternal speech stand or fall together.

Imam Juwayni presents a concise but powerful argument from the history of prophets. He points out that God's power has been at work saving prophets across centuries, and His speech has been revealed in scriptures. Since both are manifestations of divine attributes that have been active throughout time, consistency demands that if we affirm one as eternal, we must affirm the other as eternal. This lesson examines that reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the literal translation to understand the flow of the argument.
Notice the names of prophets and the term 'لم تزل' (never cease).
2
Identify the two manifestations
Juwayni mentions two types of divine activity: saving prophets (power) and revealing scriptures (speech). Identify these in the text.
3
Understand the logical implication
Juwayni argues that if power is eternal, then speech must also be eternal because both are manifested similarly across time. Reflect on the logic of this analogy.
This is the core of the proof.
4
Reflect on the misconception
Consider the misconception that because scriptures were revealed at specific times, God's speech is temporal. How does Juwayni's argument counter this?
The temporal event does not make the attribute temporal.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فقد قدر على نجاة نوح عليه السلام. ومن بعثة ابراهيم عليه السلام ومن بعده موسى عليه السلام ومن بعدهم . وبين كل نبي وغيره سنين عديدة . وكذلك ظهر كلام الله تعالى في صحف ابراهيم وموسى ومحمد عليهم الصلاة والسلام .. فمن أثبت قدرات لم تزل يلزمه اثبات كلام لم يزل
For He [God] had power over the salvation of Noah, peace be upon him. And from the mission of Abraham, peace be upon him, and after him Moses, peace be upon him, and after them. And between each prophet and other [events] are many years. And likewise the speech of God Almighty appeared in the scriptures of Abraham, Moses, and Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon them. So whoever affirms powers that never cease, he must affirm speech that never ceases.
Source pages p. 102

Commentary
This passage presents a proof (dalil) from the history of prophets. Juwayni argues that God's power (qudrah) is eternal and has been manifested throughout history in saving prophets like Noah, Abraham, Moses, and others. Similarly, God's speech (kalam) has appeared in scriptures given to Abraham, Moses, and Muhammad. Since God's power is universally acknowledged as eternal (لم تزل), consistency demands that His speech also be eternal (لم يزل). The argument is that the same divine attribute of eternity applies to both power and speech because both are manifested in temporal events (prophets' salvation and revelation of scriptures) but their source is eternal. This counters those who might affirm eternal power but deny eternal speech.

Key Terms
قدرة
لم تزل
كلام
صحف
نبوة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that because scriptures were revealed at specific times, God's speech is temporal and created.
Correction: Juwayni argues that the temporal revelation of scriptures is a manifestation of an eternal attribute, just as saving prophets at specific times manifests eternal power. The events are temporal, but the attribute is eternal.

Checkpoint
What does Juwayni use as evidence for the eternity of God's speech?
A) The preservation of the Qur'an
B) The salvation of prophets and revelation of scriptures
C) The creation of the world
D) The miracles of prophets

Key Takeaway
Juwayni establishes a logical connection: the same divine eternity that applies to power must apply to speech because both are manifested in history.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believed that the Qur'an is the eternal speech of God, not merely a historical text, how would your daily engagement with it change? What one practice will you adopt this week to treat it as His living word?
?
The argument from power to speech is based on consistency. In your own life, are you consistent in affirming both God's power and His speech? Is there an area where you acknowledge His power but neglect His guidance?
?
Would you still prioritize reading and obeying the Qur'an if you knew it was revealed centuries ago but remains eternally relevant? How does this lesson challenge any excuse you've made for not studying it?

Hearing the Qur'an and Reconciling Its Createdness
Juwayni explains hearing the Qur'an and proposes reconciliation on createdness.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's clarification that hearing the Qur'an means hearing the reciter's voice, which conveys the eternal speech of God, and how this distinction could reconcile the dispute over whether the Qur'an is created or eternal.

Core Idea
Hearing the Qur'an means hearing the reciter's voice, not God's eternal speech directly.

Juwayni addresses a key theological dispute: the nature of the Qur'an as God's speech. He explains that when we hear the Qur'an recited, we are hearing a created voice that conveys the uncreated meaning. This distinction, he argues, could have resolved the famous controversy over whether the Qur'an is created or eternal. In this passage, he provides an analogy and cites a Qur'anic verse to support his view.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the translation. Notice how Juwayni builds his argument: first stating the fact of hearing, then distinguishing between eternal speech and recited voice.
The passage is dense, so take your time.
2
Identify Juwayni's key distinction
Find where Juwayni says that 'hearing the speech of God' does not mean hearing the eternal attribute, but rather the voice of the reciter. Note how he uses the analogy of a king's message.
This distinction is crucial for the reconciliation.
3
Understand the reconciliation
Juwayni suggests that if people had understood speech (kalam) like they understood power (qudra)—as an eternal attribute with temporal manifestations—the controversy would not have occurred. Reflect on how this applies.
Both sides agree on God's oneness and eternal attributes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والقرآن الكريم كلام الله سمعه النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم على النحو المذكور فى هذه الآية : وما كان البشر أن يكلمه الله الا وحيا أو من وراء حجاب أو يرسل رسولا فيوحى باذنه ما يشاء انه على حكيم ( الشورى : ٥ ) وأمر بحفظه في الصدور وبكتابته في الأوراق . فالذي حفظ ويحفظ لم يسمع صوت الله كما تلقى النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم .. وهذا هو معنى قول الجويني رحمه الله « يجب إطلاق القول بأن كلام الله تبارك وتعالى مسموع ، وليس المراد بذلك تعلق الإدراك بالكلام الأزلى القائم بالبارى تعالى : ولكن المدرك صوت القارىء . والمفهوم عند قراءته كلام الله سبحانه ، ولا بعد في مسموع : تسمية المفهوم مسموعا . فهذا بمثابة ما لو بلغ مبلغ رسالة ملك فيحسن ممن بلغته الرسالة أن يقول سمعت الملك ورسالته ... الخ ) ولو أن الناس في زمن الامام أحمد بن حنبل - رحمه الله - فهموا الكلام كما فهموا القدرة لما قامت فتنة القول بخلق القرآن . واستطاع بعضهم أن يعذر بعضا فيما ذهبوا اليه . بل واستطاع من يريد التوفيق بين القول بقدم القرآن وخلقه أن يوفق . حيث يجمع الكل اعتراف بوحدانية الله الكامل . الصفات من قبل خلق العالم وأنه ما يزال للكوان حديث بعجائبه . وهذه الآية الكريمة ( وما كان لبشر أن يكلمه الله الا وحيا . أو وراء حجاب . أو يرسل رسولا فيوحى باذنه ما يشاء انه على حكيم تثبت الحديث المباشر بصوت بين الله والبشر بدون رؤية البشر لذات الله تعالى وذلك واضح من قوله أو من وراء حجاب .
And the Noble Qur'an is the speech of God. The Prophet, peace be upon him, heard it in the manner mentioned in this verse: 'And it is not for any human that God should speak to him except by revelation or from behind a veil or by sending a messenger to reveal by His permission what He wills. Indeed, He is Most High, All-Wise.' (al-Shura 42:51) And He commanded its preservation in chests and its writing in papers. So the one who preserved and preserves did not hear the voice of God as the Prophet received it. And this is the meaning of the saying of Juwayni, may God have mercy on him: 'It is necessary to state that the speech of God, blessed and exalted, is heard, but the intent is not the attachment of perception to the eternal speech subsisting in the Creator, exalted; rather, what is perceived is the voice of the reciter. And the understood meaning upon its recitation is the speech of God, exalted. And there is no difficulty in calling the understood meaning 'heard'. This is like the case when a message of a king reaches someone, then it is appropriate for the one who received the message to say, 'I heard the king and his message.' ...' And if the people at the time of Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal, may God have mercy on him, had understood speech (kalam) as they understood power, the trial of the saying that the Qur'an is created would not have arisen. And some would have been able to excuse others for what they held. Rather, whoever wants reconciliation between the saying that the Qur'an is pre-eternal and created could reconcile. Where all agree on the acknowledgment of the complete oneness of God, the attributes before the creation of the world, and that it never ceases, the universe has a story of its wonders. And this noble verse (42:51) establishes direct speech with a voice between God and humans without humans seeing the essence of God Almighty, and that is clear from His saying 'or from behind a veil'.
Source pages p. 102

Commentary
This passage addresses the theological controversy over the createdness of the Qur'an. Juwayni quotes verse 42:51 to show that God speaks to humans in three ways: revelation, from behind a veil, or through a messenger. The Prophet heard the Qur'an in one of these ways, and it was preserved in hearts and writings. However, what is preserved is not the eternal speech itself but the reciter's voice; the meaning conveyed is God's speech. Juwayni clarifies that saying 'the speech of God is heard' means the recited words, not the eternal attribute. He uses the analogy of a king's message: hearing the message is called hearing the king. If people during Ahmad ibn Hanbal's time had understood this distinction between God's eternal speech (as an attribute) and its temporal expression, the 'trial of the created Qur'an' would not have occurred. Reconciling the two views is possible because both affirm God's oneness and that His attributes are eternal. The verse is then re-emphasized to show that God's direct speech to prophets involves a voice but no vision of His essence.

Key Terms
القرآن الكريم
كلام الله
مسموع
صوت القارىء
خلق القرآن
قدم القرآن

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that hearing the Qur'an means hearing God's eternal speech directly.
Correction: Juwayni explains we hear the reciter's voice which conveys the eternal meaning; we do not perceive the eternal attribute itself.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what could have prevented the controversy over the createdness of the Qur'an?
A) Denying that the Qur'an is God's speech
B) Understanding that God's speech is like His power—eternal in essence, but its expression is temporal
C) Accepting the literal interpretation of all verses
D) Rejecting the use of analogy

Key Takeaway
Juwayni explains that we hear the recited Qur'an as a temporal expression of God's eternal speech, which resolves the apparent conflict between the Qur'an being created and uncreated.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you listen to the Qur'an recited, do you experience it as merely human sound, or do you truly feel you are encountering God's eternal speech? What would need to change in your heart to make that encounter real?
?
If you had been alive during the Mihna (the inquisition over the createdness of the Qur'an), would you have been able to see the reconciliation Juwayni proposes, or would you have taken a side? What does that reveal about your attachment to theological labels over divine truth?
?
Juwayni says that if we understood speech as we understand power, the conflict would dissolve. In your own life, where do you hold onto conflicts because you refuse to see the distinction between the eternal and the temporal? How can you apply this principle of reconciliation today?

The Hand of Allah: Applying the Muhkam to a Mutashabih Verse
Juwayni applies the muhkam to the hand verse.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni uses the muhkam verse 'Nothing is like unto Him' to determine the metaphorical meaning of a mutashabih verse about Allah's hand.

Core Idea
The muhkam verse resolves ambiguity by ruling out literal interpretations that imply similarity.

Juwayni now gives a concrete example of the principle he established. The verse about Allah's hand is a classic case of ambiguity (mutashabih). It could mean a literal hand or power. By referring to the clear muhkam verse that negates any similarity to creation, we determine that the intended meaning is power, not a limb. This demonstrates the proper method of interpreting mutashabih verses.

Guided Path
1
Identify the two possible meanings
Read the passage and note that the verse 'The hand of Allah is over their hands' admits two interpretations: a literal hand (limb) and power (qudra).
The ambiguity arises from the word 'hand' which has both literal and metaphorical uses.
2
Understand the role of the muhkam verse
Juwayni states that because the verse is mutashabih, we must refer to the muhkam verse 'There is nothing like unto Him' which has only one clear meaning: absolute dissimilarity between Allah and creation.
3
See the conclusion and the rejected phrase
The muhkam verse negates any similarity, so the literal hand is impossible; we adopt the meaning 'power'. Juwayni explicitly rejects saying 'hand but not like hands' because that still affirms a hand, violating the muhkam.
The correct affirmation is to describe Allah with perfection and transcendence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فمثال الأعضاء قوله تعالى : «يد الله فوق أيديهم» ( الفتح ۱۰ ) فان هذا القول متشابه لاحتماله معنيين. الأول : اليد الحقيقية أي الجارحة كيد الانسان والثاني : أنه ليس بمعنى اليد الحقيقية بل بمعنى القدرة أي قدرة الله فوق قدرة الناس . ولما كان هذا القول متشابها فانه يتعين الرجوع الى الآية المحكمة التي لا تحتمل الا معنى واحدا وهي ليس كمثله شيء ) ( الشورى ) (۱۱) وحيث أنها تنفي مشابهة الله للانسان فاذن يجب الأخذ بالمعنى الثاني وهو القدرة ، وترك المعنى الأول وهو اليد الحقيقية . ولا نقول الله يد ولكن ليست كأيدي البشر لأنه » ليس كمثله شيء » بل نقول : الله واحد متصف بكل كمال ومنزه عن كل نقص وليس كمثله شيء .
So an example of body parts is His saying: 'The hand of Allah is over their hands' (48:10). This saying is mutashabih because it admits two meanings. First: the real hand, i.e., the limb like the hand of a human. Second: that it is not in the meaning of a real hand but in the meaning of power, i.e., the power of Allah is above the power of people. And since this saying is mutashabih, it is necessary to refer to the muhkam verse which does not admit except one meaning, and it is 'There is nothing like unto Him' (42:11). And since it negates the similarity of Allah to humans, then it is obligatory to adopt the second meaning, which is power, and to leave the first meaning, which is the real hand. And we do not say 'Allah has a hand, but it is not like the hands of humans,' because 'There is nothing like unto Him.' Rather, we say: Allah is One, described with every perfection, transcendent above every deficiency, and nothing is like unto Him.
Source pages p. 104

Commentary
This passage applies the principle from the previous lesson. It takes the verse about the 'hand' of Allah – a classic mutashabih. Juwayni explains that it has two possible meanings: literal (limb) and metaphorical (power). Because the muhkam verse 'Nothing is like unto Him' negates any similarity to creation, the literal meaning is impossible, so the metaphorical meaning (power) is taken. He explicitly rejects the phrase 'hand but not like hands' because that still affirms a hand of some kind, which violates the muhkam. Instead, he affirms that Allah is described with perfection and transcends any deficiency. This refutes both anthropomorphism and the attempt to affirm attributes without ta'wil while claiming negation of tashbih.

Key Terms
يد
قدرة
محكم
متشابه
تشبيه

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: It is acceptable to say 'Allah has a hand but not like human hands' as a way of affirming the attribute while negating tashbih.
Correction: Juwayni rejects this because it still affirms a hand, which implies similarity. The muhkam verse 'Nothing is like unto Him' demands that we not affirm any hand at all. Instead, we interpret 'hand' as power.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the correct meaning of 'hand' in the verse 'The hand of Allah is over their hands'?
A) A real hand but not like human hands
B) Power (qudra)
C) The same as a human hand
D) We should not interpret it

Key Takeaway
The muhkam verse 'There is nothing like unto Him' is the decisive criterion for interpreting ambiguous verses about Allah's attributes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read 'The hand of Allah is over their hands,' does your mind instinctively try to picture a hand? Will you surrender that image to the muhkam verse, even if it feels less tangible?
?
Juwayni rejects even saying 'a hand but not like hands' because it still implies similarity. Are there areas in your understanding of Allah where you cling to comfortable comparisons, even while verbally denying them? What would it take to let go completely?
?
If 'There is nothing like unto Him' truly governed your speech, how would it change the way you describe Allah in your prayers, your conversations, and your thoughts? Will you commit to guarding your tongue from any hint of resemblance starting today?

The Salaf's Metaphorical Understanding of Forgetfulness
Juwayni uses forgetfulness verse to demonstrate salaf's metaphorical interpretation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni uses the verse 'They forgot Allah, so He forgot them' to demonstrate that the salaf interpreted certain divine attributes metaphorically to avoid anthropomorphism.

Core Idea
The salaf interpreted forgetfulness as neglect, not a literal attribute of Allah.

Juwayni continues his argument against affirming unintelligible attributes by posing a challenge: what about verses that speak of Allah forgetting? He shows that the salaf understood such verses as metaphors, not literal descriptions. This lesson examines his reasoning and the examples he provides.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let Juwayni's argument come through before you interpret.
Notice how he uses the same logic as the hand attribute.
2
Identify the two possible meanings
The passage presents two meanings of 'forgetfulness' in the verse: literal forgetfulness and metaphorical neglect. Find each one and see which Juwayni says is correct.
3
Understand why the salaf chose the metaphorical meaning
Ask yourself why the salaf would interpret this verse figuratively. Juwayni says it is because they always upheld tanzih (transcendence) for Allah. How does this connect to the principle of affirming attributes without similarity?
This step ties back to the earlier discussion on hand and other attributes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وإذا لم نقل بذلك وأثبتنا اليد مع نفي المماثلة فماذا نقول في مثل قوله تعالى نسوا الله فنسيهم ؟ هل تثبت نسيانا لله مع المماثلة ؟ إن من يثبت نسيانا لله مع عدم مماثلة نسيان الله النسيان البشر يكون كافرا وبعيدا كل البعد عن رحمة الله . ومثال صفات الأحاسيس نسوا الله فنسيهم ( التوبة : ٦٧ ) وهي تحتمل معنيين النسيان الحقيقي والكناية عن الإهمال أي أهملوا تعاليم الله وأغفلوا ذكره فأهملهم الله وتركهم من رحمته . أي خلى بينهم وبين أهواتهم. والمعنى الكنائي هو المراد . لما قلنا ، وهذا هو فهم السلف . فما سمعنا عن أحد منهم غير التنزيه لله عز وجل . وإلا يكن هذا فهمهم . فما هو تأويلهم لمثل قوله تعالى : ( نسوا الله فنسيهم ) وقوله عن سفينة نوح عليه السلام : « تجري بأعيننا » ( القمر : ١٤ ) وهل كانوا يفهمون أن للمقادير - وهي معنوية - أعنة كأعنة الخيول السريعة الجري في قول الشاعر : دع المقادير تجري في أعنتها ولا تبيتن إلا خالي البالي ما بين طرفة عين وانتباهتها تغير الله من حال إلى حال ؟
And if we did not say that and we affirm the hand with negation of similarity, then what do we say about such as His saying: 'They forgot Allah, so He forgot them'? Do you affirm forgetfulness for Allah with similarity? Indeed, whoever affirms forgetfulness for Allah while there is no similarity between Allah's forgetfulness and human forgetfulness becomes a disbeliever and far from the mercy of Allah. And an example of attributes of sensations is 'They forgot Allah, so He forgot them' (9:67). It admits two meanings: real forgetfulness and kinayah (metaphor) for neglect, i.e., they neglected Allah's teachings and forgot His remembrance, so He neglected them and left them from His mercy, i.e., He left them to their desires. And the metaphorical meaning is intended, as we said, and this is the understanding of the salaf. For we have not heard from any of them except tanzih (transcendence) of Allah Almighty. If this were not their understanding, then what is their ta'wil of such as His saying: 'They forgot Allah, so He forgot them' and His saying about the ship of Nuh: 'It sails under Our eyes' (54:14)? And did they understand that fate (which is conceptual) has reins like the reins of fast-running horses, in the poet's saying: 'Leave fate to run in its reins, and do not stay except carefree; between the blink of an eye and its opening, Allah changes a state to another state'?
Source pages p. 105

Commentary
This passage continues the argument against affirming literal attributes while negating tashbih. Juwayni poses a challenge: if you affirm a hand while negating similarity, what would you do with the attribute of forgetfulness? You would have to either affirm it literally (which implies similarity) or explain it away. He argues that affirming literal forgetfulness while denying similarity leads to kufr because it affirms an imperfection in Allah. Then he presents the example of forgetfulness (nisyan) from Quran 9:67. He explains that it has two meanings: real forgetfulness (impossible for Allah) and metaphorical neglect (kinayah). The salaf understood it as the latter. He then cites other examples: the ark sailing under Allah's 'eyes' and the poetic imagery of fate having 'reins.' The salaf would interpret these figuratively, not literally. This reinforces the method of tafwid and metaphorical interpretation when necessary, based on the muhkam principle.

Key Terms
نسيان
كناية
تنزيه
تأويل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That the salaf interpreted all verses literally without any figurative interpretation.
Correction: Juwayni shows that the salaf understood 'forgetfulness' in the verse as a metaphor for neglect, not a literal attribute of Allah.

Checkpoint
What does Juwayni say is the correct interpretation of 'forgetfulness' in the verse 'They forgot Allah, so He forgot them'?
A) Real forgetfulness on Allah's part
B) Kinayah for neglect
C) We should not interpret it
D) It means Allah plays tricks

Key Takeaway
The salaf interpreted verses like 'They forgot Allah, so He forgot them' as metaphors for neglect, preserving Allah's transcendence and avoiding anthropomorphism.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read a verse that seems to describe Allah doing something human-like (like 'forgetting'), do you immediately try to interpret it in a way that preserves His transcendence, or do you sometimes pause? What does that pause reveal about your heart’s attachment to a human-like image of Allah?
?
Juwayni warns that affirming literal forgetfulness with a denial of similarity still leads to kufr. How seriously do you examine your own thoughts and descriptions of Allah to ensure you are not inadvertently diminishing His perfection? Is there any attribute you hesitate to fully transcend?
?
The salaf were unanimous in their metaphorical understanding of such verses. Does your study of Allah’s attributes reflect the same deep reverence and caution, or have you been content with surface-level readings? What concrete step can you take today to align your understanding more closely with the salaf’s approach of tanzih?
