Divine Decree
intermediate
5 min
Juwayni's Account of Tawfiq and Tab'
Juwayni describes the divine mechanism of tawfiq and tab'.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's detailed description of how God decrees success (tawfiq) for the obedient and sealing (tab') for the disbelievers, attributing both entirely to His eternal will.

Core Idea
God's decree directly causes both guidance and misguidance.

Imam al-Juwayni presents a vivid picture of divine intervention in human destiny. He explains that when God wills good for a servant, He removes obstacles and facilitates obedience; when He wills evil, He seals the heart. This passage shows a deterministic framework where all outcomes are directly ordained by God.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic text once, focusing on Juwayni's two scenarios: for the obedient and for the disbeliever.
Notice the parallel structure in describing God's actions.
2
Identify key divine actions
List the actions God takes in each scenario (e.g., perfects intellect, removes obstacles, appoints companions; distances from good, makes evil desirable).
These actions are all directly attributed to God's decree.
3
Reflect on the conclusion
Juwayni ends by saying this sealing is 'by the decree of God.' Ask: what does this imply about human choice?
This sets up the critique in the next lesson.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
القدرة الحادثة ، ويستحيل أن يقع بعضه بقدرة الله عز وجل ، فان الفعل الواحد لا بعض له

ويتحدث الجويني عن توفيق الله للطائعين ، وطبعه وختمه على قلوب المعتدين والكافرين . فيقول » اذا أراد الله بعبد خيرا أكمل عقله ، وأتم بصيرته ثم صرف عنه العوائق والدوافع ، وأزاح عنه الموانع ، ووفق لــه قرناء الخير وسهل له سبيله ، وقطع عنه الملهيات وأسباب الغفلات والذهول ، وقيض له ما يقرب الى القربات فيألفيها ، ثم يعتادها ، ويمرن عليها . واذا أراد بعبد شرا قدر له ما يبعده عن الخير ويقصيه ، وهيأ له تماديه فى الغى ، وحبب اليه التشوق الى الشهوات ، وعرضه للآفات .. وكلما غلبت دواعى الشر خنست دواعى الخير ، ثم يستمر على الشرور على مر الدهور ، هاويا في مهاويها ، وتتعاون عليه الوساوس ، ونزغات الشيطان ونزوات النفس الأمارة بالسوء فتنشيء الغفلة غشاوة على قلبه ، بقضاء الله تبارك وتعالى وقدره ، فذلكم الطبع - عافاكم الله - والختم والأكنة ) .

لقد أرجع التوفيق والطبع والختم الى « بقضاء الله تبارك وتعالى وقدره » انه لم يثبت اختيارا فى أفعال العباد - خلافا لما فهم الشيخ الأمام زاهد الكوثرى - لم يثبت ارادة خيرة من العبد بسببها يوقفه الله ولم يثبت ارادة سيئة من العبد بسببها يطبع الله على قلبه . انه سار في أفعال العباد وفى القضاء والقدر المؤديان في نظره الى التوفيق والطبع الى ما أراده الله أزلا من العالم . فالعالم يسير حسب خطة رسمها الله فى الأزل كما يقرر . ولو أنه قرر في آية التوفيق وآيات الطبع أن الله تعالى اذا رأى من العبد اتجاها الى الخيرات وفقه الى الخيرات . واذا رأى منه اتجاها الى المنكرات خلى بينه وبين نفسه الأمارة بالسوء . لكان بهذا التقرير موضحا لعدل الله ورحمته وأنه ما يريد ظلما للعباد .
The created power, and it is impossible that part of it occurs by the power of God Almighty, for a single action has no parts.

And Juwayni discusses God's granting of success (tawfiq) to the obedient, and His sealing (tab') and stamping (khatm) upon the hearts of the transgressors and disbelievers. He says: 'When God desires for a servant good, He perfects his intellect, completes his insight, then removes from him obstacles and impediments, and clears away hindrances, and appoints for him companions of good and eases his way, and cuts off from him distractions and causes of heedlessness and forgetfulness, and prepares for him what brings him near to acts of devotion so that he becomes familiar with them, then accustoms himself to them, and practices them. And when He desires for a servant evil, He decrees for him what distances him from good and keeps him away, and prepares for him persistence in error, and makes desirable to him yearning for desires, and exposes him to afflictions. And whenever the motives of evil prevail, the motives of good retreat, then he continues in evils over the course of ages, falling into their abysses, and whisperings and the promptings of Satan and the impulses of the soul commanding evil cooperate against him, so heedlessness creates a covering over his heart, by the decree of God Almighty and His predestination. So that is the sealing (tab') — may God preserve you — and the stamping (khatm) and the covering (akina).'

He indeed attributed tawfiq, tab', and khatm to 'the decree of God Almighty and His predestination.' He did not establish any choice in the actions of servants — contrary to what Shaykh Imam Zahid al-Kawthari understood — he did not establish any good will from the servant by which God would grant him success, nor did he establish any evil will from the servant by which God would seal his heart. He proceeded in the actions of servants and in the decree and predestination that in his view lead to tawfiq and tab' according to what God willed from eternity concerning the world. So the world proceeds according to a plan that God drew up in eternity, as he asserts. And if he had stated in the verse of tawfiq and the verses of tab' that God Almighty, when He sees from the servant a direction towards good, grants him success towards good, and when He sees from him a direction towards evil, leaves him alone with his soul commanding evil, then by this statement he would have clarified God's justice and mercy and that He does not desire injustice for the servants.
Source pages p. 109

Commentary
This passage begins with a brief statement on created power (qudra haditha), asserting that it is impossible for a single action to be partially caused by God's power because an action is indivisible. Then the commentator introduces Juwayni's discussion of God's tawfiq (granting success) for the obedient and His tab' and khatm (sealing) for the wrongdoers. Juwayni is quoted describing how God, when He wills good for a servant, perfects his intellect and removes obstacles, appoints good companions, and facilitates obedience. Conversely, when He wills evil, He distances the servant from good, makes sin attractive, and allows whispers and Satanic promptings to overwhelm him, ultimately sealing his heart by God's decree and predestination. The commentator then critiques Juwayni, arguing that this view eliminates human choice entirely. Juwayni, according to the commentator, does not attribute tawfiq or sealing to any prior will or action of the servant; rather, everything is directly determined by God's eternal plan. The commentator suggests that if Juwayni had instead said that God responds to the servant's own direction (good or bad), it would better uphold God's justice and mercy.

Key Terms
القدرة الحادثة
التوفيق
الطبع
الختم
القضاء والقدر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that in Juwayni's view, human free will plays a role in tawfiq or tab'.
Correction: Juwayni explicitly states that both tawfiq and sealing occur solely by God's decree, with no mention of prior human choice.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni's statement in the passage, what causes the sealing (tab') on a person's heart?
A) The person's own evil choices
B) God's decree and predestination
C) Satan's whisperings alone
D) The person's lack of intellect

Key Takeaway
Juwayni views tawfiq and tab' as direct outcomes of God's eternal decree, with no human initiative.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God's decree is the direct cause of your faith, what do you say to yourself when you sin? Do you blame God or take responsibility? How does Juwayni's description challenge your self-perception as an autonomous agent?
?
Juwayni describes God sealing the hearts of the transgressors. Read that description and ask yourself: am I among those who have been sealed? What signs of spiritual blindness do I see in myself? This is a fearful question.
?
The lesson says that when God desires evil for a servant, He facilitates the path to sin. Does this make you complacent or more desperate for God's mercy? How does this truth affect your daily struggle with sin?

Divine Decree
intermediate
5 min
Critique of Juwayni's Determinism
Critique of Juwayni's deterministic view and presentation of an alternative.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the critique that Juwayni's view eliminates human choice and fails to uphold divine justice, and the alternative that God responds to human direction.

Core Idea
If God alone decrees all actions, human accountability is lost.

After presenting Juwayni's deterministic account, the commentator offers a sharp critique. He argues that Juwayni's system leaves no room for human will or responsibility. He then proposes an alternative: that God's tawfiq and sealing are responses to the servant's own inclination, not arbitrary decrees. This lesson examines that critique and the proposed alternative.

Guided Path
1
Read the critique passage
Read the Arabic and translation of the critique section. Note the key claims against Juwayni.
The commentator explicitly says Juwayni did not establish any will from the servant.
2
Contrast with the alternative
The commentator suggests an alternative: God sees the servant's direction and then grants success or leaves him alone. Compare this with Juwayni's view.
Note how the alternative preserves human responsibility.
3
Evaluate the implications
Reflect on which view better upholds God's justice and mercy, as the commentator argues.
This is a central debate in Islamic theology.

Source Text

ويتحدث الجويني عن توفيق الله للطائعين ، وطبعه وختمه على قلوب المعتدين والكافرين . فيقول » اذا أراد الله بعبد خيرا أكمل عقله ، وأتم بصيرته ثم صرف عنه العوائق والدوافع ، وأزاح عنه الموانع ، ووفق لــه قرناء الخير وسهل له سبيله ، وقطع عنه الملهيات وأسباب الغفلات والذهول ، وقيض له ما يقرب الى القربات فيألفيها ، ثم يعتادها ، ويمرن عليها . واذا أراد بعبد شرا قدر له ما يبعده عن الخير ويقصيه ، وهيأ له تماديه فى الغى ، وحبب اليه التشوق الى الشهوات ، وعرضه للآفات .. وكلما غلبت دواعى الشر خنست دواعى الخير ، ثم يستمر على الشرور على مر الدهور ، هاويا في مهاويها ، وتتعاون عليه الوساوس ، ونزغات الشيطان ونزوات النفس الأمارة بالسوء فتنشيء الغفلة غشاوة على قلبه ، بقضاء الله تبارك وتعالى وقدره ، فذلكم الطبع - عافاكم الله - والختم والأكنة ) .

The created power, and it is impossible that part of it occurs by the power of God Almighty, for a single action has no parts.

And Juwayni discusses God's granting of success (tawfiq) to the obedient, and His sealing (tab') and stamping (khatm) upon the hearts of the transgressors and disbelievers. Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that Juwayni's view allows for human free will because he mentions the servant's actions.
Correction: The commentator argues that Juwayni does not attribute any causal role to the servant's will; all outcomes are directly decreed.

Checkpoint
What does the commentator argue Juwayni failed to do in his account of tawfiq and tab'?
A) Attribute actions to God's power
B) Establish any will from the servant
C) Use the terms tawfiq and tab'
D) Quote from the Quran

Key Takeaway
The commentator critiques Juwayni for eliminating human choice and proposes that God's actions should respond to the servant's own direction.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you face a trial or a failure in faith, do you instinctively attribute it to 'God's decree' in a way that lets you off the hook from examining your own choices? What would change if you first looked at your own direction—your inclinations, your negligence—before invoking qadr?
?
The commentator says God should respond to the servant's direction. Is your life currently moving towards good or evil in a way that would invite God's tawfiq or His leaving you alone? What one concrete sign of 'direction towards good' can you point to in your last week?
?
This lesson shows that a wrong view of qadr can nullify human accountability. Are there areas of your life where you secretly believe that your bad habits or sins are simply 'written' and therefore unchangeable? How would rethinking that as a failure of your own will, not God's decree, change your next step?

Divine Decree
intermediate
5 min
Predetermination of Lifespan and Provision
Juwayni argues that lifespan and provision are predetermined in eternity.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's assertion that both lifespan and provision are predetermined in God's eternal knowledge, and how he supports this with scriptural evidence.

Core Idea
Lifespan and provision are set in eternity, known only to God.

Juwayni begins by establishing that lifespan (ajal) and provision (rizq) are not random or changeable in God's knowledge. He argues that God's foreknowledge necessitates their fixed occurrence, and that provision is universal for all creatures, believers and disbelievers alike. This lesson unpacks his initial claims and their scriptural basis.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Note that Juwayni uses two key terms: 'ajal' (term) and 'rizq' (provision).
Pay attention to the verse he quotes for provision.
2
Analyze Juwayni's argument for predetermination
Identify the logical flow: if God knows something will happen, it must happen; then he applies this to lifespan and provision.
3
Reflect on the universality of provision
Notice that Juwayni extends provision to both believer and disbeliever, citing the verse about all creatures.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وتحدث الجويني عن الآجال والأرزاق فقال إنهما محددان في الأزل فإذا علم الله تبارك وتعالى أن إنسانا سيقتل فلا بد من وقوع معلومه، وأن الله قسم للمؤمن رزقه، وقسم للكافر رزقه، من قبل أن يوجدا، وأطلق كلمة (الرزق) على المؤمن والكافر، للمفهوم من الآية «وما من دابة في الأرض إلا على الله رزقها» (والكافر دابة والمؤمن دابة). لو كان يجب أن يفرق بين أجل الشهداء، وأجل غير الشهداء فأجل الشهداء محدد في الأزل بقوله تعالى (قل لو كنتم في بيوتكم لبرز الذين كتب عليهم القتل إلى مضاجعهم) (آل عمران: ١٥٤) والمعنى: إن الله كتب قتل من يقتل من المؤمنين، وكتب مع ذلك أنهم الغالبون لعلمه أن العافية في الغلبة لهم وأن دين الإسلام يظهر على الدين كله وأن ما ينكبون من بعض الأوقات تمحيص لهم وترغيب في الشهادة، وحرصهم على الجهاد فتحصل الغلبة. وأجل غير الشهداء غير محدد في الأزل بقوله تعالى (وأنفقوا في سبيل الله ولا تلقوا بأيديكم إلى التهلكة) (البقرة: ١٩٥) والمعنى: النهي عن ترك الإنفاق في سبيل الله، لأنه سبب الهلاك، أو عن الإسراف في النفقة حتى يفقر نفسه ويضيع عياله، أو عن الاقبال والاخطار بالنفس، أو عن ترك الغزو الذي هو تقوية للعدو. ولذلك وجب قتل القاتل لأن في قتله حياة للناس وزجر لسولله نفسه عن القتل في قوله تعالى (ولكم في القصاص حياة) (البقرة: ١٧٩). وأجل غير الشهداء غير محدد أيضا بدليل المشاهد في الحياة من مخلوقات الله فإن إنسانا لو رأى ديكا وجدي ماعز وحصانا لقال إن الديك لو خلي بينه وبين أجله بحسب ما أودع الله في خلايا جسمه من ...
And Juwayni discussed the terms and provisions and said that they are determined in eternity. So when God, blessed and exalted, knows that a human will be killed, then His knowledge must occur. And that God divided the provision for the believer, and divided the provision for the disbeliever, before they existed. And he used the term 'provision' for both the believer and the disbeliever, based on the verse 'And there is no creature on earth except that its provision is upon God.' (Hud: 6) (And the disbeliever is a creature and the believer is a creature). If it were necessary to distinguish between the term of the martyrs and the term of non-martyrs, then the term of the martyrs is predetermined in eternity by His statement: 'Say, even if you were in your houses, those destined for slaughter would go forth to their resting places.' (Al-Imran: 154) The meaning: God wrote the killing of those who are killed among the believers, and also wrote that they are victorious, because He knows that the good outcome is their victory, and that the religion of Islam will prevail over all religion, and that whatever hardships befall them at times are a purification for them and an incentive for martyrdom, and their eagerness for jihad results in victory. And the term of non-martyrs is not predetermined in eternity by His statement: 'And spend in the way of God and do not throw yourselves into destruction.' (Al-Baqara: 195) The meaning: the prohibition of abandoning spending in the way of God because it is a cause of destruction, or overspending so that one impoverishes himself and neglects his dependents, or exposing oneself to danger, or abandoning fighting which strengthens the enemy. Therefore, it is obligatory to kill the killer because in his killing is life for the people and a deterrent for his soul from killing, in His statement: 'And in legal retribution there is life for you.' (Al-Baqara: 179) And the term of non-martyrs is also not predetermined, as evidenced by observation of the creation of God. For if a person sees a rooster, a goat kid, and a horse, he would say that the rooster, if left to its term according to what God has placed in the cells of its body of...
Source pages p. 119

Commentary
This passage is Juwayni's exposition on the predetermination of lifespan (ajal) and provision (rizq). He asserts that both are determined in eternity (muḥaddadān fī al-azal). The first point is based on divine foreknowledge: if God knows that a person will be killed, that knowledge must be realized. Then he discusses provision: God has already divided the provision for believers and disbelievers before their existence, using the verse 'There is no creature but its provision is upon God' to show that provision is common to all, not only believers. Then he contrasts the term of martyrs and non-martyrs. The term of martyrs is predetermined based on the verse about those destined for slaughter; the term of non-martyrs is not predetermined, as indicated by the prohibition of self-destruction and the command to spend. He also uses a rational observation: the natural lifespan of animals (like a rooster) is determined by their bodily constitution, implying that human lifespan can also be influenced by causes. This supports the idea that non-martyrs' terms are not rigidly fixed but subject to means (like caution, spending, etc.). However, Juwayni maintains that God's knowledge encompasses everything, so there is a sense of predetermination, but he distinguishes between the fixed term of martyrs and the conditional term of others.

Key Terms
الآجال
الأرزاق
الأزل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Predetermination means humans have no choice or responsibility.
Correction: Juwayni's view is about God's knowledge and decree, not about negating human actions; he later distinguishes between fixed and conditional terms, implying human actions play a role.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, when is provision predetermined?
A) After a person is born
B) At the moment of death
C) In eternity, before existence
D) Only for believers

Key Takeaway
Juwayni establishes that both lifespan and provision are fixed in God's eternal knowledge, without exception for believer or disbeliever.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If your lifespan is already determined in eternity, do you truly live each day as if it could be your last? Or do you assume tomorrow is guaranteed? What would change if you acted on this truth today?
?
God has already apportioned your provision before you were born. Why do you still fear scarcity? What does your worry reveal about your trust in God's decree?
?
The passage notes that even the disbeliever receives provision from God. How does this reality challenge your attitude towards those who reject faith? Do you see them as deserving of God's provision? What does that demand of you in your interactions with them?

Divine Decree
intermediate
6 min
The Term of Martyrs vs. Non-Martyrs
Juwayni distinguishes the fixed term of martyrs from the flexible term of non-martyrs.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's distinction between the fixed term of martyrs and the flexible term of non-martyrs, and how he uses Quranic verses and rational observation to support this.

Core Idea
Martyrs' term is set; non-martyrs' term can change based on actions.

After establishing general predetermination, Juwayni addresses a potential objection: if terms are fixed, why do martyrs die earlier? He argues that martyrs' terms are indeed fixed in eternity, but non-martyrs' terms are not strictly predetermined; they can be altered by factors like caution, spending, and divine retribution. This lesson examines his evidence and reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and identify the two categories
Read the Arabic passage and translation. Juwayni contrasts 'the term of martyrs' with 'the term of non-martyrs'.
2
Examine the verses for each category
For martyrs, he quotes 'Those destined for slaughter go forth to their resting places.' For non-martyrs, he quotes 'Do not throw yourselves into destruction' and 'In retribution there is life.'
3
Consider the rational proof for non-martyrs
Juwayni uses an analogy: a rooster's lifespan is determined by its nature, but it can be shortened by external causes. Similarly, a non-martyr's term is not fixed absolutely.
The passage cuts off here; the argument implies conditional predetermination.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

Misconception: If everything is predestined, then human actions like spending or fighting are pointless.

According to Juwayni, which group has a term that is not fixed in eternity?

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If your term is conditional upon your actions, what specific deed are you avoiding or delaying that might be shortening your life or depriving you of a good end?
?
Juwayni says martyrs' terms are fixed, while yours can change. Does the prospect of an uncertain term make you more eager to seek martyrdom, or does it comfort you that you still have time? Examine your heart: which motive is stronger?
?
The command 'do not throw yourselves into destruction' implies you are responsible for preserving your life. In what area of your life are you knowingly risking destruction—whether spiritual, physical, or financial—and what would it cost you to change that today?

Unseen Matters
intermediate
6 min
The Meaninglessness of Kasb and the Proof that Only Allah Creates
Juwayni refutes kasb as incoherent and proves sole divine creation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Juwayni dismisses the concept of kasb as incoherent and how he uses consensus, scripture, and reason to establish that only Allah creates.

Core Idea
No one can be a creator without full knowledge of their creation.

Juwayni takes on the controversial theory of kasb (acquisition), which attempts to attribute human action to both servant and God. He first exposes its emptiness by demanding a clear definition, then provides overwhelming evidence from early consensus, the Quran, and rational argument that creation belongs exclusively to Allah. This passage cuts to the heart of the qadar debate.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Start by reading the Arabic passage once without analyzing it. Then read the literal translation to get the overall flow of the argument.
Notice that Juwayni uses a challenging tone with the kasb proponent.
2
Identify the refutation of kasb
Find where Juwayni questions the meaning of 'kasb' and says the divisions are exhausted. Understand why he thinks the term is meaningless.
He asks 'What is kasb? What is its meaning?' and implies no answer can escape.
3
Observe the positive proof for divine creation
Read the part about early Muslim consensus, the Quranic verses (16:17, 6:102, 6:101, 35:3), and the rational argument. Notice how he combines authority and reason.
The rational argument: a creator must know details of creation; humans do not.
4
Reflect on the rational criterion
Consider why Juwayni insists that knowledge of a thing is nearer than its creation. How does this undermine the claim that humans are creators?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
من يزعم نیز وذلك أن قائلا لو قال : العبد مكتسب . وأثر قدرته الاكتساب، والرب تبارك وتعالى مخترع وخالق لما العبد مكتسب . قيل له فما الكسب ؟ وما معناه وأديرت الاقسام المقدرة (1) على هذا القائل ، فلا يجد عنها مهربا . فان قيل : لم لا تذكروا قولا مقنعا في الرد على أن العبد مخترع خالق لأفعاله ؟ قلنا : المسلمون بأجمع قاطبة قبل أن تظهر البدع والآراء ، وتبغ أصحاب الأهواء : على أنه لا خالق الا الله ( تعالى ) ، كما لهجوا بأنه لا اله الا الله ، وتمدح الله سبحانه وتعالى بالخلق فى أى من الكتاب منها قوله تبارك وتعالى : ( أفمن آی يخلق كمن لا يخلق ) ( النحل ۱۷ ) وقوله تبارك وتعالى : « خالق كل شى ( » ) الأنعام (۱۰۲ ) وقوله : « خلق كل شيء » ( الأنعام ۱۰۱ ) وقوله تبارك وتعالى : ( هل من خالق غير الله » ( فاطر (۳) ولا يشك لبيب أبن « من وصف نفسه بكونه خالقا على التحقيق فقد أعظم الفرية ) ( وأتي بما لو نطق به ناطق فى الأولين لتعرض للكبير العظيم ، والرد البليغ . وكيف ينصف العبد بكونه خالقا ) (۲) وهو لا يحيط علما بتفاصيل أفعاله ، ومن لا يعلم حقيقة ما صدر منه ، ومن يخط بمقداره ومبلغه كيف يكون خالقه ؟ والعلم بالشيء أقرب من خلقه . وهذا معنى
Who also claims? And that is if someone says: 'The servant is an acquisitor (kasib). The effect of his power is acquisition, and the Lord, blessed and exalted, is the inventor and creator of what the servant acquires.' It is said to him: 'What is kasb? What is its meaning?' And the possible divisions were circulated upon this claimant, and he finds no escape from them. If it is said: 'Why do you not mention a convincing statement in refuting the claim that the servant is an inventor and creator of his actions?' We say: The Muslims, all collectively, before the appearance of innovations and opinions and the distortion of the people of desires, agree that there is no creator but Allah, exalted, just as they continuously professed that there is no god but Allah. And Allah, glorified and exalted, praises Himself with creation in many verses of the Book, among them His saying, blessed and exalted: 'Then is He who creates like he who does not create?' (Surah An-Nahl 16:17), and His saying, blessed and exalted: 'Creator of everything' (Surah Al-An'am 6:102), and His saying: 'Created everything' (Surah Al-An'am 6:101), and His saying, blessed and exalted: 'Is there any creator other than Allah?' (Surah Fatir 35:3). And no intelligent person doubts that whoever describes himself as being truly a creator has committed the greatest falsehood and brought something that, if spoken by any speaker in the past, would have exposed him to great danger and decisive rejection. And how can the servant be described as a creator when he does not have comprehensive knowledge of the details of his actions? And whoever does not know the reality of what originated from him, and whoever errs in its measure and extent, how can he be its creator? And knowledge of a thing is nearer than its creation. And this is the meaning.
Source pages p. 45

Commentary
This passage contains two arguments. First, a refutation of the claim of 'kasb' (acquisition): if someone says the servant is an 'acquisitor' and his power merely 'acquires' while God creates, he is asked for the meaning of 'kasb' but cannot provide a coherent answer; all possible divisions are exhausted without escape. Second, a positive proof: the early Muslim consensus before innovations affirmed that there is no creator but Allah, as they professed 'there is no god but Allah'. Juwayni cites four Quranic verses that attribute creation exclusively to Allah (16:17, 6:102, 6:101, 35:3). Then he gives a rational argument: a creator must have comprehensive knowledge of the details of his creation, but humans do not know the full reality of their own actions (they often err in measure and extent). Knowledge of a thing is more immediate than its creation, so the lack of knowledge precludes being a creator. Thus, the claim that humans are creators is false.

Key Terms
كسب
خالق
مخترع
إجماع
العلم بالشيء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The doctrine of kasb provides a meaningful explanation of how human power and divine power both contribute to human actions.
Correction: Juwayni argues that the term 'kasb' is undefined and empty; when asked for its meaning, the proponent cannot give a coherent answer. The concept does not actually explain anything.

Checkpoint
What rational argument does Juwayni give to show that humans cannot be creators of their actions?
A) Humans are weak.
B) Humans do not have comprehensive knowledge of the details of their actions.
C) Humans are not eternal.
D) Humans cannot create from nothing.

Key Takeaway
The concept of kasb is incoherent because it lacks a clear meaning, and only Allah creates, as proven by consensus, Quranic verses, and the rational requirement that a creator must have perfect knowledge of his creation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever attribute your successes or achievements to your own cleverness or effort, forgetting that only Allah truly creates? Juwayni argues that you don't even fully know your own actions. How does that humble you, and what specific phrase (e.g., 'Masha Allah') will you consciously use today to redirect praise to the Creator?
?
Juwayni calls claiming to be a creator a 'great falsehood.' In your daily life, do you ever act as if you control outcomes—your future, your health, your reputation—as if you were the ultimate decider? What area of your life do you need to surrender to Allah's sole creative power, starting with a sincere dua acknowledging His exclusive role?
?
The early Muslims linked 'La ilaha illa Allah' with 'La khaliqa illa Allah.' Have you separated these in your heart? When something bad happens, do you react as if a human or circumstance created it, rather than seeing Allah's creation in it? Take a moment to repent for attributing evil to others' power, and commit to saying 'Allah created it' in your next trial.

Module 13 · Lesson 2

Unseen Matters
intermediate
6 min
Worship as Thank-Offering: Why Grace Cannot Be Repaid
Juwayni gives a second proof: worship as thank-offering cannot obligate reward.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's argument that acts of worship are a thank-offering for blessings received in this life, and therefore cannot serve as a basis for obligatory reward in the afterlife.

Core Idea
Worship repays nothing; it is a thank-you that cannot earn a wage.

This lesson presents a second proof from Imam al-Juwayni that reward is not obligatory upon God. The argument hinges on the nature of worship as a form of thankfulness (shukr) for blessings already given. Since the servant's deeds are already an exchange for those blessings, they cannot simultaneously be a means to earn further reward. This challenges the rationalist view that God must reward obedience.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the key claim: acts of worship are a thank-offering for blessings.
The author uses a rhetorical question to make his point.
2
Identify the key terms
Look for the words 'ibadat, ni'am, shukr, 'iwad. Understand how they relate: worship (ibadat) is a thank-offering (shukr) and exchange ('iwad) for blessings (ni'am).
These terms are central to the argument.
3
Reflect on the logic
Ask yourself: If worship is already an exchange for blessings, can it also be a basis for earning reward? Why or why not?
The answer leads to the conclusion that reward cannot be obligatory.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومما يقطع مادة كلامهم : أن العبادات التي يقيمها العبد لا تفي بالنعم التي تتوفر عليه من ربه ، ناجزا ، وهى تقع شكرا لأنعم الله تبارك وتعالى بل لا تفى بأقلها فاذا وقعت شكرا عوضا عما تعجل من نعم الله ، فكيف يستمر في حكم العقل استحقاق الثواب على أعمال وقعت عوضا عن نعيم توفاه العبد ؟ ثم قالوا : ليس على أهل الجنان شكر النعيمها . فانه عوض أعمال العبد ، وليس للمعوض عوض . فمن أضل سبيلا ممن يوجب على الله تبارك وتعالى ثواب أعمال العبد ، وهى عوض ما ينجز من النعم ، ولم يوجب على العبد ، شكر الثواب غدا لكونه عوضا ؟ ثم من زعم أن العقل يدل على استحقاق العقل بكفر ساعة الخلود في دركات النيران ، فقد ادعى فى مقتضى العقول محالا . هيهات ، برح الخفاء ، يحكم الله ما يريد ، ويفعل ما يشاء .
And among what cuts off the substance of their speech: that the acts of worship which the servant performs do not suffice for the blessings that come to him from his Lord, immediately, and they occur as thankfulness for the blessings of Allah, Blessed and Exalted; rather they do not suffice for the least of them. So when they occur as thankfulness in exchange for what he has received quickly of Allah's blessings, how can there persist in the judgment of reason the deserving of reward for deeds that occurred as exchange for bliss that the servant received? Then they said: The people of Paradise are not obligated to thank for its bliss, for it is an exchange for the servant's deeds, and there is no exchange for the exchange. So who is more astray than one who makes obligatory upon Allah, Blessed and Exalted, reward for the servant's deeds, while they are an exchange for the blessings that are granted, and does not make obligatory upon the servant thankfulness for the reward tomorrow because it is an exchange? Then whoever claims that reason indicates the deserving of punishment for an hour of disbelief of eternity in the depths of the Fire, he has claimed what is impossible according to the requirements of intellects. Alas! the concealment has been removed; Allah judges what He wills and does what He wishes.
Source pages p. 56

Commentary
This passage is a second proof that reward is not obligatory upon God, based on the nature of worship and blessings. Juwayni argues that the servant's acts of worship cannot repay even the least of God's blessings; rather they are a thank-offering (shukr) for those blessings received in this life. Since they are already an exchange ('iwad) for the blessings, they cannot also be grounds for earning a reward. He then cites a further argument: the people of Paradise do not owe thanks for its bliss because that bliss is itself an exchange for their deeds. So it would be inconsistent to require God to reward deeds that already serve as an exchange. Finally, he dismisses the idea that reason could obligate eternal punishment for an hour of disbelief as impossible. The conclusion is that God does as He wills; the concealment of the truth is removed.

Key Terms
عبادات
نعم
شكر
عوض
استحقاق الثواب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That Ash'aris deny reward entirely.
Correction: Ash'aris affirm reward as a promise from God, not as an obligation that reason can deduce. Juwayni is arguing against the notion of obligatory reward based on rational desert, not denying that God rewards.

Checkpoint
Why, according to this argument, can acts of worship not obligate reward?
A) Because acts of worship are few and insufficient.
B) Because they are already an exchange (thank-offering) for blessings received, so they cannot also earn a reward.
C) Because God does not promise reward for worship.
D) Because worship is only for this world.

Key Takeaway
Acts of worship are a thank-offering for blessings received, not a basis for obligating reward from God.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you perform acts of worship, do you ever catch yourself thinking, 'I've done my part, now God owes me'? How does Juwayni's argument challenge that assumption?
?
Consider the countless blessings you have already received today—breath, sight, faith. Can you truly claim that your salah or fasting repays even one of them? If not, what does that mean for your attitude in worship?
?
If worship is a thank-offering, not a wage-earning transaction, then what is your real motivation for obeying God? Are you content to worship out of sheer gratitude, or do you demand a reward?

The Inconsistency of Claiming Obligatory Reward and Punishment
Juwayni extends the exchange argument to paradise and punishment, concluding with God's absolute will.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's further argument that if deeds are exchange for blessings, then paradise is also exchange, and that eternal punishment for a moment of disbelief is irrational, leading to the absolute will of God.

Core Idea
If deeds are exchange, then paradise is also exchange — and eternal punishment for finite sin is absurd.

This lesson continues Juwayni's argument by drawing out the implications of the exchange view. He cites a companion point: the people of Paradise do not thank for its bliss because it is an exchange for their deeds. Then he highlights the inconsistency: those who obligate reward on God do not obligate thankfulness for reward. Finally, he dismisses the claim that reason obligates eternal punishment for an hour of disbelief as impossible. The conclusion is that God's will is absolute.

Guided Path
1
Read the second part of the passage
Start from 'Then they said' and read to the end. Notice the logical steps: paradise as exchange, inconsistency of obligation, and rejection of rational punishment.
The passage ends with a statement of God's absolute will.
2
Understand the parity argument
The author argues that if worship is exchange for blessings, then paradise (the reward) is exchange for worship. Hence, the people of Paradise do not need to thank for it.
This undermines the idea of a separate obligation.
3
Reflect on the punishment point
The final point targets those who claim reason obligates eternal punishment for finite sin. Juwayni calls this impossible and concludes that Allah judges as He wills.
This reinforces the Ash'ari position that God's actions are not subject to rational obligation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومما يقطع مادة كلامهم : أن العبادات التي يقيمها العبد لا تفي بالنعم التي تتوفر عليه من ربه ، ناجزا ، وهى تقع شكرا لأنعم الله تبارك وتعالى بل لا تفى بأقلها فاذا وقعت شكرا عوضا عما تعجل من نعم الله ، فكيف يستمر في حكم العقل استحقاق الثواب على أعمال وقعت عوضا عن نعيم توفاه العبد ؟ ثم قالوا : ليس على أهل الجنان شكر النعيمها . فانه عوض أعمال العبد ، وليس للمعوض عوض . فمن أضل سبيلا ممن يوجب على الله تبارك وتعالى ثواب أعمال العبد ، وهى عوض ما ينجز من النعم ، ولم يوجب على العبد ، شكر الثواب غدا لكونه عوضا ؟ ثم من زعم أن العقل يدل على استحقاق العقل بكفر ساعة الخلود في دركات النيران ، فقد ادعى فى مقتضى العقول محالا . هيهات ، برح الخفاء ، يحكم الله ما يريد ، ويفعل ما يشاء .
And among what cuts off the substance of their speech: that the acts of worship which the servant performs do not suffice for the blessings that come to him from his Lord, immediately, and they occur as thankfulness for the blessings of Allah, Blessed and Exalted; rather they do not suffice for the least of them. So when they occur as thankfulness in exchange for what he has received quickly of Allah's blessings, how can there persist in the judgment of reason the deserving of reward for deeds that occurred as exchange for bliss that the servant received? Then they said: The people of Paradise are not obligated to thank for its bliss, for it is an exchange for the servant's deeds, and there is no exchange for the exchange. So who is more astray than one who makes obligatory upon Allah, Blessed and Exalted, reward for the servant's deeds, while they are an exchange for the blessings that are granted, and does not make obligatory upon the servant thankfulness for the reward tomorrow because it is an exchange? Then whoever claims that reason indicates the deserving of punishment for an hour of disbelief of eternity in the depths of the Fire, he has claimed what is impossible according to the requirements of intellects. Alas! the concealment has been removed; Allah judges what He wills and does what He wishes.
Source pages p. 56

Commentary
This passage is a second proof that reward is not obligatory upon God, based on the nature of worship and blessings. Juwayni argues that the servant's acts of worship cannot repay even the least of God's blessings; rather they are a thank-offering (shukr) for those blessings received in this life. Since they are already an exchange ('iwad) for the blessings, they cannot also be grounds for earning a reward. He then cites a further argument: the people of Paradise do not owe thanks for its bliss because that bliss is itself an exchange for their deeds. So it would be inconsistent to require God to reward deeds that already serve as an exchange. Finally, he dismisses the idea that reason could obligate eternal punishment for an hour of disbelief as impossible. The conclusion is that God does as He wills; the concealment of the truth is removed.

Key Terms
أهل الجنان
شكر
عوض
كفر ساعة
خلود

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That reason can obligate God to punish disbelief eternally.
Correction: Juwayni argues that such a claim is impossible and that God's will is absolute; punishment is not obligated by reason.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, why are the people of Paradise not obligated to thank for its bliss?
A) Because they do not need to thank.
B) Because the bliss is an exchange for their deeds and there is no exchange for an exchange.
C) Because God does not want them to thank.
D) Because they are too busy enjoying.

Key Takeaway
If deeds are exchange for blessings, then paradise is exchange for deeds, making additional obligations inconsistent; God's will is absolute.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you look at your own good deeds, do you ever feel that God owes you something in return? What would change in your relationship with Him if you truly believed that even Paradise is purely a gift of His grace, not your right?
?
This passage argues that eternal punishment for a single moment of disbelief is 'impossible according to the requirements of intellect.' Does that thought make you more hopeful of His mercy, or does it challenge your concept of justice? How do you reconcile God's absolute will with your own sense of what is fair?
?
If reward is not an obligation, then every moment of your existence is sustained by God's free giving. How often do you live with that awareness, or do you quietly assume that your obedience puts Him in your debt? What would it mean for your prayers and worship to be offered entirely as a plea for mercy, not a demand for repayment?

Reward and Punishment: By Promise, Not Obligation
Juwayni answers the objection that reward and punishment obligate God.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's argument that reward and punishment are established by God's promise and threat, not by any obligation upon Him, using the analogy of a servant and master.

Core Idea
Reward is by God's promise, not by desert.

Juwayni now addresses a key objection to the Ash'ari position that nothing is obligatory upon God. If Shari'a mentions reward and punishment as recompense, does that not imply obligation? Juwayni responds with a clear distinction: reward and punishment are real, but they are based on God's faithful promise and threat, not on any debt or necessity. To illustrate, he gives a striking analogy of a servant serving a master.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection and answer
Read the Arabic passage and translation. Identify who is speaking – first the objector, then Juwayni's response.
Notice the objection uses 'فان قيل' (if it is said) – a common formula.
2
Understand the analogy
Extract the analogy of the servant and master. Compare the servant's service to the believer's worship. Note what the servant does not deserve.
The servant does not deserve emancipation, only maintenance.
3
Grasp the key distinction
Juwayni says reward is by promise, not desert. How does this preserve God's sovereignty? The servant becomes free by the master's word, not by his own work.
The analogy directly parallels reward by God's promise.
4
Reflect on the implication
Consider how this relates to the first pillar – that nothing is obligatory upon God. Reward and punishment are real but stem from God's own voluntary promise.
This upholds God's absolute freedom and power.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فان قيل : قد بنيتم الركن الأول على تقرير الشريعة قرارها ، واتباع مواردها . والثواب والعقاب فى الشرائع والملل ثابتان . وقد سماهما الله تبارك وتعالى : جزاء لأعمال العباد . قلنا : لسنا تنكرهما ، ولكنهما ثبتا وعدا الله . ووعده صدق ، وقوله حق . وهذا يبينه ضرب مثل يوضح ما تقدم من الكلام ، ويكشف هذا الابهام فنقول : اذا خدم العبد مولاه ، لم يستحق عليه أن يعتقه ويخلصه من أسر الرق وذل العبودية بل المقدار الذي تأسس الشرع عليه أن يكفيه مؤونته . ولا يكلفه من العمل الا ما يطيق . والثواب الخالد خالص من النصب والتعب ووصول الى الروح الأبدى وهو زائد على الحرية المزيلة للرق . فإذا كان العبد لا يستحق على مولاه وهو لا يألوا جهدا فى خدمته آناء الليل والنهار : العتق ، فكيف يستحق العبد على خالقه ومنشئه ورازقه بعباداته الخلاص السرمدى ؟ نعم او قال السيد لعبده : ان فعلت كذا وكذا فأنت حر . فاذا حقق العبد ما ذكره سيده عتق بقول سيده . لا بحكم استحقاق اقتضاه عمله . فكذلك الثواب ثابت قطعا بوعد الله تعالى ، والعقاب ثابت بوعيده .
If it is said: You have built the first pillar upon the establishment of the Shari'a and following its sources. And reward and punishment in the laws and religions are established. And Allah, Blessed and Exalted, has named them as recompense for the deeds of the servants. We say: We do not deny them, but they are established by the promise of Allah. And His promise is truth, and His word is true. And this is clarified by a similitude that explains what has preceded of the speech and removes this ambiguity. So we say: When a servant serves his master, he does not deserve from him that he should free him and release him from the captivity of bondage and the humiliation of servitude; rather the measure established by custom is that he should provide for his maintenance, and he does not burden him with work except what he can bear. And eternal reward is pure from toil and fatigue and reaching eternal life, and it exceeds the freedom that removes bondage. So if the servant does not deserve from his master – while he spares no effort in serving him day and night – emancipation, then how can the servant deserve from his Creator, Originator, and Provider, with his acts of worship, eternal salvation? Yes, or if the master said to his servant: If you do such and such, you are free. Then when the servant fulfills what his master mentioned, he becomes free by his master's word, not by a judgment of desert that his deed necessitated. Likewise, reward is established decisively by the promise of Allah Almighty, and punishment is established by His threat.
Source pages p. 57

Commentary
This passage is an objection-and-answer. The objector says: You (Ash'aris) have built your first pillar (the principle that nothing is obligatory upon God) on the basis of Shari'a, yet Shari'a itself mentions reward and punishment as recompense for deeds. So how can you deny that God is obligated to reward and punish? The answer: We do not deny reward and punishment; rather they are established by God's promise and threat, not by any obligation upon Him. Then Juwayni provides an analogy: a servant who serves his master does not deserve emancipation; at most he deserves maintenance. But if the master promises freedom for a specific act, then when the servant performs it, he becomes free by the master's word, not by desert. Similarly, reward is by God's promise, not by desert. The analogy illustrates that reward is a fulfillment of a promise, not a necessary recompense.

Key Terms
الركن الأول
الشريعة
الوعد
الوعيد
الخلاص السرمدي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think Ash'aris deny reward and punishment altogether because they say nothing is obligatory upon God.
Correction: Ash'aris affirm reward and punishment as real and certain, but they are based on God's promise and threat, not on any obligation or desert.

Checkpoint
According to the analogy of the servant and master, how does the servant become free?
A) By earning it through hard work.
B) By the master's promise, not by desert.
C) By legal right since he served enough.
D) By the master's generosity without any cause.

Key Takeaway
Reward and punishment are real and certain, but they are grounded in God's promise and threat, not in any right of the creature.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that your good deeds are not enough to earn you Paradise? If reward is only by promise, what is the basis of your hope?
?
This lesson says you do not deserve eternal salvation by your worship. Does that thought make you feel despair or relief? How should it change your reliance on your own actions?
?
If you have been relying on your deeds for salvation, what do you need to change today to place your trust solely in God's promise?

6 min
Reward and Punishment Based on God's Promise, Not Human Desert
Juwayni answers an objection about reward and punishment with a slave-master analogy.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why reward and punishment in Islam are based on God's promise and threat, not on human desert, and how the slave-master analogy clarifies this.

Core Idea
Reward and punishment are gifts of promise, not wages of deserts.

In this passage, Imam al-Juwayni responds to an objection that the Qur'an describes reward and punishment as recompense for deeds. He clarifies that the Ahl al-Sunna wholeheartedly affirm reward and punishment, but they are established by God's promise, not by any inherent right of the servant. Through a powerful slave-master analogy, he shows that eternal reward far exceeds anything a servant could deserve, and thus it can only be granted by divine promise.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without pausing. Then read the full English translation. Let the argument flow naturally before analyzing.
The passage is an objection-and-answer format with an analogy.
2
Identify the objection and Juwayni's response
Find the line where the opponent quotes the Quranic language of 'recompense for deeds' and then see how Juwayni begins his answer: 'We do not deny them...'
The opponent's point is based on the apparent meaning of verses.
3
Understand the slave-master analogy
Read the analogy carefully. Notice that the slave does not ‘deserve’ freedom even with perfect service. The master's duty is only maintenance. Then apply that to the Creator-servant relationship.
The conclusion is that if a slave cannot deserve freedom, then a servant of God cannot deserve Paradise.
4
Grasp the role of promise and threat
Juwayni explains that reward is established by God's promise, not desert. Punishment is by His threat. This is the doctrine of Ahl al-Sunna.
The final Quranic verse confirms this understanding.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فان قيل : قد بنيتم الركن الأول على تقرير الشريعة قرارها ، واتباع مواردها . والثواب والعقاب فى الشرائع والملل ثابتان . وقد سماهما الله تبارك وتعالى : جزاء لأعمال العباد . قلنا : لسنا تنكرهما ، ولكنهما ثبتا وعدا الله . ووعده صدق ، وقوله حق ، وهذا يبينه ضرب مثل يوضح ما تقدم من الكلام ، ويكشف هذا الإبهام . فنقول : اذا خدم العبد مولاه ، لم يستحق عليه أن يعتقه ويخلصه من أسر الرق وذل العبودية بل المقدار الذي تأسس الشرع عليه أن يكفيه مؤونته . ولا يكلفه من العمل الا ما يطيق . والثواب الخالد خالص من النصب والتعب ووصول الى الروح الأبدى وهو زائد على الحرية المزيلة للرق . فإذا كان العبد لا يستحق على مولاه وهو لا يألوا جهدا فى خدمته آناء الليل والنهار : العتق ، فكيف يستحق العبد على خالقه ومنشئه ورازقه بعباداته الخلاص السرمدى ؟ نعم او قال السيد لعبده : ان فعلت كذا وكذا فأنت حر . فاذا حقق العبد ما ذكره سيده عتق بقول سيده . لا بحكم استحقاق اقتضاه فكذلك الثواب ثابت قطعا بوعد الله تعالى ، والعقاب ثابت بعمله بوعيده . وهذا معنى قول السعداء فيما أخبر الله تبارك وتعالى عنهم وقالوا : الحمد لله الذى صدقنا وعده وأورثنا الأرض ) ( الزمر : ٧٤ ( فهذا مذهب أهل الحق في الثواب والعقاب
If it is said: 'You have built the first pillar on establishing the Sharia and following its sources. Reward and punishment in the revealed laws are established. God, blessed and exalted, has called them recompense for the deeds of servants.' We say: 'We do not deny them, but they are established by God's promise. His promise is truth, and His word is true. This is clarified by an example that explains the preceding speech and removes this ambiguity. We say: If a slave serves his master, he does not deserve that he should set him free and release him from the captivity of servitude and the humiliation of slavery; rather the measure established by the Sharia is that he should provide for his maintenance and not burden him with work beyond his capacity. Eternal reward is pure from hardship and toil, and reaching everlasting life, and it surpasses freedom that removes slavery. So if a slave does not deserve from his master (while he does not neglect effort in his service night and day) emancipation, how then does the slave deserve from his Creator, Originator, and Provider, through his acts of worship, eternal salvation? Nay, if the master says to his slave: 'If you do such-and-such, you are free,' and the slave fulfills what his master mentioned, he becomes free by his master's word, not by a judgment of desert that necessitated it. Similarly, reward is definitely established by God's promise, and punishment is established by his deed, by His threat. This is the meaning of the saying of the blessed ones, as God informed about them: 'Praise be to God who has fulfilled His promise to us and made us inherit the earth' (Az-Zumar 39:74). This is the doctrine of the people of truth concerning reward and punishment.
Source pages p. 57

Commentary
This passage is an objection-and-answer format. The opponent argues that the Ahl al-Sunna base their first pillar on revelation, yet reward and punishment are mentioned in the Sharia as recompense for deeds, implying they are deserved. Juwayni responds that they do not deny reward and punishment, but they are established by God's promise, not by any desert on the servant's part. To clarify, he gives an analogy: a slave who serves his master does not thereby deserve freedom; the master's obligation is only to provide maintenance and not overburden him. Eternal reward is far greater than freedom, so if a slave cannot even deserve freedom, how could he deserve eternal salvation? However, if the master promises freedom for a specified act, the slave attains freedom by the master's promise, not because he deserved it. Similarly, the reward of Paradise is granted by God's promise, not because the servant earned it. Punishment also comes by God's threat. Juwayni then quotes the Quranic verse where the blessed say: 'Praise be to God who has fulfilled His promise to us...' Thus, the correct doctrine is that reward and punishment are based on divine promise and threat, not on human desert.

Key Terms
ثواب
عقاب
وعد
استحقاق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That the Ahl al-Sunna deny reward and punishment altogether because they say they are not based on desert.
Correction: The Ahl al-Sunna affirm reward and punishment, but they base them on God's promise and threat, not on any inherent right of the servant. This preserves divine generosity and freedom.

Checkpoint
According to the slave-master example, what does the master owe the slave?
A) Freedom and emancipation
B) Maintenance and not overburdening him
C) Eternal reward
D) Nothing

Key Takeaway
Reward and punishment are based on God's promise and threat, not on any desert from human actions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever catch yourself feeling that God owes you Paradise because of your prayers or good deeds? How does the slave analogy expose that entitlement as spiritually dangerous? What would it mean to live today fully dependent on God's promise alone?
?
When you sin, do you fear punishment because you think you deserve it, or because God has threatened it? How does understanding punishment as a divine threat (not an automatic consequence) change the way you seek forgiveness and mercy?
?
The servant gets nothing but maintenance; eternal life is a gift beyond any service. In what area of your life are you still acting as if your efforts earn God's favor? Name one concrete way you can shift from a 'wage-earning' mindset to a 'promise-trusting' mindset starting today.

p. 75
Module 13 · Lesson 6

Unseen Matters
beginner
3 min
Transition to Sam'iyyat: Foundations of Faith
Juwayni transitions to revealed matters and outlines his plan to present auditory chapters

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Juwayni transitions from rational proofs of prophethood to the revealed doctrines (sam'iyyat) of faith.

Core Idea
Revelation builds on reason: sam'iyyat are accepted after prophethood is proven.

This passage marks a turning point in Juwayni's theological treatise. After establishing the rational proof of the Prophet's truthfulness, he now turns to matters known only through revelation. These 'auditory chapters' form the second major division of his work.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and notice its doxological opening
Read the Arabic passage once. Observe that Juwayni begins with praise and gratitude to Allah before stating his intention.
The Quranic verse (7:43) emphasizes divine guidance.
2
Identify the phrase 'auditory chapters from the foundations of faith'
Find the phrase 'فصولا سمعية من قواعد الايمان' and understand that these are doctrines known through revelation (sam'), not reason.
This signals the shift from rational theology to revealed theology.
3
Reflect on the prerequisite of belief in prophethood
Juwayni says he suffices with the one who hopes for these chapters after complete longing, with the obligation to believe in the Prophet's truthfulness. This shows that acceptance of revealed doctrines depends on prior belief in the messenger.
This establishes a methodological principle: reason proves the messenger, then the messenger conveys revelation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والحمد لله الذي هدانا لهذا وما كنا لنهتدي لولا أن هدانا الله (الأعراف: ٤٣) وقيت الموعودة، ولو وقفت عند انجازها لكان فيما قدمته أكمل مقنع. ولكنني بعدما أتيت بالواضحة على صدق سيد الأولين والآخرين، فأرسم فصولا سمعية من قواعد الايمان. واكتفى بالمؤمل لها بعد الاشتياق التام بوجوب اعتقاد صدقه، فنعقد بابا يحوى قصة قديم اعتقادها الايمان من.
And praise be to Allah who guided us to this, and we would not have been guided unless Allah guided us (Al-A'raf: 43). The promised protection [has been granted], and if I had stopped at its fulfillment, what I have presented would be a complete persuasion. But after I have brought clear proof of the truthfulness of the master of the first and the last, I will outline auditory chapters (fusulan sam'iyya) from the foundations of faith. And I suffice with the one who hopes for them, after complete longing, with the obligation to believe in his truthfulness. So we will open a chapter containing a story, ancient is its belief from faith.
Source pages p. 75

Commentary
This passage is a transitional doxology in which Juwayni praises Allah for guidance and then declares his intention to move from rational proofs of prophethood to the revealed (sam'iyya) matters of faith. He states that after establishing the clear proof (al-wadiha) of the truthfulness of the Prophet Muhammad (SAW), the master of all prophets, he will now present 'auditory chapters' (fusulan sam'iyya) from the foundations of faith (qawa'id al-iman). These are doctrines known through revelation (sam') rather than reason alone. He emphasizes the necessity of believing in the Prophet's truthfulness as a prerequisite for accepting these revealed teachings. The mention of 'a chapter containing a story, ancient is its belief from faith' likely refers to a specific doctrine, possibly the story of the Miraj or something similar, though the text is unclear. Overall, this paragraph marks the beginning of the section on sam'iyyat.

Key Terms
فصول سمعية
قواعد الإيمان
الواضحة
باب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that sam'iyyat can be known by reason alone.
Correction: Juwayni emphasizes they are revealed after proof of prophethood, so reason alone cannot access them.

Checkpoint
What does Juwayni plan to present after proving the truthfulness of the Prophet?
A) Rational proofs of God's existence
B) Auditory chapters (fusulan sam'iyya) from the foundations of faith
C) A detailed biography of the Prophet
D) Arguments against heretics

Key Takeaway
Juwayni's transition from rational proof to revealed doctrines shows the structure of Islamic theology: reason establishes the messenger, revelation provides the message.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you hear a hadith about the unseen, do you pause to recall that it comes through a prophet whose truthfulness you have rationally verified? Or have you let your faith become a set of habits? This week, before each prayer, mentally affirm one rational proof of prophethood and then recite a short sam'iyyat belief with conviction.
?
Juwayni says he 'suffices with the one who hopes for them, after complete longing, with the obligation to believe in his truthfulness.' Do you feel that same longing for revealed knowledge? Or are you content with what you already know? Commit to reading one chapter on a sam'iyyat topic (e.g., the Bridge or the Balance) this weekend as if hearing it from the Prophet himself.
?
Do you approach the revealed teachings of Islam with the same seriousness as rational arguments? Or do you accept them casually because you were raised with them? Identify one sam'iyyat belief you take for granted and study its textual evidence this evening as though you are discovering it for the first time.

The Principle of Certain Knowledge from a Truthful Report
Juwayni states the condition for certain knowledge from reports.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the foundational principle that a report from a truthful informant about a possible thing yields necessary knowledge.

Core Idea
A truthful report about the possible yields certain knowledge.

Juwayni opens the chapter on sami'iyyat (matters known through revelation) by establishing a key epistemological principle: if one knows the informant is truthful and the content is possible, the report produces certain knowledge. This principle underpins the acceptance of prophetic reports.

Guided Path
1
Read the opening passage
Read the Arabic and translation carefully. Notice the three conditions: truthful informant, report about a possible thing, and the resulting knowledge.
The word 'mumkin' (possible) is crucial here.
2
Identify the three conditions
List the three conditions Juwayni mentions for certain knowledge: (1) the informant's truthfulness is established, (2) the report is about a possible being, (3) the report is within God's power.
3
Reflect on why this matters
Consider why Juwayni starts with this principle. It grounds the entire chapter on revealed knowledge in a rational foundation.
This connects to the broader kalam project of harmonizing reason and revelation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
باب في السمعيات من ثبت صدق لهجته اذا أخبر عن كائن ممكن حصل العلم به لا محالة . لأن الخبر عنه ممكن ، مقدور الله سبحانه ، والمخبر صادق ثم من أسرار الدين - وهو على منصبه - أن يعلم المنثبت : أن المعلومات تنقسم الى العقليات ، والسمعيات فما كان معقولا وجد العاقل له ثلجا فى نفسه .. وانشراحا في قلبه وما تلقاه من السمع فهو غير مرتاب فيه ، ولكنه لا يجد من نفسه الثلج الذى يجده من المعقولات . فان المخبر كان صادقا فالمصدق فيه مقلد ، ولن يبلغ العالم عن تقليد الصادق مبلغ من أدرك الشيء بعقله . وانما ذكرت هذا حتى اذا وجد الموجد نفسه في السمعيات دون وجدانه نفسه في العقليات . لا يتهم إيمانه ولا يشك فى ايقانه . ثم ما يقتضيه الدين القويم والمنهج المستقيم : أن كل ما نقل عن النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم بطرق صحيحة ، مرتضاة عند أهل الاثبات ، وكان ممكنا غير مستحيل ، فان كان النقل تواترا ، علم قطعا على حد العلم بالسمعيات . وان نقل آحادا ثبت ذلك المظنون فى مأثور الأخبار .. وتلقى بالقبول . ولم يعارض بالاستبعاد فان الاستبعاد فيما هذا سبيله من شيم المرتانين في الدين .
Chapter on the Audible Things (al-sami'iyyat): Whoever has established the truthfulness of his speech, when he reports about a possible (mumkin) being, knowledge of it necessarily occurs. Because the report about it is possible, within the power (maqdur) of God, and the informant is truthful. Then, from the secrets of the religion - and he is upon his station - is that the verifier knows that information is divided into rational (al-'aqliyyat) and audible (al-sami'iyyat). So what is rational, the rational person finds for it a coolness (thalj) in his soul and expansiveness in his heart. And what he receives from the audible, he is not doubtful about it, but he does not find from himself the coolness that he finds from the rational things. For if the informant was truthful, then the one who believes him is a follower (muqallid), and the scholar (al-'alim) from following the truthful will never reach the level of one who perceives a thing by his intellect. And I only mentioned this so that when one finds himself in the audible things without finding himself in the rational things, he does not accuse his faith nor doubt his certainty. Then, what the upright religion and the straight path require: that everything reported from the Prophet ﷺ by sound chains, approved by the people of affirmation (ahl al-ithbat), and being possible not impossible, then if the transmission is mutawatir, it is known with certainty like the knowledge of the audible things. And if it is ahad, then that probability (al-maznun) is established in the transmitted reports and is accepted with acceptance, and it is not opposed by improbability (al-istib'ad), for improbability in what is of this way is from the traits of apostates in the religion.
Source pages p. 76

Commentary
This passage introduces the chapter on sami'iyyat (matters known through revelation). Juwayni begins with a principle: if one knows that a speaker is truthful, then a report from that speaker about a possible thing yields certain knowledge. The report is possible (mumkin) and within God's power (maqdur), and the informant is truthful, so the knowledge is necessary. Then he reveals a secret of the religion: knowledge is divided into rational ('aqliyyat) and revealed (sami'iyyat). Rational matters provide a rational person with a sense of coolness (thalj) and expansiveness (inshirah) in the heart. Revealed matters, though not doubted, do not produce that same internal coolness from oneself. This is because in revealed matters one relies on the truthfulness of the informant and is thus a muqallid (follower). A person who learns through taqlid of a truthful person never reaches the level of one who grasps something with his own intellect. Juwayni mentions this to reassure believers: if they find themselves experiencing less internal satisfaction with revealed matters than with rational ones, they should not accuse their faith or doubt their certainty. Then he states the requirements of the correct religion: everything reported from the Prophet through sound chains, approved by ahl al-ithbat, and being possible (not impossible) must be accepted. If the transmission is mutawatir (massively widespread), it yields certain knowledge like that of sense-perception (al-sami'iyyat itself). If it is ahad (single-chained), then it establishes probability (al-maznun) and is to be accepted without opposing it by deeming it unlikely (istib'ad). Such deeming unlikely is a trait of apostates (al-murtaddin) in religion.

Key Terms
السمعيات
الممكن
المقدور
الصادق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that knowledge from reports is always inferior to direct experience and never certain.
Correction: Juwayni shows that under specific conditions (truthful informant, possible content), a report yields certain knowledge equal to direct experience.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what three elements produce necessary knowledge from a report?
A) Truthful informant, impossible content, divine power
B) Truthful informant, possible content, divine power
C) Reliable chain, possible content, human reason
D) Multiple chains, impossible content, divine power

Key Takeaway
A report from a truthful informant about a possible thing yields certain knowledge, forming the basis for accepting revealed truths.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When the Prophet ﷺ informs you of something from the unseen, do you receive it with the same certainty you would have from witnessing it yourself? What is holding you back from that level of iman?
?
Juwayni conditions knowledge on the possibility of the reported thing. Is there any matter in the Quran or Sunnah that you secretly deem impossible in your heart, even though you know Allah is all-powerful? How can you train your soul to submit to the fact that everything Allah reports is possible for Him?
?
If you truly believed that every truthful report from the Prophet ﷺ gives you certain knowledge, how would your daily actions change? Would you hesitate less in obeying commands like hijab, prayer, or charity? Decide one specific command you will act upon with full certainty today.

Division of Knowledge: Rational and Revealed
Juwayni divides knowledge and addresses the believer's inner experience.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni divides knowledge into rational and revealed, and why the psychological experience differs without affecting certainty.

Core Idea
Rational knowledge brings coolness; revealed knowledge brings certainty without that feeling.

Juwayni reveals a 'secret of the religion': knowledge is divided into two types—rational ('aqliyyat) and revealed (sami'iyyat). While rational truths produce a sense of coolness and expansiveness in the heart, revealed truths, though equally certain, do not evoke that same internal sensation. This is to reassure believers who might otherwise doubt their faith.

Guided Path
1
Read the division passage
Read from 'Then, from the secrets of the religion' through 'he does not accuse his faith nor doubt his certainty.'
Pay attention to the imagery of 'coolness' (thalj) and 'expansiveness' (inshirah).
2
Compare the two types
Identify the characteristics of rational knowledge vs. revealed knowledge as described by Juwayni. Rational knowledge gives internal satisfaction; revealed knowledge does not, but is not doubted.
Juwayni explains this difference by noting that in revealed matters, one is a muqallid (follower) of the truthful informant.
3
Understand the reassurance
Juwayni's purpose is to comfort believers who may feel less internal satisfaction with revealed matters. Their certainty should not be questioned.
This addresses a common psychological struggle.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

And what he receives from the audible, he is not doubtful about it, but he does not find from himself the coolness that he finds from the rational things. For if the informant was truthful, then the one who believes him is a follower (muqallid), and the scholar (al-'alim) from following the truthful will never reach the level of one who perceives a thing by his intellect. And I only mentioned this so that when one finds himself in the audible things without finding himself in the rational things, he does not accuse his faith nor doubt his certainty. Then, what the upright religion and the straight path require: that everything reported from the Prophet ﷺ by sound chains, approved by the people of affirmation (ahl al-ithbat), and being possible not impossible, then if the transmission is mutawatir, it is known with certainty like the knowledge of the audible things. Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believers think that if they do not feel intellectual satisfaction with revealed doctrines, their faith is weak or invalid.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what psychological difference exists between rational and revealed knowledge?
A) Rational knowledge is doubted, revealed knowledge is not.
B) Rational knowledge provides coolness and expansiveness, revealed knowledge does not.
C) Revealed knowledge provides more satisfaction than rational knowledge.
D) Both produce the same internal feeling.

Key Takeaway
The lack of internal satisfaction with revealed knowledge does not undermine its certainty; faith remains valid.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Recall a time when you felt a deep intellectual satisfaction from understanding a rational proof in Islam (e.g., an argument for God's existence). Now recall a revealed matter (e.g., the details of Paradise) that you accept without that same feeling. Has this difference ever caused you to doubt your faith? According to Juwayni, what must you remind yourself? Will you commit to not letting the lack of emotional warmth weaken your certainty?
?
Juwayni says the one who accepts revelation on authority (muqallid) does not reach the level of one who perceives by intellect. This might sound like a shortcoming. But is it? When you accept the news of the Unseen from the Prophet (peace be upon him), are you content to be a muqallid, or do you seek to strengthen your certainty through rational reflection? What one specific step will you take this week to move from mere taqlid to deeper tahqiq in a revealed matter?
?
Juwayni's classification implies that Allah deliberately made some matters knowable through reason and others only through revelation. Why do you think Allah chose to communicate certain truths through hearing from a human messenger rather than through your own intellect? Does this humble you or irritate you? How does your answer reveal your true attitude toward divine wisdom? What will you do today to honor the means of revelation?

Rules for Accepting Prophetic Reports
Juwayni details the treatment of prophetic reports.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni classifies transmitted reports into mutawatir and ahad, and why rejecting ahad reports based on improbability is a sign of apostasy.

Core Idea
Mutawatir yields certainty; ahad yields probability, both to be accepted.

Juwayni now lays down the requirements of the 'upright religion' for handling reports from the Prophet. He specifies conditions: sound chains, approval of ahl al-ithbat, and possibility of content. Then he distinguishes between mutawatir (massively transmitted) and ahad (single-chain) reports, and warns against rejecting the latter simply because they seem improbable.

Guided Path
1
Read the final passage
Read from 'Then, what the upright religion requires' to the end. Note the conditions and the two types of transmission.
The phrase 'people of affirmation' (ahl al-ithbat) refers to reliable hadith scholars.
2
Distinguish mutawatir and ahad
Identify how Juwayni treats each: mutawatir gives certain knowledge like the sami'iyyat themselves; ahad gives probability (al-maznun) and must be accepted without opposition.
3
Understand the warning
Juwayni condemns rejecting ahad reports due to istib'ad (improbability), calling it a trait of apostates. Reflect on why this is serious.
Istib'ad here means deeming something unlikely based on personal opinion.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

Misconception & Correction

Correction: Juwayni rules that ahad reports are to be accepted and not opposed by improbability; doing so is a sign of apostasy.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the status of ahad reports?
A) They are rejected if improbable.
B) They yield certain knowledge like mutawatir.
C) They yield probability and must be accepted without opposing by improbability.
D) They are only accepted if supported by rational proof.

Key Takeaway
Both mutawatir and ahad reports are authoritative; rejecting ahad reports due to personal improbability is apostasy.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Are there any teachings of the Prophet ﷺ that you have personally dismissed or downplayed because they seemed unlikely or hard to fit into your worldview? Reflect now: if you are rejecting a sound hadith because of your own sense of improbability, what does Juwayni's warning mean for you? Will you repent and submit to the authority of the revelation, or will you persist in a trait of apostasy?
?
Think of a specific report (from hadith or sira) that you or people around you find 'hard to believe'—perhaps about the unseen, like the description of Paradise or Hell, or the signs of the Hour. Have you ever felt embarrassment or hesitation in affirming it? Juwayni says such hesitation is not from the traits of the believers but from apostates. Will you now consciously train your heart to accept such reports with full submission, even if your limited mind cannot fully grasp them?
?
Juwayni establishes that a report must be 'possible not impossible' to be accepted. But what about reports that seem possible yet your ego resists because they demand surrender of control? For example, reports about predestination, or about rewards and punishments that challenge your sense of fairness. In which areas of your life do you feel the tug of 'istib'ad'—rejecting a truth because it feels improbable to your nafs? Will you take a concrete step today to study that report with a scholar or companion to remove your resistance?
