The Decisive Text: Allah's Forgiveness and the Hadith on Faith
Juwayni appeals to Quran 4:116 and a hadith to affirm forgiveness for sinful believers.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni uses Quran 4:116 and the hadith about a grain's weight of faith to argue that disobedient believers may be forgiven or interceded for, and thus eternal punishment is not certain for them.

Core Idea
Allah forgives any sin except shirk, and no believer with faith will remain in Hell forever.

Having offered two interpretations of Quran 4:93, Juwayni now presents a decisive text from Quran 4:116 that explicitly says Allah forgives everything less than shirk for whom He wills. Combined with the hadith that whoever has faith in his heart will eventually leave Hell, this establishes the Ash'ari position that disobedient believers are not eternally damned.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage from 'النص القاطع' to the end
This passage contains the core argument from Quran 4:116 and the hadith.
2
Understand the argument from 4:116
The verse says Allah forgives anything less than shirk for whom He wills. Juwayni argues this is not conditional on repentance.
If it required repentance, the distinction between shirk and other sins would be incoherent, because shirk is also forgiven upon repentance.
3
Note the conclusion about sinful believers
The fate of a believer who dies without repenting is 'concealed': Allah may forgive, intercession may occur, or temporary punishment then deliverance.
4
Reflect on the hadith
The Prophet said no one with a grain's weight of faith will remain in Hell forever, confirming eventual salvation for believers.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد تمسكوا بأى من القرآن، فمن أظهرها عندهم ، قوله تبارك وتعالى : « ومن يقتل مؤمنا متعمدا ، فجزاؤه جهنم خالدا فيها ) ) النساء : ٩٣) وعلى ظاهر هذه الآية وجوه من الكلام ، ونحن تؤثر منها وجهين . أحدهما : ما روى عن ابن عباس (١) رضى الله عنهما أنه قال : معناه » ومن يقتل مؤمنا مستحلا قتله ( ويشهد لذلك : أن العمد أنما يتمحض مين يقدم على الشيء اقداما لا اعتقد يزعه عنه وازع . ومن أن القتل من أكبر الكبائر ، فقد يدعوه اليه هواه ، ويزعه ايمانه عنه فيقدم رجلاً مشفقا ، والعامد حقاً هو الذي لا وازع له في رأيه والدليل عليه : أنه تبارك وتعالى ذكر في آيات القصاص أحكامه ، وصدره بقلب الأيمان ، وأثبت للقاتل اسم الأخ ، آخذا من أخوة الايمان ، وندب إلى العفو عنيه ، ولم يتعرض للوعيد ولم يذكر في آية الوعيد حكم القصاص البتة، فهذا وجه . والثانى : قوله تبارك وتعالى : « خالدا فيها ( ظاهر فى التأييد ، ولا يبعد حمله على الآماد الطوال . وان كانت تنتهى . وقد تجرى فى مكالمة الملوك وتحياتهم: الدعاء بالخلود اذ يقول القائل : خلد الله ملك الملك ، ولو عنوا به تأييداً لزجروا عن سؤال المحال ، والنص القاطع في وعد الله تبارك وتعالى : التجاوز عن المدنيين وهو قوله تبارك وتعالى يا الله الله لا يغفر أن يشرك به ، ويغفر ما دون ذلك لمن يشاء ( النساء ١١٦ ( مان. ولم يرد تعالى أنه يغفر لمن تاب ، فأنه ولو أراد ذلك لما انتظم الفرق بين الشرك وغيره . والشرك مغفور له اذا تاب . فادر من مات من عصاة أهل الأيمان من غير توبة ، فأمره مغيب ان شاء ان شاء الله غفر له . أو شفع فيه شفيع، وأن شاء عرضه على النار بقدر ذنبة ، لم عاقبته الفوز الأكبر، والنجاة . قال رسول الله صلى الله عليه وآله وآله وسلم : « لا يبقى فى النار من قلبه مثقال ذرة من الايمان

According to Juwayni, what does Quran 4:116 show about forgiveness for sins other than shirk?

?

?

?

The Obligation and Two Definitions of Repentance
Juwayni states obligation and presents two definitions.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the consensus on the obligation of repentance and the two main scholarly definitions of its reality.

Core Idea
Repentance is obligatory, but scholars differ on its essential nature: composite or single.

Juwayni begins by establishing the obligatory nature of repentance by consensus. He then presents two major positions among scholars regarding what repentance truly is. This lesson will help you grasp these foundational views before Juwayni presents his own.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then read the literal translation to grasp Juwayni's presentation.
2
Identify the obligation statement
Notice that Juwayni asserts repentance is obligatory by consensus. Mark this starting point.
The word 'واجبة' indicates obligation.
3
Distinguish the two views
The passage presents two definitions: one with three pillars (regret, desistance, resolve) and another equating repentance with regret alone. Find each and note their differences.
The second view is introduced by 'وقال آخرون'.
4
Understand the critique of the second view
Juwayni points out that a persistent person cannot be truly regretful, nor can one who resolves to return. Reflect on why this critique matters.
This leads into Juwayni's own view in the next lesson.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فصل في أحكام التوبة
التوبة واجبة باجماع الأمة على كل من عصى ربه . واختلفت عبارات الأئمة في حقيقة التوبة . فقال قائلون : التوبة عبارة تحوي أركانا ، أحدها : الندم على ما سلف من الذنوب . والثاني : الانكفاف عن العصيان . والثالث : التزام العزم على ترك معاودته . وقال آخرون التوبة هي الندم بعينه ، ثم انه يقتضي حلا لعقد الاصرار ، وعزما . فان المصر على الشيء لا يكون نادما على الحقيقة ، وكذلك العازم على المعاودة لا يكون نادما . والذي أراه في حقيقة التوبة ما أبديه الآن : فالتوبة : الرجوع . من قولهم : تاب وأناب ، اذا رجع . ولكن ليس الرجوع الى الطاعة من غير صفة تتعلق بالذنب توبة [؟] فأقول : العارف تعتريه اغفال وذهول وانهماك في شهوات عندما [؟] ، فاذا عاد سطوع المعرفة دائما فهو عودة وتوبة .. وهذه الحالة توجب لا محالة ندما وعزما ، وحلا لعقد الاصرار ، وحزنا على ما تقدم وتأسفا وتمنيا أن لو لم يكن فعل . والتوبة رجوع العبد الى حقيقة حضور الذهن في المعرفة ، واليه أشار رسول الله صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم : اذ قال : « لا يزني الزاني حين يزني وهو مؤمن . أراد لو كان على حضور عرفانه ، لما زنى . ولكنه سها فعصى ، كما ينسى الصائم صومه فيأكل
Chapter on the Rulings of Repentance. Repentance is obligatory by consensus of the Community upon everyone who has disobeyed his Lord. The expressions of the imams concerning the reality of repentance have differed. Some said: repentance is an expression comprising pillars: one of them is regret for what has passed of sins; the second is desisting from disobedience; the third is firmly resolving not to return to it. Others said: repentance is regret itself, and then it necessitates the dissolution of the contract of persistence and a resolve. For the one who persists in a thing is not truly regretful, and likewise the one who resolves to return is not regretful. What I see concerning the reality of repentance is what I will now set forth: repentance is returning, from their saying 'he repented and turned back' when he returned. But returning to obedience without a quality related to the sin is not repentance [?]. So I say: the knower ('ārif) is overtaken by heedlessness, distraction, and immersion in desires when [؟], and when the illumination of knowledge returns to him he is returning and repenting. This state necessarily necessitates regret, resolve, dissolution of the contract of persistence, grief over what has passed, remorse, and wishing that he had not done it. Repentance is the servant's return to the reality of presence of mind in knowledge, and the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family) alluded to it when he said: 'The adulterer does not commit adultery while he is a believer' – he meant that if he were present in his knowledge, he would not commit adultery, but he forgot and disobeyed, just as the fasting person forgets his fast and eats.
Source pages p. 92

Commentary
This passage is a doctrinal exposition on the nature and rulings of repentance (tawba). Juwayni begins by asserting the obligation of repentance by consensus. He then presents two classical positions: some define repentance as a composite of three pillars – regret, desistance, and resolve; others equate it with regret alone, which then entails abandoning persistence and forming a resolve. Juwayni finds both incomplete and offers his own view: repentance is fundamentally 'return', but not simply a return to obedience; it must be accompanied by a quality related to the sin. He then elaborates: a true knower (ʿārif) may be overtaken by heedlessness and desires, but when the illumination of knowledge returns, that return constitutes repentance. This state necessarily produces regret, resolve, dissolution of persistence, grief, remorse, and wish not to have sinned. Finally, he defines repentance as the servant's return to the reality of presence of mind in knowledge, citing the hadith that a believer does not commit adultery while in a state of faith – interpreted as being present in knowledge; forgetfulness leads to sin, just as a fasting person may forget and eat. Juwayni thereby integrates cognitive presence (ḥuḍūr al-dhihn fī al-maʿrifa) as the core of repentance.

Key Terms
توبة (tawba)
ندم (nadam)
إصرار (iṣrār)
عزم (ʿazm)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Repentance is merely verbal confession or feeling sorry without any specific components.
Correction: The passage shows repentance has at least regret, desistance, and resolve according to one view, and regret alone according to another, but both involve internal states beyond speech.

Checkpoint
According to the first definition presented by Juwayni, how many pillars does repentance comprise?
A) Two
B) Three
C) Four
D) One

Key Takeaway
Repentance is obligatory by consensus, but its reality is debated between a three-pillar definition and a single-core of regret.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Think of a sin you have committed repeatedly. Do you truly regret it, or have you become accustomed to feeling regret without change? Today, as you read this lesson, what specific step will you take to desist from that sin and resolve not to return?
?
The passage says the one who persists in sin is not truly regretful. Is there a sin you are persisting in right now? Do not hide behind 'I feel bad about it' – ask yourself: have I actually stopped? And if not, what is stopping you from making sincere repentance now?
?
Repentance is obligatory by consensus. Have you checked your own repentance today? Not just a general 'Astaghfirullah', but have you identified a specific sin, regretted it, desisted, and resolved not to return? Write down one concrete resolution and commit to it before Allah.

Unseen Matters
intermediate

Juwayni's Definition of Repentance as Return to Knowledge
Juwayni defines repentance as return to knowledge and presents its consequences.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's unique definition of repentance as the servant's return to presence of mind in knowledge, and how it encompasses regret, resolve, and remorse.

Core Idea
Repentance is not just regret; it is the return of the knower to the illumination of knowledge after heedlessness.

After presenting the classical views, Juwayni offers his own definition: repentance is fundamentally 'return,' but a return to knowledge and presence of mind. He elaborates on how this state necessarily produces all the emotions and resolutions associated with repentance, and he supports it with the hadith about the adulterer.

Guided Path
1
Read Juwayni's definition
Read the Arabic from 'والذي أراه' to the end, then the translation. Notice that Juwayni begins by linking repentance to 'return' (رجوع).
The word 'تاب' means to return.
2
Understand 'return' in context
Juwayni clarifies that returning to obedience alone is not enough; it must be a return of the illumination of knowledge after a period of heedlessness.
This is the core of his unique view.
3
Identify the consequences of this return
The return of knowledge necessarily produces regret, resolve, dissolution of persistence, grief, remorse, and wish not to have sinned. List them from the passage.
These are natural outcomes, not separate pillars.
4
Connect to the hadith
Juwayni cites the Prophet's saying about the adulterer not committing adultery while believing. Understand how this supports his definition: forgetfulness leads to sin; presence of knowledge prevents it.
This hadith is used to illustrate the role of cognitive presence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فصل في أحكام التوبة
التوبة واجبة باجماع الأمة على كل من عصى ربه . واختلفت عبارات الأئمة في حقيقة التوبة . فقال قائلون : التوبة عبارة تحوي أركانا ، أحدها : الندم على ما سلف من الذنوب . والثاني : الانكفاف عن العصيان . والثالث : التزام العزم على ترك معاودته . وقال آخرون التوبة هي الندم بعينه ، ثم انه يقتضي حلا لعقد الاصرار ، وعزما . فان المصر على الشيء لا يكون نادما على الحقيقة ، وكذلك العازم على المعاودة لا يكون نادما . والذي أراه في حقيقة التوبة ما أبديه الآن : فالتوبة : الرجوع . من قولهم : تاب وأناب ، اذا رجع . ولكن ليس الرجوع الى الطاعة من غير صفة تتعلق بالذنب توبة [؟] فأقول : العارف تعتريه اغفال وذهول وانهماك في شهوات عندما [؟] ، فاذا عاد سطوع المعرفة دائما فهو عودة وتوبة .. وهذه الحالة توجب لا محالة ندما وعزما ، وحلا لعقد الاصرار ، وحزنا على ما تقدم وتأسفا وتمنيا أن لو لم يكن فعل . والتوبة رجوع العبد الى حقيقة حضور الذهن في المعرفة ، واليه أشار رسول الله صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم : اذ قال : « لا يزني الزاني حين يزني وهو مؤمن . أراد لو كان على حضور عرفانه ، لما زنى . ولكنه سها فعصى ، كما ينسى الصائم صومه فيأكل
Chapter on the Rulings of Repentance. Repentance is obligatory by consensus of the Community upon everyone who has disobeyed his Lord. The expressions of the imams concerning the reality of repentance have differed. Some said: repentance is an expression comprising pillars: one of them is regret for what has passed of sins; the second is desisting from disobedience; the third is firmly resolving not to return to it. Others said: repentance is regret itself, and then it necessitates the dissolution of the contract of persistence and a resolve. For the one who persists in a thing is not truly regretful, and likewise the one who resolves to return is not regretful. What I see concerning the reality of repentance is what I will now set forth: repentance is returning, from their saying 'he repented and turned back' when he returned. But returning to obedience without a quality related to the sin is not repentance [?]. So I say: the knower ('ārif) is overtaken by heedlessness, distraction, and immersion in desires when [؟], and when the illumination of knowledge returns to him he is returning and repenting. This state necessarily necessitates regret, resolve, dissolution of the contract of persistence, grief over what has passed, remorse, and wishing that he had not done it. Repentance is the servant's return to the reality of presence of mind in knowledge, and the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family) alluded to it when he said: 'The adulterer does not commit adultery while he is a believer' – he meant that if he were present in his knowledge, he would not commit adultery, but he forgot and disobeyed, just as the fasting person forgets his fast and eats.
Source pages p. 92

Commentary
This passage is a doctrinal exposition on the nature and rulings of repentance (tawba). Juwayni begins by asserting the obligation of repentance by consensus. He then presents two classical positions: some define repentance as a composite of three pillars – regret, desistance, and resolve; others equate it with regret alone, which then entails abandoning persistence and forming a resolve. Juwayni finds both incomplete and offers his own view: repentance is fundamentally 'return', but not simply a return to obedience; it must be accompanied by a quality related to the sin. He then elaborates: a true knower (ʿārif) may be overtaken by heedlessness and desires, but when the illumination of knowledge returns, that return constitutes repentance. This state necessarily produces regret, resolve, dissolution of persistence, grief, remorse, and wish not to have sinned. Finally, he defines repentance as the servant's return to the reality of presence of mind in knowledge, citing the hadith that a believer does not commit adultery while in a state of faith – interpreted as being present in knowledge; forgetfulness leads to sin, just as a fasting person may forget and eat. Juwayni thereby integrates cognitive presence (ḥuḍūr al-dhihn fī al-maʿrifa) as the core of repentance.

Key Terms
توبة (tawba)
معرفة (maʿrifa)
حضور الذهن (ḥuḍūr al-dhihn)
رجوع (rujūʿ)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Repentance is only feeling sorry and promising not to sin again, with no relation to knowledge.
Correction: Juwayni shows that true repentance is fundamentally a cognitive return to the presence of knowledge, which then produces regret and resolve naturally.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the core of repentance?
A) Regret for sins
B) Desisting from disobedience
C) The servant's return to presence of mind in knowledge
D) Firm resolve not to sin again

Key Takeaway
Juwayni redefines repentance as a cognitive return to divine knowledge, which naturally generates all the traditional components.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you sin, do you merely feel regret and resolve to do better, or do you honestly examine whether you have truly returned to the presence of Allah in your mind? Are you satisfied with emotional repentance alone?
?
Juwayni says the knower is overtaken by heedlessness and desires. Think of a recent sin: can you pinpoint the moment when knowledge was absent from your mind? What does it say about your attachment to divine knowledge that it could be displaced so easily?
?
The hadith says the adulterer does not commit adultery while he is a believer because his knowledge is absent. This means every sin is a testimony that we were not present in our knowledge at that moment. How many times today was your knowledge of Allah absent when you acted? Will you commit to seeking His presence before every action tomorrow?

The Obligation to Know the Five Pillars of Islam
Al-Juwayni concludes by urging knowledge of the five pillars and affirming the hadith's authority.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the obligation to know the five pillars of Islam and the mass-transmitted (mutawatir) nature of the hadith that establishes them.

Core Idea
The five pillars are the foundation of Islam, confirmed by a mass-transmitted hadith.

After building a theological framework, Imam al-Juwayni concludes by turning to practice. He emphasizes that knowledge of the five pillars is not optional—it is an obligation upon every Muslim. The hadith that states these pillars is not a weak or isolated report; it is transmitted by the entire community, leaving no room for doubt.

Guided Path
1
Read the concluding exhortation
First, read the full Arabic passage and its translation to grasp the flow: from recognition of Allah to the obligation of worship and the proof of the five pillars.
Pay attention to the transition from theology to practice.
2
Identify the hadith and its transmission
Al-Juwayni cites the famous hadith 'Islam is built upon five...' and then explains its transmission. Notice how he stresses that it is mutawatir, not a solitary report.
This is a key methodological point about the authority of the hadith.
3
Understand the obligation
The passage declares it obligatory for every successful Muslim to 'encompass these rules and their apparent meanings.' Ask yourself: why does al-Juwayni emphasize this after a theological discussion?
Theology leads to practice; knowledge of the pillars is a practical duty.
4
Reflect on the final encouragement
The passage ends with a note of divine aid. Al-Juwayni expresses hope that those who strive will attain the required knowledge. This is a motivational conclusion.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومن أحاط بما قدمته كان من العارفين بالله تبارك وتعالى ، ومن عرفه : تعين عليه الانتهاض المعرفة وظائف العبادات .. وقد صح في مأثور الخبر عن سيد البشر صلى الله عليه و آله وسلم ، أن قال : « بني الإسلام على خمس ( شهادة أن لا اله الا الله .. واقام الصلاة . وايتاء الزكاة .
وصوم رمضان وحج البيت الحرام من استطاع اليه سبيلا ) .
وليس هذا الحديث مما يختص بنقله الآحاد ، ويستأثر بروايته الأفراد، بل هو معتضد الملة ، ومستند النحلة ، نقلته الأمة قاطبة ، وتلقته بالقبول ولهج المسلمون كافة بالاطباق والاتفاق على صدوره ، من فلق ) فى ) رسول الله صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم
تحتم على كل موفق للاسلام ، ممن يتعبد بالتزام الأحكام ، أن يحيط بهذه القواعد ، وظواهر مفانيها ، ويستبين أوامر الله تبارك وتعالى فيها ، فمن عاضده التأييد ، وساوقه التسديد ، فدرك المقدار المتعين منها غير بعيد.
تمت العقيدة النظامية فى الأركان الإسلامية
And whoever encompasses what I have presented is among the knowers of Allah Almighty, and whoever knows Him, it is incumbent upon him to strive for knowledge of the functions of worship. And it has been established in the transmitted report from the master of mankind, peace be upon him and his family, that he said: 'Islam is built upon five: the testimony that there is no deity except Allah, establishing prayer, giving zakat, fasting Ramadan, and pilgrimage to the Sacred House for whoever has the means.' And this hadith is not one that is transmitted by single individuals or exclusively narrated by a few; rather, it is supported by the community and is the basis of the creed, transmitted by the entire ummah, received with acceptance, and all Muslims have unanimously agreed upon its authenticity, from the mouth of the Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him and his family. It is obligatory for every Muslim who is successful, who worships by adhering to the rulings, to encompass these rules and the apparent meanings of their branches, and to understand the commands of Allah Almighty within them. So whoever is aided by divine support and accompanied by divine guidance, then attaining the required amount thereof is not far. Completed is al-Aqida al-Nizamiyya on the Islamic pillars.
Source pages p. 95

Commentary
This passage is the concluding exhortation of the book. Al-Juwayni states that whoever has understood the previous content is among the knowers of Allah. Then he stresses the obligation to learn the specifics of worship. He cites the well-known hadith of the five pillars, emphasizing that this hadith is mutawatir (mass-transmitted) and not ahad, thus its authority is undeniable. He then asserts that it is mandatory for every Muslim to master these foundational rules and their apparent meanings. The passage ends with a note of hope that divine aid will facilitate this. Finally, the book's title is restated.

Key Terms
أركان الإسلام (Pillars of Islam)
متواتر (mass-transmitted)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The hadith on the five pillars is transmitted by only a few individuals and therefore uncertain.
Correction: Al-Juwayni explicitly states that it is mass-transmitted (mutawatir), transmitted by the entire ummah and accepted unanimously.

Checkpoint
How does al-Juwayni describe the transmission of the hadith on the five pillars?
A) It is a solitary hadith
B) It is mass-transmitted (mutawatir)
C) It is weak
D) It is only known to scholars

Key Takeaway
The five pillars are the foundation of Islamic practice, established by a mass-transmitted hadith, and every Muslim is obliged to know them.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have read that whoever encompasses the preceding knowledge is among the 'knowers of Allah.' Do you truly approach learning your faith as a means of knowing Allah, or is it merely information you accumulate? What would change if you studied every lesson with this intention?
?
Al-Juwayni says that knowledge of Allah obligates you to strive to learn acts of worship. When did you last feel that inner compulsion to seek Islamic knowledge because of your love for Allah? If that drive is weak, what is blocking it?
?
The five pillars are not optional suggestions but binding obligations confirmed by a mass-transmitted hadith. Have you treated any pillar as a cultural habit rather than a divine command? Which one, and what will you do today to restore its weight in your heart?

The Two Categories of Quranic Verses: Muhkam and Mutashabih
Defines muhkam and mutashabih with examples.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definitions of muhkam and mutashabih verses and how they relate to each other.

Core Idea
Muhkam verses are the foundation; mutashabih verses refer back to them.

The Quran describes itself as containing both clear and ambiguous verses. This lesson from Tafsir al-Kashshaf explains what these terms mean and gives concrete examples. Understanding these categories is essential for interpreting the Quran correctly.

Guided Path
1
Read the verses and definitions
Read the Arabic verse and the Kashshaf's definitions. Note how 'muhkam' is defined as precise and unambiguous.
2
Examine the examples
Look at the four examples of mutashabih verses. Notice how they appear contradictory but can be reconciled by referring to muhkam verses.
3
Understand the relationship
Realize that muhkam verses are the 'mother of the Book' – the reference point for understanding mutashabih.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وتفسير قوله تعالى » هو الذي أنزل عليك الكتاب • منه آيات محكمات من أم الكتاب . وأخر متشابهات . فأما الذين في قلوبهم زيغ فيتبعون ما تشابه منه . ابتغاء الفتنة ، وابتغاء تأويله . وما يعلم تأويله الا الله . والراسخون فى العلم يتولون آمنا به . كل من عند ربنا ، وما يذكر الا أولو الألباب » ( آل عمران : ٧ ( هكذا في تفسير الكشاف : ( محكمات ( أحكمت عبارتها بأن حفظت من الاحتمال والاشتباه ) متشابهات ( مشتبهات محتملات (هن أم الكتاب ( أى أصل الكتاب تحمل المتشابهات عليها ، وترد اليها . ومثال ذلك « لا تدركه الأبصار » - « الى ربها ناظرة ) - ( لا يأمر والفحشاء ) - ( أمرنا مترفيها « فإن قلت : فهلا كان القرآن كله محكما ؟ قلت : لو كان كله محكما لتعلق الناس به لسهولة مأخذه ، ولأعرضوا عما يحتاجون فيه الى الفحص والتأمل من النظر والاستدلال ) وما يعلم تأويلة الا الله والراسخون في العلم ( أى لا يهتدى الى تأويله الحق الذى يجب أن يحمل عليه الا الله وعباده الذين رسخوا في العلم ، أى ثبتوا فيه وتمكنوا وعضوا فيه بضرس قاطع .. ومنهم من يقف على قوله « الا الله » ويبتدىء » والراسخون فى العلم يقولون « ويفسرون المتشابه بما استأثر الله يعلمه ، وبمعرفة الحكمة فيه من آياته كعدد الزبانية ونحوه .. والأول هو الوجه . ويقولون كلام مستأنف موضح لحال الراسخين بمعنى : هؤلاء العالمون بالتأويل ) يقولون آمنا به ( أى بالمتشابه ) كل من عند ربنا ( أى كل واحد منه ، أو من المحكم من عنده ، أو بالكتاب كل من متشابهه ومحكمه من عند الله الحكيم الذى لا يتناقض كلامه ، ولا يختلف كتابه « اهـ . رحمه الله -
And the interpretation of His saying 'It is He who sent down to you the Book. From it are verses muhkamat (clear) — they are the mother of the Book — and others mutashabihat (ambiguous). As for those in whose hearts is deviation, they follow what is ambiguous from it, seeking discord and seeking its interpretation. And none knows its interpretation except Allah. And those firmly grounded in knowledge say, 'We believe in it; all is from our Lord.' And none remember except those of understanding.' (Aal-Imran: 7) Thus in the Tafsir al-Kashshaf: 'Muhkamat: whose wording has been made precise by being preserved from probability and ambiguity. Mutashabihat: ambiguous, carrying probabilities. They are the mother of the Book, i.e., the origin of the Book; the ambiguous verses are based on them and referred to them. And an example of that is 'Vision does not perceive Him' and 'To their Lord they are looking' and 'He does not command immorality' and 'We order its affluent'... If you say: Why was not the entire Quran muhkam? I say: If it were all muhkam, people would cling to it because of its easy accessibility, and they would turn away from what they need to examine and ponder of investigation and inference.' And 'None knows its interpretation except Allah and those firmly grounded in knowledge' — i.e., none is guided to its true interpretation, which it must be interpreted as, except Allah and His servants who are firmly grounded in knowledge, i.e., established in it, skilled, and have bitten it with a decisive molar. Among them are those who stop at His saying 'except Allah' and begin 'And those firmly grounded in knowledge say' and they interpret the ambiguous by what Allah has exclusively known, and by knowing the wisdom in it from His signs like the number of the Zabaniyah and the like. And the first is the correct way. And they say (it is) an independent statement clarifying the state of those firmly grounded, meaning: these knowers of interpretation say, 'We believe in it' (i.e., in the ambiguous), 'all is from our Lord' (i.e., each one of them, or the muhkam from Him, or the entire Book, its ambiguous and its muhkam, is from Allah, the Wise, whose speech does not contradict and whose Book does not differ.' End quote. May Allah have mercy on him.
Source pages p. 105

Commentary
This passage is an excerpt from Tafsir al-Kashshaf explaining the classification of Quranic verses into muhkam and mutashabih. It defines muhkam as clear and unambiguous, and mutashabih as ambiguous. It gives examples of mutashabih verses, such as those about seeing Allah and divine commands. It then addresses the question of why the entire Quran is not muhkam: it would make people lazy and uninterested in deeper reflection. A key point is the discussion of the pause after 'إلا الله' in the verse 'وما يعلم تأويله إلا الله والراسخون في العلم'. The Kashshaf presents two interpretations: stopping at 'إلا الله' (meaning only Allah knows the true interpretation) and continuing the phrase (meaning the firmly grounded know it as well). He states that the first interpretation (stopping) is the correct one, as it aligns with the idea that the ambiguous is known only to Allah, and the next phrase is a separate statement describing the firmly grounded who say 'we believe in it'.

Key Terms
محكم
متشابه
أم الكتاب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think mutashabih verses are meaningless or can be interpreted arbitrarily.
Correction: Mutashabih verses have meaning but are ambiguous; they must be referred to the muhkam verses for correct interpretation.

Checkpoint
According to the Kashshaf, what are muhkam verses?
A) Verses that are difficult to understand
B) Verses whose wording is precise and unambiguous
C) Verses that contradict each other
D) Verses that only scholars understand

Key Takeaway
Muhkam verses are clear and foundational; mutashabih verses are ambiguous and must be interpreted in light of the muhkam.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read the Quran, do you find yourself drawn more to verses that are ambiguous or easy to twist to your desires? How can you ensure you are not among those 'in whose hearts is deviation'?
?
This lesson says muhkam verses are the foundation. In your own life, are you building your faith on the clear commands of Allah, or are you using ambiguous interpretations to justify your actions? What is one area where you might be doing this?
?
What would it look like for you to be 'firmly grounded in knowledge' when you encounter a difficult verse? Write down one specific step you will take the next time you read a verse you don't understand.

Why Not All Verses Muhkam?
Answers why the Quran contains mutashabih verses.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the wisdom behind the presence of mutashabih verses in the Quran.

Core Idea
Mutashabih verses encourage deeper study and reflection.

A natural question arises: why did Allah not make the entire Quran clear and unambiguous? The Kashshaf provides a concise answer that highlights the purpose of ambiguous verses: to stimulate intellectual effort and prevent complacency.

Guided Path
1
Read the question and answer
Read the rhetorical question and answer in the passage. Note the reasoning.
2
Analyze the purpose
Understand that mutashabih verses push believers to engage in deeper investigation (nazar and istidlal).
3
Reflect on the alternative
Consider what would happen if all verses were muhkam: people would not strive and reflect, potentially missing deeper truths.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وتفسير قوله تعالى » هو الذي أنزل عليك الكتاب • منه آيات محكمات من أم الكتاب . وأخر متشابهات . فأما الذين في قلوبهم زيغ فيتبعون ما تشابه منه . ابتغاء الفتنة ، وابتغاء تأويله . وما يعلم تأويله الا الله . والراسخون فى العلم يتولون آمنا به . كل من عند ربنا ، وما يذكر الا أولو الألباب » ( آل عمران : ٧ ( هكذا في تفسير الكشاف : ( محكمات ( أحكمت عبارتها بأن حفظت من الاحتمال والاشتباه ) متشابهات ( مشتبهات محتملات (هن أم الكتاب ( أى أصل الكتاب تحمل المتشابهات عليها ، وترد اليها . ومثال ذلك « لا تدركه الأبصار » - « الى ربها ناظرة ) - ( لا يأمر والفحشاء ) - ( أمرنا مترفيها « فإن قلت : فهلا كان القرآن كله محكما ؟ قلت : لو كان كله محكما لتعلق الناس به لسهولة مأخذه ، ولأعرضوا عما يحتاجون فيه الى الفحص والتأمل من النظر والاستدلال ) وما يعلم تأويلة الا الله والراسخون في العلم ( أى لا يهتدى الى تأويله الحق الذى يجب أن يحمل عليه الا الله وعباده الذين رسخوا في العلم ، أى ثبتوا فيه وتمكنوا وعضوا فيه بضرس قاطع .. ومنهم من يقف على قوله « الا الله » ويبتدىء » والراسخون فى العلم يقولون « ويفسرون المتشابه بما استأثر الله يعلمه ، وبمعرفة الحكمة فيه من آياته كعدد الزبانية ونحوه .. والأول هو الوجه . ويقولون كلام مستأنف موضح لحال الراسخين بمعنى : هؤلاء العالمون بالتأويل ) يقولون آمنا به ( أى بالمتشابه ) كل من عند ربنا ( أى كل واحد منه ، أو من المحكم من عنده ، أو بالكتاب كل من متشابهه ومحكمه من عند الله الحكيم الذى لا يتناقض كلامه ، ولا يختلف كتابه « اهـ . رحمه الله -
And the interpretation of His saying 'It is He who sent down to you the Book. From it are verses muhkamat (clear) — they are the mother of the Book — and others mutashabihat (ambiguous). As for those in whose hearts is deviation, they follow what is ambiguous from it, seeking discord and seeking its interpretation. And none knows its interpretation except Allah. And those firmly grounded in knowledge say, 'We believe in it; all is from our Lord.' And none remember except those of understanding.' (Aal-Imran: 7) Thus in the Tafsir al-Kashshaf: 'Muhkamat: whose wording has been made precise by being preserved from probability and ambiguity. Mutashabihat: ambiguous, carrying probabilities. They are the mother of the Book, i.e., the origin of the Book; the ambiguous verses are based on them and referred to them. And an example of that is 'Vision does not perceive Him' and 'To their Lord they are looking' and 'He does not command immorality' and 'We order its affluent'... If you say: Why was not the entire Quran muhkam? I say: If it were all muhkam, people would cling to it because of its easy accessibility, and they would turn away from what they need to examine and ponder of investigation and inference.' And 'None knows its interpretation except Allah and those firmly grounded in knowledge' — i.e., none is guided to its true interpretation, which it must be interpreted as, except Allah and His servants who are firmly grounded in knowledge, i.e., established in it, skilled, and have bitten it with a decisive molar. Among them are those who stop at His saying 'except Allah' and begin 'And those firmly grounded in knowledge say' and they interpret the ambiguous by what Allah has exclusively known, and by knowing the wisdom in it from His signs like the number of the Zabaniyah and the like. And the first is the correct way. And they say (it is) an independent statement clarifying the state of those firmly grounded, meaning: these knowers of interpretation say, 'We believe in it' (i.e., in the ambiguous), 'all is from our Lord' (i.e., each one of them, or the muhkam from Him, or the entire Book, its ambiguous and its muhkam, is from Allah, the Wise, whose speech does not contradict and whose Book does not differ.' End quote. May Allah have mercy on him.
Source pages p. 105

Commentary
This passage is an excerpt from Tafsir al-Kashshaf explaining the classification of Quranic verses into muhkam and mutashabih. It defines muhkam as clear and unambiguous, and mutashabih as ambiguous. It gives examples of mutashabih verses, such as those about seeing Allah and divine commands. It then addresses the question of why the entire Quran is not muhkam: it would make people lazy and uninterested in deeper reflection. A key point is the discussion of the pause after 'إلا الله' in the verse 'وما يعلم تأويله إلا الله والراسخون في العلم'. The Kashshaf presents two interpretations: stopping at 'إلا الله' (meaning only Allah knows the true interpretation) and continuing the phrase (meaning the firmly grounded know it as well). He states that the first interpretation (stopping) is the correct one, as it aligns with the idea that the ambiguous is known only to Allah, and the next phrase is a separate statement describing the firmly grounded who say 'we believe in it'.

Key Terms
فحص
تأمل
نظر
استدلال

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think mutashabih verses are a flaw or unnecessary.
Correction: Mutashabih verses are intentional to encourage deep reflection and prevent lazy reading.

Checkpoint
According to the Kashshaf, why are some verses mutashabih?
A) Because Allah could not make them clear
B) To encourage people to engage in deep reflection and inference
C) To confuse the disbelievers
D) Because the Quran was revealed gradually

Key Takeaway
Mutashabih verses exist to motivate believers to think deeply and use reasoning.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you tend to read only the easy, clear verses of the Quran while avoiding the difficult ones? This week, commit to studying one mutashabih verse with tafsir and reasoning, rather than skipping over it. How does the author's argument challenge your current approach?
?
When you encounter an unclear verse, do you feel frustrated or motivated to investigate? The author says mutashabih is meant to stimulate reflection. What does your reaction say about your willingness to engage deeply with Allah's words, even when they require effort?
?
How often do you rely solely on translations without seeking deeper understanding? The passage warns against superficial clinging. Have you ever turned away from deeper study because it seemed hard? What would change if you embraced that difficulty as a gift from Allah to draw you closer to Him?

Who Knows the Interpretation of Mutashabih? The Pause After 'إلا الله'
Explains the pause after 'إلا الله' and the two qira'at.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the two interpretations regarding who knows the true meaning of mutashabih verses, and which view the Kashshaf prefers.

Core Idea
Only Allah knows the exact interpretation; the firmly grounded affirm belief.

The verse 'وما يعلم تأويله إلا الله والراسخون في العلم' can be read with a pause or without. This lesson explores the two readings and their theological implications, drawing on the Kashshaf's preference.

Guided Path
1
Read the discussion
Read the passage about the pause after 'إلا الله'. Note the two opinions.
2
Understand the first view
The first view stops at 'إلا الله', meaning only Allah knows the interpretation. The rest is a separate statement.
3
Understand the second view
The second view includes 'الراسخون' as also knowing the interpretation, but the Kashshaf rejects this.
4
Note the favored view
The Kashshaf says the first is the correct one (الأول هو الوجه).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وتفسير قوله تعالى » هو الذي أنزل عليك الكتاب • منه آيات محكمات من أم الكتاب . وأخر متشابهات . فأما الذين في قلوبهم زيغ فيتبعون ما تشابه منه . ابتغاء الفتنة ، وابتغاء تأويله . وما يعلم تأويله الا الله . والراسخون فى العلم يتولون آمنا به . كل من عند ربنا ، وما يذكر الا أولو الألباب » ( آل عمران : ٧ ( هكذا في تفسير الكشاف : ( محكمات ( أحكمت عبارتها بأن حفظت من الاحتمال والاشتباه ) متشابهات ( مشتبهات محتملات (هن أم الكتاب ( أى أصل الكتاب تحمل المتشابهات عليها ، وترد اليها . ومثال ذلك « لا تدركه الأبصار » - « الى ربها ناظرة ) - ( لا يأمر والفحشاء ) - ( أمرنا مترفيها « فإن قلت : فهلا كان القرآن كله محكما ؟ قلت : لو كان كله محكما لتعلق الناس به لسهولة مأخذه ، ولأعرضوا عما يحتاجون فيه الى الفحص والتأمل من النظر والاستدلال ) وما يعلم تأويلة الا الله والراسخون في العلم ( أى لا يهتدى الى تأويله الحق الذى يجب أن يحمل عليه الا الله وعباده الذين رسخوا في العلم ، أى ثبتوا فيه وتمكنوا وعضوا فيه بضرس قاطع .. ومنهم من يقف على قوله « الا الله » ويبتدىء » والراسخون فى العلم يقولون « ويفسرون المتشابه بما استأثر الله يعلمه ، وبمعرفة الحكمة فيه من آياته كعدد الزبانية ونحوه .. والأول هو الوجه . ويقولون كلام مستأنف موضح لحال الراسخين بمعنى : هؤلاء العالمون بالتأويل ) يقولون آمنا به ( أى بالمتشابه ) كل من عند ربنا ( أى كل واحد منه ، أو من المحكم من عنده ، أو بالكتاب كل من متشابهه ومحكمه من عند الله الحكيم الذى لا يتناقض كلامه ، ولا يختلف كتابه « اهـ . رحمه الله -
And the interpretation of His saying 'It is He who sent down to you the Book. From it are verses muhkamat (clear) — they are the mother of the Book — and others mutashabihat (ambiguous). As for those in whose hearts is deviation, they follow what is ambiguous from it, seeking discord and seeking its interpretation. And none knows its interpretation except Allah. And those firmly grounded in knowledge say, 'We believe in it; all is from our Lord.' And none remember except those of understanding.' (Aal-Imran: 7) Thus in the Tafsir al-Kashshaf: 'Muhkamat: whose wording has been made precise by being preserved from probability and ambiguity. Mutashabihat: ambiguous, carrying probabilities. They are the mother of the Book, i.e., the origin of the Book; the ambiguous verses are based on them and referred to them. And an example of that is 'Vision does not perceive Him' and 'To their Lord they are looking' and 'He does not command immorality' and 'We order its affluent'... If you say: Why was not the entire Quran muhkam? I say: If it were all muhkam, people would cling to it because of its easy accessibility, and they would turn away from what they need to examine and ponder of investigation and inference.' And 'None knows its interpretation except Allah and those firmly grounded in knowledge' — i.e., none is guided to its true interpretation, which it must be interpreted as, except Allah and His servants who are firmly grounded in knowledge, i.e., established in it, skilled, and have bitten it with a decisive molar. Among them are those who stop at His saying 'except Allah' and begin 'And those firmly grounded in knowledge say' and they interpret the ambiguous by what Allah has exclusively known, and by knowing the wisdom in it from His signs like the number of the Zabaniyah and the like. And the first is the correct way. And they say (it is) an independent statement clarifying the state of those firmly grounded, meaning: these knowers of interpretation say, 'We believe in it' (i.e., in the ambiguous), 'all is from our Lord' (i.e., each one of them, or the muhkam from Him, or the entire Book, its ambiguous and its muhkam, is from Allah, the Wise, whose speech does not contradict and whose Book does not differ.' End quote. May Allah have mercy on him.
Source pages p. 105

Commentary
This passage is an excerpt from Tafsir al-Kashshaf explaining the classification of Quranic verses into muhkam and mutashabih. It defines muhkam as clear and unambiguous, and mutashabih as ambiguous. It gives examples of mutashabih verses, such as those about seeing Allah and divine commands. It then addresses the question of why the entire Quran is not muhkam: it would make people lazy and uninterested in deeper reflection. A key point is the discussion of the pause after 'إلا الله' in the verse 'وما يعلم تأويله إلا الله والراسخون في العلم'. The Kashshaf presents two interpretations: stopping at 'إلا الله' (meaning only Allah knows the true interpretation) and continuing the phrase (meaning the firmly grounded know it as well). He states that the first interpretation (stopping) is the correct one, as it aligns with the idea that the ambiguous is known only to Allah, and the next phrase is a separate statement describing the firmly grounded who say 'we believe in it'.

Key Terms
تأويل
راسخون في العلم
الزبانية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that the firmly grounded know the exact interpretation of all ambiguous verses.
Correction: The correct view is that only Allah knows the exact interpretation; the firmly grounded know its wisdom and believe in it.

Checkpoint
According to the Kashshaf, which reading of the verse is correct?
A) Stopping at 'إلا الله'
B) Continuing without pause
C) Both are valid
D) Neither

Key Takeaway
Only Allah knows the exact interpretation of mutashabih verses; the firmly grounded affirm their belief and recognize the wisdom.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you encounter a verse or teaching in Islam that is unclear to you, does your soul rest in saying 'We believe in it, all is from our Lord', or does it become anxious and demand a full explanation? This lesson calls you to humble submission—are you ready to trust Allah's wisdom even when your intellect cannot grasp every detail?
?
The firmly grounded scholars did not need to know the exact number of Zabaniyah to have certainty; they affirmed belief. Is there an area in your life where you feel you need all the answers before you can commit fully to Allah? What would it cost you to admit that some knowledge is beyond you and still hold firm?
?
If you were to truly internalize that only Allah knows the ultimate interpretation of some matters, how would that change your approach to theological debates or questions that bother you? Would you still argue or would your heart settle into peace? Commit today to one mutashabih-like issue you will consciously entrust to Allah.

Punishment and Bliss in the Grave
Comparison of Islamic and Ahl al-Kitab eschatology

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the unique Islamic belief in punishment and bliss in the grave, and how it differs from the resurrection-focused eschatology of the People of the Book.

Core Idea
Grave punishment is uniquely Islamic; Ahl al-Kitab only affirm resurrection and judgment.

Imam al-Juwayni, in his discussion of transmitted matters (samiyyat), affirmed the punishment and bliss of the grave as part of Islamic doctrine. This lesson examines a passage where the author contrasts this belief with the scriptures of Jews and Christians, noting that they only affirm resurrection and a subsequent judgment, not any intermediate state of reward or punishment.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then read the literal English translation. Notice that the author begins by stating al-Juwayni's affirmation, then contrasts it with his observation of Ahl al-Kitab scriptures.
The passage references several of the author's own works; don't dwell on them now.
2
Identify the two contrasting positions
Identify the Islamic position (affirmation of grave punishment and bliss) and the Ahl al-Kitab position (affirmation of only resurrection and recompense in a second life). Note that the author says he has not seen in their books any mention of grave punishment or bliss.
3
Reflect on the evidence
Consider how the author supports his comparison: he references his own writings where he quotes Ahl al-Kitab sources affirming bodily and spiritual resurrection, and notes that this has parallels in clear Qur'anic texts. Think about how this lesson helps distinguish Islamic eschatology from other traditions.
The final unclear word 'الخونضع' may need later verification.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
لما تكلم الجويني في باب السمعيات، أثبت عذاب القبر ونعيمه. ولم أر في كتب أهل الكتاب إثبات لعذاب في القبر أو نعيم، وإنما رأيت إثبات البعث من الأموات في حياة ثانية، ويسأل الله الناس في افتاده لحياة الثانية عما عملوا في الحياة الأولى الحياة الدنيا، ويجازيهم تنی ما عملوا. وقد بينت ذلك في كتابي 'الله وصفاته في اليهودية والنصرانية والإسلام' وفي تقديمي للتوراة السامرية. وفي تعليقي على 'إظهار الحق' وفي تقديمي لكتاب 'يقظة أولي الاعتبار فيما ورد في ذكر النار وأصحاب النار' ونقلت عنهم قولهم بالبعث الجسدي والروحي وهذا له ما يوافقه في القرآن من نصوص محكمة منها الخونضع [؟].
When al-Juwayni spoke in the chapter on transmitted matters (samiyyat), he affirmed the punishment of the grave and its bliss. And I have not seen in the books of the People of the Book an affirmation of punishment in the grave or bliss, but rather I have seen the affirmation of resurrection from the dead in a second life, and Allah will ask people on their return to the second life about what they did in the first life, the worldly life, and reward them for what they did. And I have explained that in my book 'Allah and His Attributes in Judaism, Christianity, and Islam' and in my introduction to the Samaritan Torah, and in my commentary on 'Izhar al-Haqq', and in my introduction to the book 'Yaqzat Uli al-I'tibar regarding what has been mentioned of the Fire and its companions' and I have quoted from them their statement of bodily and spiritual resurrection. And this has what agrees with it in the Qur'an from clear texts, including [؟].
Source pages p. 114

Commentary
This passage discusses the belief in punishment and bliss in the grave (ard al-qabr wa na'imuh), which is a matter of samiyyat (transmitted knowledge). The author notes that al-Juwayni affirmed this in his work. However, the author observes that in the scriptures of Ahl al-Kitab (Jews and Christians), there is no mention of punishment or bliss in the grave; instead, they only affirm resurrection (ba'th) for a second life where judgment occurs. The author references his own works where he has demonstrated that the belief in bodily and spiritual resurrection among Ahl al-Kitab does have parallels in the Qur'anic clear texts, though the specific belief in intermediate state punishment is absent. The final unclear word may refer to a specific Qur'anic verse or term.

Key Terms

عذاب القبر
نعيم القبر
بعث
أهل الكتاب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: All Abrahamic faiths share the belief in punishment or reward in the grave.
Correction: The author states that the People of the Book do not affirm any punishment or bliss in the grave; only resurrection and subsequent judgment.

Checkpoint

A) The People of the Book believe in annihilation of the soul
B) The People of the Book do not affirm punishment or bliss in the grave
C) The People of the Book deny resurrection of the body

Key Takeaway
Grave punishment and bliss are uniquely Islamic doctrines not found in the scriptures of Jews and Christians.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?

Affirming Punishment in the Grave and the Deniers' Arguments
Juwayni affirms punishment in the grave and presents three Quranic proofs of its deniers.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three main Quranic arguments used by those who deny punishment in the grave and how they interpret relevant verses.

Core Idea
Deniers of grave punishment misinterpret clear Quranic texts.

Imam al-Juwayni, in his section on sam‘iyyāt (matters known only through revelation), affirms the reality of punishment and bliss in the grave. He then reports that several Muslim scholars—including Ḍirār ibn ‘Amr, Bishr al-Marīsī, and later Mu‘tazila—denied this. They based their denial on specific Quranic verses, which they interpreted in ways that preclude an intermediate life. This passage presents their arguments, allowing us to understand the controversy and the importance of distinguishing between clear (muḥkam) and ambiguous (mutashābih) verses.

Guided Path
1
Juwayni's affirmation and the deniers' identity
Read the opening lines where Juwayni affirms punishment in the grave and lists the deniers. Notice that he mentions his own works on resurrection from other scriptures.
The deniers are from early Islamic sects like the Mu'tazila.
2
First proof: verse 44:56
Examine the verse 'They will not taste death therein except the first death.' The deniers argue that if there were life in the grave, one would taste a second death, contradicting the verse.
This verse is about Paradise, but the deniers apply it generally.
3
Second proof: verse 40:11
Study the verse 'Our Lord, You have made us die twice and given us life twice.' The deniers interpret this as two deaths (pre‑birth and natural) and two lives (worldly and afterlife), leaving no room for grave life.
Their interpretation adds a fourth life if grave life is accepted.
4
Third proof: verse 40:46 and metaphorical fire
Analyze the verse about Pharaoh's family exposed to fire morning and evening. The deniers say 'fire' is metaphorical (hardships) because literal punishment before Judgment Day would contradict clear verses about requital on that Day.
They also cite verse 71:25 about Noah's people and the cave story.
5
Summarize the deniers' overall argument
Reflect on how the deniers use these verses to claim that any intermediate state (grave life) would conflict with the Quran's repeated emphasis on life after resurrection.
Juwayni does not provide his refutation here; he only reports their claims.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
لما تكلم الجويني في باب السمعيات، أثبت عذاب القبر ونعيمه. ولم أر في كتب أهل الكتاب إثبات لعذاب في القبر أو نعيم، وإنما رأيت إثبات البعث من الأموات في حياة ثانية، ويسأل الله الناس في [؟] لحياة الثانية عما عملوا في الحياة الأولى، الحياة الدنيا، ويجازيهم على ما عملوا. وقد بينت ذلك في كتابي «الله وصفاته في اليهودية والنصرانية والإسلام» وفي تقديمي للتوراة السامرية. وفي تعليقي على «إظهار الحق» وفي تقديمي لكتاب «يقظة أولي الاعتبار فيما ورد في ذكر النار وأصحاب النار» ونقلت عنهم قولهم بالبعث الجسدي والروحي، وهذا له ما يوافقه في القرآن من نصوص محكمة منها الخنضع [؟]. ثم قال: لموازين القسط ليوم القيامة فلا تظلم نفس شيئاً. وإن كان مثقال حبة من خردل أتينا بها وكفى بنا حاسبين. (الأنبياء: 47). وقد أنكر كثير من علماء المسلمين السؤال والعذاب والنعيم في القبر منهم ضرار بن عمرو وبشر المريسي وأكثر المتأخرين من المعتزلة، هم الله عز وجل، واستدلوا بقوله تعالى: «لا يذوقون فيها الموت إلا الموتة الأولى» (الدخان: 56)، فإنهم لو أحيوا في القبر لذاقوا موتتين. واستدلوا بقوله تعالى: «ربنا أمتنا اثنتين وأحييتنا اثنتين» (غافر: 11)، وتفسيرها: عدم قبل الولادة وهو موت، والموت الطبيعي وحياة في الدنيا وحياة في الآخرة، فهذان موتتان وحياتان، ولو كان في القبر حياة زادت حياة على ما نصت عليه الآية. وقالوا في قوله تعالى عن آل فرعون: «النار يعرضون عليها غدواً وعشياً ويوم تقوم الساعة أدخلوا آل فرعون أشد العذاب» (غافر: 46) أن النار ليست على المعنى الحقيقي، بل على المعنى المجازي. وعلى ذلك فالنص متشابه، يرد إلى المحكم وهو قوله تعالى: «ثم لتسألن يومئذ عن النعيم» (التكاثر: 8) وقوله تعالى: «فمن يعمل مثقال ذرة خيراً يره، ومن يعمل مثقال ذرة شراً يره» (الزلزلة: 7-8) أي في الحياة الآخرة. وجاءت النار بالمعنى المجازي في كثير من آي القرآن منها: «إنما يأكلون في بطونهم ناراً» (النساء: 10). والمعنى المجازي للنار التي يعرض عليها آل فرعون هو «الشدائد» في الدنيا بسبب معرفتهم الله وبعدهم عن شرائعه، والآية [؟] المصريين المعاندين إلى طول الزمان. وقالوا في قوله تعالى عن قوم نوح عليه السلام: «أغرقوا فأدخلوا ناراً» (نوح: 25) أغرقوا فيكثوا مدة ثم أدخلوا ناراً في الآخرة، أو تكون المدة بين الإغراق والبعث بحسب علم الأموات غير المدة بحسب علم الأحياء. فإن أهل الكهف لما ماتوا ثلاثمائة سنة وتسع سنين وبعثوا...
When al-Juwayni spoke in the chapter on sam'iyyat (matters heard from revelation), he affirmed the punishment of the grave and its bliss. And I have not seen in the books of the People of the Book an affirmation of punishment in the grave or bliss, but I have seen an affirmation of resurrection from the dead in a second life, and Allah will ask people in [؟] for the second life about what they did in the first life, the worldly life, and reward them for what they did. And I have explained that in my book 'Allah and His Attributes in Judaism, Christianity, and Islam' and in my introduction to the Samaritan Torah. And in my commentary on 'Izhar al-Haqq' and in my introduction to the book 'Yaqza Uli al-I'tibar...' and I have transmitted from them their statement about bodily and spiritual resurrection, and this has what agrees with it in the Quran from clear texts, among them al-Khunuda' [؟]. Then he said: 'The scales of justice for the Day of Resurrection, so no soul shall be wronged at all. And if there be the weight of a mustard seed, We will bring it, and sufficient are We as accountants.' (Al-Anbiya: 47). And many Muslim scholars denied the questioning, punishment, and bliss in the grave, among them Dirar ibn Amr, Bishr al-Marisi, and most of the later Mu'tazila, may Allah have mercy on them, and they adduced the saying of Allah Almighty: 'They will not taste death therein except the first death' (Al-Dukhan: 56) — for if they were revived in the grave, they would taste two deaths. And they adduced the saying of Allah Almighty: 'Our Lord, You have made us die twice and given us life twice' (Ghafir: 11), and its interpretation: non-existence before birth which is death, natural death, life in the world, and life in the Hereafter — these are two deaths and two lives, and if there were life in the grave, it would add a life beyond what the verse states. And they said concerning the saying of Allah Almighty about the family of Pharaoh: 'The Fire, they are exposed to it morning and evening, and on the Day when the Hour will be established: 'Admit the family of Pharaoh to the severest punishment'' (Ghafir: 46) that the fire is not in the literal sense, but in the metaphorical sense. And accordingly, the text is ambiguous (mutashabih), referred back to the clear (muhkam) which is the saying of Allah Almighty: 'Then on that Day you will surely be asked about the pleasures' (Al-Takathur: 8) and His saying: 'So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it' (Al-Zalzala: 7-8), i.e., in the life of the Hereafter. And fire has come in the metaphorical sense in many verses of the Quran, among them: 'They only consume fire in their bellies' (Al-Nisa: 10). And the metaphorical meaning of the fire to which the family of Pharaoh is exposed is 'hardships' in the world due to their knowledge of Allah and their distance from His laws, and the verse [؟] the obstinate Egyptians for a long time. And they said concerning the saying of Allah Almighty about the people of Noah, peace be upon him: 'They were drowned and then made to enter a fire' (Nuh: 25): they were drowned, then they remained a while, then entered a fire in the Hereafter, or the period between drowning and resurrection is according to the knowledge of the dead, different from the period according to the knowledge of the living. For the Companions of the Cave, when they died for three hundred and nine years, then were resurrected...
Source pages p. 114

Commentary
This passage is a refutation of those who deny punishment in the grave. Juwayni begins by affirming that he has established the punishment and bliss of the grave in the chapter on sam'iyyat (matters based on revelation). He notes that he has not seen evidence for it in the scriptures of the People of the Book (Jews and Christians), only for general resurrection. He then mentions his own works discussing resurrection. Then he reports that many Muslim scholars, including Dirar ibn Amr, Bishr al-Marisi, and later Mu'tazila, denied the questioning and punishment in the grave. They used several Quranic verses as proofs: (1) Verse 44:56 says the inhabitants of Paradise will not taste death except the first death; if there were life in the grave, they would taste death twice. (2) Verse 40:11 says 'You have made us die twice and given us life twice'; they interpret this as a first death before birth and natural death, and two lives: worldly and afterlife. Adding life in the grave would contradict this. (3) Regarding verse 40:46 about Pharaoh's family being exposed to fire morning and evening, they claim it is metaphorical, referring to worldly hardships, because the literal interpretation would contradict clear verses about reward and punishment only on the Day of Judgment (e.g., 102:8 and 99:7-8). Similarly, for verse 71:25 about Noah's people being drowned and then entering fire, they explain the interval as either the fire being in the afterlife or the time between drowning and resurrection being different for the dead, as in the story of the Companions of the Cave.

Key Terms
عذاب القبر
نعيم القبر
سؤال القبر
سميعات
معتزلة
ضرار بن عمرو
بشر المريسي
متشابه
محكم
آل فرعون

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Punishment in the grave is not mentioned in the Quran; it is only in hadith.
Correction: The deniers themselves used Quranic verses to argue against grave punishment, showing that they considered the Quran relevant. However, affirming scholars interpret those verses differently.

Checkpoint
What was the main argument of those who denied punishment in the grave based on verse 44:56?
A) The verse says the people of Paradise will not taste death except the first death, so if there were life in the grave, they would taste death twice
B) The verse says the people of Paradise will live forever without any death
C) The verse says death is only in this world
D) The verse says there is no punishment after death

Key Takeaway
The deniers of punishment in the grave used Quranic verses that they interpreted as precluding any intermediate life, setting up a key debate about the meaning of clear and ambiguous verses in the Quran.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever dismissed a core Islamic belief because you couldn't fully comprehend it, like the deniers did with punishment in the grave? What does that reveal about your reliance on your own intellect over revelation? Commit this week to study at least one 'unseen' matter with the humility of a student, not a judge.
?
If you were to stand before Allah tomorrow and He asked you why you denied the punishment of the grave, would you be satisfied with the excuse that you used verses to justify your denial, just as the Mu'tazila did? Write down one belief you currently hold only because it is convenient, and decide to test it against the Quran and Sunnah without bias.

The Deniers' First Argument: Contradiction with Verse About One Death
Juwayni presents the first scriptural argument of the deniers: the verse about one death.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the first argument of the deniers of punishment in the grave: that reviving the dead in the grave would contradict the Quranic statement that they taste only one death.

Core Idea
Revival in the grave would mean two deaths, contradicting the verse.

Juwayni begins by naming the scholars who denied the punishment in the grave and then presents their first proof. They cite a clear Quranic verse about the people of Paradise tasting only one death, arguing that if the dead were given life in the grave, they would experience a second death, which the verse excludes. This sets the stage for understanding their rational approach.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage identifying the deniers
Read the Arabic and translation to see which scholars are listed and the verse they cited.
The names include Dirar ibn Amr, Bishr al-Marisi, and later Mu'tazila.
2
Understand the logical structure
The deniers argue: The verse says they taste only the first death; if there were life in the grave, they would taste a second death; therefore, there is no life in the grave.
This is a simple modus tollens argument.
3
Reflect on the implication
Consider why this argument, if accepted, would undermine the doctrine of punishment in the grave.
The argument assumes the verse refers to all dead, not just those in Paradise.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد أفكر كثير من علماء المسلمين في السؤال والعذاب والنعيم في القبر، منهم ضرار بن عمرو، وبشر المريسي، وأكثر المتأخرين من المعتزلة [؟ هم الله عز وجل]، واستدلوا بقوله تعالى «لا يذوقون فيها الموت إلا الموتة الأولى» فإنهم لو أحيوا في القبر لذاقوا موتتين. واستدلوا بقوله تعالى «ربنا أمتنا اثنتين وأحييتنا اثنتين» وتفسيرها: عدم قبل الولادة وهو موت، والموت الطبيعي، وحياة في الدنيا وحياة في الآخرة، فهذان موتتان وحياتان، ولو كان في القبر حياة زادت حياة على ما نصت عليه الآية. وقالوا في قوله تعالى عن آل فرعون «النار يعرضون عليها غدوا وعشيا ويوم تقوم الساعة أدخلوا آل فرعون أشد العذاب» أن النار ليست على المعنى الحقيقي، بل على المعنى المجازي. وعلى ذلك فالنص متشابه، يرد إلى المحكم وهو قوله تعالى «ثم لتسألن عن النعيم» وقوله تعالى «فمن يعمل مثقال ذرة خيرا يره ومن يعمل مثقال ذرة شرا يره» أي في الحياة الآخرة. وجاءت النار بالمعنى المجازي في كثير من آي القرآن منها «إنما يأكلون في بطونهم نارا» المعنى المجازي للنار التي يعرض عليها آل فرعون هو «الشدائد» في الدنيا بسبب معرفتهم الله وبعدهم عن شرائعه. وقالوا في قوله تعالى عن قوم نوح عليه السلام «أغرقوا فأدخلوا نارا»: أغرقوا فيكثوا مدة ثم أدخلوا نارا في الآخرة، أو تكون المدة بين الإغراق والبعث بحسب علم الأموات غير المدة بحسب علم الأحياء. فإن أهل الكهف لما ماتوا ثلثمائة سنة وتسع سنين وبعثوا قالوا «لبثنا يوما أو بعض يوم» والكفار يسألون ويجيبون يوم القيامة «كم لبثتم في الأرض عدد سنين قالوا لبثنا يوما أو بعض يوم» أي أن طول المدة لا يحس به الأموات كما يحس به الأحياء، كما في الحديث «من مات فقد قامت قيامته». ويقولون في الأحاديث المنسوبة إلى الرسول صلى الله عليه وسلم التي تثبت السؤال في القبر والعذاب والنعيم ما يقول فيها المثبتون للسؤال والعذاب والنعيم والمثبتون لا يثبتون بها باستقلال، بل مع تأويل لآي من الكتاب. فلو كانت حجة باستقلال لما قرنوها بالآيات التي أولوها.
Many Muslim scholars have reflected upon the questioning, punishment, and bliss in the grave, among them Dirar ibn Amr, Bishr al-Marisi, and most of the later Mu'tazila [?], and they adduced His saying: 'They will not taste death therein except the first death' — for if they were revived in the grave they would taste two deaths. And they adduced His saying: 'Our Lord, You have made us die twice and given us life twice.' Its interpretation: non-existence before birth is a death, and natural death, and life in this world and life in the Hereafter; thus there are two deaths and two lives. If there were life in the grave, life would be additional to what the verse states. And they said concerning His saying about the family of Pharaoh: 'The Fire, they are exposed to it morning and evening, and on the Day the Hour will arise, cause the family of Pharaoh to enter the severest punishment' — that the Fire is not to be taken literally, but metaphorically. Accordingly, the text is ambiguous (mutashabih), to be referred back to the clear (muhkam) texts, namely His saying 'Then you will surely be asked about the pleasures' and His saying 'So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it' — i.e., in the Hereafter. And Fire has come metaphorically in many Qur'anic verses, including 'They only eat fire into their bellies.' The metaphorical meaning of the Fire to which the family of Pharaoh were exposed is 'hardships' in this world because of their knowledge of God and their distancing from His laws. And they said concerning His saying about the people of Nuh (peace be upon him): 'They were drowned and then made to enter a Fire' — they were drowned, then they remained a period, then they entered a Fire in the Hereafter; or the period between drowning and resurrection is according to the perception of the dead, different from the perception of the living. For the People of the Cave, when they died for three hundred and nine years and then were raised, said: 'We remained a day or part of a day.' And the disbelievers will be asked and will answer on the Day of Resurrection: 'How long did you remain in the earth, number of years?' They will say: 'We remained a day or part of a day' — meaning that the dead do not perceive the length of time as the living do, as in the hadith: 'Whoever dies, his Resurrection has begun.' And they say concerning the hadiths attributed to the Messenger (peace be upon him) that affirm the questioning in the grave and the punishment and bliss: that those who affirm the questioning, punishment, and bliss say about them (i.e., these hadiths) that the affirmers do not rely on them independently, but rather with interpretation of some Qur'anic verses. If they were independent proofs, they would not have accompanied them with the verses they interpreted.
Source pages p. 115

Commentary
This passage presents a series of arguments from those Muslim scholars (identified as Dirar ibn Amr, Bishr al-Marisi, and later Mu'tazila) who deny the reality of questioning, punishment, and bliss in the grave. The author, Imam Juwayni, is reporting their position as a prelude to refuting it. The passage follows a sequential logic: (1) They first cite the Qur'anic verse 'They will not taste death therein except the first death' (44:56), arguing that if the deceased were given life in the grave, they would taste a second death — which contradicts the verse. (2) They then cite the verse 'Our Lord, You have made us die twice and given us life twice' (40:11), interpreting the two deaths as pre-birth non-existence and natural death, and the two lives as life in this world and the Hereafter; adding a third life (in the grave) would violate the verse's enumeration. (3) Regarding the verse about Pharaoh's family being exposed to the Fire morning and evening (40:46), they claim this 'fire' is metaphorical, not literal, referring to worldly hardships due to their opposition to Moses. They support this by citing other verses where 'fire' is used metaphorically (e.g., 4:10), and argue that the text is ambiguous and must be referred to clear verses (muhkam) like 'Then you will surely be asked about the pleasures' (102:8) and the atom's weight verse (99:7-8), which they interpret as referring only to the Hereafter. (4) Concerning the verse about Noah's people (71:25), they offer two explanations: either there is a long interval between drowning and entering Hellfire, or the perception of time for the dead is unlike that of the living. They invoke the story of the People of the Cave (18:19) and a Qur'anic dialogue on the Day of Resurrection (23:112-113) to argue that the dead do not sense the passage of long periods, just as sleepers do. They support this with the famous hadith 'Whoever dies, his Resurrection has begun,' implying that the deceased are not in a state of conscious life. Finally, they address the hadiths that explicitly affirm punishment in the grave, claiming that even the affirmers do not take them as independent proofs but only as confirmations after interpreting Qur'anic verses. If these hadiths were self-sufficient, they would not be accompanied by such interpretations.

Key Terms
سؤال القبر
عذاب القبر
المنكرون
الموتة الأولى

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to the deniers, what would happen if the dead were revived in the grave?
A) They would taste a second death.
B) They would remain alive until Judgment.
C) They would not taste any death.
D) They would enter Paradise immediately.

Key Takeaway
The deniers' first argument is that reviving the dead in the grave would necessitate a second death, contradicting the Quranic assertion of only one death.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The deniers used a verse from the Qur'an to justify rejecting a belief that is well-attested in other texts. Are there any beliefs you hold lightly because a single verse seems to contradict them? What would it take for you to trust the broader teachings of the Qur'an and Sunnah over your own limited understanding?
?
This argument appears logically sound on the surface, yet it is flawed. Where in your own faith are you tempted to prioritize logic over revelation when they seem to conflict? Will you submit your reasoning to the full weight of scripture, even when it is uncomfortable?

The Deniers' Second Argument: No Room for a Third Life in the Grave
Juwayni presents the second argument: the verse about two deaths and two lives.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the second argument of the deniers: that the Quran enumerates exactly two deaths and two lives, leaving no room for an additional life in the grave.

Core Idea
The verse specifies two deaths and two lives – a third life would break that count.

The deniers bring another Quranic verse that explicitly states two deaths and two lives. They interpret these as pre-birth non-existence and natural death for the deaths, and worldly life and afterlife for the lives. Any life in the grave would be a third life, which the verse does not allow. This argument builds on the first by using a different text.

Guided Path
1
Read the verse and its interpretation
Read the Arabic and translation to see how the deniers interpret the verse 'Our Lord, You have made us die twice and given us life twice.'
They interpret the two deaths as non-existence before birth and natural death, and the two lives as worldly life and afterlife.
2
Follow the logic of exclusion
If there are exactly two deaths and two lives, there cannot be a third life (in the grave). The verse is a closed list.
This is an argument from enumeration.
3
Consider the implication
If the deniers are correct, then any doctrine of life in the grave would contradict this verse.
This shows how they use clear verses to challenge ambiguous ones.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد أفكر كثير من علماء المسلمين في السؤال والعذاب والنعيم في القبر، منهم ضرار بن عمرو، وبشر المريسي، وأكثر المتأخرين من المعتزلة [؟ هم الله عز وجل]، واستدلوا بقوله تعالى «لا يذوقون فيها الموت إلا الموتة الأولى» فإنهم لو أحيوا في القبر لذاقوا موتتين. واستدلوا بقوله تعالى «ربنا أمتنا اثنتين وأحييتنا اثنتين» وتفسيرها: عدم قبل الولادة وهو موت، والموت الطبيعي، وحياة في الدنيا وحياة في الآخرة، فهذان موتتان وحياتان، ولو كان في القبر حياة زادت حياة على ما نصت عليه الآية. وقالوا في قوله تعالى عن آل فرعون «النار يعرضون عليها غدوا وعشيا ويوم تقوم الساعة أدخلوا آل فرعون أشد العذاب» أن النار ليست على المعنى الحقيقي، بل على المعنى المجازي. وعلى ذلك فالنص متشابه، يرد إلى المحكم وهو قوله تعالى «ثم لتسألن عن النعيم» وقوله تعالى «فمن يعمل مثقال ذرة خيرا يره ومن يعمل مثقال ذرة شرا يره» أي في الحياة الآخرة. وجاءت النار بالمعنى المجازي في كثير من آي القرآن منها «إنما يأكلون في بطونهم نارا» المعنى المجازي للنار التي يعرض عليها آل فرعون هو «الشدائد» في الدنيا بسبب معرفتهم الله وبعدهم عن شرائعه. وقالوا في قوله تعالى عن قوم نوح عليه السلام «أغرقوا فأدخلوا نارا»: أغرقوا فيكثوا مدة ثم أدخلوا نارا في الآخرة، أو تكون المدة بين الإغراق والبعث بحسب علم الأموات غير المدة بحسب علم الأحياء. فإن أهل الكهف لما ماتوا ثلثمائة سنة وتسع سنين وبعثوا قالوا «لبثنا يوما أو بعض يوم» والكفار يسألون ويجيبون يوم القيامة «كم لبثتم في الأرض عدد سنين قالوا لبثنا يوما أو بعض يوم» أي أن طول المدة لا يحس به الأموات كما يحس به الأحياء، كما في الحديث «من مات فقد قامت قيامته». ويقولون في الأحاديث المنسوبة إلى الرسول صلى الله عليه وسلم التي تثبت السؤال في القبر والعذاب والنعيم ما يقول فيها المثبتون للسؤال والعذاب والنعيم والمثبتون لا يثبتون بها باستقلال، بل مع تأويل لآي من الكتاب. فلو كانت حجة باستقلال لما قرنوها بالآيات التي أولوها.
Many Muslim scholars have reflected upon the questioning, punishment, and bliss in the grave, among them Dirar ibn Amr, Bishr al-Marisi, and most of the later Mu'tazila [?], and they adduced His saying: 'They will not taste death therein except the first death' — for if they were revived in the grave they would taste two deaths. And they adduced His saying: 'Our Lord, You have made us die twice and given us life twice.' Its interpretation: non-existence before birth is a death, and natural death, and life in this world and life in the Hereafter; thus there are two deaths and two lives. For the People of the Cave, when they died for three hundred and nine years and then were raised, said: 'We remained a day or part of a day.' Commentary

The author, Imam Juwayni, is reporting their position as a prelude to refuting it. According to the deniers, how many lives does the verse 'Our Lord, You have made us die twice and given us life twice' permit?

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read this verse, do you feel a sense of certainty about what happens after death, or have you relied on stories and hearsay? How might this precise enumeration change your inner conviction about the afterlife?
?
If the Quran limits the lives to two, why do you still find yourself fearing an intermediate third life? Is your fear rooted in revelation or in cultural narratives? What would it take for you to fully submit to the Quran's count?
?
The disbelievers in the verse address Allah directly with this statement. When you reflect on your own death, do you speak to Allah with the same clarity and acceptance, or do you secretly hope for a second chance? Will you commit today to aligning your beliefs solely with what Allah has explicitly stated?
