The Deniers' Third Argument: Fire as Metaphor for Pharaoh's Family
Juwayni presents the deniers' argument that the fire in Pharaoh's verse is metaphorical.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the deniers' argument that the 'Fire' mentioned in the verse about Pharaoh's family is metaphorical, referring to worldly hardships, not literal punishment in the grave.

Core Idea
The fire in Pharaoh's verse is metaphorical, not literal, to avoid pre-resurrection punishment.

The deniers encounter a verse that seems to describe punishment before Judgment Day: Pharaoh's family is exposed to the Fire morning and evening. They argue that this 'Fire' is not literal but metaphorical, meaning hardships in this world. They support this by citing other verses where 'fire' is used metaphorically, and they refer this ambiguous verse to clear verses that place all requital in the Hereafter.

Guided Path
1
Read the verse and the deniers' claim
Read the Arabic and translation to see how the deniers interpret the verse about Pharaoh's family.
They claim the Fire is metaphorical, not real.
2
Examine their supporting evidence
Notice they cite other verses where fire is used metaphorically (e.g., 4:10) and they refer to clear verses (muhkam) about questioning on pleasures and atom's weight.
This shows their hermeneutical method of interpreting ambiguous verses by clear ones.
3
Understand the principle
The deniers argue that any verse suggesting punishment before the Hereafter must be interpreted metaphorically to align with clear verses that locate all recompense in the afterlife.
This argument defends their denial of punishment in the grave.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد أفكر كثير من علماء المسلمين في السؤال والعذاب والنعيم في القبر، منهم ضرار بن عمرو، وبشر المريسي، وأكثر المتأخرين من المعتزلة [؟ هم الله عز وجل]، واستدلوا بقوله تعالى «لا يذوقون فيها الموت إلا الموتة الأولى» فإنهم لو أحيوا في القبر لذاقوا موتتين. واستدلوا بقوله تعالى «ربنا أمتنا اثنتين وأحييتنا اثنتين» وتفسيرها: عدم قبل الولادة وهو موت، والموت الطبيعي، وحياة في الدنيا وحياة في الآخرة، فهذان موتتان وحياتان، ولو كان في القبر حياة زادت حياة على ما نصت عليه الآية. وقالوا في قوله تعالى عن آل فرعون «النار يعرضون عليها غدوا وعشيا ويوم تقوم الساعة أدخلوا آل فرعون أشد العذاب» أن النار ليست على المعنى الحقيقي، بل على المعنى المجازي. وعلى ذلك فالنص متشابه، يرد إلى المحكم وهو قوله تعالى «ثم لتسألن عن النعيم» وقوله تعالى «فمن يعمل مثقال ذرة خيرا يره ومن يعمل مثقال ذرة شرا يره» أي في الحياة الآخرة. وجاءت النار بالمعنى المجازي في كثير من آي القرآن منها «إنما يأكلون في بطونهم نارا» المعنى المجازي للنار التي يعرض عليها آل فرعون هو «الشدائد» في الدنيا بسبب معرفتهم الله وبعدهم عن شرائعه. وقالوا في قوله تعالى عن قوم نوح عليه السلام «أغرقوا فأدخلوا نارا»: أغرقوا فيكثوا مدة ثم أدخلوا نارا في الآخرة، أو تكون المدة بين الإغراق والبعث بحسب علم الأموات غير المدة بحسب علم الأحياء. فإن أهل الكهف لما ماتوا ثلثمائة سنة وتسع سنين وبعثوا قالوا «لبثنا يوما أو بعض يوم» والكفار يسألون ويجيبون يوم القيامة «كم لبثتم في الأرض عدد سنين قالوا لبثنا يوما أو بعض يوم» أي أن طول المدة لا يحس به الأموات كما يحس به الأحياء، كما في الحديث «من مات فقد قامت قيامته». ويقولون في الأحاديث المنسوبة إلى الرسول صلى الله عليه وسلم التي تثبت السؤال في القبر والعذاب والنعيم ما يقول فيها المثبتون للسؤال والعذاب والنعيم والمثبتون لا يثبتون بها باستقلال، بل مع تأويل لآي من الكتاب. فلو كانت حجة باستقلال لما قرنوها بالآيات التي أولوها.
Many Muslim scholars have reflected upon the questioning, punishment, and bliss in the grave, among them Dirar ibn Amr, Bishr al-Marisi, and most of the later Mu'tazila [?], and they adduced His saying: 'They will not taste death therein except the first death' — for if they were revived in the grave they would taste two deaths. And they adduced His saying: 'Our Lord, You have made us die twice and given us life twice.' Its interpretation: non-existence before birth is a death, and natural death, and life in this world and life in the Hereafter; thus there are two deaths and two lives. If there were life in the grave, life would be additional to what the verse states. And they said concerning His saying about the family of Pharaoh: 'The Fire, they are exposed to it morning and evening, and on the Day the Hour will arise, cause the family of Pharaoh to enter the severest punishment' — that the Fire is not to be taken literally, but metaphorically. Accordingly, the text is ambiguous (mutashabih), to be referred back to the clear (muhkam) texts, namely His saying 'Then you will surely be asked about the pleasures' and His saying 'So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it' — i.e., in the Hereafter. And Fire has come metaphorically in many Qur'anic verses, including 'They only eat fire into their bellies.' The metaphorical meaning of the Fire to which the family of Pharaoh were exposed is 'hardships' in this world because of their knowledge of God and their distancing from His laws. And they said concerning His saying about the people of Nuh (peace be upon him): 'They were drowned and then made to enter a Fire' — they were drowned, then they remained a period, then they entered a Fire in the Hereafter; or the period between drowning and resurrection is according to the perception of the dead, different from the perception of the living. For the People of the Cave, when they died for three hundred and nine years and then were raised, said: 'We remained a day or part of a day.' And the disbelievers will be asked and will answer on the Day of Resurrection: 'How long did you remain in the earth, number of years?' They will say: 'We remained a day or part of a day' — meaning that the dead do not perceive the length of time as the living do, as in the hadith: 'Whoever dies, his Resurrection has begun.' And they say concerning the hadiths attributed to the Messenger (peace be upon him) that affirm the questioning in the grave and the punishment and bliss: that those who affirm the questioning, punishment, and bliss say about them (i.e., these hadiths) that the affirmers do not rely on them independently, but rather with interpretation of some Qur'anic verses. If they were independent proofs, they would not have accompanied them with the verses they interpreted.
Source pages p. 115

Commentary
This passage presents a series of arguments from those Muslim scholars (identified as Dirar ibn Amr, Bishr al-Marisi, and later Mu'tazila) who deny the reality of questioning, punishment, and bliss in the grave. The author, Imam Juwayni, is reporting their position as a prelude to refuting it. The passage follows a sequential logic: (1) They first cite the Qur'anic verse 'They will not taste death therein except the first death' (44:56), arguing that if the deceased were given life in the grave, they would taste a second death — which contradicts the verse. (2) They then cite the verse 'Our Lord, You have made us die twice and given us life twice' (40:11), interpreting the two deaths as pre-birth non-existence and natural death, and the two lives as life in this world and the Hereafter; adding a third life (in the grave) would violate the verse's enumeration. (3) Regarding the verse about Pharaoh's family being exposed to the Fire morning and evening (40:46), they claim this 'fire' is metaphorical, not literal, referring to worldly hardships due to their opposition to Moses. They support this by citing other verses where 'fire' is used metaphorically (e.g., 4:10), and argue that the text is ambiguous and must be referred to clear verses (muhkam) like 'Then you will surely be asked about the pleasures' (102:8) and the atom's weight verse (99:7-8), which they interpret as referring only to the Hereafter. (4) Concerning the verse about Noah's people (71:25), they offer two explanations: either there is a long interval between drowning and entering Hellfire, or the perception of time for the dead is unlike that of the living. They invoke the story of the People of the Cave (18:19) and a Qur'anic dialogue on the Day of Resurrection (23:112-113) to argue that the dead do not sense the passage of long periods, just as sleepers do. They support this with the famous hadith 'Whoever dies, his Resurrection has begun,' implying that the deceased are not in a state of conscious life. Finally, they address the hadiths that explicitly affirm punishment in the grave, claiming that even the affirmers do not take them as independent proofs but only as confirmations after interpreting Qur'anic verses. If these hadiths were self-sufficient, they would not be accompanied by such interpretations.

Key Terms
متشابه
محكم
تأويل
مجاز

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The verse about Pharaoh's family proves punishment in the grave.
Correction: The deniers argue it is metaphorical, referring to worldly hardships, not literal punishment.

Checkpoint
According to the deniers, to what clear verses (muhkam) should the ambiguous verse about Pharaoh's family be referred?
A) Verses about Paradise and Hellfire
B) Verses about questioning on pleasures and the atom's weight
C) Verses about jihad and hijab
D) Verses about creation of Adam

Key Takeaway
The deniers interpret the 'Fire' in Pharaoh's verse as metaphorical hardships, not literal punishment, to avoid affirming punishment before Judgment Day.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read verses about punishment, do you ever find yourself wishing they were metaphorical so you don't have to face the reality of divine justice? What would it take for you to truly fear the literal Fire?
?
The deniers used reasoning to avoid affirming punishment before Judgment. Are there areas in your life where you use 'interpretation' to soften clear commands or warnings from Allah? Will you examine those today?
?
This passage shows how even clear verses can be twisted to avoid accountability. Right now, what is one truth from the Quran that you have been avoiding applying in your daily life, and what step will you take this week to live by it?

The Deniers' Fourth Argument: Time Perception of the Dead
Juwayni presents the deniers' argument from time perception.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the deniers' argument that the dead do not perceive the passage of time, so the interval between death and resurrection seems brief, and there is no conscious life in the grave.

Core Idea
The dead perceive no time – the long wait feels like a day or less, so no grave life is needed.

The deniers address a verse that seems to indicate punishment immediately after drowning (Noah's people). They offer two explanations: either the punishment is postponed to the afterlife, or the dead experience time differently. They use the story of the People of the Cave and a Quranic dialogue on Judgment Day to show that the dead perceive long periods as very short, citing the hadith 'Whoever dies has begun his Resurrection.'

Guided Path
1
Read the verse about Noah's people
Read the Arabic and translation to see the verse and the deniers' two interpretations.
The verse says they were drowned and then made to enter a Fire.
2
Understand the time-perception argument
The deniers argue that the dead do not sense the passage of time, so the period between death and resurrection seems like a short time, and there is no conscious life in the grave.
They use the People of the Cave and Judgment Day dialogue as evidence.
3
Consider the hadith
They cite the hadith 'Whoever dies, his Resurrection has begun' to support that the dead are not in an intermediate state of life.
This hadith is used to argue that death immediately leads to the Hereafter.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد أفكر كثير من علماء المسلمين في السؤال والعذاب والنعيم في القبر، منهم ضرار بن عمرو، وبشر المريسي، وأكثر المتأخرين من المعتزلة [؟ هم الله عز وجل]، واستدلوا بقوله تعالى «لا يذوقون فيها الموت إلا الموتة الأولى» فإنهم لو أحيوا في القبر لذاقوا موتتين. واستدلوا بقوله تعالى «ربنا أمتنا اثنتين وأحييتنا اثنتين» وتفسيرها: عدم قبل الولادة وهو موت، والموت الطبيعي، وحياة في الدنيا وحياة في الآخرة، فهذان موتتان وحياتان، ولو كان في القبر حياة زادت حياة على ما نصت عليه الآية. وقالوا في قوله تعالى عن آل فرعون «النار يعرضون عليها غدوا وعشيا ويوم تقوم الساعة أدخلوا آل فرعون أشد العذاب» أن النار ليست على المعنى الحقيقي، بل على المعنى المجازي. وعلى ذلك فالنص متشابه، يرد إلى المحكم وهو قوله تعالى «ثم لتسألن عن النعيم» وقوله تعالى «فمن يعمل مثقال ذرة خيرا يره ومن يعمل مثقال ذرة شرا يره» أي في الحياة الآخرة. وجاءت النار بالمعنى المجازي في كثير من آي القرآن منها «إنما يأكلون في بطونهم نارا» المعنى المجازي للنار التي يعرض عليها آل فرعون هو «الشدائد» في الدنيا بسبب معرفتهم الله وبعدهم عن شرائعه. وقالوا في قوله تعالى عن قوم نوح عليه السلام «أغرقوا فأدخلوا نارا»: أغرقوا فيكثوا مدة ثم أدخلوا نارا في الآخرة، أو تكون المدة بين الإغراق والبعث بحسب علم الأموات غير المدة بحسب علم الأحياء. فإن أهل الكهف لما ماتوا ثلثمائة سنة وتسع سنين وبعثوا قالوا «لبثنا يوما أو بعض يوم» والكفار يسألون ويجيبون يوم القيامة «كم لبثتم في الأرض عدد سنين قالوا لبثنا يوما أو بعض يوم» أي أن طول المدة لا يحس به الأموات كما يحس به الأحياء، كما في الحديث «من مات فقد قامت قيامته». ويقولون في الأحاديث المنسوبة إلى الرسول صلى الله عليه وسلم التي تثبت السؤال في القبر والعذاب والنعيم ما يقول فيها المثبتون للسؤال والعذاب والنعيم والمثبتون لا يثبتون بها باستقلال، بل مع تأويل لآي من الكتاب. فلو كانت حجة باستقلال لما قرنوها بالآيات التي أولوها.
Many Muslim scholars have reflected upon the questioning, punishment, and bliss in the grave, among them Dirar ibn Amr, Bishr al-Marisi, and most of the later Mu'tazila [?], and they adduced His saying: 'They will not taste death therein except the first death' — for if they were revived in the grave they would taste two deaths. And they adduced His saying: 'Our Lord, You have made us die twice and given us life twice.' Its interpretation: non-existence before birth is a death, and natural death, and life in this world and life in the Hereafter; thus there are two deaths and two lives. If there were life in the grave, life would be additional to what the verse states. And they said concerning His saying about the family of Pharaoh: 'The Fire, they are exposed to it morning and evening, and on the Day the Hour will arise, cause the family of Pharaoh to enter the severest punishment' — that the Fire is not to be taken literally, but metaphorically. Accordingly, the text is ambiguous (mutashabih), to be referred back to the clear (muhkam) texts, namely His saying 'Then you will surely be asked about the pleasures' and His saying 'So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it' — i.e., in the Hereafter. And Fire has come metaphorically in many Qur'anic verses, including 'They only eat fire into their bellies.' The metaphorical meaning of the Fire to which the family of Pharaoh were exposed is 'hardships' in this world because of their knowledge of God and their distancing from His laws. And they said concerning His saying about the people of Nuh (peace be upon him): 'They were drowned and then made to enter a Fire' — they were drowned, then they remained a period, then they entered a Fire in the Hereafter; or the period between drowning and resurrection is according to the perception of the dead, different from the perception of the living. For the People of the Cave, when they died for three hundred and nine years and then were raised, said: 'We remained a day or part of a day.' And the disbelievers will be asked and will answer on the Day of Resurrection: 'How long did you remain in the earth, number of years?' They will say: 'We remained a day or part of a day' — meaning that the dead do not perceive the length of time as the living do, as in the hadith: 'Whoever dies, his Resurrection has begun.' And they say concerning the hadiths attributed to the Messenger (peace be upon him) that affirm the questioning in the grave and the punishment and bliss: that those who affirm the questioning, punishment, and bliss say about them (i.e., these hadiths) that the affirmers do not rely on them independently, but rather with interpretation of some Qur'anic verses. If they were independent proofs, they would not have accompanied them with the verses they interpreted.

Commentary
This passage presents a series of arguments from those Muslim scholars (identified as Dirar ibn Amr, Bishr al-Marisi, and later Mu'tazila) who deny the reality of questioning, punishment, and bliss in the grave. The author, Imam Juwayni, is reporting their position as a prelude to refuting it. The passage follows a sequential logic: (1) They first cite the Qur'anic verse 'They will not taste death therein except the first death' (44:56), arguing that if the deceased were given life in the grave, they would taste a second death — which contradicts the verse. (2) They then cite the verse 'Our Lord, You have made us die twice and given us life twice' (40:11), interpreting the two deaths as pre-birth non-existence and natural death, and the two lives as life in this world and the Hereafter; adding a third life (in the grave) would violate the verse's enumeration. (3) Regarding the verse about Pharaoh's family being exposed to the Fire morning and evening (40:46), they claim this 'fire' is metaphorical, not literal, referring to worldly hardships due to their opposition to Moses. They support this by citing other verses where 'fire' is used metaphorically (e.g., 4:10), and argue that the text is ambiguous and must be referred to clear verses (muhkam) like 'Then you will surely be asked about the pleasures' (102:8) and the atom's weight verse (99:7-8), which they interpret as referring only to the Hereafter. (4) Concerning the verse about Noah's people (71:25), they offer two explanations: either there is a long interval between drowning and entering Hellfire, or the perception of time for the dead is unlike that of the living. They invoke the story of the People of the Cave (18:19) and a Qur'anic dialogue on the Day of Resurrection (23:112-113) to argue that the dead do not sense the passage of long periods, just as sleepers do. They support this with the famous hadith 'Whoever dies, his Resurrection has begun,' implying that the deceased are not in a state of conscious life. Finally, they address the hadiths that explicitly affirm punishment in the grave, claiming that even the affirmers do not take them as independent proofs but only as confirmations after interpreting Qur'anic verses. If these hadiths were self-sufficient, they would not be accompanied by such interpretations.

Checkpoint
What evidence do the deniers use to show that the dead perceive time differently?
A) The story of the People of the Cave
B) The verse about Pharaoh's family
C) The verse about two deaths
D) The hadith about the Prophet's night journey

Key Takeaway
The deniers argue that since the dead do not perceive the passage of time, there is no conscious life in the grave; death transitions directly to the Hereafter.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you were to die right now and then be resurrected, and you felt that only a day had passed, how would that change the way you view the time you have left? Would you live differently knowing that the 'long' period of this life is perceived as nothing by the dead?
?
The deniers use the perception of time to deny an intermediate life. But how does your own perception of time in this life—your sense of urgency or procrastination—reflect your belief in what lies after death? Are you living as if the next moment could be your final reckoning?
?
The hadith says 'Whoever dies, his Resurrection has begun.' Does that thought bring you fear or hope? How would your daily actions change if you truly believed that death is not an end but the immediate start of your ultimate accountability?

The Deniers' Approach to Hadith on Grave Punishment
Juwayni presents the deniers' critique of hadiths used to affirm punishment in the grave.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the deniers of punishment in the grave treat the hadiths that affirm it: they argue that even the affirmers only use them in conjunction with interpretation of Quranic verses, not as independent proofs.

Core Idea
Deniers claim the affirmers themselves do not rely on hadith independently, so hadith alone cannot prove grave punishment.

The deniers address the hadiths that explicitly mention questioning and punishment in the grave. They argue that those who affirm the punishment (the 'muthbitun') do not rely on these hadiths as independent proofs; instead, they also interpret certain Quranic verses to support them. If the hadiths were sufficient on their own, the affirmers would not need to bring in scriptural interpretation. This undercuts the hadiths as a stand-alone evidence.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on hadith
Read the Arabic and translation to see the deniers' argument about how the affirmers treat hadith.
The deniers claim the affirmers do not take hadith as independent proofs.
2
Understand the logic
If hadiths were sufficient, the affirmers would not need to accompany them with Quranic interpretations. Therefore, the hadiths are not strong enough alone.
This is an argument about the epistemology of hadith.
3
Reflect on the implication
This argument allows the deniers to dismiss the hadiths as relying on the very Quranic verses that they (the deniers) interpret differently.
The deniers are essentially turning the affirmers' method against them.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد أفكر كثير من علماء المسلمين في السؤال والعذاب والنعيم في القبر، منهم ضرار بن عمرو، وبشر المريسي، وأكثر المتأخرين من المعتزلة [؟ هم الله عز وجل]، واستدلوا بقوله تعالى «لا يذوقون فيها الموت إلا الموتة الأولى» فإنهم لو أحيوا في القبر لذاقوا موتتين. واستدلوا بقوله تعالى «ربنا أمتنا اثنتين وأحييتنا اثنتين» وتفسيرها: عدم قبل الولادة وهو موت، والموت الطبيعي، وحياة في الدنيا وحياة في الآخرة، فهذان موتتان وحياتان، ولو كان في القبر حياة زادت حياة على ما نصت عليه الآية. وقالوا في قوله تعالى عن آل فرعون «النار يعرضون عليها غدوا وعشيا ويوم تقوم الساعة أدخلوا آل فرعون أشد العذاب» أن النار ليست على المعنى الحقيقي، بل على المعنى المجازي. وعلى ذلك فالنص متشابه، يرد إلى المحكم وهو قوله تعالى «ثم لتسألن عن النعيم» وقوله تعالى «فمن يعمل مثقال ذرة خيرا يره ومن يعمل مثقال ذرة شرا يره» أي في الحياة الآخرة. وجاءت النار بالمعنى المجازي في كثير من آي القرآن منها «إنما يأكلون في بطونهم نارا» المعنى المجازي للنار التي يعرض عليها آل فرعون هو «الشدائد» في الدنيا بسبب معرفتهم الله وبعدهم عن شرائعه. وقالوا في قوله تعالى عن قوم نوح عليه السلام «أغرقوا فأدخلوا نارا»: أغرقوا فيكثوا مدة ثم أدخلوا نارا في الآخرة، أو تكون المدة بين الإغراق والبعث بحسب علم الأموات غير المدة بحسب علم الأحياء. فإن أهل الكهف لما ماتوا ثلثمائة سنة وتسع سنين وبعثوا قالوا «لبثنا يوما أو بعض يوم» والكفار يسألون ويجيبون يوم القيامة «كم لبثتم في الأرض عدد سنين قالوا لبثنا يوما أو بعض يوم» أي أن طول المدة لا يحس به الأموات كما يحس به الأحياء، كما في الحديث «من مات فقد قامت قيامته». ويقولون في الأحاديث المنسوبة إلى الرسول صلى الله عليه وسلم التي تثبت السؤال في القبر والعذاب والنعيم ما يقول فيها المثبتون للسؤال والعذاب والنعيم والمثبتون لا يثبتون بها باستقلال، بل مع تأويل لآي من الكتاب. فلو كانت حجة باستقلال لما قرنوها بالآيات التي أولوها.
Many Muslim scholars have reflected upon the questioning, punishment, and bliss in the grave, among them Dirar ibn Amr, Bishr al-Marisi, and most of the later Mu'tazila [?], and they adduced His saying: 'They will not taste death therein except the first death' — for if they were revived in the grave they would taste two deaths. And they adduced His saying: 'Our Lord, You have made us die twice and given us life twice.' Its interpretation: non-existence before birth is a death, and natural death, and life in this world and life in the Hereafter; thus there are two deaths and two lives. If there were life in the grave, life would be additional to what the verse states. And they said concerning His saying about the family of Pharaoh: 'The Fire, they are exposed to it morning and evening, and on the Day the Hour will arise, cause the family of Pharaoh to enter the severest punishment' — that the Fire is not to be taken literally, but metaphorically. Accordingly, the text is ambiguous (mutashabih), to be referred back to the clear (muhkam) texts, namely His saying 'Then you will surely be asked about the pleasures' and His saying 'So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it' — i.e., in the Hereafter. And Fire has come metaphorically in many Qur'anic verses, including 'They only eat fire into their bellies.' The metaphorical meaning of the Fire to which the family of Pharaoh were exposed is 'hardships' in this world because of their knowledge of God and their distancing from His laws. And they said concerning His saying about the people of Nuh (peace be upon him): 'They were drowned and then made to enter a Fire' — they were drowned, then they remained a period, then they entered a Fire in the Hereafter; or the period between drowning and resurrection is according to the perception of the dead, different from the perception of the living. For the People of the Cave, when they died for three hundred and nine years and then were raised, said: 'We remained a day or part of a day.' And the disbelievers will be asked and will answer on the Day of Resurrection: 'How long did you remain in the earth, number of years?' They will say: 'We remained a day or part of a day' — meaning that the dead do not perceive the length of time as the living do, as in the hadith: 'Whoever dies, his Resurrection has begun.' And they say concerning the hadiths attributed to the Messenger (peace be upon him) that affirm the questioning in the grave and the punishment and bliss: that those who affirm the questioning, punishment, and bliss say about them (i.e., these hadiths) that the affirmers do not rely on them independently, but rather with interpretation of some Qur'anic verses. If they were independent proofs, they would not have accompanied them with the verses they interpreted.
Source pages p. 115

Commentary
This passage presents a series of arguments from those Muslim scholars (identified as Dirar ibn Amr, Bishr al-Marisi, and later Mu'tazila) who deny the reality of questioning, punishment, and bliss in the grave. The author, Imam Juwayni, is reporting their position as a prelude to refuting it. The passage follows a sequential logic: (1) They first cite the Qur'anic verse 'They will not taste death therein except the first death' (44:56), arguing that if the deceased were given life in the grave, they would taste a second death — which contradicts the verse. (2) They then cite the verse 'Our Lord, You have made us die twice and given us life twice' (40:11), interpreting the two deaths as pre-birth non-existence and natural death, and the two lives as life in this world and the Hereafter; adding a third life (in the grave) would violate the verse's enumeration. (3) Regarding the verse about Pharaoh's family being exposed to the Fire morning and evening (40:46), they claim this 'fire' is metaphorical, not literal, referring to worldly hardships due to their opposition to Moses. They support this by citing other verses where 'fire' is used metaphorically (e.g., 4:10), and argue that the text is ambiguous and must be referred to clear verses (muhkam) like 'Then you will surely be asked about the pleasures' (102:8) and the atom's weight verse (99:7-8), which they interpret as referring only to the Hereafter. (4) Concerning the verse about Noah's people (71:25), they offer two explanations: either there is a long interval between drowning and entering Hellfire, or the perception of time for the dead is unlike that of the living. They invoke the story of the People of the Cave (18:19) and a Qur'anic dialogue on the Day of Resurrection (23:112-113) to argue that the dead do not sense the passage of long periods, just as sleepers do. They support this with the famous hadith 'Whoever dies, his Resurrection has begun,' implying that the deceased are not in a state of conscious life. Finally, they address the hadiths that explicitly affirm punishment in the grave, claiming that even the affirmers do not take them as independent proofs but only as confirmations after interpreting Qur'anic verses. If these hadiths were self-sufficient, they would not be accompanied by such interpretations.

Key Terms
المثبتون
المنكرون
الأحاديث
تأويل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The deniers reject all hadith about punishment in the grave.
Correction: They do not reject them outright; they argue that even the affirmers do not use them independently, so they are not decisive.

Checkpoint
According to the deniers, why do the affirmers of punishment in the grave not treat the hadiths as independent proofs?
A) Because the hadiths are weak in transmission
B) Because the affirmers also include Quranic interpretations
C) Because the hadiths contradict the Quran
D) Because the hadiths are few in number

Key Takeaway
The deniers argue that since even the affirmers use Quranic interpretation alongside hadith, the hadiths alone are insufficient to prove punishment in the grave.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Reflect on your own certainty about punishment in the grave. Is it based on a deep study of combined evidence from Quran and hadith, or have you simply accepted what you were taught? How would your faith be impacted if you discovered that the hadiths you rely on are not considered independent proofs even by those who affirm the belief? What concrete step can you take this week to ground your belief in stronger evidence?
?
The deniers here point out an inconsistency in the affirmers’ methodology: they claim to rely on hadith, but they still need Quranic interpretation. Have you ever held a belief based on a single proof text without considering how scholars actually use that text? Are there areas in your aqeedah where you might be relying on isolated evidence? Will you commit to studying how scholars combine sources before settling a belief?
?
This passage shows that even the strongest proponents of a doctrine do not treat certain proof texts as independent. How does that challenge your assumption that a few hadiths are enough to build a conviction? Are you willing to examine your own methodology for forming beliefs, even if it means unsettling comfortable conclusions? What would it take for you to become more rigorous in how you use evidence in matters of faith?

The Dead Do Not Perceive Time in the Grave
Citing Quranic verses and hadith to show that the dead do not perceive time passage.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that according to Islamic sources, the dead do not perceive the passage of time in the grave, experiencing the resurrection immediately upon death.

Core Idea
For the dead, time collapses; death brings immediate resurrection.

Imam al-Juwayni presents scriptural evidence for a profound truth: the dead are not aware of how much time passes in the grave. Using Quranic verses and a prophetic hadith, he shows that the afterlife begins at the moment of death, shielding the deceased from the long wait. Let's examine these proofs.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the concise style: only two verses and a hadith.
2
Understand the Quranic evidence
Identify the two Quranic verses cited. Note how both describe people on Judgment Day thinking they stayed only a day or part of a day, showing they did not perceive the long period in the grave.
Verse 18:19 is from the story of the People of the Cave; 23:112-113 speaks of disbelievers on Judgment Day.
3
Connect to the hadith
Consider the hadith 'Whoever dies has indeed established his resurrection.' How does this reinforce the Quranic evidence? It indicates that death itself is the start of the afterlife, so the dead do not experience the wait.
This is a strong statement: the moment of death and the Hour are linked for the deceased.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قالوا: (لبثنا يوماً أو بعض يوم) (الكهف: ١٩) والكفار يسألون ويجيبون يوم القيامة (كم لبثتم في الأرض عدد سنين قالوا: لبثنا يوماً أو بعض يوم) (المؤمنون: ١١٢-١١٣) أي أن طول المدة لا يحس بها الأموات، كما يحس به الأحياء، كما في الحديث «من مات فقد قامت قيامته».
They said: "We remained a day or part of a day" (al-Kahf: 19) and the disbelievers will ask and answer on the Day of Resurrection: "How many years did you remain on earth? They said: We remained a day or part of a day" (al-Mu'minun: 112-113), meaning that the dead do not perceive the length of time, as the living perceive it, as in the hadith: "Whoever dies, his resurrection has indeed occurred."
Source pages p. 116

Commentary
This passage provides scriptural evidence that the dead do not perceive the length of time they spend in the grave. It cites Quran 18:19 and 23:112-113 where people on the Day of Judgment say they stayed only a day or part of a day, showing their lack of subjective temporal awareness. It also cites the hadith 'Whoever dies has indeed established his resurrection', indicating that the moment of death is experienced as the onset of the Hour. The point is that the afterlife begins immediately for the deceased, though worldly time continues.

Key Terms
الموت
القيامة
المدة
الإحساس

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the dead are completely unaware of everything.
Correction: The passage only addresses awareness of time passage, not other forms of perception like punishment or bliss.

Checkpoint
What do the Quranic verses cited indicate about the dead's perception of time?
A) They perceive exactly the same length of time as the living.
B) They perceive it as very short.
C) They are completely unconscious.
D) They are aware of everything that happens.

Key Takeaway
The dead do not experience the length of time in the grave; they encounter the resurrection immediately upon dying.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you were to die right now, what would you be taken to account for? Are you prepared to face your Lord at this very moment, as the hadith says your resurrection has occurred? What is one thing you can change today to be ready?
?
The People of the Cave thought they slept only a day, yet centuries passed. You have been alive for years. When you stand before Allah, what will you say about your time on earth? Will you, like the disbelievers, claim it was but a day? Or will you have used it well? What specific deed have you done today that will testify for you?

The Combined Evidence for Grave Punishment
Clarifies the combined evidence methodology for grave punishment.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the Ash'ari school does not base the belief in grave punishment exclusively on hadith, but also on interpretation of Quranic verses.

Core Idea
Grave punishment is affirmed through both hadith and Quranic interpretation, not hadith alone.

This passage addresses a common methodological question: how do scholars who affirm the punishment of the grave rely on hadith? Imam Juwayni explains that they do not treat hadith as an independent proof, but combine it with interpretation of Quranic verses. This reflects a careful approach to eschatological evidence.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic passage carefully, noting the key terms and the flow of the argument.
The passage is a single sentence with a conditional conclusion.
2
Understand key terms
Identify the terms 'المثبتون', 'السؤال في القبر', 'العذاب', 'النعيم', and 'التأويل'. These are central to the discussion.
'المثبتون' refers to those who affirm the realities of the grave.
3
Identify the main claim
The passage states that the affirmers (المثبتون) do not rely on hadith independently (باستقلال), but rather with interpretation of Quranic verses.
This is the core methodological point.
4
Analyze the reasoning
Notice the logical argument: if the hadith were an independent proof, they would not have coupled them with the Quranic verses they interpreted.
This shows that the affirmers see the evidence as cumulative.
5
Reflect on the implication
Consider why this methodology is important: it prevents over-reliance on isolated hadith and maintains a balanced approach to eschatological beliefs.
This reflects the broader Ash'ari emphasis on combining scriptural sources.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقولون في الأحاديث المنسوبة إلى الرسول صلى الله عليه وسلم التي تثبت السؤال في القبر والعذاب والنعيم ما يقول فيها المثبتون للسؤال والعذاب والنعيم، والمثبتون لا يثبتون بها باستقلال، بل مع تأويل لآي من الكتاب. فلو كانت حجة باستقلال لما قرنوها بالآيات التي أولوها.
And they say regarding the hadiths attributed to the Messenger (peace be upon him) that establish the questioning in the grave and the punishment and bliss: what the affirmers of the questioning, punishment, and bliss say about them—and the affirmers do not establish them independently, but rather with interpretation of some verses of the Book. For if they were an independent proof, they would not have coupled them with the verses they interpreted.
Source pages p. 116

Commentary
This passage explains the methodological approach of those who affirm the questioning and punishment in the grave (المثبتون). They do not rely solely on the hadith reports that mention these events; rather, they also interpret Quranic verses to support the same conclusion. The author argues that if the hadith were independently sufficient as proof, they would not need to combine them with Quranic interpretation. This demonstrates a conservative methodology that requires both hadith and Quranic support for eschatological beliefs.

Key Terms
المثبتون
السؤال في القبر
العذاب
النعيم
التأويل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think that the Ash'ari school bases grave punishment solely on isolated hadith reports.
Correction: The passage shows that they actually combine hadith with Quranic interpretation, indicating a reliance on both scriptural sources.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, why do those who affirm grave punishment not rely on hadith independently?
A) Because the hadith are weak.
B) Because they also interpret Quranic verses to support the same conclusion.
C) Because they reject hadith altogether.
D) Because the Quran alone is sufficient and they do not use hadith.

Key Takeaway
The affirmers of grave punishment use both hadith and Quranic interpretation as complementary evidence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you seek certainty about matters like the grave, do you actively gather evidence from both Quran and hadith, or do you rely on a single proof? How might your conviction deepen if you followed the combined approach of the affirmers?
?
This passage shows that even scholars humbly combine sources rather than insisting on one alone. Is there an area of your faith where you have been relying on a single proof or authority? What would it look like for you to seek complementary evidence from other sources?
?
The affirmers interpreted Quranic verses to align with hadith. Do you approach the Quran with the same willingness to reconcile apparent tensions with other texts, or do you sometimes dismiss verses that seem to contradict your understanding? What does your response reveal about your commitment to truth?

Juwayni's View on the Pre-Creation of Paradise and Hell
Juwayni argues for pre-creation of Paradise and Hell, with objections and responses.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Imam al-Juwayni's argument that there is no rational impossibility in God creating Paradise and Hell before the Day of Judgment, and the key objections and responses surrounding this issue.

Core Idea
Creating Paradise and Hell before Judgment Day is not impossible; the Quran supports it.

Imam al-Juwayni engages in a theological debate about the timing of Paradise and Hell's creation. While some scholars argue they will be created only after Judgment Day, Juwayni contends that pre-creation is not impossible. He bases his view on the verse 'Prepared for the righteous' and addresses objections. This passage presents his reasoning and the counter-arguments.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once to get the overall flow, then read the translation. Notice the structure: Juwayni's claim, his proof, objections, and responses.
The passage is dense; take your time.
2
Identify Juwayni's core claim and proof
Find where Juwayni states that there is no impossibility (لا استحالة) and the verse he uses as evidence (أعدت للمتقين). Understand why this verse supports pre-creation.
The literal meaning of 'prepared' implies it already exists.
3
Analyze the three objections
The passage presents three objections: 1) 'أعدت' as future certainty, 2) contradiction between eternal fruit and universal perishing, 3) spatial impossibility of Paradise encompassing heavens and earth. Note each objection and its Quranic basis.
These objections are from other scholars.
4
Understand the responses
Juwayni implicitly responds: the first objection is countered by noting past tense for certainty; the second is left as a question; the third suggests a metaphorical interpretation (كناية). Consider how these responses defend his position.
Some responses are implied rather than explicit.
5
Reflect on the theological implications
Consider how this debate affects our understanding of divine attributes, eschatology, and Quranic interpretation. Why does it matter whether Paradise is created now or later?
This connects to broader 'samiyyat' topics.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وبين الجويني عبد الملك أنه لا استحالة في تقديم خلق الجنة والنار على يوم الجزاء، وقد دفعه إلى هذا القول قوله تعالى: (أعدت للمتقين)، ودفع غيره إلى القول بأن الجنان خارجة عن أقطار السموات والأرض فكيف تنطوي عليهما السموات، تفسيرهم بمعنى استعد كما في قوله: (أتى أمر الله) (النحل: ١)، والمراد [؟] يأتي في، وعبر الله بالماضي لتحقق الوقوع، وأن هذا الأمر كائن لا محالة. وفهمهم المعارضة بين قوله تعالى: (مثل الجنة التي وعد المتقون، تجري من تحت الأنهار، أكلها دائم وظلها) (الرعد: ٣٥) وبين قوله تعالى: (كل شيء هالك إلا وجهه) (القصص: ٤٨) فإن دوام الأكل معارض بهلاك كل شيء غير ذات الله، وعليه لو كانت الجنة مخلوقة لوجب هلاك آكلها فلم [؟] دائما. وأيضا استدلوا بقوله تعالى: (اعرضها السموات والأرض) (آل عمران: ١٣٣) ولا يتصور ذلك إلا بعد فناء السموات والأرض لامتناع تداخل الأجسام، إن لم يكن التعبير كناية عن عظم الاتساع.
And al-Juwayni, Abd al-Malik, explained that there is no impossibility in advancing the creation of Paradise and Hell before the Day of Recompense. He was driven to this statement by His saying: (Prepared for the righteous). And it drove others to say that the Gardens are outside the regions of the heavens and the earth, so how can the heavens encompass them? Their interpretation is in the sense of 'has been made ready', as in His saying: (The command of God has come) (al-Nahl: 1), and the intended meaning [?] comes in, and God expressed it in the past tense to confirm occurrence, and that this matter will inevitably be. And they understood the contradiction between His saying: (The parable of the Garden which the righteous are promised, underneath it rivers flow, its fruit is perpetual and its shade) (al-Ra'd: 35) and His saying: (Everything is perishable except His Face) (al-Qasas: 88). For the perpetuity of fruit is contradicted by the perishing of everything other than God's essence; accordingly, if the Garden were created, its fruit would necessarily perish, so it would not [?] perpetually. And also they argued with His saying: (Its breadth is as the heavens and the earth) (Aal Imran: 133), and that cannot be imagined except after the annihilation of the heavens and the earth due to the impossibility of interpenetration of bodies, unless the expression is a metaphor for vastness.
Source pages p. 116

Commentary
This passage presents Imam al-Juwayni's position that there is no impossibility (لا استحالة) in God creating Paradise and Hell before the Day of Recompense. He bases this on the Quranic verse 'prepared for the righteous' (أعدت للمتقين), which suggests an existing creation. The passage then presents the objections from other scholars: first, some interpret 'prepared' as a past tense used to indicate certainty of future occurrence, similar to 'the command of God has come' (أتى أمر الله). Second, they see a contradiction between the eternal food of Paradise ( الرعد 35) and the verse 'Everything perishes except His Face' (القصص 88). Third, the description 'its breadth is as the heavens and earth' (آل عمران 133) would require Paradise to exist before the heavens and earth are rolled up, which is impossible due to the impossibility of interpenetration of bodies. The passage ends by suggesting that this expression might be metaphorical for vastness.

Key Terms
الاستحالة
خلق الجنة والنار
أعدت
الهلاك
التقديم
الكناية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Many believe that Paradise and Hell will be created only after the Day of Judgment.
Correction: Juwayni argues that there is no rational impossibility in their pre-creation; the Quranic verse 'Prepared for the righteous' suggests they already exist.

Checkpoint
What verse did Juwayni primarily rely on to support the pre-creation of Paradise?
A) (كل شيء هالك إلا وجهه)
B) (أعدت للمتقين)
C) (اعرضها السموات والأرض)
D) (مثل الجنة التي وعد المتقون)

Key Takeaway
Imam al-Juwayni argues that there is no rational impossibility in creating Paradise and Hell before Judgment Day, using Quranic evidence and addressing objections regarding future tense, perishability, and spatial dimensions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read that Paradise was prepared for the righteous, have you truly considered that your place in it may already be created, waiting for your actions to determine your entry? What does that do to your sense of urgency in doing good deeds today?
?
The objection that Paradise cannot exist now because it would perish like everything else reveals a limited understanding of God's power. In what areas of your life do you place limitations on what God can do, and how can this lesson challenge those assumptions?
?
Juwayni argues that God can create and preserve Paradise now. How does this certainty about God's power affect your fear of His judgment and your hope in His mercy? Are you living as if these realities are already present, or as if they are distant?

Al-Juwayni's Affirmation of the Physical Bridge and Balance
Juwayni's affirmation of sensory Bridge and Balance, and the objection.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand al-Juwayni's reasoning for affirming a sensory Bridge and Balance based on the non-impossibility of God's power, and the objection that scriptural evidence is needed for creedal matters.

Core Idea
God's power can affirm physical realities, but texts are still required for doctrine.

Al-Juwayni takes a bold position on eschatological realities. He affirms that the Bridge (الصراط) is a physical bridge over Hell and the Balance (الميزان) is a physical scale, not because explicit Quranic verses say so, but because God's power makes them possible. This lesson examines his argument and the crucial objection: can rational possibility alone establish a matter of faith?

Guided Path
1
Read Juwayni's affirmation
Read the Arabic passage that states al-Juwayni's view: the Bridge is a bridge extended over the Fire, and he affirmed a sensory Balance. Notice that he says he did not establish them by a transmitted proof from the decisive Quran.
He relies on 'non-impossibility' (عدم الامتناع) of God's power.
2
Consider the objection
The text then raises an objection: does God's power over everything establish a matter not stated in the text? The author notes that doctrines are only established by the Quran, as is well-known. Reflect on why this objection is important for kalam methodology.
Al-Juwayni is not ignorant of this principle; the question is why he made an exception.
3
Analyze the reasoning
Ask yourself: can a rational argument (like non-impossibility) alone prove a creedal matter? How does al-Juwayni reconcile his affirmation with the need for texts?
The passage ends with the question: why did he interpret 'prepared' as past and the Bridge/Balance as sensory?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقال الجويني أن الصراط جسر ممدود على متن النار، وأثبت ميزانا حسيا، أي أنه يثبت جسرا حسيا، وميزانا حسيا، ولم يثبتهما بدليل سمعي من القرآن محكم، وإنما أثبتهما بعدم امتناع قدرة الله على ذلك. وهل قدرة الله على كل شيء تثبت أمرا لم ينص عليه؟ إن قدرة الله لا حدود لها. ولكن لا بد من نصوص. إن العقائد لا تثبت إلا بقرآن كما هو المشهور عند المتكلمين، والجوني يعلم ذلك... لأن مثله لا يجهله. فلماذا فسر (أعدت) بالماضي وفسر الصراط والميزان تفسيرا حسيا؟ والجواب على ذلك: سارى رأي بين علماء في زمن مضى خلاصته: أن المجاز ممتنع في القرآن، ولهذا المرأى فسر كثير من المفسرين على ظواهر الآيات دون نظر إلى المجاز - ولا بد من القول به - يقولون مثلا في قوله تعالى: (إنما يأكلون في بطونهم نارا وسيصلون سعيرا) (النساء: ١٠) أي يأكلون نارا حقيقية، أي جمرا... بينما يرى القائلون بالمجاز أنهم لا يأكلون نارا حقيقية، بدليل قوله بعد ذلك (وسيصلون سعيرا)، فإن قوله (وسيصلون سعيرا) نص في الآخرة، فتكون (في بطونهم نارا) نص في الدنيا، وهم لا يأكلون بالفعل في الدنيا نارا وإنما يأكلون أموال اليتامى المحرم أكلها، والأموال ليست نارا، ولا يأكلون المال وإنما المراد بالأموال الطعام والشراب الذي يشترونه بالأموال. وعلى هذا الرأي قال القائلون بالصراط الحسي والميزان الحسي نظرا لظواهر الآيات. وأنكرهما القائلون بالمجاز لأن الصراط المذكور في القرآن كناية عن الطريق المستقيم في الدنيا بدليل (اهدنا الصراط المستقيم، صراط الذين أنعمت عليهم، غير المغضوب عليهم، ولا الضالين) (الفاتحة: ٦-٧)، وبدليل: (وهدوا إلى الطيب من القول وهدوا إلى صراط الحميد) (الحج: ٢٤). والميزان المذكور في القرآن هو كناية عن الحق والعدل بدليل (وأقيموا الوزن بالقسط ولا تخسروا الميزان) (الرحمن: ٩). وأما قوله: (والوزن يومئذ الحق فمن ثقلت
And al-Juwayni said that the Bridge is a bridge extended over the surface of the Fire, and he affirmed a sensory Balance, meaning that he affirms a sensory bridge and a sensory balance, and he did not establish them by a transmitted proof from the decisive Quran, but rather established them by the non-impossibility of God's power over that. And does God's power over everything establish a matter that is not stated in the text? Indeed, God's power has no limits. But texts are necessary. Indeed, beliefs are only established by the Quran, as is well-known among the mutakallimun, and al-Juwayni knows that... for someone like him does not ignore it. So why did he interpret (prepared) as past and interpret the Bridge and the Balance as sensory? The answer to that: a view spread among scholars in a past time, its summary: that metaphor is impossible in the Quran, and therefore this person interpreted many of the commentators on the apparent meanings of the verses without regard to metaphor—and it is necessary to affirm it. They say, for example, in His saying: (They only eat fire in their bellies and they will soon enter a blazing fire) (al-Nisa: 10), meaning they eat real fire, i.e., embers... while the proponents of metaphor see that they do not eat real fire, because of His subsequent saying (and they will soon enter a blazing fire), for His saying (and they will soon enter a blazing fire) is a clear text about the Hereafter, so (in their bellies fire) is a clear text about this world, and they do not actually eat fire in this world, but rather they eat the wealth of orphans whose consumption is forbidden, and wealth is not fire, and they do not eat wealth, but rather what is meant by wealth is the food and drink that they buy with the wealth. And based on this view, the proponents of the sensory Bridge and sensory Balance spoke, looking at the apparent meanings of the verses. And the proponents of metaphor denied them, because the Bridge mentioned in the Quran is a metaphor for the straight path in this world, as evidenced by (Guide us to the straight path, the path of those upon whom You have bestowed favor, not of those who have earned Your anger, nor of those who have gone astray) (al-Fatiha: 6-7), and by evidence of: (And they were guided to the good of speech and guided to the path of the Praiseworthy) (al-Hajj: 24). And the Balance mentioned in the Quran is a metaphor for truth and justice, as evidenced by: (And establish weight with justice and do not diminish the balance) (al-Rahman: 9). And as for His saying: (And the weighing on that day is the truth, so those whose scales are heavy...
Source pages p. 117

Commentary
This passage discusses al-Juwayni's affirmation that the Bridge (الصراط) is a physical bridge stretched over Hell and the Balance (الميزان) is a physical scale. He asserts these not from clear Quranic texts (نصوص محكمة) but from the non-impossibility given God's absolute power. The author then raises the objection: can God's power alone prove a matter without scriptural evidence? He notes that doctrine (عقائد) is only established by the Quran. So why did Juwayni interpret 'prepared' as past and the Bridge/Balance as physical? The answer presents a historical view (سرى رأي) that some scholars denied metaphor (المجاز) in the Quran. For them, the apparent meaning is literal. Example: 'إنما يأكلون في بطونهم نارا' (النساء 10) is taken literally as eating fire (coals), while the metaphor school says it refers to consuming orphans' wealth. The literalists then apply this to the Bridge and Balance: because the apparent meaning suggests a physical bridge and scale, they affirm them as sensory. The metaphor school rejects this: the Bridge is a metaphor for the straight path (هدنا الصراط المستقيم, الفاتحة 5-6, and وهدوا إلى صراط الحميد, الحج 24), and the Balance is a metaphor for justice (وأقيموا الوزن بالقسط, الرحمن 9). The passage concludes with the verse 'والوزن يومئذ الحق' (the true weighing on that Day), but the sentence is incomplete on the page.

الميزان
الجسر الحسي
الميزان الحسي

Misconception: Some may think al-Juwayni disregarded the need for scriptural evidence entirely, relying only on rational speculation.
Correction: Al-Juwayni fully acknowledges that creeds require Quranic texts; his affirmation of sensory Bridge and Balance is not based on reason alone, but on a specific interpretive approach (literal reading of apparent meanings) that he adopted.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what did al-Juwayni rely on to affirm the sensory Bridge and Balance?
A) A clear Quranic text (نص محكم)
B) Consensus of the scholars
C) The non-impossibility of God's power (عدم الامتناع)
D) A hadith from the Prophet

Key Takeaway
Al-Juwayni's affirmation of physical Bridge and Balance is based on the non-impossibility of divine power, yet he acknowledges that creedal matters require scriptural support.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever believed something about the afterlife simply because it seemed reasonable, without verifying it from the Quran and Sunnah? What was it, and are you willing to seek the correct evidence today?
?
Al-Juwayni knew the principle that creed requires texts, yet he allowed rational possibility to shape his belief. Is there an area of your faith where you are letting your own reasoning override what Allah has explicitly revealed? Take a moment to identify it and resolve to correct it.
?
The passage ends with a question: why did al-Juwayni interpret these matters sensorily? Instead of judging him, ask yourself: Do I accept the unseen matters as they are described in the Quran, or do I reinterpret them to fit my intellect? Write down one such matter and commit to accepting it literally unless there is a clear textual reason otherwise.

Literal vs Metaphorical Interpretation of Bridge and Balance
Literalist vs metaphorist debate on interpreting Quranic eschatological terms.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the two main schools of thought regarding the interpretation of the Bridge and Balance in the Quran: the literalist school (denying metaphor) and the metaphorist school.

Core Idea
The Bridge and Balance: literal physical objects or metaphors for guidance and justice?

In response to the objection raised earlier, the author presents a historical debate among scholars. Some scholars denied the use of metaphor (المجاز) in the Quran, taking all verses at face value. This leads them to affirm a sensory Bridge and Balance. Others, however, interpret these terms as metaphors—the Bridge as the straight path, and the Balance as justice. This lesson explores their arguments and scriptural evidence.

Guided Path
1
Read the literalist position
Read the Arabic passage describing the view that denies metaphor in the Quran. Note how they interpret the verse about eating fire literally: 'they eat real fire' meaning embers. This is the basis for their affirmation of a physical Bridge and Balance.
They rely on the apparent meaning (الظواهر) of the verses.
2
Read the metaphorist position
Now read the metaphorists' counterargument. They argue that the verse about eating fire cannot be literal because it is followed by 'and they will soon enter a blazing fire,' which shows that the fire in the first part is metaphorical. They then apply this reasoning to the Bridge and Balance.
For them, the Bridge is a metaphor for the straight path (الصراط المستقيم), and the Balance is a metaphor for truth and justice.
3
Examine the evidence
The metaphorists cite Quranic verses to support their interpretation: for the Bridge, they quote al-Fatiha 6-7 and al-Hajj 24; for the Balance, they quote al-Rahman 9. Consider how these verses support a metaphorical understanding.
Passage ends mid-verse about the weighing on that Day.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقال الجويني أن الصراط جسر ممدود على متن النار، وأثبت ميزانا حسيا، أي أنه يثبت جسرا حسيا، وميزانا حسيا، ولم يثبتهما بدليل سمعي من القرآن محكم، وإنما أثبتهما بعدم امتناع قدرة الله على ذلك. وهل قدرة الله على كل شيء تثبت أمرا لم ينص عليه؟ إن قدرة الله لا حدود لها. ولكن لا بد من نصوص. إن العقائد لا تثبت إلا بقرآن كما هو المشهور عند المتكلمين، والجوني يعلم ذلك... لأن مثله لا يجهله. فلماذا فسر (أعدت) بالماضي وفسر الصراط والميزان تفسيرا حسيا؟ والجواب على ذلك: سارى رأي بين علماء في زمن مضى خلاصته: أن المجاز ممتنع في القرآن، ولهذا المرأى فسر كثير من المفسرين على ظواهر الآيات دون نظر إلى المجاز - ولا بد من القول به - يقولون مثلا في قوله تعالى: (إنما يأكلون في بطونهم نارا وسيصلون سعيرا) (النساء: ١٠) أي يأكلون نارا حقيقية، أي جمرا... بينما يرى القائلون بالمجاز أنهم لا يأكلون نارا حقيقية، بدليل قوله بعد ذلك (وسيصلون سعيرا)، فإن قوله (وسيصلون سعيرا) نص في الآخرة، فتكون (في بطونهم نارا) نص في الدنيا، وهم لا يأكلون بالفعل في الدنيا نارا وإنما يأكلون أموال اليتامى المحرم أكلها، والأموال ليست نارا، ولا يأكلون المال وإنما المراد بالأموال الطعام والشراب الذي يشترونه بالأموال. وعلى هذا الرأي قال القائلون بالصراط الحسي والميزان الحسي نظرا لظواهر الآيات. وأنكرهما القائلون بالمجاز لأن الصراط المذكور في القرآن كناية عن الطريق المستقيم في الدنيا بدليل (اهدنا الصراط المستقيم، صراط الذين أنعمت عليهم، غير المغضوب عليهم، ولا الضالين) (الفاتحة: ٦-٧)، وبدليل: (وهدوا إلى الطيب من القول وهدوا إلى صراط الحميد) (الحج: ٢٤). والميزان المذكور في القرآن هو كناية عن الحق والعدل بدليل (وأقيموا الوزن بالقسط ولا تخسروا الميزان) (الرحمن: ٩). وأما قوله: (والوزن يومئذ الحق فمن ثقلت

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read 'they eat fire in their bellies' (Q. 4:10), which interpretation feels more natural to you: literal embers or a metaphor for consuming forbidden wealth? Why does your instinct lean that way, and what does it reveal about your approach to understanding the Quran?
?
The metaphorist school uses clear reasoning from other verses to argue that the Bridge is not a physical object but a symbol of guidance. Does this challenge your own mental image of the Hereafter? Are you willing to adjust that image based on textual evidence, or do you cling to a literal picture out of comfort?
?
If you habitually interpret Quranic descriptions literally, consider a verse you find difficult (e.g., about God's hand or throne). Would you apply the same literalist principle to those, or do you make exceptions? What does that inconsistency say about your intellectual honesty?

Juwayni on the Sensory Bridge and Balance and the Literalist-Metaphorical Debate
Juwayni states his view on sensory bridge and balance, responds to objection about textual proof by explaining literalist school.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's affirmation of the sensory bridge and balance, the objection he faces, and his defense based on the literalist hermeneutical school.

Core Idea
Juwayni's sensory interpretation stems from a rejection of metaphor in the Qur’an, not from explicit texts.

This passage captures a key moment in kalam discussion of eschatological realities. Al-Juwayni affirms that the Bridge and Balance are literal, sensory objects, even though he cannot cite a decisive Qur’anic text. He then defends his position by invoking a hermeneutical school that insists on literal interpretation of all Qur’anic verses. The passage not only states a doctrinal view but also enters into a methodological debate about the permissibility of metaphorical interpretation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own argument come through before you interpret.
Notice that Juwayni begins with a direct affirmation, then shifts to an objection and answer.
2
Identify Juwayni's claim and the objection
Juwayni affirms a sensory bridge and balance. The objection is that creedal matters require textual evidence from the Qur'an, not just God's power.
The objector questions why Juwayni interpreted 'prepared' as past tense and the items as sensory.
3
Understand the literalist school
Juwayni's answer describes a school that rejects metaphor in the Qur'an. They interpret verses literally, e.g., 'eating fire' means actual fire.
This is the hermeneutical basis for his sensory interpretation.
4
See the application to Bridge and Balance
Literalists affirm sensory Bridge and Balance based on apparent meanings; metaphorists deny them, interpreting them metaphorically as path and justice.
Compare the two sets of evidence presented.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقال الجويني: إن الصراط جسر ممدود على متن النار، وأثبت ميزانا حسيا، أي أنه يثبت جسرا حسيا وميزانا حسيا، ولم يثبتهما بدليل سمعي من القرآن محكم، وإنما أثبتهما بعدم امتناع قدرة الله على ذلك. وهل قدرة الله على كل شيء تثبت أمرا لم ينص عليه؟ إن قدرة الله لا حدود لها. ولكن لا بد من نصوص. إن العقائد لا تثبت إلا بقرآن كما هو المشهور عند المتكلمين، والجويني يعلم ذلك.. لأن مثله لا يجهله. فلماذا فسر (أعدت) بالماضي وفسر الصراط والميزان تفسيرا حسيا؟ والجواب على ذلك: سرى رأي بين علماء في زمن مضى خلاصته: أن المجاز ممتنع في القرآن، ولهذا الرأي فسر كثير من المفسرين على ظواهر الآيات دون نظر إلى المجاز - ولا بد من القول به - يقولون مثلا في قوله تعالى: {إنما يأكلون في بطونهم نارا} (النساء:10) أي يأكلون نارا حقيقية، أي جمرا.. بينما يرى القائلون بالمجاز أنهم لا يأكلون نارا حقيقية، بدليل قوله بعد ذلك {وسيصلون سعيرا}، فإن قوله {وسيصلون سعيرا} نص في الآخرة، فتكون {في بطونهم نارا} نص في الدنيا، وهم لا يأكلون بالفعل في الدنيا نارا وإنما يأكلون أموال اليتامى المحرم أكلها والأموال ليست نارا، ولا يأكلون المال وإنما المراد بالأموال الطعام والشراب الذين يشترونه بالأموال. وعلى هذا الرأي قال القائلون بالصراط الحسي والميزان الحسي نظرا لظواهر الآيات. وأنكرهما القائلون بالمجاز لأن الصراط المذكور في القرآن كناية عن الطريق المستقيم في الدنيا بدليل {اهدنا الصراط المستقيم...} (الفاتحة:6-7) وبدليل: {وهدوا إلى الطيب من القول وهدوا إلى صراط الحميد} (الحج:24) والميزان المذكور في القرآن هو كناية عن الحق والعدل بدليل {وأقيموا الوزن بالقسط ولا تخسروا الميزان} (الرحمن:9) وأما قوله {والوزن يومئذ الحق فمن ثقلت موازينه فأولئك هم المفلحون، ومن خفت موازينه فأولئك الذين خسروا أنفسهم بما كانوا بآياتنا يظلمون} (الأعراف:8-9) فنص متشابه يحتمل ميزانا له لسان وكفتان ويحتمل القضاء السوي والحكم العادلة فلا تنهض به حجة قاطعة. وكذلك قوله تعالى {فاهدوهم إلى صراط الجحيم، وقفوهم إنهم مسئولون} (الصافات:22-23) والمعنى: عرفوهم طريق النار ليسلكوها. وهذا تهكم بهم وتوبيخ لهم بالعجز عن التناصر بعد ما كانوا على خلاف ذلك في الدنيا متعاضدين متناصرين.
Al-Juwayni said: Indeed, the Bridge is a bridge stretched over the surface of the Fire, and he affirmed a sensory Balance, i.e., he affirmed a sensory bridge and a sensory balance, and he did not affirm them by a textual evidence from the decisive Qur'an, but rather he affirmed them by the absence of impossibility of God's power over that. And does God's power over everything affirm a matter that has not been textually stated? Indeed, God's power has no limits. But texts are necessary. Indeed, creeds are not established except by the Qur'an, as is well-known among the theologians, and al-Juwayni knows that… for someone like him is not ignorant of it. So why did he interpret 'prepared' as past tense and interpret the Bridge and Balance as sensory? And the answer to that: A view spread among scholars in a past time, summarized as: metaphor is impermissible in the Qur'an, and because of this view, many exegetes interpreted according to the apparent meanings of the verses, without considering metaphor – and it is necessary to speak of it. They say, for example, in His statement, the Exalted: 'They only eat fire in their bellies' (al-Nisā’:10), i.e., they eat real fire, i.e., embers… while those who hold metaphor see that they do not eat real fire, evidenced by His subsequent statement 'and they will soon enter a blazing fire'. For His statement 'and they will soon enter a blazing fire' is a text about the Hereafter, so {in their bellies fire} is a text about this world, and they do not actually eat fire in this world, but rather they eat the wealth of orphans whose consumption is forbidden, and wealth is not fire, and they do not eat wealth; rather what is meant by wealth is the food and drink that they buy with wealth. And according to this view, those who affirm the sensory Bridge and sensory Balance spoke, looking at the apparent meanings of the verses. And those who affirm metaphor denied them, because the Bridge mentioned in the Qur'an is a metaphor for the straight path in this world, evidenced by {Guide us to the straight path, the path of those upon whom You have bestowed favor} (al-Fātiḥa:6-7) and by {And they were guided to good speech and guided to the path of the Praiseworthy} (al-Ḥajj:24). And the Balance mentioned in the Qur'an is a metaphor for truth and justice, evidenced by {And establish weight with justice and do not reduce the balance} (al-Raḥmān:9). As for His statement {And the weighing on that Day is truth; so those whose scales are heavy, they are the successful, and those whose scales are light, they are the ones who have lost their souls, because they used to wrong Our verses} (al-Aʿrāf:8-9), it is an ambiguous text that may mean a balance with a tongue and two pans, and may mean just judgment and fair ruling, so it does not serve as a decisive proof. And similarly His statement, the Exalted: {So guide them to the path of Hellfire, and stop them, indeed they are to be questioned} (al-Ṣāffāt:22-23). The meaning: make them know the path of the Fire so that they may follow it. And this is mockery of them and rebuke for their inability to support each other after they were in this world contrary to that, supporting and helping each other.
Source pages p. 117

Commentary
This passage is a combined statement and objection-response. Al-Juwayni first affirms that the Bridge is a physical bridge over Hellfire and that the Balance is a sensory balance. He admits he did not derive these from explicit Qur’anic texts, but from the principle that nothing is impossible for God’s power. This raises an objection: can God’s power alone establish a creedal matter without textual evidence? The objector notes that creeds are only established by the Qur’an. So why did al-Juwayni interpret 'prepared' (from the verse that the Fire has been prepared) as past tense and the Bridge and Balance as sensory? The answer begins by describing a historical view that metaphor is impermissible in the Qur’an. Adherents of this view interpret all verses literally. As an example, they take the verse 'They eat fire in their bellies' to mean literal fire. In contrast, metaphorical interpreters argue that it cannot be literal because the immediately following verse mentions Hellfire, indicating that the fire in the bellies is a metaphor for the unlawful wealth they consume. This same principle extends to the Bridge and Balance: literalists affirm them as sensory based on apparent verses; metaphorical interpreters deny that, considering the Bridge a metaphor for the straight path (as in al-Fātiḥa) and the Balance a metaphor for justice (as in al-Raḥmān). They argue that other verses like al-Aʿrāf:8-9 are ambiguous and cannot constitute a decisive proof. They interpret the verse 'Guide them to the path of Hellfire' as taunting and rebuke, not a literal path. So al-Juwayni’s answer is that he follows the literalist school, which affirms the sensory reality of these eschatological items based on apparent meanings, rejecting metaphorical interpretation in the Qur’an.

Key Terms
الصراط
الميزان
حسي
المجاز
الظاهر
قدرة الله

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Al-Juwayni arbitrarily affirmed the sensory bridge and balance without any basis from the Qur'an.
Correction: The passage shows he follows a hermeneutical principle that rejects metaphorical interpretation, which leads him to interpret the relevant verses literally.

Checkpoint
According to the literalist view mentioned in the passage, what does the verse 'They only eat fire in their bellies' mean?
A) They eat literal fire now
B) They eat unlawful wealth metaphorically
C) They will be punished with fire in the Hereafter
D) They eat hot coals

Key Takeaway
Juwayni's stance on the sensory bridge and balance is grounded in a literalist hermeneutic that rejects metaphorical interpretation in the Qur'an.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read verses about the Bridge (ṣirāṭ) and Balance (mīzān) on the Day of Judgment, does your mind instinctively try to 'explain them away' metaphorically because they seem too concrete for the unseen world? Or do you accept them as literal realities, trusting God's power even when your imagination struggles? Which inclination is stronger in you, and what does that reveal about your approach to revelation?
?
The literalists in this passage believed that metaphor has no place in the Qur'an—every word must be taken at face value. Are there verses about the afterlife that you privately find 'hard to believe' literally? What are they, and have you ever tried to reinterpret them to make yourself more comfortable? How does this lesson challenge you to submit to the text's apparent meaning even when it stretches your intellect?
?
Al-Juwayni affirmed the sensory bridge and balance based on God's power and the apparent meaning of the verses, despite the lack of a 'decisive text.' Is there an article of your faith that you hold without explicit scriptural proof, relying instead on a general principle (like God's omnipotence)? Are you at peace with that, or do you feel a nagging need for more evidence? What does your answer say about your trust in God's wisdom and the scholars who preceded you?

Juwayni’s Proofs for Intercession for Sinners
Juwayni affirms intercession with rational and hadith proofs, omitting Qur’an.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Juwayni used rational arguments and prophetic hadiths to affirm intercession for sinners, without relying on Qur’anic verses.

Core Idea
Reason and hadith, not just the Qur’an, can prove intercession.

Al-Juwayni approaches the doctrine of intercession with a methodology that combines rational inquiry and prophetic tradition. In this passage, he explicitly states his proof structure: rational arguments and hadiths. Notably absent are direct Qur’anic citations, which sets his approach apart from some other theologians. This lesson focuses on that method.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice that Juwayni affirms intercession for sinners with two types of evidence.
The passage is short but packed.
2
Identify the evidence types
Find the phrases 'rational proofs' and 'prophetic hadiths'. Consider why Juwayni might use these rather than Qur’anic verses.
3
Reflect on what is missing
Observe that Juwayni does not cite any Qur’anic verses here and does not refute the division of intercession into two types. Ask yourself why this might be significant.
This sets up the Mu’tazilite position in the next lesson.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأثبت الجويني الشفاعة للعصاة بأدلة عقلية، وبأحاديث نبوية، ولم يذكر نصوصا من القرآن.. ولم يفند رأى القائلين بتقسيمها إلى: إن شفاعة في زيادة الثواب، أو زيادة الدرجات - وإلى شفاعة عامة لكل المذنبين. قال المعتزلة - رحمهم الله تعالى - إن الشفاعة في زيادة الثواب أو زيادة الدرجات جائزة للنبي صلى الله عليه وسلم أو غير النبي بدليل وقالوا: {اتخذ الرحمن ولدا سبحانه بل عباد مكرمون لا يسبقونه بالقول وهم بأمره يعملون يعلم ما بين أيديهم وما خلفهم ولا يشفعون إلا لمن ارتضى وهم من خشيته مشفقون ومن يقل منهم إني إله من دونه فذلك نجزيه جهنم كذلك نجزى الظالمين} (الأنبياء:26-29) قال صاحب الكشاف: ومن تحفظهم أنهم لا يجبرون أن يشفعوا إلا لمن ارتضاه الله وأهله للشفاعة في ازدياد الثواب والتعظيم، وأما الشفاعة للعصاة الذين لم تؤهلهم أعمالهم لدخول الجنة فلا شفاعة لهم، ويدخلون...
And al-Juwayni affirmed intercession for sinners with rational proofs and prophetic hadiths, and he did not mention texts from the Qur’an… and he did not refute the view of those who divide it into: a shafāʿa that is an increase of reward or increase of ranks – and a general shafāʿa for all sinners. The Muʿtazila – may God have mercy on them – said: Indeed, intercession for increase of reward or increase of ranks is permissible for the Prophet, may God bless him and grant him peace, or other than the Prophet, as evidence, and they said: {They have taken the Most Merciful as a son. Exalted is He! Rather, they are honored servants. They do not precede Him in speech, and they act by His command. He knows what is before them and what is behind them, and they do not intercede except for whom He approves, and they are fearful of Him. And whoever among them says, 'I am a god besides Him,' that one We will recompense with Hell. Thus do We recompense the wrongdoers.} (al-Anbiyā’:26-29) The author of al-Kashshāf said: ‘And of their cautiousness is that they do not force themselves to intercede except for whom God has approved and made worthy of intercession for increase of reward and glorification. As for intercession for sinners whose deeds have not qualified them to enter Paradise, they have no intercession, and they enter…’
Source pages p. 118

Commentary
This passage presents al-Juwayni’s proof for intercession for sinners. He used rational arguments and prophetic hadiths, but notably did not cite Qur’anic verses. He also does not refute the division of intercession into two types: one that increases reward (for believers) and one that is general for all sinners. The Muʿtazilite position is then presented: they allow intercession only for increase of reward or ranks, and only for those whom God approves. They support this with the Qur’anic verse from al-Anbiyā’, which states that the angels only intercede for whom God is pleased with. Al-Zamakhshari (صاحب الكشاف) comments that intercession is conditional on God’s approval and is for increase of reward, not for forgiveness of sinners who are not qualified. The passage ends abruptly with 'ويدخلون' indicating more was to come.

Key Terms
الشفاعة
العصاة
أدلة عقلية
أحاديث نبوية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
What type of evidence did al-Juwayni use to affirm intercession for sinners, according to the passage?
A) Only Qur’anic verses
B) Rational proofs and prophetic hadiths
C) Only prophetic hadiths
D) Consensus of the Companions

Key Takeaway
Al-Juwayni affirms intercession for sinners using rational arguments and hadiths, not Qur’anic texts.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you rely more on rational arguments or scriptural proofs for your core beliefs? What would it take for you to accept a doctrine based on reason and hadith alone, as Juwayni did for intercession?
?
Juwayni did not refute a division of intercession that he may have disagreed with. Are there theological disagreements you are avoiding because addressing them would unsettle your current understanding?
?
If you were to defend intercession to a skeptic, would you be comfortable using only rational arguments and hadiths, or would you feel the need to find a Qur’anic verse? Examine your trust in reason as a source of certainty.

The Muʿtazilite Restriction of Intercession
Muʿtazila restrict intercession to increase of reward for the approved, citing Qur’an and tafsir.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Muʿtazilite position that intercession is only for increasing reward for those whom God approves, based on Qur’an 21:26–29 and the commentary of al-Zamakhshari.

Core Idea
Intercession is only for increased reward, not forgiveness of sinners.

After presenting al-Juwayni’s affirmation of intercession for all sinners, the passage now turns to the Muʿtazilite view. The Muʿtazila restrict intercession to the increase of reward or ranks for those already worthy, and they support this with a specific Qur’anic verse. The commentary of al-Zamakhshari reinforces the conditional nature of intercession. This lesson covers their argument and scriptural proof.

Guided Path
1
Read the Muʿtazilite claim
Read the first sentence of the Arabic passage. Note that they allow intercession only for increase of reward or ranks, and it is permissible for the Prophet and others.
2
Examine the Qur’anic proof
Read the verse from al-Anbiyā’. Notice that it says angels only intercede for those whom God approves. Reflect on how this supports the Mu’tazilite position.
The verse is cited as direct evidence.
3
Understand al-Zamakhshari’s commentary
Read the commentary from al-Kashshāf. It explains that the angels are cautious and only intercede for increase of reward for the approved. Note the exclusion of sinners who are not qualified.
The passage ends abruptly, but the point is clear.
