How a Miracle Confirms a Prophet's Truth
Juwayni explains miracle as divine confirmation analogous to verbal testimony.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how a miracle functions as divine confirmation equivalent to verbal testimony, and why it must be connected to a specific claim.

Core Idea
A miracle is God's 'Yes' to a prophet's claim.

Juwayni explains that a miracle is not just any supernatural event; it is a sign that takes the place of a direct statement from God. He uses a vivid parable of a king and a messenger to show that the miracle only indicates truth when it is directly linked to the claimant's challenge. This passage lays the foundation for understanding the evidential role of miracles in proving prophethood.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own argument come through before you interpret.
Notice Juwayni's use of a parable to make his point concrete.
2
Distinguish miracle indication from ordinary action indication
Juwayni begins by stating that a miracle does not indicate truth in the same way an action indicates its agent. Identify this distinction and understand why it matters.
3
Follow the parable of the king
Imagine the scenario: a king, a messenger, and a request for a custom-breaking action. See how the king's action, when explicitly connected to the claim, becomes equivalent to saying 'You are truthful.'
This is the heart of the argument.
4
Apply the logic to the prophet
Juwayni applies the same structure to the prophet: the miracle occurs at his request, verifying his prophethood. Understand why the connection (ittisal) is essential.
5
Reflect on the condition of connection
Juwayni emphasizes that a miracle not linked to a claim does not confirm truth. Ask yourself why this condition is logically necessary.
This guards against the misconception that any supernatural event automatically proves prophethood.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فصل
ذكر وجه دلالة المعجزة على صدق من ظهرت عليه

ليعلم الموفق لدرك هذه المعاني الشريفة: أن المعجزة لا تدل على الصدق حسب دلالة الفعل على الفاعل... فإن الفعل عينه يدل على فاعله، واختصاصه ببعض الوجوه الجائزة: يدل على إرادة التخصيص كما سبق التمهيد لهذه السبل في مفتتح العقيدة. فلا يتصور فعل غير دال على الفاعل، ولا يمتنع خارق للعادة يظهره الله تعالى بدل، من غير اتصال بدعوى مدع.
ثم لا يوصف بأنه يدل على تصديق. فوجه دلالة المعجزات على صدق مدعي النبوات: نزولها منزلة التصديق بالقول. وذلك يتضح بصورة تفرضها وتوضح الغرض منها. فنقول:
إذا جلس ملك للناس، وتصدى لدخولهم عليه وقد حز بهم أمر، وأطل عليهم مهم. فلما حضروه وأخذوا منازلهم ومراتبهم، قام قائم من خواص الملك وقال: معاشر الناس، قد علمتم ما ألم بكم وتبينتم أن الملك لم يجر اعتياده بمخاطبتكم كفاحا. وأنا رسوله إليكم في أمر يدرأ عنكم غائلة ما نزل بكم، وأنا في دعواي هذه بمرأى من الملك ومسمع.
أيها الملك، إن كنت رسولك الصادق في دعواه الرسالة فخالف عادتك وقم واقعد. فقام الملك وقعد على حسب دعوى الرسول. نزل ذلك منزلة قوله: صدقت، أنت رسولي. ولو لم يجر شيء من هذه المقدمات، فخالف الملك ما كان معتادا منه، وقام وقعد، لم يدل ذلك منه على تصديق، لأنه لم يقع موافقا للدعوى متصلا به. ومغزى هذا الفصل يرشد إلى وجه اشتراط تعلق المعجزة بالدعوى، ويبين أنها تدل من حيث تنزل منزلة التصديق بالقول.
فكذلك إذا قال النبي: معاشر الأشهاد، عرفتم بأن إحياء الموتى وقلب العصا وفلق البحر ليس مما ينال بحيلة أو يتوصل إليه بفطنة ووسيلة، وإنما هو من فعل الإله المستأثر بالقدرة الأزلية. يا رب إن كنت صادقا في دعوى النبوة فاقلب هذه العصا حية. فانقلبت كما أراد. كان ذلك قاطبة بمثابة قول الله تبارك وتعالى: صدقت أنت رسولي. وهذا يتصل مدركه بضرورات العقول.
A chapter explaining the aspect of the miracle's indication of the truthfulness of the one upon whom it appears. Let the one granted success in grasping these noble meanings know that the miracle does not indicate truthfulness in the same way that an action indicates its agent... For the very action indicates its agent, and its specification to some of the permissible aspects indicates the intention of specification, as we previously laid the groundwork for these ways at the beginning of the Creed. So no action is conceivable that does not indicate its agent, nor is it impossible for a supernatural event that Allah Most High displays instead, without connection to a claimant's claim. Then it is not described as indicating confirmation. So the aspect of the miracles' indication of the truthfulness of the claimant of prophethoods is their taking the place of verbal confirmation. And that becomes clear by a scenario we propose and clarify the purpose from. We say: If a king sat for the people and positioned himself for their entry upon him, and a matter had befallen them and an important affair had appeared to them. When they attended and took their positions and ranks, a person from the king's close associates stood up and said: 'O people, you know what has afflicted you and you have seen that the king is not accustomed to addressing you face-to-face. I am his messenger to you concerning a matter that will ward off the calamity that has befallen you, and I am in this claim of mine within the sight and hearing of the king.' 'O king, if you are his truthful messenger in his claim of messengerhood, then break your custom and stand and sit.' So the king stood and sat according to the messenger's claim. That took the place of his saying: 'You have spoken truthfully; you are my messenger.' And if none of these preliminaries had occurred, and the king broke what was customary from him and stood and sat, that would not indicate from him confirmation, because it did not occur in agreement with the claim and connected to it. And the purport of this chapter guides to the aspect of the condition of the miracle's connection to the claim, and it clarifies that it indicates by virtue of taking the place of verbal confirmation. Similarly, when the Prophet says: 'O assembled witnesses, you know that reviving the dead, turning the staff, and splitting the sea are not things attainable by a trick or reachable by cleverness and a means, but rather they are from the act of the God who exclusively possesses eternal power. O Lord, if You are truthful in the claim of prophethood, then turn this staff into a serpent.' So it turned as he intended. That altogether is equivalent to the saying of Allah Almighty: 'You have spoken truthfully; you are My messenger.' And this matter's discernment is connected to the necessities of intellects.
Source pages p. 67

Commentary
This passage is a detailed explanation of how a miracle (mu'jizah) serves as evidence for the truthfulness of a prophet. Juwayni begins by distinguishing the indication of a miracle from the ordinary indication of an action on its agent: an action naturally points to its agent, but a miracle's special role is to confirm a claim. He stresses that a supernatural event (khāriq li-l-ʿādah) performed by Allah without being linked to a claim does not automatically confirm truthfulness. The key is that the miracle takes the place (manzilat) of a direct verbal declaration of confirmation from God. To illustrate, he presents a parable of a king and a messenger. The messenger claims to be sent, and then the king breaks his custom by standing and sitting as requested. This act, when explicitly connected to the claim, is equivalent to the king saying 'You have spoken truthfully; you are my messenger.' If the same act occurred without the preliminaries and claim, it would not indicate confirmation. Similarly, for a prophet: he presents his claim to prophethood and then requests a specific miracle (e.g., turning a staff into a serpent). When it occurs exactly as requested, it is as if Allah said 'You have spoken truthfully; you are My messenger.' Juwayni concludes that this reasoning is grasped by the necessities of intellects (ḍarūrāt al-ʿuqūl), meaning it is a self-evident rational inference.

Key Terms
معجزة
دلالة
دعوى
تصديق
نزول منزلة القول
خارق للعادة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Any supernatural event automatically proves that the person performing it is a prophet.
Correction: Juwayni explains that a miracle only indicates truth if it is directly connected to a specific claim of prophethood. Without that connection, it is just a supernatural event, not a confirmation.

Checkpoint
Why is a miracle considered equivalent to divine verbal confirmation?
A) Because miracles are impossible for humans to perform by nature.
B) Because the miracle occurs precisely as requested in the prophet's claim, fulfilling the condition of connection.
C) Because miracles are always greater than what humans can do, so they must come from God.
D) Because all supernatural events are automatically confirmations of truth.

Key Takeaway
A miracle indicates a prophet's truth only when it is directly connected to his claim, serving as God's implicit confirmation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you consider the prophets, do you truly believe their miracles were a personal confirmation from God to you? How does that affect your acceptance of their message?
?
If a miracle occurred today in response to a claim, would you submit? Why does Juwayni's condition of connection matter for your own faith?
?
The king's act was a simple stand and sit. Are you looking for grand signs when God may confirm truth in subtle ways? Examine your heart: are you truly open to God's confirmations?

Miracles as Divine Attestation
Juwayni explains how miracles function as divine confirmation of prophethood.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why a miracle must be connected to a prophet's claim and how it serves as a divine verbal confirmation.

Core Idea
A miracle is God's way of saying 'You are truthful, My messenger.'

Juwayni addresses a crucial question: how does a miracle verify prophethood? He explains that a miracle does not stand alone; it must be explicitly tied to the prophetic claim. In this passage, he lays out the logic that a miracle functions exactly like a direct statement from God endorsing the prophet.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the key term 'منزلة التصديق' which means 'the position of confirmation'.
2
Identify the condition for a miracle
Juwayni states a miracle must occur 'موافقاً للدعوى متصلاً به' (in agreement with the claim and connected to it). Look for this condition and think about why it matters.
3
Understand the equivalence to speech
The passage says the miracle 'تنزل منزلة التصديق بالقول' (assumes the position of verbal confirmation). Reflect on how a physical event can be equivalent to a verbal statement from God.
Juwayni gives a concrete example of a prophet praying for a sign and it happening.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
على تصديق ، لأنه لم يقع موافقا للدعوى متصلا به . ومغزى هذا الفصل يرشد إلى وجه اشتراط تعلق المعجزة بالدعوى ، ويبين أنها تدل من حيث تنزل منزلة التصديق بالقول . فكذلك إذا قال النبي : معاشر الأشهاد . عرفتم بأن إحياء الموتى ، وقلب العصا ، وخلق البحر ، ليس مما ينال بحيلة ، أو يتوصل إليه بفطنة ووسيلة ، وإنما هو من فعل الإله المستأثر بالقدرة الأزلية . يا رب إن كنت صادقا في دعوى النبوة فاقلب هذه العصا حية . فانقلبت كما أراد . كان ذلك قطعا بمثابة قول الله تبارك وتعالى صدقت أنت رسولي . وهذا يتصل مدركة بضرورات العقول .
upon confirmation, because it [the miracle] did not occur in agreement with the claim connected to it. And the import of this chapter guides to the aspect of the condition of the miracle being attached to the claim, and it shows that it indicates insofar as it assumes the position of verbal confirmation. Thus likewise when the prophet says: 'O assembly of witnesses. You know that reviving the dead, turning the staff, and creating the sea are not attained by a trick, nor reached by intelligence and means, but they are from the act of the God who alone possesses eternal power. O Lord, if I am truthful in the claim of prophethood, then turn this staff into a snake.' And it turned as he desired. That is categorically equivalent to the saying of God, Blessed and Exalted, 'You are truthful, you are My messenger.' And this is grasped by necessities of intellects.
Source pages p. 68

Commentary
This passage is a continuation of a proof explaining how miracles serve as divine attestation. Juwayni begins by stating that a miracle must occur in agreement with the prophet's claim. The purpose of the chapter is to clarify the condition that the miracle be attached to the claim, and to show that the miracle functions like a verbal declaration of truth from God. He then gives a concrete example: a prophet declares publicly that acts like reviving the dead, turning a staff, or creating the sea are beyond any trick or human means, and then prays to God to turn a staff into a snake as a sign of his truthfulness. When the transformation occurs exactly as requested, this is categorically equivalent to God saying directly, 'You are truthful, you are my messenger.' The final sentence confirms that this understanding is grasped by necessary rational intuitions.

دعوى
معجزة
منزلة التصديق
ضرورات العقول

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Miracles prove prophethood by themselves, regardless of context.
Correction: Juwayni emphasizes that a miracle only confirms prophethood when it is explicitly connected to the prophet's claim and occurs in response to a challenge.

Checkpoint
What does Juwayni say a miracle is equivalent to?
A) A proof from reason
B) A verbal declaration from God affirming the prophet's truth
C) A sign of the prophet's own power
D) A natural phenomenon

Key Takeaway
A miracle confirms prophethood only when it is connected to the prophet's claim and functions as God's direct affirmation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you hear the Quran or see a sign of God, do you receive it as a personal affirmation that God is truth and you are on the truth? Or do you treat it as a mere intellectual proof? What would change if you heard God saying to you directly, 'You are truthful, you are My follower' through His signs?
?
The prophet publicly challenged his people to recognize the miracle. In your own life, are you willing to stand publicly for the truth of Islam, even without a visible miracle? What holds you back from such boldness?
?
If a miracle from God occurred right now to affirm your faith, what would you be willing to change about your life? Are you living as if you have already received that affirmation from the Quran and the prophethood of Muhammad (peace be upon him)?

Prophethood
intermediate
5 min
Karamat Do Not Vitiate Prophethood
Juwayni defines karamat as divine acts that do not vitiate prophethood.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why karamat (saintly marvels) are possible without challenging the unique evidence of prophethood.

Core Idea
Miracles are evidence only when attached to a prophetic claim; karamat are just God's acts.

Juwayni now tackles a critical concern: if extraordinary events can occur outside prophetic miracles, do they not undermine the proof of prophethood? He answers with a clear distinction. All breaches of custom are God's acts, but miracles have a specific evidential role tied to a claim. This lesson unpacks that distinction and shows why karamat are theologically sound.

Guided Path
1
Read Juwayni's core assertion
Begin by reading the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice how Juwayni first states that khawariq al-'adat are entirely from God, not from creatures.
This is a fundamental principle: no created being produces supernatural events.
2
Follow the argument from God's power
Juwayni argues by pointing to God's creative and eschatological acts: He who can do the greatest things can certainly do smaller extraordinary events. See how he lists cosmic transformations to emphasize divine omnipotence.
This is a qiyas al-awla (argument from the greater to the lesser).
3
Understand the conditional nature of miracles
Juwayni recalls that a miracle (mu'jiza) is not miraculous by itself; it indicates prophethood only because it occurs in accordance with a claim. This is the key premise.
Without a claim, the same event is just an act of God, not a proof.
4
Apply the distinction to karamat
Since no claim of prophethood is made by the saint, the extraordinary event (karama) does not conflict with the evidential role of miracles. Thus, karamat are permissible and affirmed by Qur'an and hadith.
Juwayni concludes that only a doubter denies them.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فأقول : خوارق العادات : ليست من فعل العباد ، وإنما هي من فعل الله تعالى وتقدس ، فمن فطر السموات والأرض ، وسيطوي السماء ، ويبدل الأرض غير الأرض ويسير الجبال ، ويفجر البحار ، وينشر الموتى قادر على أن يأتي ببديعة ، وليس في فرض الاتيان بها قدح في النبوات . فإنا ذكرنا أنها : أن المعجزة لا تثبت بعينها ، وإنما تدل من حيث تقع على وفق الدعوى في النبوة : فإذا لم تقع دعوى النبوة أوقع الله ما يشاء ، مما يعتاد ، ومما لا يعتاد ، فليس في تجويز الكرامات قدح في النبوات ، إذا وقعت الإحاطة بوجه دلالة المعجزة على ما سبق وما جاء في قدرة الله سبحانه . ولم ينخرم به الإعجاز . وقد نطق القرآن وتواترت به الآثار فلا يجحده إلا مرتاب .
So I say: Breaches of custom (khawariq al-'adat) are not from the acts of servants, but rather they are from the act of God, Exalted and Sanctified. Therefore, He who originated the heavens and earth, and will roll up the sky, and change the earth for other than earth, and set the mountains in motion, and cause the seas to burst forth, and resurrect the dead – is able to produce something unprecedented. And there is no imputation against prophethood in positing their occurrence. For we have mentioned that the miracle is not established by itself, but rather it indicates by virtue of occurring in accordance with the claim of prophethood. So when no claim of prophethood occurs, God brings about whatever He wills, both customary and non-customary. Thus there is no imputation against prophethood in allowing karamat, when one comprehends the aspect of the miracle's indication as previously mentioned, and what is [possibly] within the power of God. And the miraculousness is not impaired by it. The Qur'an has spoken of them, and traditions have been transmitted successively, so none denies them except a doubter.
Source pages p. 69

Commentary
This passage is a doctrinal definition and proof. Juwayni begins his substantive discussion by stating a fundamental principle: breaches of customary laws (خوارق العادات) – meaning supernatural events – are not caused by human beings but are exclusively acts of God. He then argues by way of God's attributes: He who created the heavens and earth and will transform the universe on the Day of Judgment is certainly capable of producing novel events. Such events do not undermine prophethood. Juwayni recalls the earlier explanation: miracles are not intrinsically miraculous; they are only evidence when they occur in conjunction with a claim of prophethood. Therefore, when there is no such claim, God may bring about extraordinary events without conflicting with the unique demonstrative role of miracles. He concludes that the Qur'an and numerous traditions affirm karamat, so only a skeptic would deny them.

Key Terms
خوارق العادات
معجزة
كرامة
إعجاز
قدرة الله

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Any supernatural event is a miracle that proves prophethood.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies that a miracle is only evidence when it accompanies a claim of prophethood. Extraordinary events without such a claim (karamat) are not miracles and do not challenge the prophetic proof.

Checkpoint
Why do karamat not disprove prophethood according to Juwayni?
A) Because karamat are less powerful than miracles
B) Because miracles are defined by being attached to a prophetic claim
C) Because karamat are from saints, not prophets
D) Because karamat are only inner states

Key Takeaway
Karamat are possible because miracles are only evidence when tied to a claim of prophethood; otherwise, they are just acts of God's power.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever felt that your spiritual experiences are insignificant because you have not witnessed a miracle? How does Juwayni's teaching that karamat are simply acts of God, not proof of prophethood, reshape your view of God's work in your life? Will you now look for God's extraordinary acts in everyday moments, knowing they are signs of His power, not claims of prophethood?
?
When you hear stories of saints performing wonders, do you compare yourself and feel spiritually inadequate? How does this lesson free you from that comparison by clarifying that karamat are not indicators of personal holiness but solely God's choice? Will you commit to thanking God for His acts without envy?
?
If you have doubted God's power because you have not seen a miracle today, how does Juwayni's argument that miracles are only tied to prophetic claims restore your confidence? Will you let this truth transform your dua, asking God to show you His power in any form, without requiring a miracle?

Mary's Signs as Evidence for Karamat and the Objection about Claims
Juwayni cites Mary's miracles and addresses karamat with claim.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni uses Mary's extraordinary signs as Quranic evidence for karamat (saintly miracles) and how he begins to address the objection that such signs might be claimed by anyone.

Core Idea
Miracles belong only to prophets, but karamat can occur before prophethood.

Juwayni now turns to Quranic evidence for karamat, pointing to the extraordinary signs granted to Mary, peace be upon her. He argues these cannot be miracles for Jesus because they occurred before his prophethood. He then raises a key objection: if karamat are possible, how do we distinguish them from miracles when someone claims them? The passage begins his answer, though it is cut short, leaving us to anticipate a fuller distinction.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then the translation. Let the author's flow come through.
Notice the concise style; the text cuts off mid-sentence at the end.
2
Identify the Quranic evidence
Juwayni cites the 'wondrous signs' (بدائع الآيات) shown to Mary. Identify this as evidence for karamat from the Qur'an.
Think about what these signs might be (e.g., provision of fruit from heaven).
3
Understand why they are not miracles for Jesus
Juwayni argues these signs cannot be miracles (معجزات) for Jesus because they occurred before his birth (قبل كونه) and before the establishment of his prophethood.
Miracles are tied to the proof of prophethood; they cannot precede it.
4
Follow the objection and partial response
The objection asks about karamat appearing with a claim (دعوى). Juwayni reports some who permit karamat claim they occur without intention (غير إيثار واختيار), but he dismisses this as ignorance.
The text cuts off; Juwayni likely continues with his own distinction.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فأما ما أتى به القرآن : فمنها ما دل على مريم عليها السلام من بدائع الآيات ويستحيل أن تقدر معجزة لعيسى عليه السلام ، فإنها جرت قبل كونه والمعجزات لا تتقدم على ثبوت النبوة . ولو ذهبت أنقل ما صح من الأخبار والآثار فيها ، لجاوزت موضوع المعتقد وحده . فان قيل : أيجوز ظهور الكرامات مع دعوى ممن تظهر عليه ؟ قلنا : ذهب بعض مجوزى الكرامات : أنها تظهر من غير إيثار واختيار وزعموا أنها بهذا الوجه تتميز عن المعجزات . وهذا قول من لم يحط بحقيقة
As for what the Qur'an has brought: among them are the wondrous signs shown to Mary, peace be upon her, and it is impossible that they be considered a miracle for Jesus, peace be upon him, because they occurred before his being, and miracles do not precede the establishment of prophethood. And if I were to attempt to narrate what is authentic of reports and traditions concerning them, I would exceed the subject of creed alone. So if it is said: Is it permissible for karamat to appear along with a claim from the one on whom they appear? We say: Some of those who permit karamat have held that they appear without intention and choice, and they claim that in this way they are distinguished from miracles. And this is the statement of one who has not encompassed the reality
Source pages p. 69

Commentary
This passage first provides Quranic evidence for karamat. Juwayni points to the extraordinary events associated with Mary (peace be upon her), such as the provision of fruit from heaven. He argues that these cannot be considered miracles for Jesus because they occurred before his birth and prophethood, and miracles only follow the establishment of prophethood. He then declines to exhaustively list all traditions to avoid exceeding the scope of a creedal work. Next, he raises an objection: is it permissible for karamat to appear together with a claim (دعوى) from the person on whom they appear? The answer begins by noting that some who permit karamat assert that they occur without intention or choice (إيثار واختيار), and they claim this distinguishes them from miracles. However, Juwayni dismisses this as the statement of someone who has not grasped the reality (لم يحط بحقيقة). The text cuts off here, likely continuing to give the correct distinction.

Correction: Juwayni clarifies that miracles are tied to the establishment of prophethood, so events before that are not miracles. He also dismisses the view that karamat are simply without intention, implying a more substantive distinction.

Proving Prophethood: The Two Methods and the Flawed Eloquence Argument
Juwayni introduces two methods for proving Muhammad's miracles and critiques the eloquence-based approach to Quranic inimitability.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's introduction to proving Muhammad's prophethood, his categorization of opponents, and his critique of the view that the Quran's inimitability lies in its unparalleled eloquence.

Core Idea
Juwayni argues that the Quran's inimitability cannot be proven by mere eloquence.

Juwayni begins the section on proving the prophethood of Muhammad (peace be upon him) by outlining a methodical approach depending on the opponent. He then introduces two methods for demonstrating miracles, focusing first on the Quran's inimitability (إعجاز). However, he quickly dismisses a common approach that attributes inimitability to the Quran's eloquence alone, labeling it as unverifiable and flawed. This lesson examines his critique and sets the stage for his promised correct method.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and identify opponent types
Juwayni mentions three types of opponents: a heretical skeptic, a Brahmin who denies prophethood, and a follower of another religion. Note how he responds to each.
This shows his methodical approach.
2
Note the two methods for proving miracles
Juwayni says there are two methods (مسلكان). The first is the Quran's inimitability. The second is not described yet.
The passage only discusses the first method.
3
Understand the common eloquence argument
Many scholars claim the Quran is inimitable due to its superior eloquence (بلاغة) and grandeur (جزالة). Juwayni summarizes this view.
This is the view he rejects.
4
Grasp Juwayni's critique
Juwayni argues that one cannot be certain that the Quran's eloquence exceeds all human capacity based on studying Arabic poetry and prose. This makes the proof unverifiable.
He calls this approach excessive and erroneous.
5
Recognize the promise of a correct method
Juwayni states he will present the true path (مسلك الحق) to prove inimitability, rebutting false accusations.
The passage ends before he delivers it.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فى اثبات نبوة نبينا محمد صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم تقول في افتتاح الكلام في ذلك : ان تعرض للطعن في نبوته ملحد معطل فالوجه : اثبات العلم بالصانع المدير عليه أولا . فان تعرض لرد نبوته برهمى أثبتنا عليه النبوات على الجملة - كما سبق - وإن كان المعترض مليا مما يحاول به مطلعنا منعكسا عليه فيمن اعترف بنبوته قاطعا . فان قيل : ما معجزة رسولكم ؟ قلنا : لنا في اثبات معجزاته مسلكان . أحدهما: التعلق بإعجاز القرآن . وقد أكثر الناس فى وجه اعجاز القرآن ، وتقطعوا فيه أيادى سبا .. وصار معظم الناس : الى أن القرآن تميز على صنوف الكلام بمزية البلاغة والجزالة خارج عن المعتاد في ذلك . ثم زعم زاعمون : أن اعجازه في شرف جزالته ، وذهب آخرون : الى أن اعجازه في الجزالة الفائقة وأسلوبه الخارج عن أساليب النظم والنثر والخطب والأراجيز .. وهذا موقف تاه فيه الأولون والآخرون، وطعن فيه الطاعنون . وأنا بعون الله تعالى وحسن توفيقه آتى فيه بمسلك الحق وأبين عن واضح الوجوه اندفاع تمويهات الرائعين واقتفاض مطاعن المبطلين . فليعلم المشتهى الى ذلك : من رام أن يثبت اعجاز القرآن بأنه في جزالته خارق للعادات مجاوز الفصاحة للبلغاء واللسن الفصحاء . فقد حاد عن مدرك الحق فان من تأمل كلام العرب في نظمها ونثرها لم يتحقق عنده انتهاء جزالة القرآن الى حد الخروج عن العادة فى الزيادة على كلام الفصحاء . ومن تكلف اثبات ذلك فقد تكلف شططا وظن غلطا وتهدف للكلام الطويل من غير تحصيل ومن أنصف وانتصف ولم يتعسف لم يلح له :
In proving the prophethood of our prophet Muhammad – may God bless him and his family and grant them peace – you say in opening the discourse on that: If a heretical skeptic (mulhid mu'attil) attacks his prophethood, the proper course is to first establish knowledge of the Creator, the Manager. If a Brahmin (barhami) attacks to reject his prophethood, we establish prophethood in general against him – as has preceded – and if the objector is one of those who has become familiar [?] with what he attempts, our initial argument is reversed against him concerning one who has cut off recognition of his prophethood. If it is said: What is the miracle of your Messenger? We say: We have two methods in proving his miracles. One of them: attaching to the inimitability (i'jaz) of the Quran. And people have discussed much concerning the aspect of the Quran's inimitability, and have cut off the hands of Seba [?] in it. And most people have concluded that the Quran is distinguished over the types of speech by a quality of eloquence (balaghah) and grandeur (jazalah) outside the ordinary in that. Then some claimants claimed that its inimitability is in the honor of its grandeur, and others went to that its inimitability is in the surpassing grandeur and its style outside the styles of poetry, prose, sermons, and rajaz verses. And this is a position in which the first and the last have gone astray, and the attackers have attacked. And I, with the help of God Exalted and His good success, will come with the path of truth in it and clarify from clear faces the repulsion of the fabrications of the falsifiers and the refutation of the attacks of the invalidators. So let the one who desires that know: Whoever aims to prove the inimitability of the Quran by its being in its grandeur extraordinary, exceeding the eloquence of the eloquent and the tongues of the fluent, has deviated from the perceiving of truth. For whoever contemplates the speech of the Arabs in its poetry and prose does not become certain that the grandeur of the Quran reaches the limit of being extraordinary in exceeding the speech of the eloquent. And whoever burdens himself to prove that has burdened himself with an extravagance and thought an error, and aims [?] at lengthy speech without achievement. And whoever acts fairly and justly and does not deviate, it does not become apparent to him: [...]
Source pages p. 71

Commentary
This passage is the opening of a new section on proving the prophethood of Muhammad (peace be upon him). Juwayni starts by outlining the approach: if the opponent is a skeptic who denies the Creator, we first establish the existence of God; if the opponent is a Brahmin (برهمى) who denies prophethood altogether, we first establish general prophethood; if the opponent is from a revealed religion, the argument is to show that Muhammad's prophethood is confirmed by miracles. Then Juwayni introduces two methods (مسلكان) for proving Muhammad's miracles: one is the inimitability (إعجاز) of the Quran. He notes that people have disputed the nature of the Quran's inimitability: some say it lies in its surpassing eloquence (بلاغة) and grandeur (جزالة), others in its unique style. But Juwayni criticizes these views, stating that those who claim the Quran's inimitability is merely its unparalleled eloquence are mistaken, because if one studies Arabic poetry and prose, one cannot be certain that the Quran's grandeur exceeds all human capacity in a way that is extraordinary. He considers this approach flawed and promises to present the correct method (مسلك الحق) to rebut the fabrications of the falsifiers and attackers. The passage ends mid-sentence, indicating that the argument will continue in subsequent pages.

Key Terms
إعجاز القرآن
الجزالة
البلاغة
المسلكان
النظم
النثر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Quran's inimitability is proven by its eloquence surpassing all human speech.
Correction: Juwayni argues that this cannot be empirically verified; one cannot be certain that the Quran's grandeur exceeds the ordinary through studying Arabic poetry and prose.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the problem with proving the Quran's inimitability through its surpassing eloquence?
A) It is a valid method but difficult
B) It cannot be established because one cannot be certain the Quran's grandeur exceeds all human eloquence
C) It is against the consensus of scholars
D) It only applies to poetry, not prose

Key Takeaway
Juwayni cautions against relying on eloquence alone to prove the Quran's inimitability and promises a sounder method.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever relied on an argument for Islam that you secretly feared might be weak? What would it cost you to abandon it and seek a sounder proof, even if it means temporary uncertainty?
?
Juwayni says that many scholars have gone astray in trying to prove the Quran's inimitability through eloquence. Are there areas of your faith where you have followed popular claims without testing them against the sources? Will you examine them today?
?
The passage shows that even the most eloquent human speech cannot be definitively compared to the Quran. When you read the Quran, do you feel your heart respond to its truth, or are you more impressed by its beauty? Which reaction is more reliable, and what does that reveal about your relationship with the divine?

Method of Proving Prophethood Against Atheists and Brahmins
Juwayni outlines the approach to proving prophethood when facing an atheist or a Brahmin.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Imām al-Juwaynī's methodological approach to proving the prophethood of Muhammad (ṣ) when faced with an atheist or a Brahmin opponent, and why the proof differs according to the adversary's prior beliefs.

Core Idea
Proving prophethood requires tailoring the argument to the opponent's worldview.

Imām al-Juwaynī begins the section on establishing Muhammad's prophethood by distinguishing between two types of opponents: the atheist (mulḥid) who denies God, and the Brahmin (barhamī) who may accept a Creator but rejects prophethood. This short passage provides a strategic framework: first lay the foundation of God's existence for the atheist, then prove prophethood in general for the Brahmin. It sets the stage for the detailed proof by miracles that follows.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage aloud
Read the Arabic text carefully, noting the structure: a chapter heading, then two conditional scenarios. Pay attention to the terms mulḥid and barhamī.
The OCR had some uncertainty; note that barhamī likely refers to a Brahmin (Hindu).
2
Identify the two opponents
Juwayni mentions two opponents: an atheist (mulḥid) and a Brahmin (barhamī). Understand that each requires a different starting point in the argument.
3
Follow the prescribed arguments
For the atheist, first prove the existence of a wise Creator (ṣāniʿ mudabbir). For the Brahmin, first establish prophethood in general (al-nubūwāt ʿalā al-jumla). Notice the phrase 'kamā sabaqa' (as previously) referring to an earlier proof.
4
Reflect on the reversed objection
Juwayni notes that if the objector is capable, his objection can be reversed against him. This is an advanced rhetorical point: the proof can be turned back on the one who acknowledges prophethood decisively.
5
Connect to the overall lesson
Understand that this passage is a methodological introduction. The actual proof of Muhammad's prophethood (likely by miracles) will follow. This framework ensures the argument is appropriately grounded.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فصل في إثبات نبوة نبينا محمد صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم تقول في افتتاح الكلام في ذلك : أن تعرض للطعن في نبوته ملحد معطل فالوجه : إثبات العلم بالصانع المدير عليه أولا . فإن تعرض لرد نبوته برهمي أثبتنا عليه النبوات على الجملة - كما سبق - وإن كان المعترض مليا مما يحاول به مطلعنا منعكسا عليه فيمن اعترف بنبوته قاطعا .
A chapter on establishing the prophethood of our Prophet Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace. You say in the opening of the discourse on that: If an atheist (mulḥid) who denies God's attributes objects to his prophethood, the correct approach is to first establish knowledge of the wise Creator and Director. If a Brahmin (barhamī) objects to it, we establish prophethood in general upon him – as previously – even if the objector is able, with what he attempts to invalidate our proof, it is reversed against him regarding one who acknowledged his prophethood decisively.
Source pages p. 71

Commentary
This passage is a methodological instruction on how to argue for the prophethood of Muhammad against different types of opponents. The author, Imam al-Juwayni, states that when facing an atheist (mulḥid muʿaṭṭil) who rejects prophethood, the correct first step is to prove the existence of God as a wise director of the universe. This is because an atheist does not accept any divine authority, so one must first establish the foundation. For a Brahmin (barhamī), who may accept some form of deity but rejects prophethood, the argument proceeds by establishing prophethood in general terms, as previously discussed, using proofs that are universally valid. The author notes that even if the objector is capable and tries to refute, his objection turns against him in the context of those who decisively acknowledge prophethood (i.e., Muslims). The passage sets the stage for the specific proof of Muhammad's prophethood by miracles.

Key Terms
نبوة (prophethood)
ملحد (atheist)
برهمى (Brahmin)
صانع (Creator)
مدير (Director)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The proof of prophethood is independent of the proof of God.
Correction: Juwayni shows that when arguing with an atheist, one must first prove the existence of a wise Creator before moving to prophethood.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the first step when someone who is an 'mulḥid muʿaṭṭil' objects to prophethood?
A) Prove prophethood directly from miracles
B) Prove the existence of a wise Creator first
C) Use the argument of the Quran's inimitability
D) Challenge him to produce a miracle

Key Takeaway
When proving prophethood, the argument must be adapted to the opponent's prior beliefs: for atheists, start with God; for Brahmins, start with prophethood in general.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Think of a specific atheist you know or have encountered. If you were to discuss prophethood with them, would you have followed Juwayni's method of first establishing the Creator? What holds you back from doing so?
?
You may know someone who believes in God but rejects the need for prophets. How does Juwayni's instruction to 'establish prophethood in general' challenge your current approach to talking with them? Are you willing to change your approach today?
?
Juwayni warns that objections can be reversed against the critic who acknowledges prophethood. Have you ever felt your arguments for Islam were turned against you? How can this lesson give you confidence in the face of criticism?

Prophethood

The Prolonged Challenge and Proof of Divine Prevention
Juwayni presents historical evidence of the ongoing challenge and concludes that the inability proves divine prevention.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's historical argument for the doctrine of ṣarf (divine prevention) and why he considers it a stronger miracle than extraordinary physical acts.

Core Idea
The prolonged inability to imitate the Qur'an, despite ability and desire, proves divine prevention.

Imam Juwayni presents a compelling historical argument for the miraculous nature of the Qur'an. He points to the Prophet's public challenge that lasted over twenty years, the Arabs' own admission that the Qur'an was within their linguistic capability, and the centuries of failed attempts that followed. This lesson unpacks that argument and its conclusion: that the inability itself is a divine act of prevention, not a failure of human capacity.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Begin by reading Juwayni's Arabic text slowly, then read the literal translation. Let the rhythm of the argument sink in before analysing.
Notice how Juwayni builds his case with specific time durations and strong motivation.
2
Identify the key components
Find these elements in the passage: the challenge (taḥaddī), the duration (over 20 years), the claim that the Qur'an is within their power, the failure, the prolonged time (over 460 years), and the conclusion that creation is prevented (mamnū‘ūn).
3
Understand the doctrine of ṣarf
Juwayni says the miracle is not the Qur'an's eloquence alone, but that God prevented people from imitating it despite their ability. This is ṣarf (divine prevention). Reflect on why he calls this a stronger proof than breaking physical laws.
The common misunderstanding is that the Qur'an was beyond human ability; Juwayni insists it was within their power but they were prevented.
4
Check your understanding with the quiz
Answer the checkpoint question to test your grasp of the key numbers and conclusion.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وتمادي على تحديه نيفا وعشرين سنة. والقرآن بلغتهم وليس بعيدا عن مبلغ اقتدارهم فى جزالته وأسلوبه، فلم يقدروا على الإتيان بمثله، ثم استأثر الله تبارك وتعالى لرسوله صلى الله عليه وسلم، وكرت الدهور، ومرت العصور، وأقطار الأرض تطفح بجميع الكفار، ذوى الفطن النافذة، وشوقهم أن يتمكنوا من مطعن في الإسلام. وفى كل قطر منهم طائفة مشتغلون بالنظم والنشر على لغة العرب، فقصرت قدرة الخلق عن المعارضة في أربعمائة وستين سنة ونيف، فتبين قطعا أن الخلق ممنوعون عن مثل ما هو من مقدورهم وذلك أبلغ عندنا من خرق العوائد بالأفعال البديعة في أنفسها.
And he persisted in his challenge for over twenty years. The Qur'an is in their language and not far from the extent of their ability in its eloquence and style, yet they could not produce its like. Then God, Blessed and Most High, preserved it for His Messenger (may Allah bless him and grant him peace), and ages passed, centuries went by, and the lands overflow with all the disbelievers, possessors of penetrating intellects, and their desire to find a flaw in Islam. In every region there is a group occupied with poetry and prose in the Arabic language, yet the capacity of creation fell short of producing a counterexample in four hundred and sixty years and more. So it became conclusively evident that creation is prevented from something that is within their power, and that is more powerful in our view than breaking customs through extraordinary acts in themselves.
Source pages p. 72

Commentary
This passage provides the evidence for the doctrine of sarfah. Juwayni recounts that the Prophet continued his challenge for over twenty years. The Qur'an was in the Arabs' own language and was not beyond their ability in eloquence and style; yet they could not produce its like. Furthermore, after the Prophet's time, centuries passed with disbelievers eager to discredit Islam, and in every region there were those skilled in Arabic poetry and prose, yet they failed to produce a comparable text for more than 460 years. This prolonged inability despite strong motivation proves conclusively that creation is prevented (mamnū‘ūn) from something within their power. Juwayni declares this proof more powerful than miracles involving extraordinary physical acts because it involves a universal and sustained failure in a domain where success would be expected.

Key Terms
taḥaddī
mu‘āraḍah
ṣarf
‘ājiz
dawā‘ī

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the Arabs' inability was due to the Qur'an's eloquence being beyond human reach.
Correction: Juwayni emphasizes it was within their power and the miracle is the prevention, not the inimitability of the text itself.

Checkpoint
How long does Juwayni say the challenge continued and how many years of failed attempts?
A) 20 years and 400 years
B) Over 20 years and over 460 years
C) 23 years and 500 years
D) 10 years and 300 years

Key Takeaway
The prolonged inability of the Arabs and later generations to produce a text like the Qur'an, despite their capability and motivation, conclusively proves that they were divinely prevented—this is the miracle of ṣarf.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If the Qur'an were truly from you, would you have the courage to face its challenge and examine your own resistance? What keeps you from doing so today?
?
You have read that the Arabs, with all their eloquence and motivation, could not produce a single chapter like it. Does this evidence stir in you a sense of accountability to submit to the Qur'an's message? If not, what is holding you back?
?
The prolonged failure of the disbelievers over centuries is presented as conclusive proof. Have you personally acted upon this proof, or have you treated it as mere academic knowledge? Write one specific change you will make today to align your life with the truth of the Qur'an.

The Superiority of the Sarf Argument Over Physical Miracles
Juwayni refutes objections and asserts the sarf argument is superior to physical miracles.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Juwayni argues that the divine prevention (sarf) explanation for the Quran's inimitability is more profound than physical miracles like turning a staff into a snake.

Core Idea
The sustained inability to imitate the Quran is a stronger miracle than any physical wonder.

Juwayni concludes his creedal work with a powerful argument: the miracle of the Quran lies not merely in its eloquence, but in the fact that God has prevented all creation from producing its like, despite strong motives. He shows how this explanation turns objections into support and surpasses physical miracles.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then read the English translation. Let Juwayni's own flow come through before interpreting.
Notice the confident tone: Juwayni declares that objections actually reinforce the argument.
2
Identify how objections become support
Juwayni mentions that critics either deny the Quran's eloquence or admit it but deny its miraculous nature. Find how he uses both positions to strengthen the sarf argument.
3
Understand the logical proof for sarf
Juwayni argues: the closer speech is to human capability, the more likely it is to be challenged. Since the challenge failed despite strong motives, the only explanation is divine prevention. Trace this logic in the passage.
Compare to the prophet saying people cannot stand up—this highlights that the prevention is the miracle.
4
Appreciate the superiority over physical miracles
Juwayni concludes that the sarf argument is more profound than turning a staff into a snake. Why? Because a physical miracle might be attributed to special knowledge, but sustained inability to imitate the Qur'an over centuries cannot be explained away.
This is the key takeaway: the prevention is clearer and more definitive.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومن هدى لهذا المسلك: فقد رشد الى الحق المنير، وانعكس كل مطعن ذكره الطاعنون عضدا وتأييدا. فانهم تارة يدعون سقوط القرآن عن رتبة الجزالة وولوجه فى الركيك، وتارة يسلمون شرف الجزالة. ويدعون أنه غير خارق للمادة. وكيف تصرفت أسئلتهم فصرف الله الخلق عن الاتيان بمثله أوقع وأيجع. اذ الكلام كل ما كان أقرب مأخذا، وأبعد عن الغاية القصوى، كان أحرى أن يبتدر الى معارضته، فاذا لم تجر المعارضة، لم يبق لامتناعها، مع توفر الدواعى عليها، محمل الا صرف الله الخلق. وهذا يشابه ما لو قام النبي وقال: آيتي أنه يمتنع القيام الآن على الخلق مع اقتدارهم عليه من غير زمانه وعجز. فكيف يهتدى -حرس الله مولانا- إلى اعجاز القرآن، من يحاول أن يثبت خروجه عن العادة في الجزالة وشفاء الصدور فى الحكم فان مثله من مقدورات الخلق: ولكنهم مصدودون مصنوعون بصرف الله اياهم. وهذا الفصل من أنفس ما يجرى به خاطر. وهو خاتمة العقيدة بجري فى المآخذ العقلية. فهذا بالغ جدا، وهو عندى أبلغ من قلب العصاحية ونحوه. فانه قد يسبق مبادر الى أنه من اختصاص صاحبه، بمزايا فى العلوم الى أن يرده.
And whoever is guided to this path: he has been rightly guided to the clear truth, and every objection raised by the objectors is turned into support and confirmation. For they sometimes claim that the Qur'an falls short of the rank of eloquence and enters into weakness, and sometimes they concede the nobility of eloquence and claim that it is not extraordinary in its material. However, however their questions are turned, God's prevention of creation from producing its like is more effective and more painful. For speech, the closer it is in origin and the farther from the ultimate goal, the more likely it is to be challenged. So if the challenge does not occur, there remains no alternative for its impossibility, despite the presence of motives, except God's prevention of creation. And this resembles a prophet who said: My sign is that people are now prevented from standing up even though they are capable, without any time or weakness. So how can one be guided – may Allah preserve our master – to the miracle of the Qur'an, who attempts to prove that it breaks custom in eloquence and healing of hearts in wisdom, for that is within the capabilities of creation? Rather, they are blocked and prevented by God's diversion of them. This chapter is among the finest that one can write. It is the conclusion of the creed which runs in rational inquiries. This is very profound, and in my view it is more profound than turning a staff into a snake and the like. For it may occur to a beginner that that is a special prerogative of its possessor, due to advantages in knowledge, until he is guided.
Source pages p. 73

Commentary
Juwayni demonstrates why the sarf argument is superior. He first states that anyone who follows this path is guided to the truth, and objections actually reinforce it. He says that critics alternately claim the Qur'an is weak in eloquence or admit its eloquence but deny it breaks custom. Whichever way, the fact of God's prevention remains effective. The logic: the more a text is within human reach, the more likely it is to be challenged. Since the challenge failed despite strong motives, the only explanation is God's prevention. He compares this to a prophet who claims that people cannot stand up even though they are capable – that would be a clear miracle. Similarly, the Qur'an's eloquence is within human power, yet no one has matched it. Thus, the miracle is in the prevention, not in the text's inherent quality. Juwayni concludes that this argument is even more powerful than turning a staff into a snake, because a physical miracle might be attributed to special knowledge, but the sustained inability to imitate speech across centuries cannot be explained away.

Key Terms
ṣarf
mu‘āraḍah
‘ajz
tajallī
khāriq al-‘ādah

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the Quran's inimitability is only about its eloquence, and that the miracle is solely in the text.
Correction: Juwayni shows that the prevention argument is stronger and more definitive: the miracle is that God prevents all creation from imitating it, which is a continuous miracle that cannot be explained away by human skill.

Checkpoint
Why does Juwayni consider the sarf argument more profound than physical miracles like turning a staff into a snake?
A) Because it involves more people
B) Because physical miracles are easily attributed to special knowledge
C) Because the prevention of imitation over centuries cannot be explained away by human skill
D) Because the Quran is more eloquent than a snake

Key Takeaway
Juwayni concludes that the inimitability of the Qur'an is best explained by divine prevention, which is a stronger miracle than any physical transformation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If the Quran's greatest miracle is God's continuous prevention of imitation, have you ever truly felt awe at this ongoing divine intervention? What would it mean if this argument leaves your heart cold?
?
Juwayni says critics' objections are turned into support. When you hear modern doubts about the Quran's miracle, do you let them unsettle your faith, or do you see them as opportunities to deepen your conviction? Can you recall a specific doubt and how you responded?
?
The proof rests on the failure of imitation despite strong motives. If you were to truly internalize that no human power has ever matched the Quran, how would that change the way you approach its recitation and instruction in your daily life? What concrete habit would you change starting today?

The Proof of Sarf – Divine Prevention as the Quran's Miracle
Juwayni states the proof of sarf and addresses objections.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's argument that the Quran's inimitability is not merely a matter of rhetorical excellence but is fundamentally caused by God's active prevention (sarf) of imitation.

Core Idea
The Quran's inimitability is due to divine prevention, not just human inability.

Juwayni opens by stating that people are prevented from producing anything like the Quran, and that this divine prevention is a more profound miracle than the breaking of natural norms by extraordinary acts. He then addresses the objections of critics who either deny the Quran's eloquence or claim it is not extraordinary, turning their arguments into support for sarf.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis. Then read the literal translation to grasp Juwayni's main point: that divine prevention is the key to inimitability.
Note the phrase 'خرق العوائد' (breaking of norms) which Juwayni compares to sarf.
2
Identify the core claim
Juwayni claims that sarf is 'more profound' (أبلغ) than mere extraordinary acts. Why? Because it directly prevents any attempt at imitation, regardless of human capability.
3
Examine the handling of objections
Juwayni lists two objections: that the Quran lacks eloquence, or that it is eloquent but not extraordinary. He then shows that in both cases, the failure to produce a counterexample is best explained by divine prevention, turning objections into evidence.
Notice how Juwayni uses the opponents' own premises to support his argument.
4
Reflect on the analogy
Juwayni gives an analogy: a prophet claiming his sign is that people cannot stand up at a given moment, despite their ability. This illustrates how sarf makes the impossibility a direct divine act.
The analogy reinforces that sarf is not about inherent quality but about active divine intervention.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
أن الخلق ( ممنوعون عن مثل ما هو من مقدورهم وذلك أبلغ عندنا من خرق العوائد بالأفعال البديعة في أنفسها ، ومن هدى لهذا المسلك : فقد رشد الى الحق المنير ، وانعكس كل مطعن ذكره الطاعنون عضدا وتأييدا فانهم تارة يدعون سقوط القرآن عن رتبة الجزالة وولوجه فى الركيك ، وتارة يسلمون شرف الجزالة . ويدعون أنه غير خارق للمادة .. وكيف تصرفت أسئلتهم فصرف الله الخلق عن الاتيان بمثله أوقع وأيجع اذ الكلام كل ما كان أقرب مأخذا ، وأبعد عن الغاية القصوى ، كان أحرى أن يبتدر الى معارضته ، فاذا لم تجر المعارضة ، لم يبق لامتناعها ، مع توفر الدواعى عليها ، محمل الا صرف الله الخلق .. وهذا يشابه ما لو قام النبي وقال : آيتي أنه يمتنع القيام الآن على الخلق مع اقتدارهم عليه من غير زمانه وعجز .. فكيف يهتدى - حرس الله مولانا إلى اعجاز القرآن ، من يحاول أن يثبت خروجه عن العادة في الجزالة وشفاء الصدور فى الحكم فان مثله من مقدورات الخلق : ولكنهم مصدودون مصنوعون بصرف الله اياهم وهذا الفصل من أنفس ما يجرى به خاطر . وهو خاتمة العقيدة بجری فى المآخذ العقلية . فهذا بالغ جدا ، وهو عندى أبلغ من قلب العصاحية ونحوه . فانه قد يسبق مبادر الى أنه من اختصاص صاحبه، بمزايا فى العلوم الى أن يرده شداد الفكر ، وانما يجرى لخلائق سنة ، كلام، مماثل لكلامهم ، قد بلغة رجل أمى لم يعان العلو یدارس أهلها . ولا محمل له الا صرف الله تبارك وتعالى ومنعه الخلق فهذا وجه من ذكر معجزة رسول الله صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم
That the people (are prevented from something like what is within their capability, and that is more profound in our view than the breaking of norms by extraordinary acts in themselves. And whoever is guided to this path has been rightly guided to the clear truth, and every objection raised by the objectors is turned into support and reinforcement. For sometimes they claim that the Qur’an falls short of the rank of eloquence and enters into feebleness, and sometimes they concede the honor of eloquence and claim that it is not extraordinary in composition... However their questions are managed, God’s turning away (sarf) of people from producing its like is more powerful and effective. Because speech, the closer its approach and the farther from the ultimate end, is more likely to be countered. So when no countering occurs, with motives present, there is no explanation for its impossibility except God’s turning away of people. This resembles if a prophet said: My sign is that people are now prevented from standing, despite their ability to do so without time or incapacity... How then is he guided—may God protect our Master—to the inimitability of the Qur’an who tries to prove that it is extraordinary in eloquence and healing of breasts in judgment? For its like is within the capabilities of people, but they are repulsed and prevented by God’s turning them away. This chapter is one of the most valuable that occurs to the mind, and it is the conclusion of the creed. It proceeds in rational approaches. This is very profound, and in my view it is more profound than the transformation of a staff and the like. For a person might quickly think that it belongs to his specialty, by virtues in knowledge, until intense thought prevents him [from producing similar], and that it only occurs to creation over a year, speech similar to their speech, that an illiterate man who did not study knowledge uttered it. There is no explanation except God’s turning away (sarf) and His prevention of the people. So this is one aspect of mentioning the miracle of the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace.
Source pages p. 73

Commentary
This passage presents the proof of sarf (divine prevention) as the basis for the Quran's inimitability. Juwayni begins by stating that people are prevented from producing anything similar to the Quran, and that this is a more profound miracle than the mere breaking of natural norms by extraordinary acts. He then notes that opponents of the Quran's inimitability either claim it lacks eloquence or, if they admit its eloquence, argue it is not extraordinary in composition. Juwayni counters that regardless of their objections, the fact that God has turned people away from producing its like is more powerful. He explains that the closer speech is to being easily imitated, the more likely it would be challenged; since no challenge occurred despite strong motives, the only explanation is divine prevention. He likens this to a prophet claiming his miracle is the inability of people to stand up at a given time, despite their ability. Juwayni then argues that anyone seeking to prove the Quran's inimitability through its rhetorical qualities alone is misguided, because such qualities are within human capability; the true miracle is that people are prevented by God from imitating it. He calls this section the conclusion of the creed and says it proceeds in rational approaches. He concludes that this argument is more profound than even the miracle of transforming a staff, because one might attribute that to the prophet's special knowledge, whereas the Quran was uttered by an illiterate man and no one can produce its like, which can only be explained by divine prevention.

Key Terms
صرف (sarf)
خرق العوائد (breaking of norms)
إعجاز (inimitability)
جزالة (eloquence)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Quran's inimitability is solely due to its linguistic excellence.
Correction: Juwayni argues that even if the Quran were unmatched in style, the decisive factor is divine prevention (sarf), which makes it impossible for anyone to produce its like, regardless of ability.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the primary explanation for the inability of people to produce speech like the Quran?
A) The Quran's inherent rhetorical excellence
B) God's prevention (sarf) of people from doing so
C) The Prophet's own eloquence
D) The people's lack of ability in Arabic

Key Takeaway
Juwayni's proof of sarf establishes that the Quran's inimitability is not just about its content but about God's active prevention of any imitation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have just read that God actively prevents anyone from imitating the Quran. Have you ever genuinely tested that claim by trying to produce something like it? If not, why do you dismiss it without engagement?
?
Juwayni says that sarf is 'more powerful and effective' than a mere extraordinary act. Does your heart tremble at the Quran's miraculous nature, or have you taken it for granted? What will you do today to reawaken that awe?
?
If God’s prevention (sarf) is the reason no imitation exists, then your inability to produce Quranic speech is not weakness but divine will. How does that change your responsibility toward the Quran? Will you now commit to reciting and reflecting on it daily?

Why Sarf is the Most Profound Miracle
Juwayni argues for the superiority of sarf over other miracles.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Juwayni considers the proof of sarf to be more profound than other miracles like the transformation of a staff, and how it serves as a rational argument for prophethood.

Core Idea
Sarf is a greater miracle than breaking norms because it overcomes human capability directly.

After presenting the proof of sarf, Juwayni argues for its superiority over other miracles. He contrasts it with the miracle of Moses' staff, which could be attributed to the prophet's special knowledge, whereas the Quran was revealed to an illiterate man, making divine prevention the only explanation. This section highlights the rational strength of the sarf argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the concluding part of the passage
Read the Arabic and translation from 'فكيف يهتدى' to the end. Note Juwayni's claim that this is the 'conclusion of the creed' and proceeds in rational approaches.
Look for the phrase 'خاتمة العقيدة' (conclusion of the creed).
2
Understand the comparison with other miracles
Juwayni compares sarf to the transformation of a staff (Moses' miracle). He argues that one might attribute the latter to the prophet's own spiritual excellence, but the Quran was brought by an illiterate man, leaving no explanation except divine prevention.
This comparison shows the unique strength of the Quranic miracle.
3
Identify the rational approach
Juwayni emphasizes that this proof is based on rational approaches (مآخذ عقلية). It is not based solely on tradition but on logical necessity.
This aligns with the kalam methodology of using reason to support revelation.
4
Reflect on the final claim
Juwayni concludes that this argument is 'very profound' (بالغ جدا) and is the most effective way to demonstrate the Quranic miracle.

Source Text

A) Because the staff miracle was only temporary

D) Because sarf involves many people

Key Takeaway

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read the Quran, do you marvel more at its literary beauty or at the fact that God actively prevents anyone else from producing it? Which response truly makes you feel His power over your own abilities? Commit to asking God to show you His prevention in your life.
?
The Prophet was illiterate—he could not rely on human learning. Does this humble your intellectual pride? In what area of your knowledge do you feel self-sufficient, forgetting that even your ability to understand comes from God? Write down one thing you will do this week to acknowledge your utter dependence on Him.
?
Juwayni calls the argument of sarf the 'conclusion of the creed.' Is your faith built on a rational conviction that this Quran could only come from God, or is it just a habit inherited from your environment? If God removed His ‘prevention’ and allowed people to produce something similar, would your faith still stand? Take a moment to examine your heart and ask: what is the real foundation of my iman?

The Second Proof: Tawatur of Miracles in Meaning
Juwayni introduces the second proof: tawatur of miracles in meaning.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the second proof of prophethood: that the Prophet's miracles are established by tawatur of meaning from multiple reports.

Core Idea
Collective tawatur makes the Prophet's miracles certain even if each report is not individually mutawatir.

Juwayni presents the second proof for prophethood, moving beyond individual miracles to the cumulative testimony of many reports. He argues that the widespread transmission of extraordinary events in the context of the Prophet's call establishes his prophethood with certainty. This lesson explains the concept of tawatur of meaning and the examples Juwayni provides.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that Juwayni lists several miracles as examples.
2
Identify the concept of tawatur of meaning
Juwayni says it is 'mutawatir in meaning' (tawatur min tariq al-ma'na) that extraordinary events occurred. Understand that this means the overall idea is certain, not each individual report.
This is a key methodological point in kalam.
3
Examine the examples
Look at the examples: splitting of the moon, wolf speaking, water flowing from fingers, multiplication of food. Ask yourself how these collectively support the claim of prophethood.
These are well-known miracles in Islamic tradition.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والمسلك الثانى : أنه تواتر من طريق المعنى: أنه جرت عليه الخوارق عادات في قصده الدعاء الى تصديقه . كشق القمر، وأمكالمة الذئب اياه ، وتبع الماء من بين أصابعه ، وتكثير الطعام القليل ، حتى يكفى الجمع الكثير ، والجم الغفير الى غيرها ، مما وردت به الأخبار .. وكل قصة من ذلك القصص وإن لم توافر في نفسها . فقيد ثبت بمجموعها : أن محمدا صلى الله عليه وسلم كان يجرى عليه في معرض الدعوة ( من خوارق العادة ) ما يعجز عنه غيره . والمعانى الكلية تثبت بالوقائع التي تنقل أفرادها آحادا ، وهذا كعلمنا ( بشجاعة على ابن أبي طالب عليه السلام ، هذا ضرورى مستفيض ولكنه متلقى من أقاصيص نقلت من آحاد . وكذلك الطريق فى العلم ( بسخاء حاتم الطائى الى غيره من المعانى الكلية . ثم البر في هذا الفصل .. أنه قد تحقق بالتواتر والاستفاضة تعلقه صلى الله عليه وسلم بأجناس مختلفة من البدائع ، ولو عارض شخص في واحد منها لوهت دعواه ، وانطلقت الألسن فيه ، وتحزب أصحابه إلى مرتاب فيه ، والى ذاب عنه عنه . تقليدا ، ولا تنثر نظام الأمر ، أحد المعارضته في شيء مما جاء به كان ذلك أصدق آية على تمييزه على الخلائق بالنبوة .
And the second path: that it is mutawatir in meaning that extraordinary events occurred to him in the context of his call to belief, such as the splitting of the moon, the wolf speaking to him, water flowing from between his fingers, the multiplication of little food until it sufficed a large crowd and a great multitude, etc., of what the reports have mentioned. And every story among these stories, even if not mutawatir in itself, is a proof established by its totality: That Muhammad (peace be upon him) had extraordinary events occur to him in the context of his call, which others are incapable of. And universal meanings are established by individual incidents transmitted singly. This is like our knowledge of the bravery of Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him); this is necessary and widespread but is received from stories transmitted singly. Likewise the path to knowledge of the generosity of Hatim al-Ta'i and other universal meanings. Then the proof in this chapter: That it has been confirmed through tawatur and widespread transmission that the Prophet was connected to various kinds of miracles. And if a person were to object to even one of them, his claim would be weakened, tongues would be set loose against him, his companions would become divided into those doubting him and those abandoning him out of imitation. And the system of [his] affair would not be disrupted. The fact that no one opposed him in anything he brought is the truest sign of his distinction over creation by prophethood.
Source pages p. 73

Commentary
This passage presents the second proof of prophethood: the tawatur (mass transmission) of miracles in meaning. Juwayni states that it is mutawatir in meaning that extraordinary events (kharq al-'adat) occurred to the Prophet in the context of his call, such as the splitting of the moon, the wolf speaking, water flowing from his fingers, and multiplication of food. He argues that even if each individual story is not mutawatir in itself, the cumulative body of reports establishes as certain that the Prophet performed miracles. He gives analogies: our knowledge of Ali's bravery and Hatim's generosity is based on individually transmitted stories yet yields certain knowledge. Then he says that it has been confirmed through tawatur and widespread transmission that the Prophet was associated with various kinds of miracles. The proof is that no one was able to oppose him by producing a similar miracle. If someone had objected to even one miracle, that would have weakened his claim, caused tongues to speak against him, and divided his followers. The fact that no opposition occurred in anything he brought is the truest sign of his prophethood.

Key Terms
تواتر (tawatur)
خوارق العادة (extraordinary events)
معنى كلي (universal meaning)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that each miracle report must be individually mutawatir to be accepted as proof.
Correction: Juwayni shows that collective tawatur (tawatur of meaning) from multiple reports suffices to establish certainty about the Prophet's miracles.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, how are the Prophet's miracles established?
A) Each miracle report must be individually mutawatir
B) By the collective tawatur of meaning from many reports
C) By relying only on the Quran as a miracle
D) By direct eyewitness testimony of the Prophet

Key Takeaway
The Prophet's miracles are confirmed by the collective testimony of many reports, even if each is not individually mutawatir.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have just learned that the Prophet's miracles are proven by the collective weight of many reports. Have you ever dismissed a miracle story because you heard it only once or from a questionable source? How does this lesson challenge you to reconsider your level of certainty in the prophetic miracles? What step will you take today to strengthen your conviction in them?
?
Juwayni shows that multiple signs, each individually insufficient, together give certainty. In your own life, Allah has placed many signs—in the Qur'an, in nature, in your experiences. Are you ignoring the cumulative evidence? What would change if you viewed all the signs as a whole? Commit to recognizing one overlooked sign today.
?
If you truly believe that the wolf spoke to the Prophet and the moon split, how must that affect your obedience to him? Does your daily life reflect the reality of these miracles? What is one practical change you can make this week to live as if you are certain of the Prophet's truth?

Prophethood

Universal Meanings from Individual Reports
Juwayni explains how universal meanings are established and presents the conclusive sign of prophethood.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how universal truths like the Prophet's prophethood are established through multiple individual accounts, using the analogy of Ali's bravery and Hatim's generosity, and why the lack of opposition to his miracles is a decisive sign.

Core Idea
Universal meanings are known from many single reports, just like we know Ali's bravery and Hatim's generosity.

Juwayni strengthens his proof by drawing an analogy: our certain knowledge of historical figures like Ali's bravery or Hatim's generosity comes from many individual reports, not a single mutawatir narration. Similarly, the Prophet's miracles are confirmed by collective testimony. He then argues that the fact that no one could oppose the Prophet's miracles is the clearest sign of his prophethood.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on universal meanings
Read the Arabic passage that explains how universal meanings (ma'ani kulliyya) are established by individual reports (ahad).
Pay attention to the examples of Ali and Hatim.
2
Reflect on the analogy
Juwayni uses the bravery of Ali and generosity of Hatim as analogies. How do we know these traits? From many stories transmitted singly. Apply this to the Prophet's miracles.
This is a common argument in kalam for cumulative evidence.
3
Understand the conclusive sign
Juwayni says the fact that no one could oppose the Prophet's miracles is the truest sign (asdaq aya) of his prophethood. Ask yourself why this is a strong proof.
It implies that no challenger could produce a similar miracle.
4
Connect to the overall proof
See how the cumulative testimony of miracles and the impossibility of opposition together form a complete proof.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

And every story among these stories, even if not mutawatir in itself, is a proof established by its totality: That Muhammad (peace be upon him) had extraordinary events occur to him in the context of his call, which others are incapable of. And universal meanings are established by individual incidents transmitted singly. This is like our knowledge of the bravery of Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him); this is necessary and widespread but is received from stories transmitted singly. Likewise the path to knowledge of the generosity of Hatim al-Ta'i and other universal meanings. Then the proof in this chapter: That it has been confirmed through tawatur and widespread transmission that the Prophet was connected to various kinds of miracles. And if a person were to object to even one of them, his claim would be weakened, tongues would be set loose against him, his companions would become divided into those doubting him and those abandoning him out of imitation. And the system of [his] affair would not be disrupted. The fact that no one opposed him in anything he brought is the truest sign of his distinction over creation by prophethood.
Source pages p. 73

Commentary
This passage presents the second proof of prophethood: the tawatur (mass transmission) of miracles in meaning. Juwayni states that it is mutawatir in meaning that extraordinary events (kharq al-'adat) occurred to the Prophet in the context of his call, such as the splitting of the moon, the wolf speaking, water flowing from his fingers, and multiplication of food. استفاضة (widespread transmission)
آحاد (singular reports)

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that knowledge of historical traits requires a single continuous chain of narration.
Correction: Juwayni shows that universal meanings are established by the cumulative weight of many individual reports, not by a single chain.

How does Juwayni argue that the fact no one opposed the Prophet's miracles is a sign of prophethood?
A) Because opponents would have been divine
B) Because successful opposition would have weakened the claim, but none succeeded
C) Because the Quran mentions it
D) Because all miracles are logically self-evident

Key Takeaway
Universal truths about the Prophet's miracles are established by the collective weight of many reports, and the impossibility of opposition is the ultimate proof.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The Prophet's mission was so well-established that no one could successfully oppose even one aspect of it. In what area of your life have you entertained doubts about his teachings because you have not examined the cumulative evidence? What will you do to confront that doubt today?
?
If universal meanings like Ali's bravery are known through many small testimonies, how many small signs of Allah's guidance have you dismissed in your life? Will you gather them today and let them lead you to certainty?
?
Juwayni says that if an objection were possible, the system of the Prophet's affair would be disrupted. Is your own commitment to him orderly, or have you allowed disruptions—neglect, compromise, procrastination—to weaken your faith? What one practical change will you make this week to restore order in following him?

Prophethood
beginner
