The Second Proof: Tawatur of Meaning of Miracles
Juwayni presents the second proof: tawatur of meaning of miracles

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's second proof of prophethood based on the mass transmission of meaning (tawatur al-ma'na) of miracles, even if individual reports are not mutawatir.

Core Idea
Collective meaning from multiple reports yields certainty, even when single reports are weak.

Juwayni now introduces a powerful argument: prophethood is proven not by a single miracle but by the overwhelming consensus of meaning from many miraculous reports. This lesson explores the concept of tawatur of meaning and how it applies to the miracles of Prophet Muhammad (SAW).

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on tawatur of meaning
Focus on the first section where Juwayni states the second approach and gives examples of miracles like the splitting of the moon and water gushing from fingers.
Notice that these are well-known miracles from the Quran and Hadith.
2
Understand the key concept: tawatur of meaning
Juwayni argues that even if each story is not individually mutawatir, the overall meaning that extraordinary events occurred is established by their totality.
This is different from tawatur of exact wording.
3
Reflect on the examples
Consider why these specific miracles are convincing. They are diverse and well-attested in the tradition.
4
Connect to the learning objective
This lesson establishes the foundational logic of the second proof: certainty through collective meaning.
This prepares you for the analogy in the next lesson.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والمسلك الثانى: أنه تواتر من طريق المعنى: أنه جرت عليه الخوارق عادات في قصده الدعاء الى تصديقه. كشق القمر، وأم كالمة الذئب اياه، ونبع الماء من بين أصابعه، وتكثير الطعام القليل حتى يكفى الجمع الكثير والجم الغفير الى غيرها مما وردت به الأخبار. وكل قصة من ذلك القصص وإن لم توافر في نفسها فقيد ثبت بمجموعها: أن محمدا صلى الله عليه وسلم كان يجرى عليه في معرض الدعوة من خوارق العادة ما يعجز عنه غيره. والمعانى الكلية تثبت بالوقائع التي تنقل أفرادها آحادا، وهذا كعلمنا بشجاعة على ابن أبي طالب عليه السلام، هذا ضرورى مستفيض ولكنه متلقى من أقاصيص نقلت من آحاد. وكذلك الطريق فى العلم بسخاء حاتم الطائى الى غيره من المعانى الكلية. ثم البر في هذا الفصل: أنه قد تحقق بالتواتر والاستفاضة تعلقه صلى الله عليه وسلم بأجناس مختلفة من البدائع، ولو عارض شخص في واحد منها لوهت دعواه، وانطلقت الألسن فيه، وتحزب أصحابه إلى مرتاب فيه، والى ذاب عنه عنه. تقليدا، ولا تنثر نظام الأمر، أحد المعارضته في شيء مما جاء به كان ذلك أصدق آية على تمييزه على الخلائق بالنبوة.
And the second approach: that it is mutawatir (mass-transmitted) by way of meaning: that extraordinary events occurred upon him in the context of his call to [people] to believe him, such as the splitting of the moon, and the wolf speaking to him, and the water gushing from between his fingers, and the multiplication of little food until it suffices a great multitude and a huge crowd, and other things reported in the traditions. And every story among these stories, even if it is not [individually] mutawatir in itself, yet it is established by their totality: that Muhammad (peace and blessings upon him) had extraordinary things occur upon him in the context of his call that others are incapable of. And universal meanings are established by incidents whose particular instances are reported through ahad (single) chains, and this is like our knowledge of the bravery of Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him) – this is necessary (daruri) and widespread (mustafid), but it is received from stories transmitted through ahad chains. And similarly is the way to knowledge of the generosity of Hatim al-Ta'i and other universal meanings. Then the proof in this chapter is that it has been verified through tawatur and istifada (widespread transmission) that he (peace and blessings upon him) was associated with diverse kinds of wondrous things. And if a person were to object to one of them, his claim would be weakened, tongues would be set loose against him, and his companions would divide into those who doubt him and those who abandon him out of blind imitation. And [the fact that] no one among the opponents opposed him in anything he brought is the truest sign of his distinction over creation by prophethood.
Source pages p. 74

Commentary
This passage presents the second proof (al-maslak al-thani) of the prophethood of Muhammad (SAW), relying on the concept of tawatur of meaning (tawatur min tariq al-ma'na). Juwayni argues that although individual reports of specific miracles may not be mutawatir (mass-transmitted) in their precise details, the collective meaning—that extraordinary events occurred during his call—is established through multiple chains. He lists examples: the splitting of the moon, the wolf speaking to him, water gushing from his fingers, and multiplication of food. The universal conclusion that he performed miracles is derived from these many ahad (solitary) reports. Juwayni draws an analogy with common knowledge: we know of Ali's bravery or Hatim's generosity through stories transmitted individually, yet the overall meaning is certain and widespread. He further notes that the very fact that no opponent ever successfully contested any of these miracles—had they done so, his claim would have been weakened and his followers would have doubted—constitutes the clearest sign of his prophethood.

Key Terms
تواتر المعنى
خوارق العادة
آحاد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that each miracle must be individually mutawatir to prove prophethood.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies that the overall meaning (tawatur al-ma'na) is sufficient; individual reports need not be mutawatir.

Checkpoint
What does Juwayni mean by 'tawatur of meaning' (تواتر المعنى)?
A) Every single miracle report is mutawatir
B) The overall fact of miracles is established by many reports, even if each is ahad
C) Only the Quran is used as proof
D) Meanings are known through rational deduction alone

Key Takeaway
The second proof relies on the collective certainty from multiple miracle reports, not on each report being individually mass-transmitted.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you hear about the Prophet's miracles individually (like the moon splitting), do you sometimes doubt because you only hear one narration? How does Juwayni's argument about cumulative certainty change the way you view these reports? Will you now accept them with greater conviction?
?
The passage says that the miracles occurred 'in the context of his call to believe him.' How does this remind you that every miracle was an act of invitation from Allah to you personally? Are you responding to that call in your daily life?
?
If the cumulative mass transmission of miracles gives certainty about the truth of Muhammad's prophethood, what does your level of certainty about this say about your faith? Is it based on evidence or just habit? How can you strengthen your certainty today?

Prophethood

Analogy and Confirmation of Prophethood
Juwayni uses analogy and lack of opposition to confirm prophethood

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's use of analogy with known historical figures and the argument from lack of opposition to confirm prophethood.

Core Idea
Knowledge from ahad reports can be certain, as seen in common knowledge of historical figures.

Juwayni strengthens his argument by comparing the knowledge of the Prophet's miracles to our certain knowledge of Ali's bravery and Hatim's generosity—both derived from ahad reports. He then adds a final proof: the fact that no opponent ever successfully refuted any miracle is itself the greatest sign.

Guided Path
1
Read the analogy with Ali and Hatim
Juwayni argues that we know Ali's bravery and Hatim's generosity through ahad stories, yet this knowledge is certain. Similarly, the Prophet's miracles are known with certainty.
This shows that ahad reports can yield certainty when they converge on a universal meaning.
2
Understand the final argument
Juwayni then says that if someone had objected to any miracle and succeeded, his followers would have doubted. Since no such successful objection occurred, it confirms his prophethood.
This is an argument from silence but framed as a historical fact.
3
Reflect on the logical structure
The passage moves from analogy to a direct proof: the absence of successful opposition is the truest sign.
Consider how this complements the first part of the proof.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والمسلك الثانى: أنه تواتر من طريق المعنى: أنه جرت عليه الخوارق عادات في قصده الدعاء الى تصديقه. كشق القمر، وأم كالمة الذئب اياه، ونبع الماء من بين أصابعه، وتكثير الطعام القليل حتى يكفى الجمع الكثير والجم الغفير الى غيرها مما وردت به الأخبار. وكل قصة من ذلك القصص وإن لم توافر في نفسها فقيد ثبت بمجموعها: أن محمدا صلى الله عليه وسلم كان يجرى عليه في معرض الدعوة من خوارق العادة ما يعجز عنه غيره. والمعانى الكلية تثبت بالوقائع التي تنقل أفرادها آحادا، وهذا كعلمنا بشجاعة على ابن أبي طالب عليه السلام، هذا ضرورى مستفيض ولكنه متلقى من أقاصيص نقلت من آحاد. وكذلك الطريق فى العلم بسخاء حاتم الطائى الى غيره من المعانى الكلية. ثم البر في هذا الفصل: أنه قد تحقق بالتواتر والاستفاضة تعلقه صلى الله عليه وسلم بأجناس مختلفة من البدائع، ولو عارض شخص في واحد منها لوهت دعواه، وانطلقت الألسن فيه، وتحزب أصحابه إلى مرتاب فيه، والى ذاب عنه عنه. تقليدا، ولا تنثر نظام الأمر، أحد المعارضته في شيء مما جاء به كان ذلك أصدق آية على تمييزه على الخلائق بالنبوة.

And universal meanings are established by incidents whose particular instances are reported through ahad (single) chains, and this is like our knowledge of the bravery of Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him) – this is necessary (daruri) and widespread (mustafid), but it is received from stories transmitted through ahad chains. And similarly is the way to knowledge of the generosity of Hatim al-Ta'i and other universal meanings. Then the proof in this chapter is that it has been verified through tawatur and istifada (widespread transmission) that he (peace and blessings upon him) was associated with diverse kinds of wondrous things. And if a person were to object to one of them, his claim would be weakened, tongues would be set loose against him, and his companions would divide into those who doubt him and those who abandon him out of blind imitation. And [the fact that] no one among the opponents opposed him in anything he brought is the truest sign of his distinction over creation by prophethood.
Source pages p. 74

Commentary
This passage presents the second proof (al-maslak al-thani) of the prophethood of Muhammad (SAW), relying on the concept of tawatur of meaning (tawatur min tariq al-ma'na). Juwayni argues that although individual reports of specific miracles may not be mutawatir (mass-transmitted) in their precise details, the collective meaning—that extraordinary events occurred during his call—is established through multiple chains. He lists examples: the splitting of the moon, the wolf speaking to him, water gushing from his fingers, and multiplication of food. He further notes that the very fact that no opponent ever successfully contested any of these miracles—had they done so, his claim would have been weakened and his followers would have doubted—constitutes the clearest sign of his prophethood.

المعاني الكلية
استفاضة
آحاد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that ahad reports cannot yield certain knowledge.
Correction: Juwayni shows that universal meanings (like bravery of Ali) are known with certainty from ahad reports.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, how do we know the Prophet's miracles are true?
A) Because each miracle is individually mutawatir
B) Because ahad reports of miracles give certain universal meaning, just like knowledge of Ali's bravery
C) Because the Quran explicitly states so
D) Because rational proof alone suffices

Key Takeaway
Certain knowledge of prophethood can be derived from ahad miracle reports through their cumulative meaning, and the lack of successful opposition confirms it.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Juwayni asserts that knowledge of Ali's bravery is certain despite coming from ahad reports. Do you have the same level of certainty in the Prophet's miracles? If not, what is holding you back, and what will you do today to address that hesitation?
?
The opponents had every reason to disprove the Prophet's miracles but failed. Are there any objections to Islam you have heard or harbored that you have not thoroughly investigated? Will you commit to tracing every doubt to its source until it dissolves?
?
If the lack of successful opposition is the 'truest sign' of prophethood, how does that shape your response when someone raises an objection you cannot immediately answer? Do you trust that the truth remains unshaken, or does your confidence waver? What will you do next time you encounter a challenge to your faith?

The Biblical Promise of Ishmael and Isaac
Al-Juwayni cites Genesis promises that Ishmael will become a great nation and Isaac will be blessed.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Juwayni uses the biblical promises to Ishmael and Isaac to support the prophethood of Muhammad.

Core Idea
Both Ishmael and Isaac received divine blessings, grounding Muhammad's prophethood in earlier scripture.

Al-Juwayni draws on the Torah to argue that Ishmael, the ancestor of the Arabs, was explicitly blessed by God to become a great nation. He cites two verses from Genesis to show that both Ishmael and Isaac were recipients of divine promises, providing scriptural continuity for the prophethood of Muhammad.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic text once to familiarize yourself with the wording. Note the names and key phrases.
The text is a direct quote from Genesis.
2
Identify the two verses
The passage cites two separate verses. Identify which verse refers to Ishmael and which to Isaac. Notice the specific blessings mentioned for each.
Genesis 17:20 is for Ishmael; 17:16 is for Isaac.
3
Understand the argument
Al-Juwayni uses these verses to show that Ishmael's blessing includes becoming a great nation and fathering twelve princes, which is seen as a reference to the Arab tribes. This supports Muhammad's prophethood as a descendant of Ishmael.
The blessing of Isaac also includes kings and nations, but the focus is on Ishmael's line.

This passage provides a proof from the Torah that Ishmael is blessed and will become a great nation, and that Isaac will also be blessed with kings and nations. Al-Juwayni quotes Genesis 17:20 and 17:16 to show that both Ishmael and Isaac are recipients of divine promises, which supports the prophethood of Muhammad as a descendant of Ishmael. The mention of twelve princes for Ishmael is seen as a reference to the Arab tribes.

The Prophecy of Shiloh Points to Muhammad
Al-Juwayni interprets Genesis 49:10 as referring to Muhammad.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Juwayni uses a biblical prophecy to argue that Muhammad is the anticipated prophet of peace.

Core Idea
The prophecy of Shiloh foretells the coming of Muhammad.

Al-Juwayni cites a well-known biblical prophecy from Genesis to show that the kingship and law among the Israelites would persist only until a prophet from the line of Ishmael appears. He identifies 'Shiloh' as that prophet of peace—Muhammad. This passage demonstrates how Muslim theologians engaged with earlier scriptures to support the prophethood of Muhammad.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Start by reading the Arabic passage once to get the flow, then read the literal translation. Notice the biblical quotation and its interpretation.
The passage includes a scriptural citation from Genesis 49:10.
2
Identify the claim and evidence
Al-Juwayni makes a claim about the duration of kingship among the descendants of Isaac. What is the claim, and what evidence does he provide?
The evidence is the prophecy itself.
3
Understand the interpretation of 'Shiloh'
Al-Juwayni interprets 'Shiloh' as 'the prophet of peace and security.' Why does he interpret it this way, and what does this reveal about his method?
He also mentions variant readings, which reinforce his point.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٣ - وبين أن الملك لن يزول من نسل اسحق ولن تزول الشريعة الا اذا ظهر نبي من آل اسماعيل ) لا يزول صولجان من يهوذا ومشترع من صلبه حتى يأتي شيلو وتطيعه الشعوب ( تكوين ٤٩ : ١٠ ) لقد رمز بشيلو أى نبى السلام والأمان لهذا الغرض . وفى بعض النسخ شيلون وفي التوراة السامرية ( سليمان )
3 - And it is made clear that the kingship will not depart from the descendants of Isaac, nor will the law depart, until a prophet from the family of Ishmael appears: 'The scepter shall not depart from Judah, nor the lawgiver from between his feet, until Shiloh comes, and to him shall be the obedience of the peoples' (Genesis 49:10). By 'Shiloh' he meant the prophet of peace and security for this purpose. In some copies it is 'Shilon', and in the Samaritan Torah it is 'Solomon'.
Source pages p. 85

Commentary
This passage cites the well-known prophecy of Jacob regarding Judah: the scepter and lawgiver will not depart until Shiloh comes. Al-Juwayni interprets Shiloh as the Prophet of peace, i.e., Muhammad, and notes that the Samaritan Torah reads 'Solomon' which also supports that the prophecy refers to a future prophet. The argument is that the kingship of the Israelites (descendants of Isaac) will persist until a prophet from Ishmael appears, and then it will cease.

Key Terms

يهوذا

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some interpret 'Shiloh' as a place name (the city of Shiloh) rather than a title for a person.
Correction: Al-Juwayni explains that 'Shiloh' here means the prophet of peace and security, not a location. The context of the prophecy (the scepter and lawgiver) indicates a person, not a place.

Key Takeaway
Al-Juwayni interprets the biblical prophecy of Shiloh as pointing to Muhammad, using scriptural evidence to affirm his prophethood.

The Prophecy of a Prophet Like Moses: A Fulfillment in Muhammad
Al-Juwayni cites and interprets Deuteronomy's prophecy of a prophet like Moses, argued to be Muhammad.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Juwayni uses biblical prophecy to argue that the promised prophet like Moses is Muhammad, not an Israelite.

Core Idea
The prophet like Moses must come from Ishmael's lineage because no such prophet arose from Israel.

Al-Juwayni turns to the Torah to support the prophethood of Muhammad. He cites Deuteronomy's prophecy of a prophet like Moses and argues that since Deuteronomy 34:10 confirms no prophet like Moses arose in Israel, the promised one must come from the 'brethren' of Israel—the Ishmaelites. This passage shows how interfaith scripture can be used in Islamic theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the biblical prophecy
Read the passage from Deuteronomy 18:15-22 as cited by Al-Juwayni. Notice the description of the prophet: one who speaks God's words and must be obeyed.
This prophecy is central to the argument.
2
Understand the term 'brethren'
Al-Juwayni interprets 'from among your brethren' to mean the Ishmaelites, the brothers of Israel, not Israelites themselves.
This interpretation is key to identifying the prophet as Muhammad.
3
See the proof from Deuteronomy 34:10
Deuteronomy 34:10 states that no prophet like Moses has arisen in Israel. This confirms the prophet must be from outside Israel.
This verse seals the argument.
4
Conclude with Muhammad
The prophet described in Deuteronomy 18 matches Muhammad: he came from the Ishmaelites, spoke God's words, and performed signs. Reflect on how this fits Islamic belief.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٤ وذكر الله أوصاف النبى الآتى من اسماعيل البركة فى هذا النص : « لك الرب الهك نبيا من بينكم . اخوتكم مثلى له تسمعون أقيم لهم نبيا من بين أخوتهم مثلك وألقى كلامى فى فيه ، فيخاطبهم بجميع ما آمره به . وای انسان لم يطع كلامى الذى يتكلم به باسمى فاني احاسبه عليه . واى نبى تجبر فقال باسمى قولا لم مره أن نقوله أو تنبأ باسم آلهة آخر فليقتل ذلك النبي ، فان قلت في نفسك : كيف يعرف القول الذى لم يقله الرب ؟ فان تكلم النبي باسم الرب ، ولم يتم كلامه ولم يقع فذلك الكلام لم يتكلم به الرب بل لتخبره تكلم به النبي فلا تخافوه ) تثنية ١٨ : ١٥ - ٢٢ ( ووضحت التوراة أنه لن يأتى ۱۸ من ' بنى اسرائيل مثل موسى ' ولم يقم من بعد نبى فى اسرائيل كموسى الذي عرفه الرب وجها الى وجه في جميع الآيات والمعجزات التي بعثه الرب ليصنعها .. الخ وفى السامرية ) ولا يقوم .... الخ ) ( تثنية ٣٤ : ١٠ )

But the prophet who presumes to speak a word in my name that I have not commanded him to speak, or who speaks in the name of other gods, that prophet shall be put to death. And if you say in your heart, "How may we know the word that the Lord has not spoken?" When a prophet speaks in the name of the Lord, if the word does not come to pass or come true, that is a word that the Lord has not spoken; the prophet has spoken it presumptuously; you need not be afraid of him.' (Deuteronomy 18:15-22) And the Torah made clear that no prophet like Moses has arisen from among the Children of Israel: 'And there has not arisen a prophet since in Israel like Moses, whom the Lord knew face to face, in all the signs and wonders that the Lord sent him to do...' etc. In the Samaritan: 'Nor will arise...' etc. (Deuteronomy 34:10).
Source pages p. 85

Commentary
This passage presents the key prophecy of a prophet like Moses from Deuteronomy. Al-Juwayni argues that this prophet cannot be from Israel because no prophet like Moses arose from them, as Deuteronomy 34:10 states. Therefore, the prophet must be from the 'brethren' of Israel, i.e., the Ishmaelites. The description of the prophet—one who speaks God's words and is obeyed—fits Muhammad. The test of a true prophet is also given: his words must come true.

Misconception: The prophecy in Deuteronomy 18 refers to Joshua or another Israelite prophet.
Correction: Deuteronomy 34:10 explicitly states there has been no prophet like Moses in Israel, so the prophecy must refer to a non-Israelite prophet, which Muslims believe is Muhammad.

Al-Juwayni uses Deuteronomy 18:15-22 and 34:10 to show that the prophet like Moses must be Muhammad, as no Israelite prophet fulfilled the description.

Moses' Blessing and Ishmael's Dwelling in Paran
Al-Juwayni cites Moses' blessing and Ishmael's dwelling in Paran to argue for Muhammad's prophethood.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Juwayni uses biblical references to Mount Paran and Ishmael's dwelling to support the prophethood of Muhammad.

Core Idea
The location of Ishmael in Paran points to the prophet from Arabia.

This passage from Al-Juwayni's 'Al-Aqida al-Nizamiyya' presents a biblical argument linking Moses' blessing, which mentions God shining from Mount Paran, with the dwelling of Ishmael in the wilderness of Paran. By identifying Paran as the Arabian land, Al-Juwayni establishes a scriptural foundation for the prophethood of Muhammad, who came from the descendants of Ishmael. This lesson will guide you through the biblical verses and their interpretation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Note the two biblical quotations from Deuteronomy and Genesis.
Pay attention to the names of places: Sinai, Seir, Paran.
2
Identify the argument's structure
Al-Juwayni first cites Moses' blessing (Deut 33:1-3) which mentions God shining from Paran. Then he cites the story of Hagar and Ishmael (Gen 21:17-21) showing Ishmael lived in Paran. Finally, he adds another verse (Gen 16:11-12) about Ishmael's dwelling.
The key is the geographical connection to Arabia.
3
Understand the significance of Paran
Reflect on why identifying Paran as Arabia is crucial. It links the biblical prophecy to the land of the Arabs, from whom Muhammad came.
This is a typical argument from biblical prophecy used by Muslim scholars.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
أقبل الرب من سيناء وأشرق لهم من سعير وتجلى من جبل فاران راتى من ربي القدس وعن يمينه نار شريعة لهم انه أحب الشعب جميع قديسيه فى يدك ، وهم ساجدون عند قدمك يقتبسون من كلماتك » ( تثنیه ۳۳ : ۱-۳ ) وبين موسى أن اسماعيل سكن في جبل فاران فقيد نادى ملاك الله هاجر : « وقال لها : مالك يا هاجر لا تخافي فان الله قد سمع صوت الغلام حيث هو . قومى فخذى الغلام ولتكن يدك معه . فانى جاعله أمة كبيرة وكشف الله عن عينيها فرات بئر ماء فمضت وملأت القربة ماء وسقت الغلام. وكان الله مع الفلام حتى كبر . فأقام بالبرية وكان راميا بالقوس وأقام ببرية فاران واتخذت له أمه امرأة من أرض مصر » ( تکوین ۲۱ : ۱۷ ۲۱ ) وبين أن فاران فى الأرض العربية مقابل سكنى بنى اسرائيل فيها . بنو اسرائيل فى الشمال وبنو اسماعيل فى الجنوب فقد نادى ملاك الله هاجر : « وقال لها ملاك الرب : ها أنت حامل وستلدين ابنا وتسمينه اسماعيل لأن الرب قد سمع صوت شقائك ويكون رجلا وحشيا يده على الكل ويد الكل عليه وامام جميع اخوته يسكن ( ) تكوين ۱٦ : ۱۱ - ۱۲ )
'The Lord came from Sinai and dawned from Seir upon them; he shone forth from Mount Paran; he came from the ten thousands of holy ones, and at his right hand was a fiery law for them. Yes, he loved the people; all his holy ones are in your hand; they sat down at your feet, each one receiving your words.' (Deuteronomy 33:1-3) And Moses explained that Ishmael dwelt in Mount Paran, for the angel of God called to Hagar: 'And he said to her: What is the matter with you, Hagar? Do not fear, for God has heard the voice of the lad where he is. Arise, lift up the lad, and let your hand be with him, for I will make him into a great nation.' And God opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water, and she went and filled the waterskin and gave the lad a drink. And God was with the lad, and he grew up, and he lived in the wilderness and became an expert with the bow. And he lived in the wilderness of Paran, and his mother took a wife for him from the land of Egypt.' (Genesis 21:17-21) And he explained that Paran is in the Arabian land opposite the dwelling of the Children of Israel. The Children of Israel are in the north and the Children of Ishmael in the south, for the angel of the Lord called to Hagar: 'And the angel of the Lord said to her: Behold, you are pregnant and shall bear a son and shall call his name Ishmael, because the Lord has heard your affliction. He shall be a wild donkey of a man, his hand against everyone and everyone's hand against him, and he shall dwell over against all his brothers.' (Genesis 16:11-12)
Source pages p. 86

Commentary
This passage connects the geographical location mentioned in Moses' blessing—Mount Paran—with the dwelling place of Ishmael. Al-Juwayni cites Deuteronomy 33:1-3 which describes God coming from Sinai, Seir, and Paran. He then shows from Genesis that Ishmael lived in the wilderness of Paran. Paran is identified as the Arabian land, which matches the home of the Ishmaelites (Arabs). This links the prophet to come (Muhammad) with the region of Paran. Additional verses confirm Ishmael's dwelling among his brothers and his wild nature.

Key Terms
جبل فاران
سعير
سيناء
برية فاران

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think Paran is in the Sinai Peninsula, not in Arabia.
Correction: Al-Juwayni explicitly states that Paran is in the Arabian land, opposite the dwelling of the Children of Israel, based on the biblical account of Ishmael's dwelling.

Key Takeaway
Al-Juwayni uses biblical references to Mount Paran and Ishmael's dwelling to show that the prophesied blessing from Sinai, Seir, and Paran points to the prophet Muhammad from Arabia.

?

?
The blessing from Sinai, Seir, and Paran depicts God's favor arriving through different prophets. Ask yourself: Are you truly grateful for the final favor (Muhammad and the Qur'an) in a way that transforms your actions, or do you take it for granted?

The Prophecy of the Foolish Nation in Deuteronomy
Al-Juwayni cites Deuteronomy 32:21 as a prophecy about the transfer of prophethood to the Arabs.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Juwayni interprets Deuteronomy 32:21 as a prophecy about the Arabs and their prophet Muhammad.

Core Idea
A biblical prophecy foretells the transfer of kingdom and law from Israel to an illiterate Arab nation.

Al-Juwayni, in arguing for the prophethood of Muhammad, points to a verse from Deuteronomy where God says He will provoke Israel with a 'foolish nation.' This passage explains that this nation refers to the Arabs, the children of Ishmael, whom the Jews considered illiterate. Thus, the Torah itself predicted the rise of Islam and the final prophet.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the literal translation to grasp the meaning.
Notice the biblical citation style.
2
Identify key terms
Find the terms 'زوال الملك والشريعة', 'ليسوا شعبا', 'بغبياء', and 'أمة أمية' in the Arabic. These are central to the argument.
Consider why 'illiterate nation' is significant.
3
Understand the interpretation
Al-Juwayni interprets 'those who are not a people' and 'foolish nation' as the Arabs, children of Ishmael. Ask yourself: why does he make this connection?
The Jews considered the Arabs illiterate, hence 'foolish'.
4
Note additional evidence
Samaritan and Hebrew scholars claim that 'jiddan jiddan' and 'a great nation' refer to Muhammad. Reflect on how this supports the primary argument.
Al-Juwayni references his own introduction to a book for further proof.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٦- واكد موسى على زوال الملك والشريعة الى الابد من بنى اسرائيل فى يوم من الأيام بقوله على لسان الله تعالى . هم اغاروني بمن ليس الها وأغضبونى بأباطيلهم وأنا أغيرهم بمن ليسوا شعبا . يقوم غابياء أغضبهم ). ) تثنية ۳۲ : ۲۱ ) يقلد العرب بنو اسماعيل لأنهم في نظر اليهود : امة امية . ) نقل نصوص التوراة من ترجمة الآباء اليسوعيين في بيروت سنة ۱۹٦۸ م ) ويقول علماء من اليهود السامريين والعبرانيين أن ( جدا جدا ) وكذلك » أمة عظيمة » يشيران الى اسم محمد محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم وقد بينـا ذلك في تقديمنا لكتاب » اظهار الحق » للشيخ الإمام رحمت الله الهندي وفي كتب غيره
6 - And Moses affirmed the removal of the kingdom and the law from the Children of Israel forever in a day, by saying on the tongue of Allah the Exalted: 'They have made me jealous with what is no god; they have provoked me to anger with their idols. So I will make them jealous with those who are not a people; with a foolish nation I will provoke them.' (Deuteronomy 32:21) He means the Arabs, the Children of Ishmael, because in the view of the Jews they are an illiterate nation. (The texts of the Torah are taken from the translation of the Jesuit fathers in Beirut in 1968 AD.) And scholars from among the Samaritan and Hebrew Jews say that 'exceedingly' (jiddan jiddan) and 'a great nation' refer to the name of Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and we have demonstrated that in our introduction to the book 'Izhar al-Haqq' by the eminent Imam Rahmat Allah al-Hindi and in other books.
Source pages p. 86

Commentary
This passage argues that the kingdom and law will be taken from Israel and given to a 'foolish nation' referred to in Deuteronomy 32:21. Al-Juwayni identifies this nation as the Arabs, the children of Ishmael, whom the Jews considered illiterate. This, like 'jiddan jiddan' and 'great nation' in earlier verses, is interpreted as referring to Muhammad. The author also references his own introduction to Rahmat Allah al-Hindi's book as further evidence.

Key Terms
زوال الملك والشريعة
ليسوا شعبا
بغبياء
أمة أمية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The 'foolish nation' in Deuteronomy 32:21 refers to any pagan nation, not specifically the Arabs.
Correction: Al-Juwayni explicitly identifies it as the Arabs, the children of Ishmael, because they were considered an illiterate nation by the Jews.

Checkpoint
How does al-Juwayni interpret the 'foolish nation' in Deuteronomy 32:21?
A) As the Romans
B) As the Arabs, children of Ishmael
C) As the Babylonians
D) As the Philistines

Key Takeaway
Deuteronomy 32:21 prophesies the transfer of prophethood from Israel to the Arab nation, and Jewish scholars themselves see references to Muhammad in the Torah.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read that God chose a nation considered 'foolish' and 'illiterate' by others, do you see yourself as part of that humble lineage? Or do you still cling to pride in your own background, thinking you are better than others because of your lineage or education?
?
The Jewish scholars themselves saw Muhammad (ﷺ) in their scriptures. If you are a Muslim, do you seek out and learn from the original scriptures of previous nations with humility, recognizing that God's guidance has always been reaching humanity? Or do you dismiss them entirely, missing the traces of truth they contain?
?
This prophecy speaks of a transfer of leadership and law. In your own life, are you ready to accept that God may remove blessings or roles from you if you become ungrateful, and give them to others who seem less deserving? How does that change the way you appreciate what you have right now?

Prophethood

Biblical prophecies about Ishmael and Muhammad
Juwayni cites Torah and Genesis to show prophecies of the Prophet Muhammad.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni uses biblical scriptures (Torah and Genesis) to prove that the Prophet Muhammad was foretold.

Core Idea
The Torah and Gospel foretold Muhammad; Juwayni proves it from the Bible itself.

Juwayni, a master of Islamic theology, turns to the Torah and Genesis to show that even earlier scriptures point to the coming of Muhammad. In this passage, he cites specific verses and connects them to Ishmael's dwelling in Paran, Arabia. This was a powerful argument in interfaith debates, showing that Islam fulfills previous revelations.

Guided Path
1
Read the biblical citations
Juwayni quotes three passages: Deuteronomy 33:1-3, Genesis 21:17-21, and Genesis 16:11-12. As you read, note how each relates to Ishmael and Arabia.
The citations are from the Torah; notice the editorial note about the translation source.
2
Understand the geographical link
Juwayni highlights that Mount Paran (Faran) is in Arabia, where Ishmael settled. This links the prophecy of God coming from Paran to the Arab prophet.
3
See the replacement prophecy
The final citation from Deuteronomy 32:21 speaks of God replacing Israel with a ‘foolish nation’. Juwayni identifies this as the Arabs, specifically the sons of Ishmael.
He also quotes Jewish scholars who interpret ‘very great’ and ‘great nation’ as referring to Muhammad.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
أقبل الرب من سيناء وأشرق لهم من سعير وتجلى من جبل فاران راتى من ربي القدس وعن يمينه نار شريعة لهم انه أحب الشعب جميع قديسيه فى يدك ، وهم ساجدون عند قدمك يقتبسون من كلماتك » ( تثنیه ۲۳ : ۱-۳ ) وبين موسى أن اسماعيل سكن في جبل . فاران فقيد نادى ملاك الله هاجر : « وقال لها : مالك يا هاجر لا تخافي فان الله قد سمع صوت الغلام حيث هو . قومى فخذى الغلام ولتكن يدك معه . فانى جاعله أمة كبيرة وكشف الله عن عينيها فرات بئر ماء فمضت وملأت القربة ماء وسقت الغلام. وكان الله مع الفلام حتى كبر . فأقام بالبرية وكان راميا بالقوس وأقام ببرية فاران واتخذت له أمه امرأة من أرض مصر » ) تکوین ۲۱ : ۱۷ ۲۱ ) وبين أن فاران فى الأرض العربية مقابل سكنى بنى اسرائيل فيها . بنو اسرائيل فى الشمال وبنو اسماعيل فى الجنوب فقد نادى ملاك الله هاجر : « وقال لها ملاك الرب : ها أنت حامل وستلدين ابنا وتسمينه اسماعيل لأن الرب قد سمع صوت شقائك ويكون رجلا وحشيا يده على الكل ويد الكل عليه وامام جميع اخوته يسكن ( ) تكوين ۱٦ : ۱۱ - ۱۲ ) ٦- واكد موسى على زوال الملك والشريعة الى الابد من بنى اسرائيل فى يوم من الأيام بقوله على لسان الله تعالى . هم اغاروني بمن ليس الها وأغضبونى بأباطيلهم وأنا أغيرهم بمن ليسوا شعبا . يقوم غابياء أغضبهم ». ) تثنية ۲۲ : ۲۱ ) يقصد العرب بنو اسماعيل لأنهم في نظر اليهود : امة امية . ) نقل نصوص التوراة من ترجمة الآباء اليسوعيين في بيروت سنة ۱۹٦۸ م ) ويقول علماء من اليهود السامريين والعبرانيين أن ( جدا جدا ) وكذلك » أمة عظيمة » يشيران الى اسم محمد محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم وقد بينـا ذلك في تقديمنا لكتاب » اظهار الحق » للشيخ الإمام رحمت الله الهندي وفي كتب غيره
The Lord came from Sinai and dawned from Seir and shone forth from Mount Paran... (Deut 33:1-3). And Moses showed that Ishmael dwelt in Mount Paran... (Gen 21:17-21)... And he showed that Paran is in the Arabian land opposite the dwelling of the Children of Israel... So the angel of the Lord called to Hagar... (Gen 16:11-12). And Moses affirmed the removal of kingship and law from the Children of Israel forever... (Deut 32:21). He meant the Arabs, the sons of Ishmael, because in the eyes of the Jews they are an illiterate nation. (Translated from the Jesuit fathers in Beirut 1968). And some Samaritans and Hebrew scholars say that 'very great' and 'great nation' refer to the name Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace. And we have explained that in our introduction to the book Izhar al-Haqq by Shaykh Rahmatullah al-Hindi and in other books.
Source pages p. 86

Commentary
This passage is a proof from biblical scriptures that the Prophet Muhammad was foretold. The author quotes Deuteronomy and Genesis to show that the prophecy about a prophet from Mount Paran (Faran) refers to Ishmael's dwelling, and that Ishmael would become a great nation. He then links this to the Arabs and specifically to Muhammad. The author also notes that some Jewish scholars recognize this prophecy. The citation serves as evidence for the truth of Islam from earlier scriptures.

Key Terms
توراة
إسماعيل
فاران
محمد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some Muslims think the Bible contains no prophecies about Muhammad.
Correction: Juwayni shows that the Torah and Genesis explicitly mention a prophet from Arabia, identified with Ishmael and later Muhammad, and even Jewish scholars acknowledge this.

Checkpoint
Which biblical figure did Juwayni cite as residing in Paran?
A) Ishmael
B) Isaac
C) Moses
D) Abraham

Key Takeaway
Juwayni uses the Torah and Genesis to prove that the Prophet Muhammad was foretold by earlier scriptures.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever hesitated to share the truth of Islam with Jews or Christians because you felt your knowledge of their scriptures was insufficient? After seeing Juwayni’s bold use of the Bible, what is stopping you from studying their texts to find the same signs, and then speaking with confidence? Will you commit this week to learning one biblical prophecy about our Prophet?
?
If the Samaritans and Hebrew scholars of old could recognize that 'great nation' refers to Muhammad, why do you sometimes doubt the clarity of Allah’s signs when they come through unexpected sources? Where in your heart have you closed the door to guidance because it came from a place you did not expect? What will you do today to open that door?
?
The Jews considered the Arabs an 'illiterate nation' (أمة أمية) and yet Allah chose from them the final Prophet. How does this truth challenge your own pride in lineage, education, or status? Are you looking down on any community that Allah may have honored? What step will you take to purify your heart from such disdain?

Prophethood
beginner
4 min
Juwayni's Definition of Prophethood and Miracle as Proof
Juwayni defines prophethood and states that the miracle is its proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's definition of prophethood and why miracles serve as the primary proof of prophethood.

Core Idea
Prophethood is proven by miracles, not by reasoning alone.

Juwayni begins his discussion of prophethood by establishing what it is and how we know it is true. He defines prophethood and then immediately identifies the miracle as the sign that verifies a prophet's claim. This lesson lays the foundation for understanding why miracles are central to Islamic theology.

Guided Path
1
Understand Juwayni's definition
Read Juwayni's definition of prophethood. He does not elaborate much here, but note that it is the starting point.
Defining prophethood is necessary before discussing its proof.
2
Identify the proof of prophethood
Juwayni states that the miracle (mu'jizah) is the indication of the truth of prophethood. Understand that a miracle is not just any extraordinary event but one that challenges and incapacitates others.
This is a key concept in kalam.
3
Distinguish miracle from karamah
The passage mentions the difference between a miracle and a karamah. While not detailed here, note that a karamah is a grace given to saints, not a proof of prophethood.
This distinction prevents confusion.
4
Affirmation of Muhammad's prophethood
Juwayni affirms the prophethood of Muhammad (SAW). Understand that this is the practical application of the theoretical framework.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وتحدث الجويني عن النبوات ، وعرف النبوة ، ودلالة ثبوت النبوة وهى المعجزة ، والفرق بين المعجزة والكرامة . ثم أثبت نبوة سيدنا ومولانا محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم . وشروط المعجزة عنده ۱ - أن تكون فعلا الله تبارك وتعالى أو في معنى الفعل . ٢ - وأن تكون خارقة للعادة . ٣ - وأن يعجز النبى الخلائق عن معارضته . ٤ - وأن يدعى النبوة ثم تظهر المعجزة مع دعواه لها وتحديه الخلائق بها فتقع على حسب ايثاره في وقت اختياره مطابقة لدعواه و أن لا تظهر مكذبة له . وهل يشترط أن لا تكون المعجزة متقدمة على الدعوى ، بل مقارنة لها لأن التصديق قبل الدعوى لا يعقل أم لا يشترط ؟ لم يفصل الجويني كلام فى هذا الشرط كما فصل الكلام فيه صاحب المواقف فى علم الكلام وهو الامام عضد الله والدين القاضي عبد الرحمن بن أحمد الايجى المتوفى سنة ٧٥٦ هـ . يقول هذا الامام - رحمة الله تعالى عليه - : « لو قال أى نبى معجزتي ما قد ظهر على يدى من قبل أن أدعى النبوة لم يدل قوله هذا صدقه ، بل يطالبه الناس باعادة المعجزة وقت دعوة النبوة ، فلو
And Juwayni discussed prophethood, and defined prophethood, and the indication of the proof of prophethood which is the miracle, and the difference between a miracle and a karamah (grace). Then he affirmed the prophethood of our master and lord Muhammad, peace be upon him. The conditions of the miracle according to him are: 1. That it be an act of God Almighty or in the meaning of an act. 2. That it be a breach of custom. 3. That the prophet incapacitate the creatures from opposing it. 4. That he claim prophethood, then the miracle appears along with his claim and his challenge to the creatures with it, and it occurs according to his choice at the time of his choosing, matching his claim, and that it does not appear disproving him. And is it a condition that the miracle not precede the claim, but rather be concurrent with it, because affirmation before the claim is not conceivable, or is it not a condition? Juwayni did not elaborate on this condition as the author of Al-Mawaqif fi 'Ilm al-Kalam elaborated, who is Imam 'Adud al-Din al-Qadi 'Abd al-Rahman ibn Ahmad al-Iji, died 756 AH. This Imam says: 'If any prophet says, my miracle is what appeared at my hands before I claimed prophethood, this statement does not prove his truthfulness, but rather people would demand the repetition of the miracle at the time of the claim, for...'
Source pages p. 111

Commentary
This passage discusses Juwayni's treatment of prophethood. It states that Juwayni defined prophethood, that the proof of prophethood is the miracle, and he distinguished between a miracle (mu'jizah) and a grace (karamah). He affirmed the prophethood of Muhammad (SAW). The passage then lists four conditions for a miracle according to Juwayni: (1) it is a divine act, (2) it breaks natural custom, (3) the prophet challenges others to produce its like and they are unable, (4) the miracle must accompany the claim of prophethood, match the claim, and not be contradicted. The passage then raises a disputed condition: whether the miracle must be concurrent with the claim or can precede it. Juwayni did not elaborate, but al-Iji (author of Al-Mawaqif) argued that if a prophet says his miracle occurred before his claim, it does not prove his truthfulness; people would demand a new miracle at the time of the claim. The passage cuts off but the argument is clear.

Key Terms
النبوة
المعجزة
الكرامة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that any supernatural event proves prophethood.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies that only events that meet specific conditions (like challenging and incapacitating) are considered miracles; other extraordinary events may be karamahs.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the indication of the proof of prophethood?
A) Rational arguments
B) The miracle
C) The character of the prophet
D) Consensus of scholars

Key Takeaway
Juwayni teaches that the primary evidence for prophethood is the miracle, which must be a divine act that breaks custom and challenges others.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If the primary proof of prophethood is miracles, does your belief in Muhammad (SAW) rest on his miracles like the Quran and splitting of the moon? Or have you let doubts creep in because you haven't witnessed them yourself? Examine your heart: are you fully convinced that his prophethood is proven by these signs?
?
You believe that miracles are divine acts that break custom. Yet do you live as if the One who performed those miracles is still in control of your life? When you face difficulty, do you remember that the same God who sent miracles to confirm His prophets can also help you? Challenge yourself: what is one area of your life where you need to trust in the miraculous power of Allah today?
?
Juwayni distinguishes between miracles (proof for prophets) and karamat (graces for saints). Have you ever mistakenly elevated a saint or a spiritual leader's wonder to the level of prophetic proof? Reflect: is there anyone besides the prophets whose words or actions you treat as infallible or as evidence of truth? Repent from any misplaced reverence and recenter your faith on the clear proofs of prophethood.

Prophethood
beginner
5 min
The Four Conditions of a Miracle According to Juwayni
Juwayni lists four conditions a miracle must satisfy.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will be able to list and explain the four conditions Juwayni sets for a true miracle.

Core Idea
A miracle must be divine, custom-breaking, unchallengeable, and exactly matching the claim.

Juwayni does not leave the concept of a miracle vague; he specifies precise conditions that a true miracle must fulfill. These conditions ensure that a miracle is a genuine proof of prophethood and not just an extraordinary event. Let's examine each condition.

Guided Path
1
Read the list of conditions
Read the Arabic passage listing the four conditions. Notice that each condition is numbered.
This is a clear enumerative structure.
2
Understand condition one
The miracle must be an act of God or in the meaning of an act. This means it is directly caused by Allah.
3
Understand condition two
The miracle must break natural custom (khariq lil-'adah). It should be something that cannot happen normally.
4
Understand condition three
The prophet must challenge people to produce something similar, and they must be unable to do so.
This is the aspect of challenge (tahaddi).
5
Understand condition four
The miracle must appear at the time of the claim, match the claim, and not disprove the prophet.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وتحدث الجويني عن النبوات ، وعرف النبوة ، ودلالة ثبوت النبوة وهى المعجزة ، والفرق بين المعجزة والكرامة . ثم أثبت نبوة سيدنا ومولانا محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم . وشروط المعجزة عنده ۱ - أن تكون فعلا الله تبارك وتعالى أو في معنى الفعل . ٢ - وأن تكون خارقة للعادة . ٣ - وأن يعجز النبى الخلائق عن معارضته . ٤ - وأن يدعى النبوة ثم تظهر المعجزة مع دعواه لها وتحديه الخلائق بها فتقع على حسب ايثاره في وقت اختياره مطابقة لدعواه و أن لا تظهر مكذبة له . وهل يشترط أن لا تكون المعجزة متقدمة على الدعوى ، بل مقارنة لها لأن التصديق قبل الدعوى لا يعقل أم لا يشترط ؟ لم يفصل الجويني كلام فى هذا الشرط كما فصل الكلام فيه صاحب المواقف فى علم الكلام وهو الامام عضد الله والدين القاضي عبد الرحمن بن أحمد الايجى المتوفى سنة ٧٥٦ هـ . يقول هذا الامام - رحمة الله تعالى عليه - : « لو قال أى نبى معجزتي ما قد ظهر على يدى من قبل أن أدعى النبوة لم يدل قوله هذا صدقه ، بل يطالبه الناس باعادة المعجزة وقت دعوة النبوة ، فلو

Key Terms

خارقة للعادة
تحدي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think any amazing event can be a miracle even if no challenge is issued.
Correction: Juwayni requires that the prophet issue a challenge (tahaddi) and that others be unable to match it.

Which condition ensures that the miracle is not a natural event?
A) It must be an act of God
B) It must be a breach of custom
C) It must involve a challenge
D) It must match the claim

Juwayni's four conditions provide a clear framework to identify a true miracle, preventing confusion with mere marvels or magic.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have learned that a miracle must match the prophet's claim exactly. Have you ever demanded a sign from Allah before believing, or do you accept the miracles already given? What would it take for you to truly submit without a personal miracle?
?
Juwayni insists the miracle must be an act of God, not of the prophet. Do you sometimes attribute your own successes or 'miracles' in life to yourself rather than to Allah? How can you shift your gratitude to the true source?
?
The condition of 'inimitability' means no one can rival the prophet's miracle. In your daily life, do you try to rival or compete with Allah's power by relying on your own abilities? Where do you need to admit your utter incapacity?

Prophethood

The Timing of the Miracle: Must It Be Concurrent with the Claim?
Juwayni raises the condition of miracle timing and quotes al-Iji's argument.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the scholarly dispute over whether a miracle must be concurrent with the prophetic claim or can precede it.

Core Idea
A miracle that precedes the claim may not prove truthfulness; people would demand a new miracle at the time of the claim.

After listing the four conditions, Juwayni raises an additional issue: must the miracle occur exactly at the time of the claim? He does not settle the matter himself, but he quotes al-Iji's argument that a preceding miracle is insufficient proof. This lesson explores the reasoning behind this condition.

Guided Path
1
Read the disputed condition
Read the passage where Juwayni asks whether the miracle must be concurrent with the claim. Understand the question.
Juwayni mentions this as an additional condition not agreed upon.
2
Understand al-Iji's argument
Al-Iji argues that if a prophet says 'my miracle is what happened before I claimed prophethood', this does not prove his truthfulness because people would require a miracle at the time of the claim.
The reason is that the miracle must directly support the claim, not be a separate event.
3
Consider the implication
Think about why concurrency matters: it links the miracle directly to the claim, making it a clear sign.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وتحدث الجويني عن النبوات ، وعرف النبوة ، ودلالة ثبوت النبوة وهى المعجزة ، والفرق بين المعجزة والكرامة . ثم أثبت نبوة سيدنا ومولانا محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم . وشروط المعجزة عنده ۱ - أن تكون فعلا الله تبارك وتعالى أو في معنى الفعل . ٢ - وأن تكون خارقة للعادة . ٣ - وأن يعجز النبى الخلائق عن معارضته . ٤ - وأن يدعى النبوة ثم تظهر المعجزة مع دعواه لها وتحديه الخلائق بها فتقع على حسب ايثاره في وقت اختياره مطابقة لدعواه و أن لا تظهر مكذبة له . وهل يشترط أن لا تكون المعجزة متقدمة على الدعوى ، بل مقارنة لها لأن التصديق قبل الدعوى لا يعقل أم لا يشترط ؟ لم يفصل الجويني كلام فى هذا الشرط كما فصل الكلام فيه صاحب المواقف فى علم الكلام وهو الامام عضد الله والدين القاضي عبد الرحمن بن أحمد الايجى المتوفى سنة ٧٥٦ هـ . يقول هذا الامام - رحمة الله تعالى عليه - : « لو قال أى نبى معجزتي ما قد ظهر على يدى من قبل أن أدعى النبوة لم يدل قوله هذا صدقه ، بل يطالبه الناس باعادة المعجزة وقت دعوة النبوة ، فلو
And Juwayni discussed prophethood, and defined prophethood, and the indication of the proof of prophethood which is the miracle, and the difference between a miracle and a karamah (grace). Then he affirmed the prophethood of our master and lord Muhammad, peace be upon him. The conditions of the miracle according to him are: 1. That it be an act of God Almighty or in the meaning of an act. 2. That it be a breach of custom. 3. That the prophet incapacitate the creatures from opposing it. 4. That he claim prophethood, then the miracle appears along with his claim and his challenge to the creatures with it, and it occurs according to his choice at the time of his choosing, matching his claim, and that it does not appear disproving him. And is it a condition that the miracle not precede the claim, but rather be concurrent with it, because affirmation before the claim is not conceivable, or is it not a condition? Juwayni did not elaborate on this condition as the author of Al-Mawaqif fi 'Ilm al-Kalam elaborated, who is Imam 'Adud al-Din al-Qadi 'Abd al-Rahman ibn Ahmad al-Iji, died 756 AH. This Imam says: 'If any prophet says, my miracle is what appeared at my hands before I claimed prophethood, this statement does not prove his truthfulness, but rather people would demand the repetition of the miracle at the time of the claim, for...'
Source pages p. 111

Commentary
This passage discusses Juwayni's treatment of prophethood. It states that Juwayni defined prophethood, that the proof of prophethood is the miracle, and he distinguished between a miracle (mu'jizah) and a grace (karamah). He affirmed the prophethood of Muhammad (SAW). The passage then lists four conditions for a miracle according to Juwayni: (1) it is a divine act, (2) it breaks natural custom, (3) the prophet challenges others to produce its like and they are unable, (4) the miracle must accompany the claim of prophethood, match the claim, and not be contradicted. The passage then raises a disputed condition: whether the miracle must be concurrent with the claim or can precede it. Juwayni did not elaborate, but al-Iji (author of Al-Mawaqif) argued that if a prophet says his miracle occurred before his claim, it does not prove his truthfulness; people would demand a new miracle at the time of the claim. The passage cuts off but the argument is clear.

Key Terms
المعجزة
الدعوى
التصديق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that any miracle that occurred before the claim can still be cited as proof.
Correction: Al-Iji argues that if the miracle precedes the claim, people would demand a concurrent miracle, so a preceding miracle is insufficient.

Checkpoint
According to al-Iji, if a prophet says his miracle occurred before his claim, what would people demand?
A) Accept it as valid
B) Repetition of the miracle
C) A different sign
D) Proof from reason

Key Takeaway
The concurrent timing of a miracle with the prophetic claim is a debated condition, with al-Iji arguing that earlier miracles do not suffice as proof.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you hear a claim about God or faith, do you require present, living evidence, or do you rely solely on past events recorded in history? How does your answer affect your relationship with God today?
?
Are there areas of your life where you are like the people demanding a 'new miracle'—waiting for a sign before you fully commit? What would it take for you to act on what you already know?
?
If a miracle from your past (e.g., a answered prayer) is not enough to sustain your faith, what would it take for you to trust God in the present moment?

Conditions of Miracles
Juwayni enumerates four conditions for a miracle.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the four conditions that a true miracle must meet according to Imam al-Juwayni.

Core Idea
A true miracle must be from Allah, break natural laws, be inimitable, and accompany the claim.

Imam al-Juwayni lays out the essential criteria for what constitutes a genuine miracle. These conditions help distinguish true prophetic miracles from other extraordinary events, such as karamas.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once to get a sense of Juwayni's structure. Then read the translation to understand the conditions.
Notice the numbered list format.
2
Identify each condition
List the four conditions in order. Reflect on why each is necessary for a genuine miracle.
Condition 4 mentions concurrency with the claim.
3
Reflect on the purpose
Consider how these conditions protect the integrity of prophethood and prevent confusion with karamas.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وتحدث الجويني عن النبوات ، وعرف النبوة ، ودلالة ثبوت النبوة وهى المعجزة ، والفرق بين المعجزة والكرامة . ثم أثبت نبوة نبينا ومولانا محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم . وشروط المعجزة عنده
۱ - أن تكون فعلا الله تبارك وتعالى أو في معنى الفعل .
۲ - وأن تكون خارقة للعادة .
۳ - وأن يعجز النبى الخلائق عن معارضته .
٤ - وأن يدعى النبوة ثم تظهر المعجزة مع دعواه لها وتحديه الخلائق بها فتقع على حسب ايثاره في وقت اختياره مطابقة لدعواه و أن لا تظهر مكذبة له .
وهل يشترط أن لا تكون المعجزة متقدمة على الدعوى ، بل مقارنة : ها لأن التصديق قبل الدعوى لا يعقل أم لا يشترط ؟ لم يفصل الجويني كلام فى هذا الشرط كما فصل الكلام فيه صاحب ) المواقف فى علم الكلام ) وهو الامام عضد الله والدين القاضي عبد الرحمن بن أحمد الايجى المتوفى سنة ٧٥٦ هـ . يقول هذا الامام - رحمة الله تعالى عليه رحمة الله تعالى عليه - : « لو قال أى نبى معجزتي ما قد ظهر على يدى من قبل أن أدعى النبوة لم يدل قوله هذا صدقه ، بل بل يطالبه الناس باعادة المعجزة وقت دعوة النبوة ، فلو عجز كان كاذبا قطعا • فان قيل : فما تقولون فى كلام عيسى فى المهد (۱) ، وتساقط الرطب الجنى عليه من النخلة اليابسة (۲) ؟ قلنا : انما هى كرامات وظهورها على الأولياء جائز ، والأنبياء قبل نبوتهم لا يقصرون عن درجة الأولياء » .
(۱) كرامة لأمه مريم
(۲) كرامة لأمه مريم: والكرامة جائزة للصالحين من الاحياء دلالة على قرب الله منهم وهي ممتنعة من الاموات
Al-Juwayni discussed prophethood (al-nubuwwat), defined prophethood (al-nubuwwa), and the proof of its establishment is the miracle (al-mu'jiza), and the difference between the miracle and the karama. Then he affirmed the prophethood of our Prophet and Master Muhammad (peace be upon him). The conditions of the miracle according to him:
1. That it is an act of Allah (subhanahu wa ta'ala) or in the meaning of an act.
2. That it is a breaking of the normal course of events (kharq al-'ada).
3. That the prophet (al-nabi) renders the creation incapable of opposing it.
4. That he claims prophethood (al-nubuwwa), then the miracle appears with his claim and his challenging the creation, occurring according to his choice at the time of his choosing, matching his claim, and that it does not appear as debunking him.
And is it a condition that the miracle should not precede the claim, but rather be concurrent with it, because confirmation before the claim is not rational, or is it not a condition? Al-Juwayni did not elaborate on this condition as was elaborated by the author of al-Mawaqif fi 'ilm al-kalam, who is Imam 'Adud al-Din al-Qadi 'Abd al-Rahman ibn Ahmad al-Iji, d. 756 AH. This Imam says: 'If a prophet says: "My miracle is what appeared at my hands before I claimed prophethood," this statement does not indicate his truthfulness. Rather, people would demand him to repeat the miracle at the time of his claim of prophethood. If he fails, he is definitively a liar. If it is said: What do you say about the speech of Jesus in the cradle (1) and the falling of fresh ripe dates upon him from the dry palm tree (2)? We say: These are merely karamas (favors) and their manifestation upon saints is permissible, and the prophets before their prophethood are not inferior to saints.'
(1) A karama for his mother Mary.
(2) A karama for his mother Mary: and the karama is permissible for the righteous among the living as an indication of their closeness to Allah, and it is impossible for the dead.
Source pages p. 111

Commentary
This passage provides a definition and conditions for prophethood and miracles. Al-Juwayni first states that he discussed prophethood, defined it, and identified the miracle as the proof of prophethood, distinguishing miracles from karamas. He then affirms the prophethood of Muhammad. The conditions of a miracle are enumerated: it must be an act of Allah or similar to an act; it must break the normal course of events; the prophet must challenge and render others unable to produce its like; and the miracle must appear matching the claim at the time of claim, not debunking him. Then a debated condition is raised: must the miracle be concurrent with the claim of prophethood, or can it precede? Al-Juwayni did not elaborate, but a quote from al-Iji's al-Mawaqif explains that a miracle preceding the claim is not proof; people would demand a repetition. The example of Jesus speaking in the cradle and the dry palm tree providing dates is raised. These are considered karamas for Mary, not miracles proving Jesus' prophethood before his claim, since prophets before prophethood are not inferior to saints. Two footnotes clarify that these are karamas for Mary, and karama is permissible for the living righteous as a sign of nearness to Allah, but impossible for the dead.

Key Terms
المعجزة
خارقة للعادة
التحدي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, which condition requires that the miracle appears at the time of the claim?
A) Condition 1
B) Condition 2
C) Condition 3
D) Condition 4

Key Takeaway
A true miracle must be from Allah, break natural law, be inimitable, and occur with the prophetic claim.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever been tempted to attribute a miraculous event to someone claiming prophethood today? How do Juwayni's four conditions help you evaluate such claims? Will you commit to applying these conditions before accepting any miraculous claim?
?
Juwayni insists that a miracle must accompany the claim of prophethood. When you recite the Quran, do you personally experience it as a miracle that meets the condition of inimitability? If not, what is blocking your heart from recognizing it as the ultimate proof?
?
The condition that a miracle must be an act of Allah challenges our tendency to attribute wonders to human power. In your own life, when you witness something extraordinary, do you immediately think of Allah's power, or do you look for natural explanations? What would it mean for you to live with the conviction that miracles are from Allah alone?

The Concurrency Debate: Must Miracles Coincide with the Claim?
Al-Iji argues that miracles must be concurrent with the claim, and explains karamas.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the scholarly debate on whether a miracle must be concurrent with the claim of prophethood, and how karamas are distinguished.

Core Idea
A miracle before the claim is not proof; it is a karama.

Al-Juwayni mentions a debated condition: must the miracle appear at the same time as the claim? He does not elaborate, but al-Iji provides a detailed argument on why miracles must be concurrent, using examples from the life of Jesus to illustrate the distinction between miracles and karamas.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on concurrency
Read the Arabic passage and translation to understand the issue. Pay attention to the objection and answer.
The passage includes a quote from al-Iji.
2
Understand al-Iji's argument
Notice that al-Iji argues that a pre-claim miracle does not prove truthfulness; people would demand a repetition. This shows the necessity of concurrency.
3
Recognize the karama distinction
The examples of Jesus are reinterpreted as karamas for Mary, not miracles for Jesus. Understand why that distinction matters.
Prophets before their prophethood are not inferior to saints, so karamas are possible.

?

?

?

The Inimitability of the Qur'an and Sensory Miracles
Juwayni enumerates the miracles of Muhammad and explains the theory of sarfa.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the two categories of miracles (i'jaz al-Quran and sensory miracles) and the theory of sarfa regarding the Qur'an's inimitability.

Core Idea
Muhammad's prophethood is proven by the Qur'an's inimitability and witnessed miracles.

Al-Juwayni begins his proof for the prophethood of Muhammad by presenting the miracles (mu'jizat) granted to him. These are divided into two types: the inimitability of the Qur'an (i'jaz) and sensory miracles such as the splitting of the moon. He then explains that the inimitability of the Qur'an is according to the theory of sarfa—Allah prevented the Arabs from matching it despite their ability. This section establishes the foundational evidence for Muhammad's prophethood.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic passage and its translation to get the overall argument.
Notice the two types of miracles.
2
Identify the two miracle types
List the two categories of miracles mentioned: i'jaz al-Quran and sensory miracles.
What are the examples given?
3
Understand sarfa
Focus on the definition of sarfa: the Arabs were capable but Allah deterred them. This is a key theory in i'jaz.
Why would Allah deter them?
4
Connect to prophethood
Realize that these miracles serve as direct proof that Muhammad is a true prophet, as they are beyond human capacity.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويثبت الجويني نبوة نبينا محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم بمعجزاته وهي :
١ - إعجاز القرآن
٢ - والمعجزات الحسية كشق القمر ومكالمة الذئب للنبي صلى الله عليه وسلم ونبع الماء من بين أصابعه ... الخ .
ويقول أن إعجاز القرآن بالصرفة أي كان العرب قادرين على الإتيان بمثل القرآن ولكن الله صرفهم وصاحب ) المواقف فى علم الكلام ) يذكر أن إثبات نبوة نبينا محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم في ما يلى :
١ - إعجاز القرآن والمعجزات الحسية
٢ - الإستدلال بأحوال النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم قبل النبوة ، وحال الدعوة ، وبعد تمامها ، وأخلاقه العظيمة ، وأحكامه الحكيمة ، واقدامه حيث يحجم الأبطال .. ولولا ثقته بعصمة الله إياه من الناس لامتنع ذلك عادة ، وأنه لم يتلون حاله ، وقد تلونت به الأحوال من أمور من تتبعها علم أن كل واحد منها - وإن كان لا يدل على نبوته لكن مجموعها - مما لا يحصل إلا للأنبياء ، فلا يرد ما يحكى من أفاضل الحكماء من الأخلاق العجيبة التي جعلها الناس قدوة لأحوالهم في الدنيا والآخرة.
Al-Juwayni affirms the prophethood of our Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) by his miracles, which are:
1. The inimitability of the Qur'an (i'jaz al-Qur'an)
2. And sensory miracles such as the splitting of the moon, the wolf speaking to the Prophet (peace be upon him), the gushing of water from between his fingers, etc.
And he says that the inimitability of the Qur'an is by way of sarfa (deterrence), meaning that the Arabs were capable of producing something like the Qur'an, but Allah deterred them. And the author of al-Mawaqif fi 'ilm al-kalam mentions that the affirmation of the prophethood of our Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) is as follows:
1. The inimitability of the Qur'an and sensory miracles.
2. Reasoning from the state of the Prophet (peace be upon him) before prophethood, during the call, and after its completion, his great character, his wise rulings, and his boldness where heroes hesitate. And were it not for his confidence in Allah's protection of him from people, that would normally be impossible. And his condition did not change, while conditions changed around him. From following these matters, one knows that each one of them – even if it does not individually indicate his prophethood – their combination is such that it is not attained except by prophets. So no objection stands from the tales of virtuous philosophers about wonderful qualities that people have taken as an example for their states in this world and the Hereafter.
Source pages p. 112

Commentary
This passage presents Juwayni's evidence for the prophethood of Muhammad. It lists two types of miracles: the inimitability of the Quran (i'jaz) and sensory miracles. For the Quran, Juwayni adopts the theory of sarfa, which holds that the Arabs were capable of producing similar literature but Allah prevented them. Then a quote from al-Mawaqif adds a second category of proof: reasoning from the Prophet's life—his state before prophethood, during the call, and after; his character, wisdom, and courage; his unwavering consistency despite changing circumstances; and the cumulative weight of these signs. Even if each sign alone does not prove prophethood, their combination is unique to prophets. This refutes objections based on the extraordinary qualities of virtuous philosophers, which are not equivalent.

Key Terms
إعجاز القرآن
الصرفة
المعجزات الحسية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the Qur'an is inimitable because Arabs were simply unable to produce its like.
Correction: The sarfa theory holds they were able but Allah prevented them.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the theory of sarfa regarding the inimitability of the Qur'an?
A) The Qur'an is inimitable because Arabs were incapable of producing similar literature
B) The Qur'an is inimitable because Allah prevented the Arabs from producing similar literature despite their ability
C) The Qur'an is a sensory miracle
D) The Qur'an is a proof only through its content

Key Takeaway
Muhammad's prophethood is proven by the Qur'an's inimitability (through sarfa) and sensory miracles.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The wolf spoke to the Prophet. If you had heard that story from a stranger, would you believe it? What does your reaction reveal about your trust in revelation versus rational skepticism?
?
Al-Juwayni says the Arabs were capable of imitating the Qur'an but Allah deterred them. When you read the Qur'an, do you feel the awe of its inimitability, or has familiarity dulled its miracle? What one change can you make today to restore that wonder?
?
You claim to believe that Muhammad is a prophet based on these miracles. But does that belief translate into action? In your daily life, are you living as if the One who split the moon is watching you? If not, what is holding you back?

Cumulative Evidence from the Prophet's Life
Juwayni presents the cumulative argument from the Prophet's life as a proof of prophethood.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the cumulative signs from the Prophet's life—his character, wisdom, courage, and consistency—collectively prove his prophethood beyond individual miracles.

Core Idea
No single sign alone proves prophethood, but their combination is unique to prophets.

After listing the miracles, Juwayni (citing al-Mawaqif) adds a second category of proof: reasoning from the comprehensive state of the Prophet (sallallahu alayhi wasallam). This includes his life before prophethood, during his call, and after, his great character, wise rulings, unmatched courage, and unwavering consistency. Even if each point alone is not conclusive, their cumulative weight points decisively to prophethood. This refutes objections based on the extraordinary qualities of philosophers.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic and translation to see the list of evidences from the Prophet's life.
Notice the structure: before, during, after prophethood.
2
Identify the components
List the elements: his state before prophethood, during the call, after completion, his character, rulings, courage, and consistency.
Why is consistency important?
3
Understand the cumulative argument
Realize that each sign alone might not be sufficient, but their combination is unique to prophets. This is the key insight.
Compare to the philosophers' qualities.
4
Respond to objections
The passage preemptively refutes objections from virtuous philosophers by stating that their qualities are not equivalent to the prophetic combination.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويثبت الجويني نبوة نبينا محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم بمعجزاته وهي :
١ - إعجاز القرآن
٢ - والمعجزات الحسية كشق القمر ومكالمة الذئب للنبي صلى الله عليه وسلم ونبع الماء من بين أصابعه ... الخ .
ويقول أن إعجاز القرآن بالصرفة أي كان العرب قادرين على الإتيان بمثل القرآن ولكن الله صرفهم وصاحب ) المواقف فى علم الكلام ) يذكر أن إثبات نبوة نبينا محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم في ما يلى :
١ - إعجاز القرآن والمعجزات الحسية
٢ - الإستدلال بأحوال النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم قبل النبوة ، وحال الدعوة ، وبعد تمامها ، وأخلاقه العظيمة ، وأحكامه الحكيمة ، واقدامه حيث يحجم الأبطال .. ولولا ثقته بعصمة الله إياه من الناس لامتنع ذلك عادة ، وأنه لم يتلون حاله ، وقد تلونت به الأحوال من أمور من تتبعها علم أن كل واحد منها - وإن كان لا يدل على نبوته لكن مجموعها - مما لا يحصل إلا للأنبياء ، فلا يرد ما يحكى من أفاضل الحكماء من الأخلاق العجيبة التي جعلها الناس قدوة لأحوالهم في الدنيا والآخرة.
Al-Juwayni affirms the prophethood of our Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) by his miracles, which are:
1. The inimitability of the Qur'an (i'jaz al-Qur'an)
2. And sensory miracles such as the splitting of the moon, the wolf speaking to the Prophet (peace be upon him), the gushing of water from between his fingers, etc.
And he says that the inimitability of the Qur'an is by way of sarfa (deterrence), meaning that the Arabs were capable of producing something like the Qur'an, but Allah deterred them. And the author of al-Mawaqif fi 'ilm al-kalam mentions that the affirmation of the prophethood of our Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) is as follows:
1. The inimitability of the Qur'an and sensory miracles.
2. Reasoning from the state of the Prophet (peace be upon him) before prophethood, during the call, and after its completion, his great character, his wise rulings, and his boldness where heroes hesitate. And were it not for his confidence in Allah's protection of him from people, that would normally be impossible. And his condition did not change, while conditions changed around him. From following these matters, one knows that each one of them – even if it does not individually indicate his prophethood – their combination is such that it is not attained except by prophets. So no objection stands from the tales of virtuous philosophers about wonderful qualities that people have taken as an example for their states in this world and the Hereafter.
Source pages p. 112

Commentary
This passage presents Juwayni's evidence for the prophethood of Muhammad. It lists two types of miracles: the inimitability of the Quran (i'jaz) and sensory miracles. For the Quran, Juwayni adopts the theory of sarfa, which holds that the Arabs were capable of producing similar literature but Allah prevented them. Then a quote from al-Mawaqif adds a second category of proof: reasoning from the Prophet's life—his state before prophethood, during the call, and after; his character, wisdom, and courage; his unwavering consistency despite changing circumstances; and the cumulative weight of these signs. Even if each sign alone does not prove prophethood, their combination is unique to prophets. This refutes objections based on the extraordinary qualities of virtuous philosophers, which are not equivalent.

Key Terms
الاستدلال بأحوال النبي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to the passage, why does the cumulative evidence from the Prophet's life prove prophethood?
A) Each individual sign is sufficient by itself
B) Their combination is unique to prophets
C) Philosophers have similar qualities
D) The Prophet's character alone is enough

Key Takeaway
The cumulative signs from the Prophet's life—his character, courage, and consistency—collectively prove his prophethood in a way that no single sign alone could.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
What single aspect of your own life would you be most afraid to have examined cumulatively, and why does the Prophet's consistency under similar scrutiny leave you without excuse?
?
If the combination of signs in the Prophet's life is unique to truth, what does the absence of such a combination in your own claims about faith say about you?
?
When you look at your own character under different circumstances—failure and success—do you find the same steadfastness the Prophet had? If not, what will you change today?
