Definition of Karamat and Their Non-Contradiction with Prophethood
Juwayni defines karamat and argues they do not contradict prophethood.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's definition of karamat as acts of Allah and how they do not contradict the uniqueness of prophetic miracles.

Core Idea
Miracles of saints (karamat) do not threaten prophethood; only claimed prophetic miracles do.

Juwayni addresses a contentious issue: the possibility of miracles performed by saints (karamat). He grounds his argument in the principle that extraordinary events are acts of Allah, not humans, and that prophetic miracles are distinguished by their context. This lesson unpacks his reasoning and its implications for the integrity of prophethood.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Let Juwayni's own words set the stage before analysis.
Notice the concise style; each sentence carries weight.
2
Identify the core definition
Juwayni states that extraordinary phenomena (khawariq al-‘adat) are not from human action but from Allah. This is the foundation.
This distinguishes karamat from human feats.
3
Understand the key distinction
A prophetic miracle (mu‘jiza) indicates prophethood only when it occurs in support of a claim. Without such a claim, miracles can happen freely.
This is the crucial insight that allows karamat.
4
Examine the Quranic example
Juwayni cites Mary's miracles, which occurred before Jesus' prophethood, showing they were karamat, not mu‘jizat.
This solidifies the argument with scriptural evidence.
5
Reflect on the overall argument
Juwayni concludes that karamat do not invalidate prophethood; they are simply acts of Allah's power.
The argument preserves both the integrity of prophethood and the possibility of saintly miracles.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فصل في الكرامات: بقلعة في أنه قد كثر خبط الناس في إثباتها ونفيها، وقد ألفت في إثباتها، والرد على الشكرية، كتابا. وأنا أذكر الآن لبابه، في أسطر إن شاء الله جل وعزة فأقول: خوارق العادات ليست من فعل العباد، وإنما هي من فعل الله تعالى وتقدس، فمن فطر السموات والأرض، وسيطوي السماء، ويسدل الأرض غير الأرض ويسير الجبال، ويفجر البحار، وينشر الموتى، قادر على أن يأتي ببديعة، وليس في فرض الإتيان بها قدح في النبوات. فإذا ذكرنا أنها: أن المعجزة لا تدل بعينها، وإنما تدل من حيث تقع على وفق الدعوى في النبوة: فإذا لم تقع دعوى النبوة أوقع الله ما يشاء، مما يعتاد، ومما لا يعتاد، فليس في تجويز الكرامات قدح في النبوات، إذا وقعت الإحاطة بوجه دلالة المعجزة على ما سبق وما جاء في قدرة الله سبحانه. ولهم ينخرم به الإعجاز. وقد نطق القرآن وتواترت به الآثار فلا يجحده إلا مرتاب. فأما ما أتى به القرآن: فمنها ما دل على مريم عليها السلام من بدائع الآيات ويستحيل أن تقدر معجزة لعيسى عليه السلام، فإنها جرت قبل كونه والمعجزات لا تتقدم على ثبوت النبوة. ولو ذهبت أنقل ما صح من الأخبار والآثار فيها، لجاوزت موضوع المعتقد وحده.
Chapter on Karamat (miracles of saints): It is well known that people have differed greatly in affirming and denying them, and I have written a book affirming them and refuting the [؟] (al-Shakriyya). Now I mention their essence in a few lines, if Allah, the Mighty and Exalted, wills. I say: Extraordinary phenomena are not from the actions of servants, but rather from the action of Allah, the Exalted and Holy. So He who created the heavens and the earth, will roll up the sky, spread out the earth differently, move the mountains, cause the seas to burst forth, and raise the dead, is able to bring about a wondrous thing, and there is no disparagement of prophethood in assuming His bringing them about. For we have mentioned that the miracle (mu'jiza) does not indicate by itself, but rather indicates insofar as it occurs in accordance with the claim of prophethood. So if there is no claim of prophethood, Allah brings about whatever He wills, whether customary or not, so there is no disparagement of prophethood in allowing karamat, when one comprehends the aspect of the miracle’s indication as previously mentioned and what has come in the power of Allah, the Exalted. [؟] And the inimitability of the Quran would be invalidated? The Quran has spoken and reports have been transmitted successively; none denies it except a doubter. As for what the Quran has brought: among them are what indicate about Mary, peace be upon her, of wondrous signs, and it is impossible to consider a miracle for Jesus, peace be upon him, for they occurred before his existence, and miracles do not precede the establishment of prophethood. If I were to transmit what has been established of reports and traditions concerning them, I would exceed the subject of belief alone.
Source pages p. 69

Commentary
This passage is a definition and proof passage. Juwayni opens a chapter on karamat (miracles of saints), noting that people have disagreed extensively. He has written a separate book on the topic. Now he gives the core principle: extraordinary phenomena (khawariq al-‘adat) are not acts of servants but acts of Allah. He then argues from Allah’s power: the Creator of the universe is certainly able to bring about extraordinary events, and doing so does not undermine prophethood. The key point is that a prophetic miracle (mu‘jiza) does not indicate prophethood by its mere occurrence; it indicates it only when it occurs in support of a claim to prophethood. Therefore, when no such claim is made, miraculous events (karamat) can occur without harming the concept of prophethood. He then cites Quranic examples, such as the miracles associated with Mary before the birth of Jesus, which could not be prophetic miracles because they preceded his prophethood. This solidifies the distinction. He ends by noting that he could narrate many traditions but that would exceed the scope of his creedal work.

Key Terms
كرامات
معجزات
خوارق العادات
نبوات
إعجاز

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Karamat (miracles of saints) contradict the uniqueness of prophetic miracles and thus undermine prophethood.
Correction: Juwayni argues that karamat do not contradict prophethood because prophetic miracles only indicate prophethood when they accompany a claim of prophethood; without such a claim, karamat can occur without harming prophethood.

Checkpoint
Why do karamat (miracles of saints) not contradict prophethood according to Juwayni?
A) Because they are performed by saints themselves
B) Because miracles only indicate prophethood when they accompany a claim of prophethood
C) Because karamat are weaker than mu'jizat
D) Because karamat only occur for prophets

Key Takeaway
Juwayni establishes that karamat are acts of Allah that do not undermine prophethood, as prophetic miracles derive their significance from accompanying a claim to prophethood.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever attribute extraordinary events in your life to your own worth, rather than recognizing them as pure acts of Allah? How can you shift your perspective to see all wonders as from Him alone?
?
Have you ever been tempted to claim a special status for yourself because of some spiritual experience? Juwayni reminds us that without a claim of prophethood, such experiences are simply favors from Allah—does your heart take pride in them? Will you now repent and attribute all to Allah?
?
When you witness something unexplainable, do you immediately assume it must be a sign of prophethood or saintly intervention? Juwayni teaches that the context matters: is there a claim? This lesson calls you to examine your assumptions: are you too quick to assign supernatural meanings? Can you trust Allah's power without needing such signs?

Distinguishing Karamat from Miracles: Juwayni's Response to an Objection
Juwayni answers the objection that karamat might accompany a false claim.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's argument that karamat do not undermine prophetic miracles because miracles are defined by their connection to a true claim of prophethood, while other extraordinary events are either saintly karamat or divine trials.

Core Idea
Karamat don't challenge miracles; only miracles confirm true prophethood when they match the claim.

Juwayni was a master of theological arguments. In this passage, he tackles a clever objection: if saints can perform karamat, how do we know that miracles are exclusive to prophets? He responds by clarifying the nature of miracles and showing that extraordinary events are not all equal. As you read, pay attention to how he distinguishes the role of a claim in defining a miracle.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection and the flawed answer
First, understand the objection: can karamat appear alongside a claim? Juwayni presents a flawed answer that says karamat occur without intent, distinguishing them from miracles. Then he refutes this.
2
Juwayni's correct principle
Juwayni explains that a miracle (mu'jiza) does not indicate prophethood by being connected to any absolute claim, but only when it occurs in accordance with a claim to prophethood. This is the key.
Contrast with the flawed view that they differ by willful intent.
3
The court analogy
Consider the analogy of a person in a king's court. If someone claims closeness to the king and the king acts accordingly, it confirms the claim. Similarly, miracles confirm prophethood when God supports the claim.
4
Answering the test case of false claimants
Anticipation of an objection: what about false prophets and the Dajjal? Juwayni explains these are trials (fitan), not genuine signs of truth. They do not break the principle because miracles only indicate truth for true claimants.
Important for defending the uniqueness of prophetic miracles.
5
Grasp the overall conclusion
Juwayni concludes that whoever understands this principle can answer all related objections. Reflect on how this argument safeguards the role of miracles in proving prophethood.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فان قيل: أيجوز ظهور الكرامات مع دعوى ممن تظهر عليه؟ قلنا: ذهب بعض مجوزي الكرامات: أنها تظهر من غير إيثار واختيار وزعم أنها بهذا الوجه تتميز عن المعجزات. وهذا قول من لم يحط بحقيقة الإعجاز. فإن المعجزة لا تدل من حيث تتعلق بالدعوى المطلقة المرسلة وإنما تدل على النبوة، من حيث تقع على وفق دعوى النبوة. فإن تعلق خارق عادة، بدعوى أخرى، دل على صدق تلك الدعوى. واذا استشهد من قام في المجلس المشهود الذي صوره ياه. وقال أيضا الملك: إني من المقربين عندك، والمختصين في مجلسك: فإن كنت كذلك ذلك، فقم واقعد ففعل الملك ذلك. دل على تصديقه. ثم ما يجرى من لا يدل على أن مثل هذا، لو جرى متعلقا بدعوى الرسالة، لم يدل على صدق مدعيها. نعم. لست أنكر أن سنة الله تبارك وتعالى إظهار الكرامات في الأغلب، من غير إيثار واختيار. والذي ذكرناه في التجويز، لا في الإخبار عما تجري به سنة الله جلت قدرته، ولا على القاعدة الممهد: أن يظهر الله فتنة على أيدي مدعي الربوبية من العباد، كما ورد في الأقاصيص: من إجراء الله النيل مع فرعون حيث دار. وكما ورد في الأخبار مما سيجري الله من الفتن، وخوارق الموائد على المسيح الدجال، وليست هذه الأشياء خارقة للمعجزة: فإنا كررنا مرارا: أن المعجزة لا تدل لعينها. وإنما تدل النبوة، وليس مع من يدعي الإلهية لهية، طلب تصديق فقال: إذا وافق ما جاء به دلت على التصديق من الله وكان هذا نازلا منزلة قوله صدقت. ومن أحاط بما ذكرناه، هان عليه درك الجواب عن كل ما يرد عليه مما سواه، وبالله التوفيق.
If it is said: Is it permissible for karamat to appear with a claim from the one upon whom they appear? We say: Some of those who allow karamat have held that they appear without intent and choice, and they claim that in this respect they are distinguished from miracles. But this is the statement of one who has not encompassed the reality of inimitability (i‘jāz). For the miracle does not indicate by being connected to an absolute, unqualified claim; rather, it indicates prophethood by occurring in accordance with the claim of prophethood. So if an extraordinary phenomenon is connected to another claim, it indicates the truth of that claim. For example, if a person standing in a witnessed assembly says to the king: 'I am among those close to you and the ones especially selected in your council,' and then he says, 'If you are indeed that, then stand and sit,' and the king does that, this indicates his confirmation. Then what occurs from someone who does not claim [prophethood] does not mean that such a thing, if it occurred connected to a claim of messengership, would not indicate the truth of its claimant. Yes, I do not deny that the way (sunnah) of Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, is to manifest karamat mostly without intent and choice. And what we mentioned is about permissibility, not about informing what the way of Allah, whose power is exalted, runs upon, nor about the established principle: that Allah brings about tribulations at the hands of those who claim divinity from among servants, as reported in the stories: that Allah caused the Nile to flow for Pharaoh when it turned around? And as reported in the traditions about the tribulations that Allah will bring, and the extraordinary tables of plenty upon the Antichrist (al-Masīḥ al-Dajjāl). These things are not breaking the [unique character of] miracle; for we have repeated many times that the miracle does not indicate by itself. Rather, it indicates prophethood. And the one who claims divinity has no request for confirmation, so if what he brings matches [his claim], it indicates confirmation from Allah, and this is akin to saying 'You have spoken truthfully.' Whoever encompasses what we have mentioned will find it easy to grasp the answer to everything that might be raised against him aside from this. And with Allah is success.
Source pages p. 69

Commentary
This passage is an objection-and-answer. The objection asks whether karamat can appear along with a claim from the person experiencing them. Juwayni first refutes a flawed answer given by some proponents of karamat who say that karamat are distinguished by occurring without intent (ikhriyar). He argues that this shows they do not grasp the true nature of i'jaz. He then explains his own correct principle: a miracle (mu'jiza) does not indicate by being attached to any absolute claim; rather, it indicates prophethood only when it occurs in accordance with a claim to prophethood. Therefore, if an extraordinary event is connected to another type of claim (like a saint's status), it indicates the truth of that claim. He gives the analogy of a person in a court claiming closeness to the king and the king performing an act at his request—this confirms the claim. He anticipates another objection: what about extraordinary events that occur without any claim (like karamat of saints), and what about events that occur to false prophets? He clarifies that such events do not undermine the principle; they are part of Allah's sunnah to test people, like the Nile for Pharaoh and the signs of the Dajjal. These do not break the unique character of mu'jizat because mu'jizat only indicate when they support a genuine claim of prophethood. For false claimants, the occurrence of extraordinary events is a trial (fitna). The conclusion is that whoever understands this can answer any related objection.

Key Terms
كرامات
معجزات
دعوى
إعجاز
فتنة
مسيح دجال

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that karamat are distinguished from miracles by occurring without intent (ikhriyar), and that this is what keeps them from challenging prophethood.
Correction: Juwayni argues that this is not the correct distinction. The real distinction is that miracles are defined by their connection to a true claim of prophethood, while karamat are not claims to prophethood but are simply signs of saintly status, often occurring without intent.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, why do extraordinary events that occur at the hands of false prophets (like the Dajjal) not break the unique status of prophetic miracles?
A) Because they are weaker
B) Because they occur before prophethood
C) Because miracles only indicate truth when they accompany a true claim of prophethood, while others are test/trial
D) Because they are actually performed by saints

Key Takeaway
Miracles are defined by their connection to a true claim of prophethood; other extraordinary events are either saintly karamat or divine trials, and do not undermine the unique evidential power of prophethood.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you witness or hear about an extraordinary spiritual event, do you instinctively accept it as a sign from Allah, or do you pause to check what claim it supports? What would Juwayni advise you to do?
?
Juwayni emphasizes that miracles are not about dazzlement but about truth. Are you more impressed by displays of power or by alignment with truth? How can you cultivate discernment so that you are not deceived by the Dajjal's signs?
?
Think of a time you were drawn to a person because of their apparent spiritual gifts. Did you ever question whether those gifts confirmed a true claim or were merely a trial? What would change if you applied Juwayni's criterion to that situation?

Miracles

8 min
Refutation of Eloquence as the Quran's Miracle
Juwayni criticizes those who claim the Quran's miracle is its eloquence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's critique of those who rely on the Quran's eloquence to prove its inimitability, and why he considers this approach flawed.

Core Idea
Eloquence cannot prove the Quran's miracle because no one can be certain it exceeds all human capacity.

Juwayni tackles a popular but, in his view, misguided argument for the Quran's inimitability: that its unparalleled literary excellence proves it's a miracle. He argues that such a claim is not empirically verifiable and opens the door to doubt. This lesson walks through his refutation and shows why he insists on a different approach.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Note Juwayni's critical tone and his promise to provide the correct path.
2
Identify the two approaches
Juwayni states there are two approaches to establishing the Prophet's miracles. Find them. Then note that the rest of the passage focuses on the first one (Quran's inimitability) and refutes a common view about it.
3
Understand the critique of eloquence
Juwayni argues that claiming the Quran's eloquence is superhuman is not logically sound. He gives examples of famous Arab poets and says one cannot be certain of superiority. Consider why this critique undermines the eloquence argument.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فان قيل : ما معجزة رسولكم ؟ قلنا : لنا في إثبات معجزاته مسلكان . أحدهما: التعلق بإعجاز القرآن . وقد أكثر الناس في وجه إعجاز القرآن ، وتقطعوا فيه أيادي سبا .. وصار معظم الناس : إلى أن القرآن تميز على صنوف الكلام بمزية البلاغة والجزالة خارج عن المعتاد في ذلك . ثم زعم زاعمون : أن إعجازه في شرف جزالته ، وذهب آخرون : إلى أن إعجازه في الجزالة الفائقة وأسلوبه الخارج عن أساليب النظم والنثر والخطب والأراجيز .. وهذا موقف تاه فيه الأولون والآخرون، وطعن فيه الطاعنون . وأنا بعون الله تعالى وحسن توفيقه آتي فيه بمسلك الحق وأبين عن واضح الوجوه اندفاع تمويهات الرائعين واقتفاض مطاعن المبطلين . فليعلم المشتهى إلى ذلك : من رام أن يثبت إعجاز القرآن بأنه في جزالته خارق للعادات مجاوز الفصاحة للبلغاء واللسن الفصحاء . فقد حاد عن مدرك الحق فان من تأمل كلام العرب في نظمها ونثرها لم يتحقق عنده انتهاء جزالة القرآن إلى حد الخروج عن العادة في الزيادة على كلام الفصحاء . ومن تكلف إثبات ذلك فقد تكلف شططا وظن غلطا وتهدف للكلام الطويل من غير تحصيل ومن أنصف وانتصف ولم يتعسف لم يلح له : أن شعر امرئ القيس والذبياني والجعدى وزهير وأعشى باهلة ، والمعلقات السبع وغيرها من أشعار المفلقين ، تقصر في الجزالة عن القرآن ثم من يديخ ما - أنبه عليه سلمى رأى قولا قلت: أنه لو ظهرت زيادة في ترقى القرآن عن مراتب الكلام فليس منع • فانه قد يتفق في بعض الأعصار رجل قد فرد في شعر أو كثر لا يدرك شأوه، ولا يلجـ منصبه في الفصاحة . وقل ما يخلو عصر عن مبرز عصر عن مبرز لا يوازى عنه ولا نيارى ولا يساري فيما اختص به ولا يثبت الإعجاز بمثل ذلك
If it is said: What is the miracle of your Messenger? We say: We have two approaches in establishing his miracles. One of them: relying on the inimitability (iʿjāz) of the Quran. People have spoken much about the aspect of the inimitability of the Quran, and they have scattered like the hands of Saba'. Most people have concluded that the Quran is distinguished from all types of speech by a quality of eloquence and stylistic excellence beyond the customary in that. Then some claimed that its inimitability lies in the nobility of its style, and others went that its inimitability lies in its superior style and its mode beyond the modes of poetry, prose, sermons, and rajaz verses... This is a position in which the earlier and later scholars have gone astray, and the critics have criticized it. And I, with the help of Allah Almighty and His good success, will present in this the correct path and clarify from clear aspects the warding off of the deceptions of the flailing and the cutting off of the objections of the falsifiers. So let the one desiring this know: Whoever aims to prove the inimitability of the Quran on the basis that its stylistic excellence is miraculous, exceeding the eloquence of the eloquent and the tongues of the articulate, has deviated from the point of truth. For whoever contemplates the speech of the Arabs in its poetry and prose will not ascertain that the stylistic excellence of the Quran reaches the limit of exceeding the customary in surpassing the speech of the eloquent. And whoever burdens himself with proving that has burdened himself with enormity and thought mistakenly, and aimed for lengthy speech without achievement. And whoever is fair and moderate and does not distort will not perceive that the poetry of Imru' al-Qays, al-Dhubyānī, al-Ja'dī, Zuhayr, and A'shā Bāhila, and the seven Mu'allaqāt and other poems of the outstanding, are lacking in stylistic excellence compared to the Quran? Then whoever claims that – I will point out – upon careful consideration, one would say: even if there appears an increase in the elevation of the Quran over the ranks of speech, that is not prevented, for it may happen in some ages a man who excels in poetry or is great, whose standing is not attained, and rarely does an age lack an outstanding person who is not matched, and no one is comparable or equal in what he specializes. And the miracle is not established by such [eloquence].
Source pages p. 71

Commentary
This passage is a critical refutation of the common argument that the Quran's miracle lies in its superior eloquence. Juwayni begins by stating the question: What is the miracle of the Messenger? He then presents the two approaches, one of which is the inimitability of the Quran. He notes the extensive discussion on the nature of its inimitability, comparing the scholars' divergence to the scattered tribes of Saba'. He summarizes the popular view: that the Quran is distinguished by its unparalleled eloquence. Some say its inimitability is in the nobility of its style, others in its unique mode beyond all literary forms. Juwayni declares this approach misguided and promises to provide the correct path. He then criticizes anyone who tries to prove that the Quran's eloquence is superhuman. He argues that anyone familiar with Arabic poetry and prose cannot be certain that the Quran's style surpasses all human capability by an extraordinary margin. He gives examples of famous pre-Islamic poets and the Mu'allaqat, implying that their poetry is not manifestly inferior. He further argues that even if the Quran had a higher degree of eloquence, that does not prove a miracle, because exceptional talent can appear in any era and may not be matched. Thus, making the miracle dependent on eloquence is unreliable and does not withstand scrutiny.

Key Terms
إعجاز القرآن
جزالة
بلاغة
تحدي
معجزة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Quran's miracle is proven by its unparalleled eloquence, which no human can match.
Correction: Juwayni argues that such a claim is not rationally certain because one cannot be sure the Quran's eloquence exceeds all human capacity; outstanding poets exist in every age, so eloquence alone does not establish a miracle.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, why cannot the Quran's superior eloquence alone prove its miracle?
A) Because the Quran is not eloquent enough
B) Because it is impossible for anyone to be certain that it exceeds all human capability
C) Because the challenge was not issued
D) Because the Arabs did not try to imitate it

Key Takeaway
Juwayni rejects the eloquence-based proof of the Quran's inimitability because it is not objectively verifiable and does not logically necessitate a miracle.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever accepted a common belief about Islam just because it was popular, without rationally verifying it? Juwayni challenges a widely held view. Are there areas of your faith where you rely on popularity rather than proof? What will you do this week to investigate one such belief critically?
?
If the Quran's eloquence is not the miracle, then what is the true basis of its inimitability? Have you personally experienced the Quran in a way that moves you to obedience—not just emotionally, but as a clear sign from Allah? What is one step you can take today to deepen your engagement with the Quran beyond its linguistic beauty?
?
Juwayni warns against subjective judgments when it comes to matters of faith. In your own life, do you base your certainty about Allah and His Messenger on solid intellectual evidence, or on feelings and assumptions? What is one assumption you hold about Islam that you have not thoroughly examined? Will you commit to seeking clarity this week?

Miracles
intermediate
6 min
The Quran's Miracle: The Challenge and Historical Failure
Juwayni establishes the miracle of the Quran through the continuous challenge and failure to produce its like.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's argument that the Quran's miracle is proven by the continuous challenge and the inability of humanity to produce its like.

Core Idea
The Quran's miracle is proven by history's failure to match it.

Juwayni argues that the Quran's miraculous nature is not a subjective claim about its eloquence but is established through a verifiable historical fact: the Prophet's challenge to the Arabs and their failure over centuries to produce anything comparable. This lesson unpacks that argument step by step.

Guided Path
1
Understand the condition for a miracle
Juwayni begins by reiterating that a miracle must involve a breaking of the norm (khāriq lil-ʿādah) beyond human capacity. This sets the standard for what counts as a miracle.
This condition is crucial for the proof that follows.
2
Note the shift from eloquence to challenge
Juwayni deliberately avoids claiming that the Quran's stylistic excellence itself is miraculous. Instead, he centers the proof on the challenge (taḥaddī) issued by the Prophet.
This shift avoids subjective judgments.
3
Examine the challenge's duration and context
The Prophet challenged the eloquent Arabs for over twenty years. The Quran was in their own language and within their perceived capability, yet they failed.
The challenge was public and persistent.
4
See the historical evidence
Juwayni notes that over 460+ years, despite the presence of skilled poets and critics hostile to Islam, no one has produced a counterpart. This historical fact makes the miracle definitive.
The proof is empirical and cumulative.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قدمنَا أَنا تشترط في المعجزة أن يجاوز في خرق العادة حدود الطول، ويبلغ مبلغا لا يتوقع الانتهاء إليه بشرية علم ، وجودة قريحة، والفنان طبع وثقابة رأى ، واصابة فكر، وبعد الموز، وإذا تقرر ذلك؟. فالوجه أن لا يدعى جزالة القرآن مبلغ تخرق العادة ، بل تقول : التحدى الرسول صلى الله عليه وسلم فصحاء العرب بأن يأتوا بمثل القرآن كما أنبأ عنه قوله تبارك وتعالى : ( قل لئن اجتمعت الإنس والجن على أن يأتوا بمثل هذا القرآن لا يأتون بمثله ) . وتمادي على تحديه نيفا وعشرين سنة .. والقرآن بلغتهم وليس بعيدا عن مبلغ اقتدارهم في جزالته وأسلوبه ، فلم يقدروا على الإتيان بمثله ، ثم استأثر الله تبارك وتعالى لرسوله صلى الله عليه وسلم ، وكرت الدهور، ومرت العصور ، وأقطار الأرض تطفح بجميع الكفار ، ذوي الفطن النافذة ، وشوقهم أن يتمكنوا من مطعن في الإسلام . وفي كل قطر منهم طائفة مشتغلون بالنظم والنشر على لغة العرب ، فقصرت قدرة الخلق عن المعارضة في أربعمائة وستين سنة ) ونيف ، فتبين قطعا
We have previously [stated] that we require in the miracle that it surpass in breaking the norm the limits of extent, and reach a level that is not expected to be attained by human knowledge, excellence of aptitude, artistry of nature, sharpness of opinion, correctness of thought, and depth of insight? When this is established, then the correct approach is not to claim that the stylistic excellence of the Quran reaches the level of breaking the norm, but rather we say: The Messenger, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, challenged the eloquent Arabs to produce the like of the Quran, as His statement, blessed and exalted, informed: (Say: If all of humans and jinn gathered to produce the like of this Quran, they would not produce its like). And he persisted in his challenge for some twenty years. And the Quran is in their language and is not far from the extent of their ability in its stylistic excellence and style, yet they were unable to produce its like. Then Allah, blessed and exalted, reserved it for His Messenger. Ages passed, eras elapsed, and the regions of the earth teem with all the disbelievers, people of penetrating intellects, and their desire to find a point of attack in Islam. And in every region among them a group occupied with poetry and prose in the Arabic language, yet the ability of creation fell short of producing a counterpart in four hundred and sixty years and some more, thus it became definitively clear...
Source pages p. 72

Commentary
This passage presents the correct proof for the miracle of the Quran according to Juwayni. He begins by reiterating the condition for a miracle: it must involve a breaking of the norm (khāriq lil-ʿādah) that is beyond the reach of human capability, including knowledge, intellect, natural talent, and insight. Having reminded the reader of this condition, Juwayni then argues that the eloquence of the Quran should not be claimed to be miraculous in itself. Instead, the proof lies in the challenge (taḥaddī) issued by the Prophet Muhammad (SAW) to the eloquent Arabs to produce even one chapter like the Quran. This challenge is recorded in the Quran itself, in the verse from Surah al-Isra: 'Say: If all of humans and jinn gathered...' The Prophet persisted in this challenge for over twenty years during his lifetime. The Quran was in their own language and within the scope of their capabilities, yet they failed to meet the challenge. Moreover, after the Prophet's time, throughout the centuries, there have been countless disbelievers and skilled literary figures who would have loved to discredit Islam, but no one has been able to produce anything comparable. Juwayni specifies an extended period of four hundred and sixty years and more, which definitively demonstrates that the inability is not due to a lack of effort but is a proof of the Quran's divine origin. This proof is based on observable historical fact, not on a subjective judgment of eloquence.

Key Terms
معجزة (miracle)
تحدي (challenge)
إعجاز القرآن (inimitability of Quran)
خرق العادة (breaking the norm)
معارضة (producing a counterpart)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The miracle of the Quran is simply its unmatched eloquence.
Correction: Juwayni argues that the true proof is the historical failure to meet the challenge, not a subjective judgment of eloquence.

Checkpoint
What is the key condition for a miracle according to Juwayni in this passage?
A) It must be eloquent
B) It must involve a breaking of the norm that is beyond human capability
C) It must be witnessed by many
D) It must be accompanied by a prophecy

Key Takeaway
The Quran's miracle is proven by the historical fact of the challenge and the persistent failure to produce its like, not by a mere claim of eloquence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If the Quran is so beyond human ability that even the most skilled poets and thinkers over centuries have failed to imitate it, why do you often treat it as an ordinary book? What would change in your daily life if you truly believed every word is from Allah and unmatched by any human speech?
?
The disbelievers desperately wanted to discredit the Quran but could not. Have you ever doubted its divine origin? What concrete step will you take this week to deepen your certainty that this book is from Allah?
?
You carry the Quran in your hands. The challenge is not just to produce its like, but to live by its teachings. Are you failing where the disbelievers failed? In what area of your life are you not responding to its challenge, and what will you do about it starting today?

Miracles
beginner

The Correct View on I'jaz al-Qur'an
Critique of Juwayni's sarfah view and affirmation of i'jaz in lafz and ma'na

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the correct view of the inimitability of the Qur'an as being inherent in both wording and meaning, universal and timeless, and why the sarfah view is mistaken.

Core Idea
The Qur'an's miracle is in its words and meanings for all people and all time.

In this passage, the author critiques a famous but problematic view by Imam al-Juwayni regarding the inimitability of the Qur'an. He then presents the correct position, which emphasizes that the miracle lies in both the wording and meaning of the Qur'an, and that it is not limited to any particular era or language group.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Begin by reading the Arabic passage once without pausing, then read the English translation. Notice the contrast between the critique and the affirmation.
The passage contains a poetic couplet that illustrates the flaw in Juwayni's argument.
2
Identify the two views
Distinguish Juwayni's sarfah view from the author's correct view. Sarfah claims God prevented imitation; the correct view says the Qur'an itself is inherently inimitable in wording and meaning.
3
Understand the critique via the example
The poetic couplet is used to show that Juwayni's own reasoning leads to a contradiction—he tried to avoid one problem but fell into another. Focus on the idea that the Qur'an's inimitability is intrinsic, not external prevention.
The exact wording of the couplet is obscure, but the point is clear: Juwayni's solution is self-defeating.
4
Reflect on the universal scope
Notice that the author stresses the i'jaz is not for Arabs only, not for the prophetic era only, and not limited to one branch of knowledge. This universal claim strengthens the miracle's significance.
This corrects a common misconception that i'jaz is merely a linguistic challenge.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والجويني غير مصيب في قوله ان اعجاز القرآن بالصرفة، قد وقع بقوله هذا فيما فر هو منه في معنى هذا البيت:
القله في اليم اليم مكتوفا وقال له
إياك إياك أن تبتل بالماء
والصواب: أن إعجاز القرآن باللفظ والمعنى، وليس للعرب وحدهم بل للعالم، وليس في عصر النبوة فقط بل في جميع العصور، وليس في نوع من أنواع العلم، بل في جميع أنواع العلوم. وقد فصلنا هذا في كتابنا «إعجاز القرآن».
Al-Juwayni is not correct in his statement that the inimitability (i'jaz) of the Qur'an is by way of prevention (sarfah); he has fallen into that which he fled from in the meaning of this verse: 'al-qalla [؟] in the deep sea bound and said to him: Beware, beware of being immersed in water.' The correct view is that the inimitability of the Qur'an is in its wording and meaning, and it is not for the Arabs alone, but for the world, and not only in the era of prophethood, but in all eras, and not in one type of knowledge, but in all types of knowledge. And we have detailed this in our book 'I'jaz al-Qur'an'.
Source pages p. 113

Commentary
This passage critiques Juwayni's opinion that the inimitability (i'jaz) of the Qur'an is merely by way of 'sarfah' (God preventing people from imitating it). The author argues that Juwayni's own example (a poetic couplet) shows the flaw in his reasoning. The correct view is that i'jaz is inherent in both the wording (lafz) and meaning (ma'na) of the Qur'an. It is not limited to the Arabs or the time of revelation, but is universal and timeless, covering all fields of knowledge. The author refers to his own work 'I'jaz al-Qur'an' for further detail.

Key Terms
إعجاز
الصرفة
اللفظ
المعنى
النبوة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The inimitability of the Qur'an is only a linguistic challenge for the Arabs of the prophetic era.
Correction: The author affirms that i'jaz is in both wording and meaning, for all people, all times, and all types of knowledge.

Checkpoint
According to the author, what is the correct understanding of the inimitability (i'jaz) of the Qur'an?
A) It is only through prevention (sarfah) of imitation
B) It is in its wording and meaning, for all times and people
C) It is limited to the Arabs of the prophetic era
D) It is only in its meaning, not its wording

Key Takeaway
The inimitability of the Qur'an is a comprehensive miracle in its wording and meaning, for all people and across all ages.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Is there an area of your life where you limit the Qur'an's miracle to something merely intellectual, ignoring its power to transform your heart and actions? What would it look like to embrace its challenge today?
?
Do you treat the Qur'an as a miracle only for Arabs or for your own time? Or have you convinced yourself that its challenge is not for you personally? How would your relationship with the Qur'an change if you believed it was speaking directly to you right now?
?
When reading the Qur'an, do you seek to encounter both its wording and meaning, or do you settle for one while neglecting the other? What would you need to change in your daily routine to engage with both deeply?

Miracles
beginner
4 min
Sufficiency of the Qur'an as a Proof of Prophethood
Refutation of the need for sensory miracles by citing Quranic sufficiency

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why sensory miracles are not necessary for proving prophethood, as the Qur'an itself is a sufficient miracle.

Core Idea
The Qur'an alone suffices as a miracle – no sensory signs required.

Juwayni addresses a common challenge to prophethood: why didn't the Prophet bring sensory miracles? He responds by citing a Qur'anic verse that declares the Book itself to be sufficient. This lesson explores that argument and its implications for the role of miracles in proving prophethood.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing, then read the translation. Notice how the author sets up the discussion.
The passage begins with 'maqal' indicating a point of contention.
2
Identify the Qur'anic argument
Locate the verse quoted from Surah al-Ankabut. Pay attention to the rhetorical question: 'Is it not sufficient for them that We have sent down to you the Book?'
This question is the core of the refutation.
3
Reflect on the sufficiency
Consider why the Qur'an itself is considered a sufficient miracle. How does this answer the demand for sensory miracles?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأما الاستدلال بالمعجزات الحسية ففيه مقال لما ورد في القرآن: وقالوا: لولا أنزل عليه آيات من ربه. قل إنما الآيات عند الله وإنما أنا نذير مبين. أو لم يكفهم أنا أنزلنا عليك الكتاب يتلى عليهم. إن في ذلك لرحمة وذكرى لقوم يؤمنون. فقد بين كفاية الكتاب.
As for the inference from sensory miracles (mu'jizat hissiyyah), there is a discussion due to what has been mentioned in the Qur'an: 'And they say: Why are not signs sent down to him from his Lord? Say: The signs are only with Allah, and I am only a clear warner. Is it not sufficient for them that We have sent down to you the Book which is recited to them? Indeed, in that is a mercy and a reminder for a people who believe.' He has thus clarified the sufficiency of the Book.
Source pages p. 113

Commentary
This passage addresses the argument from sensory miracles (mu'jizat hissiyyah) as proof of prophethood. The author notes that there is a discussion (maqal) regarding this because the Qur'an itself responds to those who demanded such miracles. The verse from Surah al-Ankabut is quoted, where Allah tells the Prophet to say that signs are with Allah alone, and that he is only a warner. The rhetorical question 'Is it not sufficient for them that We have sent down to you the Book?' highlights that the Qur'an itself is a sufficient miracle. The commentator concludes that this verse clarifies the sufficiency (kifayah) of the Book as proof.

Key Terms
معجزات حسية
آيات
كفاية الكتاب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: A prophet must bring sensory miracles to prove his prophethood.
Correction: The Qur'an itself is a sufficient miracle, as indicated by the verse, so sensory miracles are not necessary.

Checkpoint
Why does the commentator argue that sensory miracles are not necessary for proving prophethood?
A) Because miracles are impossible
B) Because the Qur'an itself is a sufficient sign
C) Because the Prophet refused to perform them
D) Because they only work for the Arabs

Key Takeaway
The Qur'an's own internal evidence is sufficient to prove the prophethood of Muhammad, without requiring additional sensory miracles.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?

?
If someone told you they need a miracle to believe, what would you say based on this lesson? Are you ready to defend the sufficiency of the Qur'an even when your own heart wavers?

The Quran as a Sufficient Miracle
Juwayni argues that the Quran is a sufficient miracle, quoting a verse.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Juwayni argues that the Quran itself is a sufficient miracle, outweighing the demand for sensory miracles.

Core Idea
The Quran's miracle is comprehensive and self-sufficient.

Juwayni begins by asserting that the Quran's inimitability extends to all fields of knowledge, not limited to one type. He then addresses objections about sensory miracles and demonstrates that the Quranic revelation itself is enough evidence for prophethood.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on Quranic miracle
Read the Arabic passage starting from 'وليس في نوع من أنواع العلم' to 'فقد بين كفاية الكتاب'. Notice how Juwayni establishes the Quran's comprehensive miracle.
The verse quoted (29:50-51) is key.
2
Reflect on the verse
Consider the disbelievers' demand for signs and Allah's response. Why does Juwayni use this verse to argue for the Quran's sufficiency?
3
Connect to the conclusion
Juwayni concludes that the Quran suffices. Think about how this counters arguments for sensory miracles.
Notice Juwayni mentions his own book 'I'jaz al-Quran' for further detail.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وليس في نوع من أنواع العلم، بل في جميع أنواع العلوم. وقد فصلنا هذا في كتابنا «إعجاز القرآن». وأما الاستدلال بالمعجزات الحسية ففيه مقال لما ورد في القرآن: وقالوا: لولا أنزل عليه آيات من ربه. قل: إنما الآيات عند الله وإنما أنا نذير مبين. أو لم يكفهم أنا أنزلنا عليك الكتاب يتلى عليهم؟ إن في ذلك لرحمة وذكرى لقوم يؤمنون. (العنكبوت 50-51) فقد بين كفاية الكتاب. وأما عن أخبار الأنبياء به قبله فهذا واضح من نبوءات موجودة إلى الآن في التوراة رغم التحريف. يقول اليهود في مدلولها إنها تشير إلى نبي لم يظهر بعد، ويقول النصارى إنها تشير إلى المسيح عيسى بن مريم عليه السلام. ونقول نحن المسلمين إنها تشير إلى محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم، وقولنا هو الحق لأن أوصاف هذا النبي المشار إليه مسايلة لموسى. وقد نصت التوراة على أنه لن يظهر نبي في بني إسرائيل مثل موسى، وحيث قد نصت التوراة بركة للأمم في آل إسماعيل عليه السلام، فإن هذا النبي يكون منه. كما ذكرنا في كتب حققناها، ومنها شفاء الغليل في بيان ما وقع في التوراة والإنجيل من التبديل... للإمام الجويني.
And it is not in one type of knowledge, but in all types of knowledge. And we have detailed this in our book 'I'jaz al-Quran'. As for inference by sensory miracles, there is discussion therein due to what came in the Quran: And they said: 'Why were not signs sent down to him from his Lord?' Say: 'The signs are only with Allah, and I am only a clear warner.' Is it not sufficient for them that We sent down to you the Book which is recited to them? Indeed, in that is mercy and reminder for a people who believe. (Al-Ankabut 50-51). Thus He has shown the sufficiency of the Book. And as for the reports of the prophets before him about him, this is clear from prophecies existing until now in the Torah despite alteration. The Jews say in its indication that it refers to a prophet who has not yet appeared, and the Christians say that it refers to the Messiah Jesus son of Mary, peace be upon him. And we Muslims say that it refers to Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and our statement is the truth because the descriptions of this prophet referred to are similar to Moses. And the Torah has stated that no prophet will appear in the Children of Israel like Moses, and since the Torah has stated a blessing for the nations in the family of Ishmael, peace be upon him, then this prophet will be from him. As we have mentioned in books we have verified, among them Shifa' al-Ghalil on explaining what occurred in the Torah and the Gospel of alteration... by Imam al-Juwayni.
Source pages p. 114

Commentary
This passage is an argument about miracles and prophecies. Juwayni first states that miracles are not limited to one type of knowledge but encompass all sciences, referring to his own book on the inimitability of the Quran. Then he discusses sensory miracles (physical miracles) and notes that there is debate about them, but the Quran itself is sufficient as a miracle. He quotes the Quranic verse (29:50-51) where the disbelievers demand signs, and Allah replies that signs are with Him and that the Quran is enough. Then he turns to prophecies in the Torah that point to Prophet Muhammad. He explains that Jews and Christians interpret these prophecies differently, but Muslims hold that they indicate Muhammad because his description matches that of Moses, and the Torah states that no prophet like Moses will appear within the Israelites, and a blessing for the nations is promised through Ishmael. Therefore, the prophet must come from Ishmael's lineage. Juwayni references his own works on the distortion of the Torah and Gospel.

Key Terms
إعجاز القرآن
معجزات حسية
كتاب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some people think the Quran is not a miracle because it is just words.
Correction: Juwayni shows that the Quran's inimitability spans all knowledge, and Allah Himself declares it sufficient as a sign.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what does the verse (29:50-51) demonstrate?
A) That sensory miracles are invalid
B) That the Quran is sufficient as a sign
C) That the Quran is only for believers
D) That miracles occur only through the Quran

Key Takeaway
The Quran is a comprehensive and sufficient miracle, as declared by Allah in the Quran itself.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly experience the Quran as a sufficient miracle, or do you find yourself seeking signs and proofs elsewhere to reassure your faith? What would change if you treated the Quran as enough?
?
When you read the verse 'Is it not sufficient for them...', do you feel its weight as a divine challenge to your own heart? Have you ever truly considered the Quran's content as the ultimate sign, or do you take it for granted?
?
If the Quran encompasses all types of knowledge, why are you hesitant to rely on it as your complete guide in every area of your life? What specific area do you hold back from submitting to its authority?

Prophecies in the Torah About Muhammad
Juwayni discusses prophecies in the Torah about Muhammad and defends the Muslim interpretation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Juwayni uses prophecies in the Torah to argue that Muhammad is the prophesied prophet.

Core Idea
Torah prophecies, despite alteration, point to Muhammad.

Juwayni turns to prophecies in the Torah that foretell a coming prophet. He explains the interpretations of Jews, Christians, and Muslims, and defends the Muslim position that the prophet is Muhammad, based on his similarity to Moses and the blessing through Ishmael.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on Torah prophecies
Read the Arabic passage starting from 'وأما عن أخبار الأنبياء به قبله' to the end. Notice the three interpretations.
Juwayni references the Torah's statement about no prophet like Moses.
2
Identify the three claims
List the Jewish, Christian, and Muslim interpretations of the prophecy. What are their key arguments?
Jews say a prophet yet to come; Christians say Jesus; Muslims say Muhammad.
3
Analyze Juwayni's reasoning
Juwayni argues that Muhammad is correct because: (1) his description matches Moses, (2) the Torah says no prophet like Moses will arise in Israel, so he must be from Ishmael, (3) the blessing for nations is in Ishmael's line.
This is a logical deduction from the Torah text.
4
Reflect on the implication
Consider how Juwayni's argument uses existing scriptures to support Islam, even acknowledging alteration.
Juwayni notes the Torah has been altered but still contains valid prophecies.

Introduction to the Chapter on Karamat
Juwayni introduces the chapter on karamat and states his prior work.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the context of Juwayni's discussion of karamat: the problem of people erring about affirming or denying them, and Juwayni's purpose in summarizing his earlier work.

Core Idea
Karamat are a contested topic; Juwayni wrote a book to defend them.

Imam Juwayni begins a new chapter on the miraculous acts of saints (karamat). He notes that many people have gone astray in the debate over whether to affirm or deny such occurrences. He informs the reader that he has already written a dedicated book on the subject and now intends to present its essence. This short introduction sets the stage for a deeper discussion.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing, then read the literal translation. Let the author's own words set the context.
The passage has OCR uncertainties; focus on the general meaning.
2
Identify the problem Juwayni addresses
Notice that Juwayni says 'people have erred much' regarding affirming and denying karamat. Ask yourself: Why would this topic be controversial?
3
Understand the purpose of this chapter
Juwayni states he has written a full book on the topic and will now present its core in summary. This tells you that the following discussion is a condensed treatment.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فصل في الكرامات . [وقد قال]؟ في أنه قد كثر خبط الناس في إثباتها ونفيها ، وقد ألفت في إثباتها ، والرد على [المنكرة]؟ كتابا . وأنا أذكر الآن لبابه ، في أسطر إن شاء الله جل وعزة . فأقول :
Chapter on Karamat. [And he said]? that indeed many people have erred much regarding affirming them and denying them, and I have authored a book on affirming them and refuting the [deniers]?. And I will now mention its core, in lines, if God Almighty wills. So I say:
Source pages p. 69

Commentary
This passage is an introductory transition. Juwayni begins a new chapter entitled 'On Karamat' (miraculous acts of saints). He observes that many people have gone astray (خبط) in the debate about affirming or denying such occurrences. He informs the reader that he has already written a separate book on affirming karamat and refuting those who deny them (possibly the Mu'tazila or others). He then states that he will now present the essence (لباب) of that discussion in summary form, and then begins the substantive discourse with 'So I say:'.

Key Terms
كرامات
إثبات
نفي
خبط

C) Erred much in affirming and denying them
D) Ignored the topic

Key Takeaway
Juwayni introduces his chapter on karamat by noting widespread error and his intention to summarize his earlier work.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?

?

intermediate
6 min
Repentance from One Sin While Persisting in Another: Al-Juwayni's Resolution
Al-Juwayni presents the dispute on repentance and offers his resolution based on the state of the repenter.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Al-Juwayni's nuanced position on whether repentance from a single sin is valid when one persists in other sins, and how the validity depends on the spiritual state of the repenter.

Core Idea
Repentance's validity depends on the repenter's spiritual station — gnostics cannot persist, but ordinary believers may.

Al-Juwayni tackles a subtle theological question: can a person truly repent of one sin while still committing others? He reviews earlier opinions, critiques an unsatisfactory answer, and then offers his own insightful division between the gnostic and the ordinary believer. This lesson walks through his reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Understand the dispute
The passage opens with the two opposing views: the Ash'ari permissibility vs. the Mu'tazilite impossibility of repentance from one sin while persisting in others. Note the positions of the 'People of Truth' and Abu Hashim/al-Jubba'i.
This sets up the problem.
2
Examine the weak answer
Al-Juwayni presents and then critiques an answer from some Imams: that repentance is about a general sentiment of gravity, not specific to one sin. He finds it unconvincing.
Notice why he dismisses it.
3
Grasp Al-Juwayni's solution
He divides repenters into two categories: the gnostic ('arif) and the ordinary believer (mu'taqid). Understand how each type's state affects the validity of their repentance.
This is the key insight.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فصل
عظيم الموقع أجعله مختتم العقيدة
(١)
اضطرب رأى الناس . ( فى أنه ( هل تصح التوبة عن ذنب ، مع الاصرار على غيره من الذنوب ؟ فنقل الناقلون عن أهل الحق : أن ذلك جائز . وذهب أبو هاشم والجبائي (۱) : الى أن ذلك ممتنع . وتمسك بها عيسى من أئمة الحق في الجواب عنه (۲) فقال : التوبة النصوح انما يجب عليها : استشعار تعظيم مخالفة الله تبارك وتعالى . واكبار مبارزة الفاطر بالذنوب . وهذا اذا فحص حقا لم يخص ذنبا وهذا واقع جدا ، ولم يذكر الأئمة جوابا مقنعا . وأنا أقول : التائب عن الذنب ينقسم الى عارف بالله تبارك وتعالى واثق بنفسه . والى معتقد لا يتصف بثلج النفس .. فان كان صاحب الواقعة من العارفين فسبب معصيته : ذهوله عن صفوة المعرفة ، وتوبته عودة الى حضور الذهن . ومن حضرته المعرفة ، وسطعت عليه أنوارها ، لم يصر على ذنب من الذنوب ، ومن كان متمسكه عقدا - كما سبق وصفه اذا ضعفت شهوته في فن من المعاصى ، قوى فيه عقله ، ولاحت توبه ، وهو يصر على بقايا ذنوبه التي بقيت شهواته فيها
وهذا لا يدركه : الا فطن مدرك غواص ) والله المستعان ، وعليه
التكلان ) (۳)
Chapter of great importance, I make it the conclusion of the creed. The opinions of people differed concerning whether repentance from one sin is valid while persisting in other sins. The transmitters reported from the People of Truth that it is permissible. Abu Hashim and al-Jubba'i held that it is impossible. And a certain person from the Imams of the Truth clung to a difficult answer regarding it and said: 'True repentance necessarily requires sensing the gravity of opposing Allah Almighty and magnifying the audacity of confronting the Creator with sins.' When this is truly examined, it does not apply to a particular sin, and this is very real, and the Imams did not mention a convincing answer. And I say: The repenter of sin is divided into a knower of Allah Almighty who is confident of himself, and a believer who is not characterized by tranquility of the self. If the person concerned is among the knowers, then the cause of his sin is his heedlessness of the essence of knowledge, and his repentance is a return to presence of mind. And whoever has knowledge present and its lights shining upon him does not persist in any sin. And whoever holds by a mere creed – as previously described – when his desire weakens in a type of sin, his intellect strengthens and his repentance appears, but he persists in the remnants of his sins in which his desires remain. This is grasped only by an intelligent, discerning diver. And Allah is the Succour, and upon Him is reliance.
Source pages p. 94

Commentary
This passage is a theological discussion about the validity of repentance from one sin while persisting in others. It begins by stating the differing opinions: the People of Truth (referring to the Ash'aris) hold that it is permissible, while the Mu'tazilites Abu Hashim and al-Jubba'i hold it is impossible. Al-Juwayni then mentions a response attributed to some Imams: that true repentance is about having a sense of the gravity of sin, and this general feeling does not single out a particular sin; thus, if one truly repents of a sin, that sentiment should cover all sins. However, al-Juwayni criticizes that this answer lacks persuasive merit. He then offers his own solution by dividing repenters into two categories: (1) the 'arif (gnostic) who knows Allah deeply and is secure in his knowledge – his sin arises from a moment of heedlessness, and his repentance is a return to presence of mind; once knowledge returns, he cannot remain on any sin. (2) The ordinary believer who holds by bare creed – when his desire for a particular sin weakens, his intellect overcomes and he appears to repent, but he still persists in other sins for which his desire remains. This nuanced explanation shows that the ruling depends on the state of the repenter. The passage concludes that this understanding is accessible only to a perceptive and deep thinker.

Key Terms
توبة
عارف
معتقد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that repentance is entirely invalid if any sin remains, but al-Juwayni shows it can be valid for the ordinary believer in a limited sense.
Correction: Al-Juwayni distinguishes between the gnostic, whose repentance is complete, and the ordinary believer, whose repentance may be partial while other sins persist.

Checkpoint
According to al-Juwayni, what is the cause of sin for the 'arif (gnostic)?
A) Weakness of desire
B) Heedlessness of the essence of knowledge
C) Lack of creed
D) Persistence of habit

Key Takeaway
Al-Juwayni resolves the question by distinguishing between the gnostic, whose repentance is complete due to renewed presence of knowledge, and the ordinary believer, whose repentance may be partial while other sins persist.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you repent of a sin you find easy to leave, do you quietly excuse your persistence in other sins you still enjoy? What does that reveal about the state of your knowledge of Allah?
?
Al-Juwayni says the gnostic 'does not persist in any sin.' If you claim to know Allah, why do you still choose to sin? Is your knowledge alive in your heart, or is it merely a mental belief?
?
Can you point to a sin you have repented from but still commit in a different form? What would it cost you to truly leave it behind, and are you willing to pay that price today?

The Obligation of Appointing an Imam: Majority View and Dissent
Juwayni presents the majority view on imamate and al-Asamm's dissent.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the majority view on the obligation of appointing an imam and the dissenting opinion of Abu Bakr al-Asamm.

Core Idea
The appointment of an imam is obligatory by consensus, with only a rare dissent from Abu Bakr al-Asamm.

Imam Juwayni begins by stating the near-universal agreement among Muslim scholars on the necessity of having a single leader for the community. He then presents the sole dissenting voice, that of Abu Bakr al-Asamm, who argued that the community could manage without a central imam as long as they collectively fulfill religious duties and maintain justice. This passage sets up a foundational debate in Islamic political thought.

Guided Path
1
Read the Passage Carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the phrase 'لا بد من نصب امام وخليفة' which emphasizes necessity.
2
Identify the Two Positions
The passage presents two views: the majority view that an imam is obligatory, and al-Asamm's view that it is only permissible. Find the key phrases that define each.
Al-Asamm's view uses 'غير واجبة' and 'يسوع' (permissible).
3
Note the Author's Treatment
Juwayni introduces the dissent as an exception ('الا ما روى عن أبي بكر الأصم'). This signals that the majority view is the standard position.
The author does not refute al-Asamm here; he simply records it.
4
Reflect on the Significance
Ask yourself why this debate matters. What does it tell us about the importance of leadership in Islam? How does al-Asamm's view challenge the consensus?
This connects to broader discussions about governance and community order.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قال كثير من العلماء : انه لا بد من نصب امام وخليفة ويطاع ، التجتمع به الكلمة ، وتنفذ به أحكام الخليفة ، ولا خلاف في ذلك بين الأمة ، ولا بين الأئمة الا ما روى عن أبي بكر الأصم من كبار رجال المعتزلة - رحمهم الله تعالى - فقد قال : « انها غير واجبة [على] الذين ، بل يسوع ذلك ، وأن الأمة متى أقاموا حجهم وجهادهم ، وتنا صفوا فيما بينهم ، وبذلوا الحق من أنفسهم ، وقسموا الغنائم والفيء والصدقات على أهلها ، وأقاموا الحدود على من وجبت عليه ، أجزأهم ذلك ، ولا يجب عليهم أن ينصبوا اماما يتولى ذلك ».
Many of the scholars said: There must be the appointment of an imam and a caliph who is obeyed, through whom the word is united and the rulings of the caliph are executed. There is no disagreement on this among the Ummah or among the imams, except what is narrated from Abu Bakr al-Asamm, one of the leading men of the Mu'tazila - may Allah have mercy on them - for he said: 'It is not obligatory upon the people, rather that is permissible. When the people perform their Hajj and jihad, treat each other fairly, give rights from themselves, divide the spoils, fay', and charity to their recipients, and establish the prescribed punishments upon those who become obligated, that suffices them, and they are not obligated to appoint an imam to undertake that.'
Source pages p. 127

Commentary
This passage presents the standard Sunni position on the obligation of appointing an imam (caliph), followed by a rare dissenting opinion. The author begins by stating the consensus: a leader is necessary to unite the community, execute rulings, and maintain order. He notes that there is near-consensus on this across the Ummah and its scholars, with the sole exception of Abu Bakr al-Asamm of the Mu'tazila. Al-Asamm argued that the community can manage without a central imam as long as they collectively perform religious obligations and maintain justice. This dissenting view is presented for refutation but not explicitly refuted here; the author simply records it as an exception.

Key Terms
نصب الإمام
أبو بكر الأصم
المعتزلة
وجوب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That Abu Bakr al-Asamm's view represents mainstream Mu'tazila thought.
Correction: Al-Asamm was a leading figure, but his view was a minority even among the Mu'tazila. The passage presents it as an exception to the consensus.

Checkpoint
According to the majority of scholars, why is appointing an imam necessary?
A) To avoid war among the Ummah
B) To unite the word and execute the caliph's rulings
C) To collect taxes and distribute wealth
D) To lead military campaigns

Key Takeaway
The majority of scholars hold that appointing an imam is obligatory, with Abu Bakr al-Asamm as a rare dissent who argues the community can manage collectively without a single leader.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The consensus of scholars insists on the necessity of a leader to unite the word and execute rulings. In your own life, are you contributing to unity or division? Will you pledge today to be a source of reconciliation in your family or community?
?
Al-Asamm thought that if each person fulfills their duties (Hajj, justice, etc.), no central leader is needed. But honestly, do you rely on others to solve communal problems while you focus only on your own worship? What will you change starting now to take responsibility for the collective well-being of those around you?
?
If the appointment of an imam is so critical, why have so many Muslims become apathetic about leadership and governance? Does this lesson convict you to become more informed and active in seeking righteous leadership, even in small ways? What is one concrete step you will take this week to support a just cause or a righteous leader?

Three Opinions on How One Becomes Imam
Juwayni lists three views on becoming imam: nass, group nass, or consensus.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three classical views on how a person becomes imam, focusing on the role of nass and consensus.

Core Idea
The imamate is established either by direct designation or by community selection.

This passage from Imam al-Juwayni presents the three main scholarly opinions on how an imam is legitimately installed. You'll see that the first two involve explicit designation (nass) by the previous imam, while the third relies on the consensus of qualified electors. Understanding these views clarifies the Sunni framework for political authority.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own distinctions come through.
Notice that the passage is structured as a clear enumeration of three opinions.
2
Identify the three opinions
Find each of the three mechanisms for becoming imam: nass on an individual, nass on a group, and consensus of the electors.
The third opinion is described more extensively than the first two.
3
Analyze the third opinion
Understand why the consensus of the people of the capital city is binding on all Muslims, and the condition that the imam not be openly sinful.
The passage emphasizes unity and avoiding discord as the reason for obligation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واختلف فيما يكون به الامام اماما . على ثلاثة أقوال . أحدها : اذا نص الامام على واحد معين من بعده فانه يكون اماما . والثاني : اذا نص الامام على جماعة يختارون منهم واحدا . والثالث : اجماع أهل الحل والعقد ، وذلك أن الجماعة فى مصر من أمصار المسلمين ، إذا مات امامهم ولم يكن لهم امام ، ولا استخلف فأقام أهل ذلك المصر الذي هو حضرة الامام وموضعه اماما لأنفسهم اجتمعوا عليه ورضوه فإن كل من خلفهم وأمامهم من المسلمين في الآفاق يلزمهم الدخول في طاعة ذلك الامام اذا لم يكن الامام معلنا بالفسق والفساد ، لأنها دعوة محيطة بهم تجب إجابتها ولا يسوغ لأحدا التخلف عنها لما في اختلاف اقامة امامين من فسخ الكلمة وفساد ذات البين
And they differed over what makes the imam an imam, on three opinions. One of them: if the imam explicitly designates a specific person after him, then he becomes imam. The second: if the imam explicitly designates a group from whom they choose one. The third: the consensus of the people of solution and contract. That is, when a group in one of the cities of Muslims - when their imam dies and they have no imam, and he has not appointed a successor - so the people of that city which is the seat and place of the imam appoint an imam for themselves, they agree upon him and are pleased with him, then everyone behind them and before them from the Muslims in the regions is obliged to enter into obedience to that imam, as long as the imam is not openly sinful and corrupt, because it is a call encompassing them, which it is obligatory to answer, and it is not permissible for anyone to lag behind it, due to what lies in the appointment of two imams of disagreement in the word and corruption of relationships.
Source pages p. 127

Commentary
This passage classifies the scholarly views on how a person becomes imam. The author delineates three opinions. First: the current imam explicitly names his successor (nass on an individual). Second: the imam names a committee who then select one among themselves. Third: the consensus of the people of solution and contract (ahl al-hall wa al-aqd) — those qualified to elect. The author elaborates the third view: if the people of the capital city, where the imam resided, choose an imam after his death without a designated successor, their choice is binding on all Muslims everywhere, provided the chosen imam is not openly sinful. This is because obedience is obligatory to maintain unity and avoid civil strife. The passage emphasizes the danger of having two imams, which leads to discord and corruption.

Key Terms
نص
جماعة
أهل الحل والعقد
إجماع
فسخ الكلمة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That consensus (ijma') of ahl al-hall wa al-aqd is only for the whole Ummah.
Correction: Here, the consensus refers to the agreement of electors in the capital city, not the entire Ummah, and it is binding on all Muslims in the regions.

Checkpoint
According to the third opinion, who is obligated to obey the imam chosen by the people of the capital city?
A) Only the people of that city
B) All Muslims in the regions
C) Only the scholars
D) Only the military

Key Takeaway
Juwayni presents three views on imamate establishment: nass on an individual, nass on a group, and consensus of electors.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you were living in a city where the imam died without appointing a successor, and the electors chose someone you personally disliked, would you still obey? What would prevent you from rebelling?
?
Juwayni warns that appointing two imams leads to 'corruption of relationships.' Look at your own life: where do you prioritize your own opinion over unity with the community, even when the leadership is not perfect?
?
How often do you honestly submit to the decisions of those in authority over you, even when you disagree? What fear or pride holds you back from obeying as this lesson demands?

Binding Nature of a Single Elector and Seizure by Force
Juwayni argues that a single elector can contract imamate and that seizure by force by a qualified person is valid.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's argument that imamate can be validly established by a single qualified elector or by forceful seizure by a qualified person, and the obligations of obedience in both cases.

Core Idea
Imamate is valid when contracted by one qualified elector or seized by a qualified person.

Imam Juwayni addresses two key questions about how legitimate authority is established. First, does it require a group of electors, or is a single person sufficient? Second, what if a qualified individual seizes power by force? This passage presents the Sunni position that both can produce a binding imamate, and that obedience is required to maintain order.

Guided Path
1
Understand the dispute about electors
Read the first part where Juwayni states that a single qualified elector can contract imamate, binding on others. Note the dissenting view requiring a group.
2
Grasp the consensus claim
Juwayni claims that once contracted by one elector, the imam cannot be deposed without cause, and it is a matter of consensus.
This claim seems to downplay the known disagreement.
3
Examine seizure by force
Read the second part: a qualified person who seizes power by force becomes imam. Note the citation of Sahl al-Tustari on duties of obedience.
This reflects the Sunni principle of avoiding fitna.
4
Reflect on practical implications
Consider how these rulings maintain political stability and guard against chaos, even with a forceful ruler.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فان عقدها واحد من أهل الحل والعقد فذلك ثابت ويلزم الغير فعله ، خلافا لبعض الناس حيث قال : لا تنعقد الا بجماعة من أهل الحل والعقد . قال الامام أبو المعالى : ( من انعقدت له الامامة بعقد واحد ، لزمت ، ولا يجوز خلعه من غير حدث وتغير أمر . وهذا مجمع عليه . فان تغلب من له أهلية الامامة وأخذها بالقهر والغلبة . فانه يكون اماما فقد سئل سهل بن عبد الله التستري : ما يجب علينا لمن غلب على بلادنا وهو امام ؟ قال : تجيبه وتؤدى اليه ما يطالبك من حقه ، ولا تنكر فعاله ولا تفر منه ، واذا ائتمنك على سر من أمر الدين لم نفشه
If a single one of the people of solution and contract contracts it (the imamate), then that is established and it is obligatory for others to act upon it, contrary to some people who said: it is only contracted by a group of the people of solution and contract. Imam Abu al-Ma'ali said: 'Whoever has the imamate contracted for him by a single contract, it becomes binding, and it is not permissible to depose him without an event and change of affairs. This is a matter of consensus.' And if one who qualifies for the imamate seizes it by force and domination, then he becomes imam. Sahl ibn Abd Allah al-Tustari was asked: 'What is incumbent upon us regarding one who has overcome our land and he is an imam?' He said: 'You respond to him and pay him what he demands from you of his right, do not deny his actions, do not flee from him, and if he entrusts you with a secret concerning religious matters, do not disclose it.'
Source pages p. 128

Commentary
This passage addresses two controversial issues: the number of electors needed to establish an imam, and the validity of a seizure of power by force. The author first states his position: a single qualified elector (from ahl al-hall wa al-aqd) can contract the imamate, making it binding on everyone else, and the imam cannot be removed without some new event or change. He claims this is a matter of consensus, though he had just mentioned a dissenting view requiring a group. Then he turns to the case of a qualified person who takes power by force and domination. Such a person becomes imam, and the people owe him obedience. He supports this with a narration from Sahl ibn Abd Allah al-Tustari, who instructs the questioner to obey the usurper, pay his due rights, not criticize him, not flee, and to keep secrets. This reflects the Sunni principle of accepting de facto authority to avoid chaos.

Key Terms
أبو المعالى
عقد واحد
أهل الحل والعقد
التغلب
سهل بن عبد الله التستري

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think that imamate can only be established by a group of electors or that seizure by force never produces legitimate authority.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies that both a single elector and a qualified forceful seizure can produce a valid imamate, and obedience is required in both cases.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, when can an imam be deposed?
A) At any time by popular demand
B) Only after a new event and change of affairs
C) When he seizes power by force
D) When a single elector contracts him

Key Takeaway
The imamate can be validly established either by the contract of a single qualified elector or by the forceful seizure of a qualified person, and both scenarios demand obedience to avoid disorder.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you face a leader you did not choose or one who gained power through means you consider unjust, does this passage demand that you set aside your reservations and obey? What is stopping you from doing so right now?
?
Sahl al-Tustari instructs 'do not deny his actions' and 'do not flee from him.' Which of these commands challenges your natural reaction the most? What concrete step can you take this week to align your attitude with this teaching?
?
The passage claims that imamate contracted by a single elector is binding and cannot be deposed without cause. If your community chose a leader you disagree with, would you be able to accept their contract as legitimate and submit? Why or why not?
