Witnessing, Appointing the Inferior, and the Sinful Imam
Juwayni discusses disagreements on witnessing, appointing an inferior, and a sinful imam (incomplete).

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Sunni positions on requiring witnesses for the imamate contract, the pragmatic permission to appoint a less qualified imam, and the incomplete discussion on a sinful imam.

Core Idea
In Sunni political theology, preventing fitna can override the preference for the most qualified imam.

Juwayni now turns to subsidiary issues in imamate: whether witnesses are required for the contract, and whether a less qualified person can be appointed when a more qualified one exists. These discussions reveal the pragmatic flexibility of Sunni political thought. The passage also begins to address the case of an imam who falls into sin, but the text is incomplete.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Notice the three distinct topics introduced.
The last sentence is cut off; we will only discuss the first two issues in depth.
2
Examine the witness disagreement
Identify two levels of disagreement: first, whether witnesses are required at all; second, among those who require them, what number is sufficient.
The text does not specify the numbers; this is a recognized difference of opinion among scholars.
3
Understand the principle of appointing the mafdul
Focus on the phrase 'خوف الفتنة' (fear of fitna) and 'يستقم أمر الأمة' (the affair of the Ummah being set straight). This shows that preventing harm and maintaining order are overriding considerations.
This principle is often invoked in Sunni political theory to justify pragmatic choices.
4
Reflect on the truncated third issue
The text begins to discuss an imam who becomes sinful after appointment but breaks off. Consider what the majority position might be: they generally hold that such an imam is not removed unless he changes religion.
This shows the importance of stability in leadership; the full view is found in other sources.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واختلف فى الشهادة على عقد الامامة بين مثبت وقاف . واختلف المثبتون فى عدد الشهود . ويجوز نصب المفضول مع وجود الفاضل خوف الفتنة ، وألا يستقم أمر الأمة . والامام اذا نصب ، ثم فسق بعد انبرام العقد ، قال الجمهور : ...
And they differed concerning the witnessing of the contract of imamate between affirmers and abstainers. And the affirmers differed on the number of witnesses. And it is permissible to appoint the inferior (mafdul) when the superior exists, because of fear of fitna, and so that the affair of the Ummah may be set straight. And the imam, when he is appointed, then becomes sinful after the contract is concluded, the majority said: ...
Source pages p. 128

Commentary
This passage covers three subsidiary issues. First, there is disagreement on whether witnessing is required for the validity of the imamate contract, with some requiring it and others abstaining from requiring it. Among those who require witnesses, they differ on the necessary number. Second, it is permissible to appoint an inferior candidate (mafdul) over a superior candidate to avoid civil strife and maintain order. This reflects a pragmatic Sunni principle: preventing harm takes precedence over appointing the best. Third, regarding an imam who becomes sinful after his contract (bay'a) is completed, the author notes the majority position but the text cuts off before stating it; from context, the majority view is that he is not removed unless he changes religion or something. The passage is incomplete, so commentary is limited to what is present.

Key Terms
الشهادة
عقد الإمامة
المفضول
الفسق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some assume that appointing an inferior imam is always forbidden regardless of circumstances.
Correction: The text explicitly permits it when it prevents fitna and maintains community stability, showing a flexibility in Sunni political thought.

Checkpoint
Why is it permissible to appoint an inferior (mafdul) imam despite the existence of a superior candidate?
A) The superior is not available
B) To avoid civil strife and set the Ummah's affairs in order
C) The inferior has more wealth
D) The inferior is more pious

Key Takeaway
Appointing a less qualified imam is permitted to avoid fitna, reflecting Sunni emphasis on communal stability over individual merit.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When have you insisted on the 'best' candidate for leadership in a community setting, stoking division, when a less qualified but more uniting person could have been appointed? Will you now reconsider your priorities in light of this lesson's permission to appoint the inferior to preserve unity?
?
If you were in a position to appoint a leader, would you choose the most competent person or the one who would maintain unity? What does that say about your trust in Allah's wisdom in allowing imperfect rulers for the sake of avoiding fitna?
?
The passage allows an inferior imam to prevent fitna. When have you contributed to fitna by insisting on your own preference in a matter of leadership? What will you do differently to prioritize communal stability over personal preference?

The Eleven Conditions for the Imam
Juwayni enumerates eleven conditions for the imam.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the eleven conditions that Juwayni lists for the imam, including areas of scholarly agreement and disagreement.

Core Idea
The imam must meet eleven specific conditions, from lineage to competence and character.

Juwayni provides a concise enumeration of the conditions required for the imam, drawing on classical Sunni scholarship. Some conditions are universally agreed upon, while others, like Quraysh lineage, have been disputed. This list summarizes the key qualifications for leadership.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic list of conditions and its translation. Notice the numbering and the brief comments on agreement or disagreement.
The passage includes a doxology at the end.
2
Identify the conditions
Go through each of the eleven conditions. Note which ones Juwayni explicitly marks as agreed or disputed.
Condition 5 is unclear due to a textual issue.
3
Notice the textual issue
Condition 5 has a problematic reading. The author notes uncertainty with [؟]. Consider how scholars deal with such ambiguities.
This is not a major doctrinal point but a technical one.
4
Reflect on the purpose
Juwayni concludes that this is a summary for benefit. Why is it important to define these conditions? How do they safeguard the Islamic polity?
This connects to the broader discussion of imamate in Islamic theology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأما شرائط أحد عشر شرطا فهي : ١- أن يكون من صميم قريش ( وقد اختلف فى هذا ) . ٢- أن يكون ممن يصلح أن يكون قاضيا من قضاة المسلمين مجتهدا لا يحتاج الى غيره فى الاستفتاء فى الحوادث ( وهذا متفق عليه ) . ٣- أن يكون ذا خبرة ورأى حصيف بأمر الحرب وتدبير الجيوش وسد الثغور وحماية الاسلام وردع الأمة والانتقام من الظالم والأخذ للمظلوم . ٤- أن يكون ممن لا تلحقه رقة فى اقامة الحدود ، ولا فزع من ضرب الرقاب ولا قطع الأبشار . ٥- أن يكون جها . حرما [؟] . ٦- أن يكون مسلما . ٧- أن يكون ذكرا . ٨- أن يكون سليم الأعضاء . ٩- أن يكون بالغا . ١٠- أن يكون عاقلا . ١١- أن يكون عدلا . هذا موجز ما يكتبه العلماء في موضوع ( الامامة ) ذكرناه للفائدة . *** والله أعلم وأعز وأكرم . وصلى الله وسلم وبارك على محمد نبي الرحمة ، وعلى آله وصحبه . ومن تبعهم بخير الى يوم الدين .
And as for the conditions: eleven conditions: 1- That he be from the pure stock of Quraysh (and there is disagreement about this). 2- That he be one who is fit to be a judge from the judges of the Muslims, a mujtahid who does not need others for giving legal opinions on events (and this is agreed upon). 3- That he possess experience and sound opinion regarding warfare, management of armies, guarding frontiers, protecting Islam, restraining the nation, taking revenge from the oppressor, and taking for the oppressed. 4- That he be one whom softness does not affect in establishing punishments, nor fear from striking necks nor cutting limbs. 5- That he be ... [؟] . 6- That he be a Muslim. 7- That he be male. 8- That he be sound of limbs. 9- That he be adult. 10- That he be sane. 11- That he be just. This is a summary of what the scholars write on the subject of the imamate; we mentioned it for benefit. *** And Allah knows best and is Most Mighty and Most Generous. May Allah send blessings and peace upon Muhammad, the Prophet of mercy, and upon his family and companions, and those who follow them in goodness until the Day of Judgment.
Source pages p. 130

Commentary
This passage is an enumeration. It lists eleven conditions for the imam. 1) Quraysh lineage (disputed). 2) Must be a qualified mujtahid judge (agreed). 3) Experienced in war and governance. 4) Firm in applying punishments without fear. 5) Unclear condition (possibly related to jihad or courage). 6) Muslim. 7) Male. 8) Sound limbs. 9) Adult. 10) Sane. 11) Just. Then a concluding note that this is a summary. The passage ends with a doxology praising God and blessing the Prophet.

Key Terms
شروط الإمام
قريش
مجتهد
قاض
عدالة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: All conditions for the imam are universally agreed upon by all scholars.
Correction: Juwayni explicitly notes that the condition of Quraysh lineage is disputed ('وقد اختلف فى هذا').

Checkpoint
Which condition for the imam does Juwayni mention as being agreed upon?
A) Being from Quraysh
B) Being a mujtahid judge
C) Being male
D) Being just

Key Takeaway
Juwayni outlines eleven necessary conditions for the imam, emphasizing that while most are agreed upon, the requirement of Quraysh lineage remains debated.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Which of these eleven conditions do you find most challenging to embody in your own leadership roles, and what concrete step will you take this week to strengthen that area?
?
Juwayni says the imam must 'not be affected by softness in establishing punishments.' When have you compromised a principle out of fear or softness? How will you prepare yourself to be firm for the sake of justice?
?
The imam must be a mujtahid, not needing others for legal opinions. Are you relying on others to tell you what is right, or have you taken the responsibility to learn and judge for yourself? What will you study next to grow in this?

Faith and Disbelief
beginner
5 min
The Definition of Faith as Tasdiq and the Necessity of Verbal Confession
Juwayni defines faith as tasdiq and explains the necessity of verbal confession.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's definition of faith as tasdiq (affirmation) and the distinction between inner conviction and verbal confession, along with the ruling on those who stubbornly refuse to confess.

Core Idea
Faith is inner conviction plus verbal confession; stubborn silence turns knowledge into disbelief.

Juwayni opens the chapter on faith with great emphasis, outlining the four pillars of the discussion. The first and foundational pillar defines the reality of faith (iman) as tasdiq (affirmation/assent). He grounds this in both linguistic usage and Quranic evidence, and then explains the conditions under which inner knowledge saves or does not save a person. This passage sets the stage for the entire doctrine of faith in the Ash'ari tradition.

Guided Path
1
Identify the four pillars
Read the passage and list the four pillars Juwayni mentions for the chapter on faith.
The pillars are: reality of faith, fate of sinful believers, increase/decrease of faith, and the Salaf's saying.
2
Focus on the definition of faith
Juwayni defines faith as tasdiq. Note that he says this is both the linguistic and technical meaning.
Tasdiq means affirmation or assent, not just knowledge.
3
Observe the Quranic evidence
Juwayni cites Quran 12:17 to show that 'mumin' means 'musaddiq' (one who confirms).
This supports his linguistic argument.
4
Understand the role of verbal confession
Juwayni explains that inner conviction alone benefits only if accompanied by verbal confession. If one refuses out of obstinacy, he is a disbeliever.
Examples: Pharaoh and the Jewish scholars who recognized Muhammad's prophethood but denied it.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فصل في الايمان ومعناه وذكر مصير المؤمنين ومآلهم من الجنة والنار وهذا فصل يتعين صرف الاهتمام اليه ، والاعتناء بدرك ما فيه و مضمون الفصل : أربعة أركان، أحدها : فى الايمان وذكر حقيقته . والثانى : فى ذكر مصير العصاة من أهل الايمان . والثالث : فى زيادة الايمان ونقصانه . والرابع : معنى قول سلف الأمة : أنا مؤمنون ان شاء الله عز وجل .. فأما الأول : فحقيقة الايمان عندنا التصديق ، وهو معناه في اللغة واللسان .. قال الله تبارك وتعالى : ( وما أنت بمؤمن لنا ( يوسف : ۱۷) معناه : وما أنت بمصدق . والمؤمن على التحقيق : من انطوى عقدا ، على المعرفة بصدق من أخبر عن صانع العالم وصفاته وأنبيائه . فان اعترف بلسانه ما عرفه بجنانه ، فهو مؤمن ظاهرا أو باطنا . وان لم يعترف بلسانه معاندا ، لم ينفعه علم قلبه ، وكان فى حكم الله تبارك وتعالى من الكافرين به ، كفر جحود وعناد وكذلك كان كفر فرعون ، وكل معاند جحود ، وكذلك عرف أحبار اليهود (۱) نبوة محمد صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم ، وصادفوا نعته في
Chapter on Faith and its Meaning, and Mention of the Destination of the Believers and Their Final Abode of Paradise or Hell. This is a chapter that requires directing attention to it and caring to grasp what is in it. The content of the chapter: four pillars. First: concerning faith and mentioning its reality. Second: concerning mentioning the fate of sinners among the people of faith. Third: concerning the increase and decrease of faith. Fourth: the meaning of the saying of the Salaf of the Ummah: 'We are believers if Allah wills.' As for the first: the reality of faith according to us is tasdiq (affirmation/assent), and that is its meaning in language and speech. Allah (Blessed and Exalted) said: 'And you are not a believer to us' (Yusuf:17), meaning: you are not a confirmator/musaddiq. And the believer, in truth, is one who inwardly holds a conviction of the truthfulness of the one who informed about the Maker of the world, His attributes, and His prophets. So if he acknowledges with his tongue what he knows in his heart, then he is a believer outwardly and inwardly. And if he does not acknowledge with his tongue out of obstinacy, then the knowledge of his heart does not benefit him, and he is, in the judgment of Allah the Exalted, among the disbelievers in Him—disbelief of denial and obstinacy. And such was the disbelief of Pharaoh, and every obstinate denier. And likewise, the Jewish scholars (1) recognized the prophethood of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family) and found his description in...
Source pages p. 84

Commentary
This passage is the opening of a new chapter on faith (iman). Juwayni first states the importance of the chapter and then outlines its four pillars: the reality of faith, the fate of sinful believers, increase and decrease of faith, and the meaning of the Salaf's saying 'we are believers if Allah wills.' He then explains the first pillar: faith is tasdiq (affirmation/assent), both linguistically and in Islamic law. He supports this with Quran 12:17, where 'mumin' means 'musaddiq' (one who confirms). The true believer is one who inwardly holds a firm conviction (yaqin) regarding the truth of the message about God, His attributes, and His prophets. If this inner conviction is accompanied by verbal confession (iqrar bil-lisan), then the person is a believer both outwardly and inwardly. However, if one has inner knowledge but refuses to confess out of stubbornness (inad), that inner knowledge is of no benefit; such a person is considered a disbeliever—specifically disbelief of denial (kufr juhud) and obstinacy (inad). Juwayni gives the example of Pharaoh and the Jewish scholars who recognized Prophet Muhammad's prophethood but rejected him out of obstinacy, thus being treated as disbelievers in Allah's judgment.

Key Terms
إيمان
تصديق
مؤمن
كافر
كفر جحود
عناد
لسان
جنان
اعتراف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that mere knowledge in the heart suffices for faith, even without verbal confession.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies that without verbal confession when able, especially if done out of obstinacy, one is not a believer but a disbeliever of denial.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the ruling on a person who knows the truth in his heart but refuses to verbally confess out of obstinacy?
A) He is a believer inwardly.
B) He is a disbeliever due to denial and obstinacy.
C) He is excused because his heart knows.
D) He is a believer but sinful.

Key Takeaway
Faith (iman) is essentially tasdiq (inner affirmation), but without verbal confession when capable, especially if done out of obstinacy, one is considered a disbeliever of denial.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever known something to be true about Allah—His oneness, His prophets, His commands—but remained silent out of fear of what others might think or say? After reading Juwayni’s words, how do you regard that silence now? Will you take a step today to say with your tongue what your heart believes?
?
Think of an area where your intellect accepts the truth of Islam, but your actions or public words do not match that conviction. Juwayni warns that obstinate silence can render inner knowledge useless. Is there a specific truth you have been hiding? What would it cost you to confess it openly, and is that cost greater than being counted among the obstinate deniers?
?
The Jewish scholars recognized the Prophet ﷺ but rejected him out of pride. Is there a truth about yourself—a sin you cling to, a repentance you delay, a duty you avoid—that you know is right but refuse to admit? What would it mean for your faith if you continued to refuse? Before Allah, can you afford to let your tongue contradict your heart?

The Reality of Iman as Tasdiq and Its Permanence Despite Sin
Juwayni defines iman as tasdiq and proves it remains despite sin.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that iman is inner tasdiq (affirmation) and that committing major sins does not remove the name of iman.

Core Idea
Iman is inner affirmation; sin does not remove it.

This passage from Imam Juwayni establishes the foundational Sunni definition of iman as tasdiq and defends it against those who equate sin with loss of faith. By examining Quranic evidence and logical distinctions, Juwayni shows that a person can commit major sins and still be called a believer. Let's unpack his argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the English translation. Let the author's own structure come through before you interpret.
Notice that Juwayni organizes his chapter into four pillars; this passage covers the first pillar.
2
Identify the definition of iman
Find where Juwayni states the reality of iman and his Quranic proof. Note that he equates iman with tasdiq (affirmation).
3
Understand the inner-outer distinction
Juwayni distinguishes between inner conviction, outward acknowledgment, and obstinate denial. See how he categorizes the true believer, the obstinate denier, and the hypocrite.
Pay attention to the examples of Pharaoh and the Jewish scholars.
4
Grasp the proof that iman remains despite sin
Juwayni argues that the name 'iman' does not disappear due to sin, citing that Quranic verses address the believers with obligations, implying they remain believers even when sinning.
This is a key Sunni position against the Kharijites and Mu'tazilites.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فصل في الايمان ومعناه وذكر مصير المؤمنين ومآلهم من الجنة والنار
وهذا فصل يتعين صرف الاهتمام اليه ، والاعتناء بدرك ما فيه و مضمون الفصل : أربعة أركان، أحدها : فى الايمان وذكر حقيقته . والثانى : فى ذكر مصير العصاة من أهل الايمان . والثالث : فى زيادة الايمان ونقصانه . والرابع : معنى قول سلف الأمة : أنا مؤمنون ان شاء الله عز وجل ..
فأما الأول : فحقيقة الايمان عندنا التصديق ، وهو معناه في اللغة واللسان .. قال الله تبارك وتعالى : ( وما أنت بمؤمن لنا ( يوسف : ۱۷) معناه : وما أنت بمصدق . والمؤمن على التحقيق : من انطوى عقدا ، على المعرفة بصدق من أخبر عن صانع العالم وصفاته وأنبيائه . فان اعترف بلسانه ما عرفه بجنانه ، فهو مؤمن ظاهرا أو باطنا . وان لم يعترف بلسانه معاندا ، لم ينفعه علم قلبه ، وكان فى حكم الله تبارك وتعالى من الكافرين به ، كفر جحود وعناد
وكذلك كان كفر فرعون ، وكل معاند جحود ، وكذلك عرف أحبار اليهود نبوة محمد صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم ، وصادفوا نعته في التوراة ، فجحدوه بغيا وحيدا [؟] ، فأصبحوا من الكافرين ، ومن أضمر الكفر وأظهر كلمة الايمان فهو المنافق الذى يتبوأ الدرك الأسفل من النار ، واسم الايمان لا يزول بالعصيان . والدليل عليه : أن معظم آيات التكليف ، مصدرة بذكر المؤمنين ، كما قال الله تبارك وتعالى : « يا أيها الذین آمنوا كتب عليكم الصيام » ) البقرة : ۱۸۳ ) .
Chapter on iman and its meaning, and mention of the destiny of the believers and their final abode in Paradise and Hell. This is a chapter that requires directing attention to it and being careful to grasp what is in it. The content of the chapter is four pillars: one of them: on iman and mentioning its reality. The second: on mentioning the destiny of sinners from among the people of iman. The third: on the increase and decrease of iman. The fourth: the meaning of the saying of the Salaf of the Ummah: 'We are believers, if Allah wills, Mighty and Majestic.' As for the first: the reality of iman, according to us, is tasdiq (affirmation), and it is its meaning in language and speech. Allah, Blessed and Exalted, said: 'And you are not a believer for us' (Yusuf 17) meaning: you are not an affirmer (musaddiq). The mu'min (believer) in reality is one who inwardly holds a conviction of knowledge affirming the truthfulness of the one who informed about the Maker of the world, His attributes, and His prophets. If he acknowledges with his tongue what he knows in his heart, then he is a believer outwardly and inwardly. If he does not acknowledge with his tongue, being obstinate, his heart's knowledge does not benefit him, and he is, in the judgment of Allah, Blessed and Exalted, among the disbelievers in Him, a disbelief of denial and obstinacy. Thus was the disbelief of Pharaoh, and every obstinate denier. Likewise, the Jewish scholars knew the prophethood of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family) and found his description in the Torah, but they denied him out of transgression and envy [؟], so they became among the disbelievers. And whoever conceals disbelief and manifests the word of iman, he is the hypocrite who occupies the lowest depths of the Fire. And the name of iman does not disappear due to sin. The evidence for that is that most verses of obligation begin with addressing the believers, as Allah, Blessed and Exalted, said: 'O you who have believed, fasting is prescribed for you' (Al-Baqarah 183).
Source pages p. 84

Commentary
This passage is a definition and proof passage. It opens by introducing a chapter on iman, its meaning, and the destiny of believers, stating that the chapter contains four pillars. It then immediately begins with the first pillar: the reality of iman. The author defines iman as tasdiq (affirmation), which he says is its linguistic meaning as well, quoting Quran 12:17 as evidence. He then elaborates on the true mu'min: one who inwardly holds a conviction (in the sense of a firm belief) of knowledge affirming the truthfulness of the one who informed about God, His attributes, and His prophets. Next, he distinguishes between outward and inward: if one acknowledges with his tongue what he knows in his heart, he is a believer both outwardly and inwardly; if he does not acknowledge due to obstinacy (mu'anada), his inner knowledge does not benefit him, and he is considered a disbeliever—specifically a kafir juhud (denier) and 'anid (obstinate). He gives examples: Pharaoh, and the Jewish scholars who recognized the prophethood of Muhammad (peace be upon him) and found his description in the Torah but denied him out of transgression and envy. Then he describes the hypocrite (munafiq) who conceals disbelief and outwardly professes iman, whose abode is the lowest depth of Hell. Crucially, he asserts that the name 'iman' does not disappear due to sin (i.e., a believer who commits major sins is still called a believer). He provides evidence: most verses of obligation (ayat al-taklif) begin with 'O you who have believed,' such as the fasting verse (2:183), indicating that the address includes sinners who are still believers. This is a foundational Sunni position against the Kharijites and Mutazilites who held that major sin expels one from iman.

Key Terms
الإيمان
التصديق
المؤمن
الكافر
الجحود
العناد
المنافق
العصيان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That iman is merely verbal profession without inner conviction, or that major sin removes one entirely from iman.
Correction: Juwayni shows that inner tasdiq is essential, and that sin does not negate the name of iman; a sinner is still a believer.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, what is the reality of iman?
A) Tasdiq (affirmation)
B) 'Amal (action)
C) Ma'rifa (knowledge without affirmation)
D) Iqrar (verbal confession only)

Key Takeaway
Iman is inner affirmation (tasdiq) and remains even with major sin.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever find yourself acknowledging Allah in your heart but refusing to let that knowledge change your actions? How does Juwayni's warning about the obstinate denier challenge you to align your inner conviction with outward practice?
?
If iman is not lost by sin, does that make you careless, or does it remind you that even in your worst moments the name 'believer' still applies—and with that comes accountability? What sin have you normalized that you need to repent from while still holding onto your iman?
?
When you recite 'O you who have believed' in the Quran, do you realize that Allah is speaking directly to you even if you have sinned? How should that reality change the way you approach His commands today?

The Hypocrite and the Persistence of Faith Despite Sin
Al-Juwayni defines hypocrisy and provides Qur'anic evidence for faith with sin.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Juwayni defines hypocrisy and uses Qur'anic evidence to argue that the name of faith remains with believers who sin.

Core Idea
Faith remains with the sinner; hypocrisy is inward disbelief with outward profession.

Imam al-Juwayni tackles a key theological question: does committing sins remove the name of faith from a believer? He defines hypocrisy clearly and then provides a powerful Qur'anic proof that the term 'believer' continues to be used for those who sin. This passage establishes a fundamental principle of Sunni theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice how al-Juwayni first defines the hypocrite before presenting his evidence.
2
Identify the definition of hypocrisy
Find the sentence that defines the hypocrite. Contrast inward disbelief with outward profession.
3
Understand the evidence
Al-Juwayni cites the Qur'anic address 'O you who have believed' in obligation verses. Why does this show the name of faith persists?
The address includes those who sin, so they are still called believers.
4
Reflect on the theological significance
Consider why this matters: it protects the status of Muslims who sin from being declared unbelievers.
This counters Kharijite and Mu'tazilite views.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
التوراة ، فجحدوه بغيا وحيدا ، فأصبحوا من الكافرين ، ومن أضمر الكفر وأظهر كلمة الايمان فهو المنافق الذى يتبوأ الدرك الأسفل من النار ، واسم الايمان لا يزول بالعصيان . والدليل عليه : أن معظم آيات التكليف ، مصدرة بذكر المؤمنين ، كما قال الله تبارك وتعالى : « يا أيها الذین آمنوا كتب عليكم الصيام » ( البقرة : ۱۸۳ ) .
The Torah, so they denied it out of transgression and envy, thus they became among the disbelievers. And whoever conceals disbelief and displays the word of faith, he is the hypocrite who will occupy the lowest level of the Fire. And the name of faith does not cease due to disobedience. The evidence for this is that most of the verses of obligation begin with mentioning the believers, as Allah the Blessed and Exalted said: 'O you who have believed, fasting is prescribed upon you' (Al-Baqarah: 183).
Source pages p. 85

Commentary
This passage defines hypocrisy and argues that the name of faith remains with sin. The author begins by referring to the Torah and the denial of it, but then defines a hypocrite as one who inwardly disbelieves but outwardly professes faith. He asserts that the designation 'believer' is not removed by committing sins. The evidence is that Allah addresses sinners as believers in the Qur'an, such as the verse on fasting which addresses 'O you who have believed' despite including those who may sin. This shows that the term 'iman' continues to apply to them.

Correction: Al-Juwayni shows that the Qur'an addresses sinners as believers, so the name of faith remains despite sin.

Key Takeaway
Faith is not removed by sin; hypocrisy is the concealment of disbelief with outward profession of faith.

Qur'an and Consensus: Faith Persists with Sin
Al-Juwayni provides Qur'anic and consensus evidence that sinners remain believers.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Juwayni uses a Qur'anic address and scholarly consensus to prove that sinful believers remain within the fold of faith.

Core Idea
Sinners are still believers — the Qur'an and consensus confirm.

Al-Juwayni continues defending the position that sin does not remove faith. He brings forward two powerful proofs: a direct Qur'anic verse addressing sinners as believers, and the consensus of the Muslim community that the worship of a sinful person is valid. These two arguments together solidify the doctrine that faith persists even with major sins.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation to get the overall argument. Notice that it presents two distinct pieces of evidence.
The passage is concise — each sentence carries weight.
2
Identify the Qur'anic evidence
Find the verse cited (Quran 66:8) and note how Al-Juwayni interprets it: Allah addresses sinners as 'O you who have believed' and commands them to repent. This proves they are still believers despite sin.
The command to repent does not remove the title of faith; it reinforces it.
3
Identify the consensus evidence
Look at the two consensus statements: (1) acts of worship are only valid from believers, and (2) the sinful person's fasting, prayer, and Hajj are valid. The logical conclusion is that the sinful person is still a believer.
This is a strong argument from the collective agreement of scholars.
4
Reflect on the overall conclusion
Ask yourself: why does this matter? It shows that sin does not expel one from faith, and the name 'believer' remains. This counters the view that major sins nullify faith.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فكل من يخاطب بتفاصيل التكاليف مندرج تحت اسم المؤمنين . وقد خاطب الله العصاة وأمرهم بالتوبة : ( فقال (۱) : ( يا أيها الذين آمنوا توبوا إلى الله ) ( التحريم : ٨ ( فخاطبهم بالايمان ، وأمرهم بالتوبة ( وأجمع المسلمون على أن العبادات لا تصح الا من المؤمنين . ثم أجمعوا على أن الفاسق يصح صومه ، وصلاته ، وحجه .
For everyone addressed with the details of obligations is included under the name of the believers. And Allah addressed the sinners and commanded them to repent: for He said: 'O you who have believed, repent to Allah' (At-Tahrim: 8). So He addressed them by faith and commanded them to repent. And the Muslims have agreed that acts of worship are not valid except from believers. Then they have agreed that the sinful person's fasting, prayer, and Hajj are valid.
Source pages p. 86

Commentary
This passage provides further evidence that the name of faith does not leave a person due to sin. First, the Qur'an addresses sinners as 'believers' when commanding them to repent (At-Tahrim: 8). Second, there is a consensus (ijma') that acts of worship are only valid from believers, yet the sinful person's worship (fasting, prayer, Hajj) is accepted. This proves they remain believers despite sin.

المؤمنين
العصاة
التوبة
الفاسق
إجماع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some people think that a sinner's acts of worship are invalid or rejected, or that sin removes the name of faith entirely.
Correction: Al-Juwayni shows that the Qur'an addresses sinners as believers, and consensus affirms that their worship (fasting, prayer, Hajj) is valid, proving they are still believers.

Checkpoint

A) The verse 'O you who have believed, repent'
B) The consensus that a sinful person's fasting and prayer are valid
C) The hadith about the intercession
D) The logical proof that faith is a light

Key Takeaway
The Qur'anic address and scholarly consensus both confirm that a person remains a believer even when committing major sins, and their worship is valid.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?
If you knew that a person's worship is accepted even if they sin, would you look at others who struggle with sin differently? What would it take for you to treat them as fellow believers rather than judging them?
?
Are there any sins you quietly think are too great for Allah to still call you a believer? What does the verse 'O you who have believed, repent to Allah' say to that hidden assumption?

Faith and Disbelief
beginner
4 min
Validity of Worship for Sinful Believers
Juwayni states that sinful believers are still addressed as believers and their worship is valid.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that sinful believers remain within the category of believers and their acts of worship are valid according to Islamic consensus.

Core Idea
Sinful believers are still believers; their worship is accepted.

This lesson addresses a common concern: do sins nullify one's faith and invalidate worship? Imam al-Juwayni clarifies that the Qur'an addresses sinners as believers and commands their repentance, and the Muslim consensus affirms that acts of worship from a sinful person are valid. Let's examine his concise argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Carefully read the Arabic text and its literal translation to grasp Juwayni's direct statement.
Notice the use of Qur'anic evidence immediately after the general claim.
2
Identify the two consensus points
The passage presents two separate consensuses: first, that acts of worship are only valid from believers; second, that the sinful believer's fasting, prayer, and Hajj are valid. Understand how the first supports the second.
3
Reflect on the implication
Consider why this matters: if worship from a sinner were invalid, repentance would be impossible. But the verse shows sinners are commanded to repent while still being addressed as believers.
This connects to the broader theme of faith and sin.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فكل من يخاطب بتفاصيل التكاليف مندرج تحت اسم المؤمنين . وقد خاطب الله العصاة وأمرهم بالتوبة : فقال (۱) : ( يا أيها الذين آمنوا توبوا إلى الله » ( التحريم : ٨ ( فخاطبهم بالايمان ، وأمرهم بالتوبة ( وأجمع المسلمون على أن العبادات لا تصح الا من المؤمنين . ثم أجمعوا على أن الفاسق يصح صومه ، وصلاته ، وحجه .
Everyone who is addressed with the details of the religious obligations is included under the name of the believers. And Allah addressed the disobedient and commanded them to repent, saying: (1) 'O you who have believed, repent to Allah' (at-Tahrim 66:8). So He addressed them with faith and commanded them to repent. And the Muslims have agreed that acts of worship are only valid from the believers. Then they agreed that the sinful person's fasting, prayer, and Hajj are valid.
Source pages p. 86

Commentary
This passage is a statement of consensus regarding the status of sinful believers. The author begins by stating that anyone addressed with the full details of religious obligations is included under the name of believers. This implies that even sinners are still considered believers. He then cites the Quranic verse commanding believers to repent, showing that the sinful are addressed as believers despite their sin. The consensus of Muslims is that acts of worship are only valid from believers, so the sinful believer's worship is valid. Further consensus confirms that the fasiq's fasting, prayer, and Hajj are valid.

Key Terms
المؤمنين
العصاة
العبادات
الفساق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believe that committing major sins removes a person from faith entirely and invalidates all their acts of worship.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies that sinful believers are still believers, as shown by the Qur'an addressing them with faith, and consensus affirms the validity of their worship.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, are the acts of worship of a sinful believer valid?
A) Yes, by consensus
B) No, they are invalid
C) Only prayer is valid
D) Only Hajj is valid

Key Takeaway
Sinful believers remain under the category of faith, and their worship is valid by consensus.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?
Allah addresses sinners like you as 'O you who have believed' while commanding you to repent. Does that title make you feel honored or exposed? How will you live up to that name today?
?
You know your worship is valid even with sins. Does that knowledge make you careless about sin, or does it drive you to repent? Be honest: which effect is actually happening in your life, and what will you change?

Worldly Rights of Sinful Believers
Juwayni enumerates the worldly rights and duties of sinful believers.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that sinful believers retain all worldly rights and duties within the Muslim community, including financial obligations, burial rites, and supplication.

Core Idea
Sin does not strip a believer of their communal rights or duties.

Juwayni now turns to the practical implications of classifying someone as a sinful believer. He lists the worldly rights and duties that such a person shares with the rest of the community. This passage clarifies that sin, while serious, does not sever a person from the ummah.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the translation. Note the list of rights and duties mentioned.
2
Identify the underlying principle
Notice that each listed item treats sinful believers like other believers. What principle does this reflect?
Think about the balance between sin and community membership.
3
Reflect on the significance
Consider why Juwayni includes this list. How does it prevent extremism and maintain community cohesion?
This connects to the broader Ahl al-Sunna position on sin.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم أثبتوا ا للفسقة ، ما يثبت للمؤمنين ، فأثبتوا عليهم ما أثبتوا عليهم المغائم والمغارم وأنفقوا عليهم من مال المسلمين ، وصلوا عليهم ودفنوهم مقابر المسلمين . وترحموا عليهم ، ولم يمتنعوا من الدعاء لهم ، وسؤال الله العفو عنهم
Then they established for the sinful believers what they establish for the believers: they imposed upon them what they imposed of taxes and expenditures, and they spent upon them from the wealth of the Muslims, and they prayed over them and buried them in the graves of the Muslims, and they showed mercy to them, and they did not refrain from supplicating for them and asking Allah for forgiveness for them.
Source pages p. 87

Commentary
This passage describes the worldly rights and duties of sinful believers in the Muslim community. The author lists several points: sinful believers are subject to the same financial obligations (like zakat) and receive from the public treasury. When they die, the community prays over them, buries them in Muslim cemeteries, and supplicates for them. This shows that they are not excluded from the community despite their sins.

The Distinction Between Iman and Islam
Juwayni distinguishes Iman and Islam with Quranic evidence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the relationship between Iman (faith) and Islam (submission) according to Juwayni, and why outward submission does not necessarily imply true faith.

Core Idea
Every believer is a Muslim, but not every Muslim is a believer.

Juwayni addresses a key theological question: are Iman and Islam the same? He clarifies that the term 'Islam' can refer either to the inner reality of faith or to mere outward submission. The Quranic verse about the bedouins illustrates this distinction. In this passage, Juwayni establishes the principle that true faith entails submission, but outward submission alone is not faith.

Guided Path
1
Understand the question
The passage begins with a hypothetical question asking whether there is a distinction between Iman and Islam. Notice that this is a common point of debate.
2
Read Juwayni's answer
Juwayni explains that 'Islam' has two possible meanings: it can refer to Iman (faith) or to outward submission. Read the Arabic carefully to see the two uses.
3
Analyze the Quranic proof
The verse from Surah Al-Hujurat (49:14) is cited. The bedouins said 'We have believed,' but Allah instructed them to say 'We have submitted' instead. This shows that claiming faith is not enough; it must be genuine.
The verse distinguishes between the claim of faith and the reality.
4
Draw the logical conclusion
From this, Juwayni deduces that the believer is the one who truly submits (inwardly), but outward submission does not make one a believer. Therefore, every believer is a Muslim, but not every Muslim is a believer.
This is the key takeaway.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فان قيل : هل تفرقون بين الايمان والاسلام فرقا ؟ قلنا : قد يطلق الاسلام والمراد به الايمان . وقد يطلق والمراد به الاذعان والاستسلام ظاهرا من غير انسان حقيقة الايمان . قال الله تبارك وتعالى قالت الأعراب آمنا قل : لم تؤمنوا . ولكن قولوا أسلمنا ) ) الحجرات : ١٤ ) .. فالمؤمن اذن : المستسلم . وقد لا يكون المستسلم مؤمنا . فكل مؤمن على ذلك مسلم . وليس كل مسلم مؤمنا
If it is said: Do you distinguish between Iman and Islam? We say: Islam may be used meaning Iman, and it may be used meaning outward submission and surrender without the reality of Iman. Allah said: 'The bedouins say, "We have believed." Say, "You have not [yet] believed; but say, "We have submitted.""' (Quran 49:14). So the believer is the one who submits, but the one who submits may not be a believer. Therefore, every believer is a Muslim, but not every Muslim is a believer.
Source pages p. 87

Commentary
This passage addresses a question about the relationship between Iman (faith) and Islam (submission). The author explains that the term Islam can refer either to the inner faith or to external submission. He cites the Quranic verse where the bedouins claimed faith but were told they have only submitted outwardly. Thus, the believer is one who inwardly submits with conviction, but outward submission alone does not make one a believer. Hence, every believer is a Muslim (since true submission accompanies faith), but not every Muslim (outward submitter) is necessarily a believer. This is a standard Ash'ari distinction.

Key Terms
الإيمان
الإسلام
الإذعان
الاستسلام

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Iman and Islam are exactly the same thing and always interchangeable.
Correction: While Islam can sometimes refer to Iman, it can also refer merely to outward submission, as shown in Quran 49:14. Thus, not every Muslim is a believer.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, is every Muslim also a believer?
A) Yes
B) No
C) Only if they perform Hajj
D) Only if they are righteous

Key Takeaway
Every believer is a Muslim, but not every Muslim is a believer.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you perform your daily prayers, do you ever pause to examine your heart: is this an act of genuine faith or just a habit? What would change if you truly believed that Allah sees your inner state?
?
This lesson shows that not every Muslim is a believer. If you were to be judged based on your inner convictions alone, would you be counted among the true mu'mineen? What specific steps can you take today to deepen your inner faith?
?
The bedouins were told to say 'we submitted' rather than 'we believed'. Have you ever exaggerated your own faith? What would it take for you to move from outward compliance to the reality of iman in your heart?

The Wa'idiyya Doctrine on Major Sinners
Juwayni states the Wa'idiyya view on major sinners.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Wa'idiyya belief that even a lifetime of obedience cannot save a person from eternal punishment if they die without repenting from a single major sin.

Core Idea
One major sin without repentance leads to eternal Hell, according to the Wa'idiyya.

Juwayni begins the second pillar of his chapter on sinners from the people of faith by presenting the position of the Wa'idiyya. This group, comprising Khawarij, Zaydis, and Qadariyya, held an extreme view on the fate of major sinners. Let's examine their claim before turning to Juwayni's refutation.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic passage once carefully. Note the key terms: الوعيدية, الخوارج, الزيدية, القدرية, الكبيرة.
The passage is one long sentence that states the entire position.
2
Identify the Wa'idiyya groups
The Wa'idiyya include three groups: Khawarij, Zaydis, and Qadariyya. Recognize that these groups shared a common view on eternal punishment.
3
Understand the conditions
The claim requires: (1) a lifetime of obedience, (2) committing one major sin, (3) no repentance, (4) dying knowing Allah. Result: eternal in Fire with polytheists.
Notice the severity: even full obedience does not save from one unrepented sin.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الركن الثاني من الفصل في ذكر العصاة من أهل الايمان : ذهبت الوعيدية من الخوارج والزيدية والقدرية : الى أن من يستوعب عمره في طاعة الله تبارك وتعالى ، ثم قارف كبيرة واحدة ، ولم يوفق للتوبة عنها ومات عارفا بالله تبارك وتعالى ، فهو خالد في النار مع المشركين ، الذين ما أتوا حسنة قط (٣) . والعجب : أنهم يثبتون أحكام الله (٤) تبارك وتعالى على ما تجرى به عوائد العقلاء والذى ذكروه من أقبح القبائح فى مقتضى العقول شاهدا . وان زعموا : أن الحسنات تحيط بسيئة واحدة لتناقضهما . فهلا أحبطوا السيئة بالحسنات ؟ ولو فعلوا ذلك لشهدت لهم آية من كتاب الله تبارك وتعالى وهى قوله : « ان الحسنات يذهبن السيآت » ) هود : ١١٤ ) ..
The second pillar of the chapter concerning the sinners from the people of faith: The Wa'idiyya among the Khawarij, Zaydis, and Qadariyya held that whoever spends his whole life in obedience to Allah, then commits one major sin, and is not granted repentance from it, and dies knowing Allah, is eternally in the Fire along with the polytheists who never did any good deed. And it is strange: they establish Allah's rulings according to the customs of rational people, and what they mentioned is among the ugliest of ugly things in the judgment of reason. And if they claim that good deeds surround a single evil deed because they contradict each other, why do they not cancel the evil deed with the good deeds? If they did that, a verse from the Book of Allah would testify for them: 'Indeed, good deeds erase evil deeds' (Hud 11:114).
Source pages p. 87

Commentary
This passage is a refutation of the view of the Khawarij, Zaydis, and Qadariyya (collectively called Wa'idiyya) who believed that a person who commits a major sin without repentance goes to Hell forever, even if he had a lifetime of obedience. The author finds this strange because such a ruling is contrary to rational custom. He then points out an inconsistency: if good deeds surround and contradict evil deeds, why do they not cancel each other? The Quran itself states that good deeds erase evil deeds, implying that a sinful believer can be forgiven through his good deeds. Thus, eternal punishment is not necessarily the fate of every major sinner who dies without repentance; good deeds can intercede.

Key Terms
الوعيدية
الخوارج
الزيدية
القدرية
الكبيرة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to the Wa'idiyya, what happens to a person who commits a major sin and dies without repentance, even if they spent their life in obedience?
A) They are forgiven due to their obedience.
B) They are eternally in the Fire with the polytheists.
C) They spend a limited time in Hell then enter Paradise.
D) They are reincarnated.

Key Takeaway
The Wa'idiyya believe that a single unrepented major sin condemns a believer to eternal Hell, regardless of prior obedience.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you were to die tonight, would you be like the person the Wa'idiyya describe—having many good deeds but an unrepented major sin? What specific sin do you need to repent of right now?
?
The Wa'idiyya erred by denying God's mercy, but their view forces us to take major sins seriously. Which major sin in your life have you been treating as 'small' or as something you can deal with later? What will you do about it today?
?
Imagine standing before Allah with a lifetime of obedience wiped out by one sin you neglected to repent from. How does that possibility change the way you approach repentance tonight?

Juwayni's Refutation and Quranic Evidence
Juwayni refutes the Wa'idiyya using Quran 11:114.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Juwayni's rational critique of the Wa'idiyya position and his use of Quranic evidence to argue that good deeds can erase evil deeds.

Core Idea
If good deeds surround evil deeds, why not cancel them? The Quran says they do.

Juwayni now turns to critique the Wa'idiyya claim. He finds it strange that they apply human customs inconsistently, and he points out a logical contradiction. Finally, he invokes a Quranic verse that directly supports the idea that good deeds erase evil deeds, thus undermining the Wa'idiyya position.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the passage and note Juwayni's tone of astonishment: 'والعجب' (And it is strange).
2
Analyze the logical inconsistency
Juwayni points out that if good deeds 'surround' evil deeds (i.e., outweigh them), then they should cancel the evil deed. The Wa'idiyya fail to apply that logic.
Note the question: 'فهلا أحبطوا السيئة بالحسنات؟' (why do they not cancel the evil deed with the good deeds?)
3
Examine the Quranic evidence
Juwayni cites Surah Hud 11:114: 'Indeed, good deeds erase evil deeds.' This verse supports the cancellation and contradicts the Wa'idiyya view.
The verse directly states that good deeds remove evil deeds, implying forgiveness for major sinners through their good deeds.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الركن الثاني من الفصل في ذكر العصاة من أهل الايمان : ذهبت الوعيدية من الخوارج والزيدية والقدرية : الى أن من يستوعب عمره في طاعة الله تبارك وتعالى ، ثم قارف كبيرة واحدة ، ولم يوفق للتوبة عنها ومات عارفا بالله تبارك وتعالى ، فهو خالد في النار مع المشركين ، الذين ما أتوا حسنة قط (٣) . والعجب : أنهم يثبتون أحكام الله (٤) تبارك وتعالى على ما تجرى به عوائد العقلاء والذى ذكروه من أقبح القبائح فى مقتضى العقول شاهدا . وان زعموا : أن الحسنات تحيط بسيئة واحدة لتناقضهما . فهلا أحبطوا السيئة بالحسنات ؟ ولو فعلوا ذلك لشهدت لهم آية من كتاب الله تبارك وتعالى وهى قوله : « ان الحسنات يذهبن السيآت » ) هود : ١١٤ ) ..
The second pillar of the chapter concerning the sinners from the people of faith: The Wa'idiyya among the Khawarij, Zaydis, and Qadariyya held that whoever spends his whole life in obedience to Allah, then commits one major sin, and is not granted repentance from it, and dies knowing Allah, is eternally in the Fire along with the polytheists who never did any good deed. And it is strange: they establish Allah's rulings according to the customs of rational people, and what they mentioned is among the ugliest of ugly things in the judgment of reason. And if they claim that good deeds surround a single evil deed because they contradict each other, why do they not cancel the evil deed with the good deeds? If they did that, a verse from the Book of Allah would testify for them: 'Indeed, good deeds erase evil deeds' (Hud 11:114).
Source pages p. 87

Commentary
This passage is a refutation of the view of the Khawarij, Zaydis, and Qadariyya (collectively called Wa'idiyya) who believed that a person who commits a major sin without repentance goes to Hell forever, even if he had a lifetime of obedience. The author finds this strange because such a ruling is contrary to rational custom. He then points out an inconsistency: if good deeds surround and contradict evil deeds, why do they not cancel each other? The Quran itself states that good deeds erase evil deeds, implying that a sinful believer can be forgiven through his good deeds. Thus, eternal punishment is not necessarily the fate of every major sinner who dies without repentance; good deeds can intercede.

السيئات
العجب

Misconception & Correction

Correction: Juwayni argues that good deeds can cancel evil deeds, and the Quran supports this, so a major sinner is not necessarily eternally punished.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?

?
The Wa'idiyya relied on their own rational customs to judge God's mercy. Where in your life have you replaced God's revealed promise of forgiveness with your own harsh judgment? Will you repent from that attitude right now?
?
Juwayni finds it strange that people establish Allah's rulings based on human customs. In what ways do you rely on societal norms or your own logic rather than the Quran when it comes to your hope for forgiveness? How will you change that today?

Faith and Disbelief

The Salaf on Faith: Actions Included
Juwayni presents the Salaf's view that faith includes actions and thus increases and decreases.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the early Salaf defined faith as including actions (ma'rifah, iqrar, 'amal) and how this leads to the conclusion that faith increases and decreases.

Core Idea
Faith includes actions, so it increases with obedience and decreases with sin.

Imam Juwayni begins the third pillar of his discussion by presenting the position of the Salaf (the early pious generations). For them, faith was not merely an internal state but comprised three elements: heart knowledge, tongue confession, and limb actions. This comprehensive definition has direct implications for whether faith can vary in intensity.

Guided Path
1
Identify the three components of faith
Read the passage and note three components: knowledge in the heart, affirmation with tongue, action with limbs. The Salaf considered all acts of obedience as part of faith.
This is a definitional claim.
2
Analyze the Qur'anic evidence
Juwayni cites Quran 2:143 where prayer is called 'faith'. This supports that actions are included in the term 'iman'.
The Companions understood the verse to refer to prayer towards Jerusalem.
3
Connect to increase and decrease
Since actions are part of faith, increasing good actions increases faith, and decreasing them decreases faith. This is the logical consequence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الركن الثالث: في زيادة الإيمان ونقصانه. ذهب أئمة السلف إلى أن الإيمان معرفة بالجنان، وإقرار باللسان، وعمل بالأركان. فهؤلاء أدرجوا الطاعات كلها تحت اسم الإيمان. وهذا غير بعيد في التسمية، وقد سمى الله تبارك وتعالى الصلاة إيمانا، في قوله: «وما كان الله ليضيع إيمانكم» (البقرة: 143) أراد الصلاة التي صلوها إلى بيت المقدس. فمن أطلق اسم الإيمان على الطاعات كلها يقول على مساق أصله: يزيد الإيمان بزيادة الطاعات، وينقص بنقصانها. ومن قال: الإيمان هو التصديق. فمن علم وعرف حقا، فلا يتفاوت التصديق بالأعمال زادت أو نقصت. وهذا كما أن العاقل قد ينكف عن ارتياحه ومساره لعلمه بالموت، والمنهمك في لذاته واتباع شهواته عالم بالموت علمه، ولكن غلبة هواه تستحثه على ما يتعاطاه.
The third pillar: concerning the increase and decrease of faith. The imams of the Salaf held that faith is knowledge in the heart, affirmation with the tongue, and action with the limbs. So these included all acts of obedience under the name of faith. And this is not far-fetched in naming, and Allah, Blessed and Exalted, called prayer faith in His saying: 'And Allah would never let your faith be lost' (Al-Baqarah: 143) meaning the prayer which they prayed towards Jerusalem. So whoever applies the name of faith to all acts of obedience says, following his principle: faith increases with the increase of acts of obedience and decreases with their decrease. And whoever says: faith is assent. Then one who knows and truly knows, then assent does not differ with actions whether they increase or decrease. And this is like the intelligent person may refrain from his joy and his path due to his knowledge of death, and the one engrossed in his pleasures and following his desires knows death with his knowledge, but the dominance of his desire urges him to what he does.
Source pages p. 89

Commentary
This passage presents the classical theological dispute regarding the definition of faith (iman) and whether it increases and decreases. Juwayni begins the third pillar (al-rukn al-thalith) by stating the position of the early Salaf: faith comprises three components: knowledge in the heart (ma'rifah bi al-janan), verbal confession (iqrar bi al-lisan), and action of the limbs ('amal bi al-arkan). By including actions, they subsume all acts of obedience under the term 'faith'. Juwayni supports this by citing the Qur'anic verse 'And Allah would never let your faith be lost' (Q 2:143), which the Companions understood to refer to their prayer towards Jerusalem, thus calling prayer 'faith'. Consequently, if actions are part of faith, faith increases with obedience and decreases with disobedience. Juwayni then presents the opposing view: those who define faith exclusively as assent (tasdiq) argue that assent is a single, indivisible state; a person either believes or does not. Just as a person may know death is certain yet indulge in pleasures, similarly a believer's faith (assent) remains unchanged even if his actions vary. The analogy of the wise person (al-'aqil) and the pleasure-seeker (al-munhamik) illustrates that knowledge alone does not guarantee action; thus, actions are not constitutive of faith but are manifestations of it. Juwayni does not decisively side with either view here but records both, noting that the fourth pillar will clarify further. The lesson thus identifies the core disagreement: whether faith is merely internal assent (tasdiq) or includes external actions, and whether it is subject to increase and decrease.

Key Terms
الإيمان
المعرفة بالجنان
الإقرار باللسان
العمل بالأركان
زيادة الإيمان
نقصان الإيمان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some assume the Salaf defined faith only as internal belief without actions.
Correction: The Salaf explicitly included actions as part of faith, as Juwayni shows.

Checkpoint
According to the Salaf, how does faith change with actions?
A) Faith is constant regardless of actions.
B) Faith increases with obedience and decreases with disobedience.
C) Faith only increases, never decreases.
D) Actions are irrelevant to faith.

Key Takeaway
The Salaf define faith as including actions, so faith can increase and decrease based on one's deeds.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you sinned this week, did you feel any decrease in your faith, or did you separate your actions from your belief? How would your response to sin change if you truly believed that every disobedience diminishes your very faith?
?
If your prayer is considered an act of faith itself, not just a demonstration of it, how would you prepare for and perform your next prayer differently? What would it mean to approach prayer as faith in motion?
?
You claim to have knowledge in your heart and affirmation on your tongue. But if your actions were the evidence, would they testify to a strong faith or a weak one? What one action—starting today—can you add to visibly increase your faith?

Faith and Disbelief

The Counter-View: Faith as Indivisible Assent
Juwayni presents the counter-argument from those who define faith as assent, denying increase or decrease.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the opposing view that restricts faith to assent (tasdiq) and why this view denies that faith can increase or decrease.

Core Idea
Faith is assent, which is indivisible — it does not change with actions.

After presenting the Salaf's comprehensive definition, Juwayni now turns to a significant counter-position. Some scholars argued that faith is nothing other than internal assent (tasdiq), which is a single, all-or-nothing reality. This leads to a very different conclusion about the variability of faith.

Guided Path
1
Understand the definition of faith as tasdiq
The opposing view defines faith solely as assent (tasdiq). This is a single act of the heart that does not admit of degrees.
2
Analyze the argument that assent does not change with actions
If one truly knows (assents), then increasing or decreasing actions does not affect the assent. Assent is one and the same regardless of external behavior.
3
Examine the analogy of the intelligent person and the pleasure-seeker
Juwayni gives an analogy: a sane person avoids joys due to knowledge of death, but a pleasure-seeker knows death yet follows desires. This shows that knowledge (assent) can be present without corresponding action, and action does not affect the assent.
The analogy illustrates that actions are not constitutive of faith.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الركن الثالث: في زيادة الإيمان ونقصانه. ذهب أئمة السلف إلى أن الإيمان معرفة بالجنان، وإقرار باللسان، وعمل بالأركان. فهؤلاء أدرجوا الطاعات كلها تحت اسم الإيمان. وهذا غير بعيد في التسمية، وقد سمى الله تبارك وتعالى الصلاة إيمانا، في قوله: «وما كان الله ليضيع إيمانكم» (البقرة: 143) أراد الصلاة التي صلوها إلى بيت المقدس. فمن أطلق اسم الإيمان على الطاعات كلها يقول على مساق أصله: يزيد الإيمان بزيادة الطاعات، وينقص بنقصانها. ومن قال: الإيمان هو التصديق. فمن علم وعرف حقا، فلا يتفاوت التصديق بالأعمال زادت أو نقصت. وهذا كما أن العاقل قد ينكف عن ارتياحه ومساره لعلمه بالموت، والمنهمك في لذاته واتباع شهواته عالم بالموت علمه، ولكن غلبة هواه تستحثه على ما يتعاطاه.
The third pillar: concerning the increase and decrease of faith. The imams of the Salaf held that faith is knowledge in the heart, affirmation with the tongue, and action with the limbs. So these included all acts of obedience under the name of faith. And this is not far-fetched in naming, and Allah, Blessed and Exalted, called prayer faith in His saying: 'And Allah would never let your faith be lost' (Al-Baqarah: 143) meaning the prayer which they prayed towards Jerusalem. So whoever applies the name of faith to all acts of obedience says, following his principle: faith increases with the increase of acts of obedience and decreases with their decrease. And whoever says: faith is assent. Then one who knows and truly knows, then assent does not differ with actions whether they increase or decrease. And this is like the intelligent person may refrain from his joy and his path due to his knowledge of death, and the one engrossed in his pleasures and following his desires knows death with his knowledge, but the dominance of his desire urges him to what he does.
Source pages p. 89

Commentary
This passage presents the classical theological dispute regarding the definition of faith (iman) and whether it increases and decreases. Juwayni begins the third pillar (al-rukn al-thalith) by stating the position of the early Salaf: faith comprises three components: knowledge in the heart (ma'rifah bi al-janan), verbal confession (iqrar bi al-lisan), and action of the limbs ('amal bi al-arkan). By including actions, they subsume all acts of obedience under the term 'faith'. Juwayni supports this by citing the Qur'anic verse 'And Allah would never let your faith be lost' (Q 2:143), which the Companions understood to refer to their prayer towards Jerusalem, thus calling prayer 'faith'. Consequently, if actions are part of faith, faith increases with obedience and decreases with disobedience. Juwayni then presents the opposing view: those who define faith exclusively as assent (tasdiq) argue that assent is a single, indivisible state; a person either believes or does not. Just as a person may know death is certain yet indulge in pleasures, similarly a believer's faith (assent) remains unchanged even if his actions vary. The analogy of the wise person (al-'aqil) and the pleasure-seeker (al-munhamik) illustrates that knowledge alone does not guarantee action; thus, actions are not constitutive of faith but are manifestations of it. Juwayni does not decisively side with either view here but records both, noting that the fourth pillar will clarify further. The lesson thus identifies the core disagreement: whether faith is merely internal assent (tasdiq) or includes external actions, and whether it is subject to increase and decrease.

Key Terms
التصديق
العاقل
المنهمك
الهوى

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that if actions are bad, faith must be less.
Correction: This view insists that faith (assent) is unaffected by external deeds; a sinful person can still have full assent.

Checkpoint
According to those who define faith as assent, why does faith not increase or decrease?
A) Actions are not part of faith, and assent is uniform.
B) The Qur'an says faith is complete.
C) The Prophet fixed faith at one level.
D) Actions are too variable to measure faith.

Key Takeaway
The opposing view holds that faith is purely internal assent, which does not increase or decrease based on external actions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You know death is coming. But do you live as though you do? The 'intelligent person' in the passage refrains from joy because of his knowledge. Where does your knowledge of death actually stop you from indulging? Be honest: name one pleasure you have not restrained despite knowing it harms your akhirah.
?
The engrossed one knows death, but his desires win. You cannot claim ignorance. So if you sin today, what is the real explanation? Not that your faith is weak—you assent fully—but that you choose your desires over your knowledge. Will you own that choice, or hide behind 'my iman is low'?
?
This passage forces you to separate your faith from your actions. If your faith is intact even when you sin, then you have no excuse to soften your resolve. What are you going to change right now that you have been postponing because you thought you needed to 'increase iman' first?

The Secret Behind 'I Am a Believer If Allah Wills'
Juwayni explains why many lay believers say 'I am a believer if Allah wills', attributing their doubt to lack of certain knowledge.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the Salaf said 'I am a believer if Allah wills' and the distinction between correct belief (ʿaqd) and certain knowledge (maʿrifah).

Core Idea
Correct belief without evidence is not certain knowledge.

Juwayni opens the fourth pillar of his creed by addressing a practice of the early Muslims: adding 'if Allah wills' when declaring faith. He reveals a theological 'secret' that many may not realize — the masses possess correct belief, but it is not knowledge because it lacks evidential certainty. This passage explains why doubt about one's own faith is not necessarily a flaw but a sign of intellectual humility.

Guided Path
1
Read the source passage
Read the Arabic passage and its translation to understand Juwayni's argument.
2
Identify the 'secret'
Look for why Juwayni says the masses say 'insha Allah'. Note the distinction between ʿaqd and maʿrifah.
The secret is that their belief is not knowledge.
3
Understand the role of evidence
Juwayni states that knowledge requires evidence (adillah) and the masses cannot provide it independently. This is key to the doubt.
4
Consider the implication for faith declaration
Reflect on why saying 'insha Allah' is a mark of honesty about one's level of certainty, not doubt in God.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الركن الرابع: في قول من سلف: إنا مؤمنون إن شاء الله عز وجل. وها أنا أذكر في ذلك سرا لا أستجيز إخلاء هذه العقيدة الشريفة منه، فأقول: جماهير الخلق من أهل السنة على عقد صحيح في الدين، يتعلق بالمعتقد على ما هو به، ولكن عقدهم ليس بمعرفة. فإن المعتقد لا يعرف ضرورة، وجماهير الخلق لا يستقلون بالأدلة. ولو امتحن الملقبون بالإمامة فضلا عن العوام بدلالة قاعدة واحدة، لبقوا فيها حيارى. فإذا كانت المعرفة لا تثبت دون الأدلة،
The fourth pillar: concerning the statement of the predecessors: 'We are believers if Allah, Mighty and Majestic, wills.' And here I mention about that a secret which I do not allow this noble creed to be empty of it. So I say: The masses of people from Ahl al-Sunnah have a correct belief (ʿaqd) in the religion, which attaches to the object of belief as it is, but their belief (ʿaqd) is not knowledge (maʿrifah). For the object of belief is not known necessarily (Ḍarūrah), and the masses of people are not independent with evidence. And if those called imams, let alone the common people, were tested by the indication of a single principle, they would remain perplexed about it. So when knowledge (maʿrifah) is not established without evidence.
Source pages p. 89

Commentary
This passage is the opening of the fourth pillar (al-rukn al-rabi‘), addressing the well-known practice of the Salaf (predecessors) to add the qualification 'if Allah wills' (inshā’ Allāh) when declaring one's faith. Juwayni presents a theological 'secret' (sirr) behind this: the masses of Ahl al-Sunnah possess a correct creed (ʿaqd ṣaḥīḥ) in terms of affirming the correct object of belief (al-muʿtaqad), but their creed is not knowledge (maʿrifah). Why? Because knowledge requires certainty derived from evidence (dalīl), and the common people do not have the ability to deduce proofs independently. Even those labeled as imams would struggle if tested on a single principle (qā‘idah wāḥidah). Since knowledge cannot be established without evidence, and the masses rely on taqlīd (imitation), their belief is not certain knowledge. Therefore, when they say 'I am a believer if Allah wills', they express their uncertainty about the completion of faith or its acceptance, or they indirectly acknowledge that their belief is not based on definitive proof. Juwayni implies that true certainty (yaqīn) comes from demonstrative reasoning (istidlāl). The lesson ends mid-sentence at the page break, but the core point is clear: the qualification is a mark of humility in recognition of the lack of independent verification.

Key Terms
عقد صحيح
معرفة
ضرورة
أدلة
تقليد
استدلال

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Salaf said 'I am a believer if Allah wills' because they doubted God's promise.
Correction: Juwayni explains that the qualification is due to the lack of certain knowledge (maʿrifah) derived from evidence, not doubt in the object of belief itself.

Checkpoint
According to Juwayni, why do the masses of Ahl al-Sunnah say 'I am a believer if Allah wills'?
A) Because they doubt God's existence.
B) Because their belief is not based on certain knowledge from evidence.
C) Because they fear hypocrisy.
D) Because they imitate the predecessors without understanding.

Key Takeaway
The Salaf's use of 'if Allah wills' when declaring faith reflects the humility of acknowledging that one's belief, though correct, may not be based on certain knowledge derived from evidence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite 'I believe in Allah' or your shahada, do you truly know why you believe, or are you relying on the faith of your parents, teachers, or community? Are you willing to honestly examine the evidence for your creed, even if it means facing your own intellectual limitations?
?
Juwayni says that even those called imams would be perplexed if tested on a single proof. Does this humility resonate with you, or do you assume your faith is more certain than it actually is? What would it look like for you to say 'If Allah wills' about your own state of belief today?
?
The 'secret' Juwayni reveals is that the Salaf's qualification was not doubt but honesty about lacking independent knowledge. Have you ever felt the weight of that honesty? Are you willing to stop pretending your faith is unwavering and instead ask Allah for certainty, even if it means admitting you are still searching?

The Rationale for Saying 'In sha Allah' about Faith
Juwayni explains the reason for saying 'in sha Allah' when professing faith, and warns against extreme interpretations.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the early scholars said 'in sha Allah' when professing faith, and that it reflects humility about the stability of one's conviction, not doubt in the creed.

Core Idea
Saying 'in sha Allah' about faith is humility, not doubt.

Juwayni addresses a common practice among early Muslims: adding 'if Allah wills' when stating one's faith. Some might think this implies uncertainty about God's existence. In this passage, Juwayni clarifies that the exception is due to the nature of belief itself—it is not knowledge, and one cannot guarantee its firmness. Even the most devout can experience moments of weakened certainty. This lesson unpacks his reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the literal translation. Notice the key terms: 'aql (عقد), 'ilm (علم), yaqeen (يقين).
2
Understand the core argument
Juwayni argues that since belief (al-‘aqd) is not knowledge, it has no fixed measure. This leads to uncertainty about whether one's belief meets the required limit. Thus, saying 'in sha Allah' is a precaution.
3
Reflect on the state of the knower
Juwayni adds that even the knower may lose the 'taste of certainty'. This shows the exception is not for doubt about the creed but about one's own stability.
This aligns with the salaf's practice.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم اذا لم يكن العقد علما ، لم يكن له ضبط ، ولم يدر أن العقد المأتى به فى الاستقرار في الحد المطلوب أم هو دونه ؟ وهو فى ملتطم الظنون ، وتعارض الشبهات فلما كان كذلك حسن على حسب ذلك ، أن يقولوا : انا مؤمنون إن شاء الله . والعارف قد تعتريه حالة يعدم فيها مذاق اليقين . فهذا وجه الاستثناء .
Then if the belief is not knowledge, it has no fixed measure, and it is not known whether the belief brought forth is stable at the required limit or below it? And it is in the turmoil of opinions and the conflict of misconceptions. So since it is so, it is appropriate accordingly that they say: 'We are believers, if Allah wills.' And the knower may be overtaken by a state in which he lacks the taste of certainty. So this is the face of the exception (al-istithnā’).
Source pages p. 90

Commentary
This passage explains the reasoning behind the practice of saying 'in sha Allah' (إن شاء الله) when professing faith. Juwayni argues that if belief (al-‘aqd) is not knowledge (al-‘ilm), then it has no fixed measure or guarantee. One cannot be sure whether one's belief has reached the required level of stability or is below it. Moreover, belief is subject to the turmoil of opinions and conflicting misconceptions. Therefore, it is appropriate to say 'We are believers, if Allah wills' as a precaution. He adds that even the knower (al-‘ārif) may experience a state where he loses the taste of certainty. Thus, the exception is not due to doubt about the truth of the belief, but out of humility and awareness of the uncertainty of one's final state and the stability of one's conviction. This aligns with the practice of the early salaf, who said it out of fear of a bad end, not doubt.

Key Terms
استثناء
عقد
يقين
عارف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think saying 'in sha Allah' implies doubt about the existence of God.
Correction: Juwayni clarifies it is about the stability of one's faith and fear of the end, not doubt in the creed.

Checkpoint
Why is it appropriate to say 'in sha Allah' when professing faith, according to Juwayni?
A) Because faith is uncertain and may be false.
B) Because one cannot be sure if one's belief is at the required level of firmness, and faith is subject to doubts.
C) Because one must always doubt one's belief.
D) Because the knower never has certainty.

Key Takeaway
Saying 'in sha Allah' about faith expresses humility about the stability of one's conviction, not uncertainty about the creed.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you say 'in sha Allah' about your own faith, do you genuinely feel the weight of your uncertainty about your final state, or has it become a mere habit? What would it mean for you to say it with full awareness of your dependence on Allah's will?
?
Have you ever experienced a 'loss of the taste of certainty' in your faith? How did you respond? Are you willing to admit that such states are part of your journey, or do you feel pressured to always project certainty?
?
This lesson teaches that the exception is humility, not doubt. If you truly believed that your faith is not within your own control but a gift from Allah, how would that change the way you speak about your iman to others and to yourself?

Faith and Disbelief
